Usage guidelines 

Google is proud to partner with libraries to digitize public domain materials and make them widely accessible. Public domain books belong to the 
public and we are merely their custodians. Nevertheless, this work is expensive, so in order to keep providing this resource, we have taken steps to 
prevent abuse by commercial parties, including placing technical restrictions on automated querying. 

We also ask that you: 

+ Make non- commercial use of the files We designed Google Book Search for use by individuals, and we request that you use these files for 
personal, non-commercial purposes. 

+ Refrain from automated querying Do not send automated queries of any sort to Google's system: If you are conducting research on machine 
translation, optical character recognition or other areas where access to a large amount of text is helpful, please contact us. We encourage the 
use of public domain materials for these purposes and may be able to help. 

+ Maintain attribution The Google "watermark" you see on each file is essential for informing people about this project and helping them find 
additional materials through Google Book Search. Please do not remove it. 

+ Keep it legal Whatever your use, remember that you are responsible for ensuring that what you are doing is legal. Do not assume that just 
because we believe a book is in the public domain for users in the United States, that the work is also in the public domain for users in other 
countries. Whether a book is still in copyright varies from country to country, and we can't offer guidance on whether any specific use of 
any specific book is allowed. Please do not assume that a book's appearance in Google Book Search means it can be used in any manner 
anywhere in the world. Copyright infringement liability can be quite severe.

PREFACE. 



A CAREFUL author will so concoct his prefaxje as to entice praise from the unwary 
critic. That dangerous animal, it is well known, feeds only on prefaces, with, for 
sauce, a snilF of the paper knife with which he has cut a few leaves of the volume 
under review. Fortunately a preface addressed to such kind and indulgent critics 
as the members of our Club need not resort to these ignoble devices. 

The Editors have all but finished their work, and the Sixth Volume of the 
Gazette rises against them in judgment. It has been their aim to make it a faithful 
history of the life of the Club during the year 1883 ; and a history which shall 
be worthy of the traditions of the Club by the manner in which it has been told. 
If they have succeeded, their thanks are due to the members of the Club who have 
supported them so constantly, and especially to Theo. Godlee, Ashley Barrett, Fred. 
Williams, H. G. A. Brown, and the N. H. family. One name has been omitted, 
and it is a great satisfaction to the Editors to feel that the Club has secured as Editor 
of the Gazette for the ensuing year so constant and valued a contributor in the 
past as R. G. Francis. For him they ask a continuance of the kindness which has 
been shown to them.

Answer to Correspondent 8 



Tks next Gazeite will be published on Thursday, 8th 
February. Copy should be sent in not later than 
Tuesday the 6th prox. 

As our members will have learnt by the Hon. See's 
circular, the Committee have decided not to make any 
official nominations of Officers or Committeemen to the 
next General Meeting. A feeling has pervaded the Club 
for some time that nominations should be made by indi- 
vidual members and not by the Committee as a body, and 
we believe that it is in deference to this feeling that our 
present Committee have come to the conclusion expressed 
in the circular sent out. Whether this conclusion is wise 
or not will be decided by the action of the 1883 Committee, 
and though we are strongly of opinion that any Committee 
of our Club is or will be the best judge of the merits of 
embryo Club politicians, we must confess to a wish to see 
the nominations to office made by individual members if it 
is at all practicable. Nominations by a Committee must 
allways be liable to the suspicion of being the nomination 
of a clique, though there is not the least doubt that in the 
case of the "London" the suspicion is unfounded and 
unjust. We could wish that the rule relative to the 
attendance of the rank and file at Committee Meetings 
were less stringent, so that unofficial members could attend 
and judge for themselves how the business of the Club is 
carried on. Surely a rule which gave the Committee 
power to exclude ordinary members from its debates would 
be sufficient. 



It behoves those members who are in favour of the new 
system on trial for 1883 to be up and doing, as we under- 
stand that if it is unsuccessful the old plan will be resorted 
to for next year. 



COMMITTER 



At a meeting of the Committee, held on Monday, 4th 
December, 1882, the following gentleman was elected a 
member of the Club : — 

Namoi Froposer. Seconder. 

Auguste Malempre, 19, Lower 
PhUUmore Place, Kensington ... W. T. Thorn. O. Thorn. 

It was decided to award Racing Caps to the following 
gentlemen : — W. K. Adam, M. H. Jephson, C. A. E. Pollock, 
H. R. Reynolds, and J. S. Whatton. 

The following resolution was carried unanimoasly 

That no Officer or Committeeman be nominated officially 
by the Committee at the next General Meeting for the 
1883 Committee." 

The name of E. E. Leeds was struck off the Club list in 
accordance with Rule 18. 

At a meeting held on Monday, 18th December, 1882, the 
following resignations were accepted : — G. T. Langridge 
and M. B. O'Reilly. 

It was decided to hold the Annual General Meeting on 
Thursday, 1st February, 1883. 

A letter was read from the Stanley Bicycle Club inviting 
the L.B.C. to allow their name to appear in the list of

There were fourteen meetings of the present Committee 
last year. The following is the attendance score of the 
Committeemen : — 

Out of a possible 14 : Eniest Barrett 7, Boyce 4, H. G. A. 
Brown 14, Buckler 14, Coleman 14, Godlee* 13, Hough 13, 
Jolly 9, F. W. Morris 11, N. B. Morris 11, Naylor 3, 
Nops 14, Price 13, Scrutton 13, S. P. Smith 13, F. M. 
Williams 12, W. J. Williams 14. 

Out of a possible 12 ; Ashley Barrett 7, Arthur Herbert 6. 

Out of a possible 6 : R M. Gruggen 1. 

On 13th February F. M. Williams was elected Editor of 
the Gazette, and on 27th February Arthur Herbert was 
elected as his successor on the Committee. Ashley Barrett 
was elected on the same date. R. M. Gruggen was elected 
on 14th August in the place of H. Jennings, resigned. 

E. S. Price, Hon. Sec. 



ANNUAL REPORT. 

Members are requested to send in to me at once accounts 
of tours, and also notes of distances of 100 miles, or over, 
ridden in one day. 

Racing members are requested to let me have lists of 
prizes won by them during the year. 

I must apologise for not having this notice put in the 
last Gazette, and shall be much obliged if members will 
reply to this promptly, E. S. Pbice, Hon. Sec. 



BLUE BOOK. 
Any alterations of addresses, districts, etc., that members 
wish to make, should be sent in to me as soon as possible. 

E. S. Price, Hon. Sec. 



GENERAL MEETING. 
The Annual General Meeting will be held at the 
"London" (late "King's Head") Tavern, Fenchurch 
Street, E.C., on Thursday, Ist February, 1883, at 
6.30 p.m. 

Under Rule 30, no nomination or other motion can be 
brought forward at the meeting, unless notice in writing 
of the motion shall have been sent to me at least 14 days 
before that date ; the last day is therefore the 18th instant. 

An agenda paper, containing particulars of all motions, 
etc. to be considered at the meeting, will be sent to members 
as soon after the 18th January as possible. 

The report and balance sheet for 1882 will also be sent 
round in due course. 

The following motions and nominations have already 
been received : — 

Motion by Mr. W. A. Smith— 

"ThAt the Club award a certain number of medals (such number 
to be hereafter decided upon) to such membexB of the L.B.G* who, on 



the day of the Bath Trial, shall complete the 100 miles from Bath to 
Hounslow, on tricydes, within twelve hours. The medals to be similar 
to those given for the District Attendance Prizes, but of gold instead 
of silver, and suitably engraved." 

Motion by Mr. H. R. Reynolds — 

"That the Annual Bace Meeting of the L.B.C. for 1883 be held at 
the Crystal Palace." 

Motion by Mr. T. E. Scrutton— 

(1.) "In Rule 11, to insert before 'member of the Committee,' 
' past and present,' and to insert after such words, ' or members of the 
Club, being at the time an officer of either the O.U.Bi.C. or C.U.Bi.C.' 

(2.) " That there be an entrance fee of half a crown for the Bath 
Trial."

L.B.C. DRAMATIC SOCIETY. 
(Copy of Circular sent round December 22nd to the Club.) 

Dear Sir, — With the consent of the Captain, the Dramatic Society 
have resolved upon giving a Performance, to take place at St. George's 
HaU, Langham Place, on Saturday, February 17th, 1888. The piece 
selected is One Hundred Thousand Pounds," to be preceded by a 
Farce. 

If you are not already a subscriber, may I ask you to become one, 
since upon the support accorded it by members the success of the 
imdertaking wiU chiefly depend. The subscription is 5s., which entitles 
the subscriber to a stall, price 5s. — Awaiting your tarly reply, I am, 
dear sir, very truly yours, Alfred Barker, Hon. Sec. 

On application to the Acting Manager, R. M. Gruggen, 
Lynton Villa, Grosvenor Road, Richmond, reserved stalls, 
5s. each, or five stalls for £l Is., balcony (unreserved) 2s. 6d. 
each. Members of the Society are entitled to a stall free 
on application before the date of the performance, and it 
would greatly help the Acting Manager if an early demand 
were made, so as to estimate numbers. 

It is requested, though not compulsory, that gentlemen 
will appear in evening dress, and, to avoid disappointment, 
it is as well to state that no one except officials will be 
admitted on the sti^e. 

Any further information can be obtained of the Acting 
Manager, or of Alf. Barker, Chepstow Road, Croydon. 

N.B. — Members of 1881 will not be asked to subscribe 
again for this season, but will remain still members of the 
Society.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



CAMBRIDGE UNIVERSITY BICYCLE CLUB. 

The annual long-distance road competition in connection 
with the above club came off on Wednesday, November 1. 
The weather was very unfavourable, there being a strong 
wind against the competitors, and owing to this and the 
state of the roads the course was shortened from fifty to 
about forty-two miles. The route laid out was from St. 
Ives through Eltisley and Potton, and skirting Biggleswade, 
home by Barton. Seven riders started, which included 
E. H. Brown, last year's winner by 4min. from Whatton. 
Having travelled to St. Ives by train, Mr. 6. D. Day, 
captain of the club, sent them off shortly after twelve o'clock. 
E. H. Brown, St. John's, assumed the lead, and made the 
pace until reaching Biggleswade (20 miles), having had to 
face a very strong wind, so strong that more than one of 
the competitors walked part of the way. Upon getting out 
of the wind just after passing Biggleswade, J. S. Whatton, 
Trinity, who had been close behind Brown, took the lead, 
and at once commenced to force the pace. At Orwell Hill, 
six miles from home, Whatton began to draw away, and he 
eventually won by 3min, the full time being 3 hours 30min. ! ! 
J. D. Batten, Trinity, was third, twenty two minutes behind 
Brown ; whilst A. B. W. Whatton, Trinity, whose back- 
wheel tyre came off, was fourth, two minutes in the rear of 
Batten. The other starters were : C. L. Schwind, Trinity; 
L. C. Carr, Trinity ; and A. W. Rumney, Trinity Hall. 
J. S. Whatton, Trinity, holds the 20-guinea challenge cup 
for the year. Mr. A. J. Crichton, Trinity, the winner of 
the race in 1880, kindly attended to the requirements of 
tha riders. 

The Michaelmas Term club races came off on the bicycle 
path on Wednesday afternoon last. The weather was fine, 
but a strong wind was against the competitors in the early 
part of the lap. Notwithstanding the fact that the 
committee decreased Olive's start in the final of the mile, 
the Johnian won very easily. The freshmen* s race did not 
bring out any promising new blood. C. L. Schwind won 
the five miles race easily, as did J. S. Whatton the ten miles 
event. Judges : C. A. E. Pollock. B.A., Trinity, and G. D. 
Day, B.A., St. John's. Details : 

One Mile Handicap, — First Heat : E. H. Brown, St, 
John's, 75 yds. 1 ; J. S. Whatton, Trinity, scratch 2 ; A. S. 
Hicks, Christ's, 100 yds. 3 ; R. F. Cooke, Caius, 150 yds, ; 
M. G. Foster, Trinity, 200 yds. 0. The limit man led until 
entering the last lap, when Brown, Hicks, and W^hatton 
passed him in the order named. Whatton got into second 
place 250 yds. from home, and did try his best to beat Brown, 
who won by six yards ; a similar distance separated second 
and third ; Forster was a bad fourth. Time, 3min. 8Jsec. — 
Second Heat : E. J. Olive, St. John's, 200 yds. 1 ; A. B. W. 
Whatton, Trinity, 75 yds. 2 ; G. N. Latham, Trinity Hall, 
125 yds. 3; M. Crosse, Magdalene, 176 yds. 0. The limit 
man was never headed, and won very easily ; ten yards 



divided second and third. Time, 2min. 55sec. — Final 
Heat : Olive, 1 ; A. B. Whatton, 2 ; Brown, 0. J. S. 
Whatton did not start. Owing to Olive's performance in 
the second heat, the committee met and decided to decrease 
his allowance by fifty yards ; this, however, did not have 
any effect on the result, for the " dark one " again won 
easily, this time by about seventy-five yards ; Whatton beat 
Brown in the last lap by twenty yards. Time, 3min. Ifsec. 

Four Miles Freshmen s Race. — A. W. Rumney, Trinity 
Hall, 1 ; J. H. Masters, Trinity, 2 ; E. F. Eliot, Trinity, ; 
H. S. Izard, Trinity Hall, 0. The latter retired after 
completing three miles. Rumney assumed the lead in the 
second lap, and, although hard pressed by Masters in the 
middle part of the last lap, he held his ground and won 
easily. Masters easing off on finding pursuit hopeless ; Eliot 
was 120 yards behind second man. Time, 15min. 6sec. 

Five Miles liace. (Competitors against Oxford and 
London B.C. debarred.)— C. L. Schwind, Trinity, 1 ,- G. N. 
Latham, Trinity, 2 ; A. S. Hicks, Christ's, 3 ; J. D. Batten, 
Trinity, ; L. C. Carr, Trinity, 0. Schwind took the lead 
at the start, closely followed by Hicks. The six competitors 
rode in the same order and in close company until half the 
distance had been traversed, when Cooke began to fall into 
the rear. At the third mile the Caius rider was more than 
half a lap to the bad, whilst at the fouirth he was overlapped, 
and he shortly afterwards retired. Schwind continued to 
hold the lead until half a mile from the finish, when Latham 
put on a good spurt, and for the first time assumed the 
lead. Schwind, however, stuck close behind Latham, and 
250 yards from home passed him, eventually Avinning by 
eight yards. Hicks, who rode well, was beaten for second 
place by three or four yards ; Batten was fourth, thirty 
yards behind Hicks, Cooke was last. Time, 18min. ll|sec. 

Ten Miles Itace.—J. S. Whatton, Trinity, 1 ; A. B. W. 
Whatton, Trinity, 2 ; E. H. Brown, St. John's, 0. J. S. 
Whatton went off with the lead, and was attended by his 
brother, Brown being well up ; this order was maintained 
until the seventh lap, when Brown took a turn in front and 
remained there until completing the third mile, when J. S. 
Whatton took the lead again. At three and a half miles 
A. B. Whatton went to the front and made the pace for two 
miles, after which Brown did the same for a couple of miles, 
being displaced at the seventh mile by J. S. Whatton. The 
Johnian again led at the ninth mile, but both Whattons 
were close up to him, and half a mile from home J. S. 
Whatton spurted to the front. All were close together on 
entering the last lap, but the race was never in doubt, and | 
Whatton sen. won easily by eight yards ; about six yards 
divided second and third. j 

Mr. F. B. Westcott, who has ridden the last two years in 
the team against the London Bicycle Club, and who 
graduated as senior classic last year, has been elected to a 
fellowship at Trinity College.— From The Field, November, i 
nth, 1882. I

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



HOW I INDUCED HER MOTHER TO BRING HER 
TO THE WEST DISTRICT BALL. 

A SEASONABLE REMINISCENCE. 

I am induced to recount this incident in my bicycling 
career, not on account of there being anything strange or 
uncommon in it, but merely in the hope of its being of use 
to some other member who may possibly chance to be placed 
in the same predicament as I was. 

Deeply wrapped in thought, I was riding towards Ham- 
mersmith along a well-known wood-paved thoroughfare. 
Heaven knows I had good cause for thought ; all my 
ingenuity was at work ; all my inventive faculties were 
busy ; all my machinatory talents were occupied. But in 
vain. It was the day of the West District Ball, and her 
mother would not allow her to come to it. Now who was 
this lady ? Well, she was simply the most lovely, accom- 
plished, sweet-tempered girl I had ever seen. I liad met 

her only once, but I loved devotedly, and her name was 

but why should I divulge it ? No 1 wild horses shall not 
tear it from those deep recesses of my inner consciousness 

where it is deposited ! therefore she shall be known as . 

Ab I have said before, her mother would not allow her to 
come to the ball. Imagine my despair! In vain I begged, 
prayed, and entreated ; it was indeed in vain. The old 
lady was one of those curious people who believe 
in spiritualism ; indeed, she went further in her belief 
than most people— affirming that the spirits guided 
her in everything, and showed her what she was to do by 
their manifestations. So that the only answer I was able 
to get to my final request was—" No, it could not be ; 
unless (Oh! what vast oceans of hope that 'unless' 
spread out before my enraptured vision) — unle&s," she said, 
" the spirits clearly manifested that it was their mandate 
for her to attend the ball with her daughter." (Alas ! this 
latter part condensed my vast oceans into a microscopic 
dewdrop of hope.) After this I could obtain nothing 
further from her, and was so thrown into the state of 
perplexity I have before alluded to. I had purchased two 
ladies' tickets on the chance of the spirits following the 
example of Barkis, but hitherto they had remained 
obdurate. At last the day of the ball arrived. Ever 
since buying the tickets I had constantly carried them 
in my breast pocket, but as yet saw no chance of their 
being used. So, as I have already said, I was riding towards 
Hammersmith, when I saw approaching me in a hansom 

and her mother. As the cab passed me, shook 

her head with a rueful countenance. I was not able to 
deduce from this whether she meant that there had been no 
manifestation, or an adverse one ; so, determined to find 
out my fate, I turned down one of the streets on my left- 
hand and sped back by a road parallel to that which I had 
recently been going along. As I was racing along, regard- 
less alike of fines or imprisonment, an idea seized me. If 
the spirits would not grant a manifestation, I must. But 



how ? At first I had not a notion, but by degrees a plan, 
vast, and at first somewhat alarming, began to loom out, 
vague and shadowy, in those inner recesses already alluded 
to. My mind travelled back to the far-distant days of 
childhood, and I remembered the bounding ball of boyhood's 
boisterous days. Was it not made of India-rubber ? Were 
not, also, the tyrea of my wheels ? If one bounded, why 
should not the other ? At any rate I could but try. Such 
were the thoughts that came hurtling through my brain, 
when suddenly I found I had again reached the road along 

which was travelling, that is to say, if she had not 

already turned off. However, I had not gone three yards 
along the crowded highway before half a mile ahead I per- 
ceived two omnibuses broken down, completely blocking 
up the road, and on the other side of them, waiting 

to pass, the cab in which was seated ; and all my 

thoughts were centred in calculating that the distance was 
just sufficient to get up the impetus necessary for the 
desperate purpose I had in my mind. I seized the two 
ball tickets in my teeth, and hurled my machine along at 
a pace never before reached by anyone, amateur or pro- 
fessional. When within a hundred yards of the obstruction 
I seized the handles in such a vice-like grasp that the 
impress of my fingers remains on them to this day, and 
springing up from the treadles, whilst at the same time I 
gripped the forks with my knees as if on horseback. 
I lifted the machine into the air with me. Seventy yards 
further on I struck the road with both wheels at once, and 
rebounding, as I had expected, I topped the omnibuses, and 
guiding the bicycle directly over the cab as I flew through 
the air, at the right moment I let go the two tickets, which 
fluttered gently into the hands of the ladies. Canting my 
wheel on one side, I turned down a quiet street, and by dint 
of putting on the brake and back-treadling I moderated 
the speed of my serial flight, and regaining terra firma not 
half a mile from the spot where I last left it, I rode gently 
home. Arrived there, I found that it only wanted four 
hours to the commencement of the ball, so I had to hurry 
up and begin dressing. On account of the excessive s|)eed 
at which I was travelling whilst in the air, I was of course 
invisible to any but the eye of love, consequently the old 
lady received the cards in the light of a manifestation from 
the spirits, and took her daughter to the ball. Needless to 

say, I danced every dance with that evening, but I 

did not marry her, because I belong to the West District, 
and consequently have not yet caught the contagion. 

DiSTRicms.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 5 



TERPSICHOREAN. 
The third of the South East District Annual Dances 
came oflF on Wednesday, 6th December, and found me 
once more in attendance at the Croydon Town Hall. I 
don't know why, but somehow or other, however strong may 
have been my resolutions to the contrary, I always end by 
taking that first step towards doing my duty in the capacity 
of Steward, though why I should have been selected for 
that office goodness (and, perhaps, myD. C.) only knows. 

Anyhow, here we are once more. I am under the wing 
of a weaker brother and his better-half, and our iBy draws 
up at the door punctually to the stroke of half-post seven. 
We are the first arrivals ; but no, her^ is our worthy District 
Captain sitting amid the desolation of the cloak room, like 
Marius among the ruins of Carthage. The first few file 
into the hall just as the last tinned tack has been driven 
home by the hammer, led by the Zulu in full " war paint." 
'Tis eight o'clock, and yet I seem to miss two or three well- 
known faces. Where was that I heard that our Captain 
would grace the occasion with his august presence? but 
still " he Cometh not" Was it " he " or " she said ? " "I 
looked but could not see " my friend the presiding genius 
of Essex byways, the Mark-Tapleyan cheerfulness of whose 
face has often revived my drooping spirits. Where is the 
hero of the Bath Koad and Lawn Tennis tournament ? or 
his "Proximo accessit?" Was it true that they had 
already begun to train for fresh laurels awaiting them in 
the New Year? There was a darkly-whispered rumour 
that one well-known member who was expected was 
suffering from the effects of a surfeit of pastry, but I scorn 
to repeat such scandal. It was ten minutes or so past 
eight when our D.C. approached the platform where the 
band was discordantly tuning up, and the ball is opened 
with an extra waltz. 

To those of a contemplative turn of mind, a dance affords 
excellent opi)ortunity. I stood in a quiet comer and 
watched the couples as they passed on their planetary way, 
each pair revolving on their own axis, yet round a common 
though imaginary centre. There went by the familiar 
form of the elder Tliorn, guiding his fair partner through 
the crowd of dancers with the same skill as of yore he 
would drive his iron steed rouDd the cinder-clad path of 
LiUie or Stamford Bridge, though not then with that un- 
troubled mien. And then glided by, as Mercury " on wingM 
feet," an ex-Secretary from the North- West, meteor-like, 
among the less speedy orbs. But here I was buttonholed 
by our D.C.'s Substitute, who, with the Editor, turned my 
attention to matters of sterner import— as to who was to 
be the new Captain ? and wherewithal were we to manu- 
facture a new Editor ? to both of which questions an answer 
did not readily suggest itself. 

But I am straying from my subject. Among the ladies 
I noticed many familiar faces, and not a few fresh ones. 
May we hope that those of our fair visitors who were not 



present for the first time enjoyed themselves as much as on 
former occasions, and that those who had not graced our 
dance with their presence before will do so again another 
year. My descriptive powers quite fail me when I would 
describe the dresses. Imagine a background of white, 
tinged here and there with pale pink and blue, relieved by 
an occasional black, and lit up with a sprinkling of orange, 
red, and terra cotta. I pass with a sigh (believe' me, not of 
satisfaction but regret) from the airy regions of the ball- 
room to the less sentimental pleasures of tlie supper table. 
Here the refreshments were, as last year, well provided by 
Mrs. Pusey, and our friend the ubiquitous waiter simply 
surpassed himself in his readiness to see our wants supplied. 
If the daintier dishes were only half as good as was a certain 
sirloin of beef and bottled beer the supper must be voted to 
have been first-rate. 

I did not count myself, but have heard officially that 
there were about twenty-six in all fewer than last year, but 
I am sure I was never at a dance — and I have been to a 
good many in my *younger days — where there were fewer 
wallflowers among the ladies, or few men hanging about the 
doors. Every man must be considered to have done Ids 
duty, and the ladies — cela va sans dire. Even the Napo- 
leonic features of him on whom fell the brunt of the work, 
and who brought it to a most successful issue, relaxed into 
a smile as he gazed on the scene. It was past two before 
the strains of the band died away. The last adieux were 
exchanged, and our third S.R Dance was consigned to the 
records of the past. Atoycn^s. 



A THREE* DAYS' TOUR. 

October 1882 has proved, as everyone knows, a signal 
failure so far as the weather was concerned, and consequently 
I was prevented from taking my last short tour of the year 
until the last day of that month, when it became more 
settled. 

It was a fine bright morning when Elgood called for me, 
and we made a start in excellent spirits with the intention 
of reaching some town in North Essex that night. The 
roads were, as we expected, made heavy with mud, but the 
clear and invigorating air and blue sky ab6ve made amends 
to a great extent for this evil below. Bumping along the 
Finchley road we soon reached Barnet and Potter s Bar ; 
here we took the right-hand turning, and were soon riding 
along those lovely wooded lanes to Clieshunt which have 
been the admiration of so many N. W. men during the past 
season. Passing Cheshunt House, we were not long in 
striking the main Hertford road, which we found so heavy 
and bad that in spite of all our endeavours it was as much 
as we could do to drive our wheels through the sandy mud 
and shingle. At last we got clear of it, and, passing through 
Hoddesdon, we continued a mile or two onwards, and then, 
turning to the right, worked along at a ^ood pace until we 



Digitized by 



6 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



descended a hill, and over a level crossing, into the quiet 
old-fashioned town of Stanstead Abbots. I believe it has 
for long been a problem which none could satisfactorily 
solve, as to how riders in the W. and N.W. can find their 
way across country into Essex, therefore it gives me 
pleasure in recommending the above-mentioned route, 
which in summer time will be found a good one, both 
as regards the surface of the roads and the scenery. 
We had to walk up a steep hill out of the town, and then 
a pleasant ride of a mile or so brought us in sight of Roydon, 
but between us and that village the river Stort had lately 
overflown its banks, and left the fields on either side and 
about one hundred yards of the road covered with water. 
Sitting well back, we made a dash through the muddy water, 
and soon found that our back wheels were completely sub- 
merged, but by carefully steering for the centre of the road 
we both succeeded in arriving on dry land again in safety. 
At Eoydon we partook of light refreshments at an inn, and 
were shown by the landlady a splendid specimen of a death's- 
head moth, which she had caught some weeks before. It 
measured at least four inches across from the extremity of 
orie wing to the extremity of the other ; it had been en- 
trapped by the tempting smell of beer, and had attempted 
to drown itself in that enervating drink, when it was rescued 
and saved from destruction by the hands of the landlady. 
The next few miles on to Harlow were hilly and winding. 
Here we stopped for lunch at the "George" (B.T.C.), and 
did not leave again until the middle of the aftenioon. The 
country on through Sheering is undulating and fairly pretty. 
At Hatfield Broad Oak there is a fine old church, which 
had once been a Benedictine Priory, which still retains 
signs of its former beauty. From here to Dunmow the road 
was fairly level and good, but the country uninteresting. 
It now became dark and cloudy, so, lighting up our lamps, 
we faced the last nine miles to Braintree in the dark, and 
so excessively dark was it that, if my lamp had failed to 
perform its duties, as Elgood's did, we should have had no 
little diflficulty in proceeding on our way in safety. Big 
drops of rain threatened to give us a wetting more than 
once, but luckily it held over, and we hailed the lights of 
Braintree with the glad hearts which only 'cyclists feel after 
a hard day's journey. We put up for the night at a com- 
fortable Hotel, the "George," a B.T.C. house. In the 
coffee-room we made the acquaintance of its only other 
occupier, a young retired oflBcer, whose company we much 
enjoyed. After a walk round the town, which did not 
appear to possess any especial attraction, we returned to 
the hotel, and in the company of our new friend spent a 
most enjoyable musical evening. 

Next day, although windy, favoured us by being fine ; 
so, saying adieu to our friend, we were soon riding up the 
long liill out of Braintree. A few miles of undulating road 
led us to Halstead, a pleasant, clean-looking town, situated 
on the side of a hill which took all our strength to sur- 



mount. On to Sudbury, our next destination, the roads 
were undulating, and the scenery not particularly inviting. 
There is a long and somewhat steep hill leading down into. 
Sudbury, which town contains several very interesting 
churches. St. Gregory's, in particular, is of the greatest 
interest. It was also at this town that the renowned 
painter Gainsborough was born, and spent his early days 
in practising his art. A ride of some three miles took us 
on to Long Melford, a small town teeming with architec- 
tural and antiquarian interest. The church here is visited 
by people from all parts, and is a splendid building. 
Melford Hall, also, an Elizabethan structure, has an 
interesting history of its own. The next thirteen miles on 
to Bury St. Edmunds we rode at a quick rate, having the 
wind at our backs (for once), and no objects to visit on the 
road. After lunch at this ancient and to all appearance 
thriving town, we had a look at the Abbey Gate, built in 
the fourteenth century, which is especially worthy of notice, 
and contains many architectural details that deserve careful 
attention. The beautiful Norman tower near to St. James's 
Church is a splendid example of that period ; and as we 
gazed upon its simple but pleasing ornamentation, we 
marvelled how such a large and substantial tower could 
have been formed with the axe, and not the chisel. We 
also had a look into St. James's and St. Mary's Churches, 
both old and interesting piles. 

Leaving Bury early in the afternoon, we took the road to 
Newmarket, which we found in places very soft and muddy, 
while there were several long hills to surmount, and from 
Eeutford there was an almost continuous rise for three miles 
until the top of the hill leading into Newmarket waa reached. 
The wind also blew against us, and momentarily increased in 
strength, until it was with difiiculty that we were able to 
ride down hill. Having heard of excessive charges at all 
the hotels in Newmarket, and there not being a B.T.C. house 
there, we were determined, if possible, to push on to Roystou, 
although it had now become dark and the wind blew quite 
a gale. We managed to mount under the shade of some 
trees, and with all our strength to ride some hundred yards 
alongside the bleak exposed race-course, but suddenly an 
extra powerful gust caught us, and we both at the same 
moment were literally blown across the road, and were just 
able to scramble off as our machines ran on to the grass. 
We then held a council of war, and actually had the pre- 
sumption to try and strike a light, but of course failed, so, 
turning our machines towards Newmarket, were soon riding 
through that town to the railway station, where we took 
train to Royston, which town, after much dawdling, we 
reached in the evening, and put up at an old-fashioned inn 
called the "Crown," one of the numerous comfortable B.T.C. 
houses. Oh, how the signboard creaked and the wind howled 
through the narrow streets I how the window did rattle in 
a dreary, restless manner, thus putting dismal thoughts 
into one's sleepy head, as one vainly tries to smother all 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



7 



outward noise by bur3ring one's head under the clothes. 
But sleep came at last— calm, gentle sleep, whose soundness 
is the reward of a bicyclist for a good day's ride. In spite 
of the previous night's wind we found the weather on 
rising clear and fine, but no sooner had we started than we 
discovered to our annoyance that our enemy the wind was 
blowing steadily against us. There were several long hills 
to mount before reaching Buntingford, from which place the 
road was rather hilly on through Puckeridge and Ware to 
Hertford. At the latter place we had lunch, and again 
faced the wind for the remaining twenty miles home. Thus 
ended a very pleasant three days' tour, which, if it had not 
been for the wind, would have been still more enjoyable. 
In conclusion, we can thoroughly recommend the roads we 
passed over in Essex and Suffolk, and should advise all who 
have not already done so to take the first opportunity of 
visiting Bury St. Edmunds. W. T. H. 



RAVINGS OF A CYCLOMANIAC.~No. XII. 

I'm really very sorry to have to trouble your readers in 
this way once again, Mr. Editor, but you see it's the only 
outlet I have to my pent-up feelings. I can't persuade 
any of my friends to come and stay with me. I believe 
they think I'm dangerous. When these harmless attacks 
come on, and I've no one to victimize viva voce, I have to 
put my ravings down on paper, and no one except a fellow- 
sufferer like yourself and your readers can imagine what 
relief such a proceeding affords. The greatness of, and I 
might almost say the necessity for, such a relief reminds 
me of a certain doctor's criticism on a badly prepared and 
worse delivered speech : " Wliat a relief it must be to 
So-and-So to throw off so much half-digested matter, yet 
what agony he seems to suffer in the process." 

A letter from a friend has done the mischief. He writes : 
" A few weeks ago I took a bicycle by train (for which he 
ought to be ashamed of himself^but I believe it was so 
broken he couldn't have ridden), and lo ! instead of draw- 
ing up a mass of legal documents with mysterious refer- 
ences to ' fire, shipwreck, the act of Ood, and the Queen's 
enemies,' they sold me (at a great price) a neat little ticket 
with 'Bicycle' printed on it and correctly spelt '\ This 
passage in my friend's letter has sent my powerful memory 
back into the fading distance of the past> and I see an 
urchin on the Euston platform brandishing a 42-inch 
bicycle, asserting to a cloud of porters grouped picturesquely 
around him the utter foolishness, not to say wickedness, of 
expecting his bicycle (red, black and gold) to travel in an 
ordinary luggage van already three quarters full of boxes 
and perambulators. In that golden age bicycles went as 
personal luggage, and why they should not do so now is 
more than this undermined brain can discover. I can see 
that urchin talking to a silver-mounted guard (painted 
black, and chained to a whistle), who obsequiously opens a 
special luggage compartment, wherein the bicycle is care- 



fully packed, surrounded by specially chosen luggage, and 
at every station at which the express stopped, that guard 
(silver mounts, black paint, whistle and all) goes to that 
luggage compartment, unlocks it, and then reports to the 
urchin, who with anxious face looks out of a neighbouring 
carriage window, that "the wheelossypeed is all right." 
Ah, me ! them was timea I remember how the porters all 
stood on one side in awe to let the rightful owner (ake the 
" thing " out when it got to its destination, and how they 
all followed to the station yard to see the urchin ride off 
on it after the fly in which he put his luggage. In these 
degenerate days you'd probably hear some such remark as 
" Now, then, guv'nor, chuck yer jigger in 'ere— third- 
class smoking forrad." Tempora mutantur, and we are 
mutantur'd in illis. Moral — ^Don't go meddling with 
those na-a-sty trains. 

Harrison, Kensington Works, Kensington Street, Bir- 
mingham, has brought out a new stop-bell, I see, called 
the " King of the Stop-bells." I do not know the price, 
nor can I give an opinion as to its merits^ for mine hasn't 
come yet. 

The fashion at present seems to be to cut records, and I 
am seriously thinking of going into strict training during 
this Christmas to cut a record. There are but few records 
left for anyone to try and cut with any chance of success. 
I propose cutting two records at once, and thus annihila- 
ting ornithological twins with a single geological specimen. 
First of all I am thinking of devouring the greatest amount 
of plum pudding and number of mince pies in the shortest 
time, and then, when thus properly trained, mounting and 
cutting the record for a twenty-four hours $low race, or 
the shortest distance in twenty-four hours without a dis- 
mount. The roads are in splendid condition for a race of 
this sort. It takes a Gortis to win the fast races^ but it 
will require a Tortis to come in last in the slow. 

Something's happened ! For the first time for years I 
know what it is to be free from cyclomania. I can think 
of nothing more to rave about. It may be only temporary 
relief. Any way, I may thank the Gazette for this cure. 
I feel thankful ; and so, I am sure, do my fellow- victims 
that they won't have to wade through any more overflow- 
ings from the loosened tiles of " Galley Bell."

N.W., the wife of Henry Edward Millar, of a son. 

The S.E. Ball was this year as enjoyable as ever — ^good 
dancers, a good floor, a good supper, and plenty of room. 
What more could members wish for ? 

As might have been expected, H. G. A. Brown was inde- 
fatigable, and seemed to be always in the right pkce at the 
right moment 

Who was the belle of the Ball ? Well, I imagine it would 
have taken a long while to pick out tfte one amongst such a 
large number, all anp<arently with equal claims to the title ; 
at any rate, I could not find time to decide. 

♦ •«••* 

Tlie " Qreyhound Hotel " opened its hospitable arms to 
four or five members of other Districts, but it could not 
understand how anyone could want tea and toast in his 
bedroom. 

The princinal feature of the Ball was the tasteful way in 
which the Jiall had been decorated with flowers and ever- 
greens.

Arthur Herbert is enjoying fine weather at Mentone, 
where he has been ordered by his doctor. I hope we shall 
see him back in time for the opening run, with his health 
completely restored. 

I expect we shall have some exciting elections at the 
next General Meeting. I can imagine handbills being dis- 
tributed by enthusiastic members : " Vote for A , the 

Universal Keformer "Plump for B , the Financier who 

scorns five shilling calls " C is the Treasurer who does 

not ask for subscriptions "Rally round D for Secretary 

and free Race meeting tickets. Five-guinea Prizes, and no 
corruption;" "Rambler for Editor, and lots of tours," 
etc., etc. Rambler.

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir, — I desire to express my regret to the members of 
the South East District that, as one of their representatives 
oiifthe Committee, I have been unable to attend the recent 
Committee meetings owing to illness, and as I shall probably 
not return to England for some months I feel it my duty 
to retire in favour of some other member who can give the 
necessary attention to their interests and to the general 
business of the Club. I shall, therefore, not offer myself for 
re-election in January. — Yours faithfully, 

Arthur Herbert. 

Hotel Camons, Mentone, Alpes Maritimes, France, 
Dec. 13th, 1882. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sir, — I beg to enclose a card of the Chipton Lacrosse 
Club fixtures for this season, which I shall be glad if you 
can find room for in the next Gazette. 

Our Club is largely composed of members of the

C. Newman, Gerard Smith, etc.), and we shall be glad to 
welcome to our practice games any other L.B.C. men who 
may like to try their hands at Lacrosse. Our ground is 
easily reached by train from Liverpool Street Station to 
St. James Street, Walthamstow. — Yours truly, 

Enfield, Dec. 13, 1882. A. C. Smith, Hon. Sec

Musical Bicyclists. — Last night a novel experiment in 
bell-ringing took place in the City. The riders of bicycles 
have alarm bells fixed to their machines, the notes of which 
differ occasionally one from the other. This gave rise to a 
suggestion by a bicyclist that a couple of octaves should be 
provided, the performers having one or two bells affixed to 
their machines. The suggestion was carried out, the result 
being that the octaves met by the Mansion House, and 
started ofiF up Cheapside, ringing out "The Bells." This 
was continued until reaching St. Paul's Churchyard, when 
the musicians changed the theme, and gave the " Blue Bells 
of Scotland," " Auld Lang Syne," and " Home, sweet home," 
as they ran down Cannon Street, up Union and King Streets, 
and round the back of the Bank of England. After a rest 
of ten minutes, the musical bicyclists had another spin 
through the City, playing " Rule Britannia," the " March 
of the Men of Harlech," and " God save the Queen." The 
whole of the music was creditably performed. — Daily 
Chronicle, Dec. 2^h, 1882. 



Corrigenda. — On page 235, in the fourth line of our 
President's speech proi)osing "The Queen," the word 
"unlikely" should be "likely."

THE ANNUAL GENERAL MEETING. 

We have again to plead for the indulgence of our readers, 
for we have again been disappointed in our Keporter, as we 
were at the Annual Dinner last Norember. 

The General Meeting was held at the " London Tavern " 
on the 31st ultimo, at 6.30 p.m., and was not largely 
attended. T. E. Scrutton was requested to take the chair. 

It was proposed by H. R. Reynolds, Jun., and seconded 
by Harold Smith, ''That the Committee's Report be 
adopted." 

B. G. Francis moved, and The O'Reillt seconded, 
That the balance sheet be adopted ; " but an amendment 
proi)osed by A. Henry and seconded by F. M. Williams 
was carried, " That the heading of the accounts should be, 
' The Cash Account of the Treasurer with the L.B.C. 
ending 31st December, 1882.' " 

W. A. Smith and H. Middleton having been appointed 
scmtineers, the elections were proceeded with, with the 
following results : —

It was then proposed by W. A. Smith and seconded by 
A. L. Radford :— 

" That the Club award a certain nnmber of Medals (such number 
to be hereafter decided upon) to such members of the L.B.C. who, on 
the day of the Bath Trial, shaU complete the 100 miles from Bath to 
Hounslow on Tricycles within twelve hours. The Medals to be similar 
to those given for the District Attendance Prizes, but of gold instead 
of silver, and suitably engraved." 

To which an amendment was proposed by T. E. Scrutton, 
seconded by Ashlby Barrett, and carried : — 

*<That the question of a Tricycle Road Trial, together with the 
general question of the position of tricydists in the Club, and of any 
changes in Club organisation necessary to meet the growth of tricycling, 
be referred to the Committee to report on to a Special General Meeting, 
to be held within three months or as soon after as possible.'' 

H. R. Reynolds proposed and J. G. Whatton seconded \~ 

That the Annual Race Meeting of the L.B.C. for 1883 be held at 
the Crystal Palace."

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



After some discussion the following amendment, proposed 
by the Captain and seconded by Koch, was carried : — 

" That, in the opinion of this meeting, it is desirable to hold onr next 
annual Race Meeting at the Crystal Palace, in the event of the Com- 
mittee being able to dispose satisfactorily of the obligations that the 
Clitb has incurred with the L.A.C.'* 

T. E. ScRUTTON postponed to the Special General Meeting 
the next proposition on the Agenda Paper, and then 
proposed that there should be an entrance fee of 5s. for 
the Bath Trial. This was lost. 

W. J. Williams also postponed his resolution to the 
Special General Meeting. 

Thbo. Godleb proposed, and F. M. Williams seconded, 
** That a Sub-Editor be appointed for the ensuing year, and 
that he be a member of the Committee ' ex-officio.* " This 
was carried, as was also Godlee's proposal that Arthur Lloyd 
be elected Sub^Editor for the ensuing year. 

Theo. Godleb then, in well-chosen words, suggested to 
the meeting that it would be very wrong for it to dissolve 
without tendering to T. E. Scrutton its very hearty thanks 
for the able way in which he had guided the Club during 
his captaincy, and amidst much prolonged and vociferous 
cheering a vote of thanks to him was carried — a vote which 
we imagine has rarely been more deserved than by our 
talented and good tempered ex-Captain. 



DRAMATIC SOCIETY. 

Members no doubt have taken notice that the day of our 
performance has been altered to Tuesday, February 20th, 
instead of the previous Saturday. The reason will be fully 
seen on perusal of the following letter received from the 
German AthensBum : — 

German Athenaeum, 98, Mortimer Street. 

January 22nd, 1883. 

Bear Sir, — ^The members of this society intending to give a musical 
and dramatic entertainment in aid of the funds raised on behalf of their 
terribly afflicted countrymen in the Rhinehmds, they have for that pur- 
pose hired St. George's Hall for Tuesday, February 20th. His Royal 
Highness the Prince of Wales and other members of the Royal Family 
have promised to honour the society with their presence if the entertain- 
ment could be given on Saturday, February 17th, this being the only 
evening in February and March at HH.H.'s disposal. Having been in- 
formed by the manager of St. George's Hall that you have retained it 
for that evening, I now appeal to you whether you could possibly sub- 
stitute the 20th for the 17th, and thus ensure the powerful support of 
the Royal Family for the success of this urgent and charitable under- 
taking. I need scarcely add that any expenses caused by this change 
would be gladly paid by this society. If you will kindly communicate 
to me your city address, I shall take the liberty of calling upon you at 
any time you may name ; and as our circulars have to be issued within 
the next few days, and as H.R.H. expects our reply without delay, I 
shall feel greatly obliged if you will let me know your decision as soon 
as ever possible. — Trusting that it may be a favourable one, I am, dear 
sir, yours faithfuUy, E. Mstkbstein, Hon. Sec. 

Under the circumstances we could not but accede to 
their request. 

With this Gazette members will find a notice, and it is 



hoped that a good many will apply for tickets soon, that is 
if they want good seats, as we are rapidly filling up the hall. 

The cast is a very good one,, and, subject to correction, 
will be as follows : — 

"WITHERED LEAVES." 

Sir CoNTERS CoNTEBB A. E. Buckler. 

Tom CoNYEBa R. G. Francis. 

Arthdb MiDDijrroK A. Barker. 

Cecil Vane E. WiUcins. 

"ONE HUNDRED THOUSAND POUNDS." 

Gerald Goodwin- R. M. Gruggen. 

Major Blackshaw E. T. Sachs. 

Sib R. Waters Ashley Barrett. 

Ohabker A. E. Buckler. 

Job Barlow H. Naylor. 

Penktthornb F. W. Morris. 

Pyepinch A. F. White. 

Mr. Fldjleb CO. Fitch. 

Gibbiks — Henry. 

Asnsted by Misses Bristowe and Roache. 

The L.B.C. O.S. will give a selection of music during the 
interval. 

Evening dress is requested but not compulsor}^ and to 
save disappointment it is as well to state that no one 
except the actors will be allowed behind the scenes. 

Alfred Barker, Hon. Sec. 

BICYCLE UNION. 
RiPOBT OF Executive Meetings held at the Offices, 
17, Ibonmohobb Lane, up to 9th Januabt, 1888. 

The Executive having been informed that it was proposed 
to bring in a Bill for Scotland which would have the effect 
of allowing each borough to frame its own bye-laws with 
reference to bicycles and tricycles, communicated with the 
authorities on the subject, and are glad to be able to report 
that, at a meeting of the Committee appointed to consider 
this Bill, the motion of the Provost of Dumfries for the 
insertion of a general clause," founded on that recom- 
mended by the Union, was unanimously adopted. The 
Executive thereupon passed the following resolution : — 

That the Executive of the Bicycle Union record with pleasure their 
thanks to the Provost of Dumfries for his valuable assistance in con- 
nection with recent proposed legislation in Scotland, having reference 
to bicycles and tricycles. 

The Executive have under consideration the action of 
the Corporation of Liverpool, which is about to apply for 
further powers with reference to bicycles and tricycles. 

Resolved : — 

That J. AUan of Uppingham, and F. Wright of Oakham, having 
ridden for a money prize at the sports given by^ the Loyal Rutland 
Lodge of Oddfellows at Oakham on the I2th September, 1882, be and 
are hereby declared professionals. 

From a return made by Mr. G. P. Coleman, the Official 
Handicapper for the Home Counties and the South, it 
appears that during the past year he has been called upon 
to frame 79 handicaps. 

The Executive have been requested to take action 
against a ^bus driver for having wilfully run into a tricycle, 
but as the case appeared to be one of ordinary assault, not 
involving any disputed point of law, or with any special 
circumstances, the Executive felt they could not depart 
from their invariable practice and take up the case. 

Wm. Pyb-Enqlish, Hon. Sec.

NOTICE. 

Wanted, a Room for Committee Meetings. Any member 
knowing of a room in the City suitable for this purpose is 
requested to at once communicate with me. The rental 
offered is £5 per annum, and the room would be required 
on about fifteen evenings in the year. — E. S. Price, 
"Homelea," Greenhill, Hampstead. 

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB DRAMATIC SOCIETY. 
It has been decided, in addition to Byron's comedy of 
" One Hundred Thousand Pounds/' to play the amusing 
comedietta entitled " Withered Leaves," which will precede 
the comedy and commence at 7.30 p.m. As the seats are 
rapidly filling, members desirous of securing good places are 
requested to apply as early as possible to R. M. Gruggen, 
Lynton Villa, Richmond, S.W. We would again call tlie 
attention of members to the unavoidable change in the date 
of the performance from Saturday, 17th, to the following 
Tuesday, 20th February. 

A BANK HOLIDAY RIDE. 
On August 5t]i three members of the L.B.C. met at 
Kingston station, J. W. Alison, C. Taylor, and A. H. Koch. 
It was nearly five o'clock before we left Kingston for Ripley. 
We found the " Anchor " full of Beanfeasters, so went on to 
Guildford and had tea at the B.T.C. house there. We left a 
little before eight, taking over the Hog's Back to Farnham* 
We lighted up just outside the town, so rode straight 
through to Alton, putting up at the " Swan." We met three 
members of the Wanderers B.C., and spent a very pleasant 
evening with them. The next morning was lovely, the 
sun shone brightly, the roads were perfect, and the wind 
behind. After breakfast we left for Farehara. The road 
the whole way from Alton down Filmer Hill, through West 
Meon, to Wickham, was far better than most racing paths, 
so good that it nearly equalled a billiard table for surface ; 
from Wickham to Fareham it was more bumpy and hilly. 
At Fareham we halted at the " Red Lion ; " after borrowing 
towels we went for a swim in the sea. A boat, such as it 
was, was obtained, and we rowed ourselves some little way 
so as not to bathe too much in public. All went well with 
us, except that the boat leaked a good deal, until the N.G. 
wanted to get back into the boat; his struggles were 
elephantine but fruitless, so Taylor and I had to haul him 
in by force ; he arrived safely on board after some trouble, 
on the top of a wave that quite wetted our clothes and 
nearly swamped us ; however, we soon dried in the wind and 
sun. On our safe arrival at our point of departure I asked the 
boat owner " How much?" to which he replied "Threepence." 
I paid, and we all retired. This is the cheapest boating 
excursion I ever had. It is as well to note we had the 
boat in use for an hour and a half. After lunch we all felt 
very sleepy, so did not resume our journey till four o'clock, 
when we went through Litchfield to Netley Abbey. We 



found the gate of the Abbey closed, so we climbed over 
.and walked all through the grounds. The ruins are 
very grand and well worth a visit. There is no good 
distant view to be got of them, as they are completely 
hidden by trees. We crossed by the steam ferry to South- 
ampton, but did not stop, as we wished to get to Winchester 
before dark if possible. We dismounted at St. Cross and 
walked through the grounds, but it was too late to see the 
inside of the church. Arriving at Winchester, we put up 
at the " White Swan." The landlady had taken something 
that was a little too strong for her nerves ; she talked to us 
and at us nearly all the evening ; so persistent was she 
that she followed us up stairs to bed and delivered each of 
us a lecture on sheets until we put her out of the room. 
She was still heard in the passage and no doubt continued 
it to her old man, so what time he got any sleep I cannot 
say. The next morning was fine but cloudy, so we 
start^ for Basingstoke. Close to Popham we met a lot 
of artillery marching down to Southampton. There 
were six guns and some baggage-waggons. Although the 
men had only come nine wiles, they looked as if they had 
had quite enough of the dust. We were obliged to dismount 
to let them pass, but on resuming our journey we found tliey 
had quite spoiled the roads by hacking up the surface. After 
leaving Basingstoke we met another Battery close to the 
canal bridge ; they had come from Aldershot that morning ; 
they looked much better than the former lot. At Hook we 
were overtaken by a regular cad on castors ; he wanted to 
join us, but we objected, the first remark he made being 
quite enough for us : " 'Ot, ai*nt it ?" We saw him with 
his head in a pewter pot at Hartley Row, so he troubled us 
no more. Just before reaching the latter place I had a 
grand pig hunt. They (two pigs) would not face the 
bicycle, in spite of two boys administering copious whacks, 
but turned and fled ; so, seeing them go off, I went in chase, 
giving them a fine gallop of half a mile. I then left them 
exhausted at the side of the road. Taylor and Alison could 
not go the pace for laughing. Our halt for lunch was at 
the " White Hart," Blackwater, soon after which we parted 
company. Alison and Taylor went together to Chertsey, 
where they had tea ; Alison then found the rim of his hind 
wheel was broken, but he managed to ride as far as Acton, 
where he left his machine, doing the rest of his journey by 
train. Taylor arrived safely at Kensington without adven- 
ture. After leaving the others in York Town I went on to 
Frimley, then over the Ridges past Henly Park to Worples- 
don. The view from the Ridges was perfect and very 
extensive — all the Woking country was at your feet, witn 
blue hills in the distance, and the heather being in full 
bloom added a still greater beauty to the scene. I can't 
say much/or the road, as it is only a sand-track, but is just 
rideable. * Anyone fond of pottering should try this part of 
the county of Surrey — it is well worth a visit. After leaving 
Worplesdon I made for Woking— then to Ripley. I went 
for a swim in the Way, returning to town early the next 



mornmg. Total distance about 170 miles. 



A. H. Koch.

THE MENTOR. 

Plato has remarked, " Man is an imitative being and 
if, as one of the greatest thinkers of our age has maintained, 
we are the lineal descendants of the pre-historic ape, we 
need not be ashamed to find that we are endowed with his 
especial attribute. Thus it may well have happened that 
our bicycling career has owed its origin not so much to an 
actual inclination to move on wheels, as because we have 
been led or talked into it by some one who has assumed the 
role of our mentor. I am aware that this proposition is 
open to dispute ; but there must be some who will remember 
sitting at the feet of a Gamaliel learned in bicycling lore. 
Our friend will liave probably several years our senior, and 
his experience may have dated from thei era when men's 
bones were shaken on wheels of hickory. 

The first scene discloses the interior of an outhouse or 
shed, where the Mephistopheles of our drama is discovered 
cleaning- the last of his two-wheeled favourites after an 
evening spin. A youth, prima intonadque juventd, repre- 
sents Faust, and is seated a little distance off, on the edge 
of an old crate, watching the proceedings with apparent 
interest, and listening to the words of wisdom as they fall 
from his companion's lips during the intervals in his work. 
Probably the great man will expatiate on the beauty and 
particular excellence of each portion coming under his 
direct notice as the cleaning progresses. Then he will be 
led to air his opinions on the machines of different makers, 
and of course can arrive at but one conclusion — that not 
only is the maker of his machine the best, but that the 
machine itself is the finest specimen that maker has ever 
turned out. From the merits of the bicycle to those of its 
rider is an easy stage enough, and his listener's ears are 
next treated to a relation of the veteran's exploits on road 
and racing path. The glowing embers of emulation are 
fanned into a flame, and our youth, as he meditatively goes 
home that evening, has but one thought, and that is: "Why 
should not. he ride a bicycle too ? " 

Of course the governor has to be got over, and of course 
the mater has something to say about the dangers of the 
sport. It is a noticeable fact that while a mother is the 
first to dissuade her sons from enterprise, she is also the 
first to share in the triumph when success is achieved. We 
will imagine that parental objection of either kind has been 
overcome, and the rudiments of the art mastered in the 
customary fashion on a friend's superannuated crock, then. 



after a consultation with the mentor of a length propor- 
tionate to the importance of the subject, the new machine 
is ordered. Our first machine is a landmark that even 
after lapse of time still stands out in bold relief. We do 
not easily forget the keen excitement with which we 
expected its arrival, and the impatience with which we 
regarded that inevitable delay. At length it does arrive, 
and as soon as unpacked it is wheeled round for inspection 
at headquarters. We all know the fearful significance of 

those three monosyllables, ** Ah ! yes ; but " when in 

breathless expectation we are exhibiting to a connoisseur 
something that is our especial pride ; so in the present case 
there were points which the expert found, or pretended to 
find, not quite to his liking ; but on the whole, perhaps, 
the verdict was favourable, and an appointment was made 
for a short ride together at an early date. 

I do not propose to follow closely the relations between 
master and pupil as exemplified by the events that followed. 
We all are, or all ought to be — ^to put it negatively — ^not 
without some experience in the matter. We have learnt, 
or ought to have learnt, that to lean ad libitum over the 
handles going up hill has a tendency to contract the respira- 
tory organs ; and further, that it is not advisable on every 
hill to begin by throwing one s legs over the handles, and 
to end by throwing- oneself into the hedge. The first 
orthodoxical principles were gradually instilled into the 
youth's mind with occasional illustrations suggested more 
or less forcibly by the road, its surface, and diversities. 
Then the mentor was an inventive genius in his way, and 
had countless remedies of his own concoction against the 
two primary evils of bicycling— thirst and soreness. Un- 
limited, too, were the items of his paraphernalia, all of the 
most scientific kind ; and the fervid manner with which he 
would preach their efficacy was enough to ensure respect 
and carry conviction even to the most hardened heretic. 
The worst of it was, that as fa.shion changes so did these 
ideas ; some held sway longer than others, but almost in 
every case his mind, fertile in inventions, compelled him to 
abandon his old nostra for fresh improvements. Do what we 
will, time ever changes our opinions : the more firmly they 
appear rooted in our inmost self the more readily are they 
changed. We ofton hear a friend laying down the law in 
dogmatical fashion something in this style. Just listen to 
him : " A painted machine ! " — ^with disgust — " Paint's 
always coming ofi" ; why don't you have a plated one like 



Googh 



Digitized by 



14 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



mine ? " A few months after, his song is " Nothing like a 
painted machine ; haven't I always said so ? I wouldn't 
have a plated one for anything ; " and so on. These are 
the kind of difficulties our tyro had to encounter in more 
or less a degree, and it was only unbounded confidence in 
his chief that bore him safely among the pitfalls of apparent 
contradiction until at length he was able to throw aside 
the leading-strings, and perhaps in his turn set up as an 
authority. 

So much for the subject from a comical point of view ; 
and here I would pause to apologise to any who may feel 
their susceptibilities wounded by a caricature limned in 
outlines gross and imperfect. After all, the model whose 
eccentricities stand out most consjDicuously is none other 
than the writer himself. 

Though my^ joints are beginning to stiffen, and my limbs 
to lose their youthful elasticity, I well remember the time 
when my blood would quicken and my pulses beat at the 
very sight of a bicycle. What countless pleasant recollections 
does not the vision even now conjure up ! And all these I 
owe to the friend who first persuaded me by example and 
precept to take to the wheel. The youth who has been 
inspired early in life by some one or other with notions 
bicyclical, will be likely in after years similarly to incite 
others to like action, and thus may hope to experience the 
double pleasure of being himself a credit to his instructor, 
and when he has retired from active service with his 
" honours thick upon him," of seeing reproduced in his 
pupil a bicycling spirit as ardent as his own. DoDO. 



ASHRIDGE PARK, ETC. 
A joint meet of L.B.C. members and friends at 
Hemel Hempstead would have taken place on the 28th 
October, but owing to heavy rain it was postponed till the 
4th November, when luckily the weather was fine. I 
started on my own account from Kensington, and arrived 
at Watford before dark. Stones in some places very thick, 
especially near the Harrow Road Station, mud awful every- 
where. I lighted up in Watford and pushed on to Hemel 
Hempstead. About an hour after my arrival the rest came 
from Hampstead, including J. W. Alison of ours; the others 
consisted of one double "tri.," a single, and bicycles. 
Having housed the machines, we sat down, a merry party 
of ten, to tea. The next morning was fine, but a very 
strong wind blew from the north-west. Our route 
lay througli W^aterend, Nettleden, Gaddesden Green, to 
Ringshal. Here two of the party, who would get out of 
calling distance, took the wrong turning, and went to 
Dagnal. We did not see them again for some time. From 
Ringshal we went down the side of Beacon Hill, past 
Ivinghoe, to Albury, then up the hill into the park, and 
made for the Bridgewater column. Tegetmeier had written 
for an order to go up it, so we made the ascent. The two 
missing ones joined us here. The view from the top was 



very fine, but the tents were not so various as usual, owing 
to the wet and high winds. Having regained our machines, 
we rode through the park and past the house back to 
Hemel Hempstead for lunch. About four o'clock tlie party 
broke up, Alison, Green, T.B.C., and myself going home 
through Watford, Oxey Lanes, and Kenton Lanes. The 
latter were in splendid order. We had a heavy gale behind 
us as well. Alison left us at Kenton, so Green and I went 
on together down the Harrow Road, to Westbourne Park, 
where we parted company. At Willesden Junction the 
galQ blew both my lamps out ; I had great difficulty in 
re-lighting. I arrived in Kensington a trifle wet, as it had 
begun to rain ; tlie other seven went home via St. Albans. 
This is tlie third year that we have carried out this run 
witli great succe»', and we all hope to do it many times 
again. A. H. Koch.

ECHOES. 

The Dramatic is on a fair way to success, but there 
are a few members who have yet forgotten to apply for 
tickets. 

• **•»* 

The gallery is a good cheap place for small boys and 
members' brothers. 

W. A. Smith and A. B. W. Whatton were elected at the 
B.U. Council Meeting to serve on the Sub-Committee, 
suggested by the latter, to inquire into the possibility of 
the B.U. taking upon itself the regulation on professional 
racing and the management of a professional championship. 
The other members of the Sub-Committee are Messrs. 
Hillier and Sturmey, and it is probable that one or two 

professionals will be asked to serve with them. 

• ••••• 

When members wish to express the utter incapacity of 
an ex -Editor they should be sure that he has vacated the 
Editorial chair. It was more convenient that this Gazette 
should be brought out by the 1882 Editor, and it was 
amusing to see the " Genial Maclean " and him chuckling 
over the complimentary letters that arrived for the latter's 
successor, rejoicing in the prospect of " a properly edited 
Gazette." 

• •««•« 

" But I am Scriitton^mw^ " said a jocular member at 
the General Meeting, when the Scrutineers objected to 

his expressing his opinion on the validity of a voting-paper.

H. H. SherriflF, after seven years' bicycling and hard 
touring, and never having met with an accident of any 
consequence, has lately broken his leg in his own house 
whilst dancing. It is the more strange, because, being 
fond of athletics in all branches, he has taken a vigorous 
share in its dangers, especially in bicycling, and never been 
hurt. However, six weeks will see him about again, and it 
is not unlikely that he will be quite fit to take part in the 
Bath Trial Race. Ramblee. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(CONTINUBD KBOM FAOK 252, VOL. V.) 

(<?.) Thb Gbmmi (7550 feet) ; that is, to the top of the 
pass and back for the sake of the view, which is quite 
unique and undoubtedly one of the finest in Switzerland. 
It is, however, no use going except in settled weather. 
I have only to add to the usual information of the guide 
books, that somewhere about half way up there is a nice 
little lake (green this time, not blue) in a retired nook quite 
close to though unseen from the path, in which you can 
get a delicious bathe if you want one. About two and a 
quarter hours' climbing from Kandersteg brings you to some 
open pastures on your right, with ch&lets ('' Spitalmatt "). 
Shortly after passing these you come to a long straight bit 
of raised pathway on the side of which you will observe a 
little white granite stone. It indicates, I believe, the 
boundary between the Canton Bern and the Canton Valais. 
At this point you will notice on your right a smooth green 
sward narrowing up between two banks of limestone, rocks 
and firwood, and looking as if it had been sometime a lake. 
Follow this up, and at the head of the glade some four 
hundred yards distant from the path you will find the little 
lake. The water is so clear that you will be entirely 
deceived as to its depth. A header may be taken with 
perfect safety from a rock jutting out into the water on the 
left-hand side ; that is, it could be taken three years ago, 
but I daresay the amount of water varies with the season. 
I have a special interest in this little lake, for to its dis- 
covery I practically owe my life and that of my Zermatt 
guide. We were staying at Kandersteg, and were making 
an expedition up Altels, the base of which you reach by the 
Gemmi path. As we were walking along the path a little 
below this point we noticed on our left the remains of an 
avalanche spanning the glacier stream beneath us and 
forming an apparently secure snow bridge across it. With- 
out this bridge the stream was impassable, and a very 
considerable detour was necessary in order to reach our 
mountain on the other side of it ; but we saw that by its 
aid we should be able to make a short cut down the 
mountain and efTect a great saving of time in the descent. 
We determined accordingly that we would return to 
Kandersteg by this bridge. But as we were descending the 



long monotonous slope of Altels my eye was riveted b)* this 
little emerald lake as it caught the sun and sparkled like a 
jewel in the middle of the wood. The idea of a bathe was 
at that moment irrestible, and so I countermanded the 
proposed short cut, and we came down the same way as we 
had ascended, crossing the stream higher up and making 
direct for the lake. After the bathe we proceeded home- 
wards down the path and came to the point from which we 
had observed the snow bridge. At the very moment we 
got there we heard a sudden crash ; the bridge dropped into 
the stream and split into a hundred fragments, which were 
hurried away by the impetuous torrent to the precipices 
below. Such would have been our fate but for this little 
green lake ! 

As regards time, you must allow from four and a half to 
five hours for the ascent of the Gemmi, and half that time 
for the return to Kandersteg, exclusive of halts. The 
earlier you start the better, not only because you will have 
a shadier walk, but because your chance of getting the 
view is so much greater. You ought to be on your legs by 
five a.m. at the latest. 

All this might be done in two days if you were in very 
first-rate condition, and make early starts. Thus, leaving 
Thun at 5.30, you would be at the Blue Lake by about eight 
and the Victoria about 10.30, allowing for all halts. The 
(Eschinen See would take you till two, and after a lunch you 
would reach the " Bar " at three, and have time for the 
Gasteren Thai before dinner. Then leaving Kandersteg at 
dawn (say at 4.30) you would be back from the Gemmi some- 
where about 2.30, allowing for all halts ; and after a rest 
you would start back for Thun at 4.30 and reach it about 
seven. But this is a mixture of bicycling and moun- 
taineering in somewhat strong doses, and the employment 
of two distinct sets of muscles makes the business fatiguing. 
We all know, for instance, how hard it is to walk even a 
mile or two after a day's skating, and I expect most riders 
will feel similarly disinclined for bicycling to Thun so soon 
after seven or eight hours of stiff walking on the Gemmi. 
The best plan, therefore, is to sleep two nights at Kandersteg, 
giving the Gemmi a day to itself. Ton can then take the 
Blue Lake and the Gasteren Thai on the first day, the 
Gemmi on the second, and the (Eschinen See on your way 
back on the third. Early starts, however, are an advantage 
under all circumstances. 

The Kandersteg breezes, as they rushMown from the glaciers 
above, are not only fresh and bracing but gusty and tyran- 
nical, and you must not be surprised at finding yourself occa- 
sionally brought up sharp, or even blown over as you ride 
up the valley. Perhaps this keen vigour of the air may 
account for the remarkable development of observation and 
memory in the inhabitants. As I was wheeling up the 
Buhl Stutz this year, a boy, anxious (like everyone else in 
the Canton Bern) to seize any opportunity of earning coin, 
came up, and offered to relieve me of the task. He, as it

vere, claimed acquaintance with DIB on the score of having 
once seen me riding by two years ago. There was, of course, 
nothing remarkable in this, but J confess I was rather 
astonished when, after a momentary glance at the bicycloi 
he remarked : " That is the same yelocipede, sir, you had 
with you before, only you have got a new brake an; " which 
was exactly the case.

Take right-hand fork quarter mile beyond Gwatt; the 
hill lasts for a short mile only, terminating soon after a 
bend to the right ; still, if you are meditating the seventy- 
one miles in the day, it will be wise to walk it, unless 
surface and wind are favourable. Half a mile beyond 
Brothausi (or Brodhusi) the road to Wimmis crosses the 
Simme by a bridge on your left ; you follow the valley and 
river straight on, it will keep twisting round to the right at 
the back of the Stockhorn. A mile or so after passing this 
bridge road forks. The left one leads down to Diemtigen ; 
yours, the right, leads on to Latterbach, a long straggling 
village on ups and downs of road ; the last hill in it had 
better be walked. There is no possibility of your mistaking 
the road elsewhere. You have the Simme on your left, 
mostly close to you, the whole way to Garstatt, where you 
cross it to re-cross it again three quarters of a mile higher 
up. The hill between these two bridges will give you a 
convenient relief from the saddle. This, and the hill in 
Latterbach, are the only occasions for a dismount, and the 
whole return journey may be made without once quitting 
the saddle. At Zweisimmen you still follow up the main 
valley, bearing down slightly to your left for the purpose. 
Between St. Stephan and Lenk a loop leads up on your left 
to the village of Matten, you keep tike lower or main road, 
avoiding the village. There is not a single stage of this 
route which is not worth a visit for its own sake, so varied 
and delightful is the scenery, but the crowning point of all 
is Lenk, at the head of it, with the magnificent glaciers of 
the Wildstrubel contrasting with the green pastures below. 
Between Wehsenburg and Garstatt you have ten miles of 
narrow and precipitous gorge where there is only jost room 
for road and river. Should you not have time or inclina- 
tion to make the whole course, any portion of it will make 
an enjoyable ride. The famous baths and hotel of Weissen- 
burg, situated in a narrow chasm some twenty minutes 
above the road, are well worth a visit em route. Bicycles 
must be left at the roadside inn below. As places for re- 



freshment you have for choice Weissenburg (Baths), Zweisim- 
men, Boltigen, and Lenk, the hotels at the two former 
being superior to the others. Notice the beautiful chalets 
in Erlenbach. Gerard F. Cobb. 

Thunerhof, Thun, July, 1882.

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
BICYCLE UNION, 

WITH WHICH IS INCORPORATED THB TRICYCLE ASSOCIATION. 

Sir, — I am requested by the Executive of the Bicycle 
Union to make public through your columns the fact that 
they do not propose this year to call the usual meeting of 
racing men to recommend a candidate for the post of 
handicapper. They have come to this determination on 
the following grounds : — 

1. The Council of the Union is now so thoroughly 
representative in the south that there can be very little 
difficulty in ascertaining the opinion of all prominent racing 
men through the delegates to the Council of tlie Clubs to 
which such racing men belong. 

2. As Mr. G. P. Coleman, the present handicapper, 
offers himself for re-election, the necessity for ascertaining 
the opinion of racing men is not so great as if an entirely 
new man must be chosen. 

3. The time and labour required to call such a meeting 
is so considerable that the Executive in the present over- 
worked state of their officials do not feel themselves justified 
in incurring it without obvious necessity. 

The Executive have carefully limited their determination 
to this year only so as not to create any precedent against 
ascertaining directly the opinion of racing men. 

The Executive will be glad to receive any suggestions 
from racing men or others as to candidates for the post of 
handicapper or the duties of the office to guide them in 
their recommendations to the Council. Such suggestions 
should be addressed to m3rself at the Union Office as soon 
as possible. — I am, yours truly, 

Wm. Ptb-English, Hon. Sec. 

17, Ironmonger Lane, E.C., Jan. 6, 1883.
