SWAN-SONG AND CACKLE. 

A catching title is a good thing ; so also is an enigmatical 
one. We venture to say with more than usual editorial 
confidence that, of the large and intelligent circle of readers 
who have been led by the merits of preceding editors to 
await the coming of their Gazettb with eagerness, the vast 
majority will put one common question, and form one 
common resolve, on seeing the phrase at the head of this 
column. With one voice they will ask, " What on earth 
does it mean?" With one will they will determine to read 
the article and find out. 

The title has its merits. It is not, as may be conjectured 
at first, the name of a new and enterprising firm of makers 
unrepresented at the Stanley Show. No blandishments of 
eager agents have induced the incorruptible Maclean to 
advertise anybody's patent washing machines on the front 
page of this non-mercenary journal. The title— for the 
secret must out at last — is intended to express by what 
Mrs. Malaprop calls "a neat derangement of epitaphs" 
the melancholy fact that this number represents the first 
feeble cackle of the new-bom Editor, and that the L.B.C. 
has heard, to our sorrow be it spoken, the last official 
utterance, perhaps, like the swan's, sweetest at death, of 
Mr. P. M.Williams. 

Such a conjunction enables us in pulpit fashion to divide 



BNTS. 

PAOS 



Fixtures for Club Meets... 22 

Our Franco-Swiss Tour of 1882 23 

TheC.U.Bi.C 27 

All Round the Wlieel 27 

Quills 27 

Correspondence 28 

March Valentines 28 

Notice to Correspondents 28 



our subject into two heads— the work of 1882 and the 
prospects of 1883. The second head we defer till some 
future number, when we propose to give utterance to the 
Editorial views on spelling, punctuation, and things in 
general. The first part of our theme is amply sufficient 
for any of our readers to grapple with at a sitting. But 
while to write of the Gazette during the last year leads 
inevitably to the virtues of its Editor, we are under con- 
siderable difficulties in dealing with the subject satisfactorily. 
West Drayton is a little too far from the wilds of Essex for 
a midnight journey to serenade the sleeper with " Oh, teach 
me how to praise thee, Fred ! " Every method of writing 
suggests dangers. To say the post is difficult savours of 
conceit in the new Editor who has been chosen to fill it ; 
that it is easy suggests lack of appreciation of the time 
and trouble the old Editor has bestowed on the work. To 
be enthusiastic about the merits of a man who combines 
great modesty with a very violent disposition, as Mr. 
Williams does, is to expose oneself to considerable danger 
of assault and battery ; to be lukewarm in the praises of 
one so popular in the Club is to court dismissal, after brief 
but disgraceful tenure of office, by a Special General 
Meeting called by unanimous requisition of our members. 
Shall we adopt the tone of the Saturday Review, and say, 
"that in all fairness to the retiring Editor, while we have no 
wish to overlook or extenuate his undoubtedly grave errors, 
we must candidly confess that he has not been such a complete 
failure as was expected when he somehow got himself 
elected to the office." Or shall we take the pen of the 



Digitized by 




- 




— 




18 LONDON BICYCLE 


CLUB GAZETTE. 






Daily Telegraph and remark, after an exordium lightly 
touching upon Lesbia's sparrow, the Hounslow inquest, the 
Tower of Babel, and the Rucker tricycle, that "the fertilising 
power and civilising influence of the Editor's work rarely 
meets with its appropriate reward. Like the Man in the 
Iron Mask, or the Veiled Prophet of Ehorassan, his might 
is great, his name and features unknown. But when the 
identity of a wielder of leaders and shaper of paragraphs 
emerges from the fuliginous mist that closely and murkily 
enshrouds the editorial sanctum, meet it is that all honour 
be given him for his glorious work. And we doubt if ever, 
since Homer edited the chronicles of the Trojan conflict, 
since Dr. Watts bestowed his selective revision on that 
collection of sacred songs so entwined with the greatness 
of our Imperial England, down to the days of the pithy and 
powerful work of the pilot of our interesting contemporary, 
'Dirty Cottonaceous Filaments, a Seminal Radiator of 
Caloric Intensity,' — if ever, during these aeons, such master- 
ful skill of writing, such learned profundity of quotation, 
such unerring tact of selection, or generally such ineffable 
power of evolution, have ever been so transcendentally 
combined as in the person of the retiring Editor of this 
Gazettb." 

Seriously, however, we can say of Mr. Williams what he 
himself would wish to be said of him, but what to those 
who know him needs no saying, that " he did his duty in 
that station of life to which it pleased the LB.O. to call 
him." It will be a bad thing for our Club if it ever becomes 
a mere Mutual Admiration Society, but it would be still 
more unfortunate if the hard work of any member in the 
service of the Club for a series of years were allowed to pass 
without a word of recognition in the columns of the Club 
Gazette. And thus we feel that we are only expressing in 
plain and simple words what every one of our members can 
say with the greatest sincerity, that the hearty thanks of 
the Club are due and are given to Frederick Monier 
Williams for his work for the L.B.C., and especially for the 
time and trouble he has bestowed on this Gazette during 
the past year. 

COMMITTEE. 
At a meeting of the Committee held on Monday, 
12th February, the following gentlemen were elected 
members of the Club : —

The resignations of Messrs. Norman Carr, H. T. Crosby, 
J. Franklin, G. B. Hill, R. Jacks, and F. H. Townend were 
accepted. 

The resignation of Mr. D. T. Nops was accepted with 
regret. 

Mr. S. P. Smith was elected to the Committee. 

It was decided not to send a delegate to the meeting 
convened by the Finchley Tricycle Club for the purpose of 
discussing the adyisability of road races, and admission to 
the public parks, etc. 

The following gentlemen were appointed as a Race 
Committee for the present year : — Messrs. Beck, H. G. A. 
Brown, G. P. Coleman, Theo. Godlee, S. P. Smith, and 
0. Thorn, with Captain and Hon. Sec. ex officio. 

On the motion of Mr. F. M. Williams it was resolved — 
" That every encouragement be given to tricyclists to join 
our Club, and that a Sub-Committee be appointed to con- 
sider the necessary alterations in Rules, and what form the 
encouragement shall take, especially whether the holding of 
a Road Trial for tricyclists is desirable." Messrs. Theo. 
Godlee, R G. Francis, W. E. Naylor, A. L. Radford, W. A. 
Smith, and F. M. Williams were aJ)pointed for this purpose. 

It was decided that the meeting with the O.U.Bi.C. and 
C.U.Bi.C. be held at Dunstable this year, provided it is 
convenient to the two University Clubs. 

The opening run of the season was fixed for 17th March. 

It was also decided that a Smoking Concert be held in 
March. Messrs. A. Lloyd, S. P. Smith, and 0. Thorn were 
appointed a Sub-Committee for that purpose. 

TRICYCLING.-IMPORTANT 

It will be in the recollection of members that at the 
Annual General Meeting a resolution in the following terms 
was agreed to as an amendment to W. A. Smith's pro- 
position on the subject, viz. : — 

" That the qaestion of a Tricycle Road Trial, together with the general 
question of the position of tricyclists in the Club, and of any changes in 
CInb organisation necessary to meet the growth of tricycling, be referred 
to the Committee to report on to a Special Qeneral Meeting to be held 
within three months from now, or aa soon after as possible." 

Acting on this resolution, the Committee has delegated 
to a Sub-Committee the consideration of the best form of 
encouragement to be given to tricyclists^ with a view to 
inducing them to join our ranks ; and further, what altera* 
tions, if any, would be necessary in our rules to carry out 
any such change, and also the desirability of holding a 
Tricyclists' Road Trial. 

The Sub-Committee feel that they could not usefully 
make any recommendation or report without having before 
them the views of those members who have carefully con- 
sidered this question, and of those who more especially 
favour the three-wheeler. 

The time limited for the General Meeting is, however, 
very short, and I shall therefore be much obliged if any

members belonging to either of the above classes will at once 
forward me their views on the general subject, that is to 
say, what form of encouragement, if any, should be ofifered, 
and whether a Road Trial should be organised. 

Abhlet Babbett, Captain.

annual run up to town next day. 

Ashley Babbett, Captain. 



N.E. DlSTBICT. 

I hope that members of the ** Little District " will do 
their best to turn out on the 17th March. The run is 
short and easy, and the destination comfortable ; besides, 
we must try to make our average attendance at meets 
nearly as large as our average at Committees, if we can. 

Theo. Oodlee, District Captain. 



N.W. DlSTBICT. 

I shall be very much obliged if members will make an 
effort to attend the first run of the season to Shenley. The 
run is not a long one, and we shall get back by nine o'clock. 

Palmeb Dalton, District Captain. 



S.E. DlSTBICT. 

For those energetic spirits who are anxious to rub off the 
rust of the past wet months, there will be two preliminary 
runs, starting from Croydon Central Station at 4.15, viz. : 
March 3rd — Downe ; March 10th — Bletchiugley. The less 
enthusiastic are respectfully requested to reserve their 
energies for the opening run to Orpington on March 17th, 
when members will have an opportunity of showing 
whether or no the District has fallen off from the good 
form it has displayed at the opening meets of previous 
years. Stephen P. Smith has again kindly consented to 
take charge of the Blackheath Division, and I think we 
may regard that as something more than a good omen for 
the ensuing season. 

The opening run will be to Orpington, as announced 
elsewhere, the two divisions joining company at five o'clock 
on Farnborough Common. 

R. G. Fbakcis, District Captain. 



S.W. DlSTBICT. 

Members of this District will please note that the opening 
run will be on the 17tli March next, when we ride to 
Sunbury to meet the West. As the distance is short and 
the proposed route a very pleasant one, I hope that all 
members will make a preliminary effort to be present, in 
spite of wind or weather, and consequently that we shall 



secure a good representative gathering. No membei^ho 
is going in for the Bath Trial should neglect to attend any 
of our Meets previous to that date if he looks for success 
upon that occasion. The Circular Run proposed for 
Saturday the 31st March is an exceptionally attractive 
one, and if the day be fine a large muster is expected. 
The route will be by Surbiton lanes, Long Ditton Church, 
Claygate, Ockshott, and Stoke D'Abemon to Cobham, 
where we tea at the "Red Lion.'' 

R. M. Obuqgen, District Captain. 



DISTRICT MEETING. 
At a meeting of members of the Western District, held 
at Dr. Langmore's house on Thursday, the 22nd ultimo, 
Mr. H. C. Pigott was unanimously elected District Captain 
vice D. T. Nops, resigned.* 



SMOKING CONCERT. 

Members have already received notice of the above 
entertainment, which will take place at the " City Arms," 
St. Mary Axe, on Thursday, March 15th, at 7.0. p.m. 

A first-class programme is being arranged, including 
glees, solos, and instrumental pieces by the most musically 
efficient members of the Club; as well as one or more 
recitations, and a novel feature in the shape of a conjuring 
performance by a member well**known in athletic circles for 
his skill in that line. 

The price of the tickets is Is. each. The number will 
be limited to 200, and early application should be made to 
0. Thorn, 11, Ladbroke Square, W., or A. Lloyd, The 
Winus, Waltliamstow. 

Members willing to take part in the programme are 
requested to write to Stephen P. Smith, 9, Morden Road, 
Blackheath, S.E. 

Mr. Dutton, who accompanied and sang at the Dinner, 
will again help on this occasion.

L.B.C. DRAMATIC SOCIETY, 

Prendeni : Theo. Godlee. CammiUee : Aahley Barrett, A. E. Buckler, 
G. G. Fitch, Frank JoUy, M. B. Bucker, Jan., £. T. Sachs, T. E. 
ScTJtton, and F. M. WUUams. Stofe Manager: F. W. Morru. 
Aetinp Managtr: B. M. Gruggen. Honorary Secretary: Alfred 
Barker. 

The second performance of the above Society was held 
at St. George's Hall, Langham Place, on Tuesday,* 
February 20th, when H. J. Byron's Comedy, " One 
Hundred Thousand Pounds," preceded by the Comedietta, 
"•Withered Leaves," by P. W. Broughtou, was represented 
before " a large and fashionable audience." The efforts of 
the members of the Society were much assisted by the 
charming and graceful acting of Miss Ivan Bristow and 
Miss Lucy Roche, the former especially winning all hearts ; 
while, as Mrs. Barlow in the comedy, Mrs. Hodgkinson was 
much applauded for her natural and humorous impersona- 
tion of the character. 

We append the casts in the two pieces performed, and 
some account of the evening's entertainment : — 

"ONE HUNDBED THOUSAND POUNDS."

ACT IL— Scene : A room in the house of Gerald Goodwin. 
ACT III.— Scene : Same as Act I.

"WITHERED LEAVES,"

The L.B.C. Orchestral Society kindly tendered their services, and 

performed the following selection of music during the evening : —

Shortly after 7.30 the curtain rose for the performance of 
" Withered Leaves," disclosing a pretty glen with a stream 
flowing at the back, crossed by a rustic bridge. Tom 
Oonyers (R. G. Francb), having some years back had a 
misunderstanding with his father. Sir Conyers Gonyers 
(A. E. Buckler), a white-headed Anglo-Indian of about 
sixty, has left his father s house, and at the time that our 
play depicts he arrives at the glen in company with his 
bosom friend, Arthur Middleton (A. Barker), who has 
before visited the neighbourhood, and on that occasion 
fallen in love with May Rivers (Miss Ivan Bristow). May, 
ignorant of Arthur's sentiments towards her, is engaged to 
Cecil Vane (E. H. Wilkins), who later on appears in his true 
character as the villain of the piece, who, whilst professing 
to be a bachelor, has in reality been married to Arthur's 
sister, and being disappointed in his hopes of acquiring with 
her a fortune, has broken her heart by coldness and neglect. 

During his estrangement from his son, Sir Conyers has 
married a young wife, and whilst on his honeymoon with the 
new Lady Conyers (Miss Lucy Roche) visits the glen. The 
conjugal affection of the baronet and his lady, and the flir- 
tations of Tom and his undiscovered step-mother, afford 
considerable amusement, which is intensified when Sir 
Conyers surprises Tom and Lady Conyers in a very com- 
promising situation. Whilst Sir Conyers and his unrevealed 
son are settling their differences, an unexpected meeting 
between Arthur and Vane discovers to May the villainy of 
her lover. He is dismissed from her presence with scathing 
contempt, and Arthur, taking this opportunity to unbosom 
his long-concealed love, takes his rival's place in her affec- 
tions. 

Sir Conyers, irritated at the flippancy with which Tom 
receives his requests for an explanation, is about to thrash 
his unknown offspring, when Lady Conyers^ makes known 
the relationship existing between them, and with the 






Digitized by Vj^OV IC 











LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 21 




reconciliation between father and son, and the approaching 
nuptials of Arthur and May, all ends most favourably. 

After some admirable music from the Orchestral Society, 
including the "lolanthe" Quadrilles, the curtain rises on 
the back room of Joe Barlow's (H. S. Naylor) grocer's shop 
in the Borough for the first act of *' One Hundred Thousand 
Pounds." 

Here Gerald Goodwin (R. M. Gruggen), having quarrelled 
with his uncle in Australia, Mr. Desborough (who has 
married again), at the commencement of our play is 
lodging. Gerald, whose shabby attire betokens the extent 
of the reduction of his finances during his vain seekings for 
employment, arrives on the scene just as Mrs. Barlow 
(Mrs. Hodgkinson) is trying to persuade Joe to get rid of 
their impecunious lodger before he gets into their debt. 

Emboldened by the admission of Alice (Miss Ivan Bristow), 
Joe Barlow's niece, that she returns his love, Gerald forgets 
his desperate circumstances, offers himself, and is accepted. 

Whilst the lovers are discussing ways and means with 
Uncle Joe, we are introduced to Pennythorne (F. W. Morris), 
a vulgar livery stable keeper, and a would-be suitor of Alice. 
Not being aware of her engagement with Goodwin, he im- 
portunes her for an answer to his suit, and is firmly but 
politely repulsed. 

Mr. Fluker (0. C. Fitch), Uncle Desborough's lawyer, 
enters in quest of Goodwin, and mistaking Pennythorne for 
the hero, informs him of the death of his uncle, and his 
succession to the hundred thousand pounds. The joy of 
the livery stable keeper at this intelligence is more easily 
imagined than described, but his raptures are brought to 
an abrupt termination by the arrival of Gerald, and the 
consequent correction of Fluker*s mistake. 

Act II. commences with some amusing passages beween 
Pyefinch, the valet of Gerald (who is now the fine gentle- 
man), and our friend Pennythorne, followed by the arrival 
of Alice, who has prevailed upon her uncle to bring her to 
view the surroundings of the man who professed to love 
her. Surprised by the unexpected return of Gerald, 
accompanied by Major Blackshaw (E. T. Sachs), she seeks 
refuge in an adjoining room. The Major, an astute com- 
pany-monger and thorough man of the world, rallies Gerald 
upon his dejected appearance, and in the course of conver- 
sation elicits from him the confession of his affection for 
Alice, and the baseness of his neglect of her since he has 
come into his wealth. The Major sneers at the idea of his 
marrying at all, much less with a girl of such lowly position, 
and suggests, as he still loves Alice and matrimony is out of 
the question, an obvious course is open to him. Gerald, 
enrageu ab me iiuerbiiiu cuuuocio oi iiis iriciiu, ocizso iiiiii uy 
the throat and demands a retraction of the objectionable 
words, and Alice, alarmed at the struggle, rushes from her 
hiding-place. The arrival of Joe in quest of his niece and 
the announcement of Sir Rumsey Waters (Ashley Barrett) 
and Charker (A. E. Buckler) further complicate the situa- 


tion. Alice is led away by her uncle more dead than alive, 
and the self-invited guests endeavour to infuse a little life 
into their crushed host. Their efforts are aided by the 
entry of Fluker with the news of arrival of the mail which 
is to confirm Gerald's inheritance of the £100,000, but he 
is almost immediately followed by Pennythorne, who relates 
in breathless eagerness that Mr. Desborough, instead of being 
dead, has himself arrived. This intelligence creates great 
sensation amongst the guests, who bitterly reproach Gerald 
with the remembrance of the sums that they have advanced 
him, and of which they now see small chance of repayment. 
The denunciations of his quondam friends are put a stop to 
by the re-entry of Alice, who has heard the news, and her 
devotion in offering her fortune to retrieve his position 
throws into darker relief the selfishness of Gerald's false 
friends. 

Act III. finds Joe and his wife iu sad distress ; for owing 
to^the failure of speculations in some of Blackshaw's swindling 
companies they find themselves ruined. Joe, believing 
that Pennythorne is sincere iu the avowal of his disinterested 
affection for Alice, persuades her to marry him, thinking 
that as Pennythorne is reported to be wealthy he will assist 
the family in tiding over their diificulties. Pennythorne, 
who is himself in the same straits, accepts Joe's advances 
with glee, thinking that Alice's fortune will put him on his 
legs again ; and hastens off to get the licence, returning to 
find the bailiffs in possession and the family plunged in 
grief. He now learns that Alice's money has also been lost 
in the general wreck, and whilst he is denouncing the whole 
family as impostors he is himself arrested for debt by the 
sheriff's officer, Gibbons (A. Henry), to the great surprise 
of the Barlows. Whilst Joe is out endeavouring to procure 
assistance, Fluker introduces Blackshaw, who is hiding from 
his creditors and is disguised as a French count, to Mrs. 
Barlow as a proposed lodger. 

Tlie end is now at hand, for the arrival of a letter from 
Uncle Desborough, informing Gerald that Uharker, who all 
along has benefited by the schemes of his dupe Blackshaw, 
has eloped with Mrs. Desborough, also holds out the wish 
of a reconciliation with his nephew. 

The further news that Charker is captured, and that he 
absolves Blackshaw from all complicity in his frauds, paves 
the way to a generally happy ending ; Gerald, restored to 
the favour of his uncle, pays Barlow's debts, and the curtain 
falls with the promise of the speedy union of Alice and 
Gerald, the happiness of the virtuous, and the usual dis- 
comfiture of the wicked. 

We have not yet heard what the financial result of the 
evening may be, but judging from the crowded state of all 
parts of the house it should be very satisfactory. The 
Society are to be congratulated on a very successful 
evening, and we trust that their subsequent performances 
(may they be many, and may we be there to see all of 
them) may all reach the high standard they have set up 
for themselves by the first and second performances at 
St. George s Hall.

NOTES FROM THE GALLERY. 

BY A CANDID FRIEND. 

I was the fortunate possessor, by purchase and not by 
gift, of a gallery ticket for St. George s Hall on the 
memorable 20th of February ; and so, Mr. Editor, I claim 
the usual privilege of an Englishman to grumble at any- 
thing and everything, more especially as in secret I may 
confess that I spent a very enjoyable evening. 

All the same, Sir, I don't consider " Withered Leaves " a 
success. I think the " withering" had gone a stage too far, 
and I am sorry it went on to a stage so near me. I don't 
know who was responsible for the choice of the play, and I 
do know how difficult it is to choose a good opening piece, 
but still the one chosen struck me as a poor play in itself, 
and especially unsuited to an amateur company owing to 
its long dialogues, enlivened by very little incident. 

Then, Sir, I could hardly hear anything at first till 
the actors got used to the iiall. Francis and Barker looked 
very nice, but I don't know what they were taking about — 
I certainly did hear the prompter once or twice. I thought, 
however, the whole cast, especially Francis and Buckler, 
did their best to enliven a very ungrateful piece, and 
Wilkins made the most of a difficult part. 

I thought we were going to have some fun once, when 
there seemed every prospect of a fight between Barker and 
Wilkins ; but that dash which the S.E. knows so well 
seemed to have been left in the S.E. for the night. 

Now, Sir, my grumbling is over. The piece of the evening 
struck me as more of a success even than ''Cjnril's Success." 
It went admirably throughout, the first act especially so, 
and the care taken in rehearsals was fully rewarded. 

The L.B.C. is indeed fortunate in possessing four such 
actors as Morris, Fitch, Naylor and Sachs. Of course, I 
am nothing if not critical, and the first two of those gentle- 
men will pardon my humbly suggesting a possible danger 
of over-acting on their part. Each of them struck me as a 
shade too noisy : I don't think Fennythorne was meant to 
be quite so vulgar, or Mr. Fluker to be quite so much of a 
windmill ; having said which, I have nothing but praise for 
them. Their admirable playing together in the first act, 
notably the very eflFective entrance of Mr. Fluker and 



Penn3rthorne's outburst of joy on hearing of his supposed 
windfall were very fine pieces of acting, and their scenes 
with the Count Grawbowski (?) in the last act were highly 
amusing. 

Naylor's impersonation of Mr. Barlow struck me as the 
feature of the piece ; characteristic without being overdone, 
and with the most made of a very expressive face. His 
quiet retort on Mrs. Barlow — "If my hair is rough, at 
least it*s my own," was most effective. A. F. White as the 
footman was also good, and Henry's make up as the bailiff 
brought down the house. One of the best pieces of acting 
in the play was Sach's rendering of Major Blackshaw, under 
difficulties. Gerald Goodwin nearly knocked him off his chair 
by an unexpected and dashing flank attack ; and then — 
that cigarette ! — I do hear tales about that cigarette, but 
of course I'd cut my tongue out sooner than divulge them. 

As to Gerald Goodwin himself, I confess I can't imagine 
his being very attractive at any time ; but I think Gruggen 
made him as attractive as possible, even at a sacrifice of 
dramatic effect. 

A young lady near me was longing for the entrance of 
the interesting hero in rags and tatters in the first act, but 
was much disappointed when R. M. G. appeared, certainly 
looking as if he hadn't had an3rthing to eat for a fortnight, 
but otherwise scrupulously well dressed. However, we 
waited with impatience to behold him as the " complete 
masher " in the second act ; but even those scenes of 
reckless revehry, and unlimited zoedone with the labels 
carefully turned away from the audience did not rouse 
Gerald much until he went at Blackshaw with a vigour 
which would have done credit to the gamest bantam cock. 

However, this is the groundless jealousy of the male 
mind, for I am sure the feminine half of the audience were 

Goodwinites to a woman ; and, indeed, one confessed to 

me that she thought Mr. Gruggen looked " very interesting." 

Finally, I think that the Committee, the Society, the 
actors, and the Club are very much to be congratulated on 
their efforts. A very successful evening was certainly the 
result, and the ringing applause of the crowded house 
was a fit termination to the second performance of the 
L.B.C. Dramatic Society.

OUK FRANCO-SWISS TOUR OF 1882. 

It has loDg been the opinion of those best qualified to 
give advice on the subject, " that if you have a story to 
tell, the most approved method is to begin at the begin- 
ning." In our case we find considerable difficulty in 
following this time-honoured usage, inasmuch as our expe- 
dition can hardly be said to have had a definite commence- 
ment, our start bearing more resemblance to a cunningly 
contrived handicap than anything else. Langmore, the 
limit man, having time at his disposal, set out a couple of 
days ahead of us, intending to proceed by easy stages until 
we overtook him ; whilst on the other hand Buckler left 
town a day later than the main body. Whilst the last- 
named is hastening on his way to form a junction with 
the early starters, we will give a short account of the con- 
struction and aims of our expedition. 

Our party consisted of the convenient number of four, 
viz., Dr. Langmore, N. B. Morris, W. J. Williams, and 
A. E. Buckler (the three last being survivors of the 
Pyrenean five of 1879). Morris, who had been over the 
ground before, fulfilled the duties of captain and guide, 
and also kept the bag. Our intention was to devote a week 
to the ride across France, from Dieppe to N^uch&tel, in 
which time we hoped, by the aid of a judicious piece of 
training through some of the least interesting country, to 
reach our Swiss cruising ground ; the following fortnight 
to be devoted to a round through Western and Northern 
Switzerland, finishing with the Oberland. 

While dealing with these introductory remarks, it may 
be as well to say that we took our cycles to Switzerland 
not with a view of doing any great distances or other feats 
worth recording as bicycling, but solely for the purpose of 
enabling us to get about from place to place ; just as, 
without their assistance, we should have made use of dili- 
gences and hired carriages for the transport of ourselves 
and baggage. 

A few words on the weather and the roads, and we shall 
be ready to start on our travels. It was our fate not only 
to follow the disastrous weather so prevalent on the Con- 
tinent during the autumn, but also to have a large share of 
it for a travelling companion, so that in common fairness 
we roust say that our experience of the roads was an 
exceptional one. Leaving out the almost perfect roads of 
Normandy, we rode mostly over wet and greasy limestone, 
worthy of Derbyshire. Sometimes we got a piece of dry 
riding, but generally the surface was so completely removed 
by the heavy rains, that after a few miles of what our 
medico pithily, if not^ politely, described as " blisters and 
blasphemy/' we wished ourselves back again at the less 
painful mud-ploughing. 

Morris and Williams left London on the night of the 
15th September, and after a calm and uneventful passage 
landed at Dieppe the following morning. The route to 
Rouen has been travelled by too many members of the 



L.B.G. to make any description here needful Pouring rain 
and heavy roads were our first experiences, a fitting intro- 
duction, as it afterwards proved. Nearing Rouen the 
steam trams are a great nuisance. At their passing places 
they take up nearly all the road, and threaten to Juggernaut 
the cyclist as they swing rapidly round the severe curves. 
The bleating of the penny trumpets, used instead of whistles, 
is rather comical. Distance to Rouen, 37 miles. 

Here we tried the B.T.O. hotel—" Hotel de Commerce," 
but did not find it a first-class house. We were pleased to 
find a note from our truant doctor. It is very nice to 
have a medical man with your party," said our friends at 
home—" So useful in case of accidents." So we thought 
when we found that the doctor was two days ahead and 
steadily ploughing along. The following day (Sunday) 
was pleasantly spent in a visit to the Cathedral, the fine 
church of St. Ouen, and the many places of interest in this 
city so closely connected with the annals of our own 
country. 

Monday morning saw the three of us (for Buckler had 
joined company the preceding day) on our way in fair time. 
Everything was charming — bright sunshine and fine country, 
resembling in its wooded coombes Dorset or Devonahire, 
whilst every now and then, as our route followed the ever- 
winding Seine, we were reminded of some of the happiest 
pieces of Thames scenery. 

Near the hundredth kilo stone from Paris there is a nasty 
piece of bad pavS over a bridge at the bottom of a steep hill ; 
but thanks to information received from L., we were on the 
look out. We were not so lucky with a wretched gully half- 
way down another hill, for forgetting our warning until too 
late, we had an anxious half second, but fortunately escaped 
with a severe shaking. After this little excitement we kept 
a bright look out, and avoided like pitfalls in future. 
Dejeuner at Gaillon (26^) good. 

The afternoon turning out badly — ^rain with tropical fury 
for Ja time followed by a steady downpour — we were not 
sorry to reach Mantes (49 — " Hotel Cheval Blanc") We now 
reaped the advantages of our comprehensive wardrobes, for 
all carried plenty of baggage (about 15 lbs. apiece). The 
extra weight is no doubt a drawback, but we think the 
luxury of a complete change, including trousers, slippers, 
and a tweed hat for the sun or for use when not riding, 
amply repays the additional labour in transport. 

The next morning, affairsnot looking particularly pleasing, 
we trained into Paris, and oul again the other side as far as 
Melun, to avoid our bete noir the pav4. Just as we were 
starting after lunch, ("Hotel St. Nicolas"), down came the 
rain again ; we seemed in good quarters, and there was no 
need to hurry. It ended in our staying. For sometime we 
were quite contented with our lot, and contrived to make 
ourselves very comfortable. The dinner deserves a special 
mention ; it was a masterpiece, even in this land of cordons 
bletis. The company were all townspeople, and interested 



Digitized by 



24 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



us as much as our presence did them. Better hands at the 
trenches we never saw, and though we tried hard to uphold 
the honour of our country, only succeeded in making 
ourselves uncomfortable. 

Melun is celebrated as the earthly resting place of those 
French kings whose reputation was not sweet enough to 
admit of their interment at St. Denis. It is also renowned 
for its b — flats, alas ! unburied during our stay. But we 
are anticipating. The three of us were housed for the 
night in a rough tiled room over the kitchen. Lights were 
extinguished, and we tried to sleep, but sundry irritations 
prevented that wished-for result. Suddenly the Capitaine 
jumped out of bed, exclaiming " I can't stand this any 
longer ! " and struck a light. A cry of horror ensued, which 
brought the N.W. official to his aid. A scene was disclosed 

which caused language " ." The invading army was 

establishing itself in force, swarming over the pillow in 
skirmishing order. The S.W. D.C. brought up the Gatling 
gun and discharged a heavy fire of Keating, which rather 
disconcerted the hungry punaises, who scuttled off as fast as 
possible. Although worsted for the moment, they held the 
position too strongly to be dislodged, and " M. le Capitaine 
was forced to find other quarters in one of the adjacent 
beds. 

The next morning an early start was made to make up 
for lost time, but our enemy the rain gave us no peace, and 
by the time we reached Montereau (20) we were pretty wet. 
A halt to admit of the weather clearing formed an excuse 
for dejeuner, but matters became worse instead of better, 
and the afternoon turned out the proverbial "cats and 
dogs." This was an opportunity not to be missed. We 
now knew we must avail ourselves considerably of the rail- 
way if we were to reach Switzerland by the end of the week, 
even if the weather improved. So we put on dry things, 
made for the station, booked straight away for Dijon, and, 
snugly ensconced in a comfortable carriage, smiled beamingly 
as the rain beat against the windows. Dijon, " Hotel de 
Bourgogne," good. 

As we were now ahead of our programme, there was no 
need to hurry off the next morning. Starting, we ran to 
Ddle (30 miles), and spent the rest of the day exploring 
that town (" Hotel de Geneve," rather expensive). Weather 
still unsettled and rainy. Friday morning was pouring wet. 
We waited some time for it to clear, and then gave up hope 
and started. Presently it gave over for awhile, but the 
improvement was only temporary, and before long it was 
coming down with its usual monotony. Near Mouchard we 
had a fine pig hunt. The victim was just such a specimen 
as one sees at Islington shortly before Christmas. He ran 
well and straight, keeping up his spurt until he arrived at 
his own farmyard (about a mile), and never was piggy more 
thankful to bolt into his sty than our poor blown porker. 

By the time Salins was reached we were amongst the 
Jura range, and were well satisfied with the foretaste of 



the scenery to be expected further on. From Salins the 
road mounts up tremendously ; the hill out of the town — 
5 miles long by the kilo stones — is a great grind. Darkness 
overtook us in a dense pine forest some way short of 
Levier (40^). Arriving at the village in the darkness, we 
were not particularly pleased with its appearance, and 
almost despaired of procuring quarters for the night. But 
after one vain attempt at a wretched looking auberge we 
unexpectedly obtained even luxurious accommodation, and, 
contrary to our former experiences of village auberges, not 
only fared well but slept comfortably. Levier is about 
2500 feet above sea level, and it was bitterly cold. We had 
no doubt missed seeing some fine scenery to-day owing to 
the mist and rain. 

The next day, Saturday, we rode through Pontarlier and 
by the lovely Val de Travers to Neuch4tel (47). After the 
experiences of M. and W. in Italy two years ago, we had 
been expecting some difficulty with the frontier Customs, 
but to our surprise we were permitted to sail past both the 
French and Swiss douanes without hindrance. 

We were quite delighted to be at last in Switzerland, 
and one of our party who is contemplating an early sur- 
render of his personal freedom went to the extravagance of 
announcing the fact by wire. This was, although by no 
means a fine one, the first day without rain since we started. 

At NeuchAtel we stayed at the Belle Vue/' pleasantly 
situated at the edge of the lake, which we found a superior 
hotel in all respects, charges included. 

Sunday was everything that could be desired, brilliant 
sunshine all day. M. and W. went in search of the English 
Church, whilst B. went to the native one. At the latter 
the service (reformed) was in French, and our representative 
not being very au fait in the language, could not make 
much out of it. By his own account the portion that he 
understood most about was the finish of the sermon, which 
wound up with allez-vaus-en, followed immediately by a 
general stampede. 

In the evening, to our great satisfaction, (for we were 
uneasy at not having heard of him), the Doctor arrived. 
He had pluckily ridden the entire distance from Diei)pe, 
by himself, through wretched weather and over awful roads. 

The next morning saw our small column en route for 
Lucerne. Our journey that day was to Olten (57). The 
road at first ran close alongside the lakes of Neuch&tel and 
Bienne. After passing through the town of the latter 
name wet traversed for the most part a wide fertile valley, 
with pretty if not striking views on either hand. As we 
neared our destination, the horizon clearing — as it often 
does towards evening — disclosed on our right a grand 
prospect of big snow mountains all aglow with the rays of 
the setting sun. 

Near Neuch&tel there resides a "tyke" — a great big 
smooth-haired brute— just such an animal that you hesitate 
to say a disrespectful word to — at least, unless you are on 



Digitized by Vj^^^ie 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



25 



the other side of a six-foot wall, with many glass bottles on 
top. This dog may be an exemplary husband and a fond 
father, but he certainly was not at all polite to Williams, for 
he ran alongside him and tried to pull him off by the leg. 
Although sympathising very much with the troubles of W., 
who we thought must every moment succumb to the fierce 
onslaught of the enemy, and thinking that our turn would 
come next, we oould not avoid smiling at the ridiculousness 
of the situation : W. riding with one leg pointing nearly 
to the zenith, whilst the dog, disappointed of his prey, made 
frantic springs at the elevated beaux mallets, the envy of 
the French belles from the Channel to the Mediterranean. 
We are, however, fortunately spared the paiu of chronicling 
the catastrophe that seemed imminent, for the " tyke " was 
by this time too winded to continue the chase, and to our 
joy suffered the rest of us to pass unmolested. 

We lunched sumptuously at Soleure ; and, just as we 
were beginning to find out that many veal cutlets induce 
much laziness, in came the patron to say that two local 
bicyclists would esteem it an honour if we would allow them 
to accompany us a short distance. Of course we acquiesced, 
and after a little conversation the six of us rode off, much 
to the delight of the townspeople, who lined the streets and 
windows to see the show. Our new companions were ex- 
cellently mounted on a light "D.H.F." and "Arab" res- 
pectively, so that with our heavy luggage and our morning's 
ride of some five and thirty miles, we rather dreaded the 
result should they force the pace. The unwished for always 
happens, so there was no alternative but to do our best* 
The doctor, whose digestive organs had raced ours in the 
difficult task of getting the better of our too recent repast, 
took the lead ; whilst our Bath Medallist, riding quietly in 
the rear, in reserve, endeavoured to assimilate into his sys- 
tem the weight that pressed so heavily on his chest. The 
subtlety of of our strategy was soon apparent, for after a few 
miles W. went to the front, and, although the rider of the 
"Arab" put down his head and made several mad rushes, he 
succeeded in forging ahead. Soon after this the strongest 
rider of our Swiss friends suggested that it was time for 
them to return ; so, after an adjournment to drink " bon 
voyage," we separated, well pleased with our new ac- 
quaintances and the afternoon's diversions. Hotel at 
Olteu, " The Schweizerhof," good and moderate. 

The following day we were early astir, but as usual did 
not get off until later than we intended. The distance 
being short (33} miles) we contemplated getting into 
Lucerne by midday. The road for a short distance runs 
alongside the swiftly-rushing Aar, and then for awhile 
passes through a rather tame agricultural country ; but 
before the old town of Sursee is reached, on mounting a 
ridge a change comes over the spirit of the scene. Close to 
us is the pretty lake of Sempach, with the neighbouring 
hills, trees and ch&lets, reflected on its placed blue surface ; 
whilst in the distance the mountain peaks, rising like grim 



giants, tell us that we have reached our goal. Those of us 
who have not seen these wonders before are delighted with 
our first view of the Rigi and Pilatus, the latter sparkling 
in its mantle of snow. Again we have to record a cauine 
episode. A beast of a dog rushed out of a farmyard full 
tilt at Morris. Into the wheel he banged ; down came the 
crank on his uplifted jaws, and then the back wheel did its 
little best to complete the discomfiture of the brute. 
Luckily M. was going fast, and the momentum of the 
machine and baggage saved what might have been a serious 
accident. The next time this dog contemplates lunching 
on cyclists' legs he will doubtlessly show more science in 
his mode of attack. 

Arriving at Lucerne we put up at the " Hotel Schwann " 
on the Schweizerhof Quay (recommended). Having still 
the afternoon before us, we sallied out to see the Lion, and 
other wonders of this most interesting town. We spent 
some time watching the drilling of the fire brigade. It is 
to be hoped that fires are not very prevalent, as, unless the 
men are a little smarter in action than in practice, the 
flames must have it pretty much their own way. The 
tactics of a big fat man amused us mucK Each time the 
order was given to clear for action, he seized the spare nozzle 
piece, (a puny thing in his big grasp), and retreated to a 
quiet corner, with his burden at attention, only returning, 
(mopping his face as if he had been doing all the work), 
when everything was over and the gear stowed away again. 
In the evening we went to the Cathedral to hear the big 
organ, and spent a chilly hour in semi-darkness. The 
musical members of our party were rather disappointed ; 
while the uninitiated thought the performance very grand. 
They told us at the hotel that most likely the instrument 
was pUyed by a pupil of the organist ; this would account 
for the verdict of our learned ones. 

Wednesday, we spent on the lake ; staying for an hour 
or two at Fluellen. The boats are very good, and amid the 
magnificent scenery our day was very pleasantly spent. On 
the return journey we landed at Vitznau, and after inspect- 
ing the railway started to .walk up the Rigi, to spend the 
night on the summit. The rain, which we had almost for- 
gotten, the last few days having been so lovely, came down 
heavily soon after we commenced the ascent, and before we 
reached the top we were beautifully moist. In addition to 
other discomforts; in the early darkness we had some diffi- 
culty in groping our way to the Rigi Eulm Hotel, and when 
we arrived things were not very cheering. It was bitterly 
cold, our clothes were very wet, and we had imprudently 
omitted to bring anything with us except our sleeping 
tackle. The vast hotel, all but empty, was most dismal, 
and the attention, (we suppose the lateness of the season in 
some measure accounts for this), most indifferent. After 
dinner, despairing of getting warm by any external method, 
for the comfortless German stove was but a poor apology 
for a fire, we consumed (what was called) whisky and 



Digitized by VjU^O^IC 



26 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



chartreuse vert until we began to take a more hopeful 
view of things in general. We retired to roost early, in 
order that we might be ready for the sunrise. But the 
sunrise was distinctly a failure. We turned out of bed, 
but one glance was sufficient. It was snowing heavily, and 
the view might easily have been measured with a yard 
measure, or even a foot rule. In a twinkling we were back 
again snugly in bed, with only our noses exposed to the 
extremely bracing atmosphere. About midday we started 
on the walk down to Weggis, and the weather clearing we 
were rewarded with some fine views. Returning to 
Lucerne, we spent the afternoon examining the quaint 
pictures in the covered bridges. 

It rained all night, and when we got up the next morning 
was still coming down, but fortunately gave over by the 
time we were ready to start. We skirted the lake as far as 
Alpnach, a pretty ride, and we daresay good going in fine 
weather ; but we suffered some miles of sticky limestone 
mud in patience. It fairly beat record, and we had sampled 
some good specimens lately. After a time Sarnen is reached, 
and the road again takes to the water, running in close 
proximity to the beautiful lake (Sarnen) until the ascent to 
Lungern commences. Near here we were the innocent 
cause of a little diversion. Williams and the doctor riding 
some distance ahead of the other two, fell in with a man 
driving some cattle ; a fine cow, frightened at the cycles, 
kicked up her heels and bolted ; the man hung on to her 
horns, endeavouring to stop the skittish female, but it was 
only after he had lost his hat, broken his stick over her 
head, and uttered manystrange oaths in an unknown tongue, 
that he succeeded in his efforts. Some little time afterwards, 
N. and B., quite unaware of this little performance, met the 
same pair. The result was almost identical, the unfortunate 
man again being dragged at express speed up the mountain 
slope. We lunched at Lungern, and immediately on leaving 
commenced the ascent of the Briinig. The road rises in 
long windings through a thick wood : the gradient is not 
steep, and the ascent from the village is not more than three 
and a half miles. We considered it child's play compared 
with some of the passes we negotiated in the Pyrenees; (the 
Tourmalet, for instance, was eleven miles to the summit, 
nearly 7000 feet, and pretty stiff the entire distance). We 
were able to mount some distance before the top (3396 feet) 
was reached, and rode for some way down the other side, 
until the brakes so punished our wrists that we were 
compelled to walk most of the remainder of the descent. At 
the bottom we doubled back up the valley to Meiringen 
(37i). It had been raining nearly all the afternoon, and 
we were completely saturated by the time we reached the 
" Hotel Sauvage," (very good). 

Before leaving the next morning, we paid a visit to the 
Alpbach and Reichenbach falls, which, owing to the wet 
weather, we saw at their best ; though, owing to the height 
of the neighbouring clififs, it was difficult to realise their 



true size. Resuming our journey, we ran down the valley 
to Brientz. Across the lake we had a distant view of the 
Giessbach, but had no time to visit that celebrated fall. 
From Brientz to Interlaken the road runs along the 
northern shore of the lake, and the ride was most interesting 
and enjoyable. The doctor amused us by coming to a 
standstill whilst crawling behind a drove of our friends the 
cows ; a friendly wall saving him from falling, and the 
whole performance looked so premeditated that the un- 
initiated thought our friend was doing a little "fancy" for 
their amusement. We were much tempted at Interlaken 
by the fine display of shops. Fortunately for our exchequer 
we had no means of conveying any of the pretty things so 
enticingly displayed, but contented ourselves with adding a 
good many capital photos to our collection. On the windows 
of a pharmacy we discovered a most unique specimen of 
literal translation. It informed the passer-by that mineral 
waters were kept there " from the most usited sources." 

Interlaken is a favourite and fashionable resort in the 
summer time, but at this advanced season (30th September) 
it was all but empty. The view, looking south from the 
Hoheweg, of the Lauterbrunnen Valley and the Jungfrau, 
is most grand. It was here that we afterwards found that 
the loccJ paper devoted its attention to us. The notice 
ran very much like this : — " Tliere arrived here to-day 
four English gentlemen who are travelling through Europe 
with their velocipedes. They did not appear at aU fatigued, 
and after lunch proceeded on their journey to Grindelwald." 

The Grindelwald road follows the valley fsave the mark) 
of the Schwarze Lutschine. The rise in tne 121 miles is 
1600 feet. The gradients are trifling at first, heavy for 
some miles, and then ease oGf again before Grindelwald 
(3,468 feet) is reached. We think that Mr. Cobb, in 
suggesting the possibility of this stage being covered in the 
saadle, rather over-estimates the enduring powers of a 
rider. Our opinion is — and one of us tried it — that 
the piece below Burglauenen, where the road zigzags 
backwards and forwards across the gorge four times in a 
short distance, would give most riders the catip de grace. 
We had to walk a considerable portion of the ascent, but 
could no doubt have ridden more had the mud been less 
intense, and our luggage anywhere but where it was. 

Arriving at our destination (33 miles, counting an error 
in our road out of Interlaken), we made for the " Hotel de 
L'Ours," and never before, as will be seen presently, did we 
fall so completely on our feet. Grindelwald lying as it does 
at the foot of the Eiger, Mettenburg, and Wetterhorn, is 
one of the most favourite starting places for mountain excur- 
sions, and although it was not our intention to go in for 
any real climbing, there are the glaciei*s and many other 
expeditions of great interest to which a few days can be 
well devoted, so we contemplated staying here for some 
little time. This being the anniversary of an eventful day 
on which one of our party was hatched, we celebrated the 
festival at dinner in Swiss champagne, which we can recom- 
mend. Presently we were further cheered by the arrival of 
two Gazettes, and by a curious coincidence they contained 
Mr. Cobb*s account of the route we had been following. 

A. E. B. 

(To be continued.)

THE C.U.BI.C. 

Perhaps it may interest readers of the Gazbtte, paii;ica- 
larly those who have the pleasure of knowing our ex-uaptain 
G. 1). Day, to hear that on Wednesday last (February 21st) 
he kindly invited the Club to come over to St. Ives to have 
tea at his house. 

We were favoured with perfect weather overhead, but 
the roads were too heavy to admit of a large muster. 
Three miles out, nearly opposite Girton, the Captain 
snapped his treadle-pin and fell, involving another member 
in his ruin. The wind and road making it impossible to 
ride with one leg, he left the Club without a shepherd, 
and executed a weary tramp to Fenstanton (7 miles), 
where another treadle was awaiting him. Day was met 
at about half distance and piloted the Club to his house, 
where we enjoyed a luxurious tea and a long and very 
pleasant talk afterwards. A little before eight the moon 
was reported to be in working order, and the Club lit up 
and effected a start, accompanied by Day as far as Fen- 
stanton. A strong wind behind, and the glorious full moon, 
made the run home a great contrast to the outward journey, 
especially to the Captain. There were present E. H. Brown, 
L. 0. Carr, W. H. Fox, G. N. Latham, A. W. Rumney, 
A. B. W. Whatton, and J. S. Whatton, Captain. 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 

Under this head the Editors have asked me to give, 
shortly and sweetly, the latest and most startling infor- 
mation on the topics of the day outside our Club, for the 
education of our members in •'cyclo-political" mysteries 
and the providing of copy for the Gazette. 

The B.T.C. is dead— Long live the Cvclists' Touring 
Club. I hear sub rosd that the poll of the Club has 
resulted in a change of name by a majority of two to one. 
It is notable that only half the members of the Club, i.e,, 
3000, took the trouble to return their voting papers. 

I also hear that the Club have taken central offices in 
London. This is a step which, in view of the mass of 
metropolitan members and the central position of London, 
was bound to come. 

The next Council Meeting of the B.U. is fixed for 
Thursday, 8th of March. The place is that fixed for our 
Smoking Concert, the " City Arms," in St. Mary Axe. 
Except that the Officers and Executive for the year are to 
be elected at this meeting there is nothing very striking on 
the agenda. Mr. Todd's name appears for the first time 
as Honorary Secretary. 

I am sure that the Council of the Union will express in 
some suitable way their sense of Mr. English's work as 
secretary. I know no njan who works more and talks less 
than Mr. English, and that class of man ought to receive 
the utmost encouragement. 

The Executive Report contains the information that the 
Championships for the year are fixed as follows :— 
5 MUes Bicycle and 1 MUe Tricjcle ... 80th Jnne or 7th July, at 

Birmmgham. 

1 Mile Bicycle and 10 Miles Tricycle .. 14th July, at Crystal Palace 

50 Miles Bicycle 2l8t July, at Crystal Palace 

25 Miles Bicyde Date to be fixed, at Cam- 

bridge. 

6. P. Coleman, of ours, was re-elected Union Handicapper 
for London and the South at the last Council Meeting. I 
am glad to hear that he is already nearly overwhelmed 
with Easter handicaps. The Fboitt Wheel Hub. 



QUILLS. 

" Aristotle and Pliny deliver it that the porpentine casteth off his quills 
when he would attack, or defend himself, but Dioscorides affirmeth it a 
point of folly to believe it."— Sir T. BaowKB. 

«••••• 

" tempora, mores." A quotation which has the 
great advantage of admitting of any interpretation that 
the reader chooses to give it. In this instance it signifies 
that time and the manners of the L.B.C. have brought 
about the usual annual change in the personality of the 
irresponsible and anonymous author of this column. 

The present writer feels it a great honour to succeed 
such celebrities as "Eavesdropper," "The Man with the 
Muck Rake," " Rambler," and the other great men of the 
past, and he trusts that, as in their case, so he may be 
allowed the usual licence in his Club jokes and his Club 
scandal. That licence is always tolerated by the Club 
good temper, and will, he hopes, always be restrained 
within due limits by the Club good feeling. 

It is true I am anonymous, and therefore irresponsible to 
the Club. The Editors however permit me to say that 
they accept fully the responsibility for anything appearing 
in these columns. (See the " Editorial Declaration.' — Edd.) 

For our mathematical men. How many members of the 
L.B.C. make an L.B.C. orchestra ? 

It is not true that the Orchestral Society will perform a 
fantasia on that well-known air, " For he s a Jollt/ good 
fellow," by the full strength of the Company at the 
approaching Smoking Concert 

Aforesaid Smoking Concert will commence at 7.30, not 
7.0, as previously stated.

lately published. 

H. R. Reynolds, B. A., has passed the Intermediate Exam, 
of the Incorporated Law Society. 

The Yorke Priae, awarded annually to a graduate for 
the best essay upon some subject relating to the " Law of 
Property : its Principles and Historv in Various Ages and 
Countries," has been awarded to T. R Scrutton, B.A., 
Trinity College, Cambridge.

eve to realism in his character of riparian masher — ransacked 
the City for the most approved shape of tile, for a charming 
young lady in the audience to say sweetly, " Oh that I could 
have that hat for a few moments, I should so like to sit upon 
it!''

say that the success of " Withered Leaves" depended to a 
great extent upon the indefatigable exertions of Mr. Sparkes.

ATTENDANCES AT RUNS. 
To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sir, — The many difficulties which have arisen from time to 
time in construing the regulations with reference to the atten- 
dance prizes have led the Committee to appoint a small 
Sub-Committee to revise these regulations in such a way as 
to remove these difficulties, and further to draw up a few 
rules which may enable the District Captains to araw up 
their reports upon a uniform basis, ana thus render the 
various reports in future an accurate record of the relative 
attendances in the diiFerent districts. I shall therefore be 
obliged if all present ^nd past D.C.s will forward me, for 
the guidance of the Sub-Comnaittee, with as little delay as 
possible, a note of any difficulties which may occur to tnem 
in the meaning of the attendance regulations, and also a 
note of the method on which they have habitually calcu- 
lated, if they have ever done so, the average and other 
attendances published in the Blue Book. — Tours truly, 

Walthamstow. Theo. Godlee. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sir, — Allow me, on behalf of the Dramatic Society, to 
thank those members who so conduced to the success of 
our performance (February 20th) by their efforts and 
cordial support, thanks being specially due to the 
Orchestral Society and the stewards. 

I would also request members to remit me as soon as 
possible for their tickets, to enable me to close the 
accounts. — I am, dear sir, yours truly, 

Alf. Barker, 
Hon. Sec. and Treasurer Dramatic Society.

Tks next Gazette will be published on Thursday, 6th 
April, and will then appear weekly. Copy should be 
sent in not later than 6 p.m. on Tuesday, the 3rd. 

CLUB RUNS; THEIR HISTORY AND THEIR 
LESSON. 

It is appropriate to rentilate this subject at the time of 
issuing our first Meet Card for the present year, and in 
order to drive more fully home to backsliders the admonition 
with which we conclude, the latter shall be preceded by a 
few remarks on "The Natural History of Club Runs." 
Even in the dark ages, before the appearance of our 
Gazette, Club Runs existed (as may be gathered from 
fragmentary notices in contemporary papers), taking their 
rise, perchance, in that tendency of the early wheelmen to 
band themselves together for defence against the roughs 
and dogs of the period — their common foes. Some such 
idea at any rate, coupled with the desire for companionship, 
must have brought scattered riders together before they 
aggregated into clubs. The leading spirits in the latter 
soon found in the Saturday Run a valuable means of sus- 
taining esprit de corps, and, enlightened as we may claim 
to be by the experience of past years, we can boldly assert 
that without its Club Runs the healthy life of a club must 
depart. The chance is then afforded of meeting distant 
friends and of making new ones, and many a warm friend- 
ship has been struck up between Club men who, but for the 
Club Run, would never have met at the Club Tea. It is at 
this frugal meal — severe in its Spartan simplicity — that the 
breast of the young athlete kindles to the fire of emulation^ 



and, building castles in the air. he decorates their walls with 
Enamelled Medals and Silver Gups. 

What though the eggs be hard and weak the tea ? What 
though the veal be lukewarm and tardy in appearance ? 
The LB.C. man whose heart is in the right place sticks 
not at such trifles. They serve but to amuse, while he 
reflects that his suffering is as a fleabite to the anguish of 
his chief, on whose shoulders rests the responsibility of the 
happiness of all. Grumble he probably will— as an English- 
man it is his duty to do so — but let him do it inwardly, 
" beneath a smiling countenance hiding his aching heart,'' 
lest his discontent demoralize others. 

And then there is a sense of happiness, not to be readily 
described but none the less real, that goes with the home- 
ward ride, when stars are beginning to peep through the 
warm evening air, when tobacco and small talk reign 
supreme and the District Captain has relaxed the severity 
of his rule. This to our mind is the best part of a Club 
Run. A dolcefar niente pervades the party— for the flyers 
have ceased to vex, and have toned down to the gentler 
pace of their steadier comrades. The theme is a pleasing 
one, and the liberal support afforded to Club Runs proves 
that our members sympathise with them. But alas ! our 
pot of ointment is not without its fly. There are those who 
vote the Club Run flat, stale, and unprofitable. To such 
let us say one word, and let us say it ex cathedra-^QO to a 
DOZEN of them THIS YEAR ! This will cure your disbelief ; at 
least if you diligently strive, as we hold all should, to give and 
take fairly, and to make allowance for the failings of others, 
remembering that " to err is human — to forgive divine."

'committee. 

At a meeting of the Committee held on Monday, the 
5th March, the following gentlemen were elected members 
of the Club :— 

Name and Address. Proposer. Seconder. 

Alfred Chas. Fimter, 1, Cumber- 
land Terrace, Lloyd Square, W.O. W. W. Howard. Ajshley Barrett. 

Alfred Acton Kelsey, 18, Pembury 
Road, Clapton A. Uoyd. ' . R. B. Hough. 

The resignation of Mr. H. S. Heal was accepted. 
The resignation of Mr. Juo. Williams, Jun., was accepted 
with regret. 

It was decided that the Club funds should provide 
the Field, Cyclist, and Iricydist, for the Editor's benefit, 
when he should think necessary. 

In reference to the proposal of the London Tricycle Club 
that they should hold a 100 Miles Trial in conjunction with 
the L.B.C. from Bath to London on Whit Monday, the 
Captain reported that T. K Scrutton and himself had 
conferred with three delegates from the L.T.C., and had 
pointed out very forcibly, both verbally and in a subsequent 
letter, the grave objections existing to the scheme. He 
further reported the receipt of a letter from the Committee 
of the L.T.C. stating that tliey had resolved to abandon 
their proposed trial, on which it was unanimously i^esolved 
that the thanks of the Committee of the L.B.C. should be 
forwarded to the L.T.C. for their courteous response to our 
representations. 

The Sub-Committee appointed to find a suitable office 
reported that they had engaged the use of one of three 
rooms at the " City Arms" Tavern, St. Mary Axe, for any 
number of nights not exceeding sixteen per annum, at the 
rate of £5 6s. per annum. Tlie Committee thereupon 
approved and adopted such action. 

At a meeting of the Committee held on Monday, 19th 
iust., the following gentlemen were elected members of the

The resignation of Mr. E. H. Carr was accepted. 

It was decided that the Secretary of the L.A.C. be 
informed that we are prepared to take less than twelve 
guineas for the Stamford Bridge Grounds on 30th June. 



in ^tmotUm. 

We regret to record the death of one of our oldest 
members. Charles Taylor, of 180, Kennington Park 
Road, died suddenly on March 4th after a few hours' 
illness. He was elected in February 1876, and was 
well-known in the S.W. Division of the Club. We 
trust his family will accept our sincere condolences 
on their loss. 



SUBSCRIPTIONS.— IMPORTANT. 

I beg to remind members that subscriptions for the 
current year were due on January 1st. I shall be pleased 
to receive all subscriptions not yet paid as early as possible. 

151, Amhurst Road, Hackney. R. B. Houan. 



SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
17th March. The opening run was among the most 
successful of that class of assembly which this District has 
enjoyed, as thirteen members in all took part in it. It was 
cold and cheerless at the starting time, and only three 
members started with the D.C. from the Comer, and rode 
by the upper road to Woodford and down Snake's Lane. 
At Woodford Bridge the Editor was waiting, and took part 
in the run to Chigwell, but did not wait for tea. Weeks 
came in soon after, having waited in Snaresbrook, and then 
various hard-training members who had been further afield 
dropped in one by one, the Captain finally completing the 
party. The later arrivals were damp, as rain began about 
5.0, and the return journey, after a quiet smoke and chat, 
was also in the rain. The " King's Head " was frequented 
by more than one other Club, including the London Scottish. 
The District only managed five officers, which the D.C. 
considers a poor allowance. Present : Ashley W. Barrett 
(Captain), G. H. Gibbs, R. B. Hough, S. .G. Hough, A. A 
Kelsey, Large, Lloyd, Long, Lyne, T. E. Scrutton, E. D. 
Seweli, E. A. Weeks, and 

Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 



N.W. DiSTEICT. 

Our opening run was not very well attended, only ten 
members and two visitors putting in an appearance. It 
may be that some members have not yet started riding — let 
us hope that they have not given it up altogether. We 
started from " Jack Straw's Castle " six strong, and arrived 
at " Tally-ho 1 " without incident except a sharp shower of 
hail, which quickly turned into sleet and then rain: A 
short stay was made for shelter and to talk to our reinforce- 
ment, which brouglit our number*up to twelve. At Whet- 
stone we were again caught in a snowstorm, but found a 
friendly cowshed just in time. We turned to the left at 
the church in Barnet, and again to the left; near Well 
House, and then on by Rowley Green to Shenley, where we 
arrived at 6.15 p.m. The descent to the watersplash is 
rather rough just now, but the roads further on were very 
good. Everything was extremely comfortable at the " Black 
Lion," and the time spent there was passed in the usual 
jovial manner. No time was wasted, however, as the roads 
were rapidly getting heavy with the water that had fallen 
in various forms during the afternoon. It is a pretty sight 
to see a dozen lamps alight (when they will keep so) winding 
through the trees, but we had to keep a sharp lookout, as

the lanes were not too light. An easy pace was kept all 
the way home> the ran terminating at 9.15 p.m. Distance 
25 miles. Palmbr Dalton, District Captain. 

S.W. District. 
17 th March. It might be said that the opening run of 
the S.W. District was numerously attended ; it might be 
related that the weather was all that could be desired ; 
it might also be affirmed that the District Captain graced 
the proceedings with his presence ; but, as a candid cor- 
respondent, we must confess that the run was carried out 
by only two members, the weather was most unpleasant, 
and the District Captain was unavoidably absent. Under 
these circumstances, we think it best not to hazard our 
reputation for truthfulness by stating that our first run of 
tlie season was quite as successful as it might have been. 
Two members and an honorary member (with four legs) 
left Kingston at 4.30 and rode by Ether and Walton to 
Sunbury, where, at the "Magpie," they found a large 
muster of the W., the two Kinders and Hawley. After a 
satisfactory meal, pleasant conversation assisted by the 
usual consumption of tobacco made the charms of the 
fireside too apparent to be rashly exchanged for the cold 
drizzle out of doors. The homeward journey was short, 
and if the jam-like condition of the road, over which the 
river has been phiyfuUy rolling during the recent floods, 
is taken into consideration, it was certainly sweet as well. 
Present : H. L. Ryan and A.«K Buckler. 

For the D.C. His Dbputy. 

S.E. District. 

Od Saturday, March 3rd, six members joined in a pleasant 
run to Downe, where a good tea was provided by the land- 
lord of the " Oeorge Inn." Present : P. L. Adam, 6. P, 
Beck, A. Day, Alfred Herbert, S. E. Whealler, R. G. Prancis, 
and Adam's fox-terrier "^l\pap." 

Saturday, March 10th was cold and wintry. Only two 



men turned up at the starting place, and after spending 
some time in arranging a refractory tyre, made a short 
circular run. Present : W. C. Oswald and R. G. Francis. 

The weather was more vernal on Saturday 17th March, 
the day of the Opening run ; but frequent showers had the 
effect of reducing the attendance considerably. However, 
the thirteen who left the Fire Station at Croydon at half- 
past four were lucky enough to avoid a wetting, and occa- 
sional gleams of sunshine quite compensated for the muddy 
state of the roads. After a short rest on Farnborough 
Common, the Blackheath Division hove in sight — five in 
number, with Stephen Smith at their head, and when 
Orpington was reached a few stragglers coming in brought 
up the score to twenty-one, including one from the North 
West. The quality of the tea at -the " White Hart," 
Orpington, was below the average, but the music and sbngs 
which followed helped to remove the impressions caused by 
a not over civil host and not too artistic waiters. Mr. James 
Doyle, of the Rovers B.C. kindly presided at the piano and 
his songs were generally appreciated, as were those given 
by Beck, Oswald, and Morgan. At half-past eight lamps 
were lighted and there was an almost universal stampede 
homewards. The O'Reilly made a desperate attempt to 
ride up to the meet from Brighton, but unfortunately the 
adverse state of the elements prevented his getting beyond 
Croydon. Present : P. L. Adam, A. Barker, G. P. Beck, 
C. A. Brown, H. G. A. Brown, R. G. Francis, Alfred Herbert, 
S. Herbert. E. H. Hindley (N.W.), H. G. Morgan, A. Morley, 
J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, H. N. Sharp, R B. Smith, 
S. P. Smith, S. B. WheaUer, R C. Willis, W. Wyndham, 
J, B. Young, J. W. Young, and three visitors. 

It is to be hoped that those who were prevented by the 
weather from attending the Opening Run will have no such 
excuse next Saturday, when a good muster at the " Clayton 
Arms," Godstone, may be confidently expected. 

R. Francis, District Captain.

BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(COHTIZnTID rSOM PAOB 16.) 

§ 11. Thun to Fribaurg (vid Bern) and bad by the 
Valleys of the Sarine and Simme, 

A. — FIRST DAY. 

Thun to Pribourg (or Freyburg), 41 miles. 
As you will have to sleep out at Fribourg, send bag by 
train and get your ticket for it before starting. The two 
roads to Bern are given in § 4. If you have ahready seen 
Bern you will find the Kirchdorf road the best, as it leads 
into the Fribourg end of Bern, and you can ride straight on 
without encountering paved streets (you turn to your left 
at the top of the hill by which you come up into Bern). 
If, however, this is to l)e your only visit to Bern, most cer- 
tainly take the Heimberg and Miinsingen road and wheel 
through the town. The hill leading down into it may be 
ridden (brake essential), but don't forget the bears at the 
bottom. 

Between Bern and Fribourg there is no occasion to dis- 
mount, but there is a longish rise of 350 feet between the 
ninth and twelfth miles which will give you some work, and 
you will have to ride down the big hill into Fribourg with 
caution, especially if it happen to be market day. The 
road passes through Nieder Waugen (3| miles), Torishaus 
(6J). and Lanthen (13}) to Fribourg (20). If you are not 
prepared to ride the whole forty miles from Thun without 
a meal, arrange to make it in Bern, as I cannot recommend 
any place further on. An experiment made at Torishaus 
proved an utter failure. The hill by which you descend 
into Fribourg leads you down to the chief suspension bridge, 
but instead of turning at once to your right across it, it will 
be better to ride (or wheel) up the hill in continuation of 
your road another quarter of a mile to the further suspen- 
sion bridge (Pont de Gotteron). After seeing this, return 
again and cross the other into the town. Ton must not 
career across this too rapidly, or you might be fined, as 
vehicles are only allowed to traverse it au pas. Beyond it 
there is pavement. As an early start will have to be made 
next morning, I recommend you to wheel up to the " Grand 
Hotel de Fribourg." It is at the far end of the town, not 
far from the railway station from which you will have to 
send your bag, and from which you will proceed on your 
journey by a road crossing underneath the railway just 
short of the station itself. 

Of course you will go and hear the famous organ after 
dinner — it is generally played about eight, but you had 
better ask at your hotel (where you will get tickets) to 
make sure. If you are a musical critic you must not raise 
your expectations too high. No doubt it is a fine instru- 
ment in its way, and it is fairly well played, which cannot 
be said of many of the Swiss organs, but it is a very long 
way indeed from being the finest organ in the world, or 
from bearing comparison with any of our first-class English 



organs. The fact is, when we English go abroad making 
holiday, we leave our critical faculties behind us. The first 
effect of such a total change of scene and habits is to make 
us take a rosy " view of everything, and sights and sounds 
seem to come to us through the medium of a mysterious 
glamour which invests them in many cases with attributes 
far transcending their real merits. When once, too, a 
reputation has been started in this way, and has received 
the orthodox ratification of the double star in Baedeker, 
no one ever thinks of questioning its claims, but is prepared 
at once to fall down and worship in rapt and reverent 
obedience. I do, however, think that it is high time that 
these Swiss organs should be a bit " blown " upon. The 
two best, so far as real quality of tone and workmanship 
are concerned, are those least publicly performed on, and 
therefore least known. The organ in the old (Protestant) 
Cathedral of St. Pierre at Geneva is a noble instrument, 
with rich broad diapason tone. It was built by an asso- 
ciation of co-operative workmen in Paris some twenty years 
ago — an association, I regret to say, which did not survive 
the days of the siege and the Commune, and it is the only 
production of the Society with which I am acquainted. In 
the Cathedral at B&le, too, is a good organ. Neither of 
these are such elaborate productions as those at Lucerne, 
Bern, and Fribourg, but they are superior in all the essential 
qualities of a good organ. The organ at Lucerne is a noisy, 
blaring, discordant thing, rendered still more painful to 
listen to by the astounding incompetence and bad taste of 
the performer ; and as for that at Bern, it seems to me to 
be permanently in the condition in which a waiter at the 
Bernerhof once represented it to be, in reply to a friend of 
mine, who, on asking if there was to be a performance that 
day, was told, " No, sir ! The organ, sir— she is sick ! " 
The Fribourg organ has also a little of the " blare " about 
it, and is defective in the broad mellow undertone so 
noticeable in our English organs ; but it is decidedly better 
than the other two, as well as better played. They have 
just had a new organ built for the parish church at 
Thun, and as there is still a debt on it I gave them a 
recital this summer in aid of the organ fund. The 
quieter work in the organ is exceedingly good, but the 
"reeds" are very inferior to those of English make. Whilst 
speaking of the church at Thun I cannot help recalling a 
characteristic display of Continental Protestantism on the 
part of the sacristan who admitted me to the church, and 
who kept a disreputable hat on his head and a filthy cigar 
in his mouth the whole time we were in it. After this you 
will be a little surprised to hear that the Church contains 
a regular altar with full canonical ornaments — crucifix, 
candles, etc. This, however, is the property of the " Old 
Catholics " who— some eight only in number, I am told — 
are allowed to use the church for their vernacular mass on 
Sundays. The only other church "ornament" in the 
place is a significant-looking hour-glass occupying the usual

place of the crucifix in the pulpit. I never victimised 
myself with a Protestant sermon at Thun, but if the good 
people have to submit to an hour of such blatant twaddle 
as I heard a Sunday or two ago in the principal church at 
Zurich, I pity them.

Frihaurg to Thun by BuUe, Chateau d^Oew, etc.
