Considering the amount of climbing to be done, as well as 
the distance, the above represents a tolerably hard day's 
work, and it is therefore necessary to start at daybreak. 
The earliest hour at which you could book your bag at the 
station would be 4.45, as a train leaves for Thun at 5.10, 
and you should certainly be ready with it then. But if it 
be in June or July, when day dawns earlier, I should not 
wait to book the bag in person, but should leave the hotel 
porter to do it, giving him at the same time a stamped 
envelope — addressed to the Thunerhof or wherever you 
may wish to send it — in which he will enclose the ticket 
for the bag, and post it at once in the '* Poste Ambulante " 
attached to the same train. It costs about fifty or sixty 
centimes to send an ordinary bag to Thun. Of course 
you will settle your bill overnight, and, instead of ordering 
a regular breakfast at so impracticable an hour, have a 
roll and a glass of milk brought to your room when you go 
to bed for you to take before you start, and postpone 
breakfasting till you reach Bulle ("Hotel Cheval Blanc"). 
You will find this a useful plan on many excursions. Few 
things are so irritating to the system as to find the virtue 
of your own early rising rewarded by the pardonable dila- 
toriness of gaping waiters and yawning cooks, at whose 
mercy you entirely place yourself whenever you set your 
heart upon a regular meal at such an hour. The fresh 
morning air is in itself food and drink, and when once you 
are in the saddle you will be astonished to find how far you 
can ride before you begin to remember that you have had 
nothing but a crust of bread for breakfast. After 20 or 25 
miles have been left behind, the rest entailed by a halt for 
breakfast is not unwelcome ; the inn you arrive at is by 
that time in full working gear — everyone wears their 
brightest morning smile, and is in the most obliging and 
serviceable mood. There is sure to be some [church or 



other building of interest close by for you to inspect whilst 
your meal is preparing, or perhaps there may be a stream to 
bathe in ; if not, there will be the usual admiring and 
inquisitive crowd of natives to amuse you. In any case you 
will have the satisfaction of feeling that the time you are 
thus kept waiting for your breakfast is a time of useful rest 
after your first stage of riding, whereas to have to wait for 
it before you can start is only so much time lost. 

There is no need for any special directions as regards this 
route ; when once you have crossed under the railway and 
taken the road to the left to Bulle you cannot well miss 
your way anywhere the whole day long. From the third 
to the seventh miles out from Fribourg you must be pre- 
pared for some sharp ups and downs, which you will find 
it best to take with a rush. You had better walk some of 
the ascent up from La Tine, and there is a stiffish hill just 
through Les Moulins, but as you will dismount at Chateaux 
d'Oex (Pension Berthed) for your lunch and principal rest 
you may not find it necessary to walk this. I should wheel 
up the hill into Rougemont, as the road is rough and the 
village paved. The long hill up from Saanen to the water- 
shed dividing the Sarine (or Saane) from the Simme has a 
very good surface if dry, and the gradient is not severe ; 
still, on a long day like this, you will not be able to ride 
all of it. There is ti path cutting off the bend, but you 
will find it rough work wheeling up it — ^still it can be done. 
The hill down to Zweisimmen is a most lovely bit of riding ; 
but I shall never forget the mess I was in at the bottom of 
it from the splutter of the brake, for unfortunately my 
day's work was rendered additionally hard by a succession 
of severe thunderstorms, and consequent muddy roads. 
The hill is a continuous descent of 1000 feet in about four 
miles, or a gradient of 1 in 20, and if you avoid the dili- 
gence hour you are not likely to meet many vehicles on it. 

You sometimes need to give an occasional eye to your 
bicycle when left outside the inn at which you halt. Whilst 
I was enjoying my breakfast at Bulle the local smithy was 
taking professional stock of my ** Neues Fuhrwerk," and 
had already removed a screw or two with a view to an ana- 
tomical inspection of my bearings when I caught him 
redhanded at it. This is, however, the only occasion on 
which I ever found any real liberties taken. The favourite 
occupation of the boys, when you are not by, is to fix your 
brake on — a harmless amusement enough when once you 
have acquired the habit of invariably releasing it the first 
thing on coming out of the hotel. It will not take you long 
to acquire this habit — ^at any rate it will not be the boys' 
fault if it does. 

Should you wish to make a two days' expedition of B, 
Chateau d'Oex would be the natural place for your halt, 
and you would send your bag on there from Fribourg. If 
properly forwarded it ought to be there by one o'clock, but 
when at Bulle, where the railway ends, you had better see 
that it has been transferred from train to diligence. The

train reaches BuUe at 7.50. On the whole the safest plan 
would be to book it by train from Fribourg (4.40 a.m.) to 
Bolle only, and there get it yourself at the station whilst 
waiting for breakfast ; then you can hand it on to the 
conductor of the diligence in person, and so insure its im- 
mediate transmission. This last process will probably have 
to be effected at the post office. 

At BuUe, by the bye, you will not fail to wind up your 
breakfast with Gruyire cheese, for the sake of being able to 
remark to yourself with the waiter in Punch that it " grew 
here ; " on the whole, however, you will come to the con- 
clusion that (as with the fish at Yarmouth) the best of it 
all goes to London. 

The above completes my list of bicycle excursions from 
Thun. But before passing on to Lucerne, my next head- 
quarters, I will add a few other stray suggestions of a 
possibly useful kind, jotting them down just as they come 
into my head. 

Baedeker mentions Baths in the Lake '* at Thun. There 
are none. There are only the river baths at the far end of 
the town, and very nice they are ; but it is well to acquaint 
yourself beforehand with the " hours and regulations " — 
the hotel porter has them — otherwise you may find yourself 
there at an hour when they are reserved for ladies. Should 
this ever be the case employ your time of waiting by 
walking down the river to see a magnificent lime tree, by 
far the largest I ever saw. It stands in a meadow some 
ten minutes further on. There is a seat under it, and you 
will get a good view of the Jungfrau, etc.^ from the river 
bank. 

Do not omit going to the Oiessbach Falls on the Lake of 
Brienc They are not accessible by road, but you can 
either (a) ride to Brienz and take a boat or steamer across ; 
or (b), leaving your bicycle at Interlachen or Bonigen, you 
can walk along the south side of the lake (char road as far 
as Iseltwald and mountain path after); or (c) you can make 
it a day of rest, and go the whole way by (1) steamer, (2) 
train, (3) steamer, and (4) rope-incline. The falls will give 
you ample opportunity for making experiment of the 
following singular optical delusion. Stand facing one of 
them, not too close, and fasten your eye on some particular 
patch of water at the top ; follow this with your eye fixed 
upon it down to the bottom of the fall ; repeat the process, 
say, from fifteen to twenty times, then suddenly look at the 
bank at the side. You will find that it is palpably and 
distinctly mamng upwards with great velocity. The last 
time I was there it rained in torrents the whole day, and 
we could not venture out of shelter, but looking at the falls 
from the covered balcony of the hotel, I found that with a 
little patient perseverance I could make the whole hillside 
move heavenwards in this way at a terrific rate. It is an 
optical delusion somewhat similar to that by which, when 



you are looking out of the window at a stationary train and 
it begins to move, you are driven to suppose that it is your 
own train that is in motion. 

Another expedition you ought to make is to the Schq/hch 
— mountain boots and alpenstock (or axe) required. Take 
the early steamer to Gunten, and ask for Johann Siegrist, 
a carpenter, as guide. As he is sometimes in Thun, it is 
well to inquire about him by post or otherwise (see § 5 a) 
beforehand. The landlord (Orabe) of the little inn at the 
landing stage can tell you where* to find him. The Schafloch 
is one of those curious limestones caves — of enormous size — 
in which there is a "glacifere," i.^., a reservoir of ice all 
the year round. It is supplied partly by water filtering 
through from above, partly by winter snows which are 
drifted in by the wind, but mainly, I fancy, by the 
constant condensation going on in sununer, owing to the 
contrasts of temperature between the iced air of the cave 
and the hot wind from outside. It is a most interesting 
place, and the walk to and from it is lovely. You will 
come down by the Justiz-Thal abready alluded to, and will 
cross the scene of a considerable landship which took place 
in July of last year. 

And now farewell to Thun. With Lucerne we wiU begin 
another chapter of our story. 

§ 12. Lucerne.— Circuit cf the Lake. 

If you have already made the excursion (see § 8) to 
Lucerne by the Brunig and back by the Emmenthal, you 
will naturally ride there from Thun by the latter. If you 
have not, and wish to choose between the two routes, the 
only remark I would offer is that though the Brunig is the 
more sensational the other is to my mind the more enjoy- 
able ride of the two. You must not go to Lucerne any 
time between the middle of July and the beginning of 
September without sending a wire early in the morning to 
order your room. When we were there this year in August 
the Schweizerhof and the National were sending disap- 
pointed travellers away at the rate of 150 a day, and on 
some occasions the hotel omnibuses had to do duty for 
" chambres d unlimited number of lits*' after the manner 
of the bathing machines in Punch. My personal experience 
of the Lucerne hotels is limited to the two just mentioned, 
of which I slightly prefer the latter ; but there are many 
other hotels in the place on a less extensive and perhaps 
less expensive scale ; or, if you are going to make a pro- 
longed stay, there are plenty of excellent pensions in and 
about the town. The objection, however, to the latter is 
that they mostly " dine early," so that if you wish to be 
out all day you would have to make special arrangements 
about your meals, and for this you would probably have to 
pay as much as if you were in a hotel. One advantage of 
the two big hotels is tiiat they have table d'hote as late as 
7.30, so that you can get a longer day out. At the 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



35 



National they are particularly strong in ices, which appear 
on the menu at least five days out of seven and are 
exceptionally good. Perhaps it is on this account that I 
have a lingering preference for it over the Schweizerhof : 
the thought of them gives one such a delicious sense of 
consolation when you are worrying up a hot hill. It is 
also in convenient proximity to the Casino, where very 
creditable musical entertainment is provided. I should, 
however, warn thirsty members of the L.B.G. that the 
patronage of the ''American Drink" bar at the National, 
which is presided over by a goddess attired in tlie full 
dress of the country, will be found a very expensive and 
unsatisfactory diversion. I hear the " Hotel du Lac " well 
spoken of, and as it is close to the railway station and the 
lauding-stage'of the principal steamers its situation is very 
convenient. 

The first thing you should do at Lucerne is to master 
the routes and habits of the lake steamers. There are 
three sets of them. There are the big boats which go to 
Fluelen, at the head of the main portion of the lake, 
some calling at all places on the vray on both sides, others 
only stopping at a few : then there are the two cross arms 
of the lake, the northern or Eiissnacht bay, and the southern 
or Alpnach bay, each with a separate boat service. Again, 
there are three landing-stages, two on the hotel or north side 
of the lake, and another on the south side near the railway. 
On the north side the Fluelen boats start from a stage near 
the Schweizerhof; the other boats from one nearer the 
National. The Kiissnacht boats ply from the north side 
only ; most but not all of the others call at the railway stage 
as well, and you can always save time, as at Thun (see § 7), 
by using this stage rather than the other. After this 
explanation, which a reference to the map will render 
intelligible, yon have only to tear out the page of your 
" Biirkli " which contains the boat time-table and keep it 
in your purse, and you will never be at a loss if convenience 
or necessity should at any time prompt you to a lift by the 
way. I use the word " necessity " advisedly, for though I 
have headed this section " Circuit of the Lake " there are 
physical diflSculties in the way of performing it entirely on 
a bicycle. Some of the promontories jutting out into the 
lake are so precipitous that no attempt has yet been made 
to carry the road along them, and in other places where 
there is a road it is so exposed in wet and stormy seasons 
to landslips that its continuity is liable to be temporarily 
interfered with. Such, for instance, was the case this 
summer with the road between Gersau and Brunnen. You 
will be dependent therefore on the steamboat service to 
supply the missing links in your course. You can get 
excellent breakfast, lunch, etc., on board the Fluelen boats 
— a, great convenience in^ case of an early start. I will now 
give the itinerary of the lake. Starting from Lucerne in 
the Pihitus direction, that ia towards the south side of the 
lake, we have—

I6i Beckenried. 

As far as Stanzstad the road is the*Brunig road (see § 8a). 
After leaving Lucerne it does not touch the lake till a mile 
beyond Horw. Stanzstad is at the point where the Alpnach 
Bay begins. The lake here is so narrow that a bridge has 
been run across it. From Stanzstad you again quit the lake 
for the 5^ miles to Buochs, as a big rocky promontory (the 
Biirgenstock, on which there is an enormous hotel) shuts 
you out from it. The precipitous N.E. arm of this forms 
the western or lower of the two "Noses," as they are called 
("Untere," u. "Obere Nase"), the eastern or upper one 
being the portion of the Rigi between Vitznau and Oersau, 
across which again there is no road. In Stanz you turn 
down to your left-half, opposite the fountain — ^the con- 
tinuation road is that to Engelberg. You will probably 
dismount at Stanz to see the Winkelried Monument 
and the Church— otherwise there is no need to get 
off, for the pavement is excellent, a very model of what 
street paving ought to be. I only wish our English paviors 
and street architects could be sent there to study it. As a 
general rule the paved streets of Swiss towns are very 
superior to those at home, and can be ridden with compara- 
tive comfort in at least two cases out of three, though some- 
times, as at Rheinfelden for instance (see § 1), they are very 
bad indeed. At Beckenried your riding on this side of the 
lake ends, and you must transfer yourself to some other 
point by steamer. The road does indeed lead further on 
up to the Seelisberg, through the village of Emmetten, and 
past the little Seelisberg Lake, but it is a continuous ascent 
of 1350 feet. I have not been along it recently, but my 
pedestrian recollections of it years ago are those of a rough 
mountain track, quit unfit for bicycles. But it is possible, 
now that the Seelisberg and its big hotel have become such 
a fashionable resort, that it has developed into something 
better ; in any case there is a proper carriage-road, though 
steep and zigzagging, from the Seelisberg down to the lake 
again at Treib. But the gradient of this descent, as far as 
I can estimate it from the map, must be an average of 1 in 
13, which probably means 1 in 10 in places, so that it would 
require to be taken " gingerly." Oerabd F. Cobb. 

Thunerhof, Thun, August, 1882.

SMOKING CONCERT. 

If we may judge from the large attendance at the City 
Arms "Tavern on March 15th, and from the enthusiasm 
evoked by the various items on the programme, there can 
be no doubt about the success of the first and last official 
Smoking Concert of the past winter, and the result was 
very gratifying to the Sub-Committee instructed to pro- 
mote it. In comparing it with similar entertainments 
given in the past at short intervals, it shows, we think, the 
advantage of not repeating them too frequently, and to 
give more than two during the winter, is in our opinion 
" killing the goose." 

The company was not only more numerous, but more 
representative than usual of the various divisions of the 
Club. University and country members, heroes of the 
path and the Bath road, record-holders and potterers, 
active and lazy, met to take advantage of the free and eas^ 
character of the proceedings. A little too much licence 
was taken in this way, and less noise would certainly have 
been more in harmony with some of the songs. 

Owing to the late arrival of some of the performers, and 
to the unfortunate illness of G. E. Schmettau, which 
necessitated the abandonment of two trios in which he was 
to have taken part, the first half of the programme under- 
went, as the Captain pertinently put it, ''some slight 
variations;" but at half-past seven Mr, C. P. Little 
opened the concert with a pianoforte solo, " Norwegian 
Bridal March," W. A. Smith following with a song, "Jessie, 
the flower of Dumblane," which was given in his usual 
excellent style. The quintette, " Crown diamonds," was 
then efiFectively played, after which C. Y. Lyne and P. N. 
Large were warmly applauded for the duet, The moon 
hath hid her light." Mr. C. P. Little succeeded in 
convulsing the audience by the humour with which he 
invested "The young man who went out to a party," and the 
room was not quieted until he had promised to sing again 
later on. Successes were also gained by the song, " Three 
merry men," by R. Newman, and the scherzo for violin and 
viola. C. Y. Lyne was quite himself in " I'm not myself at 
all," and Mr. H. Dutton again delighted his hearers with 
his sympathetic rendering of "Oft in the stilly night." 
At this point a break was made, and a very good one it 
proved, an honorary member, E. T. Sachs, giving a very 
clever conjuring performance ; after exhibiting some won- 
derful card tricks, he produced florins from every impossible 
place with such skill that those who were sitting right 
under him were quite as puzzled as the rest. The applause 
when the performance was finished was so great that in 
order to silence it the conjuror was obliged to respond by 
showing the handkerchief-knot trick, which was done so 
skilfully as to baffle discovery. ^ 

The second part of the programme was then commenced 
with the duet, "Friendship," by the N.E. Inseparables, 
and after G. N. Latham had sung " Awful little scrub," 



Mr. H. Dutton sang " The meeting of the waters " with 
rare feeling. A valse by Labitzlcy was also well rendered 
by the Quintette. Loud applause greeted M. D. Rucker 
after he had sung " To Anthea,** and Mr. J. D. Butler in 
" Medje," and Mr. C. P. Little in " The Chancellor's Song," 
from lolanthe, both gained well-merited approval, as also 
did F. N. Large in the song, " My little sweetheart," and 
W. A. Smith^ in " Tell her I love her so," M. D. Rucker 
bringing a most enjoyable evening to a spirited ending by 
a recitation, in which he introduced a most astounding fall, 
only to be compared with a similar effort by the same 
performer between Upminster and Purfleet. 

The room was crowded throughout, and the festive bowl 
circulated freely. Some slight difficulties were experienced 
with the ventilation, owing to the impossibility of satisfying 
at the same time the men sitting under the windows and 
the men on the other side of the room. Several serious 
illnesses were threatened, but we have not heard of their 
fulfilment.

W. District. 

(Jteceived too late for iruertion vnth other Club Runt. J 

We had a very fair attendance at the opening run on 
Saturday tiie 17 th March, to Sunbury. Taking the wet 
afternoon into consideration, for only one or two escaped 
with dry coats on the outward journey, and none who 
attempted to return at night escaped the drenching rain 
that came down so persistently, nine West, one S.W., one 
N.W., and three S.E., making a total of fourteen, spent a 
very pleasant evening at the " Magpie ; several members 
varying tlie entertainment with the aid of cards, billiards, etc. 

I would ask all who can, to attend as many runs as possible 
during the coming season, and to give as much of their 
support to the District as possible, and, by so doing, endea- 
vour to raise our attendance from the state of lethargy into 
which it appears to have fallen. The runs chosen for the 
coming Saturdays are pleasant and various, and, given fine 
weather, nothing further should be wanting as an induce- 
ment to all members to support them. 

Hoping to receive that support which I confidently hope 
for during the coming season, — Believe me. Yours faithfully, 
Harolo C. Pigott, District Captain,

OUR FRANCO-SWISS TOUR OF 1882. 
{Continued from p, 26.) 

The first of October was the finest day this part of the 
country had seen for a month. L., W., and B., (for 
unfortunately M. was a little out of sorts), in company 
with a gentleman staying at the hotel, had a splendid walk 
to the top of the Wengem Alp. Our path skirting the 
base of the Eiger, we saw many fine avalanches. For some 
distance we had to wade through plenty of snow, which on 
approaching the summit (6,224 feet) was some three feet 
deep. This was quite a new experience for us, and we had 
plenty of fun laughing at each other's misfortunes, for, the 
ground being uneven beneath its treacherous covering, our 
falls were frequent and often ludicrous, sometimes both 
legs and an arm disappearing from view. 

The last piece to the inn was a succession of flounders, 
and when we at last reached the summit, tired and panting 
with the novel exertion, we found that our progress had 
been watched with more interest than we could have 
imagined. An American gentleman his two daughters 
and little boy, (whom we had met two days back at Lungern), 
had ridden up from Lauterbrunnen (the snow being slight 
on that side), and having discharged their horses were 
about to commence the descent to Grindelwald on foot 
when they caught sight of us struggling up, the bad weather 
we were making taking them rather aback. Our opinion 
on the matter being asked, we could not, considering the 
many undignified attitudes we had involuntarily struck, 
conscientiously recommend the ladies to try the descent, 
although of course we tendered our assistance should they 
make the attempt. After considering the state of affairs 
for some little while they made up their mind to stay the 
night at the inn, trusting to the off chance of the surface 
of the snow freezing sufficiently hard during the night to 
bear them. We may add that they arrived at our hotel 
the following day, but from the reticence of the girls on 
the subject, and the heap of clothes to be dried, we guessed 
that the frost was a delusion. 

The view from the Wengem Alp is one not likely to fade 
from one's memory in a hurry. The tremendous mass of 
the Juug&au, covered with spotless snow, stands out 
against the clear blue sky, while the Monch, the Silberhorn, 
and her other companions complete the beauty of the scene. 
The glories of the scene reminded us of a similar day three 
years ago, when we scrambled up to the lofty Porte de 
Venasque and gazed on the Maladetta, the highest of the 
Pyrenees. 

The following morning iu most auspicious weather we 
started, accompanied by a guide, fully equipped for a day 
on the Lower Glacier. Zigzagging up the mouth of the 
gorge for some little distance, we presently came to the 
commencement of the glacier and reached its surface by 
swarming up a moderately long ladder. In consequence of 
the large quantity of firesh snow the avalanches rendered 



Digitized by 



Google 



38 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



the usual route skirting the base of the Mettenberg unsafe, 
and necessitated our proceeding by a new track, which the 
guide had to pilot out for us. At first, the ice being very much 
broken, we got on but slowly, as step-cutting is rather 
tedious work, but after a time the travelling was decidedly 
easier, and we made good progress. A little farther on a 
big avalanche, about the size of an elongated Hyde Park 
more or less, blocked the way, and had to be scrambled 
over. It was our intention to have pushed on to the 
Chalet of Zasenberg, ** the last human habitation amongst 
the giants of the Bernese Alps," but by the time we had 
penetrated as far as the centre of the Mer de Glace the 
crevasses were so completely hidden by the deep snow that 
our guide did not think it prudent to proceed farther. 
Lunch seemed the most appropriate thing to do next ; so 
the basket was unpacked, the wine immersed in the snow, 
and, thanks to the care of our thoughtful hosts at the Bar, 
we were soon busy with a capital meal Having lightened 
the basket, we linger awhile contemplating the wild waste 
of snow and ice, hemmed in without visible outlet by 
majestic mountains. It is a gi*and sight, rendered more 
impressive by the extreme solitude in which we seemed to 
be the only living creatures. Nature can hardly be quieter — 
except when disturbed now and again by the dull roar of 
an avalanche — even at the North Pola We returned by a 
lofty path along the cliffs of the Mettenberg. All of us 
were greatly interested with our day's experiences, and, with 
the exception of W., much enjoyed the fun of scrambling 
and sliding about. He, who is more at home on his cycle 
than swarming up slippery slopes and crazy ladders, says 
that you'll never catch me on a glacier again ; I'd rather 
ride another Bath Race." His verdict may be altered 
perhaps by this time. 

What is more wretched than a thoroughly wet day away 
from home? In your own house you can always find 
plenty of occupation, but in a small village three thousand 
five hundred feet above the sea, enveloped all day in dense 
cloud, what is to be done ? If you do brave the elements 
and go out, what is the profit ? The view of clouds and 
mist can be seen to just as much advantage from the hotel 
door. Our hosts, ever on the alert to make us comfortable, 
seeing that the time hung heavily with us and that we 
were likely to smoke ourselves silly for want of other 
employment, fetched us out a rook rifle, and, a target being 
quickly improvised in front of the porch, we had some 
capital fun. This, with sundry small excursions to the 
village, beneath the big carriage umbrella that covered the 
four of us, to watch the smith — a clever young fellow — ^fit 
a new spoon to W.'s brake, which had worn through, helped 
to pass the time away. Indoors we condoled with our 
American friends on the miserable state of affairs, and 
derived a good deal of amusement from some of their 
remarks. Asking them as to their intended movements, 
we had : 1 guess if it gits fine we'll take a run up the 



Glazier" followed by, Wal, I don't care much if it don't; 
we're goin' on to Ghamouny, and I reckon there are plenty 
of Glaziers there ! " A. E. B.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 

The B.U. Council Meeting resulted in the usual amount 
of talk, though a very decisive rebuke was administered to 
one " orator " in his place among the rejected candidates 
for the Executive, while another only found himself eleventh 
out of the sixteen elected. S. P. Smith and H. R. Reynolds, 
of the L.B.C., were re-elected on the Executive ; T. E. 
Scrutton resigned his office of Treasurer and his seal on the 
Executive. 

• ••••• 

The election of President was marked by some very 
childish opposition, happily coming frt)m very junior mem- 
bers of the Union. W. A. Smith was proposed in opposition 
to Mr. 6. E. Cobb, the founder of the Union, and his pro- 
poser conveyed to the meeting the impression that 
McCullum had been consulted and consented to stand. It 
is only fair to say that our well-known member had dis- 
tinctly refused to allow himself to be nominated for the 
post. 

• # • • ' # • 

The Birmingham Championship has been definitely fixed 
for the 7th July. There is some doubt as to the Cambrulge 
fixture, which may, I hear, be transferred to Oxford or 
London. 

• ••••• 

The Pickwick B.C. hold their race meeting on June 9th, 
at the Crystal Palace. Their Invitation Race is to be a 
Half-mile Tricycle Race. I should imagine it is about as 
unfair and dangerous a distance as could be selected for that 
course. 

• ••••• 

One of the recent B.T.C. candidate writes that he is 69 
years of age, and has ridden 53,111 miles — 35,493 on 
bicycles and 17,618 on tricycles — without an accident, 
and without having caused an accident. Can any of our 
members beat this ? 

There is some suggestion of the B.U's. changing its name, 
to keep pa^ with the C.T.C. (late B.T.C.). 

• • • • • • * 

H. 6. A. Brown, of ours, was appointed one of the Auditors 
of the B.U. for last year's accounts. The Treasurer is 
quaking. 

• • • • • 

E. R. Shipton, one of our members, has become Secretary 
of the 'Cyclists' Touring Club, a financial arrangement 
having been made which enables him to devote all his time 
to the Club. He has also been elected a member of the 
B.U. Executive. The Fbont Whbbl Hub.

DRAMATIC SOCIETY. 
To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sm, — The following details may perhaps be of interest 
to members : — ^Financially our performance was a fair 
success, there being a balance of about ten guineas to 
the credit of the Society. 

The following papera were represented at our per- 
formance : — The Bicycling Times, Tricyclist, Bicycling 
News, B.T,C. Gazette, Cyclist, Chronicle, Daily News, 
Era, Illustrated Sporting and Dramatic News, and The 
Referee, 

Two stall tickets each were given to the Oxford and 
Cambridge Universities, the Belsize, Druids, Pickwick, 
Stanley, Surrey, Wanderers, and the West Kent Clubs, and 
the majority, on acknowledging the same, cordially congra- 
tulated us on our success. 

About 600 tickets were taken at the door, two-thirds of 
which were stalls. Forty-one members subscribed to the 
Society this year. — ^Tours truly, Alfred Barker. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
Sir, — While yielding to no one in a desire to encourage 
the accession of tricyclists to our ranks, I can scarcely 
believe that we shall succeed to any extent in so doing. 
Numerous influential Tricycle Clubs are now established in 
the metropolis : it is only reasonable to conclude that riders 
of the three-wheeler, the great majority of whom are of a 
different stamp to bicyclists, will join those clubs exclu- 
sively devoted to their own form of cycling, in which they 
would be associated with companions of their own calibre. 
As for any alteration in the established rules and regulations 
affecting our Bath Trial, with a view to a similar one for 
tricyclists, there appears to be a very general feeling in the 
Club that such a move would be, to say the least of it, 
premature, and, as far as I can learn, unpopular. Excluding 
two or three old members who (temporarily, let us hope) 
have exchanged the bicycle for the tricycle, we have no 
new members who are exclusively tricyclists ; surely it will 
be quite time enough to adopt fresh departures for the 
Bath Trial when we find that we have amongst us a number 
of tricyclists of the stamp likely to enter for such an ordeal. 
I forbear discusung the question of the twelve hours' time 
limit proposed, but I think it is pretty plain that the 
100 miles on the Bath road should be easily accomplished 
under that time on some of the fast machines now turned 
out — I am, sir, yours faithfully, ^ "Brbpkbt." 

March 11th, 1883. 
[Note. — We shall be glad to hear from some of our tri- 
cycling members on this subject. — Ed.] 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir, — In September last I ventured to call the attention 
of the Qub to the liberality of those in authority in giving 



away more gold medals every year than the rules of the 
Club, as printed in the Blue Book, permit. Now though, 
by strictly interpreting the rules, we should most probably 
have to reduce die number of medals, I do not believe that 
the framers of the existing laws ever intended that 1^ than 
ten medals should be given each year to non-medallists 
except in the case of the " Enamel " being won by an old 
hand ; and I really think at the Special General Meeting, 
which is to be called before long, the Club should be asked 
to alter the phraseology of the Bath-day regulations so as 
to legalise the action of the officers in past years, and allow 
the spirit of the present law to coincide with the letter. — 
Yours, Red Tape.

The experience of the laat three years shows that the 
season should open with a General Meet at Blackheath. 
Last Saturday, for the third consecutive time, the Blackheath 
Division went to the opening run, leaving fine weather 
behind them, and found rain and mud awaiting them six 
miles off ; on their return they found, as in 1881 and 1882, 
that no rain had fallen in their absence. 

Probably few S.E. men were aware on Saturday that the 



" White Hart," Orpington, is the favourite summer haunt 
of writers engaged in preparing pantomimes for the lower 
class of London theatres; this is however the fact, and 
amply accounts for the circumstance that the chops, eggs, 
and marmalade made their " entries" by trap-doors in the 
floor. The waiter had also imbibed the spirit of the place 

in more sense )}han one.

THE DISTRICT CAPTAIN. 
Lasfc week we spoke of Club runs, and this week we 
propose to refer to a prominent feature of Club runs — the 
District Captain. We have heard it said that ** a conscien- 
tious District Captain has as hard work as the Hon. Sec./' 
but it must be added that the originator of this remark had 
been a D.C. but never an Hon. Sec, and persons more 
likely to judge of both positions fairly are sceptical on the 
point. There is no doubt, however, that if a D.C. does 
seriously set himself to make his District successful he has 
not an easy time of it. We ventured to say last week that 
the healthy life of the Club was dependent upon the Club 
runs ; we may almost add that the healthy life of Club 
runs depends on the District Captain. We say this without 
any wish to unduly magnify the office, but rather with a 
desire to impress upon the D.C.'s the very serious respon- 
sibility which rest43 upon them. First and foremost among 
their duties is regular attendance at meets, in which we 
may fairly say that past D.C.'s have been well up to the 
mark, as last year the attendance medal was taken by the 
D.C. in every District but one, and in that he was only one 
point behind the medallist. Then it is always necessary to 



INTS. 

PAOB 

AU Round the Wheel 46 

Correspondence 46 

Quills 47 

Racing Fixtures 48 

Exchange List 48 

Gleanings from a Club Tea Table 48 

A Novel Spin on the Goodwin Sands on a Bicycle 48 

Notice to Correspondents 48 

keep up the interest of members if possible by some change 
in destination or in the routine of Club runs, paperchases 
and picnics being fair samples of the method of arriving at 
this latter result. The means adopted to induce attendance 
in diflferent Districts will naturally vary with circumstances. 
One man may effect wonders with postcards, while another 
may find personal applications possible and more effective, 
but we fancy the time has gone by when a D.C. can sit 
still and expect his District to flock to meets without any 
encouragements. 

It is in the actual conduct and pace of runs, however, 
that his responsibility is most apparent. A D.C. may 
increase or diminish the attendance more than is some- 
times imagined by too wild a rush, which disheartens the 
beginner, or by too funereal a crawl, which disgusts the 
flyer, and unless he can duly regulate his District he may 
as well give up the attempt to make his year of office 
successful. So far District Captains may perhaps think we 
are directing our remarks rather pointedly to thera, but in 
conclusion we have this to say to the rest of the members, 
if the duties of the D.C. are difficult, it rests with you to 
lighten them. The ideal D.C. would only have to ride at 
the head of a District and preside at the frugal tea at its 
termination, spending the rest of his days in peace and 
quietness. Let all members, therefore, endeavour to ob- 
tain for the District Captain a near approach to this ideal, 
and we venture to promise that they will themselves reap 
the benefits of their efforts.

SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
31st March. This district did not manage so good a 
muster to meet the N.W. as might reasonably have been 
expected from the fine weather and pleasant destination. 
The Captain and two other members left the Corner with 
the D.C., and proceeded by Higham Hill to Chingford, the 
Captain and Lloyd negotiating the hill up to the church. 
The Editor joined us at this point ; and in the fruitless 
effort to avoid stones the District made a long circuit by 
the " Forest Hotel " and High Beech and so to Waltham. 
The Treasurer, who had come by the direct route, joined 
the District at Waltham Abbey, but the Editor had left 
just before, so we were only five strong going into Cheshunt. 
However, three more were waiting at the " Green Dragon," 
and we were hardly off our machines when the N.W. rode 
up. Tea was as usual good and cheap, if simple, and the 
attendants very attentive. It was just dark as the District 
started home, Lyne and Stunt returning chiefly with the 
N.W., and the main body making their way over much 
loose stones via the direct route between Waltham and 
Chingford, meeting S. G. Hough near the former town, and 
reaching Walthamstow about ten o'clock. Present : Ashley 
Barrett (Captain), R. B. Hough, S. G. Hough, A. A. Kelsey, 
Lloyd, Lyne, T. E. Scrutton, E. D. Sewell, G. N. Stunt, 
Weeks, and Thbo. Godlee, District Captain. 



S.W. District. 

The sentiments expressed in the leader in our last issue, 
entitled "The Natural History of Club Runs," we trust 
will always apply to the S.W. District, and we must 
earnestly hope that our^oung athletes, in the construction 
and mural decoration of any " airy castles " they may con- 
ceive, will not lose sight of the great duty they owe to 
their Club and neglect its Saturday Runs. 

Without any fear of causing 'disappointment we can, 
doubtless, safely promise to visitors to the S.W. a better 
and more tempting spread than the melancholy indication 
of an indigestible skeleton Club tea contained in the in- 
teresting article above referred to. The district force 



mustered at Kingston Station three strong, and after 
allowing ample time for any possible addition to the ranks, 
but without securing any welcome reinforcements, the com- 
pany commenced its circular run. For the convenience of 
any members or visitors who may arrive late at Eangstou, 
I propose to leave a card upon the mantelpiece opx>08ite the 
booking office at that station, containing the latest imfor- 
mation for tardy members, as to our numbers, direction, 
and place for tea, so that if by any chance the destination 
of the district were to be modified or altered by the wish of 
a majority at the last moment, others desiring to join us 
would, by this arrangement, be enabled and perhaps 
encouraged to do so. 

It is a melancholy fact that this short circular and 
attractive run had charms for only three members, but this 
was all that could be recorded when we took to the sunny 
lanes at Surbiton, and pursued our wa3rward course through 
Claygate, Ockshott, Stoke d'Abemon, and Church Cobham 
to Cobham proper, where we stayed for tea at the " White 
Lion." The surfaces of the lanes we traversed were variable, 
and the combined mud and rough roads afforded us oppor- 
tunities for healthy exercise. The frugal repast at Cobham 
was below our usual average, except in the matter of 
charges. An hour and a half was devoted to amusement 
here, which included much of the fragrant weed, and after 
this period — ^with only two lamps to do duty for three — we 
took to the main Portsmouth road, and reached Kingston 
about 8.30 p.m. Distance 20 miles. Members present : 
A. L. Gruggen, H. L. Ryan, and 

R. M. Gbuogen, District Captain. 

Next Saturday we run to Ripley to meet the West at 
the "Anchor" — that favourite reiidezvous for all the wheel 
world — and I earnestly hope that all our members will 
make a point of being present themselves and use their 
influence to induce others to follow their example. 



S.E. DiSTBICT. 

The last day of March was quite a butterfly day, but 
there were several butterflies of the two wheeled order who 
refused to be enticed out by the sunshine which graced our 



Digitized by 



Uoogle 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



48 



second meet. Not that energy was wanting in the South 
lEast, but the energy was localized, and confined to a few. 
The O'Reilly rode up from Brighton ; Douglas Smith started 
from Oxford, and turned up at the Fire Station to the 
minute, looking as fresh as paint and as hard as nails ; 
and J. S. Whatton came down by road with the latest news 
of the doings of racing men at Stamford Bridge. The ride 
out was conspicuous by the entire absence of the conven- 
tional, men dropping in in dribblets of twos and threes. 
Altogether seventeen members and three visitors sat down 
to tea at the " Clayton Arms," nor was the traditional veal 
absent from the festive board ; in fact it may be looked on 
as quite a genius loci, second only to the time honoured 
ghost. Songs and recitations followed, in which H. 6. A. 
Brown, L. Potter, J. G. Oswald, Schmettau, and Stephen 
Smith were to the fore. The complete harmony of the 
scene was only marred by the absence of not a few familiar 
faces who have come to be associated with this, one of the 
favourite meets of the year. At about half-past eight the 
party broke up, and Croydon was reached without mis- 
adventure. Present : A. Barker, Q. F. Beck, C. A. Brown, 
H. G. A. Brown, R G. Francis, J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, 
A. L. Potter, J. F.. Potter, L. Potter, The O'KeiUy, C. F. 
Schmettau, H. N. Sharp, D. R Smith, S. P. Smith, J. S. 
Whatton (W.), S. E Whealler, and three visitors. 

The next run is to Wrotham, in the Blackbeath country. 
That division will, it is hoped, show their appreciation by 
turning up in force. The Croydon Division will start 
at 4 punctually, and meet them at Famingham at 5.15. 
Wrotham is also easily accessible to the Sevenoaks con- 
tingent. We shall have tea at the " Bull," one of the best 
Inns in one of the most picturesque villages in Kent. 

R. G. Fbahcis, District Captain. 



W. DlSTRIOT. 

March 81st. Remembering the fact that the average 
attendance of this District at the 24 meets last season was 
6.37, some may have been disposed to think that the D.C.'s 
remarks about a " state of lethargy " in the last number of 
the Gazette, were rather uncalled for. But the fact that 
on a fine afternoon, with dry roads, and a short run, only 
one member appeared to ki&p the D.C. company, seems 
quite to justify the statement. Perhaps the run was too 
short, and those to Chenies and Leith Hill will show a better 
attendance. Let us hope so. 

The Western members got to Harrow about 5.30, and had 
just made enquiries about tea, when Alison appeared from 
the opposite direction. The view from the churchyard was 
beautifully clear. The "King's Head " gave us a capital two 
shilling meat tea, and a very pleasant evening was spent 
afterwards in company with some musical members of the 
Chiswick Club. Altogether it was voted a very enjoyable 
little run. Present : J. W. Alison, J. W. Langmore, and 
H. C. PiQOTT, District Captain.

THE LATE CHARLES TAYLOR. 

I cannot let the sad news of Charles Taylor's death go by 
without writing a few lines — which I feel sure will be 
thoroughly endorsed by all who knew him — as to his many 
admirable qualities. 

Mr. Charles Taylor joined the L.B.C. when it was auite 
in its infancy, and continued until his lamented deatn to 
be one of the few who, from such an early period as 1875, 
took a lively and thorough interest in all its doings. He 
was a firm supporter of the S.W. Division, and his genial 
face was well Known in his Division. So many Club Runs 
had he attended, that at last a S.W..Run was not looked 
upon as complete without the pleasant jovial face of 
Cnarles Taylor. 

I have had the pleasure of two short tours in his company, 
and never in all my 'cycling experience have I come across 
a more genuine, opeuhearted, and unselfish companion ; 
and now that he has departed this life I feel, with his other 
companions, that we have lost a friend of no mean value, 
and one whose like we may not often meet again. 

W. T. Hill. 

OUR FRANCO-SWISS TOUR OP 1882. 
(Continued from p. 38, and concluded.) 
Early the next morning M. and B. were off to the Paul- 
horn. W.'s feet having given way a bit with the two heavy 
days' walking, it was not prudent for him to attempt 
another stiff day, so he and L. stayed behind. Leaving 
the valley close by the B&r we soon commenced the 
ascent in earnest. Our guide, the same that we had 
on the glacier, seemed determined to test our abilities 
to the utmost, for, scornfully disregarding the paths, 
he marched us straight away up the side of the moun- 
tain. We covered the first 2000 feet in the hour, and 
at this rate of progression were soon amongst the snow. 
In places it was frozen hard and the going was capital, but 
as we neared the top it was not only soft but deep (about 
five feet) as well, and we plunged about a good deal. 
Arriving at the summit (8803 feet) there were too many 
clouds about to admit of our seeing much of the splendid 
panorama with which we ought to have been rewar^ied, but 
we saw quite enough to make us wish for more. After 
hoisting the L.B.C. colours, lunch was the order of the day, 
and luckily we had brought it with us, for the inn was 
deserted, only an outbuilding being left open for the shelter 
of travellers. It was freezing hard, and it was only by 
keeping on the move that we were able to maintain the 
circulation. In these mountain expeditions we derived 
great benefit and instruction from an aneroid B. had 



Digitized by 



44 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



brought with him. Several times during the earlier and 
wetter stages of our trip we thought seriously of heaving 
this "Jonah" overboard, but could not quite agree whether 
it was the barometer or the sun-hats that attracted the 
rain, so both were spared, and in the end both paid for the 
trouble of transport. Our downward route was varied by 
striking towards the Great Scheideck. This change gave 
us the full benefit of the snow. Where the surface was 
frozen hard we got along merrily, glissading down the 
steeper slopes like express trains ; but for a greater portion 
of the way the upper crust was so fragile that it let us 
through at nearly every step, rasping our shins sweetly in 
the process. Presently we swung down an almost perpen- 
dicular piece of greasy turf, and passing close by the fine 
Muhlbach Fall after a while rejoined the valley. W. and 
L. had been having a good time of it. After a turn at 
practice with a chamois rifle of the Swiss military type 
they joined a shooting party for the afternoon, but did not 
meet with any very marked success^ although seven guns 
and two dogs were on the war path. 

The time had come for us to commence our homeward 
journey, so the next morning we got out our neglected 
cycles, and prepared ourselves to depart from the moun- 
tains amongst which we had spent so many happy hours. 
An ancient writer says, " Call no man happy until he is 
dead we say, after a fair share of experience, " Consider 
no hotel satisfactory until the bill comes." The " Hotel 
de rOurs " stood even this test most satisfactorily. Our 
comfort had been studied in every way, and the charges 
were most moderate. After cordial " good byes " from our 
obliging hosts (the brothers Boss), we commenced the 12^ 
miles descent to Interlaken. Little did we think that, as 
we reluctantly took our last look at Orindelwald, before a 
month had elapsed this pleasant little village would be 
devastated by a hurricane the like of which fortunately is 
not often seen in Europe. 

From Interlaken we ran close alongside the Lake of Thun, 
a pretty ride over the best piece of road we had seen in 
Switzerland. We spent some little time in Thun examining 
the quaint streets and shops, getting photos, etc., and after 
lunch went on to Berne. There is always a feeling of 
melancholy as the mountains are one by one dropped 
behind, and we experienced the truth of the lines— ^ 

" Ab when the traveUer, with reluctant feet, 
Prom Alpine Bummits pasaeg to the plain, 
Turns and looks upward from the vale, to greet 
The Eiger or the Jungfrau once again, 
Towering above the snow-capped mountain chain. 
So turn I lovingly 

An uneventful ride brought us to the top of the descent 
into Berne (47^). Prom this spot the view of the city is 
most striking. Before entering the town we had a look at 
the famous bears. They look jolly enough, and so they 
ought to, considering the comfortable annuities they enjoy. 
Walking over the pave to the hotel (Schweizerhof), a dog 



took an affection to the doctor's calves. One subtle 
insinuation of his toe beneath the brute's nether jaw and 
the animal, turning a somersault in the air, landed on 
his back in the middle of the stream that runs down the 
main street ; (exit dog). 

Friday saw the division of our party. Morris, our guide, 
philosopher, friend, and banker, (leaving the rest of us to 
push on to Neuch^tel to complete our Swiss round), having 
time to spare returned to Orindelwald, where he had a very 
jolly time of it climbing, walking, and chamois-hunting ; 
the only drawback being that unfortunately, by the up- 
setting of a dogcart into the river, he lost his^multum the 
contents of his portmanteau and his overcoat — a catastrophe 
which at one fell swoop reduced his wearing apparel to the 
clothes he stood up in. We, the survivors, riding by a 
cross country route, reached Neuch&tel safely, (our friend 
the "tyke," whose house we had to pass, not being at 
home), and left by the afternoon train for Paris. Proceeding 
to Dieppe, we crossed by the day boat and landed at 
Newhaven between nine and ten p.m. W. went up to 
town by the boat train, leaving the doctor and B. to enjoy 
a much needed night's repose, and to ride home the 
next day. 

Our little story is ended, and there only remains to add 
a few words for the benefit of those readers who have not 
as yet bicycled on the other side of the silver streak. In 
England the touring cyclist meets with but scant civility. 
He often deems himself lucky if a native hurls at him a 
hard word or a coarse jest instead of a half brick. The 
hotel landlord loves him not ; his cycle is consigned to the 
sweetly smelling stable, and himself very often to the attic. 
But cross the channel — how differently we are treated. 
The labourer leaves off breaking his stones to raise his hat ; 
the street boy grins and wishes bon voyage; the sentinel 
presents arms ; the officer salutes the uniform ; the village 
priest politely bows ; whilst the hotel proprietor, all smUes, 
stands our cycles in the hall, or perhaps in a spare salle ct 
manger, and produces the best of everything for the 
"gentlemen with the velocipedes." If, then, you like the 
description^ go next summer and try for yourself. 

A.E. B. 



THE EASTER RUN FROM BATH. 
In spite of the biting N.E. wind, the party found by the 
Captain at the "White Lion" on Sunday evening was 
larger than is usually collected there at Easter. There 
were H. 0. A. Brown and Do.uglas Smith, who had ridden 
down by easy stages ; A. S. Morris and Hewitt, who had 
been riding in company for the two previous days ; H. L. 
Ryan, who had ridden down alone; while Charles Newman, 
Barker, and Walter Meyer had accompanied the Captain 
in a circuitous archaeological ramble. Of the doings of this 
last party prior to its arrival at Bath something may be 
said. Its rendezvous on the evening of Good Friday was 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



45 



Winchester, and there were to be found about 10 p.m., 
fighting their battles o'er attain in the smoking room of the 
" George " hotel, John Kinder, Fred Williams, Barker, Chas. 
Newman, Meyer, and the Captain. The first three men 
had ridden straight down before the wind, through Alton, 
and talked much of mighty flies down never-ending hills ; 
Meyer had travelled alone for the most part, while Newman 
and the other man had run through Silchester and Basing- 
stoke. At the former place they had visited the ruins of 
Calleva, the lately explored Boman city on the estate of 
the Duke of Wellington, but the terribly cold wind pre- 
vented them from staying long in its deserted streets. The 
recently exhumed tesselated pavements are now very per- 
fect, but it is to be feared that the weather and the earth- 
worms will ere long serve to break them up. The bronze 
and iron implements exhibited in the small museum are few 
in number and of no very great interest, but probably the 
best specimens are removed and shown elsewhere. A handy 
guide-book to the place might also with advantage be placed 
at the disposal of visitors — ^the more so since at present the 
only source of information is a resident rustic of apoplectic 
appearance and intemperate habits. On the following day, 
Saturday, a short run only was made, from Winchester to 
Salisbury; so short, indeed, was it, that the two Flyers were 
compelled to expend their exuberant energy in walking till 
midnight around the Cathedral city. The two married men 
trained home, greatly to their companions' regret, leaving 
Meyer and the Captain to attend evening service and the 
Turkish Baths. On the following day Bath was reached, 
after riding through Amesbury and across the Plain. The 
ruins of Old Sarum Castle were scaled, and mighty steep 
they were. This is now a table mountain ; but before 
reaching its top a deep fosse has to be crossed. Unfortu- 
nately the hill is surrounded by arable land, and the 
suspicious inhabitants guard it so jealously that any 
assault, to be successful, must be planned and executed 
with much vigour and skill. Unaware of our danger, we 
were descending carelessly and in happy ignorance of our 
peril, when our right front was attacked by a formidable 
person with a large stick, who was afterwards discovered 
to be an uncle of two small boys that had guided us across 
his fields. The latter were rewarded with sixpence for so 
doing, while their angry relative was subsequently appeased 
with beer at a neighbouring inn. Before reaching the 
bottom of the lower slope our flank was turned and retreat 
almost cut off by more agriculturists. A bold stand, how- 
ever, was made, and Amesbury at length was reached with 
thankful hearts. There can be no doubt that, though 
Salisbury may be dull, its surrounding inhabitants are 
most truculent and far from simple. Future visitors to its 
old castle had best make their explorations under cover of 
darkness. This at least is the opinion of one of our party 
not unversed in the wily strategies of savage warfare. Over 
the Plain the air was keen, cold, and clear, as it always is. 



Stonehenge was sighted, but not visited, though Tambury 
Castle was well explored. Then a hare was coursed, but 
unsuccessfully, and Bath at length reached in good time 
for dinner and a subsequent stroll. On Monday morning 
a start was effected at eight o'clock, but unfortunately 
without Walter Meyer, who had scalded his foot rather 
severely by an unfortunate accident. The oolite was 
sticky and so greasy that in Denizes Morris fell and had 
to train home. The party soon tailed out, as sauve 
qui pent seems always the motto on the via sacra. 
Smith and Hewitt about Silbury Mound disappeared, 
though, as the tombstones say, they were not lost, but 
gone before, since at Reading Hewitt was reported to be 
"still plugging Douglas Smith." Later accounts from 
Oxford relate that the latter reached Maidenhead in 
7 hr. 5 min., and then rode on to Oxford (1 14 miles), the 
latter part against what was described as a "roaring 
tempest." The others reached home safely by various 
means and at various times, Newman riding all the way up 
with that profound contempt for the train that always 
characterises him, others of weaker moral fibre not dis- 
daining its aid ; and one there was, since this narrative is 
nothing if not truthful, who scorned not to ride therein 
from Keading to Paddington. The name of this degenerate 
shall never be revealed by the veracious, and, since his 
Easter holiday, voracious historian of this Easter run to 
Bath. A. W. B. 

EASTER RUNS. 

On Easter Monday, A. Lloyd started from Walthamstow 
at 3 a.m., and rode through Colchester and Ipswich 'to 
Stratford St. Andrew, Suffolk (85). Returning home by 
the same road he was caught in a snowstorm near Chelmsford, 
but continued till the gathering darkness, and the depth of 
the snow, obliged him to seek refuge in a wayside inn. 
Distance ridden, 147 miles. 



R. B. and S. G. Hough started from Eew Bridge at 8 p.m. 
on Thursday, with E. D. Sewell, and were blown down with 
the dust to Reading, where they stayed the night. Next 
day, B. D. Sewell left them, and they proceeded to Bath. 
On Saturday they rode over bad roads to Warminster, 
thence good to Salisbury, where they met Price and Lown, 
with whom they went on to Bournemouth, meeting 
E. D. Sewell and a friend. Stopped there over the Sunday, 
riding home with Sewell and friend through Southampton 
and Basingstoke on the Monday. Total Distance, 286 miles. 

Oliver Thorn rode to Newbury on Friday, and next day 
on to Hungerford, back to Newbury, then to Shaw, Hamp- 
stead Norris, Goring Bridge and Heath, Reading, Maiden- 
head, and home. Roads in splendid condition^ and most 
pleasantly varied country.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
The racing season has £et in with its usual severity. 
Bank Holiday witnessed a large number of race meetings, 

and the official handicapper had his hands full. 

• ••••• 

Last year's scratch men were not very successful At 
Coventry, Lowndes could not beat 3 min. 5 sees, in his 
heat, the final being won by Auster of the Speedwell with 

twenty-five yards start in 2 min. 58^ sees. 

• ••«•• 

At Leicester, Moore, the One and Twenty-five Mile 
Ghampion, was second in the three miles, taking over ten 
minutes to cover the distance ; and at Brighton, Gaskell 
from scratch could not do anything in either the one or 
three miles, his three mile time being over 10 min. 15 sees. 
The final of the open mile was won in 2 min. 54 sees, by a 
local man with 120 yards start. 

At Taunton, Whish and Fry both failed to come off. 
J. R. Walter, of Ours and the O.U.Bi.G., took second prize 
in the five miles with 400 yards start, the time being slow. 

The Fifty Mile Professional Ghampionship at Leicester 
was won easily by the Frenchman, De Civry, in 3 hrs. 
13 min. 14 sees ; but in the Ten Mile Ghampionship he 
was quite outpaced, and Howell won a good race by three 

yards from Wood of Leicester in 33 min. 34} sec. 

• « • • ~ • • 

The Executive of the B.TJ. have elected A. R. Sheppee, 
of the Homsey B.G., their Ghairman ; Hampton Roberts 
continues Librarian ; and H. R. Reynolds is still responsible 

for all the dangerous hills in the country. 

• • • • • • 

6. P. Goleman has been appointed Official Timekeeper 
to the B.U. 

As the touring season will soon begin, members should 
know that the B.U. Reference Library is open free every 
Monday and Friday, from 6.30 to 8.30 p.m. It contains 
the 1-inch ordnance survey of the whole of England, as 
well as every road-book that has yet appeared, and bound 
volumes of all bicycling literature. 



The first Spring Meeting of the L.A.G. at Stamford 
Bridge last Saturday resulted in another win for a L.B.G. 
man, Buckley easily securing first prize in the mile handicap 
with twenty-five yards start, in the fast time of 2 min. 
54| sees. 0. Thorn, with seventy yards, ran second to 
Buckley in his heat, and F. L Adam was third in the same 
heat. Wyndham and Leeds also ran. 

Oaskell was scratch, but could do no better than about 
3 min. 5 sec. Mr. Griffin's handicapping was slightly 
eccentric ; F. L. Adam was asked to give Buckley ten 
yards ! The three heats were processions, and the final 
was won very easily, Medcalf (95 yards) being second, and 
H. F. Wilson (75) third.

A NOTE OF INTERROGATION. 
To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sib, — Does the down of youth adorn the upper lip of 
J. S. W., or do the snowy locks of age enframe a wrinkled 
face? Is it as a simple lad, or is it aa a miserable misogynist 
that he has involved the wrath of Saint Valentine by 
addressing verses to the sterner sex ? — ^verses, too, not in 
the language of love, but in that of ancient Rome and 
Greece. 

Oh ! J. S. W., what a waU of woe went up from female 
relatives of the members of the L.B.G., when they found 
within the innocent pages of the Gazette something tliat 
they could not understand. Tes, there was the sharpest 
prick that goaded us to madness. Tou will perhaps remark, 
we might have asked to have them translated. This is my 
experience, and it is far from a solitary one. I asked a 
relative — a solicitor in large practice. He had forgotten 
his Latin ! I sneezed twice, so sent for a medical man of 
great renown. He had forgotten his Latin I ! I provided 
a dainty dinner, and invited a barrister. He had forgotten 
his Latin ! ! ! I bought some jam tarts and sent for a 
schoolboy. He did not learn Latin ! ! ! ! I was baffled, 
but not defeated. Armed with Smith's smaller Latin 
Dictionary, I began upon No. 1. But, alas ! many of the 
words could not be found, and some were spelled rather 
differently. No matter! On I went, and this is the 
result of my labours : 

When swiftly along a diuty oonne 

A body of men 

Impetuonsly seek a goal, 

To aid yourself avoid their wheels. 

If you would win a famous victory. 

That sounds all right, and no doubt Horace bought his 
experience in a chariot race, as he flourished rather before 
the age of bicycles. I pass at once to No. 8. This is, 
I should think, dog Latin, and " Procum " is the strange 
name of the dog.

The leader of a recent Club Bun is doabilesB a tease, and 
was justly punished for irritating poor " Frocum." There- 
fore the line— 

" Procom bit yon my boM." 
If my translations are not correct, pity rather than blame 
me, for having dug amongst a cemeteiy of a dead language 
only to bring up bones that will not fit Ton may perhaps 
think this is written with a quill from the porcupine (Ham- 
let's, not Sir T. Browne's), but it is not so.] 

My spirit is sad, not firetfuL If-Latin and if Oreek 
appear in the first number of a new edition — or it may be 
more correct to say a newly edited Gazette — where, oh ! 
where will it cease? Equations and logarithms will be 
succeeded by a little dentistry, or extraction of roots. 
Tours will be abandoned; the racing path a cindery desert; 
and instead of animated discussions on hubs and backbones, 
each man will sit amongst the ruins of his machine, vainly 
endeavouring to square the circle of his spokeless wheels ! 

Can viBioDB of prophetic woe 

To deeper depths of dtdlnen go ? 

A Yoioe from Eastcheap echoes " No ! ** 

A. W. Omah. 

[Note. — The Editor offers his most humble apologies to his 
fair contributor for going beyond the limits even of the 
" Higher Education of Women." His excuse is that the 
Gazette is addressed mainly to cultured males, and it is 
matter of notoriety that every candidate for the L.B.G. 
is required to speak fluently at least six languages, of 
which three must be dead ones. The Oazettb, however, 
will never be dull so long as it is enlivened by the pen of 
A, W. Oman.— Ed.]

The N.E. had the pleasure of introducing the Captain to 
Chingford Hill, High Beech, and other obscure parts of the 
North-East on Saturday. I hear that half the District 
rode up Chingford Hill in magnificent form, and nearly 
scrambled to the summit of the bed of a waterfall at 
Fainnead that calls itself a road. 

I am glad to hear that the N.E. District Captain and 
the Editor have made up their little differences, and entered 
into a defensive treaty " for taking of their dolcs/ar niente,** 
The leading article is, ** that the two contracting parties 
will dismount together at the foot of every hill they may 
meet when out on a run and walk up the same, notwith- 
standing any. rash attempt of the Captain and Sub-Editor 
to break a Uood vessel by riding up the said hills." 



J. R. Walter scored the first prize for the Club this year 
at Taunton on Easter Monday. 

• ••••• 

There is weeping and wailing in the editorial sanctum 
over the terrible muddle the West District Captain's Run 
in last Gazette got into. I hear the Editor is going to 
give himself a month's warning in consequence. 

• *•••• 

N.B. — ^All the editorials are not written by the Editor. 

• ••••• 

To be elected a member of the Club on the 12th, and 
District Captain on the 22nd of the month, is about our 
quickest on record. At the same time it means that the 
new District Captain occupies a very difficult position ; and 
in wishing the West District Captain every success, I need 
hardly ask our members to make allowance for those 
difficulties. 

«*•••• 

I hear that one of our members was elected Secretary of 
the leading Swimming Club first, and elected a member 
afterwards. This beats Pigott's record. 

• ••••• 

Marlborough Hill is a good stiff one, and is veiy seldom 
ridden. On Easter Monday a local bicyclist seemed fairly 
surprised when C. Newman rode up it with ease, and 
without a swerve of his wheel. 

• ••••• 

It is reported, and confirmed by the Captain, that the 
N.E. D.C.'s lamp was alight without intermission from 
Cheshunt to Walthamstow. The report is unlikely on the 
face of it, but the police are investigating the case. 

• • • # • • 

The Joint Meet at Cheshunt had arrived at that point 
by five or six totally different routes, and all sections had 
decided to go back some other way " to avoid the loose 
stones." 

• •#«•« 

I am glad to hear that H. H. Sherriff has so far recovered 
from his accident as to be able to ride twenty miles on Easter 
Monday, and that there is some chance of his riding on 
Whit-Monday. 

It is curious to note the crowd of traditions and legends 
that now hang about our via $acra. Here is the comer, 
they tell you, where Re]molds, Thorn, and Butler fell. 
Here it was that Appleyi^ began to cut record. At this 
tree Marlborough Hill pulls every competitor out of his 
saddle. Here Wynne lost his enamelled medal From this 
cottage the ubiquitous Chippindale requisitioned tea and 
crockery for the competitors. All lovers of sport, in and 
out of the L.B.C., will rejoice that nothing has yet occurred 
to interfere with our Boad Trial, and no one can rejoice 
more at the courteous response of the London Tricycle Club 
than— -Tour omniscient, Pobcufine.

A NOVEL SPIN ON THE GOODWIN SANDS 

ON A BICYCLE. 
A visitor, Mr. Palmer Dalton, a member of the London 
Bicycle Club, took a novel spin on his bicycle on the Goodwin 
Sands on Tuesday morning. They launched from the shore 
in Mr. Porter's galley " Undaunted " near the pier at five 
o'clock in the morning, this being necessary to meet a 
suitable state of the tide when the sands would be hard and 
dry. On arriving there Mr. Dalton was soon landed and 
mounted his bicycle, and took a spin for about a mile and 
half across the Goodwin, which afforded a capital track for 
the machine, the sands being as hard and solid, but much 
smoother than a trip on land. His further progress was 
stopped by one of the gullies which abound there. After 
having spent nearly an hour in the keen frosty air, Mr. 
Dalton with his boat's crew returned to the shore, where 
they arrived about eight, with appetites sufficient to enable 
them to partake of and enjoy a good breakfast. Tiie boat's 
crew consisted of three boatmen, including Mr. Geo. Porter 
as coxswain. A small bundle of the sand was secured by 
Mr. Dalton for conveyance to London. The keepers of the 
light ships were observed with their telescopes watching the 
movements of the /rentleman on his bicycle with great 
interest, and no douU wondering as to the object of his visit, 
— Kentish Telegram,

OUR LAZY FRIEND. 

In recalling our expeditions — bicycling and otherwise — 
there will be few amongst us who do not remember having, 
at some time or other, undergone that extreme penance to 
an energetic soul, the being yoked with a lazy companion. 
Next to the grumbler a lazy man is perhaps the worst 
company, especially if one's tendencies lie in the opposite 
direction. The friend may be a capital fellow in all other 
respects ; he may not abuse you if the weather or roads 
turn out, as they will sometimes, execrable ; and he may 
not hold you responsible for the direction of the wind ; nay, 
he may be " as jolly as a sandboy " when rain mud and 
wind are at their worst ; but if you know him to be of an 
undoubtedly indolent disposition let no such man be 
trusted or travelled with. 

But let us suppose that you do not follow the advice 
here disinterestedly oflTered ; and you, the early riser brim- 
ming over with superabundant energy, take as your com- 
panion on a tour this friend who is a sluggard, a lotos eater, 
in fact a species of moral brake. Of course an early start 
is fixed for the first morning; and the sun has scarcely 
shown his intention of rising when, eager to be off, you 
fling off the clothes and turn from your bed into your tub. 
After much vigorous splashing you are beginning to array 
yourself in your bicycling garments when a thought strikes 



Saturday Runs 

Bicycling in Switzerland , 

Correspondence 

Racing Fixtures 

Quills ^ 

Exchange List

.. 51 

.. 58 

.. 54 

.. 55 

.. 56 

.. 56 



you—" I must go and see after X." You burst precipitately 
into his room, dash up the'blinds, fling open tlie window, 
and then turn your attention to the object of your solici- 
tude. He sleeps — peaceably, and apparently undisturbed. 
But look at those tightly closed eyelids 1 and listen to those 
gentle suspirations long drawn out ! Are they real or only 
the affectations of slumber? " Now, then ! up you get 1 " 
And you yell the words into his ear in stentorian tones 
almost loud enough to waken the dead. A yawn — a stretch — 
and instead of answering briskly to your summons he only 
turns over with a faint groan. " Oh, I say, do get up ! 
It's past five, and such a lovely morning." Thus you address 
him in more conciliatory tones, and, as you think, bring 
him to book, for he answers, briskly enough, '* All right ; 
leave me, there's a good fellow. Til be down in half a 
minute." If you arc a novice in the art you will leave 
him, and will be unfeignedly astonished when you return 
in a quarter of an hour's time to find him once more wrapt 
in the arms of Morpheus or to hear him tumble out of bed 
as you turn the handle of the door. An hour or so will 
probably have been wasted by the time you have got your 
victim down stairs, though of course, as you gain in expe- 
rience, your efforts may meet with better success ; but it is 
the old story of bringing a horse to water,— you may forcibly 
eject him from his drowsy couch ; you may dowse him with 
cold water ; you may pour out upon him the vials of your 
wrath ; you may use the oblique shaft of sarcasm, or the 
direct volley of abuse ; you may silently sneer ; but you 
will never succeed in getting him up to time. Every morn- 



Digitized by 



UoogU 



50 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



ing, regularly as clockwork, there will be the same process 
to go through. And you must not imagine that when he 
is once downstairs your labours are ended. This or that 
nut will want tightening, and he has lost or mislaid his 
spanner; the machine will want oiling, and his oilcan is 
not to be found anywhere. By the time you and your 
friend are well under weigh you will feel as if you have 
done a good day's work, and be proportionately grunopy. 
But even here your troubles will not end. After every 
meal his song will be, " Just another pipe ; just another five 
minutes." Every hill-top will have a fine view, and every 
fine view furnish an occasion for a lounge and a cigarette ; 
and at last you will come actually to dread the very sight 
of a grassy bank, a shady nook, or a public-house sign, for 
fear they may remind your friend that he wants a nap or a 
drink. 

The time you had mentally apportioned to lunch and tea 
will increase to a most alarming extent. He will not be 
hurried ; and at the end of the day's march you will find 
yourself far short of your supposed destination, and with 
little to show for the day unless it be your own vexation of 
spirit. A sigh of genuine relief will probably escape your 
lips on the completion of yonr last duty for the day — ^that 
of getting your friend to bed in decent time, the difficulty 
of which will be only less than that of getting him out of it 
next morning. 

It will, I dare say, be objected that a wise man should 
adapt himself to any circumstances ; but where the adapta- 
tion involves one half of the difficulties I have attempted 
to describe, the wiser man (expirto crede) will be he who 
prefers not to wear out his very existence in attempting to 
eradicate in a few days those lazy habits his friend has 
been studiously acquiring for years. ** Dodo." 

[Note. — We shall be glad to receive a defence of the 
estimable class of individuals attacked by our esteemed 
contributor in a way suggesting that any of them who 
went touring with him had a very bad time of it. — Edd.]

THE GENERAL MEET. 

The arrangements made by the District Captains for the 
General Meet at HalHford on April 21st are as follows : — 

The N.E. will leave Lea Bridge Road at 3.0 sharp 
(there will be no Meet at Syborn's Comer). They will 
proceed to "Jack Straw's," where they should join the 
N.W., who meet at. that point at 4.0. The two Districts 
proceed together to Kew. 

The W. meet at the " Coach and Horses," Kew, at 5 0, 
and proceed with the N.E. and N.W. to Kingston Station. 

The S.W. meet at Kingston Station at 5.30, and ride 
with the N.R, N.W,, and W. to Halliford. 

The S.E. (Blackheath Division) meet at Lewisham Church 
at 3.15 sharp and join the Croydon Division, which leaves 
Central Croydon Station at 4.0 sharp and proceeds direct 
to Halliford. 

No District is expected to wait more than ten minutes 
for another District ; and in the case of the S.E., no law 
will be allowed by the Croydon District. 

AsHLET W. Babrett, Captain.
