SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
7th April. If the whole riding force of the N.E. could 
have been concentrated in one point there would have been 
a fine muster ; as it was only eight sat down to tea, but 
the skirmishers were out far and wide, and sighted a variety 
of members from time to time. Seven members left the 
Corner, but even in Leyton Long discovered a violent 
squeak, which culminated in his hind tire coming oif, so he 
retired : the District in ignorance went easy for him for 
miles after. In Buckhurst Hill Keynes found family ties 
too strong, and retired homewards. On the hill, however, 
the Editor came to hand, and a party of six riding three 
deep, with three ex-Captains to lead, was a somewhat im- 
posing sight. At Goldings' Hill one of these ex-Captains 
treated the others worse than they care to express, as in 
breach of a solemn covenant he insisted on riding up the 
hill. Fortunately Eelsey also achieved the same effect, 
however, and they were revenged. Just after the " Wake 
Arms," Weeks and a friend on a double tri. were met, but 
refused to join, and the six rode into Epping, the leaders 
indulging in some questionable racing at the finish. The 
Editor promptly went home to tea, but the party were re- 
inforced by S. Q. Hough, C. Y. Lyne, and A. Lloyd, making 
up the eight, who rode quietly home in the cool of the 
evening by the direct road, which was fairly good for the 
first time this five years. They nearly suffered shipwreck 
by two unruly hay carts, but beyond feeling a little " all- 
overish," escaped without damage. Besides those who 
actually took part in the run, E. D. Sewell is believed to 
have gone to Epping via Chelmsford, but he did not come 
to hand in time ; Baldwin was sighted by two members, 
and Harold Smith by one riding in the vicinity. J. G. 
Fowler, too, was at Abridge, but forgot the destination of 
the run ; it is very pleasant to hear that he ijs on the road 
again. Livingston and his brother were about on a double 
tri, and are reported to have met with a nasty accident ; 
altogether we might have mustered eighteen — but we didn't. 
Present at the run or some part of it : G. H. Gibbs, S. G* 
Hough, Jolly, Keynes, Kelsey, Lloyd, Lyne, Long, Rucker, 



T. E. Scrutton, and the District Captain. Present in other 
parts of the District : Baldwin, J. G. Fowler, Livingston 
and friend, Harold Smith, and Weeks and firiend. 

Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 



N.W. District. 
The meet at Cheshunt on March 31st was only attended 
by eight members ; but although the numbers were small 
yet we had a good run. Four members left ''Jack' Straw's 
Castle," and at Tally-ho I Corner four more men joined. 
We then went through Barnet and Potter's Bar, and turn- 
ing to the right passed to the left of Northaw and on by 
Goff's Oak to Cheshunt, where we met the N.K District at 
6.25. A good tea was provided at a very moderate rate. 
After a pleasant chat we made a start for home at eight 
o'clock by way of Theobald's Park, Forty Hill, Enfield, 
Winchmore Hill, Southgate, and Tally-ho 1 The lanes were 
very dark, but we managed to get along all right with the 
aid of our lamps, although we went a little out of our way. 
The run terminated at 10.15 p.m. Distance thirty-five 
miles. Present : AJken, Dalton, Elgood, C. L. Hill, Jackson, 
Lown, TuUoch, and W. J. Williams. 

Palmer Dalton, District Captain. 

Saturday, April 7th. We had a very jolly run to Eastcott, 
the attendance being slightly better. Our route lay through 
Burrows, where Jackson and Williams joined us, and across 
the Brent Reservoir to Frieru Farm, thence by Kingsbury, 
Kenton, Harrow Weald, Pinner Station, and Pinner Green 
to Eastcott, where Barnett, Hindley, and Stunt turned up. 
At the " Black Horse " we were well received, and had a 
very good tea at the small cost of one shilling each. This 
is an inn that should be patronised. ' After an extremely 
jovial tea we started home at 7.45 p.m., and kept a steady 
pace all the way home via Pinner, Harrow, Kenton, and 
The Hyde, the run terminating at 9.15 p.m. The roads on 
the whole were pretty good, but near Harrow care is 
necessary, owing to the Metropolitan Railway extension 
having destroyed the road. Distance twenty-seven miles. 
Present : Alison, Barnett, Dalton, C. L. Hill, Hindley,

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Jackson, Lown, Stunt, Tulloch, W. J. Williams, and one 
visitor. Palmer Dalton, District Captain. 



W. District. 

Saturday, 7th April. Four members assembled at 
Savernake Cottage for the start vrhich uras made at 
4.15 p.m. After going a short distance we came up with 
Hawley, who had ridden from Hampstead in " extra fast 
time." The Richmond road was fouud to be scarcely 
rideable owing to the tramways — the curse of this district — 
and Indian file had to be resorted to for a couple of miles. 
At Richmond 0. Thorn was waiting for us. We followed 
the direct road to Ripley tnd Kingston, Esher, and Cobham, 
which requires no description from me, as being too well 
known by everyone who has ever crossed the modern 
velocipede. Passing Cobham, several machines were con- 
spicuous outside the " White Lion," and recognised as 
belonging to the S.W., but how they got there history does 
not relate, for we could not believe that the owners of these 
machines were inside the hostelry which is only eight miles 
from the start. I would remark that the pace was more 
conducive to pain than pleasure, as the rivalry of Leeds and 
Thorn knew no bounds, and required an extra effort on the 
part of the D.C. to maintain his position at the head of the 
team. (Query. — Would it not be better for D.Cs. to ride 
last instead of first ? ) Fourteen in all sat down to a good 
tea at the " Anchor." Afterwards, Hawley, Thorn, and 
Naylor departed for Aldershot, and the main body for town. 
An enjoyable ride home took place, marred only by the 
involuntary dismounting'of Meyer at Surbiton, who, however, 
fortunately escaped with a shaking only. We parted with 
the S.W. at Kingston, and continuing on reached Richmond, 
where the D.C. left the major portion consisting of Whattons, 
Meyer, and Langmore, and rode via St. Margarets, Isleworth, 
and Brentford to Acton, thus avoiding theexecrable Richmond 
road. Distance 43 miles. Roads very dusty and stony at 
Ham and Red Hill. Present ; J. W. Langmore, W. Meyer, 
0. Thorn, E. Naylor, J. S. Whatton, A. B. W. Whatton, 
E. Scott, Leeds, J. D. Belfield, (W.) A. Hawley, (N.W.) 
Gruggen, A. E. Buckler, Ryan, (S.W.) ; and 

H. C. PiGOTT, District Captain. 
N.B. — Please note that on special recommendation the 
Black Horse " will be the rendezvous at Eastcott, and not 
the " Ship " as previously stated. 

S.W. District. 
The meeting at Kingston Station on Saturday consisted 
of three members of the West — ^including a tricyclist — and 
three S.W. men, a fair average contingent for us ; and if 
the two visitors who had promised to favour us had fulfilled 
their engagement, we should have indeed made a goodly 
show ! The tricyclist, starting first, led the way at a good 
speed to Cobham, where we waited for a possible contingent 
from the West District, which, it was understood, was 



coming in considerable force. We had not long to wait 
here before the West arrived in close order, six strong, 
evidently bent upon being first at Ripley, for in taking 
advantage of the lead they gained by not stopping, they 
are to be congratulated upon the very successful and highly 
creditable riding they displayed in maintaining it until 

tibbles" was reached at about 5.45 p.m., where the two 
districts met very shortly afterwards. Thirteen members 
and one visitor sat down to a good and plentiful tea, which 
apparently gave very general satis&ction at a minimum 
outlay of Is. 3d. per head. About 8 p.m. the company 
began to separate, and as several members — ^tempted by 
the very favourable weather — elected to continue their 
rural researches further, only some eight men remained to 
return to Kingston. This, with only one trifling misad- 
venture, was accomplished in a little over an hour. Distance 
from Kingston 24 miles. Members present : A. E. Buckler, 
A. L. Gruggen, H. S. Ryan, and R. M. Gruggen, etc. 

The run next Saturday is to Oatlands Park, near which 
is situate a very interesting cave, most elaborately and 
tastefully decorated with shells of almost endless variety, 
and arranged in elaborate devices at, I understand, fabu- 
lous expense. This was visited by us last year, and was 
greatly appreciated ; after which we go on to Wey bridge 
for tea, where we again hope for a good muster. 



S.E. District. 
Six men started from Croydon on Saturday, under a 
bright sky, for Wrotham. Farningham was reached vid 
Orpington and Birchwood Corner, but Alfred Herbert had 
been left inhaling the healthy breezes on Hayes Common. 
The Blackheath Division were found waiting on the bridge, 
and our numbers would have been increased by five had not 
Stephen Smith and Morley been obliged to turn homewards. 
The opposing heights were scaled in loose order, and after 
falling in with the Sevenoaks contingent the main body 
reached Wrotham about seven o'clock, where H. R. Reynolds 
was found waiting. A capital tea was provided at the 
Bull," but we had no time to linger ; so at half-past eight 
lamps were lighted, and we made the best of our way 
homewards by Ightham, Seal, and the " Polhill Arms." 
Unfortunately, the looseness of the roads in places and the 
absence of a moon delayed us considerably, but the different 
sections eventually reached home safely before midnight. 
Present : G. F. Beck, A. Day, Alfred Herbert, S. Herbert, 
H. G. Morgan, A. Morley, J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, 
H. R. Reynolds, F. B. Smith, D. R. Smith, S. P. Smith, 
S. E. Whealler, C. Wigan, R. C. Willis, four visitors, and 
R. G. Francis, District Captain. 

The Captain has kindly promised to be present at our 
next Run to East Grinstead ; and in order to give him a 
hearty welcome, members are requested to make a special 
effort to attend the Meet.

The Axexutraase. 



BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(OONTINUID FBOM FAGB 96.) 

The Rigi, or northern side of the lake, naturally divides 
itself into two sections, owing to the absence of a road 
berween Vitznau and Gersau. 

(1.) 

— Lucerne. 

5i - Meggen. 

Si KliBanacht. 

104 Greppen. 

12| Weggia. 

16 Vitznau. 

(2.) 

<— Geraau 

4^ Brnnnen < 

8^ Sislkon 

12i Fluelen 

This is by far the most interesting side of the lake, and 
can be traversed by a bicycle the whole way except along 
the "Upper Nose" between Vitznau and Gersau, where 
the boat must be taken. Two miles out of Lucerne the 
road forks : you take the right-hand road nearest the lake. 
The map will show you a shorter road turning up over the 
hill a little beyond this fork, and as yott look at the road 
itself from the fork it has all the appearance of being 
the main road, with telegraph posts etc. along it : do not 
however be enticed by it. Both roads have to mount, but 
you can easily ride up the hill on the road nearest the lake ; 
the other is steeper and longer, and it is moreover a com- 
paratively new road which has evidently never come into 
fashionable use, for the stones are all on it this year with 
grass growing between them just exactly as they were two 
years ago^ rendering the descent on the other side an 
extremely uncomfortable piece of riding. In Elissnacht the 
way is troublesome, as you are apt to bear too soon to the 
right and so get down to the steamer stage from which you 
have to come back again. I can only give as a general 
direction to make a tolerably wide sweep to your left 
through the town. The proper turn to the right begins to 
mount and will take you a bit above the lake. You can 
continue in the saddle to Greppen, but here (at a bend by 
a church) you will find the road rough as well as steep, and 
a walk of half a mile or so is desirable. This is the only 
bit on either side of the lake necessitating a dismount. 
The hill down to Weggis is nowhere very steep, but there 
are generally a lot of foot passengers about. Both it and 
the absolutely level 3^ miles to Vitznau are splendid riding. 
Next to the Axenstrasse, which will be described presently, 
this is the loveliest bit of road I know -anywhere ; in one 
respect, perhaps, it is superior to the Axenstrasse, as you 
are riding quite close to the water's edge all the way, — so 
close in fact that one day, when the lake was a little rough, 
I got quite a splashing from the waves. Although I shall 
always contend that having regard to the middle and far 
distances the views on the Lake of Than are superior to 



anything else in Switzerland, there can be no question that 
as regards the near distance the Lake of Lucerne, with its 
precipitous banks and its incessant changes of shape and 
colour, is the most picturesque of all ; and it is nowhere 
seen to subh advantage as from a bicycle. At Vitznau you 
ean leave your machine and steam up the Bigi to lunch if 
the day be fine ; but it is not an entertaining expedition in 
bad weather. It will give some notion of what the weather 
has been this year in Switzerland when I state that we were 
twelve days at Lucerne without once seeing the top of the 
Rigi. 

The road between Gersau and Brunnen is well made, but 
exposed to frequent landslips : you must not therefore be 
surprised at coming across bits of new road in a temporarily 
unrideable condition, and I have a very vivid recollection 
two years ago of some decidedly deep extemporised water 
channels, one of which very nearly gave me "a buckler." 
The road from Brunnen to Fluelen is best known as the 
''Axenstrasse," and it is perhaps unrivalled as regards both 
nature and art. It is carried along the precipitous eastern 
bank of the Bay of Uri (as the head of the lake is called), 
in a succession of ups and downs, twists and turns, — along 
rocks so steep that in many, places you look direct down 
into the blue water often $ome 500 feet below you. The 
road is safer than most for riding purposes, for being in 
such an excessively dangerous situation greater care has 
been taken to protect its edges with a continuous low stone 
wall, so that no bad carriage accident is likely to occur 
In several places the road is carried actually through the 
cliffs by tunnels. These can all be ridden, but you must sit 
well back and keep a firm hold of your machine. Whether 
it is due to the winter snows being blown into them and 
drifting in uuequal masses or not I cannot tell, but the 
road inside these tunnels consists of a succession of hillocks 
and hollows, over which you go "bump, bump, bump" in 
the dark in a style sufiicient to frighten you out of your 
saddle if you did not know that it was possible to surmount 
them in safety. The first time I rode through them two 
years ago was at the end of a long and very fatiguing day 
on the St. GoUiard, and I was so tired that I determined 
nothing whatever should induce me to dismount, and I 
would sooner be spilt if it came to that ; hence I learnt, what 
otherwise I might not have learnt to this day, that there is 
no occasion to be alarmed at these tunnels. The principal 
dip in the ride is down to the village of Sisikon, where, as at 
Brunnen and Fluelen, you are at the level of the lake. The 
intervening rise on either side is, I should guess, about 500 
feet or perhaps more, but I have not been able to ascertain 
it accurately. The surface of the road, except the tunnels 
and a few yards here and there, is simply splendid. When 
we were on it the other day we had taken the steamer to 
Fluelen. The steamer was due at Fluelen at 3.0, but did 
not get in till 3.5. After disembarking its passengers and 
taking a fresh load on, it was to start again at 3.20 and go 



Digitized by 



Google 











54 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 




direct back to Brunnen in a perfectly straight course of 6;^ 
miles. They are fast boats, and our course was not only If 
miles longer and all hilly, but we had a strong wind against 
us. We were also obliged to dismount and wait at least 
three minutes at a level crossing for the St. Gothard express. 
Nevertheless, we were on the landing-stage at Brunnen 
waiting for the steamer 17 minutes before it came up, 
having done the 8 miles — hills, wind, tunnels, and train 
notwithstanding — in 42 minutes. This would have been 
impossible but for the excellence of the surface. I do not 
know whether the steamer officials had any bets on the sub- 
ject this time, but they were most utterly incredulous when 
we told them at Flueleu that they would have to pick us 
up again at Brunnen, and when they saw us on the landing- 
stage their faces wore very much the same expression as that 
of a country farmer whom a conjuror has invited to partake 
of a plum pudding brought out of his own hat with bis 
watch in the middle of it. On the occasion of this ride we 
put a Swiss dog to flight, for a wonder. He was coming 
along the road towards us, but on seeing us instantly put his 
tail between his legs and fled precipitately for nearly three 
miles : we saw him at every distant corner squinting round 
to see if we were coming, and the moment we appeared 
down went the tail and ofi* he scampered again till he Anally 
took refuge at the back of a chalet which was, I presume, 
his home ; he was so thoroughly frightened that he did not 
even come out and bark at us after we had passed, as most 
pot-valiant hounds would do. 

The Axenstrasse is a portion of the road leading by the 
St. Gothard Pass into Italy. Names, distances, and levels 
of this famous coach road are easily ascertainable from 
Baedeker. It is of course perfectly possible in midsummer 
to cross into Italy with a bicycle by this road or by the 
Simplon, but a great deal of dismounting will have to be 
encountered. These two roads, as well as the Bhone valley 
road and the Furka Pass at the head of it, are in many 
places mended with very inferior stuflF, — miscellaneous kind 
of stone rubbish fished out of the nearest glacier stream, — 
and they wear badly in consequence. The more eastern 
passes into Italy are much firmer and better. The 
St. Gothard road does not begin to rise steeply and con- 
tinuously until you leave Amsteg, which is 11 miles beyond 
Fluelen and 350 feet above it. From this point to the 
summit of the pass the distance is 23 miles and the rise 
5200 feet, an average or resultant gradient of 1 in 23, which 
means (allowing for some of the miles being level or even 
occasionally down hill) that you will have 15 miles or so 
with a gradient varying from 1 in 15 to 1 in 20. Were 
the surface of the road good, no doubt a good proportion, 
perhaps half this distance, might be covered in the saddle ; 
but though some bits of the road are fair enough, its general 
character is inferior, so that you would not probably get 
more than five or six miles of riding the whole way, and that 
only in fragments involving constant dismounts. Two years 


ago, when I was on it, the road was no doubt in an excep- 
tionally bad and dangerous condition, owing to the constant 
traffic of heavy wagons carrying stone and iron for the 
construction of the railway ; the opening, however, of 
the latter, with its consequent diminution of the coach 
traffic, weakens the chance of any permanent improvement 
in the condition of the road, as the authorities have now less 
interest in maintaining it than they had before. Altogether, 
therefore, to those who meditate a descent into Italy my 
advice would be to pack self and machine in the train at 
Amsteg and get out at Airolo. It will be better to select one 
of the stopping trains and not the express, as the luggage- 
vans will be less crowded. The descent from Airolo to Bodio, 
where the valley of the Ticino begins to be fairly level, is 2800 
feet (distance 20 miles), and I could see from the line this 
summer that some rough bits of road would have to be 
encountered. In such descents as these however occasional 
dismounts would in any case be required for the purpose of 
cooling your brake. The roads in North Italy, especially 
about the lakes, are exceedingly good ; but in summer in a 
dry season they will be found ver^ dusty, and in many 
cases there is a deficiency of shade about them. They 
would be far better riding in April, May, the latter part of 
September and October, than in the intervening months. 

Gerabd F. Cobb. 

Thunerhof, Thun, August, 1882. 

(To be continued,) 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
Sib, — May I fill a comer of your valuable and widely 
circulated periodical, (the butterman takes it every week 
and sends it wrapped round my morning sausage), with a 
suggestion that will give the Committee fits, and make the 
leader of the Conservative Opposition, (need I say I allude 
to the North-East District Captain), his hair stand on end ? 
It is this, sir. We have some very good long-distance 
riders in t)ur club. The record on roads for twenty-four 
hours is in a very unsatisfactory state. Could not the Club, 
about three weeks after Whit Monday, organize a twenty- 
four hours' ride for our members, say on the Bath Road ? 
Medals might be offered to all beating a certain distance ; 
say a bronze medal for 160 miles, silver for 200 miles, and 
gold medal for beating record, which is about 220, 1 believe. 
I think we should be surprised at the distance that would 
be covered. Why, sir, a wretched THING (capitals, please. 
Printer) that has almost the same relation to a bicycle that 
a costermonger's donkey has to a race-horse — a "Facile '* — 
does somewhere about 200 miles in the twenty-four hours. 
What ought H. R. Reynolds to do ? to say nothing of our 
President, Douglas Smith, and others whose name is legion. 
On Easter Monday one of our members rode 147 miles in 
sixteen hours. I hope that this letter may at least result 
in eliciting an expression of opinion from our long-distance 
members.— I 'am, sir, yours truly, Roabstkb.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE.

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir, — I desire to bring before the notice of your readers 
a proposal of a very revolutionary nature, but one which it 
is my sincere conviction would be for the Club's good, to 
wit, the dismemberment of the West District. I do not of 
course remember the reasons which led to the formation of 
it, and I am in the unfortunate position of Little Bo Peep" 
in that I don't know where to find them," but I do know 
that, as far back as my perusal of the Gazette goes, there 
haa been in the West, and perhaps still more in the South- 
West District, a constant and painful ^training to keep up 
appearances, and generally to keep things going. Before 
passing to probable results of a change, let me say what I 
should like to see done. Let the four Districts follow the 
example of the other great powers, and go in for a partition 
of Poland on a small scale by taking the Great Western 
Railway from Paddington as a division, and giving all the 
country north of it to the N.W., and south to the S.W., 
and let the rest of the line (to the east) be that which at 
present divides the N.W. and W. I cannot help thinking 
that many advantages of some such scheme will immediately 
present themselves to your readers ; the following are only 
a few : — 

1. Symmetry. Can anyone say why London should be 
divided north and south, as it is, through Charing Cross, 
and two Districts be made of the one side, and of the other 
three? 

2. Attendance at Meets. I believe that the chief 
reason why men in the W. and S. W. do not turn up is that 
they are never sure of meeting a respectable muster, and 
when they submit to the restraint and other disadvantages 
of Club runs as opposed to unorganised riding, they like to 
be rewarded by meeting a large number of friends, and by 
having a sort of social gathering. 

3. Racing. The membership of the W.D. would be 
divided about equally ; but of the racing members, while 
those such as Buckley and Jephson who belong to the West 
in virtue of residence at Oxford would go to the N.W., the 
majority would help to create and maintain a racing team 
in the S. W., which (barring Griffith, who, I understand, has 
permanently retired) is almost destitute of path ridera 

4. Accessibility of the present W.D. from both sides. 
The Thames forms for the eastern half of the Club a 
natural and practically impassable dividing line, but on the 
other side of the " line drawn north and south of Charing 
Cross" this is not so, for not only does the river meander 
in a way that quite unfits it to perform such an important 
duty, but it is bridged at every few miles, and either side 
is equally accessible to the average member of the W.D. 

I want to know what is the raison d'etre of my District ? 
Does it exist simply on account of the difficulty of esta- 
blishing a satisfactory boundary line between its neighbours, 
because, if so, I claim that the G.W.R. is ready to supply 
a long-felt want, or is there some other reason which I have 



entirely failed to grasp? Finally let me say that I am 
sure it is owing to no want of energy on the part of its 
officers past or present that this undoubted stagnation 
exists, but that it appears to me to be due to the inherent 
wrongness of our territorial arrangements, which the 
establishment of a scientific frontier would go far to 
rectify.— I am, sir, yours faithfully, J. S. Whattok. 
April 5th, 1883.

I wonder whether the two individuals th^ N.E. saw on 
Golding's Hill last Saturday were " small City clerks," or

by their reckless way of dismounting. 

The North East kept up its reputation as the Official 
District by riding with three former Captains of the Club 
in the front rank of the Meet. How is that for majestic 
grandeur? And there was an ex-Secretary behind as a 
reserve.

If the Epping police had not been very forbearing, the

members of the B.U. Executive on a charge of furious 
riding. " It was all the D.C.'s fault, please, sir." 

The treaty between the N.E. District Captain and Editor 
has broken down, owing to treachery on the part of one of 
the contracting parties. The Editor won't let me say which.

I hope members are keeping Saturday, the 21st, clear for

at the last two Meets at Halliford that we may expect good 
luck this time, and — a strict secret — Halliford is a capital 
place to stay the night, and have a long row on the^ 

on " one day next week." , * ' ' '^^

our well-known ex-Secretary, has been, after a severe 

contest, elected one of the Bournemouth Improvement 

Commissioners. We trust hifi abilities will not overlook 

the interest of bicyclists and tricyclists in the state of

Innocence. — Well-known racing member to road rider, 
whom he had been taking along the Ongar road at about 
seventeen miles an hour : — " What sort of pace do they 
go at, at the Club Meets ? . Do they go much faster 
than this? 

At the North West Meet on Saturday, a 60 and a 58-inch 
went straight for one another; the shock can only be 
compared to the Charge of the Heavy Brigade. 



I hear, also, that the North East had a close shave of 
being annihilated at Buckhurst Hill, when two carts swept 
down within about three inches of their line. 

As over a dozen old medallists will probably start on 
Whit Monday, it is as well to undeceive our uninitiated 
members who seem to think that the tenth medallist will 
be about the twentieth at the finish. An extended know- 
ledge of the old medallist dispels the illusion. He generally 
rides hard till he finds his chance for the enamelled hopeless ; 
whereupon he retires into the first respectable inn he sees, 
orders a big dinner, disposes of it at his ease, and rides 
slowly in when everyone else has left the winning post. 
*••«•• 

I was always a favourite with the cook, and consequently 
I generally find my bicycle what she calls " tidied," and the 
lamp trimmed ready for the Saturday Kun. The other day 
the worthy old soul got rather mixed with her bottles, and 
replenished the lamp with furniture polish instead of the 
usual sperm. I will draw a veil over the darkness of the 

night, the spluttering lamp, and the pardonable expletives. 

• ft « • • • 

I hear Livingstone of the N.E. has had a very nasty 
accident with his double tri., and I am sure the Club will be 
anxious to hear how he is getting on. 

• •••••

report to next Committee, so we may hope to hear of the 
Special General Meeting before long.

NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for ''Gazette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdays. 

GENERAL MEETS. 
"Yes, General Meets are all very well," says Slinker, 
''but I don't see the fun of toiling over miles of rough 
roads to meet a lot of fellows whom you don't know, and 
who don't care a pin whether you speak to them or not. 
Let me have my quiet trundle by myself, or with the par- 
ticular friend who thinks with me, and does care for my 
company." Slinker is not bad at choosing a pair of kid 
gloves, or in grumbling over spilt milk, but give him some- 
thing to do for his Chib and he hides his light under a 
bushel, professing much but doing little. Now just com- 
pare him with Slasher, who is not less zealous of the Club's 
good name than he is of his own : he confesses there may 
be some inconveniences in attending the General Meets^ 
but he soon shows how he enjoys doing his part: he is 
liked by all who know him ; he pleases himself in trying to 
please others ; while we have men like him in the Club 
there need be no fear of any permanent falling off in Club- 
love. Fortunately Slinker has not many disciples, for he is 
slowly but surely losing his influence, and laziness— as it 
alwajrs must in the end — is giving way before healthy work : 
members are beginning to realise forcibly the fact that in a 
large Club like ours, scattered all over London and its sur- 
roundings, it is wise to take advantage of every opportunity 
that presents for strengthening the vitality of the Club by 
bringing all our men together in friendly intercounie ; and is it 
not difficult to conceive how this end can be accomplished 



in a more natural or more agreeable manner than by the 
skilful organisation of a few General Meets during the riding 
season? 

So far our remarks have applied to the duty of the thing 
and the " opposite side," but now we may as well inform 
our doubting members that there is a deal of pleasure, 
actual pleasure, to be got out of a General Meet. It is 
always, to say the least, a jolly gathering, where we are 
sure to meet some of our friends from other Districts ; 
ceremony need not be and is not studiously observed, for 
membership must always be held to be a free pass to 
mutual recognition, so that acquaintances can and ought to 
be made amongst members previously unknown to each 
other,— and if acquaintances, why not friends ? Who knows 
but that the seeds of many a staunch friendship may be sown 
at our General Meets, as they certainly have been already in 
many instances at our ordinary runs ? Then we earnestly hope 
that those members who have not hitherto attended them will 
really make up their minds to try for themselves,— that they 
will come determined to please and be pleased,— and they will 
at least have the satisfaction of knowing they have done what 
they could to support the Club, not in a trifling but in an 
important matter, for does not the Club's growth, its very 
existence,;depend upon the efforts of its individual members? 

This is no pathetic appeal, as our friend Slinker would 
very likely call it : it is only a plain statement of what we 
believe to be the simple facts, and we have no doubt what- 
ever that those men who come to Halliford next Saturday, 
animated with the feelings we have endeavoured to portray, 
will return home well pleased.

THE GENERAL MEET. 
The final arrangements for the General Meet at Halliford 
on April 21st are as follows : — 

The N.K will leave Lea Bridge Road at 3.0 sharp 
(there will be no Meet at Syborn's Corner). They will 
proceed to "Jack Straw's/' where they should join the 
N.W., who meet at that point at 4.0. The two Districts 
proceed together to Kew Bridge. 

The W. meet at Kew Bridge at 5.0, and proceed with 
the N.E. aud N.W. to Halliford via Feltham. 

The S.W. meet at Kingston Station at 4.30 p.m. sharp, 
and proceed to Halliford. 

The S.E. (Bhickheath Division) meet at Lewisham Church 
at 3.15 sharp and join the Croydon Division, which leaves 
Central Croydon fetation at 4.0 sharp and proceeds direct 
to Halliford. 

No District is expected to wait more than ten minutes 
for another District. 

Ashley W. Barrett, Captain. 



HALLIFORD. 
N.E. District. Some members will probably proceed 
direct via Tottenham, so the party at the Lea Bridge Road 
are encouraged to start. 

Thbo. Goplbe, District Captain.

SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
14th April. The account of this run may lack the 
accuracy of detail which sometimes is observable in N.E. 
reports, from the fact that unavoidable causes prevented 
the D.C. from attending at the Corner at the time appointed ; 
it is stated however that four members as usual left that 
spot, proceeded via Red Bridge to Ilford, and thence by 
the back lanes, which were more than awfully dusty to 
Upminster. They did not know where to wait for the S.E., 
so they didn't do it. It also appears that two or three 
other members arrived by divers other routes, two visitors 
being present on a double tri. One at least of the 
skirmishers came via Chelmsford, which they as a class 
believe to be on the road to any place on earth, and, later 
still, when the S.E. B.D., had joined the party at the 
" Bell," the Captain, kept in town by urgent occupation^ 
arrived just in time for tea, having come by the main road, 
which he said was execrable, though subsequent appearances 
were against him. The D.C. meanwhile did not leave 
Walthamstow before 6.0, and coming by Chigwell Row 
found it loose decidedly ; by 7.30, however, he had reached 
the haven of rest, and had time to make a substantial tea 
and perform divers duties of his office before the rest of the 
party would consent to start. Ultimately, eighteen in all, 
the party returned, taking to the main road, and finding it 
in really capital order for it The company separated at 
Ilford, and made their divers ways homeward. Present : 
besides the S.E. B.D., Arnott, Ashley Barrett, A. C. Forster, 
G. H. Gibbs, Kelsey, Lloyd, Long, Lyne, Stunt, Weeks, 
two visitors, (Letcliford and Whitford), and 

Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 

W. District. 

Saturday, 14tlL Though somewhat stormy looking, 
Leeds and Thorn were at the starting point in good time, 
showing by their personal appearance their desire to confute 
my last week's remarks anent their riding. Starting at 
4 o'clock, we proceeded by the lanes to Hanwell, thence to 
Greenford by a somewhat circuitous route, thus avoiding 
the main road. Up Greenford Hill we experienced the 
novel spectacle of being chased by a bridleless horse, which 
apparently enjoyed the unusual sensation of seeing three 
bicyclists flee before it at top speed, thus reversing the order 
of affairs. Following the Ruislip road for about a mile, we 
branched to the right for Northolt, in preference to 
continuing along the Ruislip road. The surface was, how- 
ever, very bad, the long period of dry weather having played 
havoc with the usually good surface of these lanes. We 
arrived at Eastcott first, (5.15), and took possession of the 
" Black Horse." A quarter of an hour later Dr. Langmore 
and Meyer hove in sight, the latter looking like some knight 
of the past ages who had descended from his pedestal at the 
British Museum, for he bestrode a velocipede which brought 
to the minds of all the oft-related tales of the primeval ages, 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



59 



so ancient was the implement (of torture ?) If anyone has 
a glass case for disposal, please apply without delay to the 
owner of above. A little later Williams and Nay lor arrived, 
thereby swelling the number to Seven, not so bad after so 
discouraging an account by J. S. Whatton. A very nice 
little tea was provided at the " Black Horse/' but owing to 
the ravenous state of the N, W. on the preceding Saturday, 
the landlord was compelled to abolish the proverbial Is. 
and charge Is. 3d., care will therefore be taken for the 
future not to follow the N.W. to any of their favoured 
resorts. An early start was effected, following the road to 
Euislip and thence to Ockenham, joining the main road 
between Hillingdon and Hayes, at which juncture we parted 
from Williams and Naylor who proceeded to West Drayton. 
Nothing occurred to mar the even tenor of our ways, 
excepting the roughness of the roads from Hillingdon to 
Slough and Ealing. Here I parted from the others after a 
very enjoyable afternoon, adding yet another success to the 
much despised West. Present: Dr. Langmore, Leeds, 
Meyer, Naylor, Thorn, Williams, and Pigott. 

I would ask particularly all members to make a point of 
being at Halliford next Saturday for the General Meet, and 
by so doing endeavour to prove that the W. District is 
capable of making as good a show as the other districts, 
notwithstanding the futile attempt being made to jdis- 
frauchise us. H. C. Pigott, District Captain. 

N.W. District. 
Although the run to Mill Hill was short, there were 
plenty of hills to make up for it. Four men started from 
** Jack Straw's Castle," and at Tally Ho I Corner four more 
joined, amongst whom we were glad to see R. Newman 
a<;ain. We kept along the north road as far as the foot of 
Barnet Hill, and then turned off past Bingham's to Bamet 
Gate. The long hill here was ridden by some, and then we 
turned down towards Highwood Hill, lliis was ridden by 
all at a rather fast pace, in fact rather too fast to be 
pleasant. At Mill Hill we were very well treated at the 
" Three Hammers," where Alison, Price, and Lown rejoined 
after a short excursion of their own. After tea we had 
some singing, and then started home, the run terminating 
at about 9.15 p.m. Distance, 18 miles. Present : Alison, 
Dalton, Jackson, Lown, R. Newman, Price, Tulloch, 
Williams, and one visitor. 

Palmeb Dalton, District Captain. 

S.W. District. 
Four members and a visitor met at Kingston Station for 
the run to Weybridge and Oatlands Park ; the route 
selected was over Kingston and Moulsey bridges, thence 
through West Moulsey and Walton to our first destination 
in Oatlands Park. As previously stated, our ride on 
Saturday was to include a visit to a very celebrated grotto, 
situate in Oatlands Park, and close to the Hotel of that 
name. The grotto at once strikes the visitor as a unique 



structure of its kind, and affords to the artist and geologist 
food for thought and careful study. The interior of this 
meandering and cavernous shell-house is most tastefully 
decorated in elaborate patterns, with countless varieties of 
white shells, combined with DeTonshire spars, and a more 
valuable combination termed Bed Serpentine. Star-fish and 
other representatives of the " finny tribe " are here most 
carefully imitated and executed upon the walls and ground 
of the caverns. As the general history of this extraordinary 
place was most ably described by'A. E. Buckler, after a dis- 
trict visit here last year, in the Gazette of the 17 th August 
(Vol. v., No. 24), I will therefore merely supplement his 
historical description by a few further details we collected 
on Saturday. The Duke of Newcastle was the original 
owner, and as the grotto is reputed to have cost £40,000 
it was a valuable property ; but tradition says that the 
noble proprietor, who was addicted to habits of gambling, 
had through a run of ill-fortune at cards (Nap. ?) to make 
over his interest to the Duke of York in discharge of debts 
thus incurred. History does not appear to relate whether 
or no the rendezvous of the noble gamblers" was within 
this enchanting fairy retreat. An ample bath (8 by 6 by 
5 feet deep) within the cavern was constructed during this 
second proprietorship for the exclusive use and pleasure of 
the Duchess of York, who seems to have spent much time 
here. The caverns were evidently intended as a place 
of cool retirement from the possible scorching suns of 
summer, and would consequently seem to be a more 
appropriate luxury for more southern and sultry climes. 
The light spars are arranged in drops from the top, and 
convey the idea of suspended icicles ; thus the notion is 
decidedly cool, in spite of the varying temperature of the 
caverns. These drops appear to be first formed into shape 
in wood and plaster, and the spars and shells afterwards 
stuck on. The visitor can at once readily realise the 
immense labour such an elaborate arrangement must have 
entailed, but. that three Italian workmen should have 
expended twenty years of their lives upon the task— as 
they are reported to have done — seems almost incredible. 
In the centre of the first and principal cavern is an uncom- 
fortable but stately chair of English oak, used by Charles II., 
and around this central object are grouped four wicker 
ones, containing elaborate silken cushions, upon which are 
worked various patterns of seaweed and shells by the 
Duchess of York herself. Graceful statues also adorn this 
chamber. The fireplace in one of the numerous recesses 
is also worked in shells, but seems an almost unnecessary 
adjunct to this luxurious edifice. Those of our members 
who have not yet visited, but who perhaps have frequently 
passed this curious place, I certainly recommend to call upon 
the next opportunity; it is a pleasing novelty that well 
repays the visitor. Our duty completed in this respect, 
we continued our run to the river side and emerged close 
to Shepperton Lock, where we found a very good inn and 



Digitized by 



60 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



ourselves quickly supplied with tea. Soon after the con- 
clusion of this satisfactory meal we were recognised by 
Maddoz of the Wanderers, who had seen us at the drama- 
tic show, and whom we were glad to learn had not only 
attended but positively enjoyed the performance. We were 
pleased to see H. H. Sherriff had so far recovered from his 
recent accident as to be able again to ride with us, and to 
hear from E. J. Sherriff that he had not given up all idea 
of riding again this year. At 8 p.m. we left Shepperton 
Lock for Kingston, and followed the loose and occasionally 
stony road through Weybridge, Walton, Apps Court, and 
Moulsey, reaching Kingston soon after 9 p.m. Distance from 
Kingston about 21 miles. Members present : A. £. Buckler, 
E. J. Sherriff, H. H. Sherriff, " Arry" Ryan, H. R. Best, 
visitor, and R. M. Obuogen, District Captain. 

The run next Saturday is to Halliford, and as this is a 
General Meet I hope as many members as possible will be 
at Kingston Station at 5.30 p.m. (sharp) to ride with the 
other Districts. 

S.E. DlSTBIOT. 

Saturday was to have been quite a gala day in the South- 
East, for the Captain had promised to join the run. The 
slight rain in the morning had laid the dust ; the sun peeped 
out just at the right moment ; the D.C. was mounted on a 
brand new machine — ^all in honour of the occasion ; but it 
was all to no purpose I The main body waited half an hour 
in Croydon for our noble chief, and it was not until after 
arriving at East Grinstead that we learnt the sad news by 
telegram that most important business prevented his being 
present. Considering that the Blackheath Division had gone 
over the water into Essex, and that this is one of our longest 
runs, the attendance was a good one. Rucker, Hindley, and 
Wyndham, came down from London ; The O'Reilly showed 
his usual energy by riding up from Brighton, and Morgan 
and F. L. Smith rode across from Sevenoaks. The racing 
element was well represented by Adam, Beck, Jephson, and 
Wyndham, and the goodly show round the tea table might 
compare favourably with the meets of former years. 

The party broke up gradually, some men leaving for 



home soon after tea, while others stayed later to assist in 
the musical line ; and it was somewhere about eleven when 
the last light disappeared over the Railway Bridge. Present : 
F. L. Adam, A. Barker, G. F. Beck, Alfred Herbert, 
E. H. Hindley, (N.W.) M. H. Jephson, H. G. Morgan, 
J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, A. L. Potter, The O'Reilly, 
M. D. Rucker, Jun., H. N.Sharp, F. B. Smith, S. E. Whealler, 
W. Wyndham, four visitors, viz: McCrae, Paull, A. Potter, 
and F. Williams, and R. G. Francis, District Captain. 

It is to be hoped that the South-East will show up 
creditably next Saturday for the general Meet at Halliford. 
We shall leave Central Croydon Station at 4 o'clock sharp, 
and in order to avoid Kingston macadam, take the road 
through Sutton to Ewell, where J. Kinder has promised to 
be at the '* Glyn Arms " at five o'clock, and pilot us over 
some excellent roads by way of Walton Bridge. 

Blackheath Division. 

April 14th. East Grinstead is considered too long a 
journey by some members of the Blackheath Division, and 
accordingly a meet with our N.E. neighbours at Upminster 
was arranged for their convenience. Four members and two 
visitors left the Heath, but one being unwell had to turn 
back at Woolwich. After crossing the river the remaining 
five proceeded by very loose lanes to Beacon Tree Heath, 
where the N.E. are usually met ; as however they did not 
come in ten minutes, the visitors tore themselves away from 
the sweet scents of that particularlv uncomfortable-looking 
locality and made for Upminster, where they arrived safely, 
excepting that one of the visitors, a Marlborough College boy 
on a 48in., was thrown in the well-known water jump near 
Romford. On meeting the home District the Blackheathens 
were much disappointed to find that their D.C. was absent, 
but before tea was finished they were gladdened by the 
unexpected arrival both of him and of the Captain himself. 
The latter did the visitors the honour of accompanying them 
back as far as the Albert Dock. Present : A. Day, F. 
Kinder, S. P. Smith, N. C. Willis, and two visitors. 

The Captain very much regrets he was unavoidably 
prevented from riding to East Grinstead on Saturday last 
to meet the S.K men there. He hopes they will forgive 
him for this time.

BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(OONTIHUn) VBOM PAOK 51.)

Lucerne. 



(Variations in height unimportant.) 
You leave Lucerne by the Zurich roa4, which turns up 
by the Lucerner-hof. There is no possibility of mistaking 
your route in this expedition, nor are there any hills re- 
quiring a dismount when it is taken in this direction. If 
the direction be reversed a short dismount may be found 
needful in the " Hohle Gasse," leading up to Tell's Cliapel. 
You cross the line at Rothkreuz. We came up at a 
moment when a train was engaged in shunting, and, 
strange to say, the barrier was not up. By means of some 
capital practice for a slow race we managed to retain our 
seats till we could just tail the last carriage as it eased up, 
and we promptly crossed behind it before the engine driver 
had time to reverse. " Oh ! those English ! " I heard the 
station-master exclaim, far too much amazed at the apparent 
temerity of our proceeding to venture on any oflBcial 
remonstrance. Cham is the great centre of the Anglo- 
Swiss condensed milk business, and we noticed all the way 
along the road the milk tins which are set down and taken 
up by the company's carts on the roadside at points nearest 
the farms. Perhaps it is principally owing to this constant 
traffic that the bit of road between Kothkreuz and Cham is 
invariably out of condition. It is just one of those occa- 
sional bits which are needful to help one to appreciate how 
good the Swiss roads as a rule are. We met two of the 
company's carts full of etnpty tins coming along. The 
driver of the hindmost cart had affably condescended to 
leave his own team and occupy a seat on the box of the 
first cart for society's sake. Swiss horses seem to go crazy 
whenever they are left alone, and the consequence was that 
although the first team took to us quietly enough, the 
hindmost, directly we got up, bolted clean up into the cart 
in front of them, and a general scatteration of milk cans 
ensued. That driver will smoke his pipe in vigilant soli- 
tude for the future. The church at Cham is worthy of in- 
spection. Zug is a charming little town (paving, walk), and 
you will get an excellent lunch at moderate charges at the 
"Falcon," just at the corner of the open square in the 
centre of the town. The handsome new Government 
building close to the lake is worth a visit, if only for the 
sake of the view of the water from its windows. Up to 



this point the road (for a Swiss road) may not have been 
found particularly interesting, but for the next 13 or 14 
miles as it skirts the lake it is most enchanting. The water 
of the lake is very transparent, far more so than that of 
Lucerne, and the banks, though not so steep and imposing, 
are exquisitely beautiful. In Arth you must bo prepared 
for a perfect army of dogs. You will also come upon a bit 
of cobble paving, but it is too short to necessitate a dis- 
mount : the bend in the street prevents your seeing how 
short it is. You must be prepared for some stiff bits of 
work three miles beyond Arth, especially a hill leading up to 
and beyond a level railway crossing ; but there is no need 
to dismount, unless it be for stones. If you have a taste for 
inferior works of art in honour of that rather mythical 
personage William Tell, you will halt at the chapel which 
is on your right afl you come up a bit of hiU. The steep 
shady hill leading down from it called the Hohle Gasse,' 
or Hollow Lane," will remind you of Devonshire after a 
mild fashion. It is not a thing to strike the EngUsh eye as 
specially remarkable, but it is exceedingly unlike a bit of 
Continental roadway, and this is probably the secret of its 
reputation. When riding up it in the reverse direction one 
day we met the matrons and maids of the village coming 
down in procession from the chapel chanting an ''Ave" 
and telling their beads. Our appearance had much the 
same effect on them as the intrusion of a dog, a bird, or 
even a butterfly in church has on an English congregation, 
namely, that everyone seemed to regard it as a welcome 
and legitimate excuse for a relaxation of devotional tension, 
and a diversion of the current of mental energies into more 
secular and possibly more i^iviting channels.

Lucerne.

Schwyz. 

Brunnen. 



This excursion may be abridged by taking the boat to 
Elissnacht, as wbU as back from Brunnen, or on the other 
hand, it may be lengthened by returning to Lucerne by 
any section or sections of the Lake Round, already described 
in § 12. To Arth the road is the same as § 13 reversed. 
In Arth you turn to the right ; the paving is very slight ; 
no occasion to dismount. A mile or so beyond, road forks : 
take the right. From here to Goldau, and beyond it, there 
is a considerable amount of climbing to be done, but all 
rideable if the surface is in good condition, and if you are 
not going to make too long a day of it. The road traverses 
the scene of the great landslip at the beginning of this 
century, and few sights are more calculated to impress one 
with the prodigious character of the forces of nature. You 
will still see the scar, as it were, of the slip high up on the 



Digitized by 



Google 



62 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Bossberg on your left, and the wilderness of boulders 
through which you keep mounting on the Rigi, or right 
side of the valley, was forced up hill all this distance. 
Some of the hills down to Lowerz are steep and tortuous, 
but do not last long : they would probably have to be 
walked the reverse way. The little islands on the lake of 
Lowerz are very charming, but the colour of the water is 
more peculiar than beautiful. In Seewen bear to your left 
and cross the line; you will mount slowly up into the 
Schlag Strasse, a fine road leading from Schwyz to Sattel : 
turning into it to your right you reach Schwyz in about 
\\ miles. In Schwyz you had better dismount before 
turning the corner at the churcli, as the pavement, though 
fairly good, becomes steeper. Across the open square on 
your right, facing the church, is the " Rossli " (" Cheval ") 
where two of us had soup, omelette, and a bottle of Asti 
Spumante for the moderate charge of 4f. 70c. The road 
leading straight down at the side of the inn is the road you 
will take to Bruunen. You should go and see the church 
whilst they are getting your lunch ready. ITie road to 
Brunneii is nearly all down hill. Just before we neared 
the village of Ibach, as we were coming down a good pace, 
we saw about 150 yards in front of us a regular whirlwind. 
Dust, leaves, and sticks were suddenly caught up, and after 
eddying round and round at a terrific rate were swept up 
en mam into the sky, where they as suddenly disappeared. 
By the time we came up (after slackening a bit) everything 
was perfectly still, but some 40 yards of roadway were left 
as smooth and bare as a sheet of glass — not a particle of 
detachable matter in any shape or form was visible. I 
wonder what would have happened to us had we been just 
a quarter of a minute earlier : perhaps I might have been 
enabled to treat your readers with a section on the condition 
and gradients of the roads in the moon. 

Geraed F. Cobb. 

August, 1882. 

{To be cantimied.) 

Be WEST DISTRICT. 
To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sm, — I have the greatest possible respect for Mr. Wliatton, 
but as a member of the West District I feel bound to object 
most strongly to his scheme for cutting us up ; and I will 
deal seriatim with regard to the advantages which he 
claims for his proposal — 

1. Symmetry. I think Mr. Whatton has overlooked the 
important fact, that of the 128 members living north of the 
Thames, 91 are placed on the west side of the line drawn 
through Charing Cross, so that the existence of two Districts 
on one side, and only one on the other, is by no means an 
inexplicable point. 

2. Attendance at Meets. Sir, — I venture to state that, 
under whatever rule the country composing the W. and 



S.W. Districts may be, the river will always prove a rival 
to the road. 

3. Racing. The line proposed by Mr. Whatton would, 
as far as I can judge by looking at the map, and from ' 
personal experience as to the dwelling places of members, 
give nearly all the 39 members of the West District to the 
S.W. ; thus, with the exception of any men at Oxford who 
chanced to belong to the Club, the N.W. would not be 
much better off than it is at present. 

4. Accessibility of the present W. District from both 
sides. I was not hitherto aware that the meandering course 
of a river unfitted it for performing the office of a boundary, 
nor did I know that the divisions between the Districts 
ought to be impassable. But, perhaps, such ambitious 
designs may have been put into the heads of the surround- 
ing Districts, that in order to keep our freedom it may be 
necessary for us to destroy the bridges over the Thames 
and dig a canal up the Edgware Road. 

With regard to the raison d'etre of our District, I take 
it to be that as a certain number of our members live more 
in the heart of London, and are consequently somewhat 
differently placed with regard to meets from members of 
other Districts, it is more convenient that they should form a 
District by tliemselves, than be amalgamated with members 
living ip the suburbs. 

In conclusion, let me protest most strongly against the 
extinction of the W. District as being a step utterly un- 
called for, and, moreover, one which in all probability would 
be most objectionable to the majority of the members 
concerned. We all know Mr. Whatton to be a most en- 
thusiastic bicyclist, and one who has the welfare of the 
Club most thoroughly at heart, but, bearing in mind a 
previous correspondence on this subject, I think he has 
allowed himself to be somewhat biassed by the numerous 
tram-lines between his own house and Savernake Cottage. 
—•Yours truly, Disteictus. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir, — As one of those who fought and bled in the revo- 
lution of 1876, the outcome of which was the birth of the 
Western District, I have taken upon myself to reply to 
Mr. Whatton 8 proposal, and to point out to him the 
raison d'etre of that District, which is briefly this : The 
Club was formerly divided into four divisions — N.W., N.E., 
S.W., S.E., and the rendezvous for the Club meets of the 
respective districts were, N. W., Jack Straw's Castle, Hamp- 
stead, N.E., top of Lea Bridge Road, S.W., Kew Bridge 
and Kingston Market Place (alternately), and S.E., Croydon. 
It was found that the great majority of the N.W. members 
resided in the neighbourhood of Hampstead and Maida 
Vale, and the greater portion of the S.W. members lived 
at or near Surbiton or Richmond. A very considerable 
minority of these two districts, however, resided at Kensing- 
ton, Chiswick, Willesden, and Acton, and to attend the 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



63 



meets of their respective districts, these memhers, most of 
whom were employed in the City (and could not leave their 
business before two p.m. at earliest), had to get home, 
change their clothes, and ride, in the case of the N.W. dis- 
trict, distances varying firom six to eight miles, and in that 
of the S. W. from four to ten miles, to join their respective 
Glab meets, and this was found to be impracticable, both on 
account of the distances to be ridden and the bad roads 
over which they had to travel, and so the Western District 
was formed to meet this difficulty. The same arguments 
hold good now ; the distances are the same, and the roads 
(as old riders too well know) are worse, and a ride from 
Kensington or even Acton to Kingston is sufficient to arouse 
the wrath of even so amiable and even-tempered a man as 
the writer of this article. What with greasy macadam 
from the watering — No — Hydrostatic machine, bad surfaces, 
and tram-cars, not to speak of that exquisite specimen of 
local road mending, which forms the connecting link between 
Petersham and Ham Common, the surface of which reflects 
such credit on the Kichmond road surveyor, that eight miles 
has few attractions. Now as regards attendances ; this 
year, up to the present, the average is about eight per meet, 
out of a total of thirty-five members, which is satisfactory, 
and it must be remembered that we have fewer members in 
this district than any other, save the S.W., in addition to 
which fifteen of our number never now by any chance 
attend Club runs, either having given up riding or because 
they race regularly. Surely,.therefore, an average of eight 
out of a possible twenty is very good, and I will ask the 
question : Does the pleasure of a Club run consist in along 
string of riders, the last half of whom are smothered in the 
dust which the wheels of the first raise, or in a more com- 
pact little body, all of whom are personally acquainted ? 
The best plan, when practicable, is to meet some other dis- 
trict at tea, and this will be carried out as much as possible 
during this season. 

Again, we have just elected a new District Captain, who 
is also new to the Club, and who has to win his spurs. Is 
it too much to ask all members of the district (not excepting 
even the district visitor), to rally round their newly elected 
chief, and, by their regular attendance at their weekly runs, 
place the little Western District in as high a position among 
the attendance averages at the end of the season, as it has 
held, and does hold still, in the list of prize winners. — 
Yours truly, W. A, Smith. 



THE RACE MEETING. 
To the Editor of the London Bicyck Club Gazette. 
Sui, — Allow me to express my regret that the Race 
Committee have so far departed from the traditions of the 
Club as to shorten the distance of our Open Handicap to 
one mile. 

The only advantage of this change is the largely increased 
number of entries which is probable. 



Against this is to be set the disadvantages of a lengthier 
programme, and of the introduction on our course of an 
inferior class of riders. The tendency of all clubs lately 
has been to shorten their handicap distances, with the 
result of bringing out a class of racing men who can do 
nothing but spurt, and doing nothing to encourage staying 
power except among such men as can fairly enter into 
scratch races. Our Club has been the conspicuous ex- 
ception ; we have cared more for the improvement of 
racing than for the benefit to our balance sheet of large 
entries ; and we have steadily adhered to the rule of giving 
a handicap of a distance ranging from five to two miles. 
The best men of the year have entered for our handicaps ; 
the men who have not entered and who will now enter are 
the inferior men who can only travel a short distance with 
a long start. 

In the interests of sport and of the reputation of the 
Club I protest against a change conceived solely in the 
interests of the balance sheet of the Race Meeting. — I am, 
sir, yours truly, T. E. S. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 

Sib, — I oppose " Roadster's " proposed twenty-four hours 
ride for reasons given below : 

Firstly. Our Committee have quite enough work to do 
with their ordinary duties, without any additions. 

Secondly. Our finances are not in so flourishing a con- 
dition that we can afford to waste our money on a competi- 
tion that would not do the Club or its members any good. 

Thirdly. Much as I believe in our esprit de corps, I 
doubt if we should find enough members willing to sit 
up all night taking the 4imes at different parts of the 
Bath road. 

Fourthly. If it were taken up and carried out, what 
good would it do to bicycling as a sport ? Everyone knows 
a bicycle is far superior to a " Facile." 

Fifthly. It might do a great deal of harm to some of 
our young members, who would, no doubt, overtax their 
strength in so long a competition. — Yours truly. 

Ex Member of Committee and Medallist. 



STABLING ACCOMODATION FOR WEST DISTRICT 
MEETS. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
Sir, — For the benefit of those members who wish to 
avoid the five miles of bad macadam roads and tramlines, 
I propose to procure the use of a stable, or suitable room, 
wherein machines can be locked up in safety, at some point 
near to a main-line railway station. Also to obtain one or 
more lockers for the use of members to keep a change of 
clothes, etc., in. 

I propose Ealing as the most suitable locality, and con- 
venient as regaras railway accomodation. Will all who 
care to subscribe kindly communicate with mtf, and I shall 
also be very pleased to receive any ideas relative to the 
successful carrying out of the matter. — Yours faithfully, 
H. C. Pigott, District Captain.

A HINT TO D.CS. 
If you want to increase the average of attendances, get 
members in your di^rict to take a turn round the grass plat 



on Saturday afternoons in the intervals of lawn tennis, and 

then chalk them d^wn as " riding somewhere in the district." 
« • 1 • • • • 

It is sad to relate that the Napoleonic features of the Ex 
D.C. were conspicuous in their absence from the S.E. tea- 
table on Saturday. Has he given up riding, or is he in 

hard training for the Bath Trial ? 

• ••••«. 

His friends will be glad to hear that Arthur Herbert is 

once more at home, and hopes to be able to attend the 

General Meet on Saturday. 

The N.W. D.C. was not much hurt on Saturday. He 
only received five cuts, two sprained wrists, ditto neck, 
shoulder, and elbow, thirteen bruises and scratches, and two 
lovely black eyes. 

There is an amusing notice at Mill Hill concerning a 
funeral fund with no entrance fee. One quite longs to reap 
advantage from it. It is rumoured that the D.C. is a 
member, and has been trying to take it out of the Company."

FADS OP FORMER TEARS. 

In the days of long ago, before M.D.R's and Clytie bags 
were invented, every man had his fad for carrying luggage. 
A sleeve of an old mackintosh, sewn np at one end and 
then staffed like a sausage, was my favourite, but a friend, 
with whom I went a tour in the west, and who did not 
happen to have an old mackintosh, tried brown paper 
parcels, each article being done np separately and tied to 
the curly spring of his " Coventry." He said it would be 
80 handy. We got on all right for a while ; bnt when the 
parcels began to be shaken up a bit he was continually 
shedding them. After fl3ring a hill, H., who was riding 
behind, would frequently hail me to ride slowly while he 
went back to retrieve his night shirt, which had come 
adrift ; or the paper had chafed, and his tooth bruah was 
lying around in the road somewhere ; and I would have to 
wait on a gate, swearing softly to myself like an Indian 
gold mine shareholder. I remember I carried a pair of 
trousers rolled up in a bundle, and strapped on the bar in 
the front wheel of an Ariel ; being on one side of the bar 
it revolved in an eccentric orbit. It made the Cornish 
rustics sit up when they saw it coming down the road. 

Carrying a dog whip was a fad which prevailed for a 
short time. Its object was to chastise dogs, and more par- 
ticularly small boys, whose amusement in those days was 
to pursue the bicycle and catch hold of the backbone, in 
addition to cap throwing. On observing symptoms of 



aggressiveness in a small boy, the rider would dissemble, 
simulate unconsciousness, and lure the urchin on to his 
fate. Then the whip was produced rapidly and applied, 
the small boy straightening up immediately, applying his 
hand to the injured part, and gazing after the retreating 
cyclist with a general expression on his face denoting sur- 
prise and dissatisfaction. But it was a deadly fad, 
producing chaos ultimately in the rider, owing to the lash 
getting caught in the wheel. 

Another fad which prevailed in a portion of the N.W. 
District was the particular curl of the backbone of a 
machine. It was about the time when Keen and Timber- 
lake were supplanting the heavier makes. We would spend 
a considerable portion of our time in making diagrams 
embodying our respective views, and when a new machine 
came home, on Sunday afternoons a meeting would be 
held, and the machine placed against the wall and gazed at 
critically by the hour, as if we had been aesthetes gloating 
over a lily, with someone looking at us. The Hampstead 
division were not in this, as these Philistines all rode 
"Stassens," and were consequently not amenable to the 
refining influences of sweetness, culture, and light ; for the 
Stassenite at that time only required a dirty face, a clay 
pipe, and a man in front with a red flag to be a parish 
steam roller. 

I knew a man once who, although elderly and of full 
habit, possessed all the enthusiasm of youth and rode a 
bicycle. His only difiiculty was mounting. He had de- 
veloped a peculiar jerk in getting from the step to the 
saddle, which invariably landed him over the handles. If 



Digitized by VjlJIJ^lC 



66 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



it had only occurred occasionally he would not have minded 
it, because, being of a philosophical temperament, he would 
have welcomed it as a change ; but it happened almost 
every time. First the thing grew monotonous, and then 
it seriously annoyed him. So at last he sat down and had 
a pipe, and proceeded to think it over to see if he could 
not get ahead of it somewhere. He was not a mechanical 
man, but possessed great powers of observation, and had 
remarked that the donkey boys of Hampstead Heath, when 
wending their homeward way in the evening, did not sit in 
the centre of the donkey in the seat provided there for the 
public, but invariably sat as near the southern end of the 
animal as its construction would permit. He inferred that 
the boy took up this position so that, in case the intelligent 
quadruped should feel a yearning to kick after the manner 
of his kind, the additional weight on his hind wheel, as it 
were, would discourage him. Being a man of action he 
knocked the ashes out of his pipe and said to himself, I 
will arise and go to my plumber." So he arose and went 
to his plumber. I saw him a few days afterwards with his 
face lighted up with a smile of conscious pride, and 4lbs. of 
lead piping wound round the backbone under the step. He 
had found peace. W. R. Sbwell.

It being pointed out that in the list of candidates pub- 
lished in the April number of the C. T. C. Gazette, the 
word "London" appeared after the name of Mr. Volck- 
man. The Hon. Sec. was directed to write Mr. Shipton 
asking him to what Club this referred, and pointing out 
that we were generally known by the term of the 
" London." 

It was proposed by T. E. Scrutton and seconded by 
R. Q. Francis " That the programme of the Race Committee 
be revised by substituting a two mile open handicap for a 
one mile open handicap," to which H. G. A. Brown, seconded 
by R. M. Gruggen, moved the following amendment: 
"That the recommendation of the Race Committee be 
followed." After considerable discussion it was put to the 
meeting, when the amendment was lost andT. E. Scrutton's 
motion carried. 

It was decided that the report of the Tricycle Sub-Com- 
mittee should stand over to next Committee meeting, and 
that a copy of the report should be sent round to each 
member of the Committee. 



THE ROAD TRIAL. 
To CoMPETiTOBS. They are reminded that entries for the 
above will be received by the Hon, Sec. on and up to 
Monday, April 30th, and not after that date. I shall be 
glad if they will inform him not later than Wednesday, 
May 9th, of the day on which they intend to reach Bath, 
in order that rooms may be secured at the "White Lion " 
as in former years. If possible a special van for bicycles 
shall be obtained in the afternoon train from Paddington 
to Bath on Sunday, 13th, and I hope to meet all the com- 
petitors at the " White Lion " on Sunday evening, to draw 
for places and to receive final instructions. I shall be 
happy to convey a moderate amount of luggage from Bath 
to the hotel selected for tea on Monday evening (" The 
Milford Arms," Hounslow). All luggage must be securely 
labelled at Bath. Members are reminded that a complete 
road-riding costume must be worn in the Trial, and the 
L.B.C. uniform is preferred by the Committee. Further, I 
would point out the undesirability of members inviting 
riders outside the Club to ride up from Bath in the Trial 
with the competitors. Such a plan seems to add to the 
danger of interference on the part of the authorities, and 
appears moreover to be unattended with any practical good. 
The programme for Whit Monday will be as follows :—

Care must be exercised at these four situations between 
Bath and Melksham : — 

A. — 2;^ miles from Bath the road forks. Take the left 
hand one (the right goes under a bridge). 

B. — 5 miles from Bath (in the village of Box) the road 
forks. Take the lefb hand one by the Bear Inn." 

G. — 8^ miles from Bath, at Gorsham, the road forks. 
Take the right hand one (the left goes to Chippenham). 

D.— 8f miles from Bath, at Corsham, keep straight on at 
cross roads (the right hand one goes to railway station), 
and 100 yards beyond the cross roads, on reaching some 
curious old almshouses, take the right hand road to Melk- 
sham (5 miles) ; the road straight on leads to Lacock. 

To Timers. I shall be glad to receive the names of 
any gentlemen who will volunteer to time the competitors 
on Whit-Monday at any of the following stations, and to

learn at which place they will undertake to be present : — 
Devizes, Hungerford, Reading, Slough. (Alfred Herbert 
will be at Marlboro' Hill, W. A. Smith at Newbury, and 
A. Henry at Maidenhead.) Timers are requested to be at 
their stations, with any refreshments they may be good 
enough to provide, at least half an hour before the arrival 
of the first man. I would suggest that timers provide them- 
selves with flat glass bottles, each holding a pint of water, 
claret and water, or weak tea, equal in number to the com- 
petitors, and also with as many packets of beef or mutton 
sandwiches — without mustard or salt — wrapped in paper 
with no string outside. Attention to these details of com- 
missariat will, I feel sure, conduce more than anything to 
fast times and a possible cutting of the record, and I shall 
feel obliged if timers will inform me after the Trial of any 
expenses they may have incurred in carrying out these 
requests. They are also requested to forward to me 
through the post their records and remarks by five p.m. on 
Tuesday, May 15th. Ashley W. Baabett, Captain. 



ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 
Our Annual fiace Meeting is to take place at the Crystal 
Palace on Saturday, 16th June, which is a half-crown day. 
Tickets will be obtainable from members of the Committee 
at the reduced price of Is. 6d. The railway companies 
make no reduction on half-crown days, so that anybody 
getting their tickets for the Palace for that Saturday 
through us will save a shilling on each ticket. Members 
are particularly requested to make this known to their 
friends as much as possible. It has not yet been decided 
whether tickets will be sent round with the Gazette as here- 
tofore, or whether ladies' tickets will be supplied free. 
Notice of this will be given later on. 

As will be seen from the report of the Committee, it has 
been decided to substitute a Two Miles Open Handicap for 
a One Mile. The events will therefore stand as follows : — 

One Mile Members' Handicap. 

Two Miles Open Handicap. 

Two Miles Members' Scratch for Ladies' Challenge. 

Five Miles Open Scratch. 

Ten Miles Members' District Challenge. 
Entrance fee for the first four events 2s. 6d. each. No 
entrance fee for District Challenge. Entry forms will be 
sent round to every member later on. E. S. Price.
