THE HALLIFORD GENERAL MEET, April 21st. 

The men rode to Halliford in three groups — ^firstly the 
S.W. men whose adventures are here related by their 
D.C.- 

8, W, District Captain's Bsport. 
Probably in consequence of a slight discrepancy having 
arisen with regard to the time for making a start from King- 
ston Station, and of the conflicting announcement in the 
last Gazette, the muster at the meeting place was any- 
thing but extensive. Coleman was the first to arrive, I and 
a visitor following shortly after. The precise moment for 
our start was set down for 4.30 p.m., and after allowing ten 
minutes grace, in which to vainly look for a further display 
of force, we three took to the wheels. The run to Halliford 
being so short and with ample time at our disposal, we had 
proposed to somewhat extend our run and to ride by Esher 
and Walton, but as this route would have brought us in 
close proximity to Sandown Park and the traffic to the race 
course there, we altered our plan, and avoided that form of 
dangerous excitement by again riding over Kingston and 
Monkey Bridges; thence by App's Court and Walton bridge 
to the scene of action as in the previous week. Our S. W. 
contingent came to Halliford singly and in twos at frequent 
intervals, soon after we had arrived there. First we were 
pleased to observe the Bros. Buckler, on a new Social " 
tricycle of great promise, which seemed to have been ridden 



Digitized by VjU^I^^IC 



68 LONDON BICYCLE 



with very litUe apparent effort, and looked extremely well ; 
next came E. J. Sherriff, this making his second appearance, 
and we hope by no means his last, this season ; and finally 
upon two wheels came Harry Ryan, who had mistaken our 
District notice, and organised a separate meet at Kingston 
at 5.30 p.m. Noel Whiting, a well-known member of the 
district, sailed up stream to Halliford, thus completing a 
very successful muster for the S. W. D. 

Then came the S.E. men, concerning whom the S.E. 
District Captain writes as below : — 

S.E. District Captain's Itepart. 

Saturday, 21st April, was a marked and agreeable con- 
trast to the day of the first Halliford run last year. Ten 
men left Central Croydon Station at 4.15, and this number 
was strengthened by Stephen Smith at Waddou, A. L. 
Potter at BeddingtOD, and H. N. Sharp at Sutton. Just 
before reaching Ewell we were overtaken by Jephson and 
the brothers Adam, and at the Glyn Arms " we found our 
guide, J. Kinder, and Smither waiting for us. Here the 
two Herberts turned back, as did Sharp shortly afterwards. 
The rest, with Kinder at their head and the wind well 
behind, plunged into the intricacies of cross-country lanea 
The experiment proved a good one. Once or twice we had 
to slow up for a loose treadle or a slipping saddle, or as 
occasionally on doubling a corner in our sinuous course we 
came upon a party of racing folk driving away from San- 
down Park ; while now and again we had fairly to lie over 
our handles in answer to a desperate spurt on the part of 
one of the ex-D.C s, who, freed from the trammels of office, 
romped along as gaily as schoolboys. Crossing the main 
road at Esher we cast a passing glance at the beauties of 
the Mole, and received a passing compliment on the part of 
a by-standing rustic, on whose admiring gase the South 
East array burst like glorious vision as we swept in fair 
order under the railway bridge at Walton. But unfor- 
tunately the station bell sounding at the moment seemed 
to bring to the leaders memories of the last lap. Away 
went the flyers, and away went the symmetry of the 
South East. In vain did the D.C. try to keep in hand his 
scattering forces, until, after a final scramble, they became 
united again at the Ship," Halliford. Here Wyndham 
was found waiting, as well as The O'Reilly, who had faced 
the north-easterly wind and ridden up from Brighton. The 
South WeA were before us, but it was nearly half an hour 
later when the three other districts came in sight, and the 
South East were at last gladdened with a sight of the 
Captain — ^all the more so from their having been obliged 
to forego that pleasure the previous Saturday. It was not 
long before all were seated at tea, after which, at about 
eight o'clock, under a fair moon, the South East set the 
example by starting homewards, and Croydon was reached 
by the main body about ten o'clock. 

Then came the N.E., N. W., and W. The former District 
jUiet the Hampstead men at the usual meeting place of the 



CLUB GAZETTK 



latter. The two Districts then rode to Kew, where they 
met the W., and then made for Halliford via the Bath road. 
Near Acton, W. A. Smith was seen on foot, and unable, 
much to his friends' regret, to join the run. 

N.W. District Captain's Report. 

Seven N.W. men left " Jack Straw's Castle," with the 
N.E. District, on Saturday at about 4.20 for Halliford, and 
two joined when we got there. We went via Piatt's Lane, 
Worm Lane, Willesden (where Alison had a slight fall), 
Acton, and Kew. We then joined the W. District 
Distance 42 miles. 

W. District Captain's Report. 

The first to arrive at the starting point was R Scott, 
followed soon afterwards by the D.C. As the clock struck 
five a hurge party arrived, consisting of Meyer, Naylor, 
two Hewitts, Rucker, and three or four N.E. men whose 
names I do not know. After waiting a quarter of an hour 
the N.W. and N.E. Districts arrived. Without further 
delay all remounted, and rode back again over Kew Bridge 
expecting to meet the Captain there ; but rumour had it 
that he had gone on, so that no dismount was called for. 
Proceeding gently through Brentford, I led the way to 
Isleworth and Twickenham, and on to Hampton. After 
Sunbury the water-splash emptied several saddles. A little 
further on the head of the Captain was espied, making for 
us along the Feltham road. 

The road through Brentford was bad, owing to the laying 
down of new drainage culverts. Good running through 
Isleworth to Twickenham, rough from Hampton to Sunbury, 
good on to Halliford. 

A very good tea rewarded the fifty-four men who sat 
down to it, and at eight p.m. the party broke up, most of 
the N.E. men riding home through the City. 

Present : 8. W.^V&m. Coleman, A. K and A. Buckler, 
Noel Whiting, E. J. SherrifiF, H. L. Ryan, H. R. Best 
(visitor), and R. M. Oruggen, District Captain. S.E. — 
F. L. Adam, W. K. Adam, A. Barker, H. G. A Brown, 
A. Day, Alf. Herbert, S. Herbert, J. Kinder, A. L. Potter, 
The O'Reilly, Schmettau, Sharp, Smither, S. P. Smith, 
S. E. Whealler, Willis, Wyndham, H. Young, two visitors, 
and R. G. Francis, District Captain. N.E. — Nevill, Kelsey, 
A. C. Foster, S. N. Stunt, E. D. Sewell, Ashley Barrett, 
A. Lloyd, M. Di Rucker, jun., and Theo. Godlee, District 
Captain. N. W. — ^Alison, Aiken, Hawley, Hindley, Jackson, 
Lown, Price, Tulloch, and Palmer Dalton, District Captain. 
IT.— C. Braby, two Hewitts, Leeds, Naylor, E. Scott, Meyer, 
F. M. Williams, 0. Thorn, Jephson, and H. C. Pigott, 
District Captain. 

Ashley W. Baebett, Captain. 

The South East may be fairly congratulated on their 
muster of twenty-one, but with fine weather we hope to do 
even better things next Saturday at Riverhead. The two 
Divisions will meet on Famborough Common at five o'clock.

BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(OONTINUBD FBOM PAGE 62.) 

§' Ib.—Engelberg. 

Distance 
MUei. 

::: 

... Wolfenschiessen. 
... Grafenort, 

26' Top of HilL 

27i Engelberg. 

In Stanz, instead of turning down to your left as in § 12, 
keep straight on ; payement good. There is no other road 
after this for you to confuse with your own, which goes 
straight up the valley of the Eugelberger Aa, crossing it 
about three miles further up, after which you have the 
stream on your right the whole way to Engelberg. On each 
of the two occasions on which I have made this expedition 
it has been my fate to encounter bad weather and very 
heavy roads. The road is narrow for a Swiss road, and 
unlike most Swiss roads is flanked with hedges, which pre- 
vent the breezes from sweeping it, so it does not dry up so 
quickly as most. I have not, however, found any occasion 
to dismount until the big hill was reached. This begins 
about three-quarters of a mile beyond Grafenort, which 
consists of a very large deserted-looking house and stables 
on the right hand side of the road, and a little chapel up 
the hill to the left of it. If the surface is good it is well to 
persevere up the first straight reach of the hill, as you will 
come to a bit of level round the comer at the top ; but 
after this you will have to walk for a little over an hour. 
The road zigzags up through a pine forest nearly all the 
way. This is a great advantage in fine weather, being 
beautifully cool and shady ; on the other hand, in un- 
settled weather it keeps the road in a greasy condition, 
which makes your brake slip and renders the descent 
additionally troublesome. Of course you will make notes 
of gradients and comers as you walk up, and if botanically 
inclined you will be delighted at the splendid masses of the 
Noli me tangere, or sensitive balsam. It is always desirable 
to have a pair of gloves with you on these long hills to pro- 



tect your hands from the horseflies when you are wheeling 
up. This Engelberg hill requires a very great amount of 
caution in riding down, for you will notice that its precipi- 
tous banks are shamefully unprotected, and a " shy " must 
not on any account be risked. Add to this that the thick 
woods not only keep the road damp and increase the chance 
of the machine slipping out of hand, but they also impede 
your prospect, so that you cannot alwajrs see what is coming 
up the road. On this account I should strongly urge a 
reference to your time-table, and a selection of time for the 
descent when the road will be clear. Very few carriage'B 
indeed frequent the road except as goingf to or coming from 
the Lucerne boats at Stanastad, so that it is easy for you to 
calculate the right time, and you will probably only meet a 
stray cart or two on the road. In ascending, I should not 
be in any hurry to mount ; yoii will come upon some cor- 
ners where you will think the hill is going to cease, but if 
you mount you will be disappointed. You must wait until 
you are well within the upland valley to which the hill leads, 
and can see a real bit of level before you. You can ride 
straight through the long straggling village up to the 
Benedictine Church and Monastery. Just before you come 
to this there is a sharp little pitch — ^gradient less than 1 in 
10, 1 should think, but surface excellent. It will amuse the 
natives to see you rash up this, but their amazement knows 
no bounds if they see you come down it again at snail's pace 
and almost stand still in the middle. There is a little hotel 
("Pension MuUer") close to the church at which you can 
lunch, or you can return to the hotel " Titlis," which you 
will have passed on your right, but do not order trout at 
the latter— they charged us at the rate of something like 
28 lOd a pound for them. As trout are plentiful in the 
stream close by they probably cost them 3d at the outside, 
cooking and sauce included. The " Sonuenberg," the 
largest and most frequented hotel, lies off the village on a 
hill to your left as you ride up the valley, and it is hardly 
worth your while diverging to that unless you are going to 
stay the night. The " Titlis " and " Spannort " are ascents 



Digitized by Vj^O^iC 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



requiring aze and boots ; but an excursion over the Joch 
Pass to the little mountain inn on the Engstlen Alp might 
be undertaken, and you could sleep there and return to the 
valley the next morning. The Joch Pass is 7200 feet high, 
nearly 4000 feet, therefore, above Engelberg ; from this 
there is a descent of about 1400 feet to the Engstlen Alp, 
which may be reached in about 4^ hours from Engelberg. 
Early in the season, that is about the end of June or 
beginning of July, it is worth while to take this walk for 
the sake of the wild flowers alone, which pass all conception 
in beauty or variety ; but then, as there will still be some 
snow to traverse, you ought to have boots. 

Those who like to attend a good musical service on 
Sunday I strongly recommend to do as I did two years ago, 
and ride up to Engelberg in the morning ; but an early 
start from Lucerne is required, as the service is at nine. 
There was a very fair orchestra, as well as a good organ 
well played, and some of the unaccompanied singing was 
excellent. Next after Dresden it was about the best 
service I have heard on the Continent, and quite surprising 
in a place so comparatively remote from the musical world 
as Engelberg. High Mass at Lucerne on the other hand 
should be carefully avoided. I went there this year in 
company with an Oxford musical friend (the new organist 
of Christchurch), and we were both agreed in our verdict 
that it was really a disgrace to the place. The Swiss are 
not at all an unmusical people. Nearly every village of any 
size has its Glee Club or Manner-chor, and there are con- 
stant competitions, the performances at which are as good as 
anything that is to be heard of the kind. There is, there- 
fore, plenty of material to provide a good service at Lucerne, 
if the necessary pains and trouble were taken about it. I 
would add another reason for visiting Engelberg church on 
a Sunday morning. The monks, to whom the whole valley 
from Stanz upwards belong, seem to have done their best 
to encourage the women to keep up the old fashions now so 
rapidly disappearing in other parts of Switzerland, and it is 
quite a sight to see one side of this large church filled by 
women in their extremely picturesque costumes bedizened 
with silver ornaments, often of the most exquisite workman- 
ship, and which have been handed down in the family from 
generation to generation, to the infinite credit of their 
present owners, who have been led to resist all the recent 
allurements of the collectors and " antiquity " shops. 

August, 1882. Gbraed F. Cobb,

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
WEST DISTRICT. 
Sib, — " Districtus " answers me seriatim, and with your 
permission so will I answer him. 

1. Symmetry. He sa3rs of 128 members north of the 
Thames there are 91 west of the dividing line. Hence 
three Districts in the west half of the Club. Quite so ; but 
similarly there are some 90 members south of the Thames, 
of whom 65 are in the east quarter, and this is considered 
no sufficient reason for creating an East District. It still 
appears, therefore, "inexplicable'' that there should be 
three Districts on the west of the line drawn north and 
south, and two only on the east. 

2. Attendance at Meets. " D. " says the river will 
always prove a rival to the road. No doubt it will ; but 
that is not a reason for making three instead of two 
Districts out of a diminished number of men, but rather 
the reverse. 

3. Racing. "D." sa3rs from personal experience that 
nearly all the W. woidd go to the S.W. I say from a 
study of the Blue Book that, (begging his pardon, it is not 
so), but that the W. would be nearly equally divided. 
Moreover, that though, as I said, the majority of racing 
men would go to the 8.W., it would not be so with the 
Oxford contingent, who I may be pardoned for saying are 
at least, at present, the pick of the lot. At the same time 
I am far from saying that when once we have obtained a 
geographically correct division we should alter it solely 
because it is not evenly populated. 

4. Accessibility of the present W.D. from both sides. 
" D." says that the meandering course of a river does not 
unfit it for performing the office of a boundary. I reply 
that it must be a question of the size of each separate 
" meander " that it makes, for he would hardly, I suppose, 
still consider it a satisfactory division if the loop to the 
south at Shepperton extended far enough in that direction 
to include Ripley in the N.W. District, as it easily might 
do. Shepperton itself, (at present in the N.W.), is about 
10 miles south of London. He adds that he did not know 
that the division between Districts ought to be impassable. 
Nor did I ; nor did I say it ought. I said that it could 
not be argued that it would not be possible for men to 
get to a District meeting place on the other side of the 
river, and that therefore it must be a boundary between 
two Districts," as it is possible so to argue with regard to the 
Lower Thames. That is to say, if there is an impassable 
line (as in the east), the Club must arrange that it runs 
between two Districts and not through one ; if there is not 
(as in the west), the Club is at liberty to fix its boundary 
line as seems best to itself. I am sorry not to have made 
myself clear on this point. 

The raiswh d'etre of the W. D. is, I am told, " that a 
number of members live more in the heart of London, and 



Google 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 71 



are consequently somewhat differently placed with regard 
to Meets from members of other Districts." I am not 
certain that I understand what is meant by this statement, 
but I take it that if they live " in the heart of London," 
it would not take them much longer to get out to the N. W. 
or S.W. than it would in a westerly direction, radii being 
of equal length. 

I am greatly obliged to " Districtus " for answering me on 
the seriatim system, as it renders it infinitely easier to say 
what one wants to say concisely, and it has helped me very 
greatly in answering his objections. He concludes with an 
insinuation that I want the meeting place altered because 
I am afraid of tram-lines. Will he excuse my saying that 
in the last five years I have ridden xather more than 
thirteen thousand miles, of which a great part has been 
done in the neighbourhood of London ; that I have never 
been thrown by a tram-line, nor in fiact have I fallen 
at all inside the four mile circle since I ran into the 
back of a "Royal Oak" 'bus in '78. Moreover, I have 
had some additional experience of tram-lines at Bath 
and Cambridge. I am sorry to be egotistical, especially 
as after sa3ang what I have, and can hardly hope to 
escape without " a smash " next time I am riding in 
London ; but I wish it to be seen that it is not on my 
own account that I desire a change. 

My second opponent is more difficult to deal with ; but 
I gather that the raison iStre of the west is " that a very 
considerable minority of the two districts resided at 
Kensington, Ghiswick, Acton, and Willesden, and that to 
attend their Meets it was necessary for them to go distances 
varyingPromfourtotenmiles." Thereare three points here : — 

1st. It is at present seven miles for members in London 
to get to the West Meets. 

2nd. A very considerable minority" of the S.E. live 
at Blackheath, but they get on, as I said above, without an 
East District. 

drd. The apologists disagree as to whether the W. D. 
was formed to suit suburban riders, or those " in the heart 
of London." There really seems to be some difficulty in 
sajdng why it was formed. 

The next argument is that the present attendance in the 
West is "satisfactory, remembering that we have fewer 
members in this District than any other save the S.W." 
Surely this is only one more reason for fasing the West 
in some such way as I have suggested for the benefit of 
its neighbours, especially as the attendance in the S.W. is 
hopelessly bad. Likewise it might be pointed out that at 
the time of the publication of the Blue Book for this year, 
the N.E. also was smaller than the W., though, perhaps, it 
may not be so now. 

Lastly, it appears to me that a compact big body such 
as the S.E. can as easily become personally acquainted as a 
compact little one. — ^Yours truly, J. S. Whatton. 

Trin. Coll., Camb. 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
Tlie sagacity of the mighty men of old who selected 
the L.B.C. uniform was enormous, if we judge them by the 
extent of the imitation, the sincerest flattery, according to 
the proverb, which their choice has received. First the 
Cyclists' Touring Club adopts a not dissimilar grey for its 
thousands of members, and now the whole British Army is 
to face the foe, clad in the same useful and inobtrusive 
colour. 

• «««•« 

The Tricycle Union has afforded a striking demonstration 
of the accuracy of those who said it was not wanted, by 
adopting en masse the " objects " of the B. U. as its own. 
What programme and what achievements Messrs. Browning, 
Bell, and Co., might have shown is, unfortunately, likely to 

remain in the regions of the " might have been." 

• ••••• 

Ooe hardly feels that the racing-man is abroad till the 
Oval has been crowded with enthusiastic supporters of the 
Surrey B.C., and next Saturday witnesses that great sight. 

Among recent performances of note are — 

106 miles in 12h. 28m. (riding time llh. 18m.), by a 
member of the London Tricycle Club on a 40- in. " Facile." 

100 miles in 9h. 22m. (riding time 8h. 52m.), on a 
"Premier Roadster" Tricycle, by A. Nixon of the L.T.C. 
The course was Upper Norwood to Brighton and back. I 
commend this to those who have, if ever, to fix a time 
limit for tricycles in the Road Trial. The first 60 miles 
were ridden in 4h. 19m. Mr. Nixon also lays claim to 48 
miles in 3h. 39m. on the same road. 

Howell won a Twenty Miles Professional Championship 
at Leicester in Ih. 5m. OJs. Contrary to usual professional 
tactics the winner made the pace and covered 5 miles 

in 15m. 14s. 

• ••••• 

I am indebted to our official Handicapper for the entries 
for the Surrey Ten Miles, which are as follows : — 

Crute, W. K. Adam, Thompson, Jephson, Prentice (of 
Ipswich), King (of Salisbury), Wyndham, Wilson, Lowndes, 
Vesey, Oaskell, and Moore of Birmingham. 

Oaskell is the present holder, but in the early spring it 
is almost impossible to give any opinion as to the winner. 
In last year's form Moore should win easily, but as it is, the 
race looks very open. 

Nearly 70 entries have been received for the Mile Handi- 
cap ; among these the most prominent are Moore, F. L. Adam, 
H. R. Reynolds, King, Wyndham, Thompson, W. K. Adam, 
Lawton, Perry, Crute, and Vesey. 

The new Alexandra Palace track is now open for bicyclists, 
and though the terms for the clubs seem very high, our 
N. W. members can get a month's practice for five shillings. 
We shall watch the N.W. team for the District Challenge 
Cup with interest. The Front Wheel Hub.

I hope in the excitement of Bath Monday our members

ride to Dunstable to meet the Universities on the Saturday 
after, May 19th. Every member of the Club ought to turn 
out then. Those who remember the bands, and the Sugar

that firuit-tart and cream) — are sure to come. 

The route taken by the N.B., N.W., and W. Districts 
from Eew to Halliford was first rate, but I cannot under- 
stand why the official arrangements were ignored. 

A lot of time was wasted at Kew by crossing and re- 
crossing the bridge, and discussing the merits of the 
various routes. I hope on the next occasion that the best 
road will be chosen beforehand, and that the arrangements 

made in the Gazette will be strictly adhered to.

it would have been dark before Halliford was reached.

the hard-training tendencies of the present as compared 
with the method followed by the grand olcT competitor of 
the past, who never thought about the Trial beforehand, 

and never rode more than once a week. 

• «•«•• 

The directions of the route published in this issue are no 
doubt very useful, and last year I took Rambler's advice 
and cut them out. Riding down Box Hill on Saturday 
afternoon, I was attempting to draw them from the recesses 
of my pocket when I ran against a loose stone and was shot 
over the handles, amidst a rattle of steel and some gentle 
remarks on Rambler. Poiioupinb.

NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for " Gazette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdays. 



THE CAPTAIN. 

Let it not be imagined from the title that we propose 
this week to give a racy sketch of the Captain at Home, in 
which such interesting details as the particular vintage or 
brand of cigars the great man affects are to be divulged to 
the Club at large. Far be it from us thus to pander to idle 
curiosity. Nor are we intending a eulogistic category of 
the different men who have in turn held the Captaincy 
from the archaic period when the honour of being Captain 
was actually ridden for, down to the present more en- 
lightened era ; for do not their merits collectively shine 
forth in the flourishing condition of the Club ? This is a 
panegyric worth more than any words of ours. 

In the Club generally little is known of the duties of the 
Captaincy. The gilded symbol of dignity is not un- 
frequently supposed to bring but few cares in its train. On 
some occasions, such as the Bace Meeting, the General 
Meets, the Captain has certainly to be present ; and at the 
Annual Dinner he is expected to make a bald statement of 
facts interesting to the company assembled. Then on 
Whit-Monday he is obliged to be down at Bath, and has to 
get up at some abnormally early hour in order to despatch 
a score or so of hare-brained enthusiasts who are bent on 
spending a summer holiday amid the turmoil of the Bath 



BNTS. 

PAOB 



ClubMeeta 76 

An Round the Wheel 76 

Bicjrciing in Switzerland 77 

Correspondence 

Quills ^ 80 

Racing Fixtures 80 



Bead, — though the time may come when the Captain may 
once more so far sink his dignity as to accompany, aye, 
and perhaps show the way to his men from Bath to the 
notorious ninth milestone. 

But these are only mere details, as one would say who 
knows anything of the work comprised in the management 
of a Club like ours, or of the stern discipline of the Com- 
mittee room ; who has seen the Captain s frown suppress 
at some grave discussion the indecorous frivolity of some 
irrepressible member, or has heard him elucidate some 
knotty point in the matter of Club tradition. In addition 
to this, the burden that falls on the Captain's shoulders in 
the way of correspondence is not an easy or a light one, 
such as is involved in arranging the General Meets, and 
the matches against Oxford and Cambridge. 

Without giving offence to the Captains of our Club, past, 
present, and future, we may say that the Captain is not 
merely an ornamental appendage to be picked up haphazard, 
for on him depends in no small degree the welfare of the 
whole community. In our humble opinion the model 
Captain should be conservative to the backbone ; he should 
have a keen zest for our sport itself ; like a Bayard, he 
should be ''sans peur" and ''sans reprocke;" and he 
should have the letters L.B.C., if not engraven on his heart, 
at any rate closely woven into his existence. 

As long as the Club proves itself, as it has ever done, 
capable of producing men of stuff like this, there will be 
no need for croakers to tell us that they see premonitory 
signs in the heavens of our coming decadence.

DUNSTABLE GENERAL MEET. 
To Meet the C.U.Bi.C, and O.U.BLC. ok May 19th. 

I hope all L.B.C. men will loyally support our Club on 
this occasion. The Universities are making efforts to secure 
large attendances, and it behoves each member to do his best 
to be present at this by far the more important Meet of the 
year. At our Dunstable Meet in 1881 with the Universities, 
we sent up 65 men, and I should rejoice to see this number 
beaten now. The Town Hall has been engaged, and since 
the Oxford and Cambridge men are not compelled to leato 
early, as was the case last year at St. Albans, it is expected 
that a very pleasant evening will be passed. 

Ashley W. Barrett, Captain. 



THE ROAD TRIAL. 
To Competitors. I shall be glad if they will inform the 
Hon. Sec. not later than Wednesday, May 9th, of 
the day on which they intend to reach Bath, in order 
tliat rooms may be secured at the "White Lion" as 
in former years. If possible a special van for bicycles 
shall be obtained in the 2.30 p.m. train from Paddiugton 
to Bath on Sunday, 13th, and I hope to meet all the com- 
petitors at the " White Lion " on Sunday evening, to draw 
for places and to receive final instructions. I shall be 
happy to convey a moderate amount of luggage from Bath 
to the hotel selected for tea on Monday evening (" The 
Milford Arms," Hounslow). AH luggage must be securely 
labelled at Bath. Members are reminded that a complete 
road -riding costume must be worn in the 7 rial, and the 
L.B.C. uniform is preferred by the Committee. Further, I 
would point out the undesirabiiity of members inviting 
riders outside the Club to ride up from Bath in the Trial 
with the competitors. Such a plan seems to add to the 
danger of interference on the part of the authorities, and 
appears moreover to be unattended with any practical good. 
The programme for Whit Monday will be as follows 

5.80 a.m To call. 

6.0 „ BxeakfMt.

6.0 p.m. Tea at " Milford Arms."

Hounslow. The course is as follows : —

Care must be exercised at these four situations between 
Bath and Melksham 2— 



A. — 2J miles from Bath the road forks. Take the left 
hand one (the right goes under a bridge). 

B. — 5 miles from Bath (in the village of Box) the road 
forks. Take the left hand one by the " Bear Inn." 

0. — 8| miles from Bath, at Gorsham, the road forks. 
Take the right hand one (the left goes to Chippenham). 

D.— 8| miles from Bath, at Corsham, keep straight on at 
cross roads (the light hand one goes to railway station), 
and 100 yards beyond the cross roads, on reaching some 
curious old almshouses, take the right hand road to Melk- 
ham (5 miles) ; the road straight on leads to Lacock. 

At six p.m. tea will be provided at the *' Milford Arms *' 
for 2s. 6d. each. Warm baths also will be obtainable at a 
charge of sixpence. 

To Timers. I shall be much obliged to any gentlemen 
who will volunteer to time the competitors, and to learn 
at which place they will undertake to be present— Devizes, 
Hungerford, Reading, Slough, are as yet unoccupied.

Newbury, and A. Henry at Maidenhead) Timers are re- 
quested to be at their stations, with any refreshments they 
may be good enough to provide, at least half an hour 
before the arrival of the first man, and are also requested 
to forward to me through the post their records and remarks 
by five p.m. on Tuesday, May 15th. 

AsHLET W. Babrett, Captain.

ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 
Our Annual Race Meeting is to take place at the Crystal 
Palace on Saturday, 16th June, which is a half-crown day. 
Tickets will be obtainable from members of the Committee 
at the reduced price of Is. 6d. The railway companies 
make no reduction on half-crown days, so that anybody 
getting their tickets for the Palace for that Saturday 
through us will save a shilling on each ticket. Members 
are particularly requested to make this known to their 
friends as mucb as possible. It has not yet been decided 
whether tickets will be sent round with tiie Gazette as here- 
tofore, or whether ladies' tickets will be supplied free. 
Notice of this will be given later on. 

As will be seen from the report of the Committee, it has 
been decided to substitute a Two Miles Open Handicap 
for a One Mile. The events will therefore stand as 
follows : — 

One Mile Members' Handicap. 

Two Miles Open Handicap. 

Two Miles Members' Scratch for Ladies' Challenge. 

Five Miles Open Scratch. 

Ten Miles Members' District Challenge. 
Entrance fee for the first four events 2s. 6d. each. No 
entrance fee for District Challenge. Entry forms will be 
sent round to every member later on. 

E. S. Price. 



THE ANNUAL RACE MEETING AT THE CRYSTAL 
PALACE ON SATURDAY, JUNE 16th. 

As the Committee intend to make the Race Meeting a 
success in every way, they trust members will exert them- 
selves vigorously to sell tickets in every direction among 
their friends, remembering that for every ticket sold a 
small profit accrues to the Club. Even those who are not 
specially interested in bicycling may bo glad to buy tickets 
when they learn that these can be obtained through our 
Hon. Sec. for Is. 6d. apiece upon what is ordinarily a half- 
crown day. 

By purchasing through this channel a gain of one shilling 
upon each ticket will be effected, and the ticket so obtained 
will admit to, the Palace and grounds as well as to our 
Race Meeting. Subjoined is a comparative statement of 
expenses : —

S.W. DISTRICT.-IMPORTANT NOTICE 
Next Saturday the run is to Thames Dittou to meet the 
S.E. and W., when I earnestly hope that we may bave as 
good a muster as that which gladdened us at Halliford. If

R. M. GRuaaEN, District Captain. 

SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
28th April. For the third time running the D.C. was 
late and Kelsey and Lloyd carried out the run to Havering 
alone. Leaving the corner in fair time and proceeding by 
Woodford Bridge, Chigwell Row, and Hog Hill, and reaching 
the " Orange Tree " at about 6.40. Precisely at the same 
time the belated D.C. left Whip's Cross, and hurrying over 
the ground reached the tea table just as the original pair 
were closing their meal. He had not however had any 
attention paid to his material wants before S. 6. Hough and 
£. D. Sewell turned up from a skirmish, the latter decidedly 
the worse for wear. [Jltimately the five started home 
together, and adopting the direct route as previously 
followed which was only a little more muddy than before, 
proceeded to their various destinations. Present : S. G. 
Hough, A. A. Kelsey, A. Lloyd, E. D. Sewell, and 

Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 



N.W. District. 

Saturday was a very uninviting day for riding, the roads 
being extremely heavy. Probably this reason, and also the 
fact that " Bath Day " is so near, which perhaps necessitates 
the whole District absenting itself from the run, may account 
for the miserable attendance. 

Only Tulloch and the D.C. left Hampstead together ; after 
bumping along to Hendon Station, Williams was met, and 
then the trio rode through the Eenton lanes to Harrow, 
where Williams and Tulloch turned back. The District 
then rode through Pinner, and arrived at Rickmansworth 
rather muddy, and very glad to meet the Captain, Meyer, 
and C. L. Hill. 

We were treated at the "Swan" in a very unusual 
manner, and some unpleasantness and incivility occurred. 
The next time we go to Rickmansworth we shall go to the 
" Dragon." The return home was " much enjoyed by all," 
the pace being very good, and we did not mind the rain. 
In fact it was only after we arrived at Euston that we got 
wet. Distance 18 miles. Present: C. L. Hill, Tulloch, 
Williams, and Palmer Dalton, District Captain. 



S.R District. 
In spite of the afternoon being rather threatening, eight 
men left Croydon on Saturday for Riverhead. On Hayes 
Common, Beck and the D.C. were met with, and at Locks

Bottom, Stephen Smith, with the Blackheath Division, were 
found waiting. A slight sprinkling of rain, and some big, 
black clouds drifting down with the wind gave the candi- 
dates for Bath honours an excellent excuse for putting on 
the pace, and the leaders managed to reach the " Amherst 
Anns" just as the rain began to come down in earnest. 
Fourteen men and one visitor sat down to an excellent tea, 
and after a pleasant evening we made the best of our way 
homewards through rain and mud. Seventeen men 
altogether attended the Meet^ and considering state of 
the weather and roads the number speaks well for the 
vitality of the District. Though still suffering from a 
bad knee, W. C. Oswald very pluckily came with us the 



whole way. Present : G. F. Beck, H. G. A. Brown, R. H. M.

Given fine weather, we ought to have the best Meet we 
have had this year at Thames Ditton next Saturday. Several 
of our racing members have promised to be present, and all 
those who had tea at the Swan " last season will, I am sure, 
be anxious to repeat the visit. The Croydon Division will 
leave the Fire Station at 4.30 sharp, and proceed by Ewell, 
where a short halt will be made at the " Glyn Arms " until 
a quarter past five.

May 12.— No Official Run. 



May 19.— DuNBTABLS. General Meet with the O.U.Bi.C. and C.IJ.BLC. 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
I have just seen the Meet Card of the C.U.BLC, ^vhich 
bears traces of the energy of the new captain. The runs 
include Haileybury, Baldock, and Bedford, and in several 
cases the men return by train. This ought to lead members 
to visit places some way from Cambridge, which, but for 
the additional interest of a Club Meet, they might not be 

energetic enough to do. 

• • • ♦ • • 

The Cambridge University Trials are fixed for May 26th ; 
the Inter-University Race being on June 5th at Oxford. 
The Club Races on Monday, June 11th, include an Invita- 
tion Race of five miles. 

• •«•«• 

The C.U.BIC. has purchased a double tricycle, the use of 
which is obtained either by an extra subscription, or by 
hire for the day. I suppose the next thing I shall hear of 

will be that the Inter- Varsity Sociable Race is coming off. 

• •«••• 

The Cambridge Committee are also encouraging rising 
talent by offering time-medals to members who have not 
ridden against Oxford. The two standards are 1 mile, 
3 min. ; 10 miles, 33 min. I hope the effect of this stroke 
of statesmanship will not be an L.B.C. defeat on the 9th of 
June. At present our t^am seems likely to be rather a 
good one. 

° • • • • • • 

Both at Stamford Bridge and the Oval the long start men 
seem to have had a benefit ou Saturday. At the Oval, 
only two out of the eight heats in the Mile were won by 
men with less than 100 yards start, the two lucky men 



being Tacagni, with 55 yards, and H. F. Wilson, of the 
Surrey, with 85 yards. The final was won rather easily 
by Hay, of the Queen's 'Cycling Club, with 135 yards, 
Wilson and Cassall (150 yards) of the Barretta B.C. — 
(Has this club anything to do with the Captain's family ?) — 
running a dead heat for second. Time, 3 min. 26| sec. 
Reynolds and Wyndham were the only two L.B.C. men 
riding. 

In the Ten Miles, the competitors soon tailed off. 
Wyndham made pace at first, and Jephson showed up 
prominently for the first three or four miles; but after 
half distance there were only four men in it, and in the 
last lap Gaskell, the holder, won by ten yards from Prentice 
of the Ipswich B.C., who had beaten Moore of Birmingham 
in the last 100 yards. Time, 42 min. 9} sec. Gaskell has 
now only to win the Cup at the Autumn Meeting to become 
its absolute owner. As usual, the spectators ran all over 
the track at the end of the race. It is a pity that this 
one flaw on a splendidly managed meeting cannot^ be 
removed. 

• •««•• 

At Stamford Bridge, Leeds, with 250 yards start, secured 
the Two Miles Handicap by two yards from Pemberton 
(280) in 6min. 3j8ec., Dundas (70) being third. 0. Thorn 
(140) was just beaten by Dundas for second place in his 
heat. Milner (scratch) and J. D. Butler (80) were among 
the unplaced men. 

The Fbont Wheel Hub.

BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(CONTINUED FBOK PAOB 70.) 

§ 16. — Other rides from Lucerne, 
I have given a somewhat vague title to this section, 
because the rides I have here to liiention are either (a) of 
insufficient importance to deserve separate treatment, or 
(b) roads of which I have had as yet no personal experience. 
I have described all possible rides in a southerly direction, 
except that an excursion might be made round the Alpnach 
Bay of the Lake by combining with the Bruuig road a 
road which leads back again from Sarnen, through Kerns 
to Stanz, distance 9 miles : from Sarnen there will be an 
ascent of about 420 feet to the highest point, about 3^ miles 
off, after which there is a gradual descent of 500 feet to 
Stanz ; but I cannot bear any personal testimony to the 
condition of the road. The only westerly route is the 
Emmenthal road (see § 8b), and easterly, the only road not 
already mentioned is one from Schwyz (or rather Ibach), 
following the course of the river into the Muotta Thai, a 
valley of reputed beauty ; but this, again, I have not had 
time to explore. But to the north and north-west there 
are plenty of roads affording, by means of cross roads, an 
endless variety of rounds. I have been along most of them, 
and they call for no particular comment. Thus a nice 
found (22 miles) may be made by going to the historical 
old town and lake of Sempach, by Neuenkirchen, and 
returning by Rain and Rothenburg. If more extensive 
expeditions are in request, you can ride* to Langenthal by 
the Emmenthal road, which you leave at Wohlhausen. 
Here you keep straight on instead of turning to the left, 
and take the road to Willisau and Huttwyl. From 
Langenthal you can return by Aarburg, Zofingen, and 
Sursee. The distance for the whole round is about 80 miles, 
but trains are available for any portions of the return 
journey. Again, there are two^main roads between Lucerne 
and Aarau, one (A) by Neuenkirchen, Sursee and Shoftland, 
and (B) the return by Granichen, Munster, Hildisrieden, 
and Rothenburg, about 30 miles each way. Aarau is 
150 feet or more lower than Lhcerne, ahd the highest point 
of road (A) is Neuenkirchen, which is 400 feet above Lucerne^ 
and of road (B) Hildisrieden, which is 750 feet above it. 
There are also three roads to Zurich, of which more 
hereafter. The bicyclist ought not, therefore, to be at any 
loss for rides from Lucerne. My last ride in 1880 was 
from Lucerne to Bale by the following route : 



Height

Having started all my luggage by the first train, I left 



Lucerne at 6.0 in the morning, reaching Bale at 12.20. 
I rested fifty minutes at Sursee for breakfast, and twenty 
minutes at Olten. I had twice to dismount and wait for 
trains at crossings, and had to walk up the greater part of 
the Hauenstein hill, so that the time taken in actual riding 
is suflScient testimony to the excellence of the road. I 
timed myself over 10 kilometres (i.^. 6 miles and 240 yards) 
between Uffikon and Zofingen, which I did in twenty-four 
minutes, passing each of the 5-kilometre posts in twelve 
minutes exactly. It gives one some notion of how modern 
locomotive improvements annihilate time and space, to 
think that one could start in the very centre of Swiss 
mountains for a day's bicycle excursion (as it were), and 
yet be in rooms in college, as I was, the next evening. 

Before passing on to Zurich I will just look at my note- 
book and see if there are any further points about Lucerne, 
mention of which is likely to prove serviceable to a fellow- 
bicyclist. There are baths in the Lake about quarter of a 
mile beyond the National — charge, 80 centimes ; they are 
one of the weak points of Lucerne, which certainly ought to 
possess better accommodation in this respect ; still you can 
swim at will out into the lake as they are not enclosed. 
There is a most astonishing variation of temperature in the 
Lake; on a warm still day the water will be tepid and 
enervating, whilst, should a sudden storm spring up in the 
night and stir the water up, you will find it the next morning 
as cold and bracing as you can desire. The baths in the 
river at the extreme end of the town, on the same side of it 
as the hotels are better fitted up and cheaper, and the water 
being in rapid motion is pleasanter, but the enclosure is 
somewhat shallow for diving. You cannot ride your bicycle 
to these as you can to the Lake baths. 

The ascent of Pilatus is a mountain excursion feasible for 
bicyclists by riding either to Hergiswyl, or Alpnacht ; but 
as taking your bicycle implies descending by the same route, 
I should counsel leaving it at home and going by boat to 
Alpnacht, and making the ascent from there, and descending 
to Hergiswyl, whence you would walk back again to 
Stanzstad to get the return boat. This is by far the best 
route to take as regards views, etc. The Rigi should be 
ascended in the train, but to walk down it on either side is, 
as the Germans say, " rewarding." There is a good path to 
the top of the Mythen above Schwyz. 

Whenever you have a few spare minutes on hand before 
or after a ride, whether at Lucerne or elsewhere, it is 
worth while remembering that nine horses out of every ten 
which are likely to be met on a Swiss excursion are sure at 
^ome time or other to be waiting in the ranks at the post 
office, the railway station, or steamboat stages. To take an 
occasional turn or two up and down in front of them is 
therefore an occupation which may be of general and lasting 
service both to bicyclists and carriage tourists, and should 
never be neglected when opportunity o£fers. There is ample 
scope for this at places like Lucerne, Zurich, Interlachen — 



Googl( 



Digitized by 



78 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



in fact at all important centres of tourist traffic. You can 
thus familiarise the Swiss horse ''in gross," instead of waiting 
to take him '' in detail." 

The view from the cloisters of the churchyard at Lucerne 
is very pretty, and you will moreover hear the organ to 
decidedly the best advantage from outside, (performance 
at 6.30 p.m.) ; but you must keep a careful look out on the 
pavement before you, as well as at the view, or you will be 
the innocent and unintentional means of giving seriou^ 
offence by treading on some wreath or upsetting some vase 
of flowers placed there in tender memory of the dead. 

You are not likely to be at Lucerne a week without coming 
in for some illuminations or displays of fireworks, either in 
Lucerne itself (most generally at the " Schweizerhof,") or at 
some of the big mountain hotels, such as the " Biirgenstock" 
or *' Rigi Kulm." They are really very creditable, and the 
effect of the lights on the lake is charming. 

§ 17. — Lucerne to Zurich, 

Zurich may be reached by one of three roads : — 

(A) By Cham, Metroenstetten, and Birmensdorf. 

(B) By Zug and the Albis Pass. 

(C) By Zug, Sihlbriicke, and Horgen. 

The Albis (B) may also be taken by diverging from (A) at 
Metmenstetten. I have not been over the Albis myself, 
but it is a recognised coach road and sure to be good ; it 
takes you, however, 300 feet higher than either of the 
other two. Reserving (C) for another section (see § 19), I 
will here describe (A). 

Height. Distance. 

Feet. Miles. 

1426 — Lucerne j « lox 

1356 18J Cham j 8 18). 

1640 ISi MetmenBtetten. 

1625 2H Affoltem. 

1750 24 BoDBtetten (torning short of) 

1500 27i Birmenadorf. 

1980 80i TopofHiU. 

1850 84 Zurich. 

Be careful not to miss your turn to the left just after 
crossing the river at Cham. At Metmenstetten keep 
straight on — the broad road leading up through the village 
to the right will take you to the Albis Pass. You fork to 
the left before reaching Bonstetten and cross the railway 
(bridge) ; it is a gentle downhill to Birmensdorf (where you 
have to bear to the right), but you will find the mount up 
again across a spur of the Uetliberg a little too long to 
ride. The map shows a straight cut on to this hill through 
Bonstetten and avoiding Birmensdorf, but from what I 
could see of this I do not think it would be any gain to 
take it as it is only a bye road. The hill down into Zurich 
is good riding. There are gravel as well as paved street^ 
in Zurich, and the latter in that portion of the town by 
which you enter were very good riding two years ago, but 
I regret to have to report that that street-Moloch, the 
tram-demon, is now in full diabolical possession. There 
are some good hotels close to the station at Zurich ; but 
the ''Baur au Lac" has the greatest attraction for me, 
being on the lake and close to the baths, which are about 



the best in Switzerland. The road passing it (the Thai Oasse) 
and leading by the Botanical Gardens into the Lowen Gasse 
(see plan of town in Baedeker), though forming two sides 
of a triangle, will be your best route to and from lake and 
station as there is neither tram nor pavement in it. 

Zurich is not a place where people stay for any length 
of time ; they come for the night and pass on. The way 
in which the omnibus service is managed at the " Baur au 
Lac," with the numbers constantly coming and going at all 
hours of the day, is most creditable ; equally so their 
unusual piece of hotel discipline in closing the doors of the 
Salle k Manger at table d'h6te, and not allowing you to 
take a seat at it if yon are late. . Their beefsteaks are the 
very best I have had anywhere abroad, and they have an 
excellent stock of Ghianti, of which one seldom tires. 
Baedeker stigmatises the Hotel as ''dear" — an unusual 
thing for him — but though their prices are those of a first- 
class hotel, I cannot say that, considering how good every- 
thing was, there was any charge that we found unreasonable, 
and we were there nearly a fortnight. In the little 
colonnaded square (the Central Hof) close by is a lunch 
shop for Swiss pastry and ices (J. Capaul), which would 
suit my friend the President down to the ground ; and I 
am sure if he had once been there he would always be off to 
Zurich for training purposes whenever a big match was 
impending. The chocolate (liquid) deserves special men- 
tion. There are railways in all directions from Zurich, and 
a double service of steamers, one for each side of the lake, 
with^ubsidiary boats plying backwards and forwards between 
the two ; so you have plenty of means of extending your 
bicycling range. Two facts about the railway require men- 
tion : — 1. If you wish to go anywhere beyond the Lake in 
a S.E. direction, e.g., to Glarus or Ragatz, you will always 
make a shorter journey uf it by taking the line through 
Horgen on the W. side of the lake instead of the older line 
by Rapperschwyl on .the other side. The two train services 
unite at Ziegelbriicke, and in returning you should be 
careful to ascertain at this junction whether it is necessary 
for you to change or not. 2. There is a second station at 
Zurich called Enge, on the Horgen line, which is much nearer 
the Baur au Lac end of the town than the big station, and a 
great deal of time may be saved by using it. Going along 
the Thai Gasse from the Baur au Lac, turn to your left 
across the bridge, after which take third turning to left 
(Alpen Strasse), then again turn to the right, and you will 
find the station a little way along on your left. In this way 
you reach it without having to cross the line ; whereas, if 
you followed round by the main street you might find the 
barrier up in view of the very train you wanted to catch. 
Good music is to be heard every evening at the Tonhalle, 
but in an overpowering atmosphere of supper and tobacco. 
In case of accidents there is a large wheelwright's establish- 
ment (I have forgotten the name) in the Wiesen Strasse, 
beyond the Tonhalle, and the Zuricbers are skilful mechanics.

§ 18. — Circuit of the Lake. 

This may be made in either direction, but the views are 
seen to best advantage if you go by the eastern and return 
by the western bank. You do not complete the entire 
circuit of the lake, as the extreme upper portion of it is now 
cut ofiT from the rest by a dam, along which railway and road 
are carried from Rapperschwyl to PfaiEkon. The roads, except 
the bit leading frt)m the dam into the main-road at Pfaffikon, 
are excellent throughout, and the changes of level are not 
worth noticing. The distances are — 





East Bank. 




West Bank.

There is a nice hotel at Wadensohwyl, the "Anger' 
(Engel), which is worth a visit if only to see their public 
room for dancing, etc., a fine large room with galleries all 
round. It will give you some notion of how the good folk 
of Zurich and the district enjoy themselves if you get hold 
of a number of the Weekly Sjurich Advertiser. I have 
forgotten what the paper calls itself, but it contains adver- 
tisements only. You will find there column after column 
of announcements of "soirees dansantes" and other 
musical entertainments at the various waterside hostelries 
all down the lake. The radiant prosperity of these villages 
on the lake, with their fine houses surrounded with fruit 
trees and flowers, is in itself a sight worth visiting Zurich 
for. Our ride round the lake would have been the most 
enjoyable ride of all but for a serious catastrophe in 
Uetikon, a village between Meilen and Manedorf. Here 
one of the tallest dogs I ever saw went for me and unfor- 
tunately got me, giving me a bite above the ankle and 
involving machine and rider in very forcible collision with 
the hard ground. Beyond scars and bruises seriously 
modifying my personal appearance in the direction of 
pugilistic colouring I happily suffered little, and though 
the shaking was a severe one I managed to complete the 
remaining 83 miles of the round. The rule is for all dogs 
in Switzerland to make for you ; but beyond sending your 
heart into your mouth when you feel the breath of their 
snarl on your calf, tbey hardly ever succeed in doing any 
harm, and after a ride or two you get perfectly callous 
about them. This dog who bit and upset me, as well as 
the dog who did not make for us on the Axeustrasse 
(see S 12), are the exceptions to prove the rule. This dog 
nuisance will, I suppose, die a natural death as bicycling 
increases there ; but for the present there is no help for it. 
No mechanical deterrent that you could take with you is 
worth the bother of carrjring. The best plan is to take no 
notice of the dog unless he seems to be getting angry, then 
you should turn round and fix your eye on him, at the 
same time treating him to some not too gentle objurgation. 



Very often he is satisfied when he has had auricular evi- 
dence that you are 'the master ' man, and not merely part 
of the machine. 
August, 1882. Gerabd P. Cobb.

THE SOUTH-WEST DISTRICT. 
To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir, — In perusing Mr. J. S. Whatton's exhaustive letter 
upon the above subject in the last issue of the Gazette, I 
was surprised to see the attempt he had made to add force 
and emphasis to his arguments by an unseemly indulgence 
in positive abuse, which might, if it were allowed to pass 
unnoticed, endanger the reputation of the S.W.D. There 
might perhaps have been an additional reason for the 
amalgamation of the S.W. with the W. District if Mr. 
Whatton's erroneous denunciation of our attendances as 
" hopelessly bad " were in any way correct. Compared 
with the attendances of last year in the W. they are much 
better, and as to there being anything " hopeless " about 
us, I must, in the interests of the District, altogether re- 
pudiate the statement Frivolous denunciations such as 
these might tend, if not modified and corrected, ^to mislead 
or perhaps dishearten even some of those of our own 
District, who must be better informed, but who, like myself, 
were doubtless unprepared for so startling an announce- 
ment. I must refer Mr. Whatton to the Blue Book for 
1883, recently issued, and there on page 26 he will find 
that we have given a better account of ourselves than his 
remarks might lead others to imagine. It will at once be 
seen that proportionately we compared exceedingly favour- 
ably, not alone with the W., but even also with the S.E.D. 
last year. We had during last season only 21 men in the 
S.W. District, as against 40 in the W. and 70 members in 
the S.E., when our average attendance at meets through- 
out the riding season is set down as 6 men per meet, as 
compared with 6.37 in the W. and 14.13 in the S.E.D. 
Now I would ask in all fairness, is this hopelessly bad ? " 
and does not this account of ourselves entitle us to take a 
place among the Districts of the Club ? We cannot be held 
responsible for the fact that so few members of the Club 
reside in the S.W.D. ; in this we must always take our 
chance, but we can and do encourage those who belong to 
it to come as often as possible to our runs ; and this our 
analysis abundantly proves. I may further add that our 
average this year up to the present time is very satisfactory, 
and I earnestly hope that so long as we can continue to 
render a good account of ourselves the " harebrained chatter 
of irresponsible frivolity" will not be continued, against 
us.— -Yours truly, R. M. Grugqen, District Captain. 
[Is R. M. 6. accurate in saying that the average attendance 
for 1882 of the S.W. is 6 men ; we understood that the 
nobler sex had a large share in the average. — ^Ed.]

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
SXEL, — I am sorry to see that the Committee have thought 
fit to reverse the decision of the Race Committee, viz., that 
the distance of the open handicap at our meeting on June 
16th should be one mile instead of two. Though I sympa- 
thise in the main with most of Mr. Scrutton's argument, I 
yet think that for this year at any rate the distance should 
be one mile instead of two, and I believe most of our racing 
members are of the same opinion. My reason is th' — at 
least two other meetings unfortunately clash w \ ^ on 
June 16th, and it is therefore douhlv -Vr-^ -u the 

distance of our open handicap should be the popoluar one 
of one mile and not the comparatively unpopular one of 
two miles. I fear that the action of the general Committee 
will cost us the loss of some twenty or thirty entries at 
least, and I appeal to them for this financial reason only to 
reconsider their decision. — Yours truly, 

H. R. Reynolds, Jun. 

18, Fenchurch Street, E.C., 
April 27th.

Members must not forget that Mr. Barrington, bicycle 
manufacturer, Bath, who has done good service to our men 
in the ; ^t, is prepared, if necessary, to get up at three 
o'clock on Monday morning to execute repairs.

opened from Hounslow. This may, perhaps, be found 
useful on Whit Monday. '

I heard fearful accounts at first of F. L. Adam's accident 
while practising at the Crystal Palace, but am glad to say

and that we may expect to see him on the wheel again before 
long.

From the Detroit Free Press uf April 28th, 1883— 
TWO STABLE REMARKS. 

BT 8. CONANT FOSTEB. 

He bridled up— in horsey tones 

Whipped out this bil-ter shaft : 
" He who on cycle tires his bones 

Is cranky, weak, and daft.'* 
" A trace of choler, sir, you show " — 

Thus spoke the man of steel : 
" Your sulky life is full of whoa, 

My cranky one of wheel." 



The N.E. D.C. is talking of resigning, for fear his per- 
sistent lateness should result in a more unpleasant species 
of retirement, but it is rumoured that the rules do not 

provide for ejecting a D.C.

and muddy (!yclists could hardly sympathise with. 

POECUPINB. 

RACING FIXTURES. 

Rule 40 enacts that no member represent the Club at any race meeting 
without permission from the Captain. The foUowing list of Racing 
Fixtures has been submitted to the Captain, who sanctions our 
members racing at ary of the meetings mentioned. Any member 
wishing to ride at any meeting not included in this list is requested 
to conmiunicate with the Captain. 

May 12. — Lincoln C. and F.C. Sports. 

14.— Scottish Games and Bicycle Races at Stam- 
ford Bridge. 
26.— C.U.Bi.C. University Trials, Cambridge. 
26.— L.A.C. at Stamford Bridge. 
26.— West Kent Races at Crystal Palace. 
June 2. — Surrey United at Crystal Palace. 
2. — ^Anfield B.C. Sports at Liverpool 
6. — Inter-University Races at Oxford. 
9. — Pickwick Races at the Crystal Palace. 
9.— C.U.Bi.C. V. L.B.C. at Cambridge. 
11.— C.U.Bi.C. Club Races, Cambridge.

THE SPORTING PROPHET. 

We have felt that the near approach of our Club " Derby 
Day/' and the publication of the entries for our annual per- 
formance of that thrilling tragedy *' Trial by Ordeal," de- 
mand some recognition of the interest taken by our members 
in the great event. We are quite aware that for some time 
past Club meets and a certain proportion of Club members 
have had no other subject of conversation than the pros- 
pects of success of this or that candidate, and the best way 
of training for and riding in the event in question. Pro- 
bably a certain number of members are, by this time, utterly 
sick of the whole topic, and long for Whit-Monday in order 
that the subject may, at any rate, be enlivened by the in- 
troduction of a discussion as to why so-and-so, who ought 
to have won, didn't win. [He never does win, so that dis- 
cussion is safe to ensue.] 

But though such clouds of words have encircled the sub- 
ject, after all, we find the most deplorable ignorance and 
confusion existing in the Club. No sooner has one man 
told us that it is a very easy year than another greets us 
with " Did you ever see such a warm set of entries ? " A 
third judicially remarks that it is an easy year for the ordi- 
nary medallist, but a hard fight for the enamelled. Our 
Editorial ear has received the names of at least ten certain 



winners, and of between twenty and thirty safe medallists, 
many of whom are also to ride first past the ninth milestone 
if only the wind will blow E. by N. i K, or the roads be 
four inches deep in mud. Our readers may judge of the 
prevailing errors when we tell them that even WE have been 
accused of being " the Dark Horse." 

To clear up this confusion we felt it our duty to engage 
at vast expense a sporting prophet to report on the situa- 
tion. We did not forget the chaTacter of his class for 
inaccuracy. We had read that classic simile to the eflfect 
that "judging of the winner of a race by consulting sport- 
ing prophets is like trying to tell the time by looking in a 
watchmaker's window;" we had heard of the indignant 
peroration of the orator, who, denouncing the enormous 
perversity in error of his opponent, wound up with the re- 
mark, " You're as wrong as— a sporting prophet." But we 
reflected that even certain inaccuracy has its advantages : 
the prophet usually hedges a little by picking three or four 
candidates for first honours, at least then his prediction 
would inform the Club of three or four men who would not 
win, and so lessen existing chaos. 

So we engaged our prophet, and he sent us daily reports 
of competitors; a little peculiar, but that was probably 
owing to his early training. Reports used to come in in 
this style : — 

" Walthamstow, 9 p.m. Sir,— North-East team did 
good work this morning ; Lloyd, Sewell, and the Houghs 
quick spin to Chelmsford and back, pulling up fresh. God- 
lee's other candidates rested to-day." [We have not been 



Google 



Digitized by 



82 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



able to disabuse him of the idea that the District Captains 
are the official trainers of their respective stables.] 

We have received a mass of reports similar to this, which 
it would take up too much of our space to publish. It is 
sufficient to say that we have a quantity of most interesting 
information about the health, dietary, and daily habits of 
a large number of members of the Club which will be iur- 
valuable to future biographers. Our prophet reported that 
he had much trouble in finding where the West candidates 
trained, or indeed in finding the West District at all, but 
that he had at last come upon traces of it at Oxford. 

We waited with interest for the final report which was 
to appear in this Gazette. We regret, however, that 
when it arrived it appeared on perusal so very unsatis- 
factory that we dared not submit it to our intelligent 
members. Our wary seer had hedged with such persistence 
by using, concerning every entry, phrases which could be 
quoted to show he had foretold the success of that 
candidate should he turn out to be the winner, that we 
reckoned up that he had provided for twenty-seven 
medals and nine enamelled medallists, with a last re- 
source" of four more members from whom " a surprise might 
come." And we felt it would be positively sinful to inflict 
such a prophecy on our readers, so we declined his article, 
referred him to our solicitor, the N. E. D. C, with the in- 
formation that we didn't know his address, but had heard 
he lived up four flights of stairs and knew he was a very 
violent man. If it is necessary to add anything to prove 
the wisdom of our course, we have only to add that our 
prophet displayed a most astonishing partiality to the S.E. 
candidates. Now it is well known to all impartial members of 
the other districts that, whatever social virtues the S.E. may 
possess, they are not much good at road-riding. The in- 
ference was obvious ; our prophet had been " got at ; " 
" nobbled ; " " hocussed," or whatever other sporting term 
is most correct — probably by the S. E. D. C. So we deter- 
mined to do our sporting prophecy ourselvea 

First, of course, there are the two enamellists. We have 
had entries from heroes of this class before, but this year 
the entries are believed to be in earnest ; and as it is not 
likely that they are made merely with a view to two blue 
and gold medals on the watchchain instead of one, we may 
expect to see record beaten. Both brothers are understood 
to be going very well, the Medallist of 1881 especially so, 
and thus we have at once two candidates for our discrimi- 
nating judgment to select from. But there is also G. F. Beck, 
a very worthy antagonist, and Hiudley, who as last year*3 

dark horse " caused much trepidation to intending medal- 
lists; while Hewitt and the North-East contingent are 
both dangerous. 

But this is very much in the style of our discarded 
prophet, and we are nothing if not precise and accurate in 
our selection. So, here goes— -But before we finally 
name the medallists in order, let us remark on the names 



we do not see in the list of entries. Where are Charles 
Newman and Barker, dead-heaters, who were relied upon 
as enamellists of future years ? Where is Douglas Smith, 
reported to be in such magnificent condition ? Why are 
there no Cambridge entries? Where is our President? 
We pause for a reply, and trust that during the pause the 
consciences of the ctdprits have been very uneasy. 

To our task. As to the order of the medallists, after 
careful consideration and mature deliberation, we have 
very little hesitation in confidently predicting that the 
ten medals will be won by the ten men whose names we are 
about to giv6. And first, there can be little doubt that the 
Londoners, eagerly watching the long piece of dusty road 
that leads up to the ninth milestone, will hail as the winner 
of the enamelled medal the well-known machine and 

familiar form of 

(To be eondudsd in our next.) 

COMMITTEE. 
At a meeting of the Committee, held on Monday, 7th 
inst., the following gentleman was elected a member of the 
Club :— 

Name and Addrew. Proposer. Seconder. 

DouglM McRaa, Homefield, Mit- 
diam Common Andrew Potter. J. F. Potter. 

Owing to a severe fall, which incapacitated him from 
riding, Mr. H. Ravenshaw was allowed to withdraw his 
name from the Club without being called upon to pay 
entrance fee or subscription. 

A letter was read from Palmer Dalton, resigning the 
post of N.W. District Captain owing to a severe attack of 
illness. The Committee expressed their regret, and re- 
quested K S. Price to take the post pro tern., and to take 
the necessary steps for calling a meeting of the district to 
elect a successor. 

The report of the Tricycle Sub-Committee was discussed 
at considerable length, and its clauses agreed to. Its 
adoption, further discussion, and fixing the date of the 
Special General Meeting were adjourned to next Committee 
Meeting. E. S. P&ioe. 

N.W. DISTRICT— SPECIAL. 

As will be seen from the Report of the Committee, Mr. 
Palmer Dalton has been compelled to resign the post of 
D.C., which I am sure we all deeply regret I have been 
requested to fill the post pro. tern,, and appeal to members 
to keep up the honour of the district during the interregnum. 
The Dunstable meet is at hand, and every member diould 
make a point of riding with his district on that day. 

Notice of meeting to elect a new D.C. will be sent round 
shortly. K S. Prioe. 

CHANGE OF ADDRESS. 
J. F. Marchant, ''Castle Hotel," East Molesey. 
William Silk, Jun*; 17, Church Row, Hampstead, N.W.

EDITORIAL NOTICE. 
I shall be away from London at Whitsuntide, and the 
Gazette will be "in commission" during my absence. 
Members will help " the staff" very materially if they will 
send in all communications respecting the Road Trial to 
the Captain as early as passible in order that the Gazette 
may reach members by the Saturday morning. This is 
specially important, as the Dunstable meet is on that 
Saturday. T. E. Sobtjtton, Editor. 



THE ROAD TRIAL. 
To Competitobs. A special van for bicycles has been 
obtained in the 2.30 p.m. train from Paddington to Bath 
on Sunday, 13th, and I hope to meet all the competitors 
at the " White Lion " on Sunday evening, to draw 
for places and to receive final instructions. I shall be 
happy to convey a moderate amount of luggage from Bath 
to the hotel selected for tea on Monday evening (" The 
Milford Arms/' Hounslow). AH luggage must be securely 
labelled at Bath. Members are reminded that a complete 
road-riding costume must be worn in the Trial, and the 
L.B.C. uniform is preferred by the Committee. Further, I 
would point out the undesirability of members inviting 
riders outside the Club to ride up from Bath in the Trial 
with the competitors. The programme for Whit-Monday 
will be as follows :— 

5.80 a.m. 

6.0 „ 

7.0 „

^.0 p.in.

Hounslow. The course is as follows : —

Care must be exercised at these four situations between 
Bath and Melksham : — 

miles from Bath the road forks. Take the left 
hasd one (the right goes under a bridge). 

B. — 5 miles from Bath (in the village of Box) the road 
forks. Take the left hand one by the " Bear Inn." 

C. — 8^ miles from Bath, at Corsham, the road forks. 
Take the right hand one (the left goes to Chippenham). 

D. ^8| miles from Bath, at Corsham, keep straight on at 
cro8B roads (the right hand one goes to railway station), 
and 100 yards beyond the cross roads, on reaching some 
curious old almshouses, take the right hand road to Melk- 
ham (5 miles) ; the road straight on leads to Lacock. 



At six p.m. tea will be provided at the " Milford Arms " 
for 2s. 6d. each. Warm baths also will be obtainable at a 
charge of sixpence. 

To TmEBS. I shall be much obliged to any gentlemen 
who will volunteer to time the competitors, and to learn 
at which place they will undertake to be present — ^Devizes, 
Hungerford, Beading, Slough, are as yet unoccupied. 
(Alfred Herbert will be at Marlboro' Hill, W. A. Smith at 
Newbury, and A. Henry at Maidenhead.) Timers are re- 
quested to be at their stations, with any refreshments they 
may be good enough to provide, at least half an hour 
before the arrival of the first man, and are also requested 
to. forward to me through the post their records and remarks 
by five p.m. on Tuesday, May 15th. 

AsHLET W. Babkett, Captain.
