DUNSTABLE GENERAL MEET 

With the C.U.Bi.C. and O.U.Bi.C. on May 19th. 

All Districts will rendezvous at 5.45 p.m. St. Albans by 
the "FeaHen." 

The N.E. meet at 3 p.m. sharp at top of Lea Bridge 
Road, and ride vid Wood Green to Tally Ho ! Comer, 
where they meet the N.W. 

The N.W. meet at 3.30 p.m. sharp at "Jack Straw's 
Castle" and join the N.E. at Tally Ho ! and ride with 
them to St. Albans. 



Uoogle" 



Digitized by 











84 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 






The S.E. will meet at East Croydon Station at 3.50 
sharp, and train from there by the 4.0 p.m. to St. Albans, 
via Willesden. 

The S. W. meet at the " Coach and Horses," Kew Bridge, 
at 3.0 p.m. sharp, and ride straight to St. Albans alone. 

The W. meet at 3.0 p.m. sharp, at Savernake Cottage 
(the usual rendezvous), and ride straight to St. Albans. 

The Club then rides to Duustable, where tea will be pro- 
vided at 2s. 6d. each, in the I'own Hall, for the three 
Clubs, at 7.0 p.m., by the landlord of the "Old Sugar 
Loaf}" at which place opportunities for washing will be 
provided for all those who like to avail themselves thereof. 
As the University Clubs have to leave about 8.15 p.m., it 
is important that every District should do its best to adhere 
to the official times; moreover, no District should wait 
more than ten minutes for another. As is usual in these 
cases, only one attendance can be scored at this Meet, 
which will be gained by any member riding to Dunstable. 

As some propose starting early, it is suggested that they 
should wait for the main body at St. Albans; and it is 
hoped that from this town to Dunstable (13 miles) our men 
will do their best to keep between the two " leaders" and 
the two " rear-men," so that the Club may enter the town 
in a compact body. 

Beds will be obtained in Dunstable by the landlord of 
the " Sugar Loaf" for any member who will communicate 
with me u6t later than Thursday, 17th. 

I hope every L.B.C. man will regard attendance at this 
Meet as not only a pleasure, but also as a duty that he 
owes to his Club. Ashley W. Barrett, Captain. 

ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 
Our Annual Bace Meeting is to take place at the Crystal 
Palace on Saturday, 16th June, which is a half-crown day. 
Tickets will be obtainable from members of the Committee 
at the reduced price of Is. 6d., which will admit to the 
Palace and Grounds for the whole of the day. The railway, 
companies make no reduction on half-crown days, so that any- 
body getting their tickets for the Palace for that Saturday 
through us will save a shilling on each ticket. Members 
are particularly requested to make this known to their 
friends as much as possible. It has been decided to send 
one free ticket to eat;h member. This, together with 
handbill and entry form will be sent round with the next 
Gazette. As the Club has to account to the Palace for 
every ticket, members not using their ticket are requested 
to return it to me as soon as possible. The events are : — 

One Mile Members' Handicap. 

Two Miles Open Handicap. 

Two Miles Members Scratch for Ladies Challenge. 

Five Miles Open Scratch. 

Ten Miles Members' District Challenge. 
Entrance fee for the first four events 2s. 6d. each. No 
entrance fee for District Challenge. 

E. S. Price, Hon. Sec. 


SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
May 5th. At last the D.C. was punctual and was 
rewarded by the company of five members and one visitor 
(on a tricycle) at Syborn's Corner. One or two others 
were coming but did not come to hand. The Higham Hill 
route was taken to Chingford, and the hill by the Church 
tempted Rucker and Weeks to prove they could ride up it, 
which they did, the remainder either could'nt or would'nt, or 
both. Up to this point we had been hampered by divers 
bicyclists, who had been inclined to mix themselves into our 
ranks, but they all seemed satisfied by Chingford. The 
stones on the Waltham road were very loose and though 
rideable in daylight were not rationally compassable without 
that feature. From Waltham Cross to Hoddesdon the high 
road was in really good order in spite of a certain amount 
of liead wind, and the latter point was reached about 6.0. 
W. Meyer and Baldwin had preceded the main body in 
reaching the "Salisbury Arms," and greeted us on our 
entrance to the town. Tea ordered, some active spirits 
suggested a run to Rye House while we were waiting, and 
five or six of the party went about a mile's run, picking up 
Arthur Lloyd by the way, who had been skirmishing. At 
the Rye House Rucker insisted on standing the company a 
few of the curiosities of the institution, and a dungeon with 
somebody's boots and a bed were carefully inspected. It is 
opined the proprietor would make a good thing of it in 
the season, yet at least one member who had not been 
there before expressed a certain indifference to ever going 
there again. Tea was very comfortably and cheaply sup- 
plied at the " Salisbury Arms," Lyne and Large making 
the party up to a dozen, and the peaceful pipe all too soon 
brought us to the time for returning. The same route was 
taken, and one or two lamps were perverse, Baldwin expe- 
riencing a series of falls in consequence, but very pluckily 
coming on in spite of his misfortunes to Chingford, where 
he took the train. The District kept all together, except 
Baldwin, into Walthamstow, Weeks surmounting the hill 
into Chingford, whieh was in capital order. Owing to some 
trifling delay on the way home, it was nearly eleven before 
the company parted. Present: H. Baldwin, T. Child, 
Kelsey, Large, Lloyd, Long, Lyne, W. S. Meyer (S.E.), 
Rucker, Weeks, P. T. Letchford (visitor), and 

Theo. GoDlee, District Captain. 
, It is well known that the attendances at N.E. Meets 
increase with the distance to be run. I hope therefore, that 
the whole district will come to Dunstable on the 19th. 

Thbo. Godlee, District Captain* 

W. District. 

May 5th. At the appointed time Leeds arrived at 
the starting place, but after waiting until 4 p.m., and no 
one else making an appearance, we made a start for Eew. 
Instead of taking the usual road to Richmond, we followed 


1 




' Digitized by V^OOQIC 





LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 85 



the one leading at the back of the houses in the Eew Road, 
thus avoiding the tram lines. Kiclimond Hill was in its 
usually wet and sticky state through tbe vigorous endeavours 
of the water carts to lay the dust. From Richmond we 
followed the road to Petersham and over Ham Common to 
Kingston. Here we met 0. Thorn, who had come by a 
different route. A little farther on G. Braby was sighted. 
Arriving at the " Swan " at Ditton, at about 5.15, we there 
found a goodly gathering of S. W. men. From the time we 
arrived until six o'clock tea, a continuous stream of S.E. 
men CBJsfe up one after another, as if each man had ridden 
from a different point of the compass. A fairly good tea 
was provided at the " Ship," and a fairly good price charged 
(2s. 9d.) — thirty-eight sitting down. Afterwards the men 
soon divided and went upon their various journeys ; some 
to Newbury, Godalming, Weybridge, etc., so that the return 
journey was only carried out by a few. Present : C. Braby, 
Jephson, Koch, Leeds, Naylor, Marchant, E. Scott, 
0. Thorn, F. M. Wimams,Hindley (N.W.), Newman (N.W.), 
R. Charsley (visitor), and 

H. C. PiaoTT, District Captain. 
I trust that our members will afford a convincing proof 
of their recent revival by attending the Inter- Varsity Meet at 
Dunstable next Saturday in their thousands." 

Haeold C. Pigott, District Captain. 



N.W. District. 
May 5th. This run at first seemed likely to be a failure, 
as two out of the six men who left Tally Ho ! turned back, 
and as the D.C.'s late experiments with his gigantic 
" Catapult," (so called from its power of propelling heavy 
bodies to a distance), unfortunately prevented him turning 
up, we were left without a leader. The result was that the 
ride became a series of short spurts, chiefly up hill, until 
Barnet was passed, when the Northern Guide led us through 
retired lanes which he seemed to regard with the pride of 
an owner who had spent both time and money in getting 
them into the perfect condition they were in, no path could 
have been better— result : racing. We soon got to Colney 
Heath and ordered tea for four ; just then five more men 
arrived, and we sat down to one of those pleasant feeds 
where the monotony of eggs and jam are relieved by a 
constant flow of nonsense and anecdote. One member, re- 
ferring to the comment in last week's Gazette, spun a yarn 
of the dark ages of the Club's history which is not generally 
known. 

When the grey uniform had been decided on in com- 
mittee, the question of the colour of the stockings was left 
to a sub-committee with instructions to get samples, pre- 
ferably of some quiet tint, in keeping with the taste of the 
members of the " Young Club." At the next meeting Jolly 
reported, with pride and satisfaction beaming in his face, 
that they had made a most happy selection, having 
been luc^ enough to get a pattern embodying the Club \ 



colours, and produced a pair of football stockings, striped 
blue, black, and red! The committee were speechless 
with dismay, and the captain had coughed once or twice 
preparatory to explaining, as gently as possible, that, to put 
it mildly, the choice was not quite what they expected, 
when the culprit, amidst roars of laughter from the other 
conspirators brought out another pair of the kind now 
used, and confessed that they had got the first pair on 
purpose to hear what we should say. After tea we started 
for home. But alas ! there were Bath Racers amongst us, 
and the pace varied in consequence. Up Eidge Hill it was 
decidedly hot, for which the blame attaches to Morris in 
the first instance. Lamps were then lighted, and it was 
agreed nem. dis. that we should finish the ride quietly. It 
has always been believed that the most conceited creature 
on this earth is an amateur tenor, but this was before the 
Otto Bicycle was invented, and any one who had seen two 
men on these machines riding past a lot of cyclists uphill in 
front of us would have said the A. T. was not in it. The 
discordant grunts of the foghorns they carried so exasperated 
one or two us that it became necessary to pass them sharply. 
Result : racing again, and also the premature dispersion of 
our members ; it is believed, however, that every one got 
home safely after as jolly a ride as it is possible to conceive. 
Present : Alison, Elgood, Hill, A. S. Morris, D. Langmore, 
Jackson, Stunt, W. J. Williams, TuUock, Howard, and 

T. G. Nevill. 



S.W. DiSTBICT. 



C. Newman and J. Scott were at Kingston Station at 
4.30 p.m. on Saturday last, where Ryan joined us shortly 
after this hour. With no distance before us, slow riding 
became the order of the afternoon, and we accordingly 
wended our way at a ateady pace through Surbiton and 
Long Ditton to our destination at " The Swan." Here we 
found Marchant had already arrived, and following his 
excellent example came Sewell and Bamett, who had 
effected a landing with comparative difficulty frt)m the 
Great Waterway hard by. Soon after our arrival, A. H. 
Eoch paid us a passing visit on his way to Godalming ; 
all our combined eloquence failed to persuade him to stay 
to tea. Coleman, who had taken more extensively to the 
road than ourselves, was the next visitor ; and to complete 
our numbers the Brothers Buckler arrived on the Sociable." 
At the animated tea meeting we had a goodly sprinkling of 
competitors for the coming Bath Trial, and as is usual on 
such occasions, most of the new medallists were picked out 
of the official list and arranged in the order of their coming ! 

Thirty-six members and two visitors took more or less 
active parts at this tea, which seemed to pass off in a most 
pleasing and generally satisfactory manner. There was a 
somewhat ominous thirst noticeable in one or two instances 
among Whit Monday x^ompetitors, which we must hope will 
be greatly modified by this last week's strict and careful

training ! The S. W. D. dispersed at intervals daring the 
eyeniog. 

Members present : Pern Coleman, J. F. Marchant, A. E. 
Buckler, H. Bamett, H. L. Ryan, and 

R. M. Gbuggen, District Captain. 

I hope that several of our members will make arrange- 
ments to run with me to Dunstable on the 19th inst., and 
to return the following day. 



S.E. District. 
Eight men left the trysting place on Saturday at 4.30 
for Thames Ditton. Coleman found us on the way, and a 
steady pace was kept up to Ewell, where Wyndham and 
Smither were waiting. Two or three men turned back 
here, and the others after a short rest started along the 
Kingston road. With a favouring breeze the Bath maniacs 
were not to be restrained, and scudded along at a rare pace 
until, after a warm twenty m\nutes without a check, they 
came in sight of the " Swan." The slower division, com- 
prised of men who were not to be tempted out of their 
usual pace by the impetuosity of their fellows, arrived at 
the river bank shortly afterwards. To them are the apo- 
logies of the D.C. due for the somewhat disorderly way in 
which the run was conducted. After next Monday our 
flyers will relapse into a comparatively torpid state, aud a 
quieter order of things will prevail. Stephen Smith was 
the sole representative of the Blackheath Division ; the 
rest, it is said, were harbouring their energies for a grand 
coup at Dunstable. The O'Reilly came up to the Meet 
from Brighton, and altogether nineteen South-Easters and 
two visitors sat down to tea with the West and South- West ; 



a respectable muster^ it is true, but considering the day 
and the distance, hardly up to some of our happiest efforts. 
Present: F. L. Adam, Barker, C. A. Brown, H. G. A. 
Brown, R. H. M. Francis, Alfred Herbert, 8. Herbert, The 
O'Reilly. J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, PauU, C. T. Schmet- 
tau, S. P. Smith, S. E. Whealler, F. H. Williams, W. 
Wyndham, J. B. Toung, aud two visitors (McCrae and 
A. L. Potter). R. 0. Faancis, District Captain. 



DUNSTABLE MEET. 
Stephen Smith will be riding to Dunstable on the 
19th inst., the day of the Oxford and Cambridge Meet, and 
has kindly invited any men who can take the day, to 
breakfast at his house at 9.30, and ride with him via 
Woolwich. 

Another party will leave Croydon Central Station at 
10.0 a.m., aud proceed via Kingston. 

The main body will meet at East Croydon Railway 
Station at 3.50 punctually, and book through to St. Albans 
by the 4.0 train which calls at Norwood Junction at 4.5., 
and arrives at St. Albans at 5.40. For those who prefer to 
go through town there are trains from Broad Street at 2.40 
and 3.28, arriving at High Bamet (on the Dunstable Road) 
at 3.15 and 4.14 respectively. There are also trains back 
to London from St. Albans at 9.5 and 10.0 p.m. 

In order that I may make the necessary arrangements, I 
shall be glad to hear from any men who propose going, as to 
the route they mean to adopt, and I hope on this occasion 
the South-East will show that to them distance and incon- 
venience are mere trifles compared with the honour of 
supporting their club. R. 0. Francis.

LAWN TENNIS. 

With the Captain's consent a tournament wiU again take 
place this year, and it is hoped that members will support 
it as much as possible. 

Several shillings are still owing from last season, consequent 
no entry will be accepted without it is accompanied by this 
trifle. 

By paying attention to the following remarks, competitors 
will greatly facilitate the efforts of the managers. 



a. The entries will close on Thursday 17th inst. 

b. The result of drawing will be published in the 
Gazette, and competitors mustmake their ownarrangements 
as to time and place of their matches, and each winner 
should return the result of his tie tvithout delay. 

c. About ten days will be allowed for each tie, and no 
excuse can be accepted for the non-playing of the sama 

All communications are to be addressed to H. N. Sharp, 
Elms Lea, Sutton, Surrey.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 87 



TRIALS OP THE TRIAL. 

A trial, indeed. A man never has any rest until he goes 
in for it ; and when he does, he is no happier. He fully 
persuades himself he could have done better had not some- 
thing happened that did happen, so if he does not win a 
medal he starts again to do so. On getting one he tries for 
the enamel, and even on securing that trophy he is not 
satisfied, and again starts (presumably to beat record). 

The intending competitor is made a butt for all kinds of 
advice as to when, where, and how to proceed. Then he is 
continually being told of tremendously hot men who are in 
training, but somehow never start. It is most amusing to 
hear the difference of opinion as to the quality of the 
entries. Possessor of a medal says he does not think much 
of them. Intending medallist does. Meanwhile the older 
members congratulate themselves on the " standard " being 
kept up. 

The complete list of entries now out shows how utterly 
useless it is to question members as to their intention on 
Whit-Monday. A good half declared in the early part of 
the year they would not start. Others avoid the question 
in different ways. One goes down to ride up with a friend, 
and will not try for a medal ; another to look over the 
course, so that he can start next year ; intending Enamel- 
lists only go down for the fun of Uie thing, and no one not 
already placed means to try for anything but the last 
medal. It would be very interesting iL we could get at 
what the men really do think of their chance, and how 
they feel on the Sunday and Monday. Anything but 
happy, if experience goes for much. On Sunday a few 
start from Paddington by the 2.30 train. The victims get 
in all the way down, and victims they certainly look. All 
try to seem light and careless in the attempt to keep up 
their spirits, but it is no use ; and soon all is gloom, varied 
only by glimpses of the road when the train passes any part 
of the course. Next the Oxford contingent get in, inspiring 
considerable awe by their apparent fitness. Timers, etc., 
meet the train at various stations, [and [rehearse giving 
refreshments so that they shall be perfect on the morrow. 
At last Bath is reached, and the men ahready there meet 
the train, looking at the new arrivals with a considerable 
amount of jealousy. It is surprising how very fit everyone 
thinks everybody else looks. Then there is a rush made by 
the men who have not already ordered bedrooms — dinner — 
drawing of places, and sweepstake— paying biUs and bed, 
some very early, others retiring with their own particular 
friend and comforting one another. Morning at last — will 
it be fine ? — ^which way is the wind ? — breakfast — ^packing up 
(how much will the Captain stand ?) provisions for journey — 
getting nervous — not at all improved by the fearful and 
hideous appliances some of the men have for supplying 
themselves with liquids — ^tremendous crowd at the bridge — 
more nervousnesft— fall in line — got a drain in front to 
mount over — sure to come a cropper in it — order to start — 



no more nervousness — why don't they go faster? — everyone 
frightened of Box Hill — one or two go wrong and have to 
dismount — all still holding back for dangerous comer — 
man who has warned us most goes down about twenty miles 
an hour — glad I did not — been told to keep on eating all 
the way up — try a biscuit — very dry — wish I had brought 
something to drink — ^Devizes — which is the way? — applause 
of yokels — Marlboro' Hill — been told, best, plan is to run 
up — try, but don't succeed — Savemake Forest — flock of 
sheep — fly in my eye — wish I had never started — ^if I can 
only get a medal, will never start again^ — Newbury — 
McGullum Hill all there — first man appears to have been 
a sort of comet — told it is not possible to catch him — 
disheartened — try to eat something — can't — try to drink, 
and succeed beyond landlord's fondest hope — start — 
Reading — strong contingent of L.B.C. men with milk — 
take everything that is offered — Maidenhead — ^look at 
Gazette — how many miles now — ^getting monotonous — 
wish they would not make parish boundaries so like mile- 
stones — get to Hounslow — milestones having a lark with 
me — still one mile — ninth milestone — ^Bath medallist, 
hurrah ! — go out of training at once. N. H.

How many men, hayiDg been asked by a competitor to 
pick out the winners of the ten medals have not included 
the questioner ? From notes taken at Ditton, I should say, 
none. 

#•••#• 

If Saturday was a sample of the energy usually displayed 

by ex-officers of the S.£. District, there need be no surprise 

at their large average attendances. Quite careless of the fact 

they were helping to cut their own records, the D. C.'s of 

1881 and 1882 attempted to appropriate every new arrival 

to the S.E. score. 

«•«•«« 

They had, however, a rival in their captain of 1880, who 
was pursuing the same tactics on behalf of the West — ^the 
district in which he now resides. 

Gonundmm. — A N. W. member attends by water a joint 
meet of W., S. W., S.E. Which D.C. should by rights score 
him. 

*««•#« 

Dunstable will be tnfite during the meet of the three Clubs 
on May 19th. The Town Hall, unlimited bunting, and the 
local band to play "the conquering hero, etc," as we enter 

the town, are among the good things promised. 

• «•••* 

As the Varsity Clubs have so short a time to stay after 

our arrival, it behoves our men to lose no time between St. 

Albans and the " Old Sugar Lclf," nor to prolong their 

toilettes unduly at the latter. 

« ♦ ♦ ♦ « * 

Our President has been appointed one of the Examiners 

for the Tyrrwhitt Hebrew Scholarship. 

«*•••* 

A member of the S.W. District was oiling his machine 

at Hampton Court one day last week, when one of the 

numerous photographers in the nbighbourhood came up to 

him and said, " Take your photograph, sir," " What ! on a 

Sunday," said the L.B.C. man, ",What do you take me for ! " 

" A shilling, sir," said the man.t-iari^ Bicyclist. 

• •«««« 

S. 6. Hough is looked upon as a dark horse in the N.E., 

and it is rumoured he will take a good place. 

«•••*• 

Lloyd is reckoned safe for a medal, barring accidents. 

• •«*«• 

What Harold Smith can do on the road is a problem of 
some difficulty in his own district, as he ^as never attended 
a Club run, but his achievements in the 10 miles at Stam. 
ford Bridge points to very good nme, at any rate, if the 
weather is good. 



A new feature in the Bath road is fifty yards of loose and 
unrideable gravel, which forms part of a level crossing 
just on the London s'de of Colnbrook. Competitors beware ! 

F. L. Adam's form on a Coventry Rotary Tricycle on 

Saturday was such as to make some of the two wheelers 

look to their merits. 

«*«««« 

I hear that one of the favourites for the Bath Stakes has 

been practising fast pedalling on a facile. He says that it 

is better even than a kme trainer. 

• ««««« 

On dit that the captain is anxiously looking to the Sutton 

and West Kent Race Meetings in this month to help him 

in his anxious task of picking the London teams for our 

contests with the Universities in June. 

Next in importance to authentic times for the start and 
finish of the Trial come accurate records from the timers on 
the road. It would be a good thing if we could get these from 
each ten miles of the course, but, failing that, they should 
come from at least six stations. Unfortunately, timing 
though very needful, is very arduous, and if a man is not a 
competitor, he needs must be a coach. I fear this year 
we shall miss the white mackintosh of the ubiquitous 
Chippindale. 

In a general way the American humourist was right when 

he advised not " to prophecy till you know ; " still, as my 

anonymous predecessor last year spotted the first man, I 

am encouraged to do what I can in this direction. Though 

the chapter of accidents in tlie Trial is so varied, the 

enamelled medal muht, I think, be given to H. R Reynolds 

who, with roads and weather in their present state, should 

reach the mile stone " not very much after three p.m. 

Beck will press him hard, but if the S.W. wind brings up 

much wet, will drop off before Reading. Such weather, 

however, would suit the old stayers. Brown, R, G. Francis, 

and Walter Meyer down to the ground. The team, all 

round, is a good one, but, ^lerh^s, is hardly so warm as 

some that have left Bath in past years. 

«««•*« 

It contains, I fear, one or two men whose training can 
hardly justify them in hoping to reach Hounslow the same 
day ; at least, if they manage so to do, none among the 
visitors to the Mile-stone will be more agreeably surprised 
than yours, Pobcupinb. 

Wanted. — Can any member suggest a suitable place for 
a small reading party in the summer ? The requirements 
are mountain scenery combined, if possible, with sea air, 
and not altogether iumassable roads, and plain accommoda- 
tion for five or six. Wales or North Devon preferred. Any 
hints will oblige, J. S. Whatton.

THE COMMITTEE. 

The sheltering protection of the Editorial " we " is our 
only excuse for venturing to discuss what may be called 
the motive power of the Club and our own masters. The 
diflSculty is further complicated by the fact that we are 
ourselves integral portions of the august assembly. With 
which apologies let us review ourselves collectively. 

The duties of the Committee are so very varied that we 
have no intention of going in for a catalogue, while the 
secrets of the Committee are so very profound that we have 
no intention of disclosing them, but we doubt whether 
many members are aware of the powers which they intrust 
to the Committee, or the amount of managing they them- 
selves require. The Committee are proud to believe that they 
each represent the whole Club, and not any one section of 
it, and the identity of the separate Districts is rapidly sunk 
in the general Club interests when they are gathered round 
the Council table, save on those special occasions when any 
information as to the views of a District on a subject speci- 
fically affecting it, is required. It is at Committee meetings, 
too, that the Captain has some of his most responsible and 
some of his pleasantest duties, perhaps, to perform. 
To control a party of fifteen to twenty men who have many 
common interests quite apart from the agenda paper, to 



induce them to apply themselves to the business in hand, 
and not to apply themselves to other business, is no light 
task, and one which may well tax the administrative ability 
of our leader. It may be a merely inattentive or supremely 
indififerent member ; alas, we have heard of such. It may 
be something very nearly approaching a " regular opposi- 
tion," who may be expected to have very defined opinions 
on every subject, and may be prepared to use every lawful 
means to attain their ends, and thirdly, there is the free 
lance quite incomprehensible, whilst through it all the 
Chairman must remember that all those round him may 
be safely assumed to have, in their own fashion, only one 
object, and that one object the good of the Club, which by 
one method or another they are trying with more or less 
energy to serve. 

And what do they do ? The question is often put and is 
sometimes hard to answer. Truly the dry reports in our 
columns would hardly justify a hardworking existence, and 
we have heard too of " autocratic " officers. Truly also it 
is easy to ridicule the ever recurring topic of dress which at times 
appear week after week on the notice paper ; but perhaps 
we may be allowed to point to results, and, while allowing 
all honour that is due to the officers on whom so much 
necessarily devolves, to claim that the work of the club is on 
the whole well done by those to whom it is deputed. The 
machinery works so well, there is so little jar or rattle that 
the careless observer almost forgets that the absence of 
fi^ction enables all the more work to be done, and fancies 
at times that elections, race meetings, club discipline^ our- 



Googh 



Digitized by 







90 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 




selves, club runs, and the curious medley of extra neglects 
which seem to keep up an unintermittent stream, work " by 
themselves " and without any motive power whatever. 

There is work and hard work to be done, and it is done, 
and the responsibilities of a member of a Committee do not 
always cease when he leaves the Committee room, but other 
detail duties are delegated to individuals to be carried out 
and completed, or it may be digested for the use of a 
future Committee Meeting. 

In conclusion, let us in the first place disclaim any personal 
allusion to any individual member in our remarks above, 
and if \^e seem to speak somewhat highly of a body of 
which we are a component part, let our readers remember 
that we cease for this purpose to be individuals, and assume 
a certain abstract condition from which we view even our- 
selves with philosophic impartiality. 


S.E DISTRICT.— IMPORTANT. 

Members of the District are reminded that according to 
the notice in last week's Gazette a detachment will leave 
Croydon Central Station at 10.0 a.m. for Dunstable. For 
the convenience of members of this or other districts who 
may wish to start early, and join on the way, the following 
times will be kept : iNonsuch Park gates (Kingston side) 
10.45, Hampton Court Bridge 11.45, West Drayton 1.0 

Members to wht)m the Woolwich route is more convenient 
are asked to bear in mind Stephen Smith's kind invitation 
to breakfast at his house, 9, Morden Road, at half-past nine. 

Several men have already announced their intention of 
travelling by the 4 o'clock from East Croydon to St. Albans, 
where they will meet the other Districts at 5.45, and ride 
on with them to Dunstable. 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 


DUNSTABLE GENERAL MEET 

With the C.U.Bi.C. and O.U.Bi.C. on Mat 19th. 

All Districts will rendezvous at 5.45 p.m. St. Albans by 
the "FeaHen." 

The N.E. meet at 3 p.m. sharp at top of Lea Bridge 
Road, and ride vid Wood Green to Tally Ho ! Comer, 
where they meet the N.W. 

The N.W. meet at 3.30 p.m. sharp at "Jack Straw's 
Castle " and join the N.E. at Tally Ho ! and ride with 
them to St. Albans. 

The S.E. will meet at East Croydon Station at 3.50» 
sharp, and train from there by the 4.0 p.m. to St. Albans, 
via Willesden. 

The S. W. meet at the " Coach and Horses," Kew Bridge, 
at 3.0 p.m. sharp, and ride straight to St. Albans alone. 

The W. meet at 3.0 p.m. sharp, at Savernake Cottage 
(the usual rendezvous), and ride straight to St. Albans. 

The Club then rides to Dunstable, where tea will be pro- 
vided at 28. 6d. each, in the Town Ball, for the three 
Clubs, at 7.0 p.m., by the landlord of the "Old Sugar 
Loaf," at which place opportunities for washing will be 
provided for all those who like to avail themselves thereof. 
As the University Clubs have to leave about 8.15 p.m., it 
is important that every District should do its best to adhere 
to the ofiicial times; moreover, no District should wait 
more than ten minutes for another. As is usual in these 
cases, only one attendance can be scored at this Meet, 
which will be gained by any member riding to Dunstable. 

As some propose starting early, it is suggested that they 
should wait for the main body at St. Albans ; and it is 
hoped that from this town to Dunstable (13 miles) our men 
will do their best to keep between the two "leaders" and 
the two " rear-men," so that the Club may enter the town 
in a compact body. 

. I hope every L.B.C. man will regard attendance at this 
Meet as not only a pleasure, but also as a duty that he 
owes to his Club. Ashley W. Babbett, Captain, 


ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 
Our Annual Race Meeting is to take place at the Crystal 
Palace on Saturday, 16th June, which is a half-crown day. 
Tickets are obtainable from members of the Committee 
at the reduced price of Is. 6d., which will admit to the 
Palace and Grounds for the whole of the day. The railway 
companies make no reduction on half-crown days,so that any- 
body getting their tickets for the Palace for this Saturday 
through us will save a shilling on each one. Members 
are particularly requested to make this known to all their 
friends. One free ticket, together with handbill and entry 
form, are sent round with this Gazette. As the Club has 
to account to the Palace for every ticket, members not using 
their ticket are requested to return it to me as soon as 
possible. The events are 

One Mile Members' Handicap. 

Two Miles Open Handicap. 

Two Miles Members' Scratch for Ladies' Challenge. 

Five Miles Open Scratch. 

Ten Miles Members' District Challenge. 
Entrance fee for the first four events 2s. 6d. each. 
Three ex-Captains of the Club have kindly offered to 
present the prizes for the Club Mile. Members should show 
their appreciation by entering for this event in force. 

E. S. Price, Hon. Sec. 

ROAD TRIAL. 
Again the success of the favourite has justified our 
predictions, and with roads in worse state than on Wiiit 
Monday in last year, we may regard his performance, 
although it was two minutes longer, as better than when lie 
won his enamelled medal twelve months ago. On the 
evening before the Trial the Captain on reaching the 
" White Lion " with Rucker and seven of the competitors, 
found the others with the The O'Reilly and Jephson awaiting 
them. After tea and the usual instnictions had been 







Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



91 



disposed of, a retirement was promptly made to prepare for 
early breakfasting. On Monday morning the hotel was left 
at 7 a.m., and a good start made at 7.30 a.m. punctually, 
with no mishaps. The O'Reilly and Rucker aiding very 
much in effecting it. 

The following shows the twenty-three starters, and their 
positions when the signal was given : 

Hawley. ^ Hewitt. 

0. Thoni. L. B. ReynoldB. 

Sewell Ryan. 

R. H. M. rrancis. Lloyd. 

R. M. Gruggen. Belfield. 

H. Smith. A. S. Morris. 

Hindley. H. R. Reynolds. 

Alison. W. K. Adam. 

Wyndliam. R. J. Hough. 

S. G. Hough. Wigan. 

Forster. Beck, 

Meyer. 

Of the non-starters Brown was prevented by illness, 
Pigott and R 6. Francis by the breaking down of their 
machines, and Weeks by a badly sprained wrist. 

The first times come from Marlboro' Hill, and are given 
by Alfred and Arthur Herbert, who report as below : — 

" Rain commenced falling at 7.30 a.m., and continued 
more or less till all the competitors had passed. They 
took up their usual station at the top of the hill at a quarter 
to ten, and at a few minutes after that hour a grey uniform 
appeared in sight coming swiftly towards the hill. As soon 
as the rider came within the range of practical eyesight he 
was observed to be carrjring a large black bottle, which was 
proof positive that he was not engaged in the struggle. It 
proved to be Jephson, who had trained from Bath, and had 
come to attend upon W. K. Adam. At a quarter past ten 
the first three men came into sight together, and L. B. 
Reynolds was the first to reach the top of the hill. Five 
yards behind came Harold Smith who was half-a-minute ahead 
of H. R. Reynolds, and in a few seconds Ryan came. An 
interval of two minutes took place, and then seven men 
arrived almost in a body, and the remainder followed very 
soon. The time between the passing of the first man and 
the last was 38mins. The men who were apparently most 
fresh were L. B. Reynolds, Hindley, and Wyndham, though 
many others appeared but little the worse for their 33 miles. 

Oliver Thorn was reported to have been upset in Marl- 
borough by a dog running into his wheel broadside, and all 
the starters were accounted for except A. S. Morris and 
C. Wigan, of whom no tidings could be obtained. The 
following is the order of arrival at the top of the hill : — 

L. B. Reynolds 10.19 

Harold Smiih 10.19

MoYris and Wigan were subsequently found comfortably 
seated in the train which passed through Newbury soon 
after two o'clock, having retired from the contest early. 
Oliver Thorn was with them, and seemed none the worse 
for his encounter with the dog, beyond a slight grazing of 
his knees." 

At Newbury, W. A. Smith was posted, and reports as 
follows : — 

" At the time at which the first man appeared last year, 
the narrow street in which the " Checquers " is situated 
was quite filled by a traction engine and four wagons, 
which however luckily went towards Dounington. Nearly 
half an hour later than the time at which Weeks put in an 
appearance in 1881, the brothers Reynolds came down the 
hill, H. R. slightly leading and rather bent over his 
handles, L. B. was sitting bolt upright, and both seemed 
very fre.sh ; H. R, who had arranged with me for a supply of 
food, etc., had them handed up to him as he rode by, but 
L. B. dismounted and lost a trifle over two minutes, and 
just before he mounted Harold Smith went by at a fast 
rate — these three thus early in the trial having gained at 
least one mile upon the other competitors. The next to 
appear was W. E. Adam, with Jephson in attendance. 
While he was eating a hearty lunch. Hough, Beck, and 
Ryan came in, and the latter, after a hasty lunch, went on 
again. Beck here gave up the contest on finding that the 
brothers Reynolds were twelve minutes ahead, while Hough 
took twelve minutes to lunch, during which time Lloyd^ 
Sewell, and Hifidley came in together; the former stayed 
to luiich, but the latter, after getting a fresh relay of pro- 
visions, rode away in fine condition, coached as far as 
Reading by Mr. Hollands, the crack local rider. Sewell 
then went away, followed by Waddy Wyndham, who rode 
through without stopping in capital form. Hawley was the 
next at 11.57, and immediately after his arrival Lloyd and 
S. Hough left together. Then R. H. M. Francis, Hewitt, 
and Meyer followed in rapid succession, and all stayed a 
few minutes. Belfield and Forster rode through without 
stopping, and then came Alison, who went on again after 
having had his hind wheel attended to. Gruggen came in 
rather tired at 12.57 ; Beck and Hough were very smart in 
oiling machines, etc."

Beck, R. B. Hough, Morris, Thorn, and Wigan trained up 
the latter part of the course ; Meyer and Gruggen rode 
through very pluckily, and both reached Hounslow after 
5.30 p.m., looking as though they done a hard day s work. 

The men, on their arrival at Hounslow, adjoume^ to the 
" Milford Arms," where tea was provided. The house has 
again come into fresh hands, and though the landlord showed 
great desire to provide for our comfort, he was perhaps not 
quite so successful as his predecessor last year. Possibly 
his inexperience of hungry men, fresh from a hundred miles 
ride, prevented him from rightly gauging our appetites. 
A serious hitch, too, occurred in the conveyance of the 
luggage from Bath ; it should have reached Brentford, to 
which it was forwarded from Paddington, at 3.10 o'clock ; 
but on arrival of the train, the two large sacksful of goods 
and chattels were not forthcoming to the driver of the 
conveyance sent for them by the landlord of the " Milford 
Arms." Much disappointment resulted; but the good 
humour of the new Medallists was contagious, and the 
suflferers were more lenient with the poor Captain than he 
had any right to expect. The missing bags were found by 
him, after the dispatching of letters and telegrams right 
and left in every direction, about seven p.m. at Brentford 
Station, which place they reached shortly before that hour. 

The wind during the day was light, from the S. andS.W., 
and weather fine though rather close. Roads near Bath 
rough, sticky, and in places greasy. On the Downs the 
travelling was very good ; from Reading to Hounslow it was 
fairly good in the centre, though very rough at the sides. 
Near Colnbrook, 80 yards of soft ground were walked by 
nearly all. At Bath our thanks were due to Mr. Penner 
for his attention to our comfort ; probably at no hotel in 
the country do our men ever meet with more uniform 
courtesy and consideration. 

To the Timers— Alfred Herbert, W. A. Smith, Koch, and 
A. Henry— our thanks are certainly due for their self-denying 
and most valuable work. They are requested to tell the

Google

CLUB GAZETTE.

Captain of the expenses they may have incurred in connection 
with their commissariat. The latter on his own account 
wishes also to thank all the competitors for their kindness 
in helping him to carry through the various duties connected 
with the Trial of 1883. 

NOTICE TO COMPETITORS. 
By an accident, which I very much regret, the luggage 
sent by me on Whit-Monday from Paddington to Brentford 
direct did not reach the latter place till 6.30 p.m. There 
I found it at 7 o'clock, and as most of the men liad then 
left the " Milford Arms " I thought it best to bring it up 
to town, where it now awaits its owners who can receive 
their property by calling at any time at the address 
below. If preferred I will forward any asticles on receiving 
instructions. 

As two men left Newbury without paying for their dinners 
I shall be glad if they and any others who may have done so, 
will remit the amounts to W. A. Smith, Savernake Cottage, 
Acton, who kindly settled with the landlord of the " Chequers.' 

Ashley W. Barrett, Captain, 

* 42, Finsbury Square, RC. 

NOTES BY THE NINTH MILESTONE. 
At last my inborn merit has been recognised, and I have 
been requisitioned as special correspondent by the Editor. 
Another proof, this, of his extraordinary talents — not one 
of the members who had previously held the reins of this 
largely circulating journal having had the discernment to 
select one who is admirably placed, and who has seen all 
the finishes of the previous trials save one, when from cir- 
cumstances over which he had no control, or, in other words^ 
because Theo. Godlee would sit on him, he was unable to 
see A. D. Butler do/he best and least rewarded performance 
yet accomplished on the via sacra. 

The first occurence worthy of note was the arrival of an 
important officer, whose head-gear was of an sBsthetic kind. 
Deep was the pang I felt for him, which was rivalled by the 
ostler of a neighbouring pub., whose inclinations had led him 
to adopt the same description of tile, not, alas, of the same 
subdued colour, but of a vivid blue, bound about with 
scarlet. 

# « • * • « 

It was curious to note the interest taken in the event by 
the inhabitants of Hounslow. On H. R. Reynolds riding 
in with his well known dash, in the good time of 7h. 28m.^ 
one of the local magnates informed an admiring audience 
" That the time was two minutes longer than last year." 
Another gave out that the prizes were only medals, and 
that the real object of the competitors was to reap honour 
for the Club," and the words " They are the London Bicycle 

Club," were frequently repeated in explanatory whispers. 

• * • * « « 

The approbation of the bystanders, however, appeared to 



be confined to the L.B.C., as every other bicyclist was the 
target for a good deal of chaflF. " You'll win, sir," ejaculated 
an irrepressible yokel to a rider adorned with gold lace, etc. 
• *«*•• 

As the time drew near when the first arrivals were ex- 
pected, there was a good sprinkling of the fair sex anxiously 
awaiting the hero of the day. No less than three were on 
tricycles ; indeed, I never saw so many ladies riding, as 
several others passed without stopping. 

#••«»• 

A good many L.B.O. men were at the winning post. I 
noticed also Mr. Rucker, senior, and Mr. Hough, senior. 
«««««« 

Of the racing itself you will no doubt be informed by a 
less stationary person than myself, for after all a Milestone 
is, for reasons that can be easily understood, somewhat 
limited in his experience of this subject ; but I may point 
out that the Reynolds Brothers made a close race of it for 
the enamelled, riding together for 95 miles ; that Harold 
Smith spurted in gamely; that Wjmdham's success was 
most popular; that Adam was only thirty-five seconds 
behind Master Waddy ; that Hough looked fit ; that the 
Dead Heat was cleverly managed; and that Hindley, 
Ryan, and Hewitt were close together. 

Ten minutes after the last medal was gone, in rode 
R. H. M. Francis. I always feel sorry for the eleventh 
man, especially when he makes such good time as 8.47. 
However, he can console himself with the thought that it 
takes a very good man to ride 100 miles in that time, and 
that if he had not stopped quite so long on the road he 
might have been better placed. 

««•«•• 

But the most unfortunate man was Figott, who broke 
his bi., and missed the train to Bath by three minutea 

And now I must make up my mind to another wearisome 
period of some twelve months before I can again have the 
excitement of seeing another finish in the Bath Trial A 
bitter thought this is to your correspondent. 

The Ninth Milestone. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir,— I beg at the instance of the South-West D.C. to 
withdraw tlie expression " hopelessly bad " as applied to 
the average attendance of his District. I had not calcu- 
lated this year s average, and must have been led to 
imagine it worse than it really is by reading such accounts 
in the Gazette as " Present : H. L. Ryan and A. E. 
Buckler " ; " Present : the following four members " ; 
" Mustered three strong," etc. As to the comparison of 
last year's attendance with that of the South-East, not 
only should the Editorial foot note concerning the three 



Digitized by 



94 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



picnics not be forgotten, but it should be noticed further 
that throughout the year the S.W. include in their calcu- 
lations members from other Districts, and outsiders, while 
the S.E., who were favoured with the company of thirty-one 
visitors, are content to base their averages upon the 
number present of their own members, and to include no 
one. 

But as I have already had occasion several times to 
remark, the question of attendance is only a side issue ; for 
even if every member in the West and South West did his 
duty always in the matter of Club runs, I should still think 
it advisable to dismember the West. 

I don't, sir, blame anyone for the existing state of things, 
and I don't forget the irresistible attraction of the river ; I 
only submit that this quarter of the club is not as prosperous 
as it might be, and I bring under the notice of your readers 
a scheme by which it might in my humble opinion be 
bettered, and — 

The sole drift of my purpose doth extend 
Not a frown further. 

And now, sir, as we have reached, ad I apprehend that 
we have, the conclusion of this discussion, let me pass for a 
moment to an entirely different subject. I wish, as the 
seconder of the motion which changed the locale of our 
Annual Race Meeting, to be allowed, as the mover already 
has done, to record my opinion that to keep to a two mile 
handicap in the face of five other meetings with open events 
in the London neighbourhood is absolutely suicidal, and to 
express a hope that we at all events shall not be held 
responsible for an empty card and an empty cash box. — 
Yours truly, J. S. Whatton. 

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
What is the new name of the Bicycle Union to be? 
That is the all-important question to be decided at the 
next Council Meeting on the 14th June. The following 
names have been suggested : Bicycle and Tricycle Union ; 
'Cyclists*, or Cycle Union ; Wheelmen's Union ; British 
Union of Wheelmen ; Wheelmen's Association. 

• ♦ * ♦ * « • 

It is said that the first name is hardly comprehensive 
enough, as riders of other machines than bicycles and 
tricycles are likely to increase— quadricycles, " Otto's," etc. 
Some critics think the second name savours to much of 
slang to be acceptable, while others maintain "cycling," 
not " 'cycling," to be a perfectly legitimate word, at least 
as good as the hybrids — bicycle or tricycle. Others again 
advocate one of the last three names. For these, it is said, 
the two first would enable us to retain the name by which 
the B.U. is at present well known, the second even allowing 
us still to use the present contraction. 

*•«««« 

It has been objected in some quarters that the word 
wheelmen " is an Americanism, and therefore unfit for 



English use. But it is an Americanism which will bear 
analysis with an entirely favourable result. It is an excel- 
lent, descriptive Saxon compound which would probably 
meet with the approval of Professor Skeat. Of course, 
there is also the question — Is a new name necessary at all ? 
No doubt, if the founders of the Union were now naming 
it for the first time, they would probably not choose a name 
limited to one branch of the sport ; but still, many people 
may think it as undesirable to change the name of an 
athletic association as that of a commercial firm. I am 
sure our members would rather die on the field," or in 
any other equally convenient place, than change the beloved 
letters L.B.C. (which, according to a recent leader, are as a 
strawberry mark on the left arm of every L.B.C. man), in 
order to " encourage " any amount of tricycling. But we 
shall see what we shall see. I hope those of our members 
who will have to vote on the knotty point in June will give 
the subject a calm and dispassionate consideration, and take 
such action as shall seem fit under the circumstances. 
#••#•• 

The London Tricycle Club held their 100 Mile Road' Race 
on Monday, the ride being from Boston (Market-place) to 
Bell Bar, near Hatfield. Both wind and weather were 
against the competitors, and the roads were also rough. 
The start took place at 6.30. Nixon took the lead early 
in the race, and reached the half-distance in 4 hours 
53 minutes, being then 28 minutes ahead of Rising, and, 
gradually increasing his lead, reached the winning post in 
10 hours 38 minutes ; Rising came in second, 56 minutes 
later, Salmon and Radford (one of our members) riding in 
together half an hour later. 

The Ten Miles Tricycle record was upset at Leicester on 
Tuesday by Lowndes, who won by abdftt three-quarters of 
a mile from Moore, in the splendid time of 34 mins. 3 sees. 
Vesey was third, 8 sees, behind Moore. 

««*«*« 

H. West, of Bristol, cleared the board at the Plymouth 
B.C. Races at Devonport on Monday. He won no less than 
four races, and left some good men behind him. 

The Front Wheel Hub.

QUILLS. 

" Aristotle and Pliny deliver it that the poipentine casteth off his quills 
when he would.attack, or defend himself, but Diosoorides affirmeth it a 
point of folly to believ»it."'Sir T. Bbownx. 

Out of the ten medals, four went to the N.E., three to 
the S.E., and one to each of the other Divisions. 

«•«#•# 

The S.K however carried off the pahn, as the first, 
second, fourth, and fifth arrivals were members of the 
" big district." 

♦ »•♦•• 

Bravo, Waddy ! Judgment and pluck, my boy ! 
«••••* 

I hope that R. H. M. Francis, the " poor dog," will accept 
my sympathy, especially as he also ran close to a medal 
last year in spite of a bad fall. 

*#«««• 

There were no less than four different timers at various 
points in Reading, one of whom was Hampton Roberts. 

• ««••• 

Pour or five wags presented "Bath Oliver" with biscuits, 
synonymous with his sobriquet, at the "White Lion." 
They were pocketed with good grace, and further instal- 
ments will be received. 



A buckled wheel did not seem to disconcert the Hon. Sec. 
His face, beaming with delight from the top of a cart, quite 
refreshed the competitors and varied the monotony of the 
last ten miles. 

E. T. Sachs must look to his laurels. A rival conjuror 
entertained the competitors who went down by the five o'clock 
train on Saturday. 

«•»««* 

Two of the most striking objects on the road were the 
Zulu's bull-poop, and the Captain's 7am o' Chanter. 
««««•« 

The latter was, I hear, donned as a feeler in view of a 
coming change in our head-gear. 

«•«•»• 

The office of Bath-coach is no sinecure, I can tell you ; 
what with spurting on ahead for drinks, and after paying (?) 
to find your man nearly a mile away. I am sure no candi- 
date was more pleased at the sight of the ninth milestone 
th^n was I ! 

To a youthful aspirant to Bath fame well up at 
Marlborough Hill : 

MacU, puer, viHute nova sic itur ad Hodnslow. 

{Virffil, with variations.)

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Jephson did as good a day's work as anyone. After 

having retired to bed in the small hours of Monday morning 

he got up at about four o'clock and went down toBarrington's. 

After breakfast he took tlie train to Marlborough, and from 

there he coached W. K. Adam for the remaining 66 miles 

to the finish. Those who are unacquainted with the duties 

of this office can form but a small iJea of what it means, 

but when I tell them that one who performed them from 

Reading assured me that he would never do it again, they 

will appreciate the magnitude of Jephson's achievement. 
*«*«•« 

Another member was not content with coaching, and 
thoroughly attending to the wants, of two competitors 
from Maiaenhead, but he also provided oranges for two 
others, one of whom was a quarter of a mile in front. 

Was it admiration of the individuals or a general idea of 
the superiority of man that made the verger's wife of St. 
Mary's, Bath, make two young ladies give up their places to 
two of our members on Sunday evening last wnen the 
church was full? Needless to say that L.B.C. indignation 
was aroused and could but be satisfied by full apologies to 
the fair occupiers of the seats ; and our West member 
declares that the sweet " Certainly !" he received in reply 
made him forget the Bath Trial for the rest of the evening, 

and that is saying a great deal. 

• «««*» 

It was verily a sight to see Jephson and Wigan racing on 
a track fifty rounds to the mile the day before the Trial 

at Bath. Jephson says he did not like the corners. 

• «««*« 

The thanks of several of the competitors are due to The 
O'Reilly for the kind manner in which he saw to their 
machines. 

• ••«*• 

The manner in which bread and milk was indulged in at 
Bath shows that there are still ni^ny who cling to the 

dishes of their early years. 

• • • ♦ « • 

The longing look the Captain gave the men at the st^rt 
showed that although he followed ti e precedent of former 
years his heart was still in the Race in which he has com- 
peted for the last three years. 

» 1 • • • ♦ 

Should there be any members who have doubts as to the 
meaning of club-feeling I can strongly recommend them to 
ride in our Road Trial, and if they don't find it out then — 

they never will. 

• • ♦ ♦ ♦ 

H. R. Reynolds' times compared with last year show the 
following results. Owing to the bad surface to Devizes he 
reached Marlboro' Hill 7 minutes later than last year. 
Newbury was reached 10 minutes 4Q seconds later than the 

Erevious occasion, the roads being soCt in parts. At Reading 
e was 7^ minutes behind, but he arrived at Maidenhead 

2 minutes earlier than last year. 

• «*•«• 

Probably there is no more popular medal than that of 

Wyndham's. And none of the unsuccessful have more of 

the sympathies of members than Gru^gen and Alison. Their 

plucky persistent efforts will, I hope in a future year, if they 

run again, meet with the success they deserve. At any rate 

they entitle them at present to our respect. 

''^ • • • • * 

The usual Sweepstake at Bath resulted in giving the first 
prize, value £2 10s, to E. D. Sewell who drew H. R. Reynolds, 
the second, value £1, to Hindley who drew L. B. Reynolds^ 



and the third, value 12s 6d, to Jephson who drew H. Smith. 

Thirty-three men took shares (2s 6d each), and it contained 

four blanks which fell to Wigan, F. M. Williams, Baldwin, 

and A. S. Morris. 

«•*•«• 

This is the fourth year that Marlboro' Hill has been taken 
by the Herberts. The thanks of the Club are certainly 
their due for the self-denying work that this entails. Esprit 
de corps must still be lurking somewhere among us. 

Bath Sunday is usually a time of tribulation to compe- 
titors. Waddy, however, whose old racing habits had led 
him to discard nervousness, seemed to regard it as one of 
rejoicing, and his pleasant smile went a long way to cheer 
his companions. H. R. Reynolds was quite his old self again 
when the signal was given, though till then he seemed any- 
thing but. happy. Even Hindley was hardly proof against 
the " bad quarter of a hour " preceding the start, though 
at the breakfast, after consuming two chops and steaks and 
eggs to match, he was heard to enquire, with an air of forced 

gaiety, when breakfast would be served. 

• « • « * • ' 

The N.E. D.C. is uproariously delighted at the success of 
the little district in the Bath, and has forgiven much absence 
from Club runs. 

The North East predictions which I shed last week I 
think I may fairly refer to with pride ; all I mean to 
remark is, what did I say about Smith, Lloyd, and S. 6. 
Hough? aiid what does the report say? The Editor's sport- 
ing prophet is out of it.

NOTIOB. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for "GiMette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdays^ 



OUR ENEMIES. 
We propose to call attention to a field of usefulness 
which the Bicycle Union might well cultivate with great 
benefit to our sport, and it is this. Many agricultural 
societies keep a consulting entomologist for the purpose of 
advising on the best methods of dealing with the insect 
foes of the farmer ; and surely it would be a great benefit 
to the pursuit of cycling that a judiciously selected com- 
mittee should, in a truly scientific spirit, investigate the 
nature of the enemies that prey upon our sport, and should 
prescribe remedies of easy application and certain effect to 
be used against the most common disorders fatal to bicyclists. 
We do not pretend to do more than offer suggestions for 
this great end, and can only deal^with a part of the vast 
inquiry. 

For the subject at once divides itself into two parts; 
there are the causes which prevent people from becoming 
bicyclists at all, and the enemies whose attraction renders 
cyclists faithless to their (bicycling) love. The former 
head opens such a boundless field of inquiry, starting with 
the wide question of why people do anything, that we dare 
not touch it ; and on the latter point we can only suggest 
a few leading inquiries. 

For instance, it is a perplexing question whether the 



profession of a Potterer is at all related to that laziness 
which proves so fatal to some once ardeut cyclists, or 
whether it is not the ultra-energetic individual, represented 
by our correspondent "Dodo," who is more subject to tlie 
attacks of a strong propensity for " Taking of his dolce far 
niente" In our own view the " humble potterer ' — as the 
chief representative of that class delights to call himself— 
is more likely to attain to bicycling old age than the ten- 
miles-before-breakfast-every-morning-young-man, who ulti- 
mately succumbs to "D. F. N." in company. But we 
anticipate. 

Then, what a field for analysis in the rival attractions of 
Lawn Tennis and the River, as diminishing attendances 
at Club Meets. How interesting to scientifically test 
whether an "all-men" or a "mixed" set at tennis is 
a more powerful bait for the bicyclist ! And what a con- 
vincing refutation might be published by the Union, of the 
foolish mistake of supposing that there can be any pleasure 
in knocking "regulation balls" backwards and forwards on 
a high grassy lawn, surrounded with trees not too near to 
spoil the light or too thick to shut out the view, with a 
vision of claret-cup in the distance ! Or, again, what an 
an exposure could be made of the delusive folly of drifting 
down stream in the twilight, with oars barely rippling the 
quiet water, through the dark and mystic shade of lofty 
trees, to where the fxill moonlight shimmers on the peaceful 
river ! How a committee would enjoy investigating these 
matters in the interests of science, and how we should like 
to serve on lhat Committee ! 



uigitizea Dy 



98 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



There is, indeed, another foe the most dangerous of all, 
which we should forbear to mention were it not that the 
damage we are likely to suffer from the winged words of our 
esteemed contributor, "A. W. Oman," will be amply re* 
warded by the gain of the reader of the Gazette. Further, 
this foe lias already attacked so many of our hardest and 
sternest riders with such fetal effect, and the sufferers 
brazen out their lapse from (bicycling) virtue so stonily and 
so angrily, that we fear an allusion to their siren-like 
captors may be followed by the arrival of an angry deputa- 
tion at Eastcheap, and the retreat of the Editor up the not 
too commodious chimney of the editorial den. But duty 
calls : we therefore timidly hint that it is (we believe) a 
medical axiom that persons afflicted with disease of the 
heart should not have recourse to bicycling; and that 
(at least we are so informed on credible authority) inter- 
course with that sex which, as the poet so beautifully 
remarks, " cheers but not inebriates," and study at those 
books which, according to a brother poet, teach naught but 
folly, have a strong tendency to divert from those paths of 
duty which lead to the Club Meet. Possibly, the Committee 
might recommend an extension of the use of the " Sociable," 
which would enable, our stronger brethren to convert some 
misguided sisters (other people's) from their present 
infatuation : at any rate, the Committee would find in 
the Scientific Analysis of the Influence of Woman, and in 
experiments thereon, a most absorbing task. And though 
we cannot disguise from ourselves the danger of those 
researches we are ready to sacrifice ourselves in the causes 
of cycling and scientific research, and we have no hesitation 
in volunteering as a member of that Committee. 

COMMITTEE. 

At a meeting of the Committee, held on Monday, 21st 
May, the resignation of Mr. A. H. Garvey was accepted 
as from 1st January last. 

A letter was read from Messrs. Darling & Son, offering 
to make a deduction of 6fl. per publication of the Gazette 
after the month of May, and expressing a wish that the 
friendly relations so long existing between themselves and 
the L.B.C. would continue to be felt. It was decided that 
Messrs. Darling's new tender should be accepted. 

After considerable further discussion it was resolved 
"That the Committee having carefully considered the 
position of tricyclists in the Club, and the question of 
encouraging tricyclists to join our ranks, are of opinion 
that no alteration is advisable in the Club rules or organi- 
sation, and that it is not expedient to hold a Road Trial 
for tricyclists." 

The Special General Meeting was fixed for Monday, 18th 
June, at 6.30 p.m., at the "City Arms Tavern," 

The kind offers of Messrs. Rucker, Theo. Godlee, and 
Scrutton, to present the Club with the prizes for the Club Mile 
were gratefully accepted, and the Hon. Sec. was directed to 
write these gentlemen, thanking them most heartily.

advisability of encouraging Tricyclists to join our ranks, 
will be held at the " City Arms Tavern," St Mary Axe, 
on Monday, 18th June, at 6.30 p.m.

ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 
This event of the year, as members are already aware, 
takes place on Saturday, I6th Jane, at 3 p.m. Tickets are 
obtainable from members of the Committee at the reduced 
price of Is. 6d. A free ticket and entry form was sent 
round with the last Gazette, the latter I shall be very 
pleased to receive back filled up for at least the Club Mile. 
The great attraction of this race lies in the fact that three 
ex-Captains of the Club — Messrs. Eucker, Theo. Godlee, 
and Scrutton, liave presented the prizes this year, a thing 
that may not again occur in the annals of the Club. Mem- 
bers are urgently requested to make this meeting a success 
in every way ; those who can, by getting entries, and every- 
one by selling tickets. E. S. PfiiOE, Hon. Sec.

Club will be held on the Crystal Palace Track on Wednes- 
day, 13th June, at 6 p.m. Tickets are obtainable from me 
at Is. each. Although members do not save anything by 
purchasing them from the Club, they are particularly re-

Palace Company for one hundred tickets, whether used or

Next Saturday it is to be hoped that as many as possible 
will turn out for the run to Erith to give the N.E. a hearty 
welcome on S.E. soil. The Croydon division will start at 
4.15 punctxjuiUy, and proceed direct to Erith vid Orpington. 
We shall have tea at the " Cross Keys " inn, which stands 
on the river side. 

R. G. Francis, Dfetrict Captain. 



N.E. DISTRICT. 
I hope members will recollect that we go to Erith next 
Saturday, and that it is a well established precedent to 
have a good muster. The roads in the S.E. are going 
really to be good this time. 

Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 



W. DISTRICT. 
Next Saturday is the run to Iver, for which I hope all 
who can will manage to assemble at 3.45. The average 
attendance so far far exceeds that of any other year, so if 
each member will do his best to sustain that average through 
the entire season the result will reflect the greatest credit 
on the District, as also on those members who have given 
it their support. 

Habold G. Pioott, District Captain.

GENERAL MEET AT DUNSTABLE WITH THE 
OXFORD AND CAMBRIDGE UNIVERSITY 
BICYCLE CLUBS. 

The adventures of the various Districts are thus recorded 
by their respective D.C's. SuiBce it now to say that 
St. Albans, the appointed rendezvous, saw about forty men, 
representing all Districts but the S.E., awaiting the order 
to start at 5.45 p.m. Concerning the S.E. men, the S.E. 
D.C. writes : — 

" Shortly after ten o'clock last Saturday morning, a small 
body of South-East men might have been seen leaving the 
" Town and Borough of Croydon " for the Dunstable Meet. 
There was MacRae, Paull, Whealler, and the D.C. ; but 
the quartette thus formed was soon to be increased by one, 
for the ex-D.C. with one of his demon spurts, literally 
dashed into our midst just as we reached Sutton. Some 
little disappointment was felt at finding no one at Nonsuch 
Park Gate ; we had hoped that a kinder fate would have 



smiled on us. However, casting our care to the winds in 
true holiday style, we jogged on merrily to Kingston. 
Here, it is darkly rumoured, one or two so far forgot 
themselves as actually to indulge in iced ginger beer, while 
the ex-D.C. was engaged in one of his periodical tinkerings. 
After a steady slog against the wind over the Feltham Flats, 
we were met by our old chief, Fred Williams, who kindly 
invited the whole party to lunch. Those present will not 
soon forget the genial hospitality and good fare accorded 
them at West Drayton by Mr. and Mrs. Williams. 

With no time to spare we were soon on our way again, 
and, with F. Williams for our guide, the party was made up 
to a round half dozen. A slight tendency to spurt oc- 
casionally jarred on the complete harmony of our going, 
but, with plenty of ballast and a steadying breeze, we made 
our way through Rickmansworth and Watford — ^to borrow 
a nautical expression — with a tolerably even keel to St. 
Albans. Here we heard news of Stephen Smith and his 
merry men — four in number — who had ridden from Black- 
heath. As we did not feel quite equal to the official 
thirteen miles an hour, we made a start before the main 
body arrived, and were soon extended in loose order along 
the loose track of the Dunstable Road. The wind was a 
noser, and it wasn't nice on the whole. One man said he 
liked it, but we didn't believe him, and his machine leaning 
with a rakish air against the walls of more than one hostelry 
gave evidence in our favour. Even the long ascent through 
Market Street could not last for ever, and at last a view of 
Dunstable on ahead rewarded our exertions, and a vision of 
the ' Sugar Loaf ' itself completed our happiness. 

Dunstable i3 not very accessible to the South East, still 
there are one or two men whose consciences should prick 
them on reading the names of the sixteen S.E. men who 
were present." 

Concerning the West District, the W. D.C. writes : — 

** Four members assembled at Savernake Cottage at three 
o'clock punctually, including the 1).C. No time was lost 
in making a start, for we were due at St. Albans at 5.45, 
and the motto of this district is 'punctuality.' The route 
chosen was via Hanger Hill, Alperton, and Sudbury, where 
we struck the main Harrow Road. Branching to the right 
at the bottom of the hill we wended our way through the 
pretty lanes leading to Harrow Station, whose beauty 
was somewhat diminished by the bad surface, which caused 
rather unparliamentary language to flow glibly from the 
tongues of most of us. For preference, the Oxey Lane was 
chosen, which leads over a picturesque and undulating 
country to Watford. Passing through the town, and turning 
to the right, we joined the main Reading and St. Albans 
road, the surface of which was indeed excellent, the perfect 
pleasure of gliding over which only being marred by a 
N.W. wind, which was here encountered in full force. 
Owing no doubt to the pleasant company, and its con- 
sequent conversation on everything in general, the time and 



Digitized by VJ^O^IC 



100 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



roads passed so quickly that ure found ourselves at the 
bottom of St. Albans Hill by 5.15. Here was the first 
dismount made, only the B.C. essaying to negotiate the 
precipitous pitch leading to the 'Pea Hen.' Distance 
from Acton 23 miles, time 2 hours 15 minutes. At St. 
Albans we waited for the other divisions by order of the 
Captain ; consequently three-quarters of an hour was wiled 
away until all had assembled/when a general move was 
made for Dunstable under the excellent generalship of the 
Commander-in-Chief. Only two West essayed to ride 
home again that night, Leeds and the D.C. starting at 
9.30 ; home was reached at 1.30 a.m., after an enjoyable 
run by moonlight. ' Self praise is no recommendation,' but 
the District is to be congratulated upon the successful 
muster." 
The N.W. D.C. reports 

"Only five men left *Jack Straw's' at the appointed 
time, or rather a quarter of an hour late, and rode vid the 
usual rough road to Tally Ho ! where the N.E. and several 
N.W. were waiting. After this the District was amalga- 
mated with the main body, and had no separate history." 

The S.W. D.C. reports :— 

"Two members of the S.W. met at the 'Coach and 
Horses,' Eew, at the appointed time, and as no other re- 
presentative of this or any other District had joined them 
by 3.15 p.m., they thereupon made a start for St. Albans. 
The route selected was by Ealing Common, Pinner Hill, 
Sudbury Station, Harrow Weald, and several other interest- 
ing places, to the ' Pea Hen ' at St. Albans, which reached 
in sufficient time to start with the other Districts from 
thence to Dunstable. After so severe a trial as the recent 
one from Bath, the hill leading up to the 'Pea Hen' 
seemed to present no insurmountable difficulty to one of 
our members, who skilfully^negotiated it ! 

We overtook Naylor on a tri. wending his solitary way 
into St. Albans." 

The N.E. D.C. reports 

"The exact starting points of the N.E. are a little 
confusing. Baldwin started early. Lyne and Large got to 
Dunstable early. The Captain and Lloyd left Walthamstow 
about 3.0 ; the D.C. and Weeks about 20 minutes past. 
Gibbs and Long were a little late at the comer, and after 
waiting in divers likely spots were picked up by the D.C. 
and Weeks who were flying rather for Tally Ho ! Nevill 
came up to Tally Ho ! with the N.W. as far as can be 
gathered, and Rucker brought Kinder through London. 
Stunt reached Dunstable, but history is indistinct how. 
Forster started very late, bat got into tea. This leaves the 
main body which consisted of the brothers Hough, E. D. 
Sewell, Kekey, and Gerard Smith, who may be assumed to 
have left Lea Bridge at 3.0 o'clock sharp and proceeded to 
Tally Ho ! Of the above, thirteen left Tally Ho ! in company 
with the N.W., (it will be readily calculated who these werej 
and they proceeded with the main body to Dunstable except 



Gerard Smith who had not had his bicycle out for some 
time, and who turned back at St. Albans, so that the total 
at Dunstable was seventeen. Of these seventeen, six, 
namely the Captain, Long, Gibbs, Kelsey, Baldwin, and 
very likely Weeks, who is a problem, stayed at Dunstable, 
the remaining eleven making their ways home at divers 
times and paces." 

Report from the Captain of the O.U.Bi.C. :— 
" Nine members left the Club rooms at 12.20 for Dunstable. 
After getting clear of the town the pace was made fetirly 
fast, and with a favourable breeze, no halt was made until 
about two miles beyond Thame. Here Walter and D. R. 
Smith were sent on to order lunch at Aylesbury, and 
Andrews, who was riding a borrowed machine two sizes too 
large for him, and with a most galling saddle, changed 
machines with the Captain. Loose order was maintained 
until Aylesbury was reached at 2.40. Here Andrews, having 
beep sufficiently tortured by his saddle, left us and trained 
to Dunstable. Having made a hearty lunch at the "George," 
we started again at four o'clock. The roads being good, and 
everything in our favour, we bowled merrily along through 
Aston Clinton, and then taking a sharp turn to the left we 
made for Ivinghoe. Here we met Jephson and Wigan, and 
after a few minutes' halt we rode on across the Downs to 
Dunstable. About four miles from Dunstable, Newenham, 
through pulling up too soon, croppered and involved Jephson 
in his downfall. However, after a few minutes rest and 
having straightened the handle bars of both machines, we 
rode on, reaching Dunstable, where we found Croftou await- 
ing us, soon after 6.0 p.m. 

Bide Home from Dunstable. 
Walter, D. R. Smith and the Captain started from Dun- 
stable exactly at 8.0 p.m., with a splendid moon, and a 
breeze in our faces just sufficient to keep us cool. The pace 
was made warm, and Aylesbury, seventeen miles, was reached 
in Ihr. and 12mins. Here a halt of 25mins. was made 
and some delicious tea and hot toast partaken of at the 
" George." From Aylesbury the pace was made somewhat 
slower, and without a dismount, we reached Magdalene 
Bridge (23 miles from Aylesbury), in five minutes under 
the two hours, and we reach our respective Colleges with a 
quarter of an hour in hand." 
Report-from the Captain of the C.U.Bi.C. — 
" The Cambridge men rode down in two divisions, fast 
and slow, the latter of which was overhauled at Baldock by 
the fast lot, headed by the secretary in his best form. 
From Baldock the two divisions rode together twenty strong 
to Dunstable, which they reached at 6. 30 p.m. The two other 
members came separately from Cambridge by road, and 
one availed himself of the train for part of the distance. 
The road was rough and the wind contrary. Distance 40 
miles. After tea, the arrangements for which were greatly 
appreciated, the main body returned to Hitchin for the 
10-20 train ; four rode the whole way, and the Captain 



Google 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



101 



went on to St. Alban's with part of the Weet District and 
the Blackheath Division. The ''Queen's" thereat has 
changed hands and can be recommended. He returned via 
Tewin, Welwyn, and Hitchin, on the following day." 

Having thus conducted the University men and the 
S.E. District to Dunstable, the progress of the main body, 
made up of the four other L.B.C. Districts, from St. Albans 
to Dunstable, must be narrated. This latt^, assembled at 
the "Pea Hen," after waiting a few minutes for chance 
stragglers, left about 6 p.m. ; they were a little late, but 
hoped by punctuality and strict adherence to business to 
merit the applause of the friends at Dunstable prepared to 
welcome them at 7 p.m. However, Vhomme propose, tho* 
the wind disposes^ and the best laid plans of men and mice 
go oft awry. So was it with the Captain and his battalions. 

A fierce N.E. wind and thirteen miles of loose and dusty 
high road soon thinned out the ranks, and only by frequent 
slowiogs up could the stragglers be kept in hand. Rucker, 
Kinder, and Qruggen did duty, and did it most carefully 
and efficiently as leaders, while Lloyd and Hough, sen., 
acted as rearmen — the latter stopping behind with an 
unfortunate who had passed over his handles, and^ 
bringing him up triumphantly after a long stern chase. 
Eight miles out from St. Alban's the enforced stoppages tried 
the patience of our younger bloods too severely, and as they 
sailed ahead over the distant hills the Caplain was seen to 
be nervously searching his pockets for the accustomed six- 
shooter. Had he not by an accident ^which will no^ 
recur) omitted to provide himself with this aid to discipline 
the Club would have lost one of its most genial officers^ 
Soon the roofs and chimneys of Dunstable suggested that 
our journey was uearing its end, and the slow contingent 
having amalgamated itself with the main body, the column 
entered the town in very good form at 7J5, and with a 
passing cheer from the (Tniversity men and some of our 
own members at the Town Hall, disbanded at the sign of 
the "Old Sugar Loaf." Here they found extensive wash, 
ing appliances, and with little loss of time adjourned to 
the hall, where the capital tea, which Mr^ Saunders with 
his well-known liberality, had provided, soon banished 
all remembrance of dusty roads, head winds, and end- 
less hills. At eight o'clock the University men to our 
regret left us ; but before going, the L.B.C. was proposed 
by J. S. Whatton the Cambridge Captain — the O.U.Bi.C. 
was proposed by the L.B.C. Captain — and the C.U.Bi.C. by 
W. F. M. Buckley the Oxford Captain ; the latter gave a 
finish to the proceedings by calling three cheers for the 
Cambridge men, which was briefly acknowledged on their 
behalf by C. A. E. Pollock of their Club and our own. 
When our friends had gone, the hungry L.B.C. men settled 
down to work, and were soon so well eng^^ed that they 
failed even to notice the non-attendance of the Town Band 
— absent from the indisposition of the Trombone, who, since 
our last visit, has married and gone out of training. The 



good moon induced many to ride home, some it was said, 
among whom was the S.E. D.C.'s Substitute, making 
Blackheath at 3 a.m. Many men slept in Dunstable, and 
were well cared for by Mr. Saunders, to whom our thanks 
and those of Oxford and Cambridge are due for his kindness 
and unfailing good nature. Altogether our triple Meet this 
year may be regarded as a success, and it is pleasant to 
learn that the University men are as much gratified as 
ourselves.
