ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
The May list of candidates for the C. T. C. numbers 1058. 
As far as can be ascertained nearly 7500 names are now 
entered on the roll. 

• ••••• 

At the Annual General Meeting to be held at Birmingham 
on Saturday, the most important resolutions are proposals 
by Mr. G. C. Locket, that an entrance fee of half-a-crown 
be charged after 1st July next, and another by Mr. W. B. 
Tanner, that from the end of the current year the entrance 
fee be one shilling, and that the subscription be four 
shillings per annum, to include ticket and handbook.

102 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



The Hampton Court Meet was held for the tenth time on 
Saturday. The number of riders fell short of last year's, 
and the largest Club muster was that of the Brixton 
Ramblers, who were 39 strong. A conspicuous feature was 
the large number of riders who preferred to play the part 
of spectators. 

At the Sutton B. C. Meeting at the Crystal* Palace on 
Saturday, the Five Miles Invitation Scratch Race was won 
by H, W. Gaskell, W. K. Adam was second, Tacagni third, 



and F. L. Adam fourth. G. F. Beck also started and was 

close up at the finish. Gaskell covered the last mile in 

2 min. 50 sec. 

• ••«•• 

At the West Kent Meeting on Saturday next there are 

seventy-three entries for the Open Mile Handicap, and the 

following are amongst them, and if all goes well there ought 

to be some good racing. Gaskell, Buckley, Thompson, 

Tacagni, W. K, Adam, F. L. Adam, Wyndham, Butler, 

Mackinlay, Beck, and Latham Cli^ke. 

Thb Front Wheel Hub.

AFTER THE BATH. 
So they were I All of them ! The ex-Editor, with sim- 
plicity one would hardly expect from a long experience, 
imagines he has only to ring the bell to get a bath. Boots 
tells him he will have it ready in ten minutes, but Fred.^ 
after waiting about half-an-hour, comes down to find the 
wily ones have engaged everything that will hold water. 
But I am going away from the point before I have even 
come to it, for the bath eve>yone was after was at Dunstable. 
We had all to get to St. Albans. Not meaning to exert 
myself against a head wind I start early, with a man who 
has not ridden this year. He gives me an awful doing — 
begin to think there is not much glory about that medal I 
won on Monday if this is the kind of thing I shall have to 
put up witL St. Albans at last — Cyclomaniac appears — 
wonder what that tremendous rubber ring in front is for — 
should like to ask him, but he disappears — arrival of 
S.E., with their Captain disguised in a Reynolds' hat and 
O'Reilly shoes — we start in good order — wish there was an 
enamellist to ride on with — must be much easier than 
riding with some of these novices who will persist in spurting 
me up hills — don't like hills— think they are a great mis- 
take — Dunstable reached— Stephen P. taking the part of 
Sister Ann on balcony of Town Hall — no band this year — 
evidently afraid to make too much of us because of the 
soldiers in the town — two youthful aspirants to bicycling fame 
career wildly up and down in front of hotel on boneshakers, 
nearly capsizing late arrivals — don't think I could work one 
up the hill — have a wash — tea— only a few of the men seem 



to know one another— The O'Reilly turns up amidst great 
applause — the three Captains seem to have made speeches, 
but the hall is so bad for sound, that beyond the first two or 
three words I heard nothing — they might have coupled 
" ladies " with the ** gentlemen," as we are honoured by a 
few of the fair sex in the ladies' gallery — I hear one very 
young member remark, " there's a deal of gal about that 
gallery ; " but of course he will grow older later on — wish 
they would have some songs — remember they went down 
very well at Hitehin — a very fetching programme might be 
made up — say, for instance — ^part song — " Which way does 
the wind blow*' — by this year's starters — Baldwin might 
oblige with Let me like a soldier fall " — then a glee by 
the Bath medallists, " Oh, who will o'er the downs so free" — 
next, Qodlee might give us, " 'Tis but a little faded flower," 
and a grand finale — special engagement, for one night only, 
of the bugler attached to the regiment quartered in the 
town, so that we could recall the echoes of the past on 
hearing once again the bugle calls printed in the blue book, 
and the newer members might get an idea of the martial 
strains with which our every action was accompanied and 
set to music ; but no, — the Captain is too matter of fact, 
and soon is deep in Club business with Whatton — should 
not like to be tackled with a note book such as the L.B.C. 
Captain runs — Sewell and Thorn have come down by 
train — ^always did want to see Sewell ever since I read his 
articles in the Gazette — won't have too much to say to 
him, tho' — might find myself in his next — have never seen 
Oliver out of costume before — doesn't seem right — the boro' 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



103 



of Dunstable has evidently seen Fred on the 'bos, for they 
have fized a poster with a caution to drivers on to him — 
the Nor'-Easters start for home — don't think Qodlee can 
do justice to that flower and control his new medallists at 
the same time. 

Turn into smoking room — ^wish Titeboy would go on 
telling tales instead of the other fellows talking Bath Trial 
—nothing but L. B., H. R., and Waddy— had enough of 
them on Monday — ^the "enamel seems to partake of the 
nature of the chameleon ; one man swears to it being 
white, another blue, and both are right — go to bed — don't 
get to sleep, four festive gentlemen having a bolster fight 
overhead. Nor do Captain's duties seem to have ended, 
for I hear him having a long discussion as he takes his last 
pipe with two Committee men. At last to sleep, and 
dream of Harold Smith arriving at the ninth milestone on 
a machine with only one spoke left in it. Woke at five, 
and saw the O'Reilly start — such energy ought to be en- 
couraged among the younger members — ^tum in again until 
8.30. Great discussion at breakfast betweeitthree married 
men — Williams, Radical; Kinder, Liberal; Brown, Con- 
servative ; Barrett, Speaker. Make a start with two men 
who seldom ride, and three scorchers — ^first-named take us 
at a rare pace to St. Albans. Here we meet a lot of our 
men, and seeing Whatton a short way up the hill, send the 
N.W. infant in pursuit. J. S. W. seemed to think of 
coming back until he heard the Captain was at the 
" Peahen ; " but then, having that note book in mind, rode 
straight on. Some delay in getting dinner, as the Bishop 
engages the attention of the entire staff. Hardly the place 
one would expect to find a Bishop. Look round Cathedral 
— ordination going on — get into the saddle again — two 
casuals still taking us along ; one soon turns off, and the 
other, who is showing us the way, beguiles us gently down 
a long hill, with the wind at our backs, anc( leaves us with 
the remark that he now turned off to the right, and we 
should have turned to the left at the top. By the way, I 
suppose, there was an Affirmation Bill passed for Mr. 
Dioscorides, to save him the trouble of swearing at the 
porpentine. The flyers, on getting again to the top, make 
the pace warmer-^mildly suggest would be a nice gate to 
sit on — remark met with grim silence by my adversaries, 
who look upon it as a sign of weakness on my part, and 
make the pace very hot — express my intention of not going 
so fast now I have won a medal, and don't— ^hall be afraid 
to wear that medal when it is presented to me— everyone 
will go for me if they only know. Come to the conclusion 
there are a good many " Trials after the Trial" 

N. H., Jun. 



W. A. Smith would feel greatly obliged if members of 
the Club would refrain from calling upon him at his place 
of business during office hours, the rules of the establish- 
ment being " no followers allowed."

without permission from the Captain. The f oUowing list of Racing

members racing at any of the meetinsfs mentioned. Any member 
wishing to ride at any meeting not included in this list is requested 
to communicate with the Captain.

QUILLS. 

'* AzistoUe and Pliny deUver it that the porpentine casteth off his quills 
when he would attack, or defend himself, but Diosoorides affirmeth it a 
point of foUy to believe it"— Sir T. BBOWJn. 

Would-be prophets of the Bath Trial will be consoled to 
learn from an ingenious correspondent of the Echo that out 
of sixtyHsix published prophecies for the recent meeting at 

Newmarket, only eight proved correct ! 

• • • • • • 

The Editor is rather proud, so he tells me, of his pre- 
dictions. His leading article selected only twelve candi- 
dates, of whom eleven started; eight took medals, including 
the first three, and six out of the first eight, and he tells 
me he only refrained from mentioning W. E. Adam on 
being told on first-rate authority that he wasn't going to 
start. 

I am requested to contradict the rumour that the Captain 
has been offered a contract in connection with the new 
Parcels Post by the G.P.O. 

The Editor was not at Dunstable, missing the Inter- 
University meet for the first time. He was seen in the city 
at a suspicious hour with a suspicious looking bat, but he 
wishes it to be^known that he had a far more serious reason 
for absence than tennis, wbicb is a game he doesn't like and 

can't play at. 

• • • • • • 

At Dunstable I observed certain revolutionists who, in- 
verting the natural order of things, carried their colours — 
black, red, and blue. Such a flagrant violation of Rule 4 
strikes at the very root of all social order. 

I am pleased not to be able to agree with our censorious 
Captain's criticism upon the "Milford Arms" collation.. 
The tea, at any rate, was strong and plentiful— rarely ha/ 
I found better.

104 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



The Captain's much-vaunted map of Hertfordshire 
(3d. plain, 6d. coloured) is said by the irreverent S.W. D.C. 
to owe distinctness mostly to its having but one road 
marked upon it. 

Our Editor says he noted at Dunstable several men at 
various times during the evening jotting down in sly 
comers notes and tips to be served up in QuiUs at 
Porcupine's discretion. This pleases me much. 

• ••••• 

Our hungry member informs me he was perfectly satisfied 

last Saturday at Dunstable, for not only was there food in 

abundance, but that wherewith to feast the eyes. 

**«••• 

The little District, though it does not technically beat its 
record, never has had seventeen N.E. members to tea at 
once before. 

A very good portrait of G. P. Coleman appeared in last 

week's issue of the Bicycling News, 

««•••• 

My predecessor 8 motto, "There's a chiel among ye 
takin' notes," is being adopted as a rule of practice by an 
eminent officer of the L.B.C. I hope " he'll print them " 
to carry his motto to its legitimate end. 

«««•«« 

*' TBioTCLnro.— FoUowing the exampla of the old Bicycle Clab (!), 
the One Hundred MUes Road Bace was arranged for Whit-Monday, 
etc.** — Oraphie. 

What have we done to be antiquarianized in this way ? 

Our main body ought certainly to have been larger to 
have done credit to the bunting and the enthusiastic in- 
habitants of Dunstable. 

*•«*•• 

That we made an impression is evident. An old gentlemen 
on the road from St. Albans enquired what Club it was, 
and, being told it was the " London," he remarked, Nice 
lot," to which our irrepressible member replied " Very." 

• * • * • • *. 

In spite of the crowd of faces on the balcony, I was 
assured by a N.W. member that ha recognised Stephen 

Smith two miles off. 

• • • « « ' * 

Dunstable is described in Paterson's as " remarkable for 
the size of its larks." Perhaps this was one of them. 

• ••••« 

The programme of our Race Meeting looks very well on 

the board of the Otter Swimming Club at their baths. 
«•«••• 

There was a rehearsal of " The Bells " at Committee on 
Monday; K B. Hough as Matthias. The chimes of a 

neighbouring church sounded so much like Bath. 

• « * « • • 

L. B. Reynolds ran second in the Mile Race (flat) at the 
Sports of the Victoria Rifles on Saturday. 



Since the Road Trial was instituted, fifty men have 
gained medals, forty-six of whom are still members, which 

is about one fifoh of our present strength. 

• ••••• 

A good account of the Trial, with a brief resume of all 
the previous ones, was published in last week's Field. 

A Lacrosse F^tch will take place this evening, at Clapton, 

between a team consisting of three representatives from 

each of the four following families : viz., Barrett, Newman, 

Sachs and Arvher, against a scratch team, of whom our 

Club are represented by P. Jolly, T. Child, A. Murray, and 

the energetic Secretary, Arthur Smith. 

«••«•• 

Yesterday the king of the Belgians was thrown from his 
horse, which whs run into by a bicyclist, but his Majesty 
was not hurt. — Globe, Thursday, May 17tL 

The road from Woolwich to Erith was oflicially inspected 
on Tuesday nurning and pronounced to be in excellent 
condition with the exception of one mile in Woolwich. It 
runs along the ridge of a thickly wooded hill, commanding 

extensive and interesting views of the Thames estuary. 

• ••*•• 

Take a friend's advice, and, whatever you do, don't go out 
for a quiet ride with a man who had meant to but didn't 

start for this yt'ar's Trial. 

• * * • • • 

Stephen Smith and Day achieved a best on record on 
Saturday by riding from Blackheath to the Meet at 

Dunstable and back, a distance of ninety miles ! 

• »•••• 

Most conspicuously energetic among the other S.E. 
members who attended the Meet, were Morgan and 
P. B. Smith, who came by road from Sevenoaks, and 
S. E. Whealler, who rode from Croydon, mopt of the way 
with a broken handle-bar. 

It was a pitiable sight to see three men on the way to 
Dunstable deliberately make a funeral pile of themselves 

and their maclraes in trying to turn in a narrow lane. 

• • • ♦ • « ♦ 

To improve the occasion, the Club Scholiast commenced 
the recitation of a parallel passage from the Mantuan Bard 
— re Dido (as our legal friends would have it). •

NOTIOB. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for "Gazette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdas^ 



DUTY. 

Lately we have tried to show some of the Taried duties 
of the Captain and his Committee, and beyond doubt the 
account given must prove that office in the L.B.C. brings 
with it no repose, that atra cura rides behind the Gold 
Badge, and that the disgrace of a failure or the joy of a 
success is shared in by every man, even the least prominent 
on the Committee. But to make a Club pre-eminent, as 
the London should be, more than hard work from its 
officers is wanted. Every Clubman needs to feel that 
his responsibilities are not erased by the Treasurer's receipt 
for his annual subscription. There should be no drones in 
our social hive, nor men who— doing no work themselves — 
have nouglit but criticisms for those who are exerting them- 
selves. However, leaving generalities, it is our task to 
show how the L.B.C. man may, if he has the will, serve his 
Club. Dunstable, with its triple meet, is now a thing of 
the past ; but there still remains certain events in connection 
with which each man has it in his power to lend a hand. 
These are the two Race Meetings at the Crystal Palace — 
one, the match with Oxford on June 13th ; the other, our 
own sports on June 16th. Probably it is known that the 
Palace permits the use of its track on Wednesday, 13th, 
a shilling day, on condition that the Committee purchase 



BNTS. 

PAGE 



All Round the Wheel 110 

Correspondence HI 

QuiUs 112 

Racing Fixtures 112 

Editorial Note 112 

Notice to Correspondents 112 



of the Company at least 100, or £5 worth of tickets. In 
order to recover this amount, it therefore behoves members 
to buy of the Hon. Sec. By so doing they will help 
the Club, though they will themselves reap no special 
benefit. For our own Race Meeting on June 16th, tickets 
may, as has already been announced, be bought of any 
Committeeman for Is. 6d. each, and these admit to the 
Palace and Grounds on what is otherwise a 2s. 6d. day. 
Moreover, in addition to selling tickets, the energetic 
member will himself enter for one or more of the events 
This lie will do pour encourage Us autres, even if not 
himself much of a racing man. Of course we assume that 
his apprenticeship to tumbling has been served ; if other- 
wise, for the sake of others it would be well for him to 
refrain from starting. Then further, he will furnish himself 
with entry forms, and hunt up competitors among his racing 
acquaintances. Much should be done in this direction, 
since there are three or four other meetings on other 
grounds upon the same date as our own. It should be 
noted that the racing variety of the bicycular species is 
somewhat coy, and though yielding readily to an energetic 
canvasser, it needs a good deal of finding out. Suburban 
and Metropolitan Racing Tracks on Saturday afternoons 
form its habitat; and to close with it more eflFectually, one 
of our patriotic members, though himself no racing man, 
is known to have entered for an open handicap of another 
influential Club ;--not unlike the distinguished traveller 
who, at the risk of his life, visited the holy shrine of the 
Prophet with the crowd of Mohammedan pilgrims disguised

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



after their fashion. Such devotion cannot be expected of 
many ; still, all should recollect that the racing man is of a 
simple, confiding disposition, not always keenly alive to his 
own manifest interests, and should endeavour to convince 
him that these are located on June 16 th at the Crystal 
Palace. 

THE ANNUAL RACE MEETING AT THE CRYSTAL 
PALACE ON SATURDAY, JUNE 16th. 
As the Committee intend to make the Race Meeting a 
success in every way, they trust members will exert them- 
selves vigorously to sell tickets in every direction among 
their friends, remembering that for every ticket sold a 
small profit accrues to the Club. Even those who are not 
specially interested in bicycling may be glad to buy tickets 
when they learn that these can be obtained through our 
Hon. Sec. for Is. 6d. apiece upon what is ordinarily a half- 
crown day. 

By purchasing through this channel a gain of one shilling 
upon each ticket will be effected, and the ticket so obtained 
will admit to the Palace and grounds as well as to our 
Race Meeting. Subjoined is a comparative statement of 
expenses : —

ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 
This event of the year, as members are already aware, 
takes place on Saturday, 16th June, at 3 p.m. Tickets are 
obtainable from members of the Committee at the reduced 
price of Is. 6d. A free ticket and entry form was sent 
round with the last Gazette, the latter I shall be very 
pleased to receive back filled up for at least the Club Mile. 
The great attraction of this race lies in the fact that three 
ex-Captains of the Club— Messrs. Rucker, Theo. Godlee, 
and Scrutton, have presented the prizes this year, a thing 
that may not again occur in the annals of the Club. Mem- 
bers are urgently requested to make this meeting a success 
in every way ; those who can, b^ getting entries, and every- 
one by selling tickets. E. S. Pbice, Hon. Sec. 



L.B.C. MATCHES WITH THE UNIVERSITIES. 

C.U.Bi.C. V. L.B.C. 
Our Annual Match with the Cambridge University 
Bicyle Club will be held at Cambridge on Saturday, 
June 9th, and will commence at 3 p.m. Competitors 
should leave Liverpool Street Station for Cambridge by the 



11.55 a.m. train. If the weather is favourable I propose 
riding up, and shall be pleased to meet any L.B.C. men 
and their friends who may like to accompany me, and who 
will let me know of their intention not later than Wed- 
nesday, June 6th, by Waltham Cross at 8 a.m. punctually. 

Ashley W. Babrett, Captain. 



O.U.Bi.C. V. L.B.C. 
Our Annual Match with the Oxford University Bicycle 
Club will be held on the Crystal Palace Track on Wednes- 
day, 13th June, at 6 p.m. Tickets are obtainable firom the 
Hon. Sec. at Is. eack Although members do not save 
anything by purchasing them from the Club, they are 
particularly requested to do so, as we have to settle with 
the Crystal Palace Company for one hundred tickets, 
whether used or not. After the Match the competitors 
will dine together in the Palace, and I shall be glad to 
receive the names of any gentlemen (whether members of 
the L.B.C. or hot) who may desire to be present. Names 
should be sent into me not later than Thursday, June 7th, 
in order that the necessary arrangements may be made. 

E. S. Price, Hon. Sec.

ROAD TRIAL. 
Notice to Competitors. — Aa timers at Newbury and 
Maidenhead this year are considerably out of pocket through 
expenses incurred by them in providing champaigne, brandy, 
and other refreshments for those riding in the trial, I hope 
none of the latter will fail through forgetfulness to dis- 
charge their obligations. It need not be said that as a 
timer's work is very arduous and responsible, it would be 
most unfair to make him suffer pecuniarly. 

Ashley W. Bakbbtt, Captain^ 

NOTICE.— IMPORTANT. 
L.B.C. Matches with the Universities.

Ashley W. Babkett, Captain. 

SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
26th May. It was not a pleasing prospect, and the 
District has rather a habit of resting after an effort such as 
the Dunstable run, so that the D.C. and Lloyd were hardly 
surprised to find themselves alone at the Comer. Very 
soon after the start it began to rain, and it did not see its 
way to leave off. James's Lane, The Flats, and East Ham 
was the route adopted, and Woolwich was reached con- 
siderably under the hour. Some delay was caused by 
waiting for the ferry, and Stephen Smith was in attendance 
on the other side. The road to Erith may fairly be con- 
sidered the best bit of riding the N.E. have yet had in the 
big District; there is one big hill and a mile or two of 
tram lines in the six miles, but taken for all in all it 
is good. As for the views of the Thames I^tuary, 
the little District took them on trust — they were not 
visible with the naked eye. At Erith, Weeks and 
Amott were waiting, and the visitors felt relieved ; 
and when by and by the Captain turned up there was a 
feeling that five was not a contemptible turn out for the 
weather. Tea was prepared for forty and was about as 
good as they build them, indeed, the " Cross Keys " has 
been made a note of for future use. Just as we were 
beginning, the S.E. D.C. came in and completed the party. 
The five North-Easters returned together by the ferry in 
decided rain, and made Woolwich by daylight with the 
assistance of S. P. Smith. Present : — ^Arnott, Ashley W. 
Barrett, Lloyd, Weeks, and 

Theo. Oodles, District Captain. 

S.W. District. 
So far as I can learn, few members of the S.W. seem to 
have taken advantage of the pleasant programme carefully 
prepared for them for Saturday in the unavoidable absence, 
at the last moment, of the D.C. It is true to say that the 
day was overcast, and at times even moist, but this of 



course will not be regarded as the real reason for so very 
small an attendance; perhaps the double tricycle did 
start for Iver, bat as we have had no account of its arrival, 
or any details of the journey, we have our misgivings on 
this point, and look forward with anxiety to the publication 
of our next issue. This unaccountable lack of information 
up to the present time, and the difficulty combined with 
the improbability of obtaining any reliable facts in con- 
nection with the District's doings on Saturday last, leads 
me to hope — in the unlikely event of my being absent 
again -*tbat in future members will ''combine" to carry 
out the " official " run, and to give an interesting account 
of their proceedings. I am prompted to make this request, 
since it has come to my knowledge that the impressive 
effect produced upon one of our members who attended our 
recent Northern General Meet was so great that he ignored 
our shorter District run to Iver, and rode again to St. Albans 
and Dunstable on Saturday. No doubt this enthusiasm 
should be commended, and even encouraged, but in con- 
sequence of this fact the notion suggests itself that perhaps 
others, fascinated by some of our meeting places nearer 
home and \>y acting on similar impulses, have gratified 
their respective inclinations and neglected the interests of 
their District. 

Next Saturday the run is to Shepperton, and the meet 
at Kingston Station at 4.30 p.m., when I hope that we 
shall have a good muster. As the evenings are long and 
the distance to Shepperton short there will be ample time 
for a row on the river either before or after tea. 

B. M. Gruqgen, District Captain. 



S.E. District. 
Beneath a leaden sky and a soaking rain the D.C. left 
home on Saturday in the hopes of meeting one or two 
daring spirits on Hayes Common. After a miserable half 
hour spent in anxiously waiting for what came not among 
the dripping oaks and hawthorns, he made tho best of 
rather an unpleasant ride through the Cray Valley to Erith, 
and arrived at the "Cross Ke3's" just as Stephen Smith 
and five of the N.E., who had braved the weather, were 
setting down to tea. After a sumptuous repast, worthy of 
a better day, and as much jollity as may be supposed to 
prevail generally among half-drowned rats, the party broke 
up, and the homeward journey was made under auspices 
not much more favourable than those attending the outward 
run. Present : S. P. Smith, and 

B. G. Frai^ois, District Captain. 

W. C. Oswald and Wigan also came to the starting point, 
but did not persevere in following out the whole run. 

MEET AT ERITH. 
As I seem to have been the only member who attended 
this Meet, I suppose it is my duty to send you a short 
account of it. I started from the Crystal Palace about 



Digitized by 



108 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



3.30 and rode to Locks Bottom, hoping to meet the 
Blackheath and Croydon Divisions at that place ; not seeing 
anyone, and being far too wet to stand about doing nothing, 
I rode on to Erith. The ride would have been agreeable 
enough had it not been for the jokes of sundry people on 
the state of my back, which would have done very well to 
grow mustard and cress on, or any other small vegetables 
not requiring a great depth of soil. I also suffered from 
another cause, and that was the conviction which began 
to creep over me as I got farther on my way that I was 
rather foolish to start ; and the farther I rode, the firmer 
this idea rooted itself in my mind. On arriving at the 
" Prince of Wales Hotel " I was met by the landlady, who 
looked very serious, and who wished to know " why I had 
not sent a telegram to say the Club was coming?" Tea for 
thirty men had been prepared and laid out — she was much 
annoyed at not having received notice as promised," etc. etc. 
It seems, a gentleman from Blackheath had ridden over on 
Wednesday evening to make arrangements for tea, promising 
faithfully to telegraph on Saturday morning whether the Meet 
would be held or not. Of course I pleaded ignorance, and 
got away from her ladyship by saying I must change my 
clothes, at the same time promising to eat the thirty teas 
should the other men not arrive. Having put on some 



dry togs I had bought in the town, I set to work to feed, 
and can honestly assure you I never had a more satisfactory 
meal. After a sm4ke and a chat with the landlady's son 
(who was kind enough to lend me a good overcoat to travel 
home in), I bethought myself of getting home. It was 
quite out of the question trying to ride, for I could not get 
the saddle far enough back to permit of my mounting — 
bear in mind the thirty teas that had to be made room for. 

In conclusion, allow me to say I was treated most kindly 
at the hotel. I trust we may have a good Meet there 
ere long — we are sure to be made comfortable. — ^Yours 
faithfully, Chas. £. Parkeb. 

SOUTH-EAST PAPERCHASE. 
The Captain has signified his intention of joining our 
Annual Paperchase next Saturday, so it is to be hoped we 
shall have a fine day and a iine attendance. The Croydon 
Division will leave the Fire Station at 4.30 for Sanderstead, 
and the hares. Barker and Wyndham, will start from the 
Church at five o'clock punctually. We shall have tea at the 
" Glyn Arms," Ewell, at half-past six, and I hope anyone 
who is too late or does not care for the Paperchase will 
meet us at tea there. 

B. G. Francis, District Captain.

June 16.— ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 



BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(CONTIHrXD FBOM PAOB 79.) 

§ 19. — Zurich to JSimiedeln and Egeri, and hach 

This would be too long a round to make without loco- 
motive help. It is tlierefore best to take the train to 
EinsiedelD. We left by the first train from Enge (at 5.30), 
getting to Wadenschwyl (or WadensM?^«7, as you will find it 
and similar terminations sometimes spelt) at 6.20. Here 
you have to wait till 7.40, giving you nice time for break- 
fast at the " Engel " hard by. The railway to Einsiedeln is 
a continuous incline, and the engine pushes you up : hence 
from the end of the leading carriage you get an unimpeded 



view of the scenery in front of you, and can enjoy it in its 
own bright pure morning atmosphere undimmed by smoke 
and without ai^ riek of getting hot cinder dust in your 
eyes. It makes one think of the enjoyments in store for us 
when trains in general are driven by electricity instead of 
steam. The question of whether it is worth your while 
visiting Einsiedeln or not must be settled by a reference to 
Baedeker : if not, you will leave the train at Biberbruck, the 
station short of it. The road from Einsiedeln to Biberbruck, 
though slightly down hill, is not in very good condition, and 
I think some of the lay brethren of the monastery might 
turn their attention to it with advantage. The following 
will be your itineracy starting from Einsiedeln.

At Biberbruck you cross two streams and the railway, 
and then turn to your left up on to the moors ; there is at 
least one bit of stiff hill to encounter, before you reach the 
highest point. At Altmatt, about two miles on, and again 
at Rothenthunn, you will meet with an unusal obstruction 
in these parts in the shape of a gate across the road — not a 
toll gate, but a parish gate dividing the pasturages and 
kept closed on account of the cattle, after the manner 
of the gates on the principal upland roads in Wales 
and in our English Lake district. It is no earthly use 
your expecting the natives to open the gate for you; 
they never seem to be capable under these circumstances 
of doing more than one thing at a time, and that one 
is staring in abject astonishment at you and your 
bicycle. You had better make up your mind to dis- 
mount at once. There is a delightful descent all the 
way down to Sattel, where you will have to double back 
again a bit to your right, unless you are lucky enough to 
observe a short cut. As you approach Sattel, the road 
forks ; the right hand road leads up into the village— you 
keep on the lower one. After this you had better refer to 
your map, or a villager, if you feel uncertain, for the way 
to Egeri (the native pronunciation will be A Agerry — hard g). 
About l\ mile beyond the spot where you double back, 
the road enters a little cutting and then goes down hill 
(tortuous and steep) ; this we found stoned and requiring 
great care, but afterwards the road is very good, and it is 
a lovely ride all along the Lake of Egeri. This lake, which 
lies 1000 feet higher than its bigger neighbours of Lucerne, 
Zug, and Zurich, is a spot seldom, if ever, visited by the 
tourist. It lies completely oflf any known coach route, has 
no railway near it, and obtains the most scanty and 
parenthetical mention in Baedeker. We certainly saw it 
to great advantage on a perfectly stiU bright morning, with 
the snow peaks (visible in the distance over the head of it) 
glistening with a fresh fall during the night ; we thought it 
one of the sweetest scenes we had seen as we rode by, and 
wondered why it was so little known. But a still greater 
surprise was in store for us in the view from the brow of 
the hill, just before commencing the long descent to Zug. 
This fairly took our breath away. The road was good— 
z'gzags, but not too steep — and we were enabled to enjoy it 
to the full as we spun down it. You should hold well back 
on the sharp pitch just entering Zug. as you will have to 



dismount at the comer for paving. Between Baar and 
Sihlbrugg they were altering the levels in a few places, 
necessitating a dismount. At Sihlbrugg, after crossing the 
bridge, you keep to the right — alongside of the river — and 
you have a continuous hill of about three miles of moderate, 
not excessive steepness, somewhere about 1 in 20. It was 
one of the very few really hot days we had this summer in 
Switzerland, and we were content with riding two out of 
the three. The 4^ miles downhill to Horgen is excellent 

An expedition to the Lake of Egeri might be very well 
made from Lucerne, either (a) by taking the lefthand fork 
between Artli and Goldau (see § 14), leading up by Steinen 
to Sattel, or (6) by taking the early boat to Brunnen and 
riding by Schwyz and the Schlagstrasse to Sattel. From 
Zug you could return to Lucerne, either by the lake or by 
Bothkreuz (see § 13). 

§ 20. — Glarus, Elm, and lAnththaL 

This is an expedition in which you will have to avail 
yourself of the train, and also send a bag for sleeping out. 
We left Enge (Zurich)at 5.30and gotout at Schwanden — the 
point where the two valleys of the Sernf and the Linth unite. 
Here we breakfasted at the Adier," and saw our trout 
caught for us in the fish tank whilst we were giving our 
bicycles their morning oil. Do not order tea — ^like the 
famous green cheese of the valley, it is strongly meliloted. 
The bill was, I thought, higher than it should have been at 
such an inn, and another time I shall try breakfasting at 
the railway station, which is handier. After the first 
three-quarters of a mile, the road from Schwanden (1720 
feet) up the Sernf valley to Elm is a continual mount (1 in 
20) nearly as far as (5 miles) Engi (2550 feet), then fairly 
level to (7 miles) Matt (2720), when you have a further 
gradual mount of three miles to Elm (3175). What portion 
of this ten miles you can accomplish in the saddle must 
depend entirely on the condition of the roads. In this 
district they are mended with the slatier forms of limestone, 
and whilst in wet weather they are like so much glue, they 
are when dry (yet not too dry) the most lovely surface 
conceivable, so firm and yet so soft and even. This season, 
I fear, glue has prevailed ; it was, in full tenacity the day 
we were there, and I do not think we rode more than four 
of the ten miles at the outside. The descent, again, owing 
to the extremely yielding and viscous character of the 
mud — ^which let you form a rut and held you there — was 
also a troublesome business, requiring extra caution, though 
we managed to accomplish it within the hour. Elm is the 
scene of the great landslip of September in last year (1882), 
full accounts of which, with pictorial illustrations, appeared 
in all the English papers at the time. It is certainly on a 
large scale, and a most sad and terrible spectacle of destruc- 
tion ; but the newspaper descriptions had led us to antici- 
pate something of far vaster and more appalling dimensions 
than it really possesses, and though of course we were glad 



Google 



Digitized by 



110 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



in one sense that the scene of havoc should be found to be 
less extensive than one had imagined, we were undoubtedly 
disappointed with its character and extent, and I should 
think that it is hardly a tenth of the size of the Goldau 
landslip (see § 14). It is, however, probably one of the 
biggest, if not the biggest, Bergfall that has happened since 
that on the Rossberg. Entering the Linththal again at 
Schwanden, we pursued our way up the valley to Stachel- 
berg — a big hotel and bath establishment, close to the 
terminus station of the Linththal railway— where we were 
to slee^ the night. It had been our intention after lunch 
to ride further up the valley as far as the road goes — some 
six miles higher up — for the sake of the views of the Todi, 
etc., but the incessant rain prevented it. However, we had 
seen enough of the valley both from the train and from the 
saddle to appreciate its intense loveliness, and I should 
certainly, another time, leave the train earlier (at Nafels), so 
as thoroughly to enjoy the beauty of it. We rode dotvn it the 
next day, but there are limits to your power of constantly 
craning your neck round to look at the view behind 
you when 3rou are riding a bicycle, and nothing but a 
ride up the whole length of the valley on a fine day 
can give you an adequate notion of it. The ride 
back again to Zurich is for the most part downhill or- 
level. Tou should remember to bear to the left-hand side 
of the valley at Nettstall where the road divides. You 
cannot well go wrong elsewhere. I append the distances 
from the Hotel Todi, the highest point of road in the 
valley.

Glarus is paved, but rideable. The inhabitants of the 
valley are an enterprising race ; they use their streams not 
only for irrigating the fields that feed the cows, that give 
the milk, that makes the cheese they season with melitot, 
but also for turning the wheels that work the looms, that 
make the silks, that garnish the windows of the wonderful 
shop that Mr. Henneberg has just built close to the Baur 
au Lac at Zurich. On our way down the valley, on passing 
through one of these thriving villages, we overtook a posse 
of factory girls marching to work linked arm in arm, two 
deep, in rows extending over the whole breadth of the road. 



We did not ring our bells till we got just up behind them, 
when we suddenly gave full tongue. Such a stampede and 
screaming I never saw or heard before. They flew asunder 
as if a shell had burst among them, and cackled away like 
a brood of chickens into which a fox had sprung. 

" They vanish'd panic-striken, like a shoal 
Of darting fish, that on a summer mom 
Adown the crystal dykes at Oamelot 
Come slipping o'er their shadows on the sand ; 
But if a man who stands upon the brink 
But lift a shining hand against the sun. 
There is not left the twi^e of a fin 
Betwixt the cressy islets white in flower." 

The effect of this scatteration was so irrepressibly ludicrous, 
that for hours afterwards one or other of us would burst out 
mechanically into sudden spasms of laughter, and to the 
anxious inquiry " What was he laughing at ? " would come 
the invariably reply, " Oh ! those girls at Luchsingen." 
Even at this distance of time, and under the sobering in- 
fluences of Alma Mater, I can scarcely hold my pen as I 
write these lines and feel incipient vibrations of laughter 
simmering up inside me. 

Running parallel with the Glarus Valley, some thirty 
miles east of it, is the valley of the Rhine, leading to 
Ragatz and Chur and the many diverging passes beyond. 
We bad not time to investigate this valley on wheels, 
and it could of course only be made the scene of a 
bicycle expedition from Zurich by sleeping, out. We went 
over, however, by train one wet day to see the Gorge of 
Ffaefiers, quite one of the most interesting scenes in 
Switzerland. We observed that the roads were like those 
in the Glarus Valley — excellent in not too dry weather, but 
gluey in wet. The road from Ragatz up to Ffaefiers, though 
narrow, would be when in the right condition one of the 
most delightful bits of riding possible ; it is so well 
engineered, and so admirably taken care ot The Lake of 
Wallenstadt (considered by many the most picturesque of 
all Swiss lakes), which you pass in the train, is unfortu- 
nately not yet accessible on a bicycle. They are making 
roads at different places, but I fear they will require several 
seasons' use before they are fit for our purpose, as the trafiic 
is inconsiderable. 

August, 1882. Ge&aiud F. Cobb.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
Having two Race Meetings ^nd the Barnes Tricycle Meet 
on Saturday last the clerk of the weather should have been 
on his best behaviour^ instead of which he was in sulks of 
the most objectionable description. The West Kent Races, 
at the Falace, took place before about 150 spectators, who 
were as enthusiastic as possible under the circumstances. 
The only cheerful persons were the ducks, who flapped about 
the track in the most festive manner. 

Some idea of the state of the track can be gathered from 
the fact that Buckley at scratch, with a handicap made for 



Coogle"

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Ill 



2.49y did not do better than 8.7, and after the first two or 
three heats, i.e., as soon as the path became cut up, no heat 
was run under 3 minutes. 

The L.B.G. were well represented with Buckley (scratch), 
W. K. (15) and P. L. Adam (25), Wyndham (60), Beck 
(65), and C. A. Brown (85), while Jephson and H. R. Rey- 
nolds were on the ground. Unfortunately the weather put 
the back mark men out of it, and Buckley was the only one 
of our men who struggled into the second round. The only 
consolation for the scratch men in each heat was that they 
came in with clean faces, whereas the front men were one 
mass of cinder splashes from each other's hind wheels. 

Hay (85), who won the Surrey Mile, Wilson (75), of the 
Surrey, and Scott of the Lee B.C., 140 yards, were most 
&ncied in tiie second round ; they all met in the first heat, 
and a splendid race resulted in Hay's winning by about a 
yard from McEinlay (60), Wilson being close up. Buckley 
won the second heat pretty easily. 

The final, however, was set a little too soon after the 
second round, and though Buckley came magnificently in 
the last lap, he could only get second place, three yards be- 
hind Wilson, while McEinlay was third, a foot behind him. 

Time 3min. 6isec. 

• ♦ • • • • 

The Invitation Tricycle Race (One Mile)— in the final of 
which the Committee actually started three men, with a 
reckless disregard of the penalty attaching to manslaughter, 
— ^was won easily by Letchford, Radford of the L.B.G. and 
L.T.G. being second. 

The Invitation Bicycle Race (Three Miles) brought out 
a small field, Buckley, W. E. and F. L. Adam, throwing 
away moral certainties, and leaving Wyndham to represent 
the Club. The other starters were Thoinpson (Sutton), 
Tacagni (Canonbury), Vesey (Surrey), and McEinlay 
(Beckenham). Owing to the bad state of the track the 
time was very slow; Wyndham lay behind till the bell 
rang, and then, coming away in the old style, won all out 
by nearly thirty yards in 10 min. 47| sec. Needless to say 

" the victory was very popular." 

• ♦ • * • • 

At Stamford Bridge there was a Mile Handicap, the 
heats in which were won by Milner (scratch), Medcalf (60), 
and Thorn (70), Medcalf winning the final pretty easily 
from Milner in 3 min. 1} sec. ; Mihier's time of a shade 

over 3 min. 2 sec. is very good for the course and the day.

that Oxford will probably avenge the beatings of the last 

three years. As usual, nearly all the competitors will be

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
SiB, — As I find on carefully reading " Dodo's " spirited 
impeachment of the lazy friend that I fill the bill in every 
particular, except perhaps the disinclination to get up, I 
rush into print in defence of the noble army of potterers in 
response to the editorial hint. Our firiend " Dodo " appa- 
rently belongs to the I-will-get-there-or-break-a-suspender 
contingent, and maps out a tour from A to B irrespective 
of wind, weather, views, or objects of interest, and, fixing 
his cap firmly on his head, starts ofif determined to do and 
dare all that doth become a member of the L.B.C, The 
probability is that he will get there, even if he has to go on 
one leg, and verily he has his reward, for of such are Bath 
medallists made. But are the whole pleasures of bicycling 
to be compressed in getting there, or wrecking your hosiery 
in the attempt ! Apart from the pleasure that a man who 
is fit always feels in doing his level best, is not some of the 
pleasure prospective in being able to come home and play 
at Ananias with the other fellows about the headwinds you 
had and the hills you rode ? Any delay on the road, or a 
range of hiUs to be surmounted, causes anguish to the soul, 
because it reduces the record. Not so the potterer ; he is 
sublimely indifferent alike to time and distance. The hill 
has no terrors, because it promises a view, which taken in 
connection with a pipe prospectively fills his soul with 
peace, and he grinds up the hill cheerfully in anticipation 
of the promised rest. Many similar comparisons might be 
made, but they will only emphasise the indisputable fact 
expressed by "Dodo," that if the hare and the tortoise 
start on a tour together there will probably be trouble. 
But it can be looked at from both the hare's and the tor- 
toise's point of view ; and I can sympathise with "Dodo's" 
companion, who must have been temporarily a sort of 
bicycling "Jo ".in a perpetual state of being moved on. 
In conclusion, be not too rough on the lowly potterer, ye 
Scorchers, for he has his mission ; he pays his subscription,' 
gangs his ain gait, and enjoys himself and Nature according 
to his lights ; and often when placidly puffing his pipe in 
the shady nook or on the grassy bank he will discuss your 
doughty deeds on path and road, take pride in them as a 
fellow member, and ask no more than to bask undisturbed 
in the light of your reflected glory. — ^Yours, etc., 

W. R. Sbwell.

point of folly to belieye it."^ir T. Bbowns. 

One of the features of next winter is to be " Readings 
from the Works of Thackeray, by W. W." It was 

" the Ladies' Prize." 

• «k • • • • 

I should have thought the second prize would have suited 
" our popular member " better : — " Wedded, by Sir Francis 
Leighton." It would be followed, of course, by " Wedded, 
by the Archbishop of Canterbury," or some other high 
dignitary. 

One of our most prominent University members is oflf for 

the Antipodes two days before our Annual Race Meeting ! 

Opinion is divided as to whether he should be adjured by 

all he holds most dear to charter a special steamer (which 

certainly would be rather dear), or whether he shall be 

arrested on the day of departure, and let out on bail to 

ride for the Ladies' Challenge Cup. 

«••#•« 

I hope some of our members will ride down to Cambridge 

on Saturday week to see the Inter-Club Match. Here is 

a chance for our crack District (need I say I allude to 

the N.E.) : let them fix their Meet to Cambridge, and they 

will probably beat record. They can go via Chelmsford. 

• ••••• 

Well ! after Erith, I think the S.R had better abdicate. 
A district half their size, wliich has to cross the river to 
get at them, turns out more than double the number of 
their men present, /think the S.E. are rather butterflies. 

Why is the S.E. D.C. after me with a big stick ? Where 

is a convenient hole ? 

• ••••• 

The Cyclist of last week commits itself to the statement 
that the Bath Trial has never been done under ten hours 

with a head wind ! What about Walmsley's year ? 

• • • . • • • 

Up to this date our men have won eight prizes this year ; 
viz., four firsts, and four seconds. Buckley has taken two, 
and Wyndham, Leeds, Radford, W. K Adam, A. B. W. 

Whatton, and J. R. Walter one each. 

• ***•• 

I am told by a North-Easter that the scenery between 
Woolwich and Erith was as pretty as could be expected, 
considering the view was limited to 200 yards, and the 
falling rain only enabled it to be seen through half-shut 
eyes. 

• • * «^ • •

of the C»T.C. Gazette there appears the following: — 
"A Quiet Spin. Companion Walited. Average per day 
120 miles, etc." Riddle— If this is a " quiet spin," what is 
a scorch ? " 

• ••«*• 

" On revient tavjours d, son premier amcury I am glad 
to see that two of our older memoers, Gerard Smith and 
H. S. Naylor, after sundry coquettings with extras and 



tricycles have come back once more to the genuine article, 
in the shape of the ordinary bicycle. 

Saturday's rain wa^ enough to damp anything— except, 
of course, the energy of a D.C.

NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for "Gazette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdays. 



YOUR FRIENDS. 
(In answer to " Our Enemies.") 
Many thanks, most worthy Editor, for your courteous 
challenge. Tou have so spurred the sides of my feeble 
Pegasus with your " timid hints " that I must give him a 
. h'ttle exercise ; but as he will be rather heavily weighted 
with a few hard truths, his wings must be bound to keep 
him from soaring to realms of rhyme and sentiment, and 
ve will trot together on' the high road of common sense. 
Tou seem to look upon us as the most dangerous enemies 
of the L.B.C. Let us see if we deserve this character. 
(Happy thought !— as I am writing as the Championess of 
Woman, why not adopt the editorial "we," and drop the 
egotistical " I ? ") Even the " hardest and sternest riders," 
be they married or be they single, must, as years roll on, 
lose strength and taste for Club Races, Runs, and Teas. 
The sound of the race bell will not raise their pulses by 
one beat, and the glorious excitement of the last lap, with 
the spurt up the straight as rider after rider is overhauled, 
and the white tape marked first by their victorious wheel, 
can be theirs no more. But, shall the names which once 
rang through the air ring only in memory, and shall old 
friends of the L.fi.C. gaze down the Race Programme seeing 
there naught familiar? Let us look forward but a few 



years, and may we not hope for such a scene as this? 
A crowded Race Meeting of the L.B.C. The grand stand 
filled with ladies. We notice very many familiar faces — 
many looking more matronly than in 1883, and all with 
anxious eyes fixed on the course. A pistol is fired, and 
we, too, turn our eyea But what is this! The field 
looks as if we were gazing through the large end of a 
lorgnette ! Little feet and legs move little treadles, and 
tiny machines move swiftly along. The bell rings, and two 
or three small forms draw away in front. Then with a 
dreamy feeling we hear again the names familiar to us in 
past years shouted by bass voices, and even echoed by 
treble, and then amidst cheers from the one and sometliing 
like happy tears firom the other the first London Bicycle 

Club Boys' Race is won by ; but here our prophecy 

must end. Doth not a future like this make amends for 
the present irregular attendance of a few " hard and stem 
riders?" 

We will answer "timid hint" No. 1 by another 
" medical axiom," given to those suffering from disease of 
the heart— avoid woiry and excitement. To show this 
answer is applicable we must diagnose the special form of 
heart disease of which this article treats. We would refer 
you to Dr. Quain's Dictionary of Medicine^ but although 
no less than eighty pages are given up to various disorders 
and diseases of the heart, he does not mention that special 
one called Love. One of the first symptoms is a curious 
softening and tenderness, often accompanied by palpitation 
when the exciting cause of the malady is present. This 



Digitized by 



Google 













— 




tenderness causes a feeling of cLivalrous respect towards all 
women. (Be it known I treat only of the true disease, and 
not of any of the many spurious forms which are, alas, too 
often mistaken for it.) The sufferer feels as if the world 
and all things in it were better and brighter than he 
thought before. He becomes so sensitive that he cannot 
bear that anyone should guess from what he is sufifering, 
and looks upon his heart as a shrine enclosing the secret 
from all prying eyes. Solitude indeed has charms for such 
a sufferer. The treatment should be as gentle as po&sible, 
and when the fever has reached its height either the tonic of 
acceptance or the corrective of refusal will work the only cure. 
The heart will then resume i^s proper action, especially if 
treated with the tonic, and the patient will again become 
companionable, but with a brightness added to his whole life. 

Now we would ask you if that treatment is followed 
by the L.B.C. ; would it not rather be of this kind ? The 
patient is persuaded to come for a Saturday run, and no 
sooner are they fairly off than a fire of chaff is opened upon 
him, and his sacred secret (as he has fondly imagined it) 
dragged from its retreat and held up as a target for all to 
practise upon. Tennis, the river (moonlight included), and 
we weak women are not such deadly foes as that systematic 
chaff so much indulged in. Will the Club Scholiast tell us 
from what the word is derived? We read in times of 
famine that bread has been made of chaff, but not enjoyed 
with an appetite, as it was apt to stick in the throat. 
Believe us, so does very, much of the verbal chaff. To the 
professional chaffer (surely one / would express the name 
better) nothing is sacred, and in one evening he hurts more 
feelings and causes more irritation than a bad-tempered 
man would do in a week. With the latter it is easy to 
think it is only his affliction, or to shut him up in any way 
most effectual. But who can shut up the chaffer ? Most 
sensitive people would rather receive a good blow than be 
tormented by a swarm of midges, and to nothing is the 
chaffer more like than these tormenting little insects. Of 
course we do not mean to say all chaff is to be censured, 
as when occasional and good tempered, and not upon 
tender subjects, it is often amusing. But we women think 
no one ought to be chaffed about us, as we are certainly 
tender subjects, and when we sometimes hear what has 
been said about our friends, we cannot tell when 
it may be our turn to suffer in a like manner. It would be 
as well to try and stop this particular form of chaff, before 
ordering a Club Sociable. 

And now for a few words as to another foe which 
keeps some men from joining ours, I beg pardon, your 
ranks. It is the continual cycling talk both in and out 
of season. Horsey men are not always horsey, and 
vaCutsmen can fiqa flnmp rkfVioi* hnVkViv fitA^i 

jnwuvDUAi^u vrou OUlUc UhllvL UODOy bUaU XuQ WaVCS, 

but to very many cyclists all things are cycling (a new 
word). We would not dare mention such a very tender 
subject, had we not so very often heard this particular trait 


remarked upon. It is to be hoped the proposed Search 
Committee will not make the " Scientific Analysis of the 
Influence of Woman" too superficial.. We all like to feel 
our influence, but not only on tennis ground, rivers or waxed 
floor. We are almost as a body friends to bicycling, and in 
fact all outdoor sport, and though sometimes we may feel 
Saturdays a little dull, and have more than our share of 
doctoring cuts, sprains, and gravel rash, and seeing to coats 
and knicks, yet better ten thousand times to know Our Boys 
are amongst green fields on the rolling wheels, than to fear 
they are spending their time in other and far more dangerous 
ways. You may think I have taken a more serious view of 
this subject than necessary, but I shall not have written in 
vain if I induce one or two of you to look upon us not as 
your enemies but your friends. A. W. Oman.

L.B.C. MATCHES WITH THE UNIVERSITIES. 

C.U.Bi.C. V. L.B.C. 
Our Annual Match with the Cambridge University 
Bicyle Club will be held at Cambridge on Saturday, 
June 9th, and will commence at 3 p.m. Competitors 
should leave Liverpool Street Station for Cambridge by the 
11.55 a.m. train. If the weather is favourable I propose 
riding up, and shall be pleased to meet any men by 
Waltbam Cross at 8 a.m. punctually, if I hear of their 
intention to ride down by 5 p.m. on Friday. 

AsHLET W. Barrett, Captain.

CAMBRIDGE BIATCH. 
We understand that the L.B.C. team against Cambridge 
will probably be made up as follows : — 

1 Mfo.— F. L. Adam, J. D, Butler, W. Wyndham. 
4 Miles.— F. L. Adam, G. F. Beck, J. D. Butler. 
15 Miles.— W. Wyndham, H. R. Reynolds, M. H. Jephson. 

O.U.Bi.C. V. L.B.C. 
Our Annual Match with the Oxford University Bicycle 
Club will be held at the Crystal Palace neat Wednesday, 
13th instant, at 6 p.m. Tickets are obtainable from me 
at Is. each. Although members do not save anything by 
purchasing them from the Club, they are particularly 
requested to do so, as we have to settle with the Crystal 
Palace Company for one hundred tickets, whether used 
or not. After the Match the competitors will dine together 
in the Palace, and I shall be glad to receive the names of 
any gentlemen (whether members of the LB.C. or not) 
who may desire to be present. Names should be sent in to 
me not later than Monday, June 11th, in order that the 
necessary arrangements may be made. 

E. S.^ Price, Hon. Sec.

SATURDAY RUNS. 

N.E. DiSTEICT. 

June 2nd. — Notwithstanding the glorious weather only 
five men assembled at Syborn's Corner, of whom, alas ! the 
D.C. was not one ; he was reported as being engaged in 
sharpening his legal acumen in foreign climes. After some 
time spent in criticising the new tram-cars and discussing 
the route, each member modestly disclaiming any right to 
a casting vote, a somewhat late start was made vid Whip's 
Cross and Woodford. The Forest Road was then chosen, 
and found to have greatly deteriorated since last visited by 
the District. Here charming glimpses of forest scenery were 
obtained, and many longing glances cast at the shady glades 
lying below the road, but in spite of dust, a scorching sun, 
and a slight head-wind, stimulated by a sense of duty and 
the scent of the hawthorns, the party jogged on, and were 
rewarded with a better surface at the ''Wake Arms.*' 
Shortly after Weeks was sighted, enjoying d.f.n. under a 
shady tree in company with what he is pleased to term " his 
new meerschaum " (scoffers were heard to hint darkly at a 
more humble material). At Epping, Baldwin pulled up, 
and was left at the Old Thatched House" to be called for ; 
the remainder of the road was new to the majority, and 
the fine views over open country, a contrast to the thick 
wood of the forest, much appreciated. Harlow was reached 
shortly after six without a dismount by the original starters, 
and enquiries resulted in finding the Green Man " and a 
most urbane landlady, whose amiable qualities, alas ! did 
not appear to be shared by her "hired gal to this fact 
subsequent complaints as to the quality of the milk may 
possibly be attributable. Tea was, however, satisfactory 
on the whole, the interval preceding its preparation 
being spent in taking stock of a small fruit garden, 
some fine strawberry blossoms attracting special atten- 
tion, and awakening in the bosom of one individual 
certain selBsh, not to say greedy, plans for future opera- 
tions. Just as tobacco time arrived the Brothers Hough 
rode up, having rushed from Hackney in an hour 
and three quarters; after giving them breathing time, a^ 
start was effected about 8.20, amid cheery good wishes 
from two local riders at a cottage opposite ; the ride out 
having been uneventful a hind wheel kindly enlivened the 
return journey by shedding its tyre, but by the aid of some 
wire cork-screw arrangements was effectually secured. 
[Mem.— These same wires have already done good servicei 
by keeping a tyre secured for about 25 miles after the^ 
Dunstable Meet without perceptible wear.] j 

A halt was made at Epping to light up and call for 
Baldwin, but the bird had flown. The market carts wert

for once comparatively harmless, but some mconsistent 
behaviour on the part of the driver of a trap occasioned a 
momentary excitement. The usual breaking up commenced 
at the Waterworks in the Woodford Road, the Clapton 
contingent making for the Marshes, arriving at the top of 
Spring Hill without incident, whence your reporter wended 
his solitary way across Stamford Hill and reached home in 
safety. It was generally regretted that the Erith Meet had 
not been favoured with equally good weather. Present : 
Baldwin, R. B. Hough, S. G. Hough, Kelsey, Lloyd, Long, 
Weeks, and C. Y. Ltve. 

W. DiSTBICT. 

The D.C. having given notice that urgent private business 
would require his presence in another direction, the run on 
June 2nd was carried out in rather a casual manner. 
Langmore, starting from Paddington and going by way of 
Harrow, Pinner, and Rickmansworth, reached Chenies at 
6-30, where he found that four North-west men had already 
arrived and had gone down to the river for a bathe, where- 
upon he followed in search of them. As the party were 
returning to the " Bedford Arms," Naylor appeared from 
West Drayton, and soon after Oliver TTiorn came in from 
town, making with half a dozen North-west and one North- 
easter, a fairly respectable muster. After a satisfactory tea, 
four members started home vta Rickmansworth, Pinner and 
the Harrow Lanes, Naylor going back to West Drayton, 
whilst the others were left discussing the relative advantages 
of stopping where they were or going on to High Wycombe. 
Roads loose and stony in places, but fair on the whole. 
Present : J. W. Langmore, E. Naylor and 0. Thorn 



S.W. District. 
Owing to the unexpected absence of our respected D.O., 
I now take the earliest opportunity of reporting our adven- 
tures of Saturday last. Being favoured with the finest of 
weather, although with the dustiest of roads, Hawley and 
myself each pursued our own course to the "Anchor" at 
Shepperton, where, during vacuum filling, we were charmed 
with the sweet tone of the bugle by the well-known 'Arry 
and his pals. Judging from the paucity of numbers, it 
leads me to presume that the beauties of the Thames acted 
as a deterrent to the two wheeler. It seems somewhat dis- 
heartening to a member to ride from Hampstead, and, on 
arriving at his destination, to find he is the sole being until 
I arrive on the scene. Next Saturday will be the run to 
East Grinstead to join the S.E. Members present : 
A. Hawley and Habby L. Ryan. 

N.W. District. 
If things continue as they have been lately, the N.W. 
will very soon have a claim to the title of " the Little 
District ; " ji'ce men out last Saturday, and a blank run the 
week before, is a sad come down from our average last year. 
After waiting in vain at Hendon Station from 4.25 to 5.0 



o'clock, I rode via Watford to Rickmansworth, where I met 
Oliver Thorn, and we then proceeded together to Chenies, 
where we found two W. and one N.& man, besides four 
N.W. who had started from Hampstead an hour before the 
Meet time. These directed us to the bathing place, which 
we found to be about the size of the baths at the " Milford 
Arms," but not quite so deep. Returning to the house, 
we saw our friends ride off in detachments as they had 
come, while we sat down to a frugal meal, after which we 
rode together as far as Harrow, where we parted. Present : 
Elgood, Hill, Tulloch, Spencer, Stunt (N.E), and 

H. S. Jackson. 



S.E. Distriot. 
The clock of the little church at Sanderstead, one of the 
prettiest villages, by the way, in the district, had not long 
struck five, and around the palings were gathered a dozen 
or so of the South East last Saturday afternoon, on paper- 
chasing thoughts intent. On one side the two hares, 
Wyndham and Barker, conversed in low mysterious tones, 
while the faces of not a few were turned down the road, 
looking for first signs of the appeart^nce of the captain, nor 
were their expectations, thU time, doomed to disappoint- 
ment. Ten minutes had scarcely slipped away when the 
captain — and there was no mistaking the well-known form 
— was seen breasting the hill A moment more and he was 
the centre of an admiring group, all eager to welcome him. 
Meanwhile, the bares had managed to fix on their shoulders 
the bolster-shaped bags. At a quarter past five the signal 
was given, and away they sped down the Westerham 
Road. How long ? — Five minutes. Time's up ! and after 
them strained the whole pack of six and a half couples, 
led by an old veteran, the hero of many a bard 
day's sport. At Warlingham Green it was evident the 
hounds were at fault ; a false scent had led to a check, and 
from this point the hounds were for several minutes " all 
abroad." Some following real or imaginary tracks through 
ploughed fields, over hedges and down precipices, others by 
working along the roads, came upon the scent again 
casually. But before very long the leading hounds were 
giving tongue in hot chase back towards Croydon, over 
Riddlesdown. Hers Stephen Smith, who was not to be 
enticed by false trails, however artfully laid, held a long 
lead of the other hounds. Day and Hartridge came next, 
^ith the two Browns coming up fast. Up the hill past the 
infirmary and then through shady lanes, where one would 
fain linger on a fine summer's evening, the hares held a 
straight course for Banstead, and passing by the Station, 
they reached the "Glynn Arms" at 6.16, one hour and 
one minute from the start. The first hound (0. A. Brown) 
arrived 4| minutes later, having passed Stephen Smith— 
who was delayed by an untimely fall — half a mile from 
home. H. G. A. Brown, when well up, lost his place by 
taking a wrong turn at Banstead Station. The times of

EAST GRINSTEAD. 
A great many men have promised to come to next Satur- 
day's run to East Grinstead, so there is every chance of a 
really good Meet. The result of the Match with Cambridge 
will be telegraphed to the " Railway Arms," where we shall 
have tea at 7 o'clock. The Croydon Division will leave the 
Fire Station at 4.30 punctually. This will allow the 
Blackheath men a quarter of an hour's grace. 

R. G. Faanoib, District Captain. 

WEST DISTRICT. 
On account of the races at Cambridge on Saturday next, 
and as a great number of members will consequently be 
there, the run has been altered, by special request, from 
Leith Hill to East Grinstead, in order to meet the S.E. at 
their favourite rendezvous. Those who can manage to go, 
kindly meet at Barnes Station at 4 o'clock. 

H. C. PiooTT, District Captain.

BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(COMTIMUBD FBOM PAOB 110.) 

§ 21.-'Zurich to Schaffhausen, and the Folk qf the Bhine, 
The RhioefallB are often spoken of as a somewhat ''hack- 
neyed " sight, such as one is bound to see once in one's life 
for the sake of maintaining a position in society and not 
being taken at a disadvantage in the intercourse of the 
dinner table. But to my mind there are few sights so 
enchanting, if you only give yourself time to enjoy it at 
full leisure, and to make the regulation expeditions to the 
different points of view provided for you. If Zurich be the 
point from which you visit them I recommend you to take 
your bicycle by train to the Dachs^ station. There is a 
fairly quick train leaving Zurich at 7.20 and reaching 
Dachsen at 9.0. This will give you full leisure for enjoying 
the falb. You will ride from Dachsem to (f mile) Schloss 
Laufen, where you leave your bicycle in the courtyard. 



The road from Laufen to Schaffhausen mounts up a bit at 
first, and the top bit (after passing a fork where you keep 
to the left) may very likely be found stony. Soon after 
this you turn into the main road from Zurich to Schaff- 
hausen, and have a lovely descent of some 2^ miles into 
the town ; the road is so good that on returning we rode 
u]) it a^ain the whole way against a very strong wind. 
The following is the route from Schaffhausen to Zurich : — 

... Dachaen (RheinfaU). 

... Schaffbauaen. 

... Benken. 

... OrUnffen. 

... Andemngen. 

... HettUngen. 

... Oringen. 

... Winterthur. 

... T5sa. 

. . . Eupf erhaminer. 

... Tagelschwangen. 

... Schwameiidingen. 

... Zurich.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Besides these levels, yon have to rise about 300 feet 
between Schafifhausen and Benken, 200 between Eupfer- 
hammer and Tagelschwangen, and another 200 between 
Schwamendingen and Zurich. No occasion to dismount, 
unless for the hill in Andelfingen. It is a broad high road 
all the way, on which you cannot well go wrong. At the 
fork just beyond Toss both roads lead to Zurich — the left 
or lower one is the main road (Haupt Strasse), but it is 
quite likely that the other is just as good. The shell of 
the old Minster Church at Schaffhausen is interesting, but 
as regards fittings it is without any exception the meanest 
building of an ecclesiastical description I was ever in, and 
does not even boast of an organ. Winterthur is a nice 
bright pleasant little town with a very lively station. It is 
worth while to ride a bit about the streets. You should 
lunch at the "Lion cFOr*' and ask for some ''Schloss 
Teuffen," a most excellent red wine at 2f. 50c. the bottle. 
Musical members will be interested to know that Hermann 
Goetz, the composer of " The Taming of the Shrew," whose 
early death we all deplore, was for some time organist at 
Winterthur, in succession to Eirchner, a pianoforte com- 
poser of some note, who is still living at Leipzig. It has 
the reputation of being the most musical town in Switzer- 
land. The Winterthur road enters Zurich by the Rami 
Strasse at the back of the University Buildings, and you 
should follow this throughout, as that part of the town is 
very steep, and short cuts would only land you in diffi- 
culties. The Leonliard Gasse in particular, which curves 
down from the /rant of the University Buildings to the 
bridge near the Railway Station, should be carefully 
avoided. The upper part of it looks tempting, but round 
the corners below it is very dangerous indeed. 

§ 22.-'Zurich to Constanz and back by Schaffhausen. 

From Zurich either ride or take the train to Winterthur; 
if you ride, the other road to Toss, alluded to in the last 
section, might be followed for variety. Similarly, if you 
rode back from Schaffhausen, an alternative road through 
Marthalen might perhaps be taken for a bit of the way, 
but I have not been along it. Unless, however, you send a 
bag and sleep at Constanz, all you would be able to do in the 
day is to ride from Winterthur via Constanz to Schaff- 
hausen, taking the train at both ends for the rest of the 
way. The course can be further shortened by taking train 
to Zurich from Stein instead of from Schaffhausen.

Frauenfeld is worth a dismount irrespective of paving. 
From Pfyn you have a pretty steady mount of 600 feet, no- 
where unridable, to a mile beyond Sonterswylen, when you 
descend the same amount to Constanz. From Constanz to 
Schaffliausen you follow the Rhine all the way, and there 
are no hills to speak of after the first short pull up to the 
turning in Tagerwylen. The villages you pass through on 
the side of the Rhine are extremely pretty, and the houses 
have quite a style of their own. The " Constanzer-Hof " is 
a very good hotel, but Constanz itself is a most depressingly 
dull place — a kind of city of the dead. There is a good 
bathing place near the "Constanzer-Hof." The lake was 
very rough the day we were there, and (as Baedeker 
touchingly puts it) I went very near to experiencing all 
the horrors of seasickness." Our ride to, or rather towards, 
Schaffliausen was in the teeth of the very strongest gale I 
ever experienced in the saddle. By the time we got to 
Stein we were quite worn out with our exertions in 
buffeting it, despite our then nearly two months' training, 
80 we took to the train. The Rhine was lashed into regular 
breakers, and the colours of the river were most varied as 
well as lovely, according to the variations of exposure to 
the wind and the broken lights of cloud and sunshine 
playing upon it. 

August, 1882. Gerabd F. Cobb.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
The O.U.Bi.C. trials to select representatives for the 
match against Cambridge were held on the 0.n.A.C. 
Running Ground on Thursday, May 24th. 

• ••••• 

As Walter and Buckley had up to the week before been 
rowing in their College eights, and were consequently 
somewhat out of form, and as W. E. Adam was not up yet, 
the Committee decided to postpone the Twenty-five Miles. 

Two Miles Race.—W. F. M. Buckley, Ch. Ch., 1; 
W. M. Merry, Exeter, 2 ; L. Andrews, Ch. Ch., 0. Merry 
led for four laps, after which Buckley went to the front 
and won by 30 yards. Time 6min. 22}sec. 

# • • • • • 

Ten Miles Race.'-YI. F. M. Buckley, Ch. Ch., 1 ; D. R. 
Smith, Queen's, 2 ; W. A. G. Walter, Exeter, 3 ; 
G. Stapleton, Oriel, 0. Smith led for five miles, when 
Buckley passed him, and, going away, won by 200 yards ; 
Smith, after a capital race, beat Walter on the post for 
second place ; Stapleton close up. Time 33niin. 3sec. 
« • • • « • 

The Cambridge Trials for the " Inter Varsity " were held 
on Thursday afternoon at Cambridge, when J. S. Whatton 
beat Day in the Two Miles by four yards, in the capital 
time of 5.54j^. He also won the Ten Miles by a yard from 
his brother, in the slow time of 33.19f . The Twenty Five 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



119 



Miles resulted unexpectedly in the victory of Carr of Trinity 

by a few inches from Day, Olive being well up third. 

Time Ih. 24m. 17}sec. 

««••«« 

I am sure all our members will regret to hear of the 
resignation by Mr. Cobb, of the presidencies of the Bicycle 
Union and of the C.U.Bi.C. He has held each office prac- 
tically since the foundation of each society. To him indeed 
the B.U. owes its existence and early training, and he has 
been the life and soul of the Cambridge Club for many 
years. I am glad to hear that the members of the latter 
Club have determined to present him with some permanent 
memorial of his connection with the Club, which will pro- 
bably take the form of an addition to that musical library 
which many members of the L.B.C. have become acquainted 
with. More than £50 was collected among members of the 
Club in a fortnight towards this end. 

«••#•• 

The next Council Meeting of the B.U. is fixed for 
Thursday, June 14th, at the " City Arms Tavern." Among 
the chief matters on a very lengthy agenda paper are the 
change of name. The Executive recommend that the name 
be changed, and that " National " form- part of it, but find 
themselves unable to move anything more definite. Mr. Todd 
moves, "The National Cyclists' Union," Mr. Hampton 
Roberts, " The National Wheelmen's Union." The subject 

appears promising for talk. 

• *••«• 

An elaborate code of regulations for appeals from suspen- 
sions has been drawn up, and is to be submitted to the 
meeting. A. B. W. Whatton continues his appeals to out- 
siders by a motion designed to draw manufacturers into the 
fold. The Council will also be asked to consider racing 
tracks and advertising the value of prizes. 

But the most important resolution stands in the name of 
of Mr. Todd and is as follows : — 

" That from this date any Amateur wilf uUy competing at Sports not held 
nnder the rnles of the Union, or rules approved by the Union, shall be 
liable to be suspended for such time as the Executive shall think fit, 
subject to the same right of appeal as in suspensions for other offences.* 

This is a similar rule to that of the Amateur Athletic 
Association, and we hope very much that the Council will 
see its way to adopt it. It will do more than anything else 
to purify the racing branch of the sport. 

• • • • ' « • 

Our Evergreen Member is shooting forth with renewed 
vigour. He scored another win in a two mile handicap on 
Thursday last at an evening meeting of the L.A.C. at 
Stamford Bridge. Time 5.54^, with 70 yards from Milner. 

• ' • « ♦ • • 

Fopplewell of the Ipswich B.C. did a good performance in 
the Annual Five Mile Road Race of his Club on the Norwich 
Road ; his time for the distance being 15 miu. 30 sees. 

The Front Wheel Hub. 



THE INTER-UNIVERSITY BICYCLE RACES. 
The Inter-University Races took place at Oxford on 
Tuesday. The weather was fine, but the path very loose, 
the wind raising thick clouds of dust, and making the times 
bad by hurrying the riders down the hill at such a speed 
that they had to ease at the lower comer, and at times 
almost bringing them to a standstill coming up the 
straight. 

Two Miles Race.— W. F. M. Buckley, Oxford, 1; 
J. S. Whatton, Cambridge, 2 ; C. L. Schwind, Cambridge, 
3 ; J. R. Walter, Oxford, fell. One of the slowest races 
ever seen among first-class riders. Buckley led with 
Whatton at his heels, either waiting for the other until 150 
yards from home, when Whatton drew up nearly level, 
Buckley however responded, and quickly obtained a lead 
of five yards, by which distance he won. Time, 6.37. 

Ten Miles Race.— W. F. M. Buckley, Oxford, 1 ; J. S. 
Whatton, Cambridge, 2 ; J. Stapleton, Oxford, 3 ; L. C. Carr, 
Cambridge, ; A. W. Rumney, Cambridge, ; W. M. 
Merry, Oxford, 0. At the end of the seventh mile 
Stapleton was leading, followed by Whatton and Buckley. 
At this point Buckley suddenly took the other competitors 
by surprise by putting on tremendous steam and com- 
pletely running away from them all ; Whatton was quickly 
in pursuit, but after riding very pluckily, a hopeless stern 
chase was defeated by 300 yards. Time, 32 min. 

Twenty-five Miles Race. — W. K. Adam, Oxford, 1 ; 
W. A. G. Walter, Oxford, 2; E. T. Olive, Cambridge, 
3 ; 0. D. Day, Cambridge, ; C. Fagan, Cambridge, ; 
E. G. Frazer-Luckie, Oxford, 0. At twenty miles, the 
order was Walter, Day, Adam, and this continued until 
rounding the bottom curve for the last time, when Day 
passed Walter ; Adam put on one of his finest spurts on 
entering the straight, and forty yards from the finish had 
just taken a slight lead of Day, when most unfortunately 
the latter fell, and left Adam to finish 10 yards in front of 
Walter. Day was not hurt, with the exception of a few 
small cuts. Time, 1 hr. 27 min. 12 sec.
