NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reoeption of oopy for "Gazette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdays. 



THE EDITOR. 

Under tbis heading we do not propose to give an auto- 
biography of ourselves, which would be of little interest to 
our teaden, but to discuss shortly— ^ery shortly — the 
various duties, difficulties and cares which appertain to an 
office which we humbly submit is of some importance. 

The first and principal duty of an Editor is to be strictly 
impartial, and he should not neglect to be punctual : to 
exercise a complete control over his temper, which will be 
sorely tried. 

How difficult it is to explain to a contributor that his 
copy is irrelevant and cannot be published without hurting 
his feelings, and how much is the difficulty increased when 
the article is the result of a special appeal for copy. Take, 
as an instance, the present effusion. We can imagine our 
worthy Editor tearing his auburn locks when he peruses it, 
and exclaiming — Oh, that we had never asked that man ! 
How ttuly weak is this ! How are we to reject without 
incurring undying enmity ? And then we can see the copy 
being hurled into the waste paper basket, and an expression 
of unbending firmness coming over that face we all love so 
welL His emotion overpowers him. He cannot work, so 
he lights his pipe, and presently the unbending features 
become less stem, a laissez aUer^kiud of smile appears ; 



the copy is picked out from the basket with the tongs and 
is sent upstairs to be set up. Then comes temptation. 
Shall we have a direful revenge. It is fiill well in his 
power ; but no, the Club knew it could trust the man when 
it selected him, and it was right, he decides not to publish 
the name of the writer. 

Want of copy is a great source of care. Of padding 
there is usually plenty;, but it is the absence of those 
articles, quills and interesting letters, that is the Editor's 
real trial. He could probably write them himself if he 
could only think of good subjects, but having composed 
article after article, quill after quill, for past numbers, he 
gradually reduces his stock of subjects which L oked so in- 
exhaustible when he took the reins, and longs for ideas. 
So, dear fellow members, if you would help the Editor, 
send up ideas ; never mind how crude in form, he will put 
them into shape. 

There is one peculiarity about this important post which 
we must not pass over without honourable mention. It is, 
we believe, the only office in the Club into which the 
elected one is duly installed— or, speak more correctly, in- 
stalls himself— with due ceremony. The first act of the 
new hand is to invest in a postcard, on which be proceeds 
to indite a humble request that his predecessor will consent 
to honour him with his presence at dinner at an early date, 
and not neglect to bring the contents of the "tub" with 
. him. This is not very difficult, for, although from a sense 
of amour propre, the Editor will not allow that the '* tub " 
is not brimming over with good material, we may perhaps 



Digitized by 



Google 



122 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



80 far let our readers into State secrets bs to divulge the 
harrowing fact that the contents of the aforesaid tub can 
occasionally be carried in a waistcoat pocket. 

The invitation being accepted, and the ex-Editor having 
been beguiled into the banqueting hall, he is feasted right 
royally and plied with as many different kinds of wine as 
he can be induced to take. The moment arrives when, 
with Spartan like firmness, he refuses the sparkling nectar, 
and he is then conducted to the billiard room, and after 
being supplied with the balmiest Havanas, he is informed 
that the ladies "like tobacco!" When the Havana is 
nearly consumed — peace be to its ashes ! — the subject of 
the Gazette is introduced in the most unsuspicious manner, 
and the victim having been complimented on the brilliancy 
of the articles published during the past year which are 
assumed to be his— though probably he never wrote one of 
them — he is gradually and skilfully led to initiate the 
neophyte in all the treasured secrets which ordinary mortals 
must not know. The process of pumping having been 
successfully brought to the driest of ends the pumped one 
is consigned to bed, and next morning, after having plunged 
into a bath of the temperature of 32^ which he pretends to 
enjoy, is taken up to town and dismissed. 

Having described the sublime and pleasing ceremony of 
installation, we might proceed to initiate our readers into 
many other editorial mysteries, but honestly we dare not ; 
the stern visage of our Captain seems to appear before us 
and forbid us to continue, eo we conclude with a pleasing 
consciousness that we have written much and told little. 



NOTICE.— SPECIAL GENERAL MEETING. 
The Special General Meeting, to discuss and decide the 
advisability of encouraging Tricyclists to join our ranks, 
will be held at the " City Anns Tavern," St. Mary Axe, on 
Monday, 18th June, at 6.30 p.m. 

Agenda. 

The CommitteeVill recommend — 

" Thttt after haying oarefnlly cooaidered the ponUon of TricydistB 
in the Club, and the question of encouraging TricydiBts to join our 
ranks, they are of opinion that no alteration is advisable in the Club 
Rules or organisation, and that it is not expedient to hold a Road Trial 
for Tricyclistg." 

To consider the following motions : — 
Motions by T. E. Scrutton — 

I. In Rule 11, to insert before "members of the Committee'* 
" past and present," and to insert after such words " or member of the 
Club, being at the time an officer of either of the O.XJ.Bi.C. or 
C.U.BLC." 

II. To add to Rule 5 " not being members of any other Bicycle or 
Tricycle Club having its headquartav within twenty miles of London." 

Motion by W. J. Williams-^ 

In Rule 2, for the Prizes for Attendance at Club Meets, to alter the 
words " to the extent of the prescribed course," so as to read " to a 
point ten miles distant from the starting place.*' 

£. S. Pbics, Hon. Sec.

SATURDAY RUNS. 

N.E. DiSTEICT. 

Saturday, June 9th. Vienna, — Ty suis, fy reste, was 
the D.C/s terse account of himself. So without their 
leader four members left Sybom's Corner, and took the 
usual route through Chigwell and Abridge, the roads, as 
might have been expected, being found very dusty. At 
Fassingford Bridge a noisy spring was sufficient excuse for 
a short rest, and an unfortunate water rat was made the 
target for a shower of stones. Then on by the main road 
till the turning to Shonk's Mill was reached, and the dust 
and traffic of the high road exchanged for the seclusion of 
narrow lanes, now up, now down, now twisting and turning 
in a manner that would put to blush those ignorant beings 
who talk of Essex as flat and uninteresting. The aspect of 
the country, now wearing its prettiest garb, was frequently 
admired — the trees, untouched by the cold winds that so 
spoilt their beauty last year, having as yet lost none of 
their freshness. At two or three different points it was 
noticed that the timber was being felled, and roots 
grubbed up, and a rustic volunteer§4 tbe information 
that the Baron (Lord Carlingford) was clearing the 
ground. From the watersplash the road was stony to 
Navestock Common, where Child felt hungry, and fearing 
lest Blackmore would be capable of satisfying his appetite, 
he decided to stay at the ''Green Man." The other 
three rode on, and, after fighting their way through 
numberless Essex calves, reached Blackmore about 6.30. 
The small village was scanned for a hostelry, and in the 
process of searching the party came upon the church, which 
has a remarkable wooden tower of three stories. The door 
being open, an inspection of the interior was made — 
an altar tomb with effigies of some Elizabethan worthies, 
and a fine piece of stained glass called forth much admi- 
ration. Adjoining the church is a house that bears the 
name of Jericho, the previous building having been used 
by Henry VIII. as a country seat, and at his occasional 
disappearance his courtiers were wont to observe that 
"his Majesty had gone to Jericho." A native having 
recommended the "Leather Bottle," the machines were 
stabled ; and just as Long wa^ tryi&g to impress the 
landlord with the extent of our appetites, Weeks and 



Arnott on a " Sociable," and Lyne appeared, followed just 
as tea was ready by Stunt, who had ridden round by 
Chelmsford. The meal was chiefly remarkable for the 
scarcity of the milk— if it could be obtained anywhere, one 
would think that this would be about the best place ; but, 
no ! the only way to get it, as was suggested, would be to 
wait for the next train from London. Just as tea was 
finished the Treasurer rode in, having left Hackney at 
six o'clock. After the "Sociable" had been tried and 
approved of by all, it was thought desirable to make a 
start; but a considerable delay occurred, owing to two 
members having mysteriouly disappeared. However, they 
turned up as mysteriously as they had disappeared, and 
the return journey was made by way of Dudbrook, instead 
of Navestock Common— a shorter and more pleasant way, 
though there was scarcely light enough to appreciate the 
scenery. On reaching the Ongar road, lamps were lit, and 
a steady pace maintained for the rest of the way, though 
the riders of the " Sociable " found it rather too slow. 
It is hoped that the B.C. will take note of this, as the 
Treasurer is anxious to atone for past sins, notably on the 
occasion of the Dunstable Meet. Near Walthamstow the 
party gradually broke up, and it is presumed they all 
reached home in safety. Present: Arnott, Child, K. B. 
Hough, Kelsey, Lloyd, Long, Lyne, Stunt, and Weeks. 



S.W. DiSTBIOT. 

The S.W. storm, (for such it must be styled, as its un- 
fortunate influence appears to have been btit little exerted 
elsewhere on Saturday last), was sufficiently copious to act 
as a strong deterrent to those of our members who would 
doubtless have been filled, under ordinary circumstances, 
with something more practical than good intentions. 
Starting from home as the downpour was commencing, I 
was wet through ere I reached that convenient cabmens' 
shelter adjoining the Kingston Station. Here I waited 
patiently for any welcome companion, but in vain, and my 
only consolation, as I watched the violent and drenching 
rain, was a distant ray of brightness in the sky that sug- 
gested faintly that the weather might clear. The hoped for 
termination of thunder and rain took place about 4.35 p.m., 
and consequently, after leaving word for the benefit of any 
adventurous member, that the "District" had left for the 
double gates at Merton, I betook myself thither in the hope 
of finding some members of the W. who were understood to 
be likely to join us at this point. So local had the storm 
proved that my dripping condition occasioned much sur- 
prise on the way to Merton, and provoked frequent 
inquiries from passing cyclists as to the condition of things 
in the direction from whence I had travelled. Merton was 
reached at 5 p.m., and half an hour was spent on the con- 
stant alert for W. members, but as no single L.B.C. man 
was forthcoming, I took the road to Mitcham, proceeding 
thence through Sutton and by Burgh Heath to Reigate 



Digitized by 



124 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Hill, which was reached about 6.45 p.m. A reference to a 
map at this point showed painfully clearly that some 
seventeen more miles had to be covered ere I could reach 
the " Railway Hotel " at East Grinstead, and consequently 
that one and a half hour's more of solitary journeying must 
be undergone before I could hope to indulge in the varied 
pleasures of an extensive Club tea. These were decidedly 
unsatisfactory reflections, and further progress being 
apparently almost useless, I completed my afternoon of 
disappointment by turning the wheels for home, and thus 
afiforded myself ample opportunity for meditating on the 
sins of the District. I could, however, arrive at no satis- 
factory conclusion as to the best method of remedying our 
occasional shortcomings. On returning to Kingston, I met 
Buckler, who had been unavoidably prevented from attend- 
ing the run. Again, further on I found Sewell and Bamett, 
whose energies had apparently been expended in a some- 
what different direction, whilst Ryan was presumably busy 
on the track preparing for the District Challenge Cup. In 
conclusion, let me remind members that the meeting place 
next Saturday is at the Crystal Palace, where I tnist that 
the whole District will be found. I would also call the 
particular attention of members to the following Saturday, 
the 23rd inst., when we make our annual excursion to 
Newlands Corner and that Silent Pool, full of romantic 
traditions, even at so remote a period as the reign of King 
John. We shall have tea as usual at Albury. As our 
worthy Captain has promised to pay us a visit upon this 
occasion, we must all endeavour to obtain an exceptionally 
large muster of our members as a proof of our appreciation 
of the honour he is doing us, and at the same time we 
cordially invite members of other Districts to assist us in 
giving him a hearty welcome. 

• R. M. ORUGaEN, District Captain. 

S.K DffiTRIOT. 

The air was sultry, and thunder-clouds were hanging 
over the the tops of the surrounding hills when a small 
party of five left the Fire Station on Saturday for East 
Grinstead. Just before reaching Caterham we were over- 



taken by H. G. A. Brown, and at the ''Clayton Arms" we 
found J. Kinder, J. F. Potter, and McRae waiting for us. 
The road on as far as Blindley Heath was dusty and more 
than usually rough, but its superficial evils were borne with 
the usual resignation, or even contentment. A heavy 
shower had just passed off when we reached the foot of 
Persuasion Hill, and with the exception of one whose 
untiring zeal for records had led him to act as our pioneer, 
we of the main body were fortunate enough to arrive at 
Qur destination with dry skins. Here our forces were sup- 
plemented by F. B. Smith, Morgan and his brother, H. N. 
Sharp, and last, hut not least, The O'Reilly, who had 
encountered a succession of showers on his way up from 
Brighton. Wyndham's telegram announcing the victory of 
our men at Cambridge came as a pleasant surprise, and soon . 
after we had sat down to tea J. W. Potter (another pleasant 
surprise) arrived and made up the number to sixteen and 
two visitors. Present : H. G. A. Brown, Day, Hartridge, 
S. Herbert, J. Kinder, McRae, Morgan, W. C. Oswald, The 
O'Reilly, J. F. Potter, J. W.' Potter, Sharp, F. B. Smith, 
F. H. Williams, F. M. Williams (W.) ; Morgan and A 0. 
Potter (visitors), and 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 



W. DiSTEICT. 

Saturday, 9th. Only one member. F. M. Williams 
arrived at East Grinstead. No doubt the distance and the 
Cambridge races combined to prevent others from getting 
to the favourite haunt of the S.E. The D.C. started, but 
owing to the heavy storm of rain which descended, one 
hour's delay was caused, rendering it therefore an im- 
possibility to ride so far and to return the same evening. 
The next run is on Saturday week to Stanmore, to meet 
the N.W., when I hope all will have recovered from the 
effects of the multiplicity of runs which are taxing the 
utmost efforts of all the District, and that we shall see a 
large muster in atonement for the preceding blank runs. 
Present : F. M. Williams. 

H. C. PiaoTT, District Captain.

Kheinfelden. 


581 


Bale. 



It was stated in one of my earlier letters that the distance 
from Bftle to Zurich was 56 mile& That is the rail distance ; 
I find the road distance is a little less, as it cuts off some 
comers. This was our last ride preparatory to packing up 
our bicycles at B4le and wending homewards. There is one, 
and only one considerable change of elevation in the route, 
and that is the passage of the Botzberg or mountain range 
separating the valley of the Aare from that of the Bhine. 
The mount up from Brugg is about 800 feet. In riding 
from B4le to Zurich a dismount would be necessary at the 
top of the last steep quarter of a mile down into Brugg, for 
the road is very rough tfnd you have a sharp turn on to a 
narrow bridge at the bottom, and then, a steep pull up the 
paved streets. Except Rheinfelden, which is utterly un- 
rideable, we knew this would be our last pavement, so we 
finished off by riding down it. We had made an early 
start from Zurich, too early to admit of our getting any- 
thing but a little bread and butter for breakfast, but .on 
this and the delicious morning air we jode the whole way 
from Zurich to Rheinfelden (43 miles) in 4 hours and 20 
minutes, all hills and halts included. The villages through 
which we passed after crossing the BotzWg were a sad con- 
trast to the bright, clean, picturesque villages of other parts 
of Switzerland. Being so near the Black Forest, the in- 
habitants of the Canton B&le seem to share the manners 
and customs of their Baden neighbours, and love to line 
their streets with pestilential heaps of 'lihe fertiliser." The 
plastered houses, too, all have a mean and squalid aspect, 
utterly unlike that of the carved and festooned chalets of 
other Cantons. After having a good lunch and rest at 
Rheinfelden, we crossed the bridge and rode to B&le by the 
Baden side. This is by far the best road of the two, but we 
found that one place where we had had to dismount two 
months previously, as they were altering levels, had not yet 
settled down into a rideable condition. We reached our 
old quarters at the " Hotel Euler " about one o'clock, and 
by four, thanks to the invaluable assistance of our good 
friend Qustav, the bicycles were cleaned, dismembered, and 
safely ensconsced in their crates. I maj^ mention that they 
only took eight days on their way to Cambridge, instead of 



thirteen, so that the Oreat Eastern Railway Company were 
correct in telling me previously that they might be expected 
to take seven or eight days in transit. It is apparently 
cheaper to send a bicycle as one of two on the same note of 
consignment than to send it separately, as you get the 
advantage of a reduced rate on the joint weight. The 
" Centaur," which had been despatched by the same route 
previously by itself, cost 13s 4d to reach Cambridge; I paid 
16s 8d for the other two, a trifle more than they cost going 
out, probably on account of the greater ''terminal" charges 
at this end. 

August, 1882. Gerabd F. Cobb. 

(To be continued.) 

L.B.C. f^. C.U.BLC. 
Fortune has at last smiled upon us, and the winning of 
all three events on Saturday last should compensate for our 
defeats in the three previous matches with the C.U.Bi.C- 
The match was run on the ground of the latter, and was 
well attended, among the spectators being Koch, Jackson, 
Hindley, 0. Thorn, and Ashley Barrett, all of whom had 
ridden up, also C. A. Brown and M. H. Dicker, who had 
travelled by train. Though the weather was unspeakably 
hot, the course was in really first rate order, thanks to the 
careful watering it had undergone on the previous day from 
the Cambridge officiak. Without doubt our opponents 
suffered greatly in being deprived of the services of their 
Captain, J. S. Whatton, and the ill-health that prevented 
him from running is as much a matter of regret to our men 
as to the members of his own club. For the London, 
Ashley Barrett acted as judge, and J. 8. Whatton for the 
Cambridge ; the starter being H. Nicholls. Details of the 
racing are as follows : — 

OzfB Mile. 

Heat 1.— F. L. Adam, London, beat 0. D. Day, Cam- 
bridge, by 10 yards in 3 min. 1 sec. Adam went ahead in 
the last lap in spite of a fly, which, finding its way into his 
left eye, almost prevented him from seeing the course, and 
subsequently in the Four Miles Race hampered him very 
considerably. 

Heat 2.— A. B. W. Whatton, Cambridge, beat J. D. 
Butler, London, by 3 yards in 3 min. 4 sees. Our man led 
till last lap, when Whatton went to the fore, though Butler 
spurted gamely. 

Heat 3.— Wyndham, London, beat C. A. S. Pollock, 
Cambridge, by 12 yards in 3 min. 4| sec. Our man led 
throughout, and went away in the last lap.

This was a fine race, the men being close up throughout. 
At the end of the first mile Beck took the leading place, 
which ho held to the finish, completing the whole distance 
in the good time of 12 min. 26j sec, being separated from 
the last man by not more than 14 yards. It may be noted 
that the time for this race is 12|8ec. better than that for 
the same race over the same course in 1881, and is 438ec. 
better than that for the corresponding race last year. 
Whatton was reported to have been shut out, but then 
Adam's mishap prevented him from doing full justice to 
his powers, so we may not speculate too freely upon what 
would have happened had things been otherwise. The 
first mile was done in 3min. 18sec, Westcott leading, 
closely followed by Adam and the rest of the field ; two 
miles were ridden in 6min. 26sec., the order being Beck, 
Adam, Westcott ; three miles in 9min. 32sec., the order 
being Beck, Westcott, Adam.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
It blew a small gale at Oxford on the 5th, when the 
Inter-Varsity Races were held. This, no doubt, favoured 
the home team, who knew the track, and could negotiate it 
better under such circumstances than the Cambridge men. 



I never saw a more brilliant and better sustained spurt 
than Buckley's in the Ten Miles. He simply walked away 
from his men in the commencement of the eighth mile, and 
went on increasing his lead until the finish. At a large 
and enthusiastic meeting this would have meant^a trium- 
phal progress to the dressing room on the shoulders of 

frantic admirers. 

• # # • • • 

What a contrast to the Bicycle Union Championship 
Meeting at Leicester in 1881, when the same rider was 
allowed 75 yards start in the One Mile Handicap ! 

• »•«•• 

And what sincere regret there is to think that he may not 
be able to compete for any of the Championships of 1883. 

• ••«•• 

Day's fall within fifty yards of home, after a finely con- 
tested race, in which he uniformly maintained second place, 
was most unfortunate. The exact cause is not known, but I 
think he leant too far forward in his sudden anxiety to keep 
ahead of Adam who was just on level terms with him when 
he feU. 

*••«•* 

J. R. Walter was very little hurt after his fall in the first 
lap of the two miles funeral procession. 

Many L.B.C. men were on the ground, and Stephen 
Smith was Referee and C. A. Pollock one of the Judges, his 
colleague being the Rev. W. D*A. Crofton. 

• «•••• 

Buckley was rowing in the Christchurch boat, and is now 
in training with the four for Henley, with a day off" for 
the L.B.C. match. 

** That's where the corpses go," said a C3mical Cambridge 
man as he assisted Day, after his smash at Oxford, to a 
full length position on the long table in the Pavilion, to 
which Douglas Smith had been carried two nights before. 
There were three accidents while the C.U.Bi.C. team was 
at Oxford. 

Day's pluck in coming out again on behalf of his Club 
four days after his fall was on every tongue on Saturday. 
The fifteen miles was his last race, and his genial company 
and pretty style will be greatly missed at Cambridge and 
elsewhere. 

*««*«« 

The meeting of the Pickwick B.C. at the Crystal Palace 
on Saturday last was a great contrast to the West Kent 
meeting a fortnight before, in weather, times, and atten- 
dances. While at the West Kent only two heats were run 
under 3 minutes, and the scratch man could not do better 
than 3min. 7sec., last Saturday it was the exception for 
heats to take more than 2min. SOsec. ; and Thompson, of 
the Sutton B.C., with 20 yards, did three miles equal to 
2min. 48^860., 2min. 48sec., 2min. 49sec., from scratch.

The Open Mile was won by Frosti of tlie Brixton 
Ramblers B.C., with 120 yards start, who oddly enough

by a foot ; Hay, (55 yards start), third, a foot behind ; 
while Thompson, (20 yards start), was close up. The 
handicapping was a triumph for the Union handicapper, 
as the second round also gave some very good finishes. 
««•••• 

The only London men riding were W. E. Adam from 
scratch, and Leeds with 80 yards start, but neither was 
successful. Adam was asked to do 2min. 47fsec. in his 
heat.

32| sec, and Radford did 1 min. 32^ sec. in his heat. 
Tricycling records are suffering.

The C.U.Bi.C. held their Club Races on Monday last. 
The chief feature on the programme was the Five Miles 
Invitation Race, which resulted in a dead heat between 
Whish and Palmer, the latter coming up with a tremen- 
dous rush at the finish : A. B. W. Whatton was third, 
25 yards behind ; and Wyndham fourth. The time was 
poor, 16min. 35sec., though the last quarter of a mile was 
done in 38|sec. 

Thb Fkont Wheel Hub. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
Sir, — As an Essex calf, I beg to protest most emphatically 
against an expression of " Porcupine's," in the last isssue 
of the Gazette, about " Essex Flats and Essex Mole Hills." 
Does " Porcupine " know the road from four miles beyond 
Cambridge to Linton, and on through Sible, Hedingham, 
and Halstead to Lexham, near Colchester? Flat ! ! ! The 
scenery is not touched within the four seas ; and where 
there is any level, it is to allow the cyclist to enjoy a road 
which goes through avenues of glorious trees, like a magni- 
ficent park. Does *' Porcupine " know the view from a gate 
near Stondou ? The valley of Ongar first, and the ridge 
of hill beyond is something that even my father, who loves 
the pavement of Oxford Street, and was heard to remark 
last winter when following the hounds on foot along a 
muddy lane, that he wished there were some nice flagstones 
under foot, went into positive raptures over. " Mole Hills," 
indeed ! Has " Porcupine " ridden up South Weald, or 
Theydon Bois hills?— I mean the one between Theydon 



and Epping, not that between Theydon and the "Wake 
Arms." I grant that there is a narrow strip of fiat by the 
river by Tilbury. 

May I be pardoned for telling a little tale ? An uncle 
of mine was telling me the other day of some dreadful 
property he knew of in Essex which the proprietor left to 
someone in his will, saddled with a condition that the 
happy recipient should live there six months in the year, 
as the heir presumptive had been heard to say, that nothing 
would ever make him live in the place. I remarked, the 
property must have been near Tilbury. " Well," said my 
uncle, " I don't know the geography of Essex. The place 
was near Orsett." — Yours, etc., 

C. H. F. Christie. 



THE LAWN TENNIS QUESTION. 
To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sm, — Without attempting to criticise the wisdom of 
the Club identifying itself with the Lawn Tennis movement, 
I should like to offer a few remarks on the subject. I 
would remind the stern disciplinarian, who will own to but 
one permissible mania — that of the wheel — that it is not 
possible for everyone to keep going at a perpetual high 
state of pressure." Dulce est desipere in loco;" which, 
for his benefit, I will translate " It is pleasant to play Lawn 
Tennis occasionally." At the same time it cannot be 
denied that there are many objections that may be urged 
against our thus openly and officially encouraging a rival 
pastime. Men may not indeed play their ties on Saturday 
afternoons and evenings, but their is nothing to hinder 
their practising then for their matches; and who will 
guarantee that in giving a tournament we are not nourishing 
a viper, which, if we cannot crush altogether, we ought 
certainly to keep under as much as possible ? 

The promoters themselves must own that their venture 
at present stands on a very slender basis. It rests with 
them, and with those who have followed them through the 
gap, to show that so far from keeping men away from 
Saturday runs, the tournament has actually an opposite 
tendency in that it increases good fellowship among our 
members. Several of those whose names appear in the 
list of entries have been but very feeble supporters of their 
District, and it is said that there is one man who has not 
attended a single Club run this year. One thing is certain, 
that we ought not to run the risk of wounding the suscep- 
tibilities of some of our most zealous members, if those, at 
the instance of whom the iron rule is relaxed, cannot by 
their conduct assure us that in holding a lawn-tennis 
tournament, we are not in any way running counter to the 
primary interests of our bicycling community. 

Dodo.

who are determined to revenge Erith.

NOTIOB. 

The latest hour for reception of oopy for "Qazette"

THE ANNUAL RACE MEETING. 

In spite of threatening clouds in the morning, the clerk 
of the weather so far favoured us as not to inflict on us 
more than one sharp thunderstorm during the afternoon, 
and that was reserved for the last mile of the last race of 
the day. A rather strong wind blew in gusts, but the times 
were decidedly good. Though the want of a seated grand 
stand was much remarked on, a large number of ladies were 
present, and altogether the first meeting at the Crystal 
Palace must be pronounced a sporting and artistic success ; 
the fiuancial results are not yet ascertained. 

We give full details of the racing below. The final heat 
of the Open Handicap resulted in a magnificent race, 
though we trust that the Committee will not start seven in 
a heat next year. As it was, the six men together in the 
last lap ran great risk ; F. L. Adam scarcely got through, 
Thompson was shut out for a little while, and Perry lost a 
prize through crowding at the finish. We also earnestly 
hope that the objectionable practice of men who are not 
the judge, and who often are not in a line with the tape, at 
once announcing authoritatively that so-and-so has won, or 
is second, before the judge has given his decision, will be 
discarded in future. There were one or two very bad 
cases of this from men who ought to have known better, at 
the final of the Two Miles Open Handicap.

The Five Miles Scratch was brilliantly won by Gaskell, 
though we think if Buckley had been there he might have 
won by half a lap, if so minded. 

In the Club events Wyndham was very unlucky; a 
spoke broke at the start for the Ladies' Challenge Cup, and 
in the District Ten Miles, riding a strange machine, he 
missed his treadle in the last 50 yards, when nearly level 
with W. K, Adam, who won in the fastest time yet made 
in this race. W. C. Oswald was "thrown in" for the 
Members' Mile, with 200 yards, our men not being equal 
to 2.44, or thereabouts, and this was the only handicapping 
mistake throughout the afternoon. 

The following, besides the Committee, acted as officials :— 
Judge — W. Cornell ; Starter — ^Arthur Adams ; Timekeeper — 
G. P. Coleman ; Referees— G. L. Hillier and M. D. 
Rucker, Jun. ; Lap Takers— H. G. A. Brown and W. A. 
Smith; Clerks of the Course— A. E Buckler, T. E. 
Scrutton, C. Newman, and S. P. Smith ; Handicappers— 
The Race Committee. 

The Crystal Palace Band played a selection of music 
during the afternoon. 

After the races Mrs. F. M. Williams presented the prizes, 
and the meeting broke up after three cheers for the ladies.

waggled and went to the front, but was passed agam by

when Reynolds, by a magnificent spurt, walked away from 
him and won as he liked. Frazer-Luckie retired, as his 
treadle became loose goon after starting. Time, 5 min. 
58f sec.

Buckingham, Brixton Ramblers, 160, 0. When the bell rang, 
all the four were together, but Adam quickened up and 
took the lead, followed by King, the other two falling behind. 
Adam continued to gain and finished ten yards in front of 
King. Time, 6 min. 3f sec. 

Heat 4.— H. W. Gaskell, Ranelagh Harriers, scratch, 1 ;

C. W. Fagan, C.U.Bi.C., 160, 0. Hewitt maintained the 
lead till 200 yards from the finish, when Butler passed him

Heat 6.— R E S. Perry, Crighton B.C., 70 yards 
start, 1; L. C. Carr, C.U.Bi.C, 120, 2; H. B. Clayson, 
Brixton B.C., 160, 3 ; H. Walker, Rovers B.C., 150, ; 
H. E. R. Huber, introduced, 180, 0. Carr obtained the 
lead at the end of the mile, but Perry soon passed him. 
Carr, however, stuck to his man, and was only beaten 
by a yard and a half. Time, 6min. Of sec. 

Final Heat.— H. R. Reynolds, L.B.C., 110 yards start, 1 ; 
F. L. Adam, L.B.C., 75, 2; J. D. Butler, L.B.C., 120, 3; 
F. E. S. Perry, Crighton B.C., 70, 4 ; W. Wyndham, L.B.C., 
70, ; A. Thompson, Sutton B.C., 30, ; H. W. Gaskell, 
scratch, 0. Thompson got off well, and very soon got 
amongst the others, who were well together. Gaskell did 
not appear to improve his position, and gave up half a mile 
from the finish. At the end of the first mile Thompson 
was leading, but Reynolds immediately displaced him, and 
he in turn gave way to Perry at the beginning of the last 
lap but one, half way round which Wyndham was leading. 
When the bell rang Reynolds spurted to the fore, and 
Adam, who was last but one, now came up with a fine turn 
of speed, and, passing his men, finished second to Reynolds ; 
J. D. Butler was third, about 3 inches behind Adam, and 
Perry fourth. Time, 5min. 50|sec.

SPECIAL GENERAL MEETING. 

The above was called for Monday last at 6.30 at the 
" City Arms Tavern," a quorum of thirty being required by 
the rules. After waiting for fifty minutes, however, only 
twenty-four members put in an appearance, tliirteen of 
whom were officials and committeemen, and the Captain 
dissolved the meeting, announcing that the agenda would 
stand over to the Ordinary General Meeting next January. 

Present : Officials and Committee,— k, W. Barrett, T. B. 
Scrutton, E. S. Price, P. M. Williams, P. Jolly, E. H. Barrett, 
W. J. Williams, H. C. Pigott, C. Newman, R. G. Francis, 
G. P. Coleman, A. Lloyd, S. P. Smith. 

Private Members, — H. R. Re3nttolds, J. S. and A. B. W, 
Whatton, F. H. Williams, J. Potter, A. L. Radford, A. Hawley, 
E. A. Weeks, M. D. Rucker, H. Jackson, and S. G. Hough. 

N.W. DISTRICT. 
Mr. A. H. Lown is now our District Captain. This has 
not been officially announced before, owing to an omission 
on my part. E. S. Pmcb, Hon. Sec. 



SPECIAL NOTICE. 
Members who have not used the ticket for the Annual 
Race Meeting sent round with the Qazette are particularly 
requested to forward them to me at once, as we have to pdy 
the Palace for aU tickets not returned. 

E. S. Price, Hon. Sec. 



N.W. DISTRICT.— IMPORTANT. 

I have agreed with our new D.C. to take his place for the 
next two Saturdays. After the miserable failures of the last 
few weeks, it is not too much for me to ask and expect that 
members will turn up in something like respectable numbers. 
The average attendance at present must be the 'oery worst 
the N.W. has ever had. Lawn Tennis and the River I 
know have great attractions, but the old Club must not be 
forsaken altogether. 

We shall meet at Jack Straw's " 4 o'clock, Hendon Sta- 
tion 4.30, riding to the "Green Man," Kingsbury, where we 
shall join the W. and ride together to Stanmore. The run 
is short, so some members might attend, and by not staying 
to tea get home again in time for some tennis, etc. 

W. J. Williams, for D.C. 

S.E. DISTRICT. 
The Croydon Division will leave the Fire Station at 
4 o'clock punctually, and proceed straight to Lewisham 
Church, where Stephen Smith will be ready at 3.30 to show 
• the way to the ferry at Woolwich. The Blackheath Division 
will muster at the " Hare & Billet " at 4.45, and meet the 
Croydon men on Blackheath Common. As our last meet 
with the N.E. was more or less of a failure, I hope the district 
will do their best to obliterate the remembrance.of it by a 
brilliant success. We shall have tea at the " King's Head," 
Chigwell, about half-past six. 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 

WEST DISTRICT. 
On Saturday next I confidently anticipate a large muster 
at Stanmore, as we meet the N.W. District there. In order 
to ride some part of the route in company, we are going to 
meet the N.W. at the "Green Man," Kingsbury, at 4.45, 
so that members starting late will have an opportunity of 
catching up the District at this place, where we shall halt 
for a quarter of an hour. We then proceed by the lanes to 
Stanmore, where tea will be obtained at the "Abercorn 
Arms." All members are expected to come, as the run is 
short, and the route a pleasant one. And I therefore hope 
that members will not be beguiled into attending the 
S.W. run to the Silent Pool, which is altogether too far, 
and has none of those attractions so enticingly held out by 
the S.W. D.C. In fact, those who go will be disappointed 
at riding so far and seeing so little. Now, Stanmore is 
quite diflferent. A comfortable ride, good scenery, and a 
model hostelry are only a few of the inducements I hold 
out, and wi£h doubtful weather in view, no member can 
have two opinions as to the better place. 

Harold C. Pigott, District Captain.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



. STAMFORD BRIDGE GUARANTEE FUND. 

Members will remember that at the General Meeting I 
undertook to find twenty-five men willing to accept a con- 
tingent liability to the amount of 10s. each, in order to 
free the Club from the charge of £12 12s. Od. due to the 
L.A.C. for the rent of Stamford Bridge on June 30th. I 
had no difficulty in obtaining these twenty-five names and 
did not then try to get more. It has now become probable 
that I shall have to call upon the guarantors to redeem 
their promises, but before doing so I wish to ask if there 
are other members who are willing to add their names. I 
think it is not too much to expect that twenty-five more 
members may be willing to subscribe, in which case the 
liability of each would only be 5s. Names will be received 
by me, 

Stephen P. Smith. 

9, Morden Road, Blackheath, S.E. 



L.B.C. V. O.U.Bi.C. 
The above match, the bare particulars of which appeared 
last week, took place on the Crystal Palace track on Wed- 
nesday the 13th inst., in weather which was considered 
favourable to fast times. There was a very good attendance 
of members, particularly of the S.E. District, who took ad- 
vantage of the convenient locality and turned up in great 
force. Great was the disappointment and regret expressed 
when it was found, on arrival at the track, that Buckley 
had been placed hors de combat by an unfortunate accident 
on the Oxford track the evening before. This was a severe 
loss for the 'Varsity, though it would not have altered the 
ultimate result of the match. The J udges were G. L. Hillier 
and C. H. F. Christie ; starter, Ashley Barrett ; timekeeper, 
G. P. Coleman ; while T. E. Scrutton made an efficient 
telegraph boy. 

One Mile Race. 

Heat 1.— W. Wyndham, LB.C, 1 ; W. A. G. Walter, 
O.U.Bi.C., 2. Walter made the pace till 200 yards from 
home, when Wyndham drew out, and, gaining gradually, 
won by 15 yards. Time, 2 min. 54f sec. 

Heat 2.— R L. Adam, L.B.C., 1; M. H. Jephson, 
O.U.Bi.C, 2. This heat was well contested. Jephson got 
off with the lead, which he held till the bell rang, when 
Adam spurted and gained the lead, which he increased to 
15 yards at the flag-post. Jephson then came again^ but 
could not quite get up. Adam winning an exciting race by 
a length. Time, 2 min. 51 j sec. 



Heat 3.— W. K. Adam, O.U.Bi.C., 1 ; A. B. W. Whatton, 
L.B.G., 2. Each rider wanted the other to make the pace, 
consequently the first lap was walked, amidst great laughter 
in 73 sec. Adam then went ahead, and increasing his lead 
in the last lap, he won by 15 yards. Time, 3 min. 11} sec. 
Four Miles Raob. 

G. F. Beck, L.B.C., 1 ; F. L. Adam, L.B.C., 2 ; W. A. G. 
Walter, O.U.Bi.O., 3 ; M. H. Jephson, O.U.Bi.C., 4 ; J. D. 
Butler, L.B.C., 5; G. Stapleton, O.U.BLC, 6. Jephson 
made the pace, and at the end of the first mile was leading ; 
all the others wdl together. Beck whipping in. Towards 
the end of the seobnd mile Beck ran into first place, with 
Butler next and Walter third, lliis order was maintained 
till the bell rang, when Jephson ran up to Beck, but was 
beaten off. F. L. Adam then made his effort, coming up 
in fine style, though he could not get by Beck who won by 
three yards. Walter was third, just in front of Jephson. 
Time, 12 min. 24f sec. London thus scored thirteen points 
to Oxford's eight. 

Fifteen Miles Rage. 

H. R. Iteynolda, L.B.C., 1 ; W. Wyndham, L.B.C., 2 ; 
0. Thorn. L.RC., 8 ; W. K. Adam, O.U.BLC, 4; W. A. 
G. Walter, O.U.BiiC, 5 ; W. M. Merry, O.U.BLC, 6. 

Reynolds commenced to cut out the pace, and at the 
completion of the second lap Wyndham spurted, but gave 
way to Thorn who was leading at the completion of the 
mile. All the competitors kept well together, and each in 
turn, bar Adam, acted as pace maker. At the end of the 
thirteenth mile, which, strange to say, was completed in 
exactly the same time as the same distance in the fifteen 
miles race against Cambridge, Reynolds was leading, with 
Wyndham next, and Thorn third. Reynolds kept the lead 
and held his own against Wyndham, who spurted hard at 
the finbh, and could not get nearer than 3 yards. Thorn, 
who is riding in very good form at present, was only 
2 yards behind Wyndham. Time, 47min. 30 jsec. London 
thus scoring fifteen points to Oxford's six. 

After the races Iwenty-two men sat down to dinner, and 
a very pleasant evening was spent, in the course of which 
Ashley Barrett proposed the health of the Oxford Club, 
and referred to the regret they all felt at Buckley's mis- 
fortune; he also complimented Walter on his pluck in 
riding in the three events. The toast was responded to by 
Walter. Stephen Smith then proposed the visitors, which 
was answered briefly by W. B. Tanner, G. L. Hillier, and 
J. S. Whatton.

BICYCLING IN SWITZERLAND. 

(OONTIHUXD raOM PAQB 125, AND CONCLUDED.) 

§ 24. — Things in General 
The two summers in which I have heen bicycling in 
Switzerland have neither of them contained any long periods 
of hot settled weather, and I have had to contend with 
alternations of mud and hard-baked ruts rather than with 
dust. I cannot therefore quite say hoif far in an ordinary 
summer the dust will be found a serious inconvenience. 
Undoubtedly some roads which are badly mended, such as 
the Khone Valley Boad and its extensions over the Simplon 
and Furka, will be found very dusty ia a dry season ; but 
as regards the generality of Swiss roads they are mended 
with such excellent stone that there is no danger of their 
" going to pieces in a drought, as many, if not most of our 
English roads do. One great merit oCa Swiss road is that 
you will almost always find a good run ahng the extreme edge 
of it, the sidings being kept remarkably smooth and clean. 

In the less mountainous districts you will often find the 
road raised on a regular embankment like a railway, for 
the sake of avoiding depressions, etc.; These are the bits 
of road on which even more care is required in passing 
vehicles, etc., than on the hilly mountain roads, for here 
you have two steep banks, one on each hand, and not merely 
one ; the risk to vehicles is therefore doubled. 

In riding on long hills, such as those to be met with in 
Switzerland, it is surprising to observe the di£ference between 
the real and the apparent gradient. * fSo one going up ahill^ 
whenever a fairly long reach of it is in view, the gradient 
always looks less steep than it really is. The only way of 
estimating it is to turn round and look doum the hill. On 
the other hand, if you come to a comer, especially if the 
road curves round it in a cutting between two banks, the 
up gradient is apparently exaggerated, and you would think 
the hill is getting steeper, although the gradient may be 
perfectly uniform. This deceptiver phenomenon may be 
observed on a small scale on our Circular Bacing Track, in 
Cambridge, where, if you stand on it in the cutting beneath 
the judge's box and look in either direction, you would 
think that the path went up hill, though it is really an 
absolute level. 

Whether it is due to the chronic roar of the mountain 
cataracts and the intermittent thunder of avalanches, I 
cannot say, but it is a deplorable fact that the native Swiss 
are decidedly deaf, and as most roads run alongside of some 
noisy stream, you should provide yourself with the shrillest 
whistle you can get if you wish to maintain a clear course. 

Some kind of toothed bar for holding the brake fixed is 
absolutely essential for Swiss riding ; the hills are too long 
for any arms to maintain the strain of applying the brake 
without some such mechanical assistance. 

Maps, Distance, Measurements, Ac, — For guide book 
there is nothing like Baedeker (to be had in English as well 
as in other languages), and the Swiss Ordnance maps are, 



like their roads, models of surveying skill. Maps of a size 
approximately corresponding to our inch scale may be had 
of any part of Switzerland ; certain sections, too, may be 
had on a larger scale, containing contour lines indicating 
the rise and fall of the country. These last will be found 
particularly useful, as they enable the bicyclist to ascertain 
exactly the gradients at any portion of his route, and so to 
be prepared for the hills ; but on the ordinary scale maps 
the heights of most of the villages, etc., are given (or, if not, 
they will be found in Baedeker), so that the essential 
features of the route cau be inferred beforehand. Each 
contour line on the large scale map indicates a height 
difiereDce of 30 metres (roughly 100ft.). The most usual 
gradient will be found to be two contour lines, or 200ft., to 
a mile — i.e., a resultant or average gradient of about 
1 in 25, which is perfectly rideable up or down. Swiss roads 
are well provided with legible signposts ; but if the rider is 
in any doubt as to where he is, let him, on passing through 
the next village, look out for the red board invariably fixed 
above the shop or inn serving as post office. On this board, 
with its white cross, he will see the words " Post Ablage," 
followed by the name of the village. This is better than 
dismounting to ask, as the patois pronunciation of local 
names would deceive even the best German scholar. The 
Swiss unit of measurement, both for heights and distances, 
is a metre, which is equivalent to thirty-nine English inches. 
To translate a height in metres into English feet, multiply 
by 3^. Thus the lake of Thun, being marked as 560 metres, 
above the sea, by multiplying by three, and adding a fourth, 
we get 1820ft. The distance measurements are not quite 
so easy of computation, but an average mental arithmetician 
can easily do the sum as he rides along. In most districts 
the distance unit is a kilometre — i.e., a thousand metres. 
A metre being 39 inches is equivalent to a yard plus a 
twelfth ; 1000 metres, therefore, is 1000 yards plus the 
twelfth of a thousand, i.e,, 1084 yards (roughly), which is 
again (roughly) 200 yards more than an English half mile. 
Hence we get the following formula for converting kilometres 
into miles — ^Divide the total number of kilometres by two, 
and add to the result 200 yards for every kilometre. Twenty 
kilometres, then, will be ten miles plus (20 times 200) 4000 
yards, or (roughly) 12 J miles. Another rough and ready way 
of calculating is to take |ths of the number of kilometres. 
The roadstones are generally placed at intervals of every five 
kilometres, but the signboards will give the eicact distance 
from place to place, even to the tenth of a kilometre. In 
the canton Bern distances are indicated by " stunden " or 
" hours," a " stunde " being presumably the average distance 
covered by the Bernese wayfarer in an hour's walking. It is 
very nearly equivalent to five kilometres, being 4800 metres 
exactly, or just eighty yards short of three Euglish miles. 
In some places you will find both methods of computa- 
tion in vogue, and both kilometres and stunden posted up. 
With regard to maps. The best general map of Switzer- 



Digitized by 



Google 



134 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



land is Leuthold's ; but for more detailed use the Swiss 
Ordnance maps should be procured. A. — The ordinary scale 
map, about iths of an inch to the mile, is to be had in 
twenty-four separate sheets, and I think the price at 
Stanford's is 2s. 9d. a sheet. Several of the sections how- 
ever will be of little or no use to the bicyclist. B.—Next 
to this comes a scale twice as large again, called the 
Original Survey. This scale is only to be had at present 
for certain districts, and not for the whole of Switzerland ; 
but new numbers are being issued every year. Each sheet 
contains an area equal to i^th of that contained in a whole 
sheet of scale A ; the sheets are therefore much smaller. 
I forget the English price, but they are to be had for 
one franc abroad. These contain the contour lines. Again, 
there are certain favoured districts for which you can 
obtain sheets (C) on a scale four times that of A, i.e., about 
2^ inches to the mile. As both these scales, B and G, form 
part of the same numerical series, it is always necessary to 
look at the scale index if you wish to ascertain to which 
class the map belongs. 

The maps can be mounted most conveniently by cutting 
off all margins, and dividing sheets A into two sheets by 
a line drawn from top to bottom, i,e., from N. to S. Then 
divide each half-sheet thus obtained into three equal portions 
by lines from N. to S., and four equal portions by lines 
from W. to E. The half-sheet, thus mounted, folds up 
into a size which can be easily placed in a breast pocket. 
A little American leather case in which to enclose the maps 
is convenient ; it secures them from wet, etc. Sheets B 
and G need no halving, but can be mounted as they are by 
removing the margins and dividing them by one line from 
W. to E. and by two from N. to S. The resultant square 
will be slightly larger than that of A, so that if both sizes 
are in use, the leather case should be fitted to the size 
of B and G. 

Few forms of anticipating the pleasures of an outing are 
so rewarding as going carefully over the route intended 
on a good map, noting the villages, streams, mountains, 
lakes, and other features of it as far as it is possible for 
a map to give them. It is wonderful how readily the 
imagination conjures up a thoroughly real and lively 
presentation of the whole scene. Of course it is not quite 
80 enjoyable as the reverse process of reviving your associa- 
tions of a journey actually made by reference to the map 
months or years after ; still it is a real enjoyment — at least 
I have always found it so— and I venture to think Lhat 
if anyone intending to visit Switzerland will provide 
himself with the requisite maps at home ami travel over 
the ground beforehand, with their aid, and the help of these 
notes wjiich you have been so kind as .to publish, he will 
^ot find it so dry a business as he may be inclined to 
Imagine. For this purpose I appeiid a list of the maps 
Required for each of the routes 1 have here described, 
rhe Roman numerals are the number of the sheet of size A, 



each sheet being (where required) referred to in its divided 
state, the letters W and E representing the Western and 
Eastern ; ie., the left or right half of the sheet respectively. 
Where only the number is given, it is to be inferred that 
both halves of the sheet will be required. I have also 
added in Arabic numerals the numbers of the smaller sheets 
of size B or G wherever they exist —

In conclusion, I have to thank you, Mr. Editor, for your 
kindness in inserting this itinerary. I fear I have trespassed 
unduly on your spi^, and on your readers' patience. 
Descriptions of this kind meant to serve a purely practical 
purpose must be somewhat dry reading. They will however 
have served their end, and I shall feel amply rewarded for 
any pains I have taken with them, if they be the means of 
inducing other riders to visit the districts mentioned, and 
of saving them when there the trouble of hunting out the 
details of their route beforehand. Should anyone following 
my tracks discover any inaccuracies in those details as here 
given, I shall be very much obliged to them if they will 
kindly point them out^ It will be found that the distances 
stated are sometimes at variance with those given in 
Baedeker ; but his distances are very often those of the 
railway, and not of the coach road. 

August, 1882. Gbrabd F. Gobb.

hcVxi beelii September, 1881, instead of September, 1882.] 

ON THE TRAGK. 
Having been persuaded it was my duty to enter for the 
Glub Mile, in a weak moment I did so — Get my machine 
(a heavy roadster) up to London Bridge, and leave it at 
the booking office— they paste a label on the only bright 
part — 6o down to the Palace by 2.30— find a racing man 
has come by the same train — In a most masterly fashion 
he turns his racer upside down, and wheels it up the stairs, 
hind wheel in the air— Seems so easy, I try it myself. 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



135 



but with very disastrous results; the first being that I 
drop my bag wliich contains some tea in a glass bottle 
wrapped up in my clothes — Next, I hit a man in the 
back with the small wheel which sways about from one 
side to the other — reach the first landing — ^get desperate, 
and try to carry the machine up forcibly by the hub, 
only howeyer managing to pinch my hands, spoil my 
gloves, and make a large, black, oily streak on a white 
waistcoat I had intended to have a very telling effect 
on a certain person to whom I had given tickets — ^began to 
wish I had not done so, particularly as I had given them 
(in conformity with precedent) to the little brother, who 
will, no doubt, make rude remarks about my appearance 
— ^As I find my efforts at canning the bi rather exhausting, 
I engage a porter who calmly wheels it up in the ordinary 
manner^consider that racing man has done me an injury 
— At last I arrive at the track, which does not exhilarate 
me ; for of all the dreary places I think this beats the lot — 
looks like the ruins of something or other — ^they might fill 
the basins up a little so as not to show quite so much iron 
piping — that bridge, too, just by the dressing room 
looks awfully dangerous — ^think it would be safer to have 
a wall about a foot high, so that should a man go over 
the edge he would shoot out some distance, and so 
fall in the water, which might be kept deeper — at present 
he would fall on the jagged stones lining the basin — 
Begin to wish I had never entered but must go on now— 
especially as I have asked her to see me — Go out to see if 
they have arrived — don't find them — ^most likely at concert 
inside the Palace — hope they will stop there until my heat 
is over — Back to dressing room — Policeman wants to see 
my competitors' ticket, which I have lost, and only get in 
by one of the officials swearing to my identity — Our ribbon 
seems to have a remarkable effect on the force — Think I 
will look at some of the machines, but having no colours on 
am not allowed through — Notice, however, that our officers 
are turning traitors^Get one to lend me half his ribbon, 
and swagger up to the custodian of the wicket^ who gives 
way before the tricolor — ^Racing commences — Waddy 
breaks a spoke and pulls up in the first fifty yards 
of the Ladies' Challenge — wish mine would do the same 
— ^make up my mind I wont start, and secretly extract 
the bolt from my crank— have reckoned, however, with- 
out my host, for a particular friend (whom I shall never 
forgive) with mistaken generosity insists on my using 
his racer. Having a long start, have to mount in front of 
all the people. Hope she has not arrived yet — order to 
mount given — startling discovery, bicycle has no step ! — 
try to get on by various means, and at last, by the help of 
a little main force from a muscular member, manage to get 
seated — ^find she is just the other side of the fence looking 
on all the time — little brother jeering — ^get very confused 
and hot — don't hear the pistol, but energetic starter does, 
and sends me off with a jerk that nearly capsizes me — go 



off as hard as I can — ^have not gone far before scratch man 
of the heat catches me up, but does not pass me — ^begin to 
think I must be better tha;n I thought I was until the bell 
rings, when he dashes away and beats me by almost half a 
lap — can hardly feel my legs, and nearly tumble down 
when I get off*— Now my part of the performance is done 
have a look at the racing — wonder what Oswald will be 
later on if he can go like that now on a roadster — Harold 
Smith rides the machine he had in the Bath — should not 
like to trust myself on it from Bath to Devizes. Jephson 
and Wyndham great favourites with the spectators — ^a 
duck nearly upsets former — Francis and Beck in the same 
heat — Former evidently decides he won't beat his best 
friend. In the sixth heat of the Two Miles the spectators 
seem to have turned into a lot of rooks — can hear nothing 
but Cawr ! Cawr ! ! — ^but find they are only urging Carr on 
as he gamely sticks to Perry— during the Ten Miles District 
I take a stroll round— can't find anyone I know for some 
time — at last find object of search — ^no seats to be 
procured nor tea in the refreshment tent — little brother 
wants to know why I didn't win — ^tell him strange 
machine, but he won't believe it— can't shake him off 
until I send him to have a ride on one of the bone- 
shakers the Palace company lets out on hire — have to point 
out all the more celebrated L.B.C. men to her — thinks they 
are all very nice — ten miles still going on— don't I think 
Waddy (everybody calls him Waddy) looks nice? — Of 
course I do, but I don't want to keep on saying so all day 
— friend of a brother comes back — torn his trousers and 
cut his knee — says it is all my fault — says his sister must 
take him home — wish be had killed himself — a long wait 
in the racing, as we have got before time and some men 
are coming on from Beckenham and Catford Bridge. All 
the scratch men ride very close to the inside edge of the 
track, going over the line sometimes — ^remember I had 
some difficulty in keeping inside the outer line — shower 
comes down, and the prizes which the Captain had taken 
so much trouble to arrange are shot all of a heap into the 
judge's box— Captain, however, rises superior to all diffi- 
culties, and all get their proper prizes — new racing medal 
much appreciated by winners — wear my new Bath medal at 
once. N. H., Jun.

Meeting perfection.

single tumble, and no one protested in the Final of the

done so. I hope the Committee will be warned against next 
year.

or thereabouts.

tinction 01 having ridden at every Annual Race Meeting 
given by the L.B.C., last Saturday being his ninth.

The editor's modesty is being much tried, I hear ; a small 
boy on Saturday rushed at him and insisted that he was 
Mr. Falconer." I hope our President feels flattered. 

• ••«•• 

" Don't you think the Gazette is getting very personal ? 

I thought that article on went too far," said a virtuous 

member the other day. He little knew he was addressing 
the writer of the article in question. 

There were loud cries in the crowd for Fred Williams' 
speech on Saturday, after Mrs. Williams had given away 
the prizes, but his natural bashfulness only led to his 
assaulting two or three members smaller than himself, and 
retiring blushing. 

I am sure all our m^bers regret Buckley's unfortunate 
accident, and the credit of the victories over the Univer- 
sities is marred by the &ct that in each case their best man 

was not riding. 

• • ♦ • • • 

If the 0.n.Bi.C. had ridden their printed team, they 
would have won the Mile by 2 to 1, and we the Four Miles 
by one point, and the Fifteen Miles by at least 3 points, 
thus winning the match by two events to one. 

The Treasurer was observed chuckling on Thursday, as 
the Club has onlv to nve 2 medals, instead of 10 as last 
If only tnere had been a Head wind in the Bath

Continental Touring, from an expert, in the tub, which 
intending foreign travellers will do well to look out for.

Last Saturdav was jjuite a field day for the Club Mashers. 
White hats, white ties, and white waiscoats were quite 

de figueur.

The West D.C. has become so elated with the success of 
his District, that he had the temerity to hint that upon its 
present form the N.W. should be divided between the 

W. and N.E. 

• * • « • « 

Is this not enough to bring out again the ancient mem- 
bers who used to beertride the trusty " Stassens " in days of 
yore and smile at a club run to Amersham and back ? 

It has been suggested that the Stamford Bridge track 

being still on our hands for Saturday week, the two 

Districts not r^resented in the District Challenge Race 

should have an afternoon's competition, as many members 

of each of the two Districts taking part in it as may choose 

to do so. The N.W., I believe, funk the N.E., or might 

challenge them as w 41 as the S. W.

Two of our membm, luid five members' brothers, pkyed 
for the South of England Lacrosse team in the match 

against the Indians at Wanstead. 

• ••••• 

"Big John," the Indian chief and field-captain, was 
evidentiv put out by something ; attired in a Moses suit 
and a felt hat, he strenuously refused to put on his native 
dress, and kept on repeating " I'm de boss here." However, 
halfway through the game he came round, and amidst 
enthusia^ic yeus from his team was seen bounding out of the 

pavilion, complete with an enormous head-dress of feathers. 

• •••••

to " Preciosa" towards the finish of the Fifteen Miles Race 

at the Oxford match.

victorious progress round the track.

NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for "Gazette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdays. 

THE TREASURER. 
It is with feelings of profound respect that we approach 
the subject of the Club finances and the Club financier. 
This is not, as some narrow-minded persons might suppose, 
because we have not paid our subscription : we should 
indeed be sorry to add another straw to the burden of our 
arrears which, to judge fi-om the notices appearing from 
time to time in our columns, our Treasurer may be supposed 
to have to bear continually on his shoulders. We can 
imagine it an easy task enough when the subscriptions flow 
in almost spontaneously, and each year's labours are crowned 
with a substantial balance ; but — ^and we must confess we 
ask the question with considerable diffidence — ^has this ever 
been the e3q[)erience of our Treasurer? Is not the work of 
collecting the golden guineas of the L.B.C. known to be 
about as arduous a task as was that of gathering the golden 
apples of the Hesperides ? and may not our Treasurer be 
said to live like the Sjrracusan despot, with a drawn sword 
suspended over his neck, in the shape of a prospective 
balance on the wrong side at the end of the year ? The 
figures in which they are conveyed may appear hyperbolical, 
but the questions themselves would, we think, be answered 
in the affirmative by anyone who can speak from experience. 
It is said that the countenance of the Treasurer is sufficient 
to show the hard work the office entails, while at the same 



time it may be regarded as a thermometer, indicating the 
height or the reverse of the Club's pecuniary resources. 
With what a smile— faint as it is rare — does he present the 
balance sheet which shows a small surplus ; but, on the 
other hand, with a deficit staring him in the face, the 
shadow of a smile no longer adorns, and it is with sternly 
set features that he advocates a policy of economy, or replies 
with a non possumus to some Quixotic scheme of Club liber- 
ality. Occasionally you see him with a well-fed sort of an 
air about him from which you gather that his arrears have 
just been lessened by the receipt of the subscription of some 
tardy member. At another time he wears a lean and 
hungry look, and you suspect that he has just returned 
empty handed from a foraging expedition up some three or 
four flights of dark and precipitous stairs. Few and far 
between are, alas, the smiles of the Treasurer I Truly when 
the north-east wind has raised the value of medals on Whit- 
Monday, or our racing men have held their own against the 
Universities, he may have been known by his most intimate 
friend to have indulged in a quiet chuckle, but seldom, if 
ever, on public occasions will his features relax from their 
sphinx-like look of seated melancholy. More seriously 
we doubt if any of the Club officers have a more irksome 
duty to perform than the Treasurer. It is true the Captain 
has a greater responsibility ; and the Editor has no easy 
task sometimes in grasping the meaning of his correspon- 
dents when they blossom forth into abstruse jokes, or deck 
theu: effusions with fragments of foreign tongues. Here 
there is scope for genius ; but in the place of IVeasurer, a 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



good man of business is worth more than half a dozen men 
of genius. Let us congratulate ourselves, and the Club, 
on always finding men able and willing to undertake the 
responsibilities of a not over flourishing exchequer ; and 
let us acknowledge the services of our Treasurer by paying 
our subscriptions punctually — a small enough thing in 
itself, though no slight boon in the eyes of the hard-worked 
official whose duties we have here taken the liberty to 
consider.

SATURDAY RUNS. 

N.E. DiSTBICT. 

June 23rd. — The little district has been so admirably 
organised by that energetic gentleman, whose address, ac- 
cording to latest post card advice, is still Hotel Tegetthoff 
(by-the-by he dovit seem to-get-ofi), that in his absence 
the runs are carried out with clock-work regularity. Jolly 
and Lyne met at the top of the Lea Bridge Road precisely 
at 3.30 (a case unprecedented in the memory of your re- 
porter), and proceeded through clouds of bunting and 
throngs of loyal spectators anxiously awaiting the return of 
H.R.H. from Buckhurst Hill, to Sybom's Corner, where 
6. Smith and Sharman were found, and were presently 
joined by K D. SeweR The five reached the Flats by the 
usual route, and were then beguiled into a so-called short 
cut worthy of the N.G. himself, consisting as it did of a 
villanous piece of road over the most exposed part of the 



Flats against a stiffish breeze, and a voyage of discovery 
through the latest failures in road making by Forest Gate 
builders, winding up with a barrier and a dismount. The 
right road was found at last, when Sewell turned back 
to make a call, and was seen no more. The monotony 
of the flat country was pleasantly varied by a difference 
of opinion between two natives of East Ham, one of 
whom was old enough and fat enough to have known 
better ; his arguments were however so convincing that his 
opponent was morally and physically floored by them. At 
the Albert Dock a long wait was occasioned by reason of the 
swing bridge being closed, or, as an official in a mildly 
corrective tone observed, open for the passage of a large 
steamer, and it was fully expected that we should find the 
visitors also detained and waiting on the opposite side. 
This did not prove to be the case, and a long wait was made 
at the pier, during which, liquid and nicotian refreshments 
were indulged in, and then the band (of North Woolwich 
Gardens) played. It was not until 5.45 that eight South- 
Easters and two visitors landed ; an immediate start was 
made, the Home District with true courtesy giving the 
place of honour to their guests, modestly bringing up 
the rear through Barking and Ilford, and striving to 
impress the natives thereof rather by an exhibition of fine 
form than speed \ so well did they carry out their princi- 
ples that the visitors were soon lost sight of, and 
arrived at Ghigwell in advance. More members were 
found at the King's Head," and an excited group 
surrounding the new Rucker Tandem bicycle, a description 
of which it is hoped may appear in these columns. A most 
unfortunate breakdown in the Commissariat department 
occurred, for which apologies are due to the S.E. ; no one's 
fault, of course ; D.C. absent. Substitute on a tour ; 
IVeasurer had left it to Substitute ; reporter " didn't know 
nothing about nobody ; " however, just as thoughts of going 
home to tea were being indulged in, the meal was announced 
and partaken of, such as it was,insomewhat disjointedfashion 
in detached arbours, the inner garden being reserved for 
our exclusive use. An early start home was made, some 
energetic members stating their intention of accompanying 
the S.E. as far as Ilford ; the rear guard having no par- 
ticular yearning to visit that spot twice in the same day, 
jogged home in a manner more befitting their years and 
dignity through Snaresbrook and Walthamstow, over the 
marshes,, and parted at Spring Hill as ten was striking. 
Present at Ghigwell : E H. Barrett, A. Barrett (visitor), 
Forster, R. B. Hough, Jackson (N. W.), Jolly, Kelsey, Lyne, 
C. Newman (N.W.), Rucker, Sharman, G. Smith, and 
Weeks. Present at start : R D. Sewell. Spoken : S. G. 
Hough. 

N.W. District. 
June 23rd. — Our run to Stanmore with the W. was 
certainly a success, although the district was not re- 



Digitized by 









LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE- 139 




presented by anything like its proper strength. Only 
Metcalfe joined me at Hendon Station, and no Hampstead 
contingent arriving we left at 4.45, riding to the " Green 
Man," Kingsbury, where we found Barnett awaiting our 
arriyal with two' W. men. Where the West D. C. was, and 
why he did not turn up was beyond the powers of the com- 
bined districts to divine. Could he have had the audacity 
after that letter of his, detracting from the charms of the 
Silent Pool, to have gone there after all ? If so, it is well 
for him he does not get rule over that portion of the N.W. 
which he covets ! Our grand muster of five made for 
St>anmore, via Kenton Lane and Harrow. Weald Common, 
from which there is a magnificent view, extending for miles 
over Rickmansworth way. At the "Abercorn Arms," 
where we were joined by Langmore, Elgood, and 6. Hill, 
they were unable, or somebody suggested unwilling, to give 
us tea, so we moved on to the " Crown," at which house 
we were accomodated in good style. During the meal 
Stunt and Alison turned up, the latter calling in on his way 
from Digswell Hill to Datchet. We left about eight, and 
made our way back by Kenton Lane and the Hide, having 
had a most enjoyable run, with roads in good order after 
the rain. Present : Alison, Barnett, Elgood, Hill, Metcalfe, 
Stunt, and 

W. J. Williams, for District Captain. 


waiting, the latter of whom acted as pioneer as far as the 
ferry at Woolwich. On the way they were joined by 
Blackheath Division, five strong, and at the landing stage 
by Beck and the D.C., and the whole party crossed over by 
steamer without mishap. A detachment of the N.E., who 
had ridden out to welcome us, were drawn up on the 
opposite shore, and with a strong breeze behind, and a sur- 
face which seemed excellent after Lewisham and Woolwich 
macadam, a good pace was kept up to Chigwell. Here we 
were expected by the main body of the North-East, but 
not by the landlord of the " King's Head." The difficulty 
of want of room was, however, soon got over by an im- 
promptu tea in some of the arbours in the garden. After a 
post-prandial pipe and a pleasant half hour spent in the 
company of the North-East, a move homewards was made 
about half-past eight. Several of the N.E. kindly accom- 
panied us back as far as Barking. Though somewhat 
delayed by the ferry and the crowded streets of Woolwich, 
the various parties reached home before the small hours. 
Some httle credit is due to those S.E. men who supported 
their District at Chigwell on Saturday, instead of choosing 
more pleasant ways of their own. Present : 6. F. Beck, 
Day, Halliday, S. Herbert, Marten, W. C. Oswald, Parker, 
S. P. Smith, S. E. Whealler, two visitors, and 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 




Next Saturday the run is one of the best of the season — 
Little Berkhempstead — and I shall fully hope to meet a 
large number of men at Tally ho ! Corner at 4.30. There 
are a great many in the district whom we have not had the 
pleasure of seeing this season, and I shall be very pleased 
if they will for once favour us with their company. I can 
promise, if the weather is fine, a splendid ride over good 
roads and through beautiful country, and probably ** maca- 
dam pie " for tea. What more do men want ? 
» 

W. District. 

June 23rd. There is nothing to be added to the account 
of Saturday's run which will be furnished by the North 
West D. C. (pro tern.) except to express surprise that for 
such a short run on a fine afternoon the attendance 'of West 
men should have been so small. The late rains had greatly 
improved the roads — even Clamp Hill, usually a mass of 
shingle, was in fair condition and rideable. There was a 
nice breeze, the scent of new mown hay everywhere, and no 
rain, though the clouds looked rather threatening as we 
were returning. Present : H. M. Hewitt, J. W. Langmore, 
and A. B. Whatton. 

S.E. District. 
Three men left Croydon last Saturday for Chigwell. At 
Lewisham Church Parker and S. P. Smith were found 


Next Saturday we run to Mickleham to meet the W. and 
S.W. The Croydon Division will wait for the Blackheath 
men at St. Katharine Street until 430, and leave punctually 
at that hour. A short halt will be made at the Glyn Arms," 
Ewell, at 5.15. A great many men who found Chigwell too 
far have promised to come, so there is every chance of a 
good meet. We shall have tea at the " Beehive " at 6.30. 

B. 0. Feancis. 

S.W. District. 
The opinion that the West D.C. candidly expressed last 
week concerning the tranquil and ever-interesting Silent 
Pool which he described as so unattractive as to be un- 
worthy of a visit, is not, we trust, the result of personal 
experience ; for it would indeed be difficult to understand 
how so romantic a spot could have thus signally failed to 
exert any influence upon him. Assuming then that he 
speaks from hearsay, we emphatically urge him to visit this 
pleasing resort and then to report Uiereon. Anyway, the 
. best thanks of the District are due to the West D.C. for 
the thoughtfulness he has displayed in re-announcing our 
run, and thus again reminding members of our destination, 
which, but for this voluntary consideration would have 
been altogether omitted ! Our meet at Kingston, in so far 
as other districts were concerned, was decidedly satisfactory, 
for nearly all were represented ; but upon the doings of our 
own men on that particular day we must needs draw a 




Digitized by V^OOQ IC 



140 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



veil Precisely at 4-15 our energetic Captain Barrett was 
,at the meeting place, followed shortly after by W. J. F. 
Potts, an old rider in our District, who, it will be remem- 
bered suddenly left us last year to join the S.E.D. At 
about 4-30 p.m. Price and Hawley rode up, they having 
come in "hot haste" from Hampstead. After a short 
delay, for the benefit of our late visitors, the run was 
commenced, and the usual route followed by Surbiton to 
Esher, where we had a short pause and found several mem- 
bers of the Wanderers B.C. preparing for a Road Trial 
Handicap, from thence to Guildford and back to Esher. 
Triflingly refreshed we were soon again pursuing our course 
over hill and dale to Cobham, and at this last named place 
no persuasive efforts on our part could deter Potts from 
quitting us ; we regretted his absence the more when we 
remembered the slight sketch he had given before start- 
ing of what his little 48-inch, machine was capable of 
accomplishing in the matter of hills and of the oppor- 
tunity that Newlands Corner would have afforded for 
practically putting both rider and wheels to the test! 
Passing over the picturesque bridge across the Wey, and 
beneath the miniature Clifton Suspension Bridge, we were 
soon (under the speedy conduct of our northern guides) at 
the Wisley Hut and its pleasing surroundings, and the 
scene of our " great picnic " of last year. At Ripley we 
stayed again, and there learning l^at the road-racing talent 
of the Wanderers B.C. was shoYtly expected to pass through, 
we awaited their coming with interest. In about five 
minutes one member (Litchfield) passed in fair form, and 
then as time went on, and no other riders made their ap- 
pearance, we were obliged to continue our run. Turning 
to the left, about a mile from Ripley, and passing through 
the quaint little village of West Clandon, we drew near to 
Newland's Comer, but before reaching this ground there is 
a very decided obstacle or formidable hill in loose and 
stoney condition to be surmounted, and up this (to their 
credit be it spoken) both Barrett and Hawley rode in 
excellent form ! Upon gaining the top and Corner, a 
splendid view is obtained of the surrounding country in 



almost every direction, but the weather was too dull to 
admit of our seeing either Windsor Castle or St. Paul's, 
which are both visible from this point. The track of the 
ancient pilgrims, on their way from Southampton to Canter- 
bury, over St. Martha's Hill, which is marked by fine old 
yew trees, could however be clearly discerned; here, 
indeed, would have been a grand place to bold a picnic ! 
The descent to the Pool is fairly rideable, but as we came 
to the steepest pitch we saw Whatton coming up, and so 
saved further risk by dismounting. We were sorry to learn 
from this representative of the West District that he had 
already had tea at Gomshall, seen the Pool, and was on his 
way to town. About 7.30 p.m. we had reached the dear^ 
refreshing-looking, and peaceful waters known as the 
Silent Pool, in which it was refreshing to watch the 
meanderings of several different specimens of the " finny 
tribe." The summer-house overlooking the upper pool was 
already occiH)ied by a festive and extensive picnic party, so 
that the customary smoke could not be indulged in there. 
Upon quitting the pools we soon reached the " Drummond 
Arms " at Albury, where we had to make the best of both 
short and indifferent rations, where even milk was at a 
premium, and no persuasiveness on our part could induce 
the good people to take an3rthing off the top shelf in the 
larder for us that might possibly be in reserve there for 
Sunday. Outside Albury, Price and Hawley left us, they 
to ride to Aldershot, and we to return to Kingston. Thus 
concluded a very pleasant annual excursion, which would of 
course have been still further incrersed had our S.W. 
members been able to give us their hoped for cordial 
support. Distance from Kingston 36 miles. It was cur- 
rently reported that the S.E. were to be at the Silent Pool, 
but they never came. Members present : Ashley Barrett, 
E. S. Price, J. S. Whatton, A. Hawley, W. J. P. Potts, 
visitor, and R. M. OBUoaBK, District Captain. 



The run next Saturday is to Mickleham to meet the 
West and S.E., when we must all endeavour to take part 
in the run.
