CONTINENTAL TOURING. 

The serenity of mind, the placid enjoyment of life and 
its pleasures^ the sweet and quiet content of a blameless 
conscience, which have hitherto been distinguishing features 
of my existence, have all been tbreittened with speedy and 
remorseless extinction since I was requested (wbich is a 
gentle and inoffensive euphemism fpr a far more imperious 
word) by the Editor to write an article for this Gazette. 
The instructions (hints would be softer^ but the truth must 
out) received were explicit and precise ; they were given 
with all that perspicuity and directum which adds such 
force to anything the Editor has to say. In obedience to 
their tenor I have therefore essayed tp carry out the obli- 
gation of an Occasional Contributor (or O.C. to vary the 
venerated initials D.C. which are sometimes seen in the 
Gazette) by contributing an article. 

My theme has been selected. It is C!ontinental Touring, 
to be treated in a practical and rational manner. Those 
are conditions easily made, and very sweet, no doubt, to 
the mind of him who made them. Whether it is possible 
for me to fulfil them or not I cannot say. 

Continental touring by bicycle is almost free from one 
great evil attendant upon home " touring " so-called, and 
that is riding a certain number of miles within a given 
time, more for the sake of scoring distance than for the 
pleasure to be derived from the journey. A man who goes 
abroad is, in a great measure, the child of chance. He has, ' 
it is true, made his plans as far as his information of his 
intended route will permit, but he knows perfectly well 
that they are liable to be changed by many circumstances 
he cannot foresee. With a simple and child-like faith that 
all will turn out well he sets upon his journey, and often 
returns having completed a tour varying largely from that 
he traced out before starting. The uncertainty whether 
fixed plans can be carried out, the consciousness that you 
cannot fix your road beforehand in "cut-and-dried" sec- 
tions and follow it without deviation, lend a certain charm 
to foreign travel which is absent from home touring. The 
moral of all this is. Don't make fixed plans. Sketch out 
the general idea of a route and leave the rest to that 
Providence which shapes your ways plan them how you 

wm. 

An inseparable adjunct to continental touring by bicycle 
is a bicycle. I say this advisedly, because there are some 
riders who will start away with that which they, dignify by 
calling a bicycle, that, however, which the calmer judgment 
of those who are unbiassed and candid decides to be a Thing. 
A Thing is a dangerous and unsatisfactory companion at all 
times, in spite of the fond ecstasies of its possessor. A good 
bicycle, tight and sound, and well found in all its various 
parts, is most essential. It should be a strong, well-made, 
reliable roadster, completely equipped with the usual 
necessaries. In addition, it is advisable to take a spare 
treadle pin, a few spare nuts, a cake of Front's elastic glue 



for possible tyre cementing, some stout whipcord, and a 
waterproof saddle cover. It is better not to carry a lamp. 
All night riding should be struck out of any programme of 
continental travel. It does not coincide with any rational 
ideas of pleasure, and in a foreign country is more than 
likely to prove dangerous, besides being against one of the 
first objects of a tour — the enjoyment of the country one 
intends to pass through. 

Next to the machine the most important consideration is 
clothing. It is above all things most desirable to take two 
complete changes, one for riding, the other for use when 
not riding. For riding there are few things to excel a thin 
pair of knickerbockers, an alpaca or light tweed coat, and a 
thin, thoroughly shrunk flannel shirt. Those who have 
once ridden in knickerbockers will agree, I think, in saying 
how infinitely superior they are for comfort to the tight 
and perspiration-producing knee breeches. Indeed, a 
crusade against the latter would be, in my opinion, as 
justifiable and laudable as that carried out against the 
hideous and vulgar jockey cap. Headgear is an important 
element of clothing fraught with various and diverse 
difficulties. I confess to many misgivings upon the question 
myself. I have tried straw hat, polo cap, cricket cap, and 
helmet. For comfort, shade, and coolness, the latter 
claims precedence over all the others, but in appearance it 
retreats before them precipitately. I can, however, 
remember no more satisfactory headgear than the well- 
ventilated helmet worn on more than one tour, imper- 
vious alike to rain or sunshine, its capacious propor- 
tions large enough almost to shelter back, shoulders and 
all. Black canvas shoes, with leather straps over the front 
part, and with leather backs and stout leather soles, are 
preferable to any others, as they more quickly dry in 
the event of a wet ride, and they are easy and comfortable 
to the feet. A macintosh can be carried or not, as the 
inclination of the individual rider dictates. 

Now as to baggage. On my last continental tour I carried 
the following : — ^A thin suit of clothes, thin cotton shirt, 
spare flannel shirt, four Indian jerseys, two pairs socks, one 
pair thin patent leather boots, six pocket handkerchiefs, 
celluloid collar, tie, cloth hat, braces, razor, toothbush, soap 
(the little books of soap-sheets are equally good), sponge, 
brush, pocket comb, tobacco, pipe. For possible tailoring 
operations, needles and thread, a few pieces of cloth, and a 
pair of nail scissors were taken, and they proved most 
useful. A tiny medicine chest consisting of a small bottle 
of arnica, and another of vaseline, some lint, a bottle of 
chlorodyne, some goldbeater's skin, and a supply of fuller's 
earth, was not omitted. All these were packed in one of 
Anderson, Abbott and Anderson's admirable handle bags, 
and a knapsack which has seen much service in various 
parts. The lattermight prove irksome to those unaccustomed 
to it, and a multum could be substituted. I cannot speak 
too highly of Anderson's bag. Its capacity is great; its 



Digitized by 



Google 



142 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



convenience and simplicity greater. It is not at all 
unlikely that by ordering an extra sized bag, a knapsack or 
multum might be dispensed with, and a small saddle bag 
substituted. 

There are one or two additions to the above which are 
indispensable. A good guide book is the first. If you are 
not a brilliant linguist, a small pocket dictionary is the 
second. For France, and countries where French is under- 
stood, nothing will beat Bellow's English-French Pocket 
Dictionary (price 10s 6d). The same author is compiling, 
or has published an English-German Dictionary on the 
same plan. The third addition is a reliable map. As a 
rule, the reduced Ordnance Survey of the particular country 
you are about to visit is the best. Such maps can always 
be obtained at Stanford's, or through his agency, and they 
well repay the trouble of carrying owing to their accuracy 
and clearness. Finally, it is always well to carry a passport. 
The cost and trouble of obtaining it (and when once obtained 
it is good for life) are absurd in proportion to the service it 
may render you at unexpected times. 

I am quite prepared to believe that many tourists will 
consider the above outfit too complete. Let the doubting 
ones give it a trial, and when they experience the comfort 
and comparative luxury it will be the means of affording 
them at the close of a hot and dusty, or perhaps wet and 
windy day's ride, they will solemnly resolve never to dis- 
pense with it in future. It will be the means, besides, of 
making them presentable and civilised-looking beings at 
crowded continental table d^Mtes, or in crowded continental 
streets, where the vagaries of the travelling Englishman are 
already suificiently pronounced to render it desirable they 
should not be enhanced by the appearance of the English 
wheelman in his somewhat decided garb. Depend upon it 
that although you may possess the gaatronomical capacity 
of an Alison, the airy spirits of a Scrutton, the pottering 
genius of a Qodlee, you will not be happy if your visible 
and invisible raiment is wet with the dews of many sweats 
and you have not the wherewithal to enable you for the 
time being to discard it. In such circumstances a change of 
clothing means a mind at rest and a body refreshed. 

A hint as to the journey from England may not be amiss. 
It is always advisible before starting to write to some re- 
sponsible Ofl&cial as to the rates of carriage for bicycles. Do 
not rely on published information ; it is often wrong. If 
more than one rider should be starting it is quite possible 
a reduction in terms may be conceded.; and do not forget 
to arm yourself with the oflBcial letter to this effect for pro- 
duction to any booking clerk who may be anxious to charge 
you fancy rates. If bicycles are charged according to weight 
do not let them be weighed with the luggage strapped on. 
It may make all the difference. 

As to the time of year for travelling it must, of course, 
depend upon the country to be visited. It is obvious that 
the height of summer cannot be chosen for a tour in 



Southern climes, or the early spring or late autumn for 
travelling in Northern latitudes. The season of travel 
must be governed by circumstances, and it will therefore be 
unnecessary to offer any superfluous remarks on the subject. 

Avoid second-rate hotels ; they are a delusion and a 
snare. Avoid equally those where English most do con- 
gregate ; they are generally dens of extortion and over- 
charge. The best hotel to go to is that which caters for 
the best class of native travellers. There you will almost 
invariably meet with good cooking, genuine foreign manners 
and customs, civility and honesty. The sanitary arrange- 
ments may, perhaps, be a trifle primitive ; but shake off 
insular prejudices, for a time at least, if you can, and allow 
yourself to make acquaintance, not only with the country 
you may be visiting, but also with its people and their mode 
of life as far as you are able. 

Keep to main roads as a general rule; by-roads are 
mostly bad ; and do not forget the difference in the rule 
of the road which obtains in every foreign country except 
Bohemia from that which obtains in England, — to keep 
to the right side. 

The cost of a continental tour will mostly depend upon 
the rider. Speaking generally it is very moderate, and I 
think the sum of ten shillings a day, exclusive of travelling, 
will be found suflBcient to cover all reasonable expenditure. 

I have done. I have tried to be exhaustive, but I feel 
how vain has been the effort, and how the threshold of the 
subject I was ordered to write about only has been touched. 
I also feel how presumptuous it has been in me to offer a 
few hints on Continental Touring in the Gazbtte of a Club 
which contains so many continental tourists of far wider 
experience. To the tender mercies of indulgent readers I 
commit this fragmentary effusion, begging them to visit the 
Editor — who was its First Cause — ^with all the blame, and to 
accord to me all the praise — ^if any is due. 

R. P. H. R. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sib, — Now that our Gazette is making its appearance 
once a w^ek, I trust I shall not be encroaching too much 
on its space to write on a subject I have long wanted to see 
noticed. I had hoped that some with more knowledge of 
the working of the Club than myself would have ventilated 
the matter ; but as no one has taken it up, I feel that 
I ought not to procrastinate any longer. I wish to say a 
few words on the d<ite of the Club dinner. Has it never 
occurred to any of those gathered round the festive board 
how very few resident 'Varsity members ever put in an 
appearance? What is the reason of this paucity ? Lack 
of interest in the Club? No. The "Spartan life" (?) 
led by them during term time producing a distaste for such 
festivities ? Possible, but highly improbable. What then ? 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



143 



Great personal inconvenience. Now, sir, I contend that 
if the date of the dinner was fixed a fortnight later — say 
between the 6th and 10th of December — ^we should have 
the pleasure of seeing more 'Varsity members present. 
Of late years, th^ date has generally been fixed sufficiently 
near the end of term to preclude the possibility of men 
getting leave of absence. In fact, last November an 
enthusiastic member of both Clubs confided to me that 
he dare not a^k for another " absit" firom his tutor, as he 
had already that term buried an uncle, grandfather and 
grandmother ; and on the last application the tutor had 
informed him that he must not have any more family 
bereavements before the end of term!! I quote the 
above, not as pointing to the moral good the Club might 
effect by accepting my humble suggestion — and so dis- 
countenancing deception — but rather as insinuating that 
the tutors — misguided race — value the results of examina- 
tions more than attendance at Club dinners. To fix our 
date within a week of the exams, is to fly in the face of 
the powers that be, and to this cause done I attribute 
what appears to be the unconviviality of 'Varsity members. 
Being mindful, sir, of your declaration, and fearful — ^mighty 
fearful— of our governing body, I shall shift the responsi- 
bility of these remarks to your shoulders and be known 
only as Delta. 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
The " National Cyclists' Union " came into existence on 
Thursday, the 14th inst., vice the "Bicycle Union," killed 
by tricyclists. About two hours' debate was expended on 
the change, Hampton-Roberts and Scmtton supporting the 
name " wheelman," and the combination, " National Union 
of Wheelmen,'' but they were left in a minority, of which 
it is hoped the quality made up for the lack of quantity. 

• »*••• 

Howell has beaten Keen in a mile at Wolverhampton in 
2.47, the half-mile being said to be done in 1.16, being 
record time. 

«••••• 

Report says that the C.T.C. Gazette are going to bring 
out a Christmas number at the end of the year in the 
shape of their handbook. 

• •»«•• 

The following time-standards have been fixed for the 
Bicycle Championships : — 1 mile, 2 min. 48 sec. ; 5 miles, 
15 min. 10 sec. ; 25 miles, 1 hr. 20 min. ; 50 miles^ 
2 hr. 50 min. 

A silver medal will be given to the winner of the fastest 

heat (other than the final) in each of the Tricycle 

Championship Races. 

«««•«« 

The Two Mile Challenge Cup at the L.A.C. Meeting on 
Saturday was a match between Wyndhain and F. L. Adam, 



which, after a waiting race, Wyndham won by less than a 

yard, in 6 min. 16^ sec. 

• • • • • • 

The Three Miles Handicap resulted in a benefit for the 
limit man ; the three placed men having respectively 460, 
250, and 300 yards. Liles was at scratch, and F. L. Adam 
(20), Wyndham (40), and 0. Thorn (160), represented the 
L.B.C. 

The Twenty-five Miles Championship is fixed for August 
2nd, at Taunton. West, King, and Fry are the only local 
men with any pretensions to championship honours ; indeed 
Birmingham looks like securing all the Championships this 
year. 

• «•««• 

The 100 miles match between Lowndes and Nixon on 

tricycles was decided last Monday at the Crystal Palace. 

Lowndes led at 50 miles, but in the 52nd mile an accident 

happened to his machine, which enabled Nixon to get ahead. 

Nixon completed 60 miles in 4hr. 12 min. 10 sec., Lowndes 

being about 3 minutes behind. The latter however retired 

in his 62nd mile, and Nixon, going on, covered the distance 

in 7 hr. 23 min. 50 sec, beating all records. I need hardly 

say this is an extraordinary performance. 

«««•«« 

The bicycle has found its way into the Law Courts ; and 
not in an assault case. In Bown v, Humber, in the Court 
of Chancery this week, Mr. Justice North has been investi- 
gating the patents as to ball bearings. The court has been 
blocked with bicycles and bicyclists, and Sir Frederick 
Bramwell has been discoursing learnedly on the engineering 
merits of different bearings. At the time of going to press 
I hear that a judgment for the defendants is probable. 

The twenty-four hours' ** Facile " race on Saturday last 
was won by Adams, covering 220 miles, 7 furlongs, or 
6 miles longer than Snook's ride of last year. The second 
man did 219 miles. 

Lowndes, in a trial spin at Surbiton on Friday last, 

clocked by 0. Atkinson, beat all tricycling records from 

1 to 10 miles ; doing 1 mile in 3 min. 5 sec, 5 miles in 

16 min. 19 sec, and 10 miles in 32 min. 33f sec 

««•««« 

Oosset, of the Temple, .has also beaten the tricycling; 

record by covering 207^ miles in the twenty-four hours 

on the road. 

«««••« 

The Editor and H. R. Beynolds played their Tennis tie 
on Wednesday evening in miserable weather, the ground 
being very slippery. H. R. started with a love set ; the 
second set was keenly fought, the score standing at " three 
games all," and " deuce " in the seventh game, when the 
Editor had a severe tumble, which spoilt his play for the 
remainder of the set, which H. R. won easily. 

The Fbokt Whbel Hub.

The latest hour for reception of copy for "Gwette"

THE PRIVATE MEMBER. 

It has been remarked with some truth that the oiBcers 
and committee of the London Bicycle Club would form in 
their own persons one of the most influential clubs in the 
metropolis. Yet in spite of the failure of the attempt made 
at the General Meeting in January, to introduce new blood 
in place of some of the old hands who were willing to serve, 
it is obvious that the constitution of tlie Committee is very 
similar to that of the tank over the problem of which we 
used to ponder at school, which was filled by the action of 
one tap and emptied by another. It is then to the ordinary 
members that the Club must look for reinforcements, and 
this is the clue to the line of conduct which ought to be 
persued by them. 

The subject of our article may be described, as we have 
said, as one who has no seat upon the Committee; not 
indeed precluding the occasional possibility of his sitting 
upon that body for a short time as at a general meeting, 
but pointing to the fact that as a rule he reposes elsewhere. 
The only question that may arise as to definition is whether 
or not the regular racing member comes under our heading. 
He is the representative of tlie Club in the racing world, 
and is acting continually under its title in public ; but on 
the other hand he can hardly be considered as a duly 
"accredited agent." 



The private member may be roughly divided into three 
classes, according to the volume of his energy. The first is 
but small, consisting mostly of ex-ofiicers, but though the 
task would be all too easy, and though we have been fur- 
nished by Mr. Labouchere with a quasi-parliamentary 
precedent, we do not propose to draw up a list of those 
who may in our opinion be entitled to a place in it. They 
may be known by their deeds. At the Annual General 
Meeting not only are they invariably present, but they 
have deeply considered the proposals to be made, and have 
evolved ideas and amendments which they are ready to 
support, if necessary, even in the teeth of the magnates of 
the Committee bench. They may be recognised at the 
General Club Meets by the fact tiiat somehow or other 
their acquaintance with members of Districts other than 
their own is unusually large, though their own men will 
remember that they have hwUy strayed once during the 
season. They are very prompt in sending up to the 
Gazette any information of which they may become pos- 
sessed, while the number of tickets and entries for which 
they are responsible would alone be sufficient to fill a small 
concert room or handicap. 

The second class is made up of the majority of the un- 
official members. They may be seen with their Districts 
on fine Saturdays, but from their point of view their own 
pleasure is the sole reason which should determine their 
movements, and if the tennis court or the river should 
appear likely to afford a more liberal supply of that which 
they seek, they feel no sort of compunction in indulging 



Digitized by 



Google 



146 LONDON BICYCLE 



their whim, Club duty being to them a thing unknown. 
An offender of this class, if taken to task, will tell you 
^hat he has the utmost confidence in the ability of the 
officers to manage the Club satisfactorily, and will begin to 
descant on its present prosperous and flourishing condition. 
Now we are of opinion that this soothing adulation to 
which we are occasionally subjected by some of our members, 
and by the outside bicycling press, is one of the most subtle 
dangers that assail us in our onward career ; and we would 
suggest that in case of a new cover being decided upon for 
the Gazette, it should be decorated with a carefully 
executed drawing illustrative of the well-known fable of 
the Brahmin and the Sheep, which would not only be an 
additional attraction in itself, but would serve as a weekly 
reminder of the sentiment that it is unwise to believe 
everything one is told on questions that intimately concern 
oneself. 

Lastly, there are those whose boast it is that they have 
never attended a general meeting, who seldom if ever 
appear on the road, and whose only friends in the Club are 
their introducer and seconder who, may be, are a little 
disgusted with their apathy. It is of course true that some 
members are unable to take any active part in the Club's life, 
and in the case of its tried servants this is quite excusable, 
but to enjoy the " otium " without having previously 
acquired the "di^nitas" is a preposterous proceeding, and 
a course which no right-minded member can possibly be 
justified in pursuing. 

With these few remarks we must leave our readers to 
decide for themselves to which class they do, and to which 
they ought to belong.

SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. Dis^&icT. 
June SOtk— The Forest Gate contingent, with the Trea- 
surer, alone represented the little district at Stifford, 
though other members were mmoared to be taking tea in 
surrounding villages. The F. G. C. were first to arrive, and 
found a capital tea ready for them at the Dog and Fart- 
ridge." Their route led through Rainham, Chaffinch Lane, 
and Aveley, and be it further noted, they rode Stifford Hill 
to a man. This also was subsequently done by the Trea- 
surer, who arrived late, and stayed only three minutes 
before remounting for his return journey alone. The 
F. G. C. then rode on to Rochester, crossing at Tilbury, 
and visiting on their way Hangman's Wood, through which 
their course lay along a delightful and rideable footpath 
about two miles long. In the centre of the wood are 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



the Dane's Ffoles, which were duly visited for the benefit 
of a member to whom these strange places were known 
only by repute; Present: R. Hough. Ashley Barrett, 
Ernest Barrett, and two visitors, Arthur Barrett and 
Fred. Barrett. 



W. District. 

I believe I was the only member of the West who fell in 
with the South East. I left the Palace after the Brixton 
Ramblers' Meeting, rode to Croydon, trained to Epsom, and 
rode to Leatherhead. I passed in succession Stephen Smith, 
H. G. A. Brown, and the main body of the South East just 
outside Leatherhead, returning. •Waited half an hour for 
any West or South West men going my way, and then as 
no one had sighted me I returned alone by Kingston aud 
Richmond. Reached home just after midnight. 

J. S. Whatton. 

The West District Captain writes from Henley-on-Thames, 
where he is enjoying his holiday, expressing his great regret 
that, owing to the unexpected amount of business he had 
to attend to during the fortnight before he left London, he 
had no time to mske arrangements for a substitute, or even 
to write to the Editor to give notice of his absence. He 
hopes to be back in time to be present at the nm to 
Bumham Beeches, and that in the meantime those of last 
Saturday and aext may be well attended and duly 
reported. 

N.W. District. 
June 30th. — No doubt the thunderstorm of tlie previous 
night, and the threatening appearance of the weathen 
combined with the heat, deterred some of our members from 
attending our favourite run to Little Berkhempstead. The 
lack of interest which is taken by the greater number of 
N.W. men in the proceeding of the district is having a 
marked effect on those few who do put in an appearance 
now and again. I hope our new D.C., as soon as he returns, 
will be able to wake men up from their state of apathy. 
Only Jackson and I met at Tally ho ! and after giving a 
few minutes grace we rode quietly to our destination by 
way of Carbon Hill and Newgate Street. The roads 
were in capital order after the rain, and the country 
looked quite freshened up, the hedges being brightened by 
large quantities of dog-roses. Hindley came up a few 
minutes after ouf 'arrival, having lost his way, as usual, in 
the meandering lanes round this little village. We were 
joined at intervals by Elgood and Price, which made oar 
muster up to five. The ride home was much enjoyed, the 
breeze against us being quite the reverse of uncomfortable. 
Present : Elgood, Hindley, Jackson, Price, and 

W. J. Williams, /<;/• District Captain. 

Run next Saturday to Shenley, when there will be two 
attractions — to meet our new D.C. (probably for the first

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



147 ^ 



time), and plenty of good strawberries. I hope there will 
be, just for once, one of our old meets — sixteen or seven- 
teen men, say. 

S.E. District. 
A more lovely afternoon than last Saturday it is not 
easy to imagine ; of course it was a trifle warm, but still 
yre were very fortunate in having such wefkther for our run 
to Mickleham and Box Hill. "We wer^ seven" who left 
the starting point soon after half-past four, and made for 
Ewel], via Duppas Uill and Beddington. Before long we 
received several additions to our ranks, and at one time 
the party numbered no less than fourteen.. The first thing 
to mar the even tenour of our way was an unfortunate 
collision between Stephen Smith and a small boy. Result : 
collapse of small boy and Smith's machine. However, as the 
occurrence took place in Sutton, and no bones were broken. 
Sharp kindly lent his machine, so that Smith was able to finish 
the run. On pulling up at the " Glyn Arms," we learnt that 
W. A. Smith was fishing close by at Ewell Mill. As we 
were rather behind time, we repressed our natural anxiety 
to see the grand basket of fish we felt sure he had caught, 
and kept steadily on through Leatherhead to the " Beehive 
Inn," between Mickleham and Dorking. Here J. C. Oswald 
and the O'Reilly were waiting for us, and as the West and 
South-west failed to put in an appearanee we sat down to 
tea at seven o'clock. After a smoke in the garden, we 



followed the zigzag road up Box Hill in Indian file. On 
the way up we met H. 6. A. Brown, m route for the 
" Beehive," and in the hope that he might catch us up on 
the way back we prolonged a contemplative pipe, under 
the influence of which we were taking in the glorious 
view from the top. As the sun sank the scene faded, 
and still he came not ; so we made a fresh start a little' 
after nine and proceeded leisurely homewards, through 
the dusky lanes by Burgh Heath and Banstead. We 
reached Croydon just at the same time as Brown, who 
made his return journey round by Leatherhead, and the 
remainder of our party broke up after one of the most 
pleasant runs we have had this year. Present : A. Barker, 
H. 6. A. Brown, Halliday, Hartridge, Alfred Herbert, 
S. Herbert, McRae, The O'Reilly, J. 0. Oswald, W. 0. 
Oswald, PauU, L. Potter, H. N. Sharp, S. P. Smith, 
Whealler, F. H. Williams, Willis, R- G. Francis, and 
Oswald's Manchester terrier "Roger." 



It is hardly necessary to call attention to our meet next 
Saturday. It may be summed up in the term "short and 
sweet." The run is certainly an easy one, and the fare at 
the " White Hart," Bletchingley, is proverbial. The Croy- 
don men will wait at the Fire Station for the Blackheath 
Division until 4.30. 

R. G. F&ANCis, District Captain.

A TRIP TO THE "HOOTER." 
Newbury, on Whit Monday about 11 a.in., is a goal to 
which many a thirsty soul panting undev the broiling sun, 
as he struggles to win the small disc of yellow metal which 
he trusts will repay him for his labour, looks with eager 
eyes ; but the same town after a residence of some eight 
days, during one half of which the flood-gates of heaven have 
ruthlessly discharged their torrents, and when rude Boreas 
has done his best to spoil the remaining few days by his 
persistent endeavours to ruin the fishing, is not perhaps so 
eagerly to be sought after, and great was the relief to the 
mind of yours truly, when a letter from J. W. Allison 
infoi«Ded him that he, the said J. W. A., would arrive at 



Newbury on Saturday, May 27th, prepared for a trip to 
that ancient relic, yclept " Ye White Horse," and the many 
mysteries of the surrounding country. Early on Saturday 
the fishing-rod was again put into requisition, in the hopes 
that sufficient of the wily trout could be lured from their 
haunts to supply the expected arrival with that toothsome 
delicacy for breakfast, but the weather was dead against 
sport, and only two brace found their way into the pannier. 
As night wore on and A. did not come, the solitary one's 
spirits began to droop, but suddenly, far away down North- 
brook Street, a long dark line appeared, and part of it was 
soon discussing Gorgonzola cheese, salad, and excellent 
bitter ale in Mr. Staple's (the confectioner's) comfortable

LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



little room. Dalton was reported to Lave just gone to 
the "Checquers Inn." The conversation soon turned 
to the most prominent topic — Bath Trial— and it is 
worthy of notice that both men picked the winner. 
The following day was luckily very fine, and, after calling 
for Dalton, the trio, mounted on a " Humber " bicycle, 
ditto tricycle, and an ** Excelsior" bicycle respectively, 
rode round by Shaw Mills to Shaw House, where the 
" Martyr King " spent the night before the battle of 
Newbury ; thence into the Ilsley road, and branching off 
to the left when opposite the ruins of Donnington Castle, 
passed over a glorious road at a great elevation through 
Snelsmore Common, and so on to a long, rough, and dange- 
rous descent called Bussick Hill." The road ran through 
some charming scenery, but we soon came to another nasty, 
rough descent, the latter part of which Dalton took full 
speed, to the intense surprise of a local 'cyclist who was 
walking up. Keeping slightly to the left at the "Blue 
Boar Inn," we found nice running to Lilly, and so on 
through Famborough Coppice, but on arriving at the fork 
in the road where the finger post points to Wantage and 
Farnborongh, we turned towards the former town and found 
the surface very bad for some distance. On a suggestion 
being made that we must be al an elevation of nearly 1000 
feet, the Northern Guide was incredulous, and said that 
Scutchamfiy Knob " was not quite so high as that ; but 
when informed that he was gazing at the summit of the 
" Knob " itself he agreed that we were tolerably well up 
in the world. Leaving the two lazy bicyclists reclining on 
the mossy turf, the three-wheeler pursued his way to the 
summit, where a magnificent expanse of country spread out 
at his feet, and after drinking in as much of its beauty as 
possible, and noting the extremely dangerous descent leading 
to Wantage, locally known as Chain Hill, he returned to 
seek the two lazy ones, but found them not. What was to 
be done? Red House and the Ridgway, or "Budge" as 
the yokels call it, had been mentioned as part of the desti- 
nation, so he commenced to wheel his tri. along the " Rudge," 
and before reaching the Red House, some two miles 
distant, had quite come to the conclusion that, although 
a grand machine to ride, for pushing purposes the 
"Humber" tri. was about the meanest thing out. Besides 
which, the driver began to find that he was sufieriug 
from a nasty dry thirst, and that the only thing he could 
obtain wherewith to quench it was rain water. Red House 
was at last reached, but no trace of the missing ones found, 
so he carefully descended the tremendous hill known as 
Red House or Workhouse Hill, leading into Wantage, at 
the foot of which the truants were discovered, and were 
easily prevailed upon to return to the " Bear," at Wantage, 
to dine. In the market-place is one of the grandest monu- 
ments I have seen. The subject is, " Alfred the Great," 
and the sculptor (Count Gleichen ?) has made the most of 
it. It was presented by Colonel Loyd Lindsay. The 



power (mental ainl physical) expressed in this work of aft 
is thoroughly characteristic of the mighty Saxon monarch, 
whose birthplace was Wantage. By the bye, I forgot to 
mention that thertwo truants had, after dodging me in some 
mysterious manner, gone straight to the town, Dalton 
actually riding down Chain Hill, a feat worthy of record. 
While dinner was preparing, we had a look at the church, 
finding some fine Norman portions ; curiously enough, like 
Romsey Abbey, it has no western porch or door, so may 
originally have been conventual. After every attention 
at " The Bear," and a capital repast with moderate charges, 
we ordered tea to be ready on our return and started off to 
find the " Blowiwg Stone," " Blawin Stwun," or " Hooter," 
as it is called locally. After one or two stiff hills the route 
became of a more undulating nature, and we soon heard in 
the distance that«ome one was already " tootling " on King 
Alfred's little "war-horn." Sure enough, there it was, 
glued to the mouth of a rustic standing in the porch of the 
little inn at the foot of '* Blawin Stwun Hill " (said to be 
the steepest In Berks). A nearer glance, however, showed 
that the article glued, etc., as aforesaid, was not the 
Stwun," but a stone mug of ale, and the real thing was 
found "under a spreading chestnut tree" which abutted 
on the road. First one and then another of the trio 
applied himself to the task of sounding the monster, 
but although they all, Dalton especially, blew until nigh 
unto bursting, no sound did the holey rock give forth. 
When, however, a small boy applied himself to the 
orifice, the trumpet sounded forth the call to arms, 
and on the heigJds of Vffington Castle the Danish host 
appeared, with waving banners, Alfred himself leads the 
attack upon them, and the Saxons fiercely Jight their way up 
the heights U7itil the gory streams pour down the rugged 
sides of St. George's Mount. Hullo ! Why, what's up 
now, McCullum ? I beg your pardon, really, but the 
whole scene enacted here some thousand years ago came 
so vividly before me that I was just going to tell you all 
about the battle of Ashdown, from an eyewitness's point of 
view. Seriously speaking, every true-born Englishman, when 
gazing on these remarkable mementos of a past age, must 
feel a thrill of exultation at the recollection of the glorious 
victory obtained here over those heathen invaders the 
Danes by the skill and bravery of him whose stone effigy 
now dominates O3f0r Wantage market-place, for it should 
not be forgotten that this victory entirely broke the Danish 
power in Britain, and made us the great Anglo-Saxon and 
Christian race of the present day. Well, mine host 
Godfirey's ginger beer was found so nice that no less than 
six bottles disappeared forthwith, and being thus fortified 
we struggled up a long rough hill, on the road skirting the 
foot of the downs, in the direction of the " White Horse." 
Presently we came to a sharp fall in the road, which at the 
bottom turned at almost a right angle, and rose very 
abruptly and stifily. This required great care, and might 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



149 



appropriately be called ''the Devil's Elbow/' and about 
another mile brought us opposite the " White Horse/' or 
rather what is left of it. This true and original relic must 
not be imagined to be like that much more modern affair 
on Cher Hill, which men riding from Bath vid Calne see on 
their right, as the outline is not anything like so distinct, 
and, moreoyer, it is in a galloping position. The people of 
the district formerly had what was call^ the "Revels" every 
few years, and your deponent has a' vivid recollection of 
seeing one of them. On these occasions the "scouring of 
the white horse," as depicted in Tom Brown* 8 School Days, 
took place, and there was great jollity ; and I well re- 
member a race down the side of the steep hill by the "horse," 
for a cheese trundled down the incline, and followed by a 
mob anxious to obtain possession of it, the said mob rolling 
over and over in their eagerness to get the prize, which was 
eventually won by a sweep. This, however, like many 
other good old English customs, has now fallen into disuse, 
and so the turf keeps encroaching, and every year renders 
the old " White Horse " less shapely. At the summit of the 
hill there is a grand British fortification called " Uffington 
Castle," and a little more than a mile to the right, along 
the ridgeway, is Wayland Smith's Cave (it stands in the 
centre of a little clump of trees and bushes). This cromlech 
has been immortalised by Sir Walter Scott. As we were 
just thinking about making our way back to Wantage, the 
thought struck me to go to Uffington Station and meet the 
men who were going down to Bath, and forthwith we carried 
it out ; and after providing ourselves with a plentiful supply 
of new milk and bread and butter, welbok possession of the 
railway platform and waited patiently for the train, which 
was, of course, very late. The cooling beverage was much 
appreciated, and after many good wishes for the morrow 
the train .crawled on to Bath, while the trio returned to 
Wantage vid Challow, over capital and level roads. Tea waa 
quite ready at " The Bear," but as we had done fairly well 
at Uffington we did not trouble it srich, but made a start 
back vid Red House. The top of this eminence being 
gained (observe the fine British camp right) the road falls 
for miles, and the N.G., who always ctamson the pace after 
tea, got right away out of sight, but was overtaken before 
reaching the junction of the Newbury and Lamboume road. 
Before reaching Welford it was getting quite dusk, and we 
had to light our lamps, eventually arriving at " The 
Chequers," at Newbury, just in time to learn that two or 
three of our men had gone through towards Bath. Thus 
ended one of the pleasantest excursions I have had for some 
time, and it seems a pity that this neglected spot (White 
Horse Vale) should not be more frequently visited by 
cyclists. The roads as a rule are good, the scenery is 
fine, and to the antiquarian the neighbourhood teems with 
interest. Later on I purpose giving some skeleton tours 
round Newbury.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
Bown V, Humber has terminated in. a verdict for the 
defendant, with costs. The court was full of bicycles and 
bicyclists for three or four days ; Keen, Cooper, Humber, 
Vesey, the Wheelers, H. R. Reynolds, and the Editor being 
all on view. The bicycle may now be considered to have 
made another stage in its progress. One shudders to think 
of a tricycle patent case, e^ecially if every tricycle is to be 

brought in. 

• ••••• 

H. Smith won a Two^ Miles Handicap at Ealing with 
30 yards start last Saturday, but the chief racing in the 
London district was at the Palace, where the Brixton 
Ramblers had 102 entries for their Open Mile. The times 
were very fast throughout the afternoon, Thompson doing 
2.44 from scratch, his first quarter being covered in 40| sec, 
thus beating Cortis' record of 41. 

• ♦ • ♦ • ^ • 

The long-start division however supplied the winners, 
Macbeth, of Clapham Park, with 115 yards, covering the 
distance in 2 min. 39| sec., rather too fast for the scratch 
men this year. 

Of the L.B.C., F. L. Adam, with 25 yards, won his heat 
in 2 min. 46} sec., and H. R. Reynolds, with 40 yards, did 
the same in 2 min. 49f sec, and Adam was third in his 
heat in the second round ; A. B. W. Whatton (25) and 
D. R. Smith (80; also rode. 

• ««*•• 

The Half-mile Scratch produced some good racing, 
Gaskell ultimately beating Thompson by ten yards in 
1 min. 20f sec, W. TL Adam being third. W. K. beat 
A. B. W. Whatton in his heat, and F. L. Adam was one 
yard behind Thompson in 1 min. 19} sec, the fastest time 
of the day, and record for the distance. 

The Championships begin on Saturday with the Five 
Miles Bicycle and One Mile Tricycle at Birmingham, where 
the Birmingham men will probably have it all their own 
way. I hear that our President is in hard training on the 
Crystal Palace track, and hopes to ride for the One and 
Fifty Miles. 

Thb Froot Wheel Hub. 



A TOUR SUBSEQUENT TO THAT OF THE 
"RHINE EIGHT," 

CoKTiNUiKo UP Stream betond Bale to the 
Falls at Schafhausen. 
The very interesting account given in the Gazette of 
the well-known tour of the "Rhine Eight" in 1879 will 
doubtless still be fresh in the memories of the older members 
of our Club, and this, together with those of more recent 
explorers, will form an invaluable guide to any future 



Digitized by ^^oo VIC 



150 LONDON BICYCLE CLTJB GAZETTE. 



members who may contemplate a similar and almost un- 
rivalled trip. Modelling our plan upon their experiences 
and with further supplementary hints from J. F. Darrell, we 
(A. H. Rolls, St. John Ryan, and R. M. Gruggen) started 
from St. Katharine's Wharf for Antwerp, to proceed thence 
by rail to Cologne. Tlie railway journey of twelve hours, 
following immediately after boating for a previous eighteen 
hours, became exceedingly tedidus ; and when are added to 
this six changes of train during the day it may readily be 
imagined that we were not sorry to be eventually rid of 
the monotony of this form of travelling. The cost of the 
passage " abaft the funnel" and of the railway to Cologne, 
including bicycle, was two pounds each, consequently we 
secured a fair start at a comparatively small cost. In one 
particular only we acted contrary to general advice, namely, 
in deciding to ride " up stream *' rather than " with it," as 
our worthy predecessors had done. Our chief reason for 
making this variation and selecting the stiffer gradient was 
to secure the possibility of seeing the Falls of the Rhine^ 
if we found on nearing them that time and funds would 
admit of our doing so. We left Cologne in the afternoon 
of Monday, the 16th August, 1880, and rode along the good 
roads and through the interesting country already described 
by the " Rhine Eight," by Bonn to Aldernach, where we 
stayed the night. Distance 40 miles. A fair here on the 
bank of the Hver Was both picturesque and amusing, and 
after loitering observantly for some little time we were 
attracted to a boneshaker roundabout." apparently one of 
the principal feature of the fair, consisting of a good round 
dozen of the most impossible-looking 'cycles attached to- 
gether, and describing a considerable circle. There was no 
steam power or system of churning to propel us ; the motive 
power was wholly personal, consequently, as bicyclists 
** wandering on a foreign strand," we felt it to be our duty 
to try and increase the ordinary rate of speed, which we 
did to a small extent at the cost of much labour on our 
parts and to the apparent satisfaction of other riders. Our 
next feat was to demonstrate the system of a rapid dis- 
mount whilst the " merry-go-round " was in motion, but in 
this the effect of the circular movement of the apparatus 
was not taken into consideration, and our representative, 
flying off at a tangent, amused rather than impressed the 
bystanders by the rapidity of his fall ! A second member 
of our party sustained a severe gash on the knee-cap 
through his leg becoming fixed between the crank and the 
underside of the handle-bar — the machine being too small 
for him. This second mishap, though painful, was con- 
cealed from the holiday makers at much personal incon- 
venience to avoid bringing ridicule upon the expedition, 
which praiseworthy conduct on the part of our member we 
greatly appreciated when we saw the extent of the wound. 
Subsequent roundabouts had no charm for us I 

Tuesday 17th. We passed through richer, and perhaps 
more lovely scenery, stopping first at the " Hotel Treves," 



at Coblentz, where the landlord was described as having 
already proved himself a " brick" in the previous year, and 
we quickly found that the lapse of time had apparently 
in no way subdued his exceeding good nature. Our host 
made many careful inquiries after all the " eight " members 
of the previous expedition, and specially referred to one of 
that party as having amused the inhabitants of Coblentz by 
treating them to a clever performance on the bicycle in the 
open space fronting his hotel After a most elaborate 
dinner, at which our host insisted upon our having cham- 
pagne, sparkling, from the neighbouring Moselle, and 
drinking success to cycling generally, and to the L.B.C. 
particularly, we quitted the premises of this most seductive 
landlord at four p.m., with a view to covering the forty-one 
more miles to Bingen. So pleasing had been this protracted 
stay, during which we had been treated with that amount 
of thoughtful attention commonly reserved for frequent 
visitors, and so unusual had been this experience that we 
hoped that these performances in public on the wheels had 
been often repeated further up the river ! This reception 
could only be attributed to the deep impression made by 
our predecessors, and their prestige we endeavoured to 
maintain by recounting to our interested and attentive 
host some of their fast performances on the road and path ! 
Coblentz to Bingen, in our opinion, proved the very finest 
part of the river until we reached Switzerland, and even 
there the magnificent grandeur of this afternoon ride would 
be difficult to surpass. We, of course, after our dissipation, 
reached Bingen^late, so much so that towards the end of 
the run the tone of the scenery was pleasingly changed and 
illumined by the light of the moon. Total distance 53 
miles. I will not repeat names of hotels, for we always 
put up at those that were recommended, and found them 
satisfactory. There are few towns on the Rhine without 
their baths constructed in the river, and these we seldom 
neglected to visit at least once a day. Bingen was very 
satisfactorily provide in this respect, and after the usual 
swim we rode to Mayence for lunch. Mayence is a strongly 
fortified town, containing a very fine Romanesque church in 
its centre. Only a very small swimming bath was to be found 
here, containing an extensive porpoise-like looking person, 
which vividly recalled to our minds visions of tanks at our 
Aquarium. Quitting Mayence we encountered paving and 
generally bad road to Ebersheim and Nierstein, and com- 
paratively indifferent scenery, until we rejoined the river 
and reached Oppenheim. The Hotel Ritter " contained 
interesting inhabitants, and proved both comfortable and 
moderate. Distance 33^ miles. 

Thursday, 19th. The ride from here through Gunters- 
'blum to Worms was unusually rough, but the excellence of 
the bath at Worms quickly dispelled all further reflections 
upon our troubles. Of course the visitor must not omit to 
examine the beauties of this celebrated Gathdral. In the 
small town of Frankenthal, which was next visited, a 

Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



151 



Yankee, with whom we casually conversed during an 
intended hasty lunch, seemed so oyerjoyed at being able 
to ventilate the amusing peculiarities of his language, where 
it had some chance of being understood, that we had much 
difficulty in getting away. In his enthusiasm we were 
assured that the Iron Foundry in the place must be visited, 
after, of course, due libations and many indifferent cigars, 
and in a weak moment we consented to accept him as 
pioneer. There was literally nothing to repay us for the 
walk of a mile, beyond a close inspection of an uninteresting 
*' shed," in which a great bell hctd been cast, and which, in 
the words of our informant, " now pealed from the tower of 
some adjacent Cathedral." The accomplishment of this 
great work had been the most important event ever 
witnessed at Frankenthal. If we had been in time to see 
its completion we might have felt more reconciled, but to 
have frittered away more hours in this useless -and 
unedifying manner seemed ridiculous. In bidding us 
farewell our talkative friend expressed his pleasure at 
the meeting, and finally concluded by hoping that any 
member of the L.B.C. visiting Fhiladelphii^ would make a 
point of calling upon him. The address, which dealt largely 
in numbers, may still be had ! Mounting on the boulders, we 
hastily left this little town, and had not covered many miles 
before each of us in turn was enabled to relate very recent 
experiences of a trip over the handles," with no serious 
damage. Next followed the disaster that bid fair to termi- 
nate the expedition. Having quietly entered Mannheim on 
our right and proper side of the way, and rounded a corner 
into the first wide suburban street, we perceived a distant 
open carriage coming towards us on dur side of the road- 
way. As it approached to within fifty yards we observed 
that the animal was becoming very restive, and on further 
perceiving that the vehicle was occupied we at once dis- 
mounted. Had the driver acted in a similar manner the 
subsequent accident must have been avoided, but instead 
of this he came steadily on, and still continued to fix his 
eyes upon the bicycles until he had driven to within about 
ten yards of us, and then apparently for the first time 
realising the excited condition of his steed he hurriedly 
endeavoured to turn round (still on our side of the way) 
in the space between the carriage and the curb. This 
space unhappily proved insufficient for the purpose, and 
the result of this error in judgment was that the wheels 
became locked, and almost before^^the male and two 
female occupants were aware of their danger they were 
quietly emptied into the wayside drains. Our part in 
this very unnecessary and somewhat amusing proceeding 
had been clearly passive, but blame was thrown upon 
us by the male victim, perhaps because he had seen 
nothing of the primary circumstances, and assumed the 
worst. The extent of the damage was a broken shaft and 
lamp, and, according to the driver's report, a broken arm. 
Having satisfied ourselves by inquiry that the ladies were un- 



hurt (we could see the male victim dripping on the pavement 
apparently full of rage, but otherwise undamaged), we 
prepared to quit the sympathetic crowd that had quickly 
assembled, and before doing so almost voluntarily left our 
intended address ("Lang's Hotel," Heidelberg)^ for the 
mutual satisfaction of this male victim and the police, 
thinking in our innocence that it would be impossible for 
any special blame to be attached to us. Their opportunity 
was not lost. Entering Heidelberg in the dark we were 
confronted by another form of danger, and this consisted 
in their adopting a single chain principle at the point 
where the railway line crossed the main street, in place of 
our system of railway gates. The novel arrangement nearly 
proved a serious snare for us, as we rode unsuspectingly 
towards this chain, which extends across the street about 
five feet above the ground. It is most difficult to see this 
black obstacle at night ; its whereabouts are made known 
to the inhabitants by placing a red lamp in the centre of 
the street immediately under it, a signal we did not 
appreciate, and narrowly escaped a spill on to the line 
in consequence. 

Friday, 20th. We stayed' the day in Heidelberg, and 
enjoyed a ramble among the ruins of the fine old castle, 
not omitting to take full advantage of the splendid baths 
in the Neckar. During the evening, the police duly waited 
upon us " touching the little affair in Mannheim of the 
previous day," which bad in this short lapse of time 
assumed grave dimensions. The difficulty could be settled 
at once by our paying a deposit of five pounds, or, 
failing this, we were politely informed that these officers 
of the law had authority to detain one of the bicycles 
as a guarantee of the expenses of a proposed trial of the 
case, in the event of our failing to put in an appearance, 
and of the learned magistrate in our possible absence 
giving the case against us. We might have prevented our 
cause going by default, as probably some limb of the 
law" could eventually have been found in the town to 
watch proceedings for us. We spent the remainder of the 
evening in searching for a lawyer, but the one to whom we 
were recommended was away for a holiday, and the 
" stripling " left in charge was far too amused at the story 
to regard it from any business-like point of view ; we left a 
written statement of the case, which possibly never reached 
its destination. The uncertainty of the time when the case 
would come on for hearing, which was variously stated at 
from one to two months, rendered further delay useless. The 
position was a trying one; to give up a bicycle must at once 
end the trip ; to pay the cash deposit demanded we for- 
tunately found would not necessarily take away all chance 
of seeing the Falls, but must prevent any intended further 
explorations. It was an annoying sacrifice to have to make 
for being mere spectators of a carriage accident, but the 
pecuniary one we eventually made, and took three elaborate 
German receipts for the same. Ours seemed to me to be a 



Digitized by 











152 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 




good case for the Bicycle Union,and I wish that we had placed 
it in their hands; but instead of trying to do this, after the 
lapse of a couple of months we settled the case out of court, 
and agreed to a compromise, accepting a paltry balance of 
thirty-three shillings to foreclose proceedings then pending; 
a bad precedent for future bicyclists. 

Saturday, 21st. We left Heidelberg with the discom- 
forting thought that we might again be the witnesses of a 
similar dilemma, and so become the victims of further legal 
and possibly pecuniary complications. The road we now 
followed has been well likened to a cinder path in many 
places. Our route lay through Bruchsal (where there is 
good bathing in the canal), Burlach, and Rastadt, to Baden, 
a distance of 52 miles. ''Hotel Trois Rois." On the following 
morning two hours were deyoted to taking a most elaborate 
and enjoyable Baden bath. In the afternoon we rode on 
over fair roads to Strasbourg by Switzheim, Steinbach, and 
Lichtenau, where we put up at the " Maison Rouge," close 
to the cathedral, an hotel which proved noisy and dear. 
Distance 32 miles. 

Monday, 23rd. The country for many miles south of 
Strasbourg was very flat and uninteresting, and it was only 
when we had passed through Ichenheim, Eensingen, and 
Emendingen, and were nearing Freiburg and the confines of 
the celebrated Black Forest, that we became once more 
favourably impressed with the surroundings. We put up 
at the ** Hotel Pfau " at Freiburg, which was capital. 
The gardens in connection with this hotel were very large, 
and contained, besides good baths and a central fountain, 
many pleasing summer-houses. Distance 45 miles. The 
cathedral should be visited, if only for the sake of ascending 
the spire to obtain the fine view that is to be had of the 
adjacent Black Forest, which can be seen distributing itself 
far into the distance.

NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for " Q-azette "

THE SECRETARY. 
Whatever may be said or thought of the adyisability or 
inadvisability of having a Club bugler, and of definiug so 
clearly in the Club rule book how his duties are to be 
performed, do doubt can be entertained as to the necessity 
of the Club possessing a secretary ; and it is our business 
in this article to discover what are the qualities needful to 
that officer, and then to make any deductions we can from 
materials we have. Our difficulty in this matter is some- 
what increased by the fact that one can seldom even see a 
good secretary, and that therefore diagnosis of the concrete 
subject is impossible. It is of no use whatever to look for 
the model quill-wielder at a race meeting — the competitors 
make such determined attempts to get on to the path 
without their numbers, the telegraph board demands such 
constant attention, and the press are so extremely bibulous 
and " grudge " so painfully " if they be not satisfied," that 
it is quite out of the question for the unassisted eye to 
follow his meteoric movements. Glimpses have, we believe, 
occasionally been caught of aur Secretary at more social 
gatherings, such as a smoking concert or a Club ball, but 
even for this it has been necessary for the programme boy 
to run short of change, the refreshment contractor's lad to 
drop a sixpenny ice-cream, a couple of couples to collide and 
fall, all in a space of somewhere about three square yards. 



From a careful weighing of the question in the abstract, 
however, we have come to the conclusion that before all 
things it is necessary that a secretary should have an in- 
exhaustible supply of vigorous hard-working energy. With- 
out this, if a man's ability be great, he is like an electric 
lamp with both conducting wires snapped ; if his ability be 
small he is like a rush candle without a wick— a mere 
mockery of light. But energy does not complete the man. 
Energy may be dangerous and is often misapplied, and 
esprit de carps, safety-valve, and sign-board, must be 
added. This love of club may, to follow out our simile, 
be again compared in another aspect to the electric 
current, the animating vivifying essence which makes the 
carbon glow and the incandescent energy shine forth 
in all the undertakings of the club. Business aptitude 
and mother-wit are, as we pointed out in a recent article, 
certainly also desirable, though their necessity varies 
with diflFerent club constitutions. With a good practical 
committee to consult, and consulted at every turn, the 
normal amount of them will generally be found sufficient. 
Many secretaries again there are, who, in answering in- 
evitable correspondence, seem quite unable to write even 
passable grammar. We have received from some well- 
known and generally respected clubs, letters containing the 
most crying pieces (for the worse the language invariably 
the louder the tone) of English as she is spoke " that it 
is possible to imagine. Of course in our " careful weighing " 
we discovered the presence, in larger or, smaller quantities, 
of many other constituting elements — ^no competent analyst 



Google 



Digitized by 



154 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



could fail to do so— euch as fact, courtesy, good memory, 
good handwriting, which it is needless to particularise fur- 
ther. There is, however, one, the importance of which has 
been perhaps somewhat overlooked by previous enquirers, 
we allude to outside position in the cycling world. When 
a club has in its ranks two ex-chairmen of the N. C. TJ. 
executive, the public handicapper, two champions, three or 
four ex-champions, as many prospective champions, the 
acknowledged finest road rider and many others who run 
him very close, at least twelve men starting from a 
shorter mark than fifty yards, numbers of tourists, legis- 
lators, and others who are in their various spheres the 
leaders of the world of wheels, this is not necessary ; but 
in the case of a less fortunate body it is of the utmost 
consequence that all the officers, and the helmsman as 
much as any, should have a standing outside the club. 
Having thus roughly sketched the ideal, and indicated 
some of the most frequent and grievous departures from it, 
we would, in conclusion, draw our readers' attention to 
four evident morals. 1st. That we have every reason to 
feel proud of the Secretary we are blessed with. 2nd. That 
some clubs have not. 3rd. That the post being a decidedly 
onerous one, it is the duty of every member to assist the 
holder of it as much as possible. 4th. That although all 
of us are not qualified to manipulate so large a goose-quill 
as the L.B.G. requires, and to undertake the concomitant 
responsibilities, yet that we need not despair of as good 
fish remaining in the L.B.C. sea as have ever been taken 
from it by the L.B.C. Committee. 

N.E. DISTRICT. 
I hope the District will excuse my protracted and most 
unwtUing absence, which has now at last come to an end, 
and will turn out in force on Saturday to meet the N.W. 

Thbo. Godlee, District Captain. 



S.W. DISTRICT.— SPECIAL NOTICE. 
The run next Saturday is to Burnham Beeches to meet 
the West, when I hope that each member of the District 
will make a point of being present, and thus ensure my 
not being left to carry out the run alone. 

R. M. Oruooen, District Captain. 



S.E. NOTICE. 
During the next two months there will be evening runs 
from the Fire Station on Wednesday evenings at 7.30 to 
the Eeston Ponds, where there is good bathing. 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 

SATURDAY RUNS. 
N.E. District. 
7th July. At last the D.C. has returned and greeted 
his District at the Comer to the number of four. Pro- 
ceeding by Whip's Cross, which was very much tramwayed 
in the direction of Snaresbrook, we were joined by Weeks, 



and so on, by the level crossing and the lower road, which 
was in better condition than usual. A halt was made at 
Abridge, and again when Passingford Bridge was reached, 
just to see if anyone else was coming, and a little 
later up rushed the Captain, explaining that his various 
brothers had, for divers good and sufficient reasons, 
kept him waiting, and then been unable to come. 
At Ongar, as we reached the "King's Head," Baldwin 
appeared; the D.C. was quite expecting him, as they 
had met in Cologne Station the previous afternoon. While 
tea was being prepared. Weeks undertook to pilot the party 
to a bathing place ; and although the place was not large, 
it was voted a great success by all who tried it, and was 
quite big enough for a satisfactory dive. Just after tea 
the Sub-Editor — also returned from his wanderings — turned 
up, making the party up to nine, and about the same time 
the party began to go home ; Jolly and Child getting off 
early because they wanted to go slowly, and it is credibly 
reported racing all the way. The rest more soberly pro- 
ceeded to their destinations. Present : H. Baldwin, Ashley 
Barrett, T. Child, S. G. Hough, Jolly, Lloyd, Lyne, Weeks, 
and Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 



N.W. District. 
It has been said that little beginnings have great endings, 
and it is to be hoped that the result of the second part of 
this season's Club runs in our District will prove the truth 
of the old " saw," for that only seven members should turn 
out for a run to Shenley with the inducements held out is 
almost incomprehensibla The programme was carried out 
literally — good company, etc., making it a very enjoyable 
run, the return being accomplished almost by daylight. 
Present : Elgood, H. M. Hewitt, S. R. Hewitt, Langmore, 
Nevill, W. J. Williams, and 

A. H. LowN, District Captain. 

Next Saturday we meet the N.E. at Cheshunt, a ride 
which will commend itself to all who have ridden there 
Northaw and Goff's Oak, so that I hope a large number 
will be at "Jack Straw's Castle," 3.45, and Tally Ho! 
Comer, 4.25. 

W. District. 

I went down to Savernake Cottage, and as at 3.45 no one 
had turned up went, on to Kingston, where I met the S.W. in 
the person of E. J. Sherriff and A. E. Buckler, with whom 
I rode as far as the turning for Ockham. Found the 
" Hautboy and Fiddle " in the hands of beanfeasters, so 
went on to Ripley. Here I found Hawley, with whom I 
had tea. Just as we were finishing Thorn came in ; after 
he had finished his tea, he and I rode on to within 
a mile of Guildford, where we met Marriott, the first 
man of the twenty-four hours L.T.C. Road Ride, coached 
by Jack Keen. We returned with them to Ripley 
(201^ miles.) Here I left Thorn, and went on to see the

finish, which took place at the "Bay Tree/' Merton, 
(219 miles), at 11.58. After waiting till 12 to see that no 
one else came up I left for home, via Wimbledon. Present : 
0. Thorn and 0. G. M. Leeds. 

44, Cathcart Road. 

S.B. District. 
Considering the counter-attractions of Henley, the river, 
and the racing path, fifteen and two visitors at Bletchingley 
ought not perhaps to be considered amiss, though it cer- 
tainly fell short of the high expectations of the D.G. That 
worthy, after tramping up and down in firont of the Fire 
Station for some time in a state of mind that is more easily 
imagined than described, was roused from his despondency 
by the timely appearance of Wyndham, whose radiant 
smiles and cheering spirits, backed up with arrival of one 
or two members more, put a more salubrious complexion on 
affairs. Among the last comers was Gerich, "positively 
his first appearance this season.'' There are still not a few 
who might well imitate his example of better late than never. 
Soon after half-past four we were off down the well-known 
road to GK>dstone; two of the younger members led the 
way, but a fast trotting horse coming alongside provoked 
in them that spirit of emulation which "springs eternally in 
the yautl^td breast/' and they disappeared in a cloud of dust 
At the top of GK)dstone Hill there was quite a bevy of men in 
grey ; and with rumours of one or two more ahead we began to 
feel that the District would not disgrace itself at Bletchingley 
after all. At the " White Hart " we found The O'Reilly^ 
who had ridden up from Brighton, having surmounted Hand- 
cross and the Redhill Station hills on the way. With such 
an example, no wonder some of our " younger, bloods," 
as they have been called, are showing such promises. 
A man who has never attended a meet at Bletchingley 
cannot know what a Club tea is, or rather what it should 
be. While we were doing justice to the good fare J. S. 
Whatton arrived, and shortly after H. G. A. Brown and 
A. B. W. Whatton coming in brought the party to its 



full complement of seventeen, including two visitors. Soon 
after eight our gathering began to dissolve, some returning 
home by Redhill or Merstham, while the main body of 
seven, all told, wended its way steadily back through God- 
stone, and reached Croydon before ten. Present : H. G. A. 
Brown, Halliday, S. Herbert, Gerich, McRae, The O'Reilly, 
W. C. Oswald, A. L. Potter, A. B. W. Whatton (W.), J. S. 
Whatton (W.), Whealler, F. H. Williams, Willis, W. 
Wyndham, two visitors (Church and A. C. Potter), and 
R. G. Francis, District Captain. 



Our next run is to Westerham where, it is to be hoped, 
there will be a good attendance. The Croydon Division 
will leave the Fire Station at 4.30 sharp, for Westerham 
direct, where they will meet the Blackheath Division at six 
o'clock. Tea at the " King's Arms " at 6.30. 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 

S.W. District. 

7th July. Two members, accompanied by Leeds (W.), 
left Kingston at the usual hour, and proceeded by the 
well-known road to Ripley. The W. run being over almost 
the same ground, it seems a pity that a joint meet had not 
been provided, which would doubtlessly have proved more 
attractive than two poorly attended meets. The absence of 
the D.C., who was engaged somewhere shouldering a rifle 
for the good of his country, had such a depressing effect 
that not even the entreaties of Hawley could induce us to 
face the festivities of the tea-table, so, after a brief stoppage, 
we commenced the return journey. Just as we were 
starting, Leeds came in from Ockham, not having found 
any wool, and at the top of Redhill we met Oliver Tl^orn 
on his way down ; after directing him where to find his 
District, we continued our stroll homewards, which termi- 
nated satisfactorily at an early hour. Present: E. J. 
Sherriff and Arthur K Buckler. 

H. H. Sherriff started late, and followed on, but unfor- 
tunately succeeded in missing us.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
As was expected the Five Miles Bicycle Championship of 
the N.C.U. at Birmingham last Saturday ended in the 
victory of a local man ; in fact Gaskell was the only visitor 
who put in an appearance, though J. S. Whatton had 
entered. Owing partly to a strong wind the times were 
slow, while, after Birmingham fashion, everything was left 
to the last lap. 

• «•««• 

Sutton, of the Edghaston Harriers, and Chambers, of the 
Speedwell B.C., were first and second respectively, the 
latter just beating Oaskell by a foot. Time, 16 min. 26) sec. 
In the second heat Auster, of the Speedwell B.C., and 
Illston, of the Royal Mail, was first and second, Whish and 
RatcliiTe also starting. Time, 16 min. 39{ sec. 

• * * • • • 

The final, after a good race, resulted in favour of Sutton 

by two yards, Anster second, and Illston close up, in 

16 min. 42f sec. 

*•«••• 

The One Mile Tricycle Championship was practically a 
match between Liles and Lowndes, and Liles getting the 
best of it in the last lap won pretty easily in 3 min. 18) sec. 
About 3000 people were present, which is a small gate for 

Birmingham. 

• «•««• 

The Lewisham B.C. had a good entry for their Mile 
Handicap at the Palace last Saturday, when the times were 
again very fast. Douglas Smith, with 80 yards, took third 
prize, beating Thompson, the scratch man» by eight yards 
in his heat, ridden in 2 min. 47 sec, winning his heat in 
the second round in 2 min. 45| sec, and running third in 
the final to Mann, of the Pickwick, with 115 yards, and 
Dray, with 120, in 2 min. 45f sec, all the placed men being 
close together. 

J. S. Whatton (10), L. B. Reynolds (80), and A. B. 
Whatton (50) also ran, but did not get placed. The late 
five mile champion does not seem to have recovered his 

last season's form. 

• ••••* 

The L.T.C. 24 hours Road Ride took place last Saturday. 
Out of 74 entries 67 men turned up at the post. The route 
lay through Brighton, Shoreham, Steyning, Finden, Arun- 
del, Chichester, Fareham (97), Romsey, Salisbury, Win- 
chester, Alton (175), Famham, Guildford, Riplej^ (201i), 
Esher, Kingston, Merton (218J), Mitcham, and Croydon. 
Bronze medals were obtainable at 100 miles, silver at 125, 
silver enamelled at 150, gold at 175, and gold enamelled at 
200. The race practically en^ed at Ripley, where Marriot 
arrived at 9.30, followed at 10.23 by Nixon, who retired 
here. Vesey arrived at 10.29, and Gosset at 11.41. 
Marriot rode on to Merton, completing 21 8 J miles in 
23 hr. 50 min. 

Thb Front Wheel Hub. 



QUILLS. 

There were numerous enquiries for the S.E. Guide at 
Bletchingley. It was currently reported that much against 
his inclination he had gone up the river to oblige some 
friends." 

Rayleigh Hill, which has long defied the attempts of all 
Essex men, has at length been compelled to lower its head 

beneath the triumphant wheel of our Captain. 

• «•••« 

The vagaries of a certain set of cyclists are evidently new 

to the man in charge of the railway crossing at Doncaster. 

He ieemed quite struck by the appearance of two bicyclists 

who had passed en route for London. " They belonged to 

some regiment, I think," he said, with a puzzled air. 
*••••• 

Great things are expected at Cheshunt on Saturday. 

There will be a big array of Club officers, and roses, it 

should be borne in mind, are now in full bloom. 

It was hardly sjrprising that the N.E. tenor, who, by 
the bye, is coming out strongly as a hill-climber, was un- 
able to overtake wbat was designated as the Slow Division 
on the return journey from Ongar. Subsequent enquiries 
elicited the fact that a veterans' match was held from 

Ongar to Abridge. 

• • • • « » 

A. B. W. Whatton rode Reigate Hill returning from the 

Club run last Saturday. 

• ••««« 

H. R. Reynolds, who had never previously ridden a 
tricycle, started in the L.T.C. road race on a borrowed 
machine, and in spite of losing an hour by misdirection at 

Fareham, he completed 175 miles by about 10.30. 

• • « • • • « 

As the N.R D.C. was in Vienna within 48 hours of the 
run the news of his return had not spread, and there was 
something very eccentric in headgear at the N.E. run.

THE AMATEUR. 

The fact has for some time been clear to a disgusted 
world that there are amateurs and amateurs. There are 
amateurs in name who are not so in spirit, and amateurs in 
spirit who are not so in name. And it is very painful to 
the happily large number of amateurs both in name and 
spirit to see the other class so conspicuously successful on 
the racing track ; distressing, not by any jealousy of the 
prizes won by such men, but by the discredit their pro- 
minence casts on the sport, and the bad effect their 
example has on younger men. The true amateur is a man 
whose sole motire is the love of the sport, the delight in 
beating worthy rivals, or in not being beaten without doing 
his best to win. He rides in races for honour and victory, 
and not for gain, and the value of the prizes he wins is not 
their selling price in pounds, shillings, and pence, but in 
the hard-won victories over men whom it was an honour to 
beat, of which they are the memorials. 

It is impossible, however, to talk with any racing men of 
the present day without hearing the very reverse of this 
about some of the most prominent riders on the path. We 
are told that A is paid so much a week by certain makers 
of bicycles nominally as a mechanic, but really for adver- 
tising their machines by racing on them ; that B is in the 
pay of a set of bookmakers, and never rides to win unless it 



BNTS. 

PAOI 



ConoemiDg CeUtiloid CoUan 162 

Table of Record Timee 162 

AU Round the Wheel 168 

The " C.T.O. Handbook and Guide " 168 

Qnillfl 164 

Racing Fixturea 164 



suits the book of his employers. We hear this, and tales 
like this, with great minuteness of detail from rider after 
rider. All agree that "something must be done," and (as 
is usual in the case of these unanimous resolves) "someone 
else ought to do something." "Someone else," on enquiry, 
is generally specified as the Union, and surprise is ex- 
pressed that the Union does not make a clean sweep of the 
field, and suspend all doubtful characters, pour encourager 
les autres. 

Now we happen to have heard some of the views of the 
leading men on the Union in this matter, and they are 
shortly to this effect: "Qive us the evidence," say the 
Executive, " and we will act on it. All you bring us is— 
* Everybody knows it ; ' or, ' C was told by D, who heard 
it from E, whose uncle's brother's nephew lives in the same 
street as the man in question ; ' or rather," the Executive 
say, " You don't bring it us ; all of you blame us for not 
formally suspending A and B for grave misconduct, and 
announcing it to the world in all the papers ; but not one of 
you has the courage to come forward yourself and charge 
A and B with definite offences, and give definite evidence 
in support of your charge. ITie hearsay you want us to 
act on would not be admitted against a little boy charged 
with stealing a penny, and you want us on it to make the 
most serious imputations against the character of men of 
some position on the path." 

And we think the reply of the Executive is fair. The 
Union has no inquisitorial power by which it can convict 
offenders without legal evidence ; no rack or dungeon to 



Digitized by VjOO^IC 



158 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



obtain information from people who keep their mouth shut. 
It has no large reserve fund, like the Jockey Club, to enable 
it to proceed without fear of the pecuniary risk of an action 
for libel ; no salaried or unsalaried detectives on its stafiF 
who can track out the cycling criminal and bring him to 
the bar of justice. The purification of the racing path 
must be efl'ected by the men on the racing path ; the Union 
has no heavenly power of helping those who will not help 
themselves. Let racing men come forward with their 
charge and their evidence, and the Union will act with 
promptitude and severity ; but there should be no grum- 
bling if the Union takes no steps when the charge is made 
but the evidence forgotten.
