IMPORTANT.— POSTPONEMENT OP GENERAL 

MEET AT EWELL. 
I have this morning received a telegram from the land- 
lord of the "Glyn Arms," Ewell, saying that he will be 
UDable to provide tea for us on Saturday next. As time 
unfortunately does not allow other arrangements to be made 
I would suggest that the Districts meet at their usual 
meeting places and then decide the direction of their runs. 

Ashley W. Babbett, Captain. 



N.E. District. 
* Uth July. The N.E. District left the Corner rather 
late, and proceeded by way of Whip's Cross and Wood 
Street to Chingford and Cheshunt. At the *' Green Dragon " 
no member of the N.W. was to be seen. After waiting 
until 7.0 the District partook of the necessary refreshment 
and tobacco, and returned by the same route as the out- 
ward journey, the roads being very fair in spite of the rain 
which had fallen. The above strictly truthful account I 
would gladly leave to tell its simple tale, yet perhaps some 
future historian might think it somewhat vague, and I 
therefore add the following details : The Captain, Lloyd, 
and Lyne met at the usual hour at the Corner, and aft^r 
seeing a thunderstorm well started they made tracks for a 
neighbouring butcher's shop, which they occupied for two 
full hours, when, the thunderstorm abating, they separated 
to their respective homes. The D.C., on the other hand, 
came a little late, and finding, just at 4 o'clock, no one at 
the Corner, and a good deal of thunder and lightning 
about, he rushed back to Whip's Cross thoroughly soaked 
through with his ride. He too waited, but a sense of 
stem duty called him. When at length the rain began to 
abate at about 5.50 he made for Cheshunt, reached the 
" Green Dragon " two minutes before 7.0, drank a frugal 
meal, lighted a cigatette, found no one had been seen or 
heard of, and withdrew hurriedly to Walthamstow. Time 
from start to finish 2hr. 25min. in spite of the roads. But 
the reputation of the District was saved. 

Thbo. Goplee, District Captain. 



Another account 
July Uth. The clerk of the weather seems to have a 
spite against the joint meets of this District, and on this 
occasion successfully frustrated the attempts of a few indi- 
viduals to maintain the prestige gained by it at Erith. 
Two members met at Sybom's Corner, and were shortly 
joined by the Captain. After a few minutes conversation 
it was discovered that a terrific storm was bearing down in 
the direction of Walthamstow, and trees not being con- 
sidered the best protection from lightning it was decided to 
make for the first shelter that offered itself. Accordingly 
a start was made, but firat some characters in the cuneiform 
style (worthy of the *' needle " in its most legible days) 
were traced in the mud for the guidance of the D.C. 
Whether he saw them is not known. Shelter was soon 
found under a shop awning in Walthamstow, and a variety 
of weather of a stormy nature transpired. The sufferings, 
mental and physical, of the trio are beyond description. 
After standing up for nearly two hours the rain condescended 
to stop, but as the storm appeared to be still travelling 
northwards, and any attempt to caiTy out the run would 
have necessitated riding into it again, it was unanimously

159 



Yoted that the official ran was at an end ; one member 
went homewards, after a reference to a six o'clock dinner ; 
the survivors made tracks for a piece of blue sky in a 
southerly direction, and a rash determination was almost 
come to to ride into Kent ; Forest Gate, however, proved 
the limit, where the hospitality of the contingent was 
kindly extended to the " private member " at their head- 
quarters. It would doubtless be unbecoming on the part 
of the present writer to abuse the kindness of his hosts by 
revealing to the general public the internal economy of the 
F.6.C. ; he will therefore merely hint at the splendour of 
the spacious stables, the fitting shop^ conservatories, and 
grounds. The guest was afterwards provided with an 
escort to the Lea Bridge Road, and sent on his way re- 
joicing. Present : Ashley Barrett (Captain), Lloyd, Iiyne, 
(Thunder, Lightning, and Rain, visitors.) 
P.S. — Must keep up the average somehow. 

8.W. District. 
There is at present a painful monotony about our S.W. 
meets, which it must be hoped Iiad reached its climax on 
Saturday last, for it really seems to be regarded as a 
sufficient reason for not attending when individual members 
are privately informed that their humble servant will attend 
as usual at Kingston. There was however a gleam of satis- 
faction in noting in our latest issue that Buckler had once 
more taken the initiative and carried out our run to Ripley, 
and there is little reason to doubt that he will continue to 
act in a similar capacity for the next two Saturdays, during 
my absence upon a short tour with Barker and Potter, in 
Normandy. The substantial showers last Saturday subsided 
about 4.45 p.m. when the District left Kingston Station, 
and the run for Bumham Beeches commenced. The route 
selected was by Hampton, and unfortunately by Kempton 
Park race course, from whence everyone seemed to be 
returning just as the District was en route for Staines. 
From Staines the run was continued along a bye lane 
by the banks of the Colne to Colnbrook, whence the 
well-known Bath road was followed to Slough, which 
was reached after much labour at 6.45 p.m. Upon 
enquiring of the fair hostess at the C.T.C. house at 



Slough, if she had ever visited the renowned Beeches at 
Bumham, which had so long been the ultimate object of the 
present excursion, and upon eliciting an affirmative reply, it 
was somewhat consoling to the exhausted energies of the 
district to be further informed that there was really little 
at Bumham to satisfy our craving after "nature's beauties." 
Although but partially convinced of the truth of this 
assertion, yet with six more miles before us, an already 
advanced hour, and a cloudy uncertain sky, it was unani- 
mously decided after careful reflection to postpone the visit 
to Stoke Pogis and the Beeches until a more favourable 
opportunity than the present one presented itself. The 
return journey along the Bath Road was accomplished in 
about an hour and a half. Distance from Kingston 37 
miles. The mn next Saturday is to Ewell, where there is 
a General Meet, and where it is extremely desirable that 
the S.W. should display their numerical strength. 

B. M. Qruogen, District Captain. 

S.E. District. 
During the bright intervals between the heavy thunder- 
showers on Saturday, thirteen men managed to find their 
way over to Westerham. Owing to an important engagement, 
it was rather late before the D.C. made his appearance at 
the festive scene, and in his absence, J. G. Oswald had kindly 
undertaken the charge of the Croydon Division. The 
Sevenoaks contingent turned up in force, but the Blackheath 
men were conspicuous only by their absence. After tea at the 
" King's Arms," and th^ usual stroll to view the church and 
the other antiquities of the place, a start homewards was 
made soon after eight, and Croydon was reached via 
Westerham Hill and Hayes Common. Present : Barker, 
Clements, Hartridge, Alf. Herbert, S. Herbert, Morgan, 
J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, L. Fetter, F. B. Smith, 
Whealler, F. H. Williams, one visitor, and 

R. 6. Francis, District Captain. 

I sincerely hope that S.E. members will do their best to 
see their District well represented at the General Meet at 
Ewell next Saturday. The Croydon Division will wait at 
St. Katharine Street for the Blackheath men until 4.30. 

R. G. Francis, District Captain.

THE AMATEUR CHAMPIONSHIPS. 
The coincidence of the London School Board fite and 
the above important fixture produced the largest number 
of spectators we ever remember to have seen at this path, 
though the weather for the most part was very un- 
favourable. The L.B.C. was not riding anything like its 
full strength, but speaking generally the competitors were 
a very representative lot, no rider in any previous cham- 
pionship having come further than from Birmingham, while 
the management was good with the exception that in the 
first heat of the Mile the bell for last lap was deserted by 
the official in charge of it, a mistake which might have led 
to much unpleasantness. The final of the bicycle event 
was spoilt by Sutton's fall, and that of the tricycle by 
Lowndes giving up, as he did last year, directly he was 
challenged. 

One Milk Bicycle Championship. 

Heat 1.— A Thompson, Sutton B.C., 1 ; T. D. Oliver, 
Jesmond AB.C, 2. Thompson led throughout, with Oliver 
on his hind wheel till halfway round the hist lap, when in 
response to the yells from the dressing room the bell sounded 
and the Newcastle man made his effort, coming up outside 
Thompson, who just won by six—some said four — inches. 
Time, 2 min. 46| sec. 

Heat 2.— R. Chambers, Speedwell B.C., 1 ; F. E. S. 
Perry, Crichton B.C., 2. Perry, trying for the time stan- 
dard, led till the last lap, when his opponent passed him 
and won by a yard, with a good deal in hand. Time, 
2 min. 50^ sec. 

Heat 3.— H. W. Gaskell, Ranelagh Harriers, l ; F. R 
Fry, Clifton B.C., 2 ; M. H. Whish, Birchfield B.C., 3. At 
the end of the first lap Fry was leading by ten yards, but 
in the last all were«close up till Gaskell, coming away over 
the water, won easily by six yards ; Fry, contrary to expec- 
tation, beating the Midlander for second place by about the 
same distance. Time, 2 min. 52} sec. 

Heat 4.— G. H. Illston, Royal Mail B.C., 1 ; F. L. Adam, 
L.B.C., 0. Our man, who had discarded his "Club" on 
the score of weakness, and has come into possession of 
Beck's " Humber," snapped its crank at starting and fell, 
leaving Illston to ride over alone, which he succeded in 
doing in 2 min. 53 sec. apparently with great ease. 

Heat 5. — F. Sutton, Edgbaston Harriers, 1 ; P. J. 
McKinlay, Beckenham B.C., 2 ; A. B. W. Whatton, 
C.U.Bi.C. and L.B.C., 3. Until the last lap McKinlay led, 
when the Harrier, who has the. gift of a very sudden spurt, 
shot out and at once gained twenty yards ; after which, 
seeing that he had his men safe, he easied considerably, 
and won by about that distance. Twenty-five yards 
separated McKinlay from the third man, who is not riding 
as well as he was. Time, 2 min. 55f sec. 

This brought the first round to a close, but the executive 
allowed F. L. Adam to ride over by himself to try for a 
time medal, noticing, possibly, that with an advancing 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



thunderstorm a strong, cold wind had sprung up, making 
the feat well-nigh an impossible one. Putting aside our 
sense of the consideration shown to one of our men, we 
cannot help thinking that the precedent thus established 
might be found a very inconvenient one in case of a com- 
petitor in the 50 miles championship claiming under it to 
ride over the distance again. 

SECOND ROUND. 

Heat 1.— H. W. Gaskell, 1 ; A. Thompson, 2. Thompson 
again put it all in from the start, leading till 200 yards 
from home, when the ex-Liverpudlian went by and won by 
two lengths. Time 2 min. 50f sec. 

Heat 2.— F. Sutton, 1 ; G. H. Illston, 2 ; T. D. Oliver, 3. 
There was no hankering after a time medal here, each man 
being content to wait for the finish, which resulted in Sutton 
winning a pretty race by three yards, five dividing second 
and third. Time 3 min. 5f sec. 

Final Heat.— H. W. Gaskell, 1 ; A. Thompson, 2 ; F. 
Sutton fell. Once more Thompson dashed off with the 
lead, Gaskell lying last until passing the dressing room in 
the last lap but one, when Sutton slipped his pedal and fell, 
Gaskell, who was close behind, getting by very cleverly on 
the gravel inside. Thompson continued to lead till passing 
the starting post for the last time, when Gaskell coming 
away at his usual time, landed himself champion by some 
five or six yards. Time 2 min. 55$ sec. Last lap 40| sec. 

Ten Miles Taigtcle Championship. 

Heat 1.— C. D. Vesey, Surrey B.C., and C. E. Liles, 
L.A.C., rode over for places in 41 min. 50^ sec.^ a very 
slow performance to watch. 

Heat 2. — M. J. Lowndes, Coventry, 1 ; G. Smith, 
Merry Rovers T.C., 2 ; R. Chambera, Speedwell B.C., ; 
R. T. Cassall, Berretta C.C., 0. Chambers had reserved 
himself for this event instead of starting in the second 
round of the Bicycle Championship — a decided miscalcula- 
tion, if he desired to do himself justice ; but as Jack Keen 
remarked to the writer, the Birmingham men sometimes 
talk to one another." Four abreast crowded the path at 
starting, very unnecessarily, considering that there were 
only two in the first heat, and Lowndes did wisely in re- 
maining at his station until the others were well on the 
move. Chambers and Cassall retired at different stages, 
and the " giant " won easily by 300 yards. 

Final Heat.— C. E. Liles, 1; G. Smith, 2; M. J. 
Lowndes, ; CD. Vesey, 0. Vesey, who was riding a 
"Rotary," geared much more highly than his strength 
allowed, was quite out of the hunt, and Smith was lapped 
before the fourth mile was completed, after which he stuck 
pluckily to the leaders. Lowndes, as usual, made the pace 
very hot, Liles waiting upon him till crossing the south 
causeway for the last time but one, when he spurted and 
drew level, at which Lowndes was attacked with cramp in 
the leg and suddenly stopped, the L. A.C. man doing the 
last lap by himself in 52| sec, and winning by about 550

LONDON BICYCLE 


CLUB GAZETTE. 161 


— 




yards. Smith finished second, Lowndes going on slowly as 
far as the dressing room, outside which his leg was attended 
to, though the majority of the spectators appeared to con- 
sider that the symptoms were rather those of cramp in the 
heart. It is needless to add that Liles' victory was a very 
popular one. J. S. W. 

SOUTH-EAST. . 
''Home keeping youth have ever homely wits." So 
wrote the man who never, so far as we know, left Warwick- 
shire except for the metropolis. But, following his precept 
rather than his example, I had been spending part of the 
"long" wandering from place to place, visiting friends, 
se'eing the country, and picking up one or two prizes at 
country meetings, and now, joining my brother (A. B.) at 
Eastbourne, we decided on returning to town by way of the 
Kent coast. 

We took the usual road over the Pevensey levels to the 
old ruin, and found the. surface better than usual, though 
the absence of any sort of shelter from the violent east 
wind suggested the peculiar aptitude of the place for the 
erection of an anemometer. We got into the Lewes and 
Battle high road at Linfield, which is situated at the top 
of a long hill, and turned to the right to Battle, leaving 
Normanhurst (Sir T. Brassey's place) above us on the left. 
It was the wrong day for the abbey, but the fine old church 
was open and was undergoing, at the hands of the rector's 
family, a magnificent preparation for the harvest festival 
At the end of the town the signpost will be a sufficient 
direction to Selescombe, at which place, instead of following 
our own inclinations — ^which would have led us by a lane 
to Broad Oak Gross — we took advice and the straight road 
up the hill to Crispe's Corner, which is certainly the right 
thing to do. From Battle to this village, where four ways 
converge upon a first-rate signpost, the road is very hilly, 
rather rough, and a little hard to find ; but all the rest of 
the way to Rye is much more level, fairly smooth, and 
unmistakable main road. It runs along the tops of the 
hills, with very fine views throughout, after the manner of 
an elongated Hog's-back, and finishes with a spectacle 
which, as far as I know, is unique — ^the two ancient towns 
of Winchelsea and Rye standing each on its rock in the 
middle of the marsh. 

At Rye we stopped at an inn which the townspeople know 
as the " Sinkey Ports " Hotel (B.T.C.), which is scarcely up 
to the mark ; but the Ypres Tower, and the Old Gates, as 
well as the Parish Church, are worth going a long way to see. 
In the visitors book at the church we noticed the signatures 
of the Lord Mayor and Lady Mayoress, who, on the occasion 
of the recent civic visit, were pleased to contribute five 
pounds to the restoration fund, a sum' which looked vast 
beside the shilling and halfcrown contributions of the more 
ordinary mortal. Leaving Rye by the bridge over the 
Rother, the traveller at once re-enters the marsh, a most 


primitive and interesting district, so much out of the way, 
that the inhabitants will tell you (See the Guide book ; we 
did not ask because we did not quite know how to put the 
question) that the world consists of Europe, Asia, Africa, 
and America, and Romney marsh. 

Brockland is a curious village, and the church spire is a 
splendid conception, detached from the rest of the building 
and constructed from the ground of tarred wood. Its 
architectural style is its own, but a model might perhaps be 
formed by breaking off the stems of three wine glasses about 
an inch from the bowl, and placing them inverted upon one 
another. From us it provoked laughter, but that a constant 
contemplation of it is apt to engender a more dangerous 
state of mind, may be inferred from a touching appeal 
signed by the churchwardens, which is painted up near the 
door, begging the parishioners to abstain from throwing 
stones at the church steeple ! As we saw no one indulging in 
this pastime, and as the proclamation is dated 1832, we 
concluded that it was only a marsh reflection of the excited 
state of public feeling at the time. However the notice 
remains. 

A mile or two beyond Blockland we crossed the railway, 
which runs right out to the end of Dungeness, whose light- 
house may be seen away to the right, glimmering over some 
six miles of marsh. Dungeness Station is a terminus, and 
I should say an unprofitable one, for the only places 
marked on the map within three miles are the said 
lighthouse, " Number One Battery," Dunge Marsh Court," 
and " Abnor Pit." The nature of the last settlement we 
did not discover. 

We passed the night at the comfortable B.T.C. house at 
New Romney, and on Thursday morning, after having 
made some progress against a violent head wind, we made 
the pleasing discovery that A. B. was no longer burdened 
by the weight of his Take-about knapsack (by no means an 
unusual incident of our touring), and he consequently en- 
joyed a fast run down the wind to recover it from the hold 
of the " Ship." Part of the way to Hythe the road runs 
under Dymchurch Wall, which is maintained at a cost of 
£5,000 a year, to prevent the encroachments of the sea, a 
somewhat difficult matter, if we may judge from the earnest 
discussion by some eight or ten greyheaded farmers, which 
we heard at the inn at New Romney, touching the election 
of a bailiff of the marsh, presumably an official whose 
duties are connected with the care of the great sea wall. 

After visiting Saltwood Castle, and the (supposed) Saxon 
bones in the crjrpt of the parish church at Hythe, there is 
nothing to detain the tourist, except the immense hill out 
of Folkestone, until Dover is reached, where we saw the 
twin ship Ccdais-Douvres, and had a miserable dinner. 
Dover to Deal is Folkestone to Dover encored, and as we 
were struggling with an even more boisterous wind than 
we encountered on the same road in '80, we had but a bad 
time of it. The day's journey ended in darkness at Sand- 






Digitized by VjOOQ iC 





162 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



wich, for the wind was sucli as to preclude the possibility of 
lighting or keeping alight any known lamp. The B.T.G. 
Hotel at Sandwich is exceedingly comfortable, and in every 
way to be strongly recommended. 

Friday. Two miles out of Sandwich is the ferry house 
for Richborough Castle, and one is punted across. It is 
"four-square/' only the outer walls remaining, which at 
the present time enclose a ploughed field ; but these are in 
a wonderful state of preservation considering their Koman 
origin, and a further attraction is found in an underground 
passage following the circuit (if one may use that expression 
of what is rectangular) of the walls. We penetrated about 
100 yards, but having burned up all our matches and the 
box, we were forced to retire. Next time we shall take in 
a head lamp. 

The next stage was Bamsgate, but Bamsgate is nothing 
without its Saturday to Monday mob, and the same remark 
applies to Margate, four miles on. The former, however, 
can boast of a first rate coffee tavern. 
[Note. — There is no doubt that limejuice is the thing to 

drink.] 

From Margate there is a capital run to Canterbury, 
whence the Watling Street is known to everyone, and as we 
cannot speak of it in terms of affection it is better left un- 
described. Sufiice it to say that we got home that night 
after a very enjoyable three days ; though it rained at 
intervals throughout, and the wind was very strong as far 
as Margate, after which, when it might have helped us, it 
died away altogether. J. S. Whatton. 



CONCERNING CELLULOID COLLARS. 
These are, undoubtedly, convenient articles, but on first 
acquaintance they are also apt to be disappointing. Perhaps 
a few hints on the subject may save a fellow-member some 
amount of vexation of spirit. I remember thinking what 
a treasure I had got when I brought home my first " ever- 
clean," and how my opinion altered when, the first time I 
put it on, one of the buttonholes split right to the edge. 
Since then I have had some experience, and these are the 
results : The first thing I do on buying a collar, is to en- 
large the centre of the buttonhole slightly with a shoe 
maker's punch— such as they use for making eyelet holes in 
boots. Next put your collar into the water-jug for a day 
or two, and if, afterwards, you have not used it for some 
time, it is better to repeat this experiment— just put it in 
soak overnight, and it will be much more comfortable to 
wear. Occasional scrubbing with soap and water and a 
nailbrush will keep them presentable for a long time, but 
they do become discoloured at last. There is some cleaning 
stuff to be got at the shops, but I have not tried it ; it is 
sold in bottles. I find a little sulphuric ether, used with 
the finger or cotton wool answers capitally, but it must not 
be used near a candle, as it is very inflammable, or, rather. 



explosive. By attention to the points I have mentioned 
these collars will be found to last much longer, and will be 
found not only convenient, but economical as well 

La-di-da.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
Another record has gone. Mr. James Lennox, of Dumfries, 
has ridden from John o' Oroats to Land's End in 10 days, 
4 hours, 30 minutes, including a Sunday, on which he did 
no riding. He rode by Inverness, Perth, Edinboro', Biggar, 
Carlisle, Penrith; Kendal, Lancaster, Warrington, Welling- 
ton, Worcester, Gloucester, Bristol, Taunton, and Exeter. 
A headwind blew the whole time, and it rained on every 
day but two. Moreover, for two days Mr. Lennox rode on a 
machine with only one handle-bar. Clearly, our President 
or H. R. will have to set to and do the distance (about 
950 miles) in the week. 

• «•*•• 

Both championships run on Saturday remain in London, 
though Sutton might very likely have won the mile, but 
for his fall, as he showed a splendid tu^i of speed in his 
heats. 

«••««• 

Gaskell's last lap in the final of the Mile was a record for 
the ground, 40f seconds for 504 yards ; this represents 35| 
seconds (about) for the quarter mile, which is well inside 
J. S. Whatton'^ record of 36f . Possibly Sutton might not 
have been equal to this, though the last lap of the Five 
Miles at Aston, which is about the same length, 501 yards, 
I think, took under 41 seconds. 

• ••••• 

F. L. Adam had very hard luck in his start; many 
starters would have pulled both the men back, but Keen 
thought he had no power to act. 

• • • • • • 

The best race of the day was undoubtedly between 
Thompson and Oliver in the first heat ; the fastest time 
of the day, 2 min 46f sec, and the closest finish. 

• « • • ♦ • 

Lowndes' defeat in the Ten Miles was a very bad case of 
want of pluck ; the moment Liles passed him he shut up, 
as he has often done before. It is very curious that a man 
who can make pace so fast and well should be such an utter 
coward when collared. 

• •••«« 

Buckley was on the ground with his arm out of a sling. 



Truly the L.B.C. is unfortunate in the show it makes for 
the championships. 

* ♦ ♦ ♦ ♦ « 

All sorts of rumours were flying about Sutton. I have 
always, however, distrusted precise statements since Hillier 
was told by a Midland cyclist who did not know him, that 
" he knew as a fact that that chap Hillier was in the pay 
of a firm of bookmakers at the Alexandra Palace." 

I am more than ever confirmed in my opinion that a 
tricycle on the racing path is a fish out of water after 
Saturday's exhibition. We shall be having " growler " 
and omnibus championships next. 

*♦♦«*• 

The Second Summer Meeting of the L.A.C., postponed 
from Saturday last, was held on Tuesday evening. The 
Four Miles Bicycle Handicap (Open) was won by H. F. 
Wilson of the Surrey, Oliver Thorn riding second. Time, 
12 min. 23f sec. Wyndham also ran in the final, but gave 
up after going two miles. There was an exciting finish in 
the second heat, Thorn coming up with a splendid spurt at 
the finish and just making a dead heat of it with Wilson. 

Thb Front Whbbl Hub. 



The "C.T.C. Handbook and Guidb : " 7th Edition, June, 
1883. Compiled and Published by E. R. Shipton* 
Secretary, 139, 140, Fleet Street, E.C. 



The long-looked-for revised edition of the C.T.C. Hand- 
book made ita appearance at the beginning of this month. 
It is neatly got up in the style of its immediate predecessors, 
and bears evident marks of careful editing. Although it 
contains some fifty pages more than last year's edition, it is 
actually less bulky ; the diminution in size being achieved 
by the use of thinner paper. In place of the roughly out- 
lined sketch map of Great Britain which appeared in last 
year s issue, we have now a carefully executed map of the 
United Kingdom with the boundaries and the chief consular 
districts clearly indicated. Besides this, the chief innova- 
tion is a French Tariff (p. 23) which, though fair, does not 
appear to us to offer any special advantages to a member of 
of the club. In Paris and some of the larger towns he 
would certainly save a franc for his bed and twenty-five 
centimes for attendance, but in country places he will save 
nothing by showing his badge or ticket. 

The chief difficulty which the C.T.C. executive has to face 
with reference to this annual publication is its constant, and 
almost inevitable, tendency to increase in size, and, conse- 
quently, to diminish in usefulness. For if the book is to be use- 
ful it must be small and portable, and we feel bound to confess 
that even last year we compromised matters by tearing out 
the leaves we thought we should want to refer to in the course 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



of our tour, and leaving the rest behind. The next important 
point is simplicity of arrangement. Now it appears to us 
that something would be gained in both these respects by 
doing away with the present separate lists of the towns in 
each chief consular district and printing them all together 
in one alphabetical list, and inserting the number or initials 
of the chief consular district after the name of the town. 
By this means a good deal of space would be saved, the 
present index of towns, which occupies twelve pages, would 
be rendered unnecessary, and the reader would be saved the 
trouble of a double reference. At present, in order to 
ascertain particulars respecting a place, one has to look it 
out in the index, and then refer back to the list of towns in 
the chief consular district. A separate list of chief consuls, 
with the boundaries of their districts, is given at pp. 32, 33, 
and this should be retained. Foreign places should also be 
combined into one list instead of six as at present, and 
perhaps at some future time it may be advisable to publish 
this portion as a separate appendix. 

Of the late appearance of the book we will say nothing, 
since this was clearly due to exceptional causes, and ought 
never to occur again. We have it at last, and are glad to 
find it in every respect superior to any of its predecessors.

they have never seen the lower half before.

NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for "Q-azette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on TuesdayB. 



AUTUMN MEETING. 
The Committee have, as stated In another column, 
decided upon an entirely novel experiment, and one 
which only special circumstances would render possible. 
As members are aware the Stamford Bridge Ground was 
engaged for a Saturday in June, and the liability of the 
Club was adopted by a number of guarantees in order to 
render it possible to hold the Annual Bace Meeting 
at the Crystal Palace. As we did not use the ground, 
however, the L. A.C. have very kindly placed the Stamford 
Bridge Ground at our disposal for the 22nd September. 
This was an offer the Committee did not like to throw 
away, but the colossal effort of an ordinary race meeting, 
got up in the dead season when everyone was away, 
showed such a reasonable prospect of a loss after all, that 
they rejected that idea as impossible. They have therefore 
decided on a purely family gathering — a race meeting with- 
out an open event, without any advertising, and practically 
without any printing. The only attempt at an open event being 
a flat race for boys under sixteen introduced by members ; 
the only advertisement being a notice in our columns ; the only 
fruit being the performances. Our members have occasion- 
ally complained that we do too much for outsiders and not 
enough for them, and now they have a good chance. The 
pri7.e3 will not of course be expensive, as for our own 



members that is wholly unnecessary to make a good race, 
whatever may be the case with outsiders. We hope 
that our readers will encourage this novel idea, and that 
a pleasant afternoon will be satisfactorily carried out. 
Entrance to the ground will be only one shilling ; members 
themselves being admitted free on showing their badge. 
The Committee have avoided the appeal to members which 
must have resulted in any attempt at an open meeting, but 
a certain amount of general co-operation will be needed to 
float the venture, which we trust the Club will not be slow 
to give. 

COMMITTEE. 

At a meeting of the Committee held at the " City Arms " 
on Monday, 23rd inst., it was resolved that " the offer of 
the L.A.C. for the free use of Stamford Bridge Grounds on 
22nd September be accepted with thanks; that a race 
meeting be held on that day— not necessarily confined to 
bicycle races — but that all bicycle races be confined to 
members, and all other competitions be confined to members 
and their friends." 

The old Race Committee was requested to make the 
necessary arrangements. E. S. Priob, Hon. Sec. 

EAST GRINSTEAD. 
Next Saturday I think we may count on having more 
settled weather, and a good attendance ought t6 be the result. 
The Croydon Division will leave the Fire Station at 4.15. 
The Blackheath men will run to Pamingham, according to 
the Meet Card. ,R. G. Prancis, District Captain.

RACE MEETING AT STAMFORD BRmOR 
In accordance mth the decision of the Committee there 

will be a race meeting at the above grounds on Saturday, 

22nd September, at 3.30 p.m. The events will be : — 
One Mile Club Handicap. Three prizes : gold, silver, 

and bronze medals. 
Twelve Miles District Scratch. Three prizes: bronze 

medals. 

Half-mile Club Scratch. Two prizes : gold and silver 
medals. 

220 yards Flat Race for boys under 16 years of age, 
introduced by members. Three prizes. 

Entry fee for each of the first three events 2s. 6d, and 
for the Flat Race, Is. The Twelve Miles District Race 
must be ridden on bond fide roadsters ; only those members 
allowed to compete who have attended at least eight meets 
throughout the season. Each District to have three starters. 
Entries close on 17th September. A member of the Com- 
mittee has kindly offered the first prize for the flat race. 

Members will be admitted free on production of Club 
Badge. No tickets will be issued ; the admission to the 
outside world will be by payment of Is. at the gates. 

It will be seen from the foregoing that an entirely new 
departure is being undertaken. The afternoon is intended 
to be a purely Club affair, and every member taking part 
in the races or turning up on the ground will make it more 
so. It will serve all the purposes of a General Meet, without 
the bother of having to get to the destination by riding all 
round London, and will have one great attraction which 
the said General Meet is void of, viz., the presence of fellow- 
members' sisters, cousins, and annts. 

E. S. Price, Hon. Sec. 



SATURDAY RUNS. 



N.E. DisTEicr. 
July 21. The weather last Saturday in this District was 
characterized by the usual thunderstorm, which came 
around just as the men ought to have been meeting at 
Sybom's Corner. The D.C. was late, but Lyne, also slightly 
so, found him waiting at Whip's Cross, and, as the thunder 
was then calm, the two departed for Epping, where the 
District had been summoned by post card, hoping to find a 
few others, but without avail. The ride out was pleasant, 
in spite of one or two showers, and after a somewhat simple 
tea a return journey was effected in daylight. Present : 
G. Y. Lyne and Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 



PAPERCHASE. 
I much regret that the illness of a relative will prevent 
my asking the District to take tea at Whip's Cross on 
Saturday next. Under these circumstances I have arranged 
for the meet to take place at 4.45 at Snaresbrook Station, 



G.KR., at which hour two hares will, I hope, be able to 
start Tea will be at the " White Hart," Abridge, at the 
close of the run. 

Thbo. Godlee, District Captain. 



W. District. 

2l8t July. After an absence of somewhat over three 
weeks I have at last returned to my duties. Certainly I 
expected to find someone at the starting place somewhere 
about 4 o'clock, notwithstanding the postponement of the 
General Meet at Ewell. But no doubt the rain deterred 
anyone from putting in an appearance, at least no one had 
done so as late as 4.30, when I decided to take a short turn 
round by myself for an hour. I went to Eew, and on to 
Brentford, Isleworth, and through the lanes to Ealing, at 
which point, failing to discover any enjojrment, I turned 
homewards, wet and uncomfortable. 

I hope all who can will try to attend the run on Saturday 
next, when I hope to be present myself. For the remainder 
of the season I shall endeavour to be at the starting point 
each Saturday at 3.45, when I hope to be supported by as 
many as can conveniently manage to be present. 

H. C. PiaoTT, District Captain. 



S.E. District. 
Wednesday, 18th July. The first Evening Run was 
favoured with fine weather, and attended by four members, 
who met at 7.30 at the Fire Station. The road taken was 
that by Shirley and West Wickham. The weather and 
surface were perfect, the only drawback being that the 
bathing-pond at Eeston was found to be dry. Owing to 
some unfortunate person having been drowned it had been 
drained to recover his body. The rustics were busy captur- 
ing, and when captured fighting over the fish — ^principally 
carp and eels. One carp that had been caught was said to 
have been no less than two feet round the belly. Present : 
Gericb, S. Herbert, W. C. Oswald, Wigan, and his fox terrier 
" Jii^o." In future the starting time from the Fire Station 
for the Wednesday evening runs will be 7 o'clock. 



Saturday, 21st July. More rain " was the reflection of 
the D.C. as he trudged over to Croydon and was soused in 
one of those showers which we ought to be getting accus- 
tomed to by this time. Only one other member, S. Herbert, 
made his appearance at the start. However, the sun came 
out to cheer our course, and with the wind did its best to dry 
us. On Hayes Common we fell in with Day, and on arriving 
at Down we found quite a cluster of men round the door of 
the " George Inn." The Blackheath Division were in great 
force, the return of their chief, Stephen Smith, from his duties 
connected with the Racing path having evidently given a fresh 
stimulus to their energy. Morgan and Smith turned up from 
Sevenoaks, and F. H. Williacns gave proof of his usual

167 



deyotion to his district, by riding over hte from BeddingtoD. 
The new landlord — new landlords seem to be a speciality 
of the " George Inn " — showed himself anxious to make us 
comfortable, and when he has settled down in his new house 
will, no doubt, be as successful in that line as his predeces- 
sors. It is to be feared that most of the men must have 
felt the effects of the shower which came on soon after we 
dispersed for home. Present : Church, Day, Halliday, 
Alfred Herbert, Arthur Herbert, S. Herbert, Morgan, P. B. 
Smith, S. P. Smith, Whealler, F. H. Williams, two visitors, 
and 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 



N.W. DiOTEIOT. 

It may be that our District, in consequence of the keen 
disappointment experienced as regards the General Meet, 
vainly tried to console themselves with lawn tennis or the 
river ; but yet the afternoon was a fine illustration of shifting 
scenery iu the ethereal world, and those who turned out 
enjoyed their run to Cheshunt very much, putting up at the 
" Green Dragon " and returning home soberly and in order. 
Next Saturday the run is to Ickenham, famous for scenery 
and roses. Tea at the Coach and Horses." Jack Straw's 
Castle," 3.45. GENERAL ORDER— To turn out in 
numbers. A. H. Lown, District Captain.

THE FIFTY MILES CHAMPIONSHIP. 

The Crystal Palace Company probably did a good stroke 
of business in again getting this event run at their grounds, 
for in spite of the very doubtful morning the public were 
present, especially toward the end of the race, in as large 
numbers as they were last year on a day good enough for 
record beating. H. F. Wilson, of the Surrey, a club that 
has turned out several first-rate long distance men, after 
his splendid performance on Thursday, and in the absence 
of our President, and of this year's winner at five miles, 
was made a strong favourite ; and he succeeded in winning, 
though, with a little more judgment on the part of Fry, it 
might have been otherwise. Result: H. F. Wilson, 
Surrey B.C., 1 ; F. R. Fry, Clifton B.C., 2 ; H. R. Rey- 
nolds, O.U.Bi.C. and L.B.C., 3 ; C. D. Vesey, Surrey B.C., 4, 

There were three " foreigners " riding, FVy, Popplewell of 
Ipswich, well known at the Oval, and King, of Salisbury, 
who has appeared at Stamford Bridge and elsewhere, and 
who on this occasion went oflF with the lead, a position 
which he shared with Tacagni, McKinlay, Popplewell, and 
W. W. Scott, late of the Imperial, for the greater part of 
the first half of the journey. In the middle of the 26th 
mile Wilson for the first time took the lead, and doing 
the 27th in 2 min. 53 sec, he got right away and caught 
the last of the opponents in the 30th, after which he 
accompanied the second man until some two miles from 
tbe finish. In the mean time. Fry and Reynolds, who 
had parted company with each other, closed up again, and 
these three rode together with a lap between each of them till 
the 48th mile when Fry, at length following the instructions 



of his trainer who, seeing that he was perfectly fresh, had been 
endeavouring for some time to get him to quicken, made 
his effort, at once left Wilson, and before the finish had 
regained rather more than half the lost lap. Reynolds, 
who seemed rather done, but who lasted wonderfully con- 
sidering that he had had no path training, also got about 
sixty yards away from Wilson, behind whom he finished 
three laps less that distance. Vesey had made elaborate pre- 
parations in the way of clothing to provide against rain, which 
did not come off, and must have been somewhat hampered 
by them, as he was not within three miles of the winner. 

There were eleven starters, five absentees, including 
Keith-Falconer and Oliver Thorn, and the time was 
2hr. 46min. 26fsec. This is the best show we have made 
hitherto in the Championships this year, but we look for 
great things firom Douglas Smith at Taunton. 

QLotxtBpmtmtt. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sir, — As your reporter of the Mile Championship, and 
also in defence of my own property, kindly allow me to 
correct an error which appeared in your last issue. 

GaskelFs last lap was officially given as 40| sec, and it 
was not discovered until some time after that this was the 
time occupied in covering, not the last lap, but the last 
qtutrter-mile, quite a different thing at the CP. 

The regular reporters were informed of the mistake in 
time to rectify it, and I believe that the Gazette shared 
the responsibility of printing it with the Referee only. — 
I am, sir, yours truly, J. S. Whatton. 

[The Field is also in error. — ^Ed.]

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
H. R. Reynolds has won two Time Medals for 50 miles, 
taking one last year in 2hr. 47min. 52^8ec., and one this 
year on a much worse day in 2hr. 48min. 16sec. 

H. F. Wilson should really try 20 miles in the hour, or 
some good performance of that sort. On Thursday, making 
his own pace, he rides 4 miles in llmin. 36sec. ; and on 
the Saturday, on a heavy track, having ridden 25 miles in 
Ihr. 24min., he does 4 miles in llmin. 54sec. ; 5 miles in 
14min. 58sec., again making his own pace. 

0. Thorn, with 15 yards start, took second prize in the 
Open Mile at the La Crosse sports. Several members and 

member's brothers also distinguished themselves. 

• •«««• 

H. Smith took the second prize at Windsor in the Two 
Miles Open with 80 yards, beating Oaskell from scratch. 

The four miles record has been lowered, and considerably, 
too, under decidedly sensational circumstances, at the 
evening race meeting of the Crighton B.C. on Thursday 
last. It will perhaps be remembered that it was at their 
last year's meeting that W. E. Adam cut the three miles 
record, which still stands to his credit. 

H. F. Wilson, .who, by his performances on Thursday and 
Saturday, has now stepped into the front rank, began 
riding at the beginning of last season, and in the autumn 
he won the Fifty Miles Championship of the Surrey B.C. 
His form has been so good or late that he started in the 
fifth heat of the Four Miles Invitation Race on Thursday, 
with the intention of cutting the record, which he succeeded 
in doing to the tune of 8^sec., a performance which is all the 

more marvellous seeing that he had no one to make the pace. 

• A • • • • 

This record, llmin. 37|sec., was only destined to stand 
for half an hour, as in the final heat, Gaskell, after the best 
race that has been seen this year, defeated Liles by a length 
in the splendid time of llmin. 34}sec., the last mile being 
covered in 2min. 45^sec., a decided contrast to Wilson's 
last mile, which took 2min. 55j8ec. Perry and Tliompson 
were the other starters in the final, the former finishing 

20 yards behind Liles and 15 ahead of Thompson. 

• • • • • • 

The preliminary heats for the Crystal Palace Challenge 
Cup were run on Tuesday evening, with the following 
results Heat 1.— F. E. S. Perry, 1 ; H. W. Gaskell, 2 ; 
J. D. Butler, 3 ; F. R. Fry, 4 ; D. Smith. 5. Time, 
47min. ITfsec. Heat 2.— C. E. Liles, 1 ; A. Thompson, 2 ; 
F. L. Adam, 3 ; W. Brown, 4 ; Tacagni, 5. Time, 

47min. 33|sec. The final will be run on Thursday night. 

• • « • ♦ ♦ 

Messrs. Alford and Gibbs, of the Stanhope B.C., have 
recently ridden 227 miles in the 24 hours. 

The Front Wheel Hub. 



NATIONAL CYCLISTS' UNION. 



July 20th, 1883. 
Report of an Executive Meeting held at the Offices on 
Tuesday, 17th July, 188S, 
The Executive considered a proposal from the Executiv'T 
of the Tricycle Union that a Committee of five should ^je 
appointed to meet five representatives of the Tricycle Ur lOn 
to discuss terms of union, and the Executive finally dec ded 



to appoint five of their number to meet the representatives 
of the Tricycle Union. 

The Committee appointed by the Council some time 
since has presented its report as to Professional racing, pro- 
posing an arrangement by which the Union may organise 
Professional Championships. This report will come before 
the next meeting of the Council for discussion. 

As the offices of the Union are now open every day, the 
Executive have decided to extend the time for opening the 
library as follows : — 10 to 5 every weekday except Saturday, 
when the hours will be 10 to 1. This is in place of the 
times formerly fixed. 

The Executive had before them a number of cases in 
which objections had been made to the amateur status of 
individuals, and investigations are being made. 

The following Clubs have joined the Union : — The Clare- 
mont B.C., the Harehills Cycling, the South Norwood 
Cyclists, the Upper Tooting Cyclists, and the Malvern 
Cycling Clubs. Robt. Todd, Hon. Sec. 

QUILLS. 

A. L. Radford did not ride in the Ten Mile Tricycle 

Championship, owing to an unfortunate difference of opinion 

with a dog on the previous day, in which the dog, by his 

biting arguments, came off victorious. 

««•*•« 

It is considered a ^ood joke on Committee to ask the 
N.W. District Captain what the general feeling of his 
District is on such and such a question. As he has'nt seen 
one of them at the hist two meets, it is a difficult question 
to answer. 

Those who are anxious to scale Chingford Hill must take 
their opportunity, as its crown is shortly to be taken off, 
and the dip between the top and the next hill is to be 
filled up, thereby making a long and easy ascent. 

Among much that is interesting the last number of the 

Wheel World contains a portrait of H. R. Reynolds, with 

a short biographical notice.

NOTICE. 

The latest hour for reception of copy for "Gazette" 
is 6.0 p.m. on Tuesdays. 



HOLIDAYS. 

The dull season has set in with its usual severity. The 
attendance at meets is disgracefully thin ; men are either 
away on their holidays, or have important business enj^age- 
ments in some way connected with lawn tennis parties, or 
picnics on the river. The District Captains remain faithful 
among the faithless, some of them, and turn up with clock- 
like regularity to ride on all but solitary runs. There is an 
exodus of officers from town ; the Treasurer retires into 
unknown regions to search for, we hope, to find, the large 
profit the Club is going to make out of a race meeting at 
the Crystal Palace, and to cook that " half-yearly balance 
sheet" which rarely turns up before the autumn. The 
Club begins to work at quarter-man power, and its work 
waits for a temporary revival, or Indian summer in Sep- 
tember, before sinking into quiescence and home-trainers 
till next spring. 

There is one branch, however, of the Club staff" to which 
the dull season means doubled work. Need we say we 
allude to the Editorial department. By the end of July 
the Editor finds the tub growing lamentably empty, its 
bottom only hidden from view by a few choice legacies 
from some of the first ex-Editors of the Club, " A Tour to 
Ripley," " On the Difference between the Front and Hind 
Wheels," and such like novel and interesting subjects. He 



begins to be painfully conscious that he is living from hand 
to mouth, and that his ideas have come to an end just 
when he must continue somehow to fill up two or three 
Gazettbs, with nothing in the tub. Then it is that the 
Editorial genius manifests itself in various ways. Some 
men rush out into a hansom cab, seek the recesses of Holy- 
well Street, and by a lucky purchase of some interesting old 
book, provide themselves with cheap and ample copy for 
the month of August. To others racking their brains for 
matter, there descends as from the skies a long and unex- 
pected tour. Others, with disgraceful cowardice, solve the 
problem of filling four pages of the Gazette, by omitting 
those four pages with great regularity. But the fact re- 
mains that the hoUdays of the Club are the busy tune of 
the Editorial Staff*. 

Members away on tours, and members who have come 
back from many lands with many tales, will ease the Editorial 
labours by sending their travelling experiences to the 
capacious bosom of the Editorial tub. It will be very little 
trouble for them when fresh from their journeyings and in 
robust health, to put down on paper where they went, and 
what they liked and what they didn't like. It will interest 
their fellow members, it will help the Editorial Staff*, and 
it will be pleasant reading for themselves in future years. 

EDITORIAL NOTICE. 
I hope to spend the next three weeks ten miles from any- 
where, and twenty miles from anybody. The Sub-Editor 
will be m charge at No. 35, Eastcheap, and the tub is very

nearly empty. Members will enable the Editor to enjoy

of it, if they will send in as much copy as possible, on as 
many subjects as possible, without delay ; otherwise some-

3En ^ztttoxx^. I 



It is with feelings of the deepest regret that we have to 
record the death of our old friend and late fellow member, 
Ernest Carr, which occurred on Thursday, the 26th inst., 
at his residence at Ouildford. A week previous to the sad 
event he was apparently in vigorous health, but on Thurs. 
day, the 19th inst., he was taken ill with diphtheria, and 
although until almost the last serious consequences were 
not anticipated, the insidious malady suddenly developed 
during Wednesday night : the most skilful and loving 
attention proved unavailing, and the life which had been 
so singularly happy, and which was so full of promise for 
the future, passed away early the following morning. 

Having joined the Club in 1877, our late friend, who 
from the earliest days of bicycling had been an enthusiastic 
rider, was known to most of us, and long shall we remember 
the kindly manner and cheerful disposition which made 
him so general a favourite. Though of a retiring disposition, 
which led him rather to shun notoriety, he was ever ready 
to lend his aid to any enterprise which the L.B.G. embarked 
upon, and often have his musical and social talents added 
greatly to our enjoyment. At Club runs, at which he was 
an assiduous attendant (once taking the Attendance Prize), 
his quaint humour and unassuming kindliness made him a 
great acquisition ; and when some time back he left Croydon 
and went to reside at Guildford he was greatly missed, and 
the good wishes of all his friends followed him to his new 
home and new business. Distance from head-quarters and 
a growing business caused him .quite recently to resign 
membership of our Club, but L.B.C. men ever found a 
kindly and hospitable welcome in his new home. He joined 
the local volunteer force ; and it is pleasing to be able to 
record that, when on Saturday last he was laid to rest in 
the quiet cemetery at Shirley, some forty members of his 
corps journeyed there from Guildford to attest their esteem 
and affection for their late comrade. 

We cannot close this brief reminiscence without, on 
behalf of the entire Club, desiring to convey to his sorrowing 
relatives expressions of our sincerest and most heartfelt 
sympathy. 

RACE MEETING AT STAMFORD BRIDGE. 

In accordance with the decision of the Committee there 
will be a race meeting at the above grounds on Saturday, 
22nd September, at 3.30 p.m. The events will be :— 

One Mile Club Handicap. Three priaes : gold, silver, 
and bronze medals. 



Twelve Miles Dbtrict Scratch. Three prizes: bronze 
medals. 

Half-mile Club Scratch* Two prizes : gold and silver 
medals. 

220 yards Flat Race for boys under 16 years of age, 
introduced by members. Three prizes. 

Entry fee for each of the first three events 2a 6d, and 
for the Flat Race, Is. The Twelve Miles District Race 
must be ridden on bond fide roadsters ; only those members 
allowed to compete who have attended at least eight meete 
throughout the season. Each District to have three starters. 
Entries close on 17th September. A member of the Com- 
mittee has kindly offered the first prize for the flat race. 

Members will be admitted free on production of dub 
Badge. No tickets will be issued ; the admission to the 
outside world will be by payment of Is. at the gates. 

It will be seen firom the foregoing that an entirely new 
departure is being undertaken. Tlie afternoon is intended 
to be a purely Club affair, and every member taking part 
in the races or turning up on the ground will make it more 
so. It will serve all the purposes of a General Meet, without 
the bother of having to get to the destination by riding all 
round London, and will have one great attraction which 
the said General Meet is void of, viz., the presence of fellow- 
members' sisters, cousins, and aunts. 

E. S. Prios^ Hon. Sec. 



SATURDAY RUNS. 



N.W. District. 
A glorious afternoon, sent evidently specially for cycling, 
and yet only two members at Jack Straw's Castle." After 
waiting quarter of an hour they proceeded to Hendon 
Station, where Williams was discovered, and the " District " 
rode to Ickenham Harrow and Pinner, a halt being made 
to go over Ruislip Cliurch, where some good old frescoes 
find themselves. Jackson was awaiting our arrival at the 
" Coach and Horses," and Hindley soon turned up. Tea 
being disposed of in much the usual manner, a stroll was 
taken in the adjoining park, where we were much diverted 
by the many coloured rabbits. The return was safely 
concluded, Hindley going on to Windsor for the night. 
Present : Clarke^ Hindley, Jackson, Williams, four visitors, 
and A. H. LowN, District Captain. 



N.B. There will be some riding to Digswell Hill next 
Saturday to tea ; Tally Ho ! 3.30.

28th July. A fine afternoon, with an absence of the 
usual thunderstorms, was quite a pleasant change. Only 
two men appeared at tbe fire station at tbe time appointed, 
but before reaching Gaterham the party was increased to 
five, and soon after Oodstone tbe B.C., who bad started late, 
overtook the main body. Tbe wind was right behind and, 
with tbe road in fair condition, a moderate pace was kept 
up to East Orinstead. Here Koch, Halliday, and Church, 
with H. R. Reynolds and Wigau were waiting, but universal 
disappointment was felt at the news tbat the O'Reilly was 
unable to attend the run. Wyndbam came in late, and 
brought tbe total up to tbe respectable number of thirteen, 
considering that so many men are away just now. Songs 
and music followed tea, and shortly after nine nearly all 
the party were off, homeward bound. Present : Belfield, 
C. A. Brown, Church, Halliday, S. Herbert, A. Koch (W.),

A TOUR SUBSEQUENT TO THAT OF THE 
"RHINE EIGHT," 

CoNTiNniNa UP Stream betond Bale to the 
Falls at Sohafhausbn. 

(OOMmnJED VROM PAQI 152, AND OONCLUDBD.) 

Tuesday, 24th. After a pleasant run of 40 miles through 
the Forest we emerged upon B&le, in Switzerland, where we 
of course put up at the " Hotel Trois Rois," and had a 
most affable reception. The landlord quickly apprised us 
of the visit of the " twelve " members of the L.B.C. who 
had honoured him the previous year. The impression 
made upon the inmates of the hotel by their formidable 
numbers had evidently been so great that, far from the 
memory of them fading, their very numbers had been 
exaggerated ! We despatched our muitums from here to 
Schafhausen by railway. 

Wednesday, 25th. On we went, and our cheerful 
smiles, etc., to parody the words of Moore, brought us in 
eventual safety, after a rough eleven miles through lovely 
scenery, close to the miniature Falls at Rheinfelden. The 
increased rapidity of the ''Rhein Strom" so far south 
rendered these falls wonderfully picturesque. From Rhein- 
felden to a village named Stein we misssed our way, and 
found ourselves on a bridle path leading through a wood, 
with the swift-flowing Rhine again within earshot. These 
surroundings were far too attractive to be neglected, and 
seemed fully to compensate us for taking a wayward 
route. Approaching the bank and seeing the stream some 
sixty feet below was irresistible ! and it was here that we 
indulged in the fragrant weed and generally encouraged 
our slothful inclinations too long, as we afterwards reflected, 
when we were doing much " dark riding " towards the end 
of the day. Having rejoined the bridle path we found it 
rideable, and after carefully threading our way through 
the woods to a little village we continued to wander onwards 
for many miles until we were eventually in the right direc- 
tion, and reached Stein about three in the afternoon. 

The road beyond Stein, through Great Laufenburg and 
Brug to the point where we crossed the Aar, was for the 
most part along the banks of this river, and as we rode upon 
another cinder path with a bright sun overhead, between 
well wooded hills which lay on both sides of the river, the 
effect was wonderfully imposing, and this, combined with 
the ease of movement, occasioned by the absence of 
muitums and the excellent surface, rendered this part 
of the expedition most enjoyable. Following this road 
for some 14 miles further progress was stopped by our 
arriving abruptly at the water's edge, and here we crossed 
in a barge to the opposite bank, where the road again con- 
tinued. The system of crossing was new to us, the motive 
power being obtained by ingeniously utilising the great 
strength of the stream for the purpose ; this is done by 
means of a line stretching from bank to bank some fifty feet 



above the river ; on this line runs a wheel, to which our barge 
was attached by a rope fixed in the bows, and thus the conse- 
quent resistance of the barge to the current quickly forces 
the wheel along the line and the boat to the opposite bank in 
the most satisfactory manner. After crossing the Aar, we 
followed up stream, forgetting that we were no longer by 
the Rhine, but considerably south of it : three miles of 
narrow road sufficed to remind us of our mistake, and of 
the necessity to avoid further difficulties, by hastily re- 
tracing our wheelings. Passing a small beer-house, the 
sight of us startled the four horses attached to a waggon, 
which immediately left for the country unattended. The 
caretaker, however, was soon upon the scene, and after 
many incomprehensive but unmistakable invectives, at 
which we were compelled to smile, he started in pursuit. 
We came up with him lat^r, which necessitated further 
remarks on his part ; but we were glad to see that all had 
gone well with the animals and that the driver would 
possibly tone down. After crossing a small tributary of 
the Rhine we were confironted by an immense hill, and felt 
inclined to admire it until we came to a sign post which 
intimated the trying fact that our road to Schafhausen lay 
over it, and not straight on as we earnestly hoped, for 
fatigue was fast taking hold of us. This formidable hill, 
called in the district a Eaiserstuhl, though there are two 
or three larger ones not far off, their peculiarity would 
appear to consist in the abrupt manner in which they rise 
out of the plain. These hills are covered with vines. The 
light was fast fading as we reached the summit, and during 
a few short moments of well-earned repose we were able to 
enjoy the full effect produced by the splendour of the 
setting sun upon the plains beneath. Weary wayfarers are 
not allowed much twilight in this southern clime, and on 
this occasion there seemed to be less of that useful com- 
modity than usual, for before we had fairly regained the 
valley on the eastern side of this Eaiserstuhl we were 
plunged in almost total darkness, and matters were 
rendered the more unpieasing because of the uncertainty 
of the direction of our road. The scene and the surround- 
ings recalled some of the famous lines of Gray's El^gy : — 

" Now fades the glixnmerisg landscape o*er the sight, 
And aU the world a solemn stillness holds, 
Save where the beetle wheels his drony flight 
And drowsy tinklings luU the distant folds.'' 

All the natives seemed to have retired with the sun, for we 
had to ride miles before we met anyone of whom we could 
make inquiries, and no guiding light was to be seen any- 
where ; the country seemed literally left " to darkness and 
to us." "Pursuing the even tenor of our way" for some eight 
miles, which under these conditions seemed exceedingly 
long, We at length saw signs of life, and, attracted by the 
distant glimmerings, our pace quickened until we reached 
a comfortable though somewhat primitive inn, where we 
gladly took advantage of the vacant beds and the promise of 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



173 



a speedy supper, to commence with hot soup, chiefly 
consifiting of water and garlic — a fair arrangement for 
nine p.m. ! Drowsiness soon overtook our party after tliis 
meandering ride of fifty-six miles. 

Thursday, 26th. In spite of numerous insects our sleep 
had been profound, and beyond a few marks by which to 
remember them we had suffered little inconvenience from 
the swarms. The inn was surprisingly cheap, only eight 
shillings for the three of us, which modest sum included a 
small breakfast ; this was our cheapest stroke of business. 

We were now on the main road from Bile, on the northern 
bank of the Rhine, with only eleven miles between us and 
Schafhausen. We quitted the village early, with a view 
to visiting the Falls in the afternoon, and to allow time to 
reclaim our multums previously despatched. A short run 
of a couple of miles brought us to the Frontier Station, 
where I suppose we ought to have expressed our willingness 
to answer any questions, and, if need be, to be searched. 
We were no longer pleased with any form of official inter- 
ference, and hence sought to avoid them. The station was 
conveniently situated (for our purpose) halfway down ahill^ 
and approached somewhat unexpectedly. As we drew near 
the lazy-looking Swiss and German officers could be seen 
either dozing, smoking, or calmly basking in the sun. The 
natural feature of the country, so much in our favour, 
formed too great a temptation to us. With "legs over 
handles" we were quickly upon the officers in question 
(the stop bells worked well !), and by the time they had 
sufficiently aroused themselves to challenge us, letting go 
our brakes we sailed away in triumph out of any possible 
difficulty. The officials were temporarily in pursuit, but that 
was apparently soon found useless, and we saw them in the 
distance quietly returning to their post of vantage, or rather 
disadvantage, for catching 'cyclists, perhaps only to be more 
" leary " in future ! Having thus dexterously avoided a 
stoppage, we contmued to wend our way through a well- 
cultivated valley, against a light head wind, until a final 
climb brought us in full view of the town of Schafhausen, 
most picturesquely situated below us, on the river bank. 
The many small foaming waterfalls seen in this part of the 
Rhine to be flowing with still greater rapidity than any we 
had previously witnessed, and the peculiar richness of 
colour in the green clear waters, further added to the 
charm of the scene stretched before us. 

A short descent brought us into the town, where we stayed 
at the " Hotel Krone." In the afternoon we went to 
Neuhausen, where are the wonderful Falls of the Rhine, 
and, descending through some pleasure grounds, arrived on 
the balcony of the "Hotel Rheinfall." This hotel is 
partially constructed in the river, and from the balcony a 
splendid view is obtained of the Falls, some fifty yards 
away. In order to appreciate more thoroughly the excep- 
tional beauty of this immense cascade, a trip in a flat- 
bottomed boat to the central rock which divides the foaming 



waters is suggested, and with some exceptions (among 
the ladies) deemed expedient. The expedition appeared 
dangerous as we approached close under the Falls and 
endeavoured to glide over these boiling waters, but as soon 
as we had got into macintoshes to keep off the spray, we 
were dexterously shot straight for the rock, and quickly 
landed. The romantic-looking pioneer of our boat, so 
picturesquely costumed, was almost a fac-simile of an 
exceptionally handsome-looking Italian secured in a similar 
capacity to steer a gondola up the course at Henley this 
year ; but the latter performance, though skilful and pretty, 
is mere bagatelle, and did not call into play the strength, 
forethought, nerve, and skill essential for this man, whose 
duty it is to land tourists daily upon a rock where the 
currents constantly vary, and where many boats are reputed 
to have been lost ; yet such was the confidence he inspired 
that we all willingly placed ourselves in his hands ! It was 
a peculiar sensation, standing for the first time on a large, 
quivering rock, and to see such an enormous volume of 
water worked into all kinds of wonderful forms, dashing and 
foaming by on both sides of us, and to hear all the hissing 
and splashing as it wildly plunged into the cauldron beneath. 
The height of the unbroken fall is about sixty-two feet. 

We had been well repaid for our visit, and, as we returned 
to the " Hotel Rheinfall," so impressed were we by the 
expedition that we wished we had put up here instead of 
at Schafhausen, for the pleaanre of passing a night close 
under these enchanting Falls. 

Friday, 27th. A careful consideration of our exchequer, 
which in the Mannheim difficulty had been so seriously 
impaired, and of the subsequent steady filtration, reminded 
us only too forcibly and sadly that we could not linger long. 
Leaving Schafhausen for Zurich by rail in the afternoon, 
we arrived in time to have a bathe in the Lake, and so 
much did the lovely weather and surroundings favour our 
enjoyment that we afterwards regarded this plunge as the 
finest we had had during the tour. There was something both 
novel and strange in being able so thoroughly to enjoy a 
tepid swim in full sight of the snow on the surrounding 
mountains. We stayed at the " H6tel de TEp^e," which 
was clean and moderate. 

Saturday, 28th. We made the ascent of the Uetliberg 
mountain by railway from Zurich, but the atmosphere 
proved too dense upon the summit to enable us to be much 
edified by the visit. 

In the afternoon we had to leave Zurich, and started for 
Dieppe, vid B4le and Paris, a long and tedious journey 
occupying twenty-two hours. 

We arrived in Dieppe in time for some bicycle races, for 
which, " of course," we could not enter, as " there was no 
time to obtain the permission of our Captain!" The 
racing, which at once^ commenced on our arrival (we were 
believed to have come on purpose), was between the clubs 
of Paris and Dieppe and the " Clarence " of London. I 



Digitized by 



174 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



remember no charge was made for a front seat, or for 
inspecting and criticising the bicycles, which we did freely. 
The French racing costume seemed to consist of much 
variety of colour, and an elaborate sash and cap, the whole 
being set off by some imposing top-boots ; each competitor 
also wore an additional band of colour as a distinguishing 
mark. The Londoners, in no demonstrative uniform, still 
managed to secure nearly all the prizes they entered for, 
amid much applause from ourselves. Other details were 
most amusing. To any members of the L.6.G. who have 
not yet visited this French watering place I unhesitatingly 
commend Dieppe both for roads, scenery, and general 
amusement, and for economy and comfort the " Hotel de 
Paris." The total distance we covered on the wheels be- 
tween Cologne and Schafhausen was 407 miles in eleven 
days, during which time we became acquainted with scenery 
and roads that we feel assured it would be very difficult to 
surpass. The return railway expenses came to about £4 
each. 

B. M. Obuogrn. 



MORE ABOUT EASTEEN COUNTIES. 

" Another way," (t^ Cookery Books). 

The following sketch of a route ridden in August, 1882, 
and which differs slightly from those described by H. H. S. 
and W. T. H. in Vol. V. No. 26 and VoL VL No. 1, may 
be found useful to members of the two Northern Districts. 

From Stoke Newington, vid Clapton, over the Lea and 
up Copper Mill Lane (to avoid Lea Bridge Road, then 
undergoing a course of preparation for a tramway to carry 
the toiling masses of the N.E. District to the Forest), Clay 
Street, Woodford, Snake's Lane, through Chigwell and 
Abridge to Ongar, by the road well-known to North-Easters, 
past the hospitable halls of Christie, then to the right to 
Chelmsford. In response to my request for dinner at the 
" Saracen's Head," the waiter produced and placed on the 
table, with the air of one performing a conjuring trick, a 
" smoking sirloin," (I believe that is the correct expression) 
with vegetable accompaniments ; indigenous respectively 
to the soils of the Emerald Isle and the sunny land of 
France ; the preparations were wound up with the trium- 
phant remark, " There, sir ! you could'nt have been served 
quicker than that in London 1 " 

From Chelmsford the roads are good through Braintree 
and Halstead to Sudbury, (" Rose and Crown "), recom- 
mended) ; thence good to Bury St. Edmund's, where, in 
addition to the ancient ruins were to be seen modem ones, 
in the shape of the ravages of a fire — quite a Wood Street 
affair on a small scale. After leaving Bury, fresh stones 
had to be traversed for a considerable distance. By care- 
fully following the usual track worn by carts, it was however 
ridden with the exception of one dismount, caused by those 
animals so dear to Bicyclists (when deceased) — ^the common 



domestic sheep. With this exception the surface was good 
to Scole ; thence the direct road leads to Norwich, the 
nature of which is unknown to this deponent. Turn to the 
right for Yarmouth and Lowestoft ; good roads and rather 
pretty scenery lo Bungay, following the course of the 
Waverly River ; stony narrow lanes to Beccles, better to 
Lowestoft. 

The above will be found a pleasanter route than the 
main road by Romford, Chelmsford, and Ipswich ; the 
surroundings are more rustic and many pretty villages are 
passed though. At the time I traversed it, harvesting 
operations were commencing. I was mentally carried back 
to the old days of chivalry by the Suffolk reapers calling 
" Gie us a largesse," as I passed, but my dreams were 
quickly dispelled, for when they found I did not comply 
with their modest request, the modern expletives which 
they hurled after me, savoured more of Whitechapel than 
of die Lists on the Tilting Tard. 

Ntlb. 



lo the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Dear Sir,— I fail entirely to see why " The Front Wheel 
Hub" should consider tricycle path racing as ''a fish out 
of water," and compare the three-wheeler to a growler or 
an omnibus. I have only seen a few tricycle races, and 
most of those were scratch ones, and even in these there 
was racing, and they were not mere processions with the 
exception, perhaps, of the West Kent. At the only handi- 
cap I have seen, viz., at Wisbech, the finish for second 
place was quite as close as any finish at any bicycle race I 
have ever seen, there being not two feet between the second 
and third men, and the fourth man about a foot behind. 
I have no doubt when the N.C.U. handicapper has a good 
field of tricyclists to handicap he will bring about some 
even closer finishes, and no doubt as time goes on we shall 
see the N.C.U. Championships better contested ; but when 
bicycle racing was in its second year I have no doubt some 
of the races then run were as much processions as some of 
the tricycle races have been. Of course I may take a 
prejudiced view of the matter, as I happen to be a 

Tbiotolisi. 



To the Editor qf the London Bicycle Club OazetU. 
Sir, — It was with mingled feelings of interest and regret 
that I took up last week's Gazettb and read in its pages 
the announcement given by the S.E. District Captain of the 
capture and consequent death of my fishy friend the Eeston 
carp. I cannot suffer my carp to. pass from the cool, watery 
scene of its happy and peaceful life without paying a tribute, 
small though it be, to its memory. I feel justified in styling 
it i" the carp " — ^it was indeed a carp among carps 1 pre- 
dominating over its Eestonian compeers, at any rate. 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



175 



in oraft and nviliness (N.B. it was likewise of moderate 
dimensions — ^but more of that anon) ; for although numerous 
attempts have been made to catch it, it has ever heretofore 
evaded the toils — ^for anjrtbing we know it may have been for 
upwards of a hundred years a denizen of the pond as the 
longevity of this fish is notorious, and a century is an era only 
in the life of a carp — ^and now it is no more I " Alas ! poor 
carp!" — my feelings are rapidly becoming too strong for 
control. I must now hasten to the contemplation of a fact 
which has caused me great surprise, viz., that a man of such 
high intellectual capacity and profound erudition as the S.K 
District Captain should deem worthy of particular mention a 
carp measuring only two feet in circumference. Why ! it 
is almost diminutive when compared with the magnitude 
to which many of its species attain. Though conscious 
that I am encroaching unpardonably on your valuable 
space, I should much like to submit to yoxu esteemed 
inspection an extract on the subject from a book I have 
been recently perusing, and which can haixlly fail to 
be considered interesting. Pallas says, that the Volga 
produces carp five feet in length." "In 1711," says 
Eleazar Block, "a carp was caught near Frankfort, on 
the Oder, which was more than nine feet long, and 
three round, and which weighed seventy pounds." In 
the lake of Zug, in Switzerland, one was taken which 
weighed ninety pounds. Monsieur Pesson-Maisoneuve 
says, " In France they reach ten or twelve pounds ; in 
Germany, they become monstrous. They are taken in 
Pomerania thirty or forty pounds in weight, and in Prussia 
fifty pounds is a common size." Now to prove my 
theory of the longevity of carp. It is said, "that the big 
old carp which are to be found in the fosse of the chateau 
at Fontainebleau, in France, were placed there in the time 
of Francis I." Buffon speaks of carp in the fosse of Pon- 
chartrian " which were one hundred and fifty years old, and 
still possessed all the vivacity and agility of ordinary fish.'' 
But I think the evidence is sufficiently strong now, without 
further instances being cited. Should any be inclined to 
doubt the veracity of these statements, I can only say that 
I think if there be one authority reliable above all others 
in matters relating to fish it is that of the watersprite 

Umdinb. 



THE 100 MILES RECORD BEATEN ON THE 
CRYSTAL PALACE TRACK. 

On Friday last, the 27th, Douglas Smith (London Bicycle 
Club), and F. R. Fry (Clifton Bicycle Club), made up their 
minds to try and cut the existing record for 100 miles ; 
so they met at the Crystal Palace, and a start was made at 
twenty-three minutes to ten, the course being in splendid 
order. In the end the previous records, both amateur and 
professional, (6 hours 29 min. 57 sec), were lowered by 
39 min. 5f sec. by F. R. Fry, who rode in a most deter- 



mined and plucky manner, as he never stopped for an 
instant after stt^ting, and kept up a good round average 
of 3 min. 30 sec. per mile till the end, seeming none the 
worse for his long ride. Smith kept with him till 
41} miles had been run and then stopped for 8 min., 
and remounting only did another four miles and then re- 
tired, leaving Fry to finish alone. C. D. Vesey, J. D. 
Butler, and F. L. Adam kindly helped to make the pace 
throughout. 

Appended are the times for each mile over fifty, all of

Sporting Life. 



N.E. DISTRICT SATURDAY RUN. 
28th July. The Annual Paperchase was fixed for last 
Saturday, and while possessing some of the eccentricities of 
former years, it as usual resulted in a good muster at tea 
time. The D.C. had been unable to follow the precedent 
of asking the District to Whip's Cross, and accordingly the 
muster was at Snaresbrook Station. Weeks arrived with 
the bags, but announced that the D.C, who was to have 
accompanied him, had wired his intention of being late ; so 
Lloyd was presssed into the service, and the pack reduced 
to the Captain, Jolly, Sharman, Sewell, and Forster. 
The original route was right up the Woodford road, 
no scent being laid for the first mile or so, then various 
digressions to left and right, and by Monkham's Lane 
to Buckhurst Hill Jolly left at George Lane, as he 
thought he knew a better way to Abridge than the 
hares appeared to have found. Sharman also improved 
upon the papered route. The Captain and Sewell lost them, 



Digitized by 



Google 











176 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 






found again, went on, down Buckhurst Hill, up Trap's Hill, 
separated and met again, made straight for They don and 
lost again, and ultimately ran in just a little in advance of 
the hares. Accidents will happen. Forster, after vainly 
searching the vicinity of Buckhurst Hill, gave up in despair, 
and retired down Snake's Lane, meaning to go straight to 
Abridge ; but in the meantime the D.C., twenty minutes 
late, had been searching for scent everywhere within a mile 
of the starting point, likely and unlikely, and having at last 
been round by Woodford Bridge, was coming up Snake's 
Lane, with a view to further enquiries ; he had already spent 
about an hour. Forster was induced willingly to turn back, 
and the D. C. was delighted to hear there really was some 
scent, and repairing to Buckhurst Hill the pair seriously, 
but by no means slowly, worked out every inch of the 
run wnich was capitally laid and a very good line.^ After 
leaving Chigwell Lane it proceeded up Golding's Hill, 
and then an amusing and chasing run or walk through 
the forest to above Theydon, where the hares had 
waited ever so long in vain, and so in to tea just as it was 
getting dark. Lyne had walked over, and there was a 
capital muster besides, so sdgs and the piano cheered a 
pleasant hour when the return journey was comfortably 
effected. Present : A. W. 'Barrett, E. H. Barrett, A. 0. 
Forster, G. H. Gibbs, F. JoUr, A. Lloyd, R. M. Long, 0. Y. 
Lyne, C. Newman (N.W.), E. D. Sewell, C. E. Sharman, 
Harold Smith, and E. A. W^ks ; also F. W. Barrett, A. P. 
Barrett, and P. T. Letchford^visitors), and
