QUILLS. . 

" Aristotle and Pliny deliyer it that the porpentine casteth off hii qnillB 
when he would attack, or defend MmBelf, but DioBOorides affizmeth it a 
point of foUy to belieye it."— Sir T. Bbownb. 

R. M. Gruggen, L. Potter, and Zulu are having a very 

jolly time in Kance with their wheels. They -started from 

Dieppe to Rouen, and then on to Troaville ; from there 

they will probably run to Paris and then home. 

• • • ♦ • 

They report the weather excellent, roads k^*^ ;rj»^^^J wine 
and rustics weak and simple, and the casinos ''cuic'* 

Les velocipMes excite the greatest wonderment, and Zulu 
was requested by an imposing Frenchman to show the mul- 
titudes at Honfleur how they were mounted. 

Melons form the staple food, and flies trouble. 

• *•«•• 

Potter's spill at Rouen was well timed — it being in the 
centre of the town — and provoked loud applause, but his 
pleasant smile not only reassured his companions, but also 
the too observant throng who had quickly gathered round. 

It was droll to see a tiny Frenchy offering to mount the 
Colossus. 

The hares in the N.E. paperchase took up a good position 
on the summit of a deep gorge in Epping Forest, just near 
enough to greet the hounds on their arrival at the opposite 
side with a derisive shout, and yet, owing to the nature of 
the ground, far enough o9 to enable them to get away 

with a good start.

Monday, upon which occasion, under favourable conditions, 

they hope to complete the list of worthy medallists ! Will 

any members kindly volunteer to be at the respective

Free translation for the benefit of A. W. Oman : — 

** Every carp has ita day." 

Jus^as we are going to press we have the following from 
Gruggen : — 

" Potter, Barker, and self, have been doing a circular run 
in Normandy, through some of the best scenery in this 
lovely province, which comprised a run from Dieppe to 
Rouen (40 miles), 4l hours, first day ; thence from Koueu 
to Honfleur, along tne left bank of the Seine (42 miles), 
upon the second day ; again from Honfleur to Trouville by 
the route t.ermed the Comiche road of Prance, on account 
of the beauty of the scenery, upon the morning of our third 
riding day ; whence again we proceed to Caen, and thence 
by boat to Havre, the tour terminating with a ride along 
the coast from Havre by Etretat to Dieppe."

NOTICE.

THE MEMBER'S BROTHER. 

Whether looked at in the light of a probable attendant 
at club runs, a probable subscriber to the club funds, or a 
probable rival in the road trial, the member's brother always 
baSj and always will be an object of curiosity and interest 
to the officials and well wishers of the Club. There is, of 
course, a variety of the species, but we shall treat them 
under two heads only — the rider and non-rider. 

The non-rider, perhaps, has nothing to do with us, but as 
he supports us on so many occasions, we can hardly let him 
go without a word. He comes to the dances and various 
entertainments got up by the Club, and is also useful at a 
race meeting to take charge of anyone his brother may take 
down. As, however, he is not likely at any time to join our 
ranks, we will pass him by and come at once to the rider. 
The member's brother, as considered in the light of a pos- 
sible member, the enthusiastic younger brother who is only 
waiting until he shall be old enough to join. D.C's are 
always anxious for him to attend runs, for they know that 
in many cases it means another one to the score, the brother 
often turning out to accompany him when he would not 
otherwise have done so. He is well up in all bicycling 
literature, knows the Blue Book and Gazette off by heart, 
and having the precepts of the L.B.C. instilled into his 
mind early in bis bicycling career, bids fair on joining our . 



ranks to rise to an official position. Look for instance at 
the present officers; four of whom have been member's 
brothers at one time. 

To members whose one topic of conversation is their 
machine or the Bath Trial, (and there are such men) he is 
always a welcome sight, for now they are sure of a hearing 
the while they unfold the (to everyone else) hidden beauties 
of their particular bicycle : and of sympathy as they recount 
the circumstances which just prevented them winning the 
enamel, and he even takes down the fact that there was a 
strong head-wind in their particular year. 

He has a steadying effect on the conduct of his brother — 
the member, and urges him to greater deeds ; for the latter 
knows that whatever he may do will be noted and if possible 
improved upon. This has often been exemplified in the 
Bath Trial. Take the first one and it will be found that; 
three prominent members, who were then member's brother's, 
and whose brothers won medals that year, have since gone' 
and done likewise. ' 

We were a member's brother once, and look back on that 
period of our bicycling infancy (before the Bath Trial had 
been invented) with pleasure not unmixed with regret when 
we remember the amount of innocent pleasure we derived 
from the mere fact that our brother was a member oi 
the L.B.C. 

Before leaving the member's brother, we cannot help re^ 
marking the number of cases in which the member subsides 
on his brother joining. This is a pity, especially whei 
week by week we see what the brothers in the Club do whei 



Digitized by 



Google 



178 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



associated together for tours, etc. Something ought to be 
done to wake these gentlemen up. We know a few of them 
are engaged and some married ; but unless the remainder 
are frightened of being eclipsed by their younger and more 
energetic brothers, we cannot account for them other than 
that they are taking the former course, in which case we 
offer them Punch's advice— don't !

to attend.

Harrow.

ride together to Harrow.

LAWN TENNIS. 

The third round ended in victory for Reynolds, Naylor, 
and Sharp, who defeated Beck, Elgood, and Francis respec- 
tively. Naylor, having drawn the bye, awaits the result of 
the contest between the other two. Reynolds will un- 
doubtedly prove the winner, barring accidents, and the 
final ought to be a splendid match as the two warmest 
members in the Club will then be face to face. 

Possibly this tie will be played off at the meeting at 
Stamford Bridge in September. H. N. Shabp. 

AN EPITAPH. 
Members of the North- West District and others who 
have visited Shenley know the countrified little church 
soon after our arrival, though not before causing a dearth 
with wooden belfry, which stands just at the foot of the 



hill on which the village stands ; we pass it in going that 
way to St. Albans, but probably few have taken the trouble 
to dismount and pay a visit to its interior. As a matter of 
fact it is scarcely worth while to do so, for the building is 
not specially interesting, either architecturally or archsao- 
logically, but just outside the south porch is the following 
quaint epitaph, certainly a curiosity in its way : — 
" Silent in dust lies mouldering here 

A Parish Clerk of voice moet dew ; 

None Joseph Rogers could excell 

In laying bricks or singing weU ; 

Though snapped his line, laid, by his rod. 

We build for him our hopes in Grod, — 

The Saviour G^od.— that He wm raise 

Again that voice to sing His praite 

In temple blest which slways stands, 

The Church of God not made with hands.'* 

The neat way in which the various accomplishments of 
the deceased are brought in does great credit to the in- 
genuity of the author of the poem.

UNOFFICIAL MEET IN THE N.W. 
"Where do you think you are going to ? " was the question 
put by several inquisitive pedestrians ; as starting rather late 
the roads seemed extra uneven as far as " Tally Ho ! " 
But it was not in vain that high pressure was put on, for at 
the ** Corner " three buttresses of the District were discovered 
pawing the ground (t.^., standing by their machines), and 
we were soon en route for Digswell Hill, some remarkable 
turns of speed being displayed by the three. As is usual 
at this time of the year the inn was in the temporary 
possession of vacillating beanfeasters, but fortunately for the 
peace and quiet of our evening meal took their departure 
of milk. During tea Langmore arrived, and having had the 
usual smoke we started on our different ways ; Neville and 
Williams riding on to St. Neots, and the rest returning 
home, making a long halt at Whetstone Bridge (so called on 
account of the many knife marks on its parapets), which 
spans the Lea. Lamps were lighted at Barnet, and the 
return accomplished by 10 o'clock. Present: Langmore, 
Neville, Price, W. J. Williams, and 

A. H. LowN, District Captain. 

Next Saturday the run is a circular one to Colney Hatch, 
a very pretty ride. Jack Straw's, 3.45 ; Tally Ho ! 
Corner, 4.25.

THE TWENTY-FIVE MILES AMATEUE 
CHAMPIONSHIP. 
This contest, the last of the Championships, was decided 
on Thursday last, on the Taunton track Ten riders fjEtced 
the starter, the most prominent absentees being Chambers, 
Illston, Palmer, Fry, and Douglas Smitk The running for 
the firstfive miles was made by King andBrock, andatfourteen 
miles Batten, the Edinburgh representative, fell and retired. 
At twenty miles Brock and Robinson were leading, with the 
rest well up, but within a mile of home, Liles came through 
with a rush, and although challenged by Sutton and West, 
won in good style by eight yards from Sutton, of the Edg- 
baston Harriers, West close up. King being fourth and 
Gaskell fifth. Time 1 hour 22 min. 432 sec. Besides those 
mentioned, C. D. Vesey and W. A. Q. Walter also 
competed. 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
The N.C.U. Championship honours have been well dis- 
tributed this year ; the twenty-five miles, the last of the 
series, having fallen to C. E. Liles, who reports himself as 
fit as ever. Thus the four bicycle contests have been 
secured by difiierent riders, three of whom hail from London, 
the metropolis having shown up better than was expected 
in the early part of the season. 

Oliver Thorn, who seems to have a great partiality for 
second prizes, secured yet another in the Open Mile Handi- 
cap of the Hammersmith B. and T.C. meeting on Thursday 
last. Harold Smith also started in the final, but could not 
get a place. The other L.B.C. competitors were J. D. 
Butler, L. B. Reynolds, and W. Wyndham. 

* • m ♦ « • 

Mr. Terry, who fhas constructed a tricycle to carry him 
by land and water, successfully crossed the channel, and 
continued his journey to Paris. The boat is said to have 
shipped a lot of water, which would scarcely improve the 
going of the wheels. The most practical use of the machine 
would be best shown by a)hare in a paperchase. 

• ••••• 

T. R. Marriott's twenty-four hours tricycle record has 
been beaten by three miles by A. Bird, of the Speedwell 
B.C., in a contest amongst the tricycling division of his 
club. The start was at Birmingham, and the route lay 
through Coventry, Dunchurch, Weedon, Northampton, and 
Bedford, to Cambridge and back, in all 218 miles, which 
was covered in 23^ hours, the winner proceeding back for 
3^ milea 

Our colours were conspicuously to the fore on Saturday, 
at Stamford Bridge, at the annual meeting of the Railway 
Clearing House, thanks to the fine riding of Oliver Thorn, 
who secured the second prize in the Four Miles Scratch 
Race, being only beaten by three yards, after a splendid 



race, by Liles. J. D. Butler was third, two yards behind 
Thorn. 

« ♦ ♦ ♦ • ♦ • 

In the Open Mile Handicap, also, Bath Oliver, who seems 
to be carrying all before him since he has been mounted on 
his new " Rucker," obtained premier honours, J. D. Butler 
again attending upon him and securing the second prize.

The numerical strength of the C.T.C. has now very nearly 
reached the desired 10,000, the latest approximate total 
being 9,931. 

The Fifty Miles Professional International Bicycle 
Championship Race was won by Wood, of Leicester, in 
the good time of 2 hours 48 minutes 10 seconds, at the 
Aylest6n Ground, Leicester, on Saturday. 

The Fkont Wheel Hub. 



AN EVIL EYE. 

Is there in these enlightened days such a thing as an 
"evil eye?'' A short time back, whilst taking a quiet 
evening spin near Waltham Abbey, I was accosted by a 
little ragged boy who requested the loan of a copper ; the 
wished-for money was, however, not forthcoming, and the 
run was continued until a dismount was necessitated by a 
steep and stony descent, when again the cry of the tattered 
urchin made itself heard. "A good job you got oflF, for 
that 'ill, sir, a gem'man like you last week on a bicycle 
tried to ride down, sir, and although I tell'd 'im to get oflF, 
he took no notice, and I see 'im fall off and break 'is neck, 
and he was took 'ome dead on a shutter, sir." Then that 
well ventilated youth became confidential, and enquired 
which road I had taken. I gratified his curiosity; the 
road was immediately recognised, and I was told how a 
fortnight before, after some very heavy rains, the road had 
been flooded, and as he was driving a cart he saw a bicyclist 
in front trying to ride through it, and how, when he got to 
the middle where the water was up to the hub, he fell 
down "with 'is werlocepede on 'im, sir, and he was 
drownded ; and I got five bob for telling the police about 
'is dead body." 

I was at the bottom of the hill ; it was lonely — very 
lonely, and although the boy was of but tender years and 
small, I felt happy when he was out of sight (he got the 
coppers), and I thought I might reach home alive if 
careful; — ^five minutes later my machine broke at the 
neck. Feen.

In the course of a prowl on Monday, C. Y. Lyne came 
across an announcement that a swimming bath had been 
opened at Hoddesdon, the water supply being described as 
coming warm from the R^rigerator ! Such a curiosity 
should certainly be visited by the N.E. 

Surgical. — Amongst the names of those recently admit- 
ted as members to the Royal College of Surgeons, England, 
was that of Mr. A. Ogier Ward, M. A., M.B., of the L.B.C.,

The Cyclist. 

Bank Holiday excursionist to member. — "Sittin' up so 

'igh, you must be wery dry." 

• • ♦ m • • 

On Saturday at four o'clock Weeks and Hindley of Ours 
met E. W. Lumley of the Chiswick, Green of the Temple, 
and several others, at Hounslow, and rode through Maiden- 
head, and thence by a circuitous but very pleasant route 
to Henley. From Henley the party went to Benson vid Net- 
tlebed Hill, and put up at the " Crown " at Benson, where 
a good supper awaited us. In the morning most of the party 
indulged in a swim, and after breakfast the majority took 
to boating, while Weeks and Lumley angled. As the dinner 
consisted of ducks and not fish, it was surmised that sport 
was slow. Those who boated rowed up to Dorchester, and 
walked across the old Roman encampment to the very 
ciirious old church at Dorchester, which well repays a visit. 
In the evening some walked to Eirelme (Ewelme), where 
there is also a fine old church and churchyard. It was 
noted that the biographies of the departed ones were writ 
large on the tombstones, some of which have very quaint 
and perhaps ridiculous inscriptions. On the Monday morn- 
ing bathing was again enjoyed. About eleven the brothers 
Bashall, who had tooled their "bus" down from town 
early on Saturday, started for Land's End, and soon after 
Weeks and Hindley rode up to Nettlebed, and were re- 
galed with tennis and lunch by friends of the former. At 
two they were to have met Alison and Eoch, who had ridden 
down to Benson the previous morning ; but as it was 2.45 
before they got to the rendezvous the Meet did not come 



off. What became of Alison and Eoch is shrouded in 
mystery, but Weeks and Hindley had a very enjoyable run 
home by way of Henley, Marlow, Burnham, Stoke, and 
Axbridge. 

• ••••• 

A quiet Bank HoUday in the Essex lanes was enjoyed by 
S. G. Hough, Eelsey, and Lloyd. Dunmow was visited, 
and the notion that the natives have of a splendid bathing 
place was not properly appreciated. The outward journey 
was made md Epping, Moreton, and Hatfield Broad Oak, 
and the return through Fifield and Ongar. The former 
route, though longer, is recommended, as the surface is 
much smoother. 

I shall make use of some strong terms presently, Mr. 
Editor. Here is a Club of about 230 members, a large 
number of whom have spent the last few days on the wheel, 
either in taking smill tours or long rides, and yet there is 
hardly a single on 9 who will volunteer the slightest in- 
formation or ezperi3nce he has gained for the benefit of his 

fellow members. 

• «*•«« 

"I hav'nt done anything worth speaking of," says So- 
and-so when you buttonhole him in the City with the 
object of squeezing something out of him. Further enquiries 
elicit a day's ride of about sixty miles, pleasant meetings 
with other members, and plenty of useful information, none 

of which goes any further. 

«••**• 

Just a few lines containing an epitome of the tour or the 

day's ride is sufficient. Paragraphs are a great deal more 

readable than long yarns, and they can be written in a few 

minutes, without the slightest trouble. Poboufine. 



Bicycles in the Alps. — ** S.," a lady, vnrites from 
Chamounix : — "Travellers in Switzerland are now exposed 
to a new danger. Crossing the T§te Noire yesterday on a 
mule, I met a yonug gentleman with a bicycle. My guide, 
muttering that the^e machines always frightened the uiules, 
called peremptorily to him to take it out of the way. This 
the owner endeavoured to do by leaning it against the 
bank, but, unfortunately, only caused the bright spokes of 
the wheels to flash more alarmingly in the sunlight, and 
my terrified mule turned violently round and fairl> ran 
away. As we haiTjiist passed the T6te Noire, travellers 
familiar with the route will appreciate that the precipice at 
that point did not lessen the uneasiness of the moment. 
This incident may perhaps moderate the enthusiasm with 
which one of your, correspondents has lately recommended 
these machines ior Continental tourists." — From the 
Times, 7th August, 1883.

Gazette' 



THE MEMBER'S SISTER 
Our members and their brothers have been discussed in 
these pages, but hitherto no article has been devoted to the 
consideration of the Member's Sister. From no lack of 
courtesy, or from want of appreciation of the importance 
of the subject has this notice been deferred, but rather may 
we claim to have held it back as the bonne baucke wherewith 
to stimulate the jaded appetites of our readers. With what 
zest will they be induced by its title to read our editorial, 
and with what conviction of our incapacity will they lay it 
down ! The dull hard facts of Club Teas and Club Meets 
may with serenity be grappled with, but justice to the 
Member's Sister can only be done by one who combines 
technical knowledge of wheel work and Club life with 
Byronic fervour and the melody of Tom Moore. By saying 
at once that we are no such Bicycular Bard, and nought 
but ''a matter-of-fact young man," we hope to disarm 
criticism, and for our part engage to confine ourselves to an 
earnest enquiry into the Member's Sister — what she has 
done for the Club, and what influence she exerts. If, over- 
mastered by our theme, we should for a moment, with Mr. 
Silas Wegg, " bust vith poetry," we promise to put on the 
break as promptly as can be, and crave the indulgence of 
our readera 

The student will note that the present attitude of the 



ladies differs greatly from that which they assumed a few 
years back towards the pioneers of our sport. The riders 
then was " an egotist," and his beloved wheel was " a selfish 
thing." In vain did he discourse sweetly of hill and dale, 
of breezy upland and honeysuckle laden hedges. By no 
such means could he arouse sympathy sufficient to induce 
his hearer to pardon him for being an absentee. Then 
came the advent of the Three-wheeler, shortly to be followed 
by the Sociable. (Blessed be he who first discovered this ! 
May he be ever venerated, even as Sir Walter Raleigh for 
his precious tobacco leaf!) These gave the means, till then 
wanting, of enabling our sisters to judge for themselves of 
the pleasures of the wheel, and that their verdict is favour- 
able one cannot doubt who notes their present attitude to 
the sport which is becoming theirs as much as our own. 
Since an axiom in diplomacy, — that no undertaking can 
prosper unless the ladies are on its side — ^may be applied 
equally to the world of sport, we may venture to hope that 
the future of the Bicycle, and more particularly of the 
L.B.C., is well assured. Nowhere, not even among our 
members, have we found such care for the well-being of the 
Club, and such intimate knowledge of its doings in the 
past as have been shown by some of our fair critics — the 
Members' Sisters. Though we ourselves are as much per- 
suaded of the existence of this class, and own that its traits 
are as well marked as are those of the " Members' Brothers," 
who a year or so back were carefully analysed and classified 
by a learned writer in these pages, still we know that among 
us are some who think otherwise. To them the Member's 



Digitized by 



Google 



182 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Sister seems but an idle phantasy conjured up by the lively 
imagination of a hard up writer. These are such men as 
would doubt the existence of the Income Tax and the 
Equator, and may be conveniently crushed by reference to 
the esprit de corps which induced our sisters to present us 
with the Ladies' Challenge Gup, to write from the earliest 
days of this piiblication a series of tales and articles for our 
amusement and instruction, and to encourage all our races 
and festivities by their welcome presence. This is what 
they have done. Now, and at all times, they show in a 
thousand unostentatious ways that the L.B.G. is not for- 
gotten by them, and though no one would dislike more 
heartily than the writer to see the Sociable ousting the 
Bicycle from Club Meets, yet we shall think our decadence, 
which the croakers are alwajns professing to see in the 
heavens, really has come upon us when the Member's Sister 
and the Member's Brother lose their interest in the 
Member's Club. 



GENERAL MEET AT HARROW ON AUGUST 18th. 

Times and places of starting for the Districts are to be 
found in last Gazette. It is hoped the full moon will 
tempt men to muster well at the " King's Head," (top 
of the hill). 

For the District Captains, 

ASHLBT W. BaRRBTT. 



RACE MEETING AT STAMFORD BRIDGR 
In accordance with the decision of the Committee there 

will be a race meeting at the above grounds on Saturday, 

22ud September, at 3.30 p.m. The events will be :— 
One Mile Club Handicap. Three prizes : gold, silver, 

and bronze medals. 
Twelve Miles District Scratch. Three prizes: bronze 

medals. 

Half-mile Club Scratch. Two prizes : gold and silver 
medals. 

220 yards Flat Race for boys under 16 years of age, 
introduced by members. Three prizes. 

Entry fee for each of the first three events 2s. 6d, and 
for the Flat Race, Is. The Twelve Miles District Race 
must be ridden on band fide roadsters ; only those members 
allowed to compete who have attended at least eight meets 
throughout the season. Each District to have three starters. 
Entries close on 17th September. A member of the Com- 
mittee has kindly offered the first prize for the flat race. 

Members will be admitted free on production of Club 
Badge. No tickets will be issued ; the admission to the 
outside world will be by payment of Is. at the gates. 

It will be seen from the foregoing that an entirely new 
departure is being undertaken. The afternoon is intended 
to be a purely Club affair, and every member taking part 
in the races or turning up on the ground will make it more 



so. It will serve all the purposes of a General Meet, without 
the bother of having to get to the destination by riding all 
round London, and will have one great attraction which 
the said General Meet is void of, viz., the presence of fellow- 
members' sisters, cousins, and aunts. 

E. S. PaiOE, Hon. Sec. 



NOTICE— RACE MEETING AT STAMFORD BRIDGE 
ON SEPTEMBER 22hd. 
With the consent of the Race Committee, a game of La 
Crosse, lasting 20 minutes, will be introduced into the pro- 
gramme. The players will consist mainly of members and 
members' brothers. 

AsHLET W. Barbett, Captain.

COPYRIGHT. 
Our old correspondent " Goethe," whose name is some- 
what rare in our pages just now, writes indignantly with 
reference to our leading article last week, and refers us to 
our issue of July 30tb, 1880, which it is true commences 
with an article signed by him with the same title as outs 
of last week. He claims copyright, for did he not invent, 
discover, classify, and name a Member's Brother " three 
years ago ? We admit it all, or partly, but our correspondent 
must believe that a good deal happens in three years, and 
it may even be that our editorial omnipotence had passed 
over the previous record. — [Edp.]

SATURDAY RUNS.' 



N.W. District. 
The run to Colney Heath (not Colney Hatch, as in last 
week's) was conspicuous by the smallness of the attendance, 
but the scenery compensated somewhat. There is a stream 
wherein Julius C»sar is said to have bathed, but anent this 
there are many sceptics. More success attended our efforts 
to obtain cocoanuts, placed on sticks outside a gipsy wig- 
wam, than to get a good tea, which latter was of the most 
primitive description. Present : Hindley, Langmore, 
Williams, and A. H. LowK, District Captain. 



S.W. DKTRicrr. 
Last Saturday being one of the finest days of the year, I 
certainly expected to find one member of our small district 
at Kingston Station, but no such luck. I left office earlier 
than usual, with the sole object of supporting my district — 
our programme being to have a picnic, but which apparently 
fell to the ground, as no one had provided it. I waited till 
4.30 p.m., at which hour I set forth to pursue my weary 
way, the route selected being via Surbiton Hill, Ewell, 
Chessington, and Hook, back to the former place, arriving 
home to tea, having ridden about ten miles. This surely 
ought to count an attendance on my side. Next Saturday 
I hope our district will show a formidable muster at 
Kingston. Member present : H. L. Btak. 

S.E. District. 
August lltL No doubt, had the District at large not 
been under the impression that their D.C. was still in some 
distant part of Cornwall, they would have flocked out to 
Walton on Saturday in their hundreds. As it was, on 

■ 



arriving at the "Swan'' somewhat unexpectedly, he was 
greeted by a small body of faithful few who, under the 
leadership of J. C. Oswald, had found their way over from 
Croydon. With the timely assistance of Church, who rode 
over from Blackheath by himself, the S.E. party assumed the 
gigantic proportion of six, who in company with the Captain 
and four of the West sat down to tea together. Weeks gave 
us a look in while we were discussing a somewhat meagre 
fare. With a larger attendance considerable difficulty as 
to supply and demand might have arisen in the Commissariat 
department. After a riverside pipe, a start homewards 
was made about eight, the South-East being favoured 
with the company of the Captain, who with that indomitable 
energy which is so well known, had determined to ride home 
to Essex by Croydon and Woolwich. In Esher lamps were 
lighted, and we had a very pleasant run homewards through 
lanes, which took us by way of Claygate and Hook, under 
the light of a crescent moon. On nearing Croydon we fell 
in with Andrew Potter and a friend on a double, and on 
reaching Duppas Hill the whole party was hospitably 
entertained by the Oswalds. After admiring the clock, 
which is now in position, we received a warning from its 
chimes that it was time for those living in outlying regions 
to be off, and the party accordingly separated about 
ten o'clock. Present: (S.E.) Church, Halliday, J. C. 
Oswald, W. C. Oswald, S. E. WheaUer, (N.E.) A. W. Barrett 
(Captain), R A. Weeks, and 

B. G. F&AHds District Captain. 



W. District. 

Saturday, 11th. On arriving at the District headquarters 
at 3.45, the robust and pleasing form of the Captain greeted 
my appearance. In response to a late invitation from my- 



Digitized by 



184 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



self to pay us one of those periodical visits which, being 
few and far between, are the more enjoyable when we are 
favoured with them, our Captain, in his usual beneficent 
style, had replied l)y being present in person instead of only 
in nama On behalf of the District, I must offer to him 
every possible apology for the very poor welcome given him, 
and must plead as an excuse that had his intended visit 
been more generally known by the members I am sure that 
our ranks would have been greatly swelled by aU who are 
only too anxious to support our worthy chief on every 
possible occasion. At 4 o'clock a start was effected, having 
been joined by Leeds and Alison. Choosing the Ealing 
lanes in preference to the Richmond road, we proceeded 
to Isleworth and Twickenham, thence past the picturesque 
residence styled Pope's Villa (to be changed to 
"Virtue's Abode" in honour of Truth), occupied by a 
worthy (?) M.P., and the estate of the late Countess 
Waldegrave, which has seen its last days as a private 
residence, adding yet another stone to the mighty heap of 
ruins created by one of nature's antagonists — the Speculator. 
Onward through Teddington to Bushey Park, the ride 
through which one admires the more as being unique 
amongst the public parks through which bicyclists are 
allowed. Turning to the right, after leaving this grand 
old avenue, with its historical associations of Wolsey, we 
passed on to and over Moulsey Bridge, a perfect structure of 
cheapness and ugliness combined, and on through East 
Moulsey and Apps Court. From here the pace, which had 
been good, gets better, being led by our worthy Captain, 
until the climax was reached at the summit of the little 
hillock entering Walton, where fortunately a sharp turn to 
the right for the " Swan " caused the pace to be slackened, 
and allowed breathing time before dismounting at our 
destination. Being the first to arrive, and the time only 
5.20, the Captain with a malicious look proposes that we 
should adjourn to that most ancient and soul-inspiring 
"Gruggen's remains;" I mean the "Grotto," situate in 
Oatlands Park. AH of us at once agree to the proposal. 
By the way, each in turn mentions the name of some ancient 
ruin — ^Tintern, Eenilworth, and even the Great Fingal's 
Gavd itself, meeting however with but scant interest in 
anticipation of the wonders to be disclosed at the far-famed 
" Grotto." Words become few and far between, and the 
pace less endurable in the anxious endeavour of each one 
to arrive at a spot which has become so renowned among 
the members of the L.B.C. ; thanks to the able pen and the 
soft flowing language of that slave to nature, the S. W. D.C. 
But at length we reached the Park Gates, and anticipation 
became more and more intense, increased to a still greater 
height by the frantic endeavours of each one to traverse the 
many circuitous paths leading to the great relic of a bye- 
gone day, and memorial of ducal imbecility. What do we 
behold ? Is this dilapidated rabbit-warren and haunt of 
vermin the long lost paradise of some former day? 



Surely not ! We must have made a mistake ; the efforts 
of so able a pen as B. M. G*s. could not have wasted 
words and praise so lavishly upon this miserable result 
of an imbecile brain, that caused a fortune to be 
wasted in building a hovel for the denizens of the field ! 
But on enquiry we found no mistake had been made, and 
that this was truly the veritable Grotto about which we had 
heard so much. We turned away in silence, and with 
dejected and crestfallen hopes, not for the Grotto, but for 
its historians who had thrown down so much genius in the 
midst of so much refuse. We hurriedly returned to the 
" Swan," where the S.E. Division was found awaiting us. 
After a somewhat uncomfortable meal, and a stroll by the 
river, we adjourned, leaving the Captain to the tender 
mercies of the S.E. D.C. Home was reached at 9 o'clock 
by the same route as on the outward journey, after a very 
pleasant afternoon. Present: A. W. Barrett, Alison, Braby, 
Leeds, Weeks (N.E.)i a visitor, and 

H. C. PiooTT, District Captain. 



P.S. On Saturday next is the General Meet to Harrow, 
in place of Ewell, postponed. All members kindly support 
the District and raise the average. Meet at Savernake 
Cottage, Acton, at 3.45. 



NOTES ON A SCOTCH TOUR. 

" Them that cann* ride mMui shank it" 

The above proverb forms a fitting heading to a description 
of Highland roads, for it is apparent that those who use their 
bicycles for traversing rugged moors, exploring glens, and 
crossing mountain passes, must necessarily find a certain 
amount of it unrideable. The intending tourist will do 
well to consider the difficulties he will encounter before 
starting, and to go with the determination to overcome all 
obstacles. And small indeed is the trouble of wheeling one's 
machine for a mile or two compared with the pleasure 
derived from scenery so grand and so varied as is to be found 
in constant succession in the 

" L«ad of brown heath, and shaggy wood — 
Land of the mountain, feU, and flood." 

For touring in Scotland the bicycle has many advantages. 
In the Lowlands the roads are for the most part very good 
in wet as well as dry weather, and they are certainly greatly 
superior to the average English one. In the Highlands the 
natural features of the country of course present many 
difficulties, but these are over-balanced by the greater 
interest one finds when travelling out of the regular groove 
in which everyone else moves ; the ordinary tourist being 
compelled to travel in a series of rounds, where everything 
is cut and dried, and where the people themselves are of a 
less pronounced stamp than those wiio dweU in their native 
glens. By travelling across country, one gets better oppor- 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



185 



tunities of observiDg and noting the yarious characteristics, 
the peculiarities of the dialect, and the unconscious humour 
of the canny Scot. His cautious answers, his slow method 
of taking things in, which have gained for him the appro- 
priate description of one who is always "keeping up a 
terrihle tliinking" cannot fail to interest and amuse. 
Another advantage is the length of the days. The prolonged 
effects of sunlight and twilight enable one to ride as late 
as eleven o'clock without a lamp, and the Gazette can be 
read at half-past ten. 

As regards expense, an objection so frequently urged 
against Scotland when a tour is in prospective, it is certain 
that ideas on this subject are greatly exaggerated. Avoid- 
ing as the bicyclist does the heavy charges of coaches and 
steamers, it will not be found any more expensive than a 
great many other holiday districts. This at least was my 
experience in the early part of the season ; had I gone in 
the rush my opinion might probably be different. 

For sketching out the route and making plans, the best 
guide books are "Anderson's Highlands;" the old editions, 
published before railways were made, ^ntain valuable in- 
formation as to the roads ; and " Baddeley s Highlands," 
which contains a number of plans, with the various altitudes 
marked in colours. The best and cheapest map is that 
published with the " Sportsman's Guide to Scotland." 
Before starting it is necessary to provide oneself with a 
mackintosh cape for protection against the proverbial Scotch 
mists, and a waterproof saddle cover should also be taken ; 
I did not take one myself, and regretted it very much. 

How to get there ? is a question which can be answered 
in many different ways ; the road, the sea, or the railway 
offering facilities which are either dependent upon the 
fancy, or dictated by the length of the hohday. The plan 
I adopted was to break the journey by riding part of the 
way and steaming the rest, and a very pleasant way it 
proved. From London the road through Peterborough and 
Lincoln to Hull was taken, and thence the steamboat to 
Dundee. The only drawback to this route is the bad 
character of the Lincolnshire roads. From Hull, steamboats 
go to Edinburgh, Dundee, and Aberdeen. For various 
reasons I chose Dundee, and the passage thither was most 
comfortable; the boats from Hull — unlike the London 
ones — are never crowded, and in this particular case there 
were only eight passengers on board. 

Having arrived at Dundee, those who wish to go direct 
to Inverness will find a good road to Perth, where they will 
join the main road through Dunkeld and Blair Athole, 
which is the one taken by the Land's End to John o' Groats 
House riders, and which has also been previously described 
in Vol. V. by W. K. Adam, and also by E. H. Wilkins in 
his account of a Highland tour, in which, by the bye, he is 
occasionally incorrect as to distances. 

From Dundee there is a very good road by the sea coast 
through Montrose to Aberdeen, though lacking in scenic 



effect. This is on the evidence of two local bicyclists, who 
were well acquainted with the road, and one of whom in- 
formed me that he had ridden down south last year to 
attend the Bamet Meet. 

Being bound for Braemar, the popular resort up Deeside, 
my route lay through Blairgowrie and over the Eastern 
range of the Grampians. A minute description of this 
road may perhaps be useful, though by far the best approach 
to Braemar is by the road from Aberdeen. 

The distance from Dundee is 53 miles, and taking into 
consideration the mountainous character of the road, it will 
be found a good day's work. Paving stones extend for the 
first two miles, until the suburb of Lochee is passed, then 
there is a gradual rise for the next three miles, after which, 
with the exception of a steep descent from the Sidlaw hills, 
the going is very good through Cupar-Angus to Blairgowrie 
(19). But so far, the road has only led over moderate hills, 
and the country is well cultivated, the lateness of the 
vegetation being very marked. It is strange to find the 
Lilac and the May in full bloom at the end of June, after it 
is finished and forgotten in England. Once through Blair- 
gowrie, which is situated on the verge of the Highlands, 
the scenery changes entirely, and for the 34 miles to 
Braemar, a constantly changing panorama of mountains is 
presented. The rise immediately on leaving Blairgowrie is 
considerable for three miles, but the surface is first-rate as 
far as the Bridge of Cally, beneath which the Ardle flows 
in a deep and rocky channel. The road for the next three 
miles to Persie Inn," is very bad, about half of it having to 
be done on foot, and it continues rough and stony till within 
three miles of the Spittal of Glenshee, when it improves. 
Meanwhile, the aspect has been gradually getting grander. 
The mountains, with the snow between their rugged masses of 
black rock, are more lofty, and the wild and gloomy scene 
is heightened by the wail of the curlew, or the mournful 
cry of the sea birds which haunt the lochs and streams. 

Leaving the Spittal of Glenshee where there is a good 
hotel, the road gradually ascends Glen Beg, the first five 
miles being rideable ; but the shoulder of the Gairnwell has 
now to be crossed, and the machine shoved up a steep 
zigzag known as the Devil's Elbow, which rises two hundred 
feet in half a mile. The top of this pass is 2200 feet above 
the sea level, and it is said that the road is the highest in 
the kingdom over which a coach is dragged. From the 
summit the road descends gradually for the remainlDg nine 
miles through Glen Clunie, and it is only by constant 
attention to the brake that the machine can be kept in 
hand. For the grandeur of the surrounding scenery the 
village of Braemar has few equals. In the neighbouring 
glens, and in the valley of the Dee, there are many 
picturesque bits which should not be missed, and for those 
who have a taste for mountain climbing the prospect, on a 
clear day from the summit of Lochnagar, or the peaks of 
the Cairngorm group, is unsurpassed in Scotland. There 



Digitized by 



186 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



are two hotels— the "Fife Arms/' and the "Invercauld 
Arms " — both of which are expensive, but first-rate. 

The ride down Deeside to Aberdeen, distance 56 miles, 
can be accomplished with great ease in a day. The road 
leads down by the Castle of Braemar, and on the right 
hand is overhung by rocky precipices, clad on every possible 
spot with pines and birches. At a distance of three miles, 
the Dee, lashed into foam by its rocky bed, is crossed by 
the new Invercauld Bridge, and close to it is the entrance 
to the deer forest of Ballochbuie— considered the finest pine 
forest in Britain. About a mile through the forest, are the 
Falls of the Garrawalt which can only be visited when the 
Queen is away from Balmoral. 

From Invercauld Bridge the road descends very gradually 
for the next five miles. In the middle of the road the 
surface is very rough, but on each side there is a narrow 
strip of smooth ground. 

Balmoral Castle, the Highland home of Her Majesty, situ- 
ated on a lovely spot by the Dee, is now passed on the right, 
and about two miles further on is Abergeldie, the property 
of the Prince of Wales. The gradient, with the exception 
of one or two hills, is favourable till close upon Ballater, 
where there is a steep brae just outside the village, the site 
of Ballater being 430 feet lower than Braemar. I had no 
personal experience of the road from Ballater to Aberdeen, 
but from the accounts of those who have ridden it, it 
appears to be a splendid one and nearly level all the way. 

From Braemar it is impossible to get to Inverness without 
traversing some very rough ground. The only road crossing 
the mountains to Speyside, the various stages of which are 
Crathie(9),Rinloan (13),CorgarflF(21),Tomintoul (30),Gran- 
town (44), Bridge of Carr (55), Inverness (79). From Crathie 
to Grantown the surface is dreadfully bad, without men- 
tioning the hills, so much so that the charge for posting 
from Braemar to Grantown is no less than five pounds. 
Though strongly advised not to attempt it, it was my in- 
tention to have done so, but an accident to my machine at 
Crathie compelled me to make for the train at Ballater, 
whence I proceeded to Aberdeen, and by rail again next 
morning to Inverness, a proceeding which did not fall in 
with my views. Railway travelling in the Highlands is 
very slow, and one can easily credit the tales that are told 
of the train being stopped to enable the guard to get a 
light from the driver. To be cooped up in a crowded 
carriage for five or six hours while the train only covers a 
Imndred miles is very poor fun, compared even with 
tramping over a moorland road. 

Inverness, finely situated on the river Ness, is a convenient 
place to stay the night, and the "Royal Hotel" will be 
found first-rate. There is nothing of great interest in the 
town itself. The Cathedral, a comparatively modern 
building, is the finest ecclesiastical building in the North of 
Scotland, but that, after all, is not sapng very much. In 
the neighbourhood there are many beautiful views, and in 



the river some large islands covered with trees are kept up 
as public gardens. 

The Great Glen of Scotland, which extends from the 
Moray Frith to Loch Linnhe in a series of lakes which form 
the Caledonian Canal, attracts a great number of cyclists, 
and if time and circumstances permit, it should certainly be 
included in a Scotch tour. The road, taken by Wilkins and 
Faxon, and described in the Gazette, follows the northern 
shore of Loch Ness, it is considerably better as regards hills 
than the one which runs along the opposite shore. But the 
famous Falls of Foyers to be seen on the way induced me to 
try the southern side, and such a magnificent sight amply 
repaid the extra trouble. 

Soon after leaving Inverness, the road passes for some 
distance through a thick avenue of beeches, and then it 
becomes hilly, until the village 6f Dores (7^) is reached. At 
this point the road falls in with Loch Ness, and a fine view 
of the lake is obtained on the top of the hill above Dores. 
From here there is a glorious ride for eight miles, the road 
running along quite close to the water, and the surface is 
perfection, with the exception of some gullies across the 
road, which however are quite rideable if care be taken. 
A considerable ascent has now to be made before the 
"Foyers Inn," 18 miles, is reached. It is advisable, if 
refreshment is needed, to take it before visiting the Falls, 
as they are a mile on the road to Fort Augustus, and a 
steep hill has to be surmounted. The lower Falls, only a 
short distance from the road, are the grandest They 
descend through a narrow gap over a height of ninety feet, 
and fall with a tremendous roar into a deep and rocky 
hollow. A good view may be obtained firom a ledge oppo- 
site the cascade. About a quarter of a mile further up the 
road are the upper Falls, which, though not so grand as the 
others, are well worth visiting. The road to Fort Augustus 
now deviates from the side of the loch, and proceeds on the 
south side of the hills which bound it. About five miles 
from the Falls, over hilly country, is " Whitebridge Inn," 
and the road now rises considerably for the next three 
miles, and then it descends to Loch TariT, a pretty lake 
studded with small islands, and after winding down Glen 
Doe, a charming little glen luxuriant with ferns, a steep 
hill two miles long leads down to Fort Augustus (32). 

As I now took the route from Fort Augustus to Fort 
William and Ballachulish, a description of which has 
already appeared in these columns, it is only necessary to 
give my general impressions of the road, which were rather 
disappointing after the favourable reports I had heard of it. 
Dismounts are very frequent, owing both to the mountain 
streams, which tear up the surface, and the numerous gates 
which cross the roads in this part of the Highlands. Great 
care must be taken in keeping to the right road, particu- 
larly on the north side of Loch Oich, where there are 
several difficult situations. A Scotch woman, who put me 
right just as I was taking the wrong road, told me that

187 



bicyclists invariably went wrong at that particular spot. 
Direction posts are almost unknown in the north of Scot- 
land, and the mile posts are nearly always illegible. 

The distance from Fort William to Ballachulisb, according 
to Anderson^ is 14 miles, probably measured to the ferry, 
while the mile posts, which made it 12|, measure to the 
beginning of the village, some distance west of the ferry. 
From Ballachulish to Eingshouse the distance is 16 miles, 
not 12, as stated by Wilkins, and from Eingshouse to 
Inveroran it is 10 miles, not 16.

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
THE MEMBER'S BROTHER. 

Snt, — ^The uninitiated, reading your article in last week's 
Gazette, would imagine that the Member s Brother — 
speak of the genus that rides — is a most desirable attendant 
at Club Runs, and should be encouraged as much as possible 
by the D. C's. But, unfortunately, he has a very great 
drawback, and has not qualified. He has not paid a sub- 
scription, nor has he passed through the ordeal of election. 
I am aware this is a very delicate subject to write upon, 
but still I venture to address you to impress upon your 
readers the advisability of forming some rules to regulate 
and decide the locus standi of the Member's Brother. For 
why should he ever join the Club if he is not a racing 
man ? He sees his brother's Gazette, and his own name 
printed amongst those of the attendants of Club Meets. 
If he tours, he can, through his brother, obtain by means of 
the Gazette all the information he requires. He can 
always depend on being in a position, through his brother, 
of buying tickets for Club Balls, Smoking Concerts, etc., 
in fact he is in exactly the same position as an Honorary 
Member {vide Rule 7) if we except the power of voting 
at General Meetings. 

Now, be it understood, I am far from wishing to prevent 
a member from bringing a brother or a friend to any Club 
meeting, but I do wish to see some regulations formed 
iiRiich will preclude him from some one of the advantages 
of the Club — not from any motive of selfishness, but simply 
that something may be left to induce him to join us, and to 
pay his guinea into the scanty coffers of the Club. I would 
suggest that members be invited to bring their brothers to 
not more than six Club runs in the year, and that at Club 
runs the latter should ride in the rear ranks. — Tours, etc., 

^ WiVKRN. 



To the Editor of the London Bicyde Club Gazette,

for 100 yards to the programme for 2^d September ?

preparatory inconvenience in the way of training would, I 

t 



feel confident, draw considerably among the members who 
vote longer distances too much trouble. 

I submit this idea for the consideration of the Race 
Committee, and feel sure, should they decide favourably, a 
good entry would be the result. — ^Yours, etc., 

14th August, 1883. Mebcubius. 



N.E. DISTRICT SATURDAY RUN. 
August 11th. Those members who did not attend the 
run last Saturday missed a treat. Even the clerk of the 
weather had made up his mind the District should be 
happy, and gave us a tail wind all the way out, and no 
wind, but a bright moon, on the way home. And the roads ? 
Well, a firm, dry gravel road can be good travelling, 
if it tries, and they did try on Saturday. Unfortunately, 
however, as these merits were not frilly detailed before- 
hand, only two members met the D.C. at the comer, 
and a certain sense of calmness being observable in 
the .party, they decided to avoid all needless hills, and 
accordingly proceeded by Redbridge and a lower road, 
some parts of which were not very familiar, and there 
was a tendency to decline the responsibility, but to accept 
the credit for such fact on the part of some of the party as 
the road appeared good or the reverse. Hog Hill was by a 
side wind comfortably compassed to the surprise of all 
concerned, and after the Havering turning the route was 
very charming with pleasant lanesovershadowed by trees, and 
with a pleasant undulation, which only added to the interest 
without materially interfering with the comfort of the riders. 
We asked the way to the Tower Arms " at last, as we were 
practically standing outside it, but we are unlikely to forget 
it A sumptuous tea for ten had been provided, and the 
dejection of the D.C. was complete until a further con- 
tingent of Barretts made up the party to five. Before a 
peaceful saunter in the garden was over the Treasurer 
turned up, and six seemed a fairish muster for the time of 
year as we made our way home by the same route as before, 
under the pleasant atmospheric conditions mentioned above. 
Present : E. H. Barrett, R. B. Hough, Jolly, Lyne, A. P. 
Barrett (visitor), and 

Thso. Godlee, District Captain. 
[Through the late arrival of the above it could not be 
inserted in the usual position. — ^Ed.]

H. R. Reynolds left Croydon jpn Tuesday, July Slst, at 
5.30 a.m., picked up W. D'A. TTrofton near the Marble 
Arch, and rode with him by a circular route to Northamp- 
ton and Leicester, where they strfjred the night (122 miles). 
The next day they went by an indirect route to Thorne, 
Yorkshire (91). On Thursday (J|ofton left, and Reynolds 
rode on to Durham (98) ; next day to Berwick (83) ; 
arriving at Edinburgh (59) on Saturday. Distance, 450 
miles in 107^ hours, all of which was done without hurry, 
cathedrals and castles being visited en route. 

In Monday's Daify Telegraph is an article entitled 
" Saturday Afternoon on Hampstead Heath." The objec- 
tionable notice-boards entirely absorbed the writer's atten- 
tion, or he could scarcely have failed to mention the 

meeting of the N.W. host. 

. • * • • • • 

One of the three who visited the S. W. Grotto last Satur- 
day reports, that Oruggen has overlooked the best thing 
about it in his account, namely, the twisted spikes over the 
gateways which give admittance to "this shell house." 
These are really fine examples of wrought iron work, and 
are evidently much older than the gates which they 
surmount, the latter being of very clumsy and inferior 
design. 

• •«*•• 

Also, that if the Duchess of Tork could not find a better 
place than a second-rate sort of cellar to wash herself in, she 
is very much to be pitied. The^hole in the floor would not 
make a bad place to pickle pork in, if provided with a well- 
fitting lid ; but as a bath it is a distinct failure. This was 
very clearly demonstrated by the sapient remark of another 

of the three, " Where did the hot water come from ? " 

• «•••• 

A cyclist was shoving his tri. up Barnet Hill, when the 
facetious " 'Arry " asked him "why he did'nt get in front 

and learn to draw ? "

pleasant surprise for the S. E. D. G. Somehow he did not 
seem to take it as such on his jetum from a fortnight's 

holiday on Saturday. 

• «•*•« 

While disowning any share in the arrangement, he hopes 
the S.E. district will, by a large attendance, assist him in 
making the best of the inevitable. 

The recent alterations in the Meet Card are due, it is 
said, to the radical tendencies of some of those in authority. 

In the August list of candidates for the C.T.G. is the 
name of our President. 



Early this month.. S. Herbert and R. G. Francis left East 
Grinstead in the morning and rode through Horsham, 
Pulborough, and Chi Chester to Pareham, where they lunched, 
and then on thro ^h Southampton and Lyndhurst to 
Ringwood. Here t* ey arrived in a soaked condition about 
seven, and stayed tl e night at the " White Hart." Next 
day owing to the re 'U a late start was made, and with the 
roads in a very stic 7 condition they reached DofcJ^ster 
for lunch, and Brid;>ort in the evening. After a ba^pin 
the sea, they left a out eight next day for Exeter, over 
roads rough and h*. \y ; passing through Axminster they 
stopped for lunch ai Honiton, and arrived at the " Half 
Moon," Exeter at Ax in the evening. The following 
morning Herbert rode on to Ilfiracombe, while Francis took 
train for Penzance.

The latest hour for reception of copy for " Q-azette"

THE HARROW MEET. 

Although everybody said that everyone was out of town, 
and though to a N.E. or S.E. man Harrow is about as 
accessible as the North Pole, a fairly representative party, 
thirty-two in number, sat down to a capital tea at the 
" King's Head." It was a lovely evening, such as one 
only meets with in the early autumn. The valley, stretching 
beyond the Hill into dim distance, wa^. already being illu- 
mined by a brilliant moon ; and, gazing at the fair scene, 
the North-easter for a moment forgot the twenty miles of 
macadam that parted him from the gravel lanes of Essex. 
Generally speaking, the roads by which our men reached 
the rendezvous were in good order ; the weather was 
supreme (although some malcontents were heard to mutter 
that it was " close"). The tea was varied and abundant, 
and the landscape, specially arranged for the occasion, 
combined with the pretty garden to make a success of 
our last Gteneral Meet in this year. 

The W., N.E., and N.W. men were the first arrivals, 
followed, in about an hour, by the S.E. 

The W. men journeyed vid Ealing Apperton, and Sud- 
bury, and if a nation without a history is happy, they may 
be assumed to have enjoyed themselves, for, like the needy 
knife-grinder of the ballad, their D.G. has no tale to tell." 

The N.W. D.C. reports :— 



''Although not possessed of the presumption displayed 
by the N.E. D.C. (vide last Gazette), yet I expected to 
see more at the rendezvous than appeared. However, in 
spite of ' everybody being out of town,' on leaving Hendon 
Station behind us we presented a formidable troupe, with 
the help of the * little D.' and the N. W. collie, as we wound 
through the Kenton Lanes. Our companions were quite 
unable to control their feelings of admiration for the 
scenery, and, even when they had perceived Harrow, con- 
tinued to roam through the lanes for the best part of the 
afternoon. The return journey was more expeditious." 

The S.E. D.C. reports :— 

" Last Saturday with its blazing sun, and not very acces- 
sible meeting place of Harrow, did not seem to appeal to 
the inclinations of many of the usual attendants at S.E. 
runs. Punctuality was the order of the day, and the clock 
had scarcely struck four when the six men assembled 
at the Fire Station started on tlieir way for the General 
Meet. Owing to a strong feeling against Kew Bridge, 
which was strengthened by a rumour that the shadowy 
legions of the South- West would not be found there in 
force, the road by Kingston Bridge was adopted. This 
alternative proved fairly successful, for after passing 
Twickenham and Hounslow with a minimum of the ob- 
jectionable macadam, a pleasant lane was hit on which 
brought the party in an almost straight line for Harrow 
Hill. Here F. H. Williams, who had ridden by Kew, 
joined the ranks, but as Hartridge, who was mounted on a 
strange machine, had turned back previously, it was still 



Digitized by 



Google 



190 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



only six in number that the S.R men arrived at the " King's 
Arms." The other Districts were already on the spot, and 
were mostly engaged in taking in the view from the garden 
terrace. After tea, and a quiet pipe in the garden, the 
moon rose on the scene, and a general move homewards 
was made about half-past eight." 
Tlie N.E. D.C. Reports :— 

" The main body may be assumed to have left the Corner 
three strong within five minutes of time and proceeded 
without a dismount to Jack Straw's, whilst the D.C. and 
Lloyd starting about the same time, proceeded by Tottenham 
direct, and also without leaving the saddle reached Jack 
Straw's shortly after the others, and thus, accumulated to five, 
were received with all hospitality by the N.W. D.C. This 
oflBcer desiring the N.R to learn that there was macadam 
even in the N.W., took them up about two miles of Edg- 
ware Road to Hendon Station, and then reinforced by Lyne, 
who had come a different route, and by two N.W. members, 
rode to the proper route for Harrow, but for unexplained 
reasons preferred riding back to Blackpot, and by a 
species of 8, succeeded ultimately in reaching Harrow by a 
route, it is calculated, some five miles further than the usual 
one. Four at least of the N.R rode up the hill, and 
Harrow was reached about 6.80. Here the Captain and 
Weeks made up the N.E. contingent to eight. The return 
journey was taken with the N.W. by way of Fiuchley, at 
a pace not calculated to alarm the most well-regulated 
rider until the N.E. D.C. became fractious and spurted, 
and then and there asserted that he was in his own district 
and proposed to lead with the Captain. Thenceforth on 
vid Muswell Hill the pace increased, an impetus being 
given when the balance of the N.W. left them, and again 
when the party was reduced to the Walthamstow contin- 
gent — a force of four members and a visitor — so that the 
pace from Wood Oreen was unusual for a Club run." 

It is feared the absence abroad of the energetic S. W. D.C. 
permitted the S.W. men to play truant. The Captain, at 
any rate, on his own account, desires to record sorrowfully 
that, according to programme, he waited for them in vain 
on Eew Bridge, after a ride down from the City, through 



crowded and greasy streets, in which he seemed to have 
lived a lifetime ; also be wishes to thank members for their 
readiness in falling in with at such short notice the altera- 
tions in the Meet Card involved in the substitution of the 
Harrow General Meet for those before arranged. 
Present : — 

W. Koch, G. S. Hewitt, H. M. Hewitt, Scott, P. M. 
Williams, W. E. Naylor, Dr. Langmore, two visitors, and 
H. C. Pigott, District Captain. 

N,W. Alison, Hindley, Jackson, Wilkins, Williams,

S.R DISTRICT.— NOTICE. 
Next Saturday the run is to Riverhead, one of the 
favourite meets of the District. The Croydon men will leave 
the Pire Station at 4. 30, and will meet the Blackheath Division 
at 5.15 on Pamborough Common. Tea at the Amhurst 
Arms," at 6.30. R. G. Praegis, District Captain. 

W. DISTRICT. 
On Saturday next I quite expect a large number will 
attend the run to Datchet, as several members from other 
Divisions haye promised to pay us a visit at this favourite 
riverside resort, where the pleasures of the wheel and the 
river amalgamate in ^orious unity. We meet at 3.45 at 
Acton, and the dbtance is only thirteen miles from the 
starting point. All who can come are welcoma Tea at 6.30. 

H. C. PiQoTT, District Captain.

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
In the One Mile Professional Championship at Wolver- 
hampton, Wood again proved his superiority over Howell, 
winning by seven yards in 2 min. 56^ sec. 

• • • • • • 

Oliver Thorn scored another win at the Ravensboume 
AG. Meeting at Stamford Bridge on Saturday afternoon, 
beating Robinson of the Ranelagh Harriers, who ^started 
from the same mark (10) by five yards in 2 min. 50| sec. 

• «•«*• 

A very sensational performance was done by F. Lees, the 
the Sheffield Professional, at the Belgrave Road Grounds* 
Leicester, on Saturday. He started with the object of 
covering 20 miles in the hour ; and so weU did he succeed, 
that when the pistol was fired he had covered 20 miles, 905 
yarda The fastest miles were the first in 2 min. 51 sec., 
the tenth in 2 min. 48 sec., the eighteenth in 2 min. 
50 sec., the nineteenth in 2 min. 51 sec., and the last in 
2 min. 47 sec. 

««•••« 

Howell, who appeared on the track. to make the pace, 
attempted to cut the record for a mile^ which he covered 
in 2 min. 41f sec. 

• •««*• 

The 24 hours record has again been beaten by J. W. 
Brown, who has accomplished 231^ miles in the day. 

• •«««• 

Considering what has been done by. the " Facile " and 
the tricycle, the record for the legitimate two wheeler is 
very poor. 

• ••••« 

Accounts of how So-and-so has lowered it by 2^ miles 
will soon get monotonous unless a crack road rider turns 
out and does something respectable. Now then, H. R. ! 

A library, containing so much thatlTusefuI in the sh^ie 
of guide books and maps, as does that of the N.G.U., should 
certainly be taken advantage of to a greater extent than it 
is at present. 

If the signatures in the register of readers include all 
who have used the library, the total for the past three 
months amounts to the insignificant number of twenty-two. 

Neither does the new arrangement of keeping it open 
from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. bring any additional readers. The 
only convenient time, viz., from 5.0 to 6.S0 p.m., has not 
yet been tried. Those who finish work at 5.0 frequently 
have engagements in town later on, and they are reduced 
to kicking their heels about in a fruitless endeavour to pass 
the time. 

The Fkont Wheel Hub. 



THE MEMBER'S BROTHER. 
To the Editor qf the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sir, — I am an old offender, though my offence has ceased 
for many years ; but, with all humility, I would venture to 
dissent from " Wyvern's** principle. 

You are mistaken, all you unrelated members, in my 
humble judgment, if even in the most distant way you 
hinder the attendance of a member's brother. There are 
two causes which will serve all practical purposea One, 
that no member's brother who attends runs often will 
stop outside the Club if he can avoid it. I found it too 
expensive a luxury at one time. Secondly, the D.G., 
if he is worth his salt, will soon intimate to the 
member or the brother that it is time we saw more or 
less of the brother aforesaid if the privilege is abused. 
I speak from experience, and I speak with a most sincere 
conviction that I am right I imagine " Wyvem " would 
not wish to exclude the member's brother from our runs ; 
but I do not think he fully realises the sensitiveness of the 
persons he is desiring, with the best of intentions, to curtaiL 
If a member's brother is informed, as " Wyvem's" letter 
informs him, that he is not wanted, believe me — for I have 
tried — he will be seen no more. — ^Yours, etc.. 

An " Ex-Mbmbeb'8-Brotheb." 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Clvb Gazette. 

Sib, — ^Although in no way doubting the sincerity of 
" Wyvem's " motives, and recognising fully the temperate 
manner in which he has stated bis views, I hope he will 
pardon me for expressing my entire dissent from them, 
llie subject is, as he says, a delicate one— so delicate, in- 
deed, that merely to give it utterance in these pages will be 
sufficient for a length of time to scare from our meets many 
a friend and visitor whomVe, and " Wyvern " I am sure, 
as much as any of us, would have wished to have been 
present. Were it a common thing for our Club Teas to be 
constantly invaded by the same well-to-do strangers there 
would have been much appropriateness in the suggestions 
of your correspondent ; but our visitors are, in my opinion, 
far too rare, and our " members' brothers," who from time 
to time come among us, a(e, alas ! too apt to be afflicted 
with that impecuniosity which has troubled most of us at 
some period of our historyT The relief of such a malady, 
however, may be usually left to time, and with its cure the 
" member's brother " may usually be found joining our ranks. 
In thinking the matter ever it seems to me that for 
many reasons we should in every way encourage visitors, 
whether "members' brothers" or no. 

They tend to sustain theinterest felt by the L.B.C. man 
in his Club, inasmuch as thefree and unrestricted privilege he 
has hitherto enjoyed of bringing them with him to his Club 
tea has been greatly valued^ Also, as was ably pointed out 



Digitized by 



192 



LONDON BICVCLE CLUB GAZEITE, 



by yourself in a recent leader, it is often the visitor who 
has helped to swell a D.G.'s average by bringing out a lazy 
member wavering between the riter and the tennis-court. 
To the tea and the run, where thoy entail neither expense 
the Club nor trouble to the B.C., they impart a certain 
freshness. To exclude them, which I submit would practi- 
cally be the result of adopting any such limitation as 
Wyvern " suggests, would go far to shut us out from what 
has proved itself in the past our best recruiting ground. I 
cannot but think, sir, that the surest mode of developing the 
member's brother into the member, is to bring him as 
frequently as possible to our meetings, and that to keep up 
our numbers, if from no better motive, we should continue 
to extend to all visitors at our Club runs the same hearty 
welcome that has been held out to them in the past.-- 
Yours, etc., 

Ashley W. Barrbtt. 



MODEST HOTEL CHARGES. 

To the Editor of the London Bicyck Club Gazette, 

Dear Sir, — ^To those of our members who are fond of 
living at the rate of several thousands a year for a short 
time, and getting very little worth for their money, I can 
highly recommend "THE ROYAL STAG HOTEL," 
Datchet, Bucks. Hot lunch— joint, pastry, cheese, 48. 6d. ; 
breakfast— chops and eggs, 3s. As the West District have 
a run to and tea at the above hotel next week, it will be a 
grand chance for the Club Mashers. The hotel opposite 
the Manor is, I believe, still dearer. — ^Yours truly,

THE POTTERER UNMASKED. 

In an article early this year on my experiences with a 
lazy friend, I was unfortunate enough to bring down upon 
me the wrath of one who took upon himself to champion the 
cause of the Potterer." I need hardly say that my remarks 
were not armed against this worthy clasef, though from one 
of them haying taken up the gauntlet, I am driven to the 
conclusion that laziness and pottering are more nearly akin 
than I thought. Qui s'eacuse s'accuse. 

Much has been written in favour of the pottering 
paragon. We are called on to admire his harmless and 
retiring disposition, the absence of any desire in him for 
pots, medals, and such-like lucre, and his love for the 
beauties of nature. AU these are admirable qualities, but 
I should like to ask whether they are really the product 
of a well-trained mind, or merely the outcome of a master- 
ful inactivity. Now it is not difficult to imagine that 
the man who lazily reclines on his back on some jM>tn^ de 
vue may have no more regard for the beauty of the scene 
than he who comes up the hill with his nose on the wheel, 
only to disappear down the other side with his legs over 
the handles ; and that many of those whom we see enjoying 
a quiet pipe amid the ruins of some feudal castle, or on the 
shady side of a Roman tumulus, have about as much anti- 



quarian research at heart as the man to whom these objects 
are but landmarks on his road to or from such and such a 
place. It is against laziness as a component part of the 
common potterer that I would raise my voice. We are 
told that because one man gets up early and braces his 
limbs in a good hard day's run, therefore he can have no 
eye for the picturesque ; and because another indulges in 
frequent repose, and is lackadaisically indolent, therefore 
he is embued with all the susceptibilities of the poet and 
the painter. No doubt there is some satisfaction for the 
lazy elf who rides little and cares for it less in " laying the 
flattering unction to his soul," that he is a distinguished 
member of the gentis Potterer, in fact a kind of pariah 
among wheelmen, and in sneering at the energetic as a 
bicyclical Philistine. He may go further, and as he dreams 
away the hours lulled by the babbling waters that flow by 
the Castle of Indolence, he may imagine what a lasting 
support he is likely to be to his Club ; but it is not im- 
possible that his bicycling animus (or the little of it that 
remains) will die out like the glimmer of a farthing rush- 
light quite as quickly as the more brilliant light of his 
energetic compeer and leave less trace behind it 

To drop metaphor, what would become of our Club if we 
all went in for being Potterers? There is far too little 
energy even now for us to depreciate what does exist. The 
noble art of pottering will probably be forced on most of 
us some time or other, whether we like it or no. Meanwhile 
by an energy tempered with moderation, we shall get the 
greatest amount of enjoyment from our sport, and at the 



Digitized by 



Google 



194 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



same time be doing our duty as members of an active 
community. A man either admires the picturesque, or he 
does not ; is interested in ruins and fossilized bones^ or is not. 
These qualities are born in him. The veritable and much 
maligned " Scorcher " — even in his most rabid stage — ^who 
has, and affects no care for these things, is I would maintain 
more entitled to our respect than the wight who hides his 
averseness to action in a simulated interest for nature's 
charms and the memories of a byegone age. Dodo. 

RACE MEETING AT STAMFORD BRIDGE. 

In accordance with the decision of the Committee there 
will be a race meeting at the above grounds on Saturday, 
22nd September, at 3.30 p.m. The events will be 

One Mile Club Handicap. Three prizes : gold, silver, 
and bronze medals. 

Twelve Miles District Scratch. Three prizes : bronze 
medals. 

Half-mile Club Scratch. Two prizes : gold and silver 
medals. 

220 yards Flat Race for boys under 16 years of age, 
introduced by members. Three prizes. 

Entry fee for each of the first three events 2s. 6d, and 
for the Flat Race, Is. The Twelve Miles District Race 
must be ridden on boiid fide roadsters ; only those members 
allowed to compete who have attended at least eight meets 
throughout the season. Each District to have three starters. 
Entries close on 17th September. A member of the Com- 
mittee has kindly offered the first prize for the flat race. 

Members will be admitted free on production of Club 
Badge. No tickets will be issued ; the admission to the 
outside world will be by payment of Is. at the gates. 

It will be seen from the foregoing that an entirely new 
departure is being undertaken. The afternoon is intended 
to be a purely Club affair, and every member taking part in 
the races or turning up on the ground will make it more so. 
It will serve all the purposes of a General Meet, without the 
bother of having to get to the destination by riding all round 
London, and will have one great attraction which the said 
General Meet is void of, viz., the presence of fellow-members' 
sisters, cousins, and aunts. E. S. Price, Hon. Sec.
