SATUEDAY RUNS. 



N;E. DijSTBICT. 

August 25th. The absence of its lawful chief in Wales 
did not prevent the little District from making a respectable 
show. Barrett, Baldwin, and Lyne, who had reached " The 
Orange Tree " at Havering-atte-Bower by different routes, 
sat down to tea, while the Treasurer and a friend arrived 
as they were about to depart. On the way home, via 
Collier Row, Sidney Hough and Sewell joined the party. 
Lamps were lit on the top of Hog Hill, which, owing to 
stones and darkness, was more than usually refractory. The 
weather was hot. There were many stones out, and as far 
as the historian of this run could learn, nothing was talked 
of but the September race meeting, speculations being nu- 
merous concerning our D.C.'s selection of representatives 
for his District. Present : Baldwin, L3me, Hough, R. B., 
Hough, S. G., Sewell, one visitor, and Jack (the District 
terrier). Ashley W. Babrbtt, Captain. 



N.W. DiSTEICT. 

It is most astonishing how the holiday season seems to 
affect our " District ; " for although numbering over forty 
members, yet only three carried out the run to Little Berk- 
hempstead last Saturday. After tea had been despatched 
and the return journey commenced, Jackson broke a treadle- 
pin at one of the hills, and had to ride home with the help 
of one foot only, which however, did not seem to incon- 
venience him in the slightest. Present : Jackson, Williams, 
and A. H. Lown, District Captain. 



W. District. 

Last Saturday I arrived at Savemake Cottage at three 
minutes after four p.m., but found no one there ; I ac- 
cordingly started immediately en route for Datchet. 
Fortunately, at the top of Gunnersbury Lane, I met 
Scott awaiting my arrival. We decided to go for a 
circular run, taking Datchet within our circle, so stri- 
king the Ealing main road we rode to Hanwell ; here, 
turning sharp to the left under the Great Western 
Railway bridges, we proceeded to Norwood Green and 
Harlington. Crossing the Bath road we made for Bedfont. 
Before running into Bedfont we turned to the right, and 
ran through Ashford and Stanwell. The roads were 
now almost uurideable on account of the dust. We ran on 
through some very pretty scenery beyond Stanwell to 
within one mile of Datchet, when, instead of going on into 
the latter place, we branched off for Staines, as both of us 
wanted to get home early. From here the main road was 
found bad as far as Bedfont, where Sc«tt stayed for tea, 
leaving me to continue the journey to Eew alone, where I 
arrived about 7.15, after a very pleasant run. Present: 
Scott, and H. C. Pigott, District Captain. 



Another Account, 
Two members rode from West Drayton to Datchet on 
Saturday, and after devoting an hour to punting on the 
river, had tea at the " Manor Hotel," one of the most 
picturesque houses in this picturesque village. For the 
benefit of " Once bitten, twice shy," they may state that 
the cost of the tea, which consisted of a lot of good things, 
was half-a-crown. Present : W. E. Naylor and 

F. M. Williams. 



S.W. DlSTMCfT. 

H. L. Ryan seems to have been the only representative 
of our District at Kingston Station on Saturday last, and 
as lameness (the result of a recent spill) prevented my being 
able to help him to carry out the run, Guildford was not 
visited at all. We note with regret, in the Gazette of the 
16th August, that an apparently unsatisfactory visit has 
recently been paid by the West District, accompanied 
by our ubiquitous Captain, to the celebrated Grotto in 
Oatlands Park, and in the account given of the expedition 
by the West D.C., we are led to infer that Buckler and 
self had in some measure contributed— by our descriptions 
of this unique spot — towards their somewhat extraordinary 
disappointment ; but in this we feel that blame can- 
not be attached to us. Our sarcastic commmentator 
upon the merits of the Grotto may perhaps be in- 
terested to know, with regard to "Gruggen's remains" 
as he facetiously calls them, that they are still lively, 
and that. Phoenix like, he has risen out of those remains, 
or as Gray beautifully observes, " even in his ashes live 
their wonted fires." We fear that our intelligent visitors 
have had little experience of Grottos, for otherwise they 
could scarcely call to mind things so utterly dissimilar, and 
have perpetrated the anomaly of comparing the phenomena 
of nature with mere works of human art ! Why our worthy 
Captain should be described as having assumed a malicious 
look, so foreign to his nature, we are at a loss to compre- 
hend ; his malice, if such it can be called, could only have 
consisted in a generous wish that his men should see all 
there was to be seen within this enchanting shell-house. 
Having great confidence in our Captain's good judgment, 
skilful discrimination, and knowledge of antiquity, I feel sure 
we shall not ask in vain when we urge him to convey to us in 
writing an impartial statement of the impression that the 
grotto in question produced upon him upon the occasion 
of this singular visit. The designation of the work as 
exhibiting " ducal imbecility," has some warrant, for this is 
only one of many instances of money lavished upon useless 
structures, which after completion have been justly denomi- 
nated as " so and so's folly." We could have given the 
West D.C. some credit for his allusion to the refuge the 
Grotto afforded for " vermin and other denizens of the field 
and air," had we not at once detected the plagiarism of 
ideas from Dickens, who in describing an arbour, designates 



Digitized by 



196 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



it as " one of those places which humane men erect for the 
accomodation of spiders." We sincerely hope that the dejec- 
tion and senseof feeling crestfallen which our cyclingneighbour 
so graphically expresses has not permanently overwhelmed 
bis peace of mind, especially when he hears that this grotto 
is acknowledged to be well worthy of a visit by all those 
who admire a special, remarkable, and most elaborate work 
involving, as it did, so much labour, and which elicited so 
much artistic skill in carrying it into execution. The run 
next Saturday is only to Sunbury, and as this distance is 
so short, and the river-side usually so attractive to our 
members, I trust that as many as possible will put in an 
appearance at Kingston Station at 4.15 p.m. 

B. M. GBUQGEif, District Captain. 



Another Account. 
We arrived at Kingston a little late on Saturday 25th> 
but hoped to find some member of the District. Not having 
that hope fulfilled we carried out the run to Guildford, 
finding the roads veiy loose and stoney. Present : J. W. 
Alison, and A. H. Koch. 



S.E. DiSTRIOI. 

The breaking of two treadle-pins, one crank, and an 
unlucky somersault, though they cannot be said to have 
added to the pleasure of last Saturday's run, certainly 
relieved it of some of its monotony. Tlie first victim was 
Barker, who snapped his treadle-pin just at the start from 
Croydon ; fortunately no bones were broken, and with the 
aid of a brother's tricycle and a gypsy's donkey, he succeeded 
in following the whole run ; and it was due to the versatile 
genius of the rider, who had never ventured far on a tricycle 
before, that the three-wheeler came through the day with 
no little credit. Twelve men and one visitor 'met on 
Famborough Common about half-past five. The visitor's 
crank gave way on the hill by Bromley Union, and Stephen 
Smith stayed behind to see it put right. If the road was loose, 
so was the order up the long rise to the *' Polhill Arms ; " and it 
was owing to the former that oneof theyouthfulflyerswho was 
scudding along with visions of Crawley and Stamford Bridge 
attending him on his way, slipped his treadle, and un- 
premeditatedly bit the dust. A nasty cut was plastered up 
by J. C. Oswald in quite professional style, and though a 
little shaken, our hero was soon mounted again, and going 
along as if nothing had happened. After a short halt for 
strawberries on the top of the hill, we ran easily down the 
other side to Riverhead. Though our arrival was somewhat 
late, tea was still more so, the landlord of the "Amherst Arms" 
having gone away without giving the necessary instructions. 
The stroll in the garden while tea was being got ready was 
diversified by anecdotes from Barker and Potter of their 
experiences in Normandy. A short half hour only was 
allowed for a postprandial pipe, and at a quarter to nine 
lamps were lighted, and the return journey commenced. 



On reaching Famborough, Whealler's treadle-pin broke, 
with no serious results beyond a short delay, while the 
spare pin which Stephen Smith had, as usual, brought 
was fitted in. Without further mishap the various parties 
reached home not long after eleven. Present : Barker, 
Hartridge, S. Herbert, McRae, J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, 
L. Potter, F. B. Smith, S. P. Smith, Whealler, P. H. 
Williams, one visitor, and 

B. G. Fraistcis, District Captain. 



Next Saturday, the Croydon Division will leave the 
Fire Station at half-past four and meet the Blackheath men 
at Addington Church at five o'clock, when the hares will 
start at that hour punctually. We shall have tea at the 
" George Inn," Downe, at 6.30. Any man who cannot join 
in the chase will, I hope, meet us thera 

B. G. Francis, District Captain. 



NOTES ON A SCOTCH TOUB. 

(COHTINUXD FBOM PAGB 187.) 

Having arrived at Ballachulish, the steamboat may be 
taken to Oban, where the tourist can get to any point he 
likes. This alternative is introduced for the benefit of 
those who may not care about crossing rougher ground 
than that which they have already experienced, as the road 
through Glencoe, across the moor of Bannoch and over 
Blackmount to Tyndrum (35) is of an exceptionally difficult 
character. The scenery is wild in the extreme, and the 
pass of Glencoe, in which the horrible massacre took place, 
is the gloomiest and most rugged glen in Scotland, though 
what the guide books say about the impending hills almost 
shutting out the daylight is great twaddla 

Leaving the ferry at BalUchulish, the road skirts Loch 
Leven for two miles as far as the slate quarries, and the 
next two miles are very fair, after which we enter the lower 
portion of Glencoe, where the road is in parts covered with 
big stones, brought down the mountain sides by the torrents 
which intersect them, and the best mode of progression is 
to lift the backbone up and wheel it over as best you can. 
Along the solitary loch, situated in the lower half of the 
glen, the road is rideable, but on getting into the upper 
half, which is wilder and totally destitute of vegetation, 
the road ascends considerably, and a great deal of shoving 
has to be done. When the head of the glen is reached, the 
road descends with a stony surface for three miles to 
" Kingshouse Inn." 

The prospect from here over the Moor of Bannoch, one 
of the largest tracts of its kind in Scotland, is wild and 
desolate to a degree ; and if the weather happens to be 
" saft " as the natives put it, the masses of vapour driving 
over the hills add to a scene which, notwithstanding its 
dreariness, has yet a certain fascination to an eye accustomed 
to the green aspect of the southern counties. The road 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



197 



for the ten miles to Inveroran crosses Blackmount, and 
some hard work must be done for the first four miles, but 
a considerable descent of about three miles is made to the 
shores of Loch Tulla, where the Inn of Inveroran is situated. 
It is a comfortable little house, surrounded by pretty 
scenery, and is a great resort of anglers. 

The next stage to Tyndrum is nine miles ; the road 
winding along the shore of Loch Tulla for some distance 
from the Inn. After an easy ride of three miles, a mile or 
so of steep hill has to be walked, and then the road dips 
again into a valley ; after which there succeeds a long rise 
over the hills above Tyndrum, the last two miles being 
down hill. Having reached Tyndrum, where there is a 
large hotel, the roads improve immensely, though wet 
weather makes them rather heavy. 

There are several sharp little ups and downs to Grianlarich, 
where, should the rider have but a short time on his hands, 
and only be able to take a cursory glance at the Trossachs, 
he had better turn off to Ardlui, distant only seven miles ; 
thence he can take the steamboat to Inversnaid, and then ride 
the four miles to Stronachlachar, again taking the steamer 
across Loch Katrine. But as my intention was to stay for 
a short time at Callender, and from there to explore the 
Trossachs, I continued through Grianlarich to Luib Inn 
(twelve miles from Tyndrum), the surface being excellent 
and the road fairly level, and the lochs and the hills of 
Glen Dochart presenting many picturesque views on the 
way. 

From Luib Inn to Eillin on Loch Tay is six miles ; but 
if bound for the Trossachs, a turning to the right two 
miles before the village of Eillin is reached should be 
taken, which leads up over some very rough surface to 
Eillin Station. Thence, through Glen Ogle, there is a 
grand piece of riding, downhill all the way with a smooth 
surface, though loose stones are rather numerous. The 
steep acclivities of the surrounding hills, which become 
narrow as Lochearnhead (10^) is approached, form a 
striking picture. The hotel here is a very good one, and 
the scenery, comprising grand views of Loch Earn, is very 
fine. The blacksmith, a marvel in the local estimation, 
is not skilled in the art of bending cranksw 

The territory made classic by Sir Walter Scott in his 
poem, " The Lady of the Lake," is now entered. Leaving 
Lochearnhead, the road passes through Strathyre, where 
readers of the aforementioned will recollect that "the 
Cross of Fire " was carried with such haste by Angus. The 
surface of the road is remarkably good, being very hard 
and smooth, and the hills easy all the way to Callender (14), 
with the exception of a short and stiff lUtle bit just after 
"Eingshouse Inn" is passed. After plissing through a 
small village. Loch Lubnaig is reached ; and the road con- 
tinues along its eastern side quite close to the water's edge, 
the overhanging trees making a glorious ride, while the 
lofty Ben Ledi is seen rising from the o^osite shore. At 



the southern extremity of the lake is the Pass of Leny, 
formed by two lofty precipices, between which the river 
Leny flows round numerous curves to Callender, the 
foam caused by its rocky bed and the rushing waters 
making a pleasant sight. The village of Eilmahog is now 
passed, and for the remaining mile the road is very bumpy. 

Callender is not much of a place in itself, but the sur- 
roundings are extremely pretty, the hills above the village 
being clothed with pine trees, and the view of Ben Ledi 
from the bridge over the river ia very fine. The " Macgreggor 
Hotel," which is the C.T.C. house, can only be called fair. 
The Trossachs and Loch Eatrine are here within easy acce&s, 
the latter being only ten miles distant, and the " Trossachs 
Hotel," situated in the midst of the most charming scenery 
and only three-quarters of a mile froni Loch Eatrine, is a 
very good place to stay at ; it is expensive, as it enjoys the 
monopoly of the district, but everything is done in first-rate 
style. The road from Callender to the steamboat pier on 
Loch Eatrine (10 miles) is a poor one, from a bicycular 
point of view ; there are numerous sharp little hills, and 
the surface is of a very uneven character — something like 
macadam, and very different from the road to Ijochearnhead. 
The scenery is interesting all the way, especially at the 
Brigg of Turk, where there is a grand view up Glenfinlas ; 
the road at several points winds very close to Lochs Achray 
and Vennachar ; the proximity of the latter I am not likely 
to forget for some little time. An old gentleman from 
Nottingham, who was on a tricycling tour, would insist upon 
my trying his " Humber," notwithstanding my frequent 
protests ; the result was that owing to the greater stiffness 
in the steering, I found myself making straight for the 
lake, and only succeeded in pulling up within an inch of 
a steep rocky slope into the water. The old gentleman, 
as may be imagined, took at least ten minutes to recover. 

Along the northern bank of Loch Eatrine there is a road 
eight miles long to Glengyle, famous as having been tenanted 
at one period by the father of Rob Eoy, which winds, with 
many charming views, round the bays of the lake ; but it 
is quite unrideable, the pedestrian having to make use of 
stepping-stones to cross some of the streams which flow 
down the sides of Ben A'an. 

The whole of this lovely district, generally known as the 
Trossachs, is, for the combined grandeur and softness of its 
beauty, unsurpassed in Scotland, and being, as it is, so 
accessible either from Edinburgh or Glasgow, it should 
certainly be visited if circumstances permit. 

The distance from Callender to Edinburgh is 52 miles, 
all of which is easy going, and a very enjoyable day's 
ride can be made of it, allowing plenty of spare time 
for the various sights on the way, the most notable 
of which is Stirling Castle. Quitting Callender by a 
hard and shaky road, the Highlands are soon left behind, 
and when Doune (8) is reached the scenery bears a very 
different aspect. From the latter place to Stirling (16) 



Digitized by 



198 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



there is a fine broad road^ which a native informed me was the 
finest in Scotland, and so straight, he added, that you can 
see several miles ahead. The surface becomes very rough 
near Stirling, and as you approach the town the lofty rock 
on which the castle is built is seen on the right, and on the 
left is the Wallace Mont, a strange-looking erection. 
Passing through Stirling, the road to the left leads to 
Falkirk (28), its principal features being long and easy 
inclines with a good hard surface, generally rough near 
towns ; the scenery, being bare and open, is tame. 
Thence to Linlithgow (35) the road bears the same good 
character, with the exception of the hills being shorter 
and stiffer. The ruins of the old palace of Linlithgow on 
the banks of a fine sheet of water are worth visiting. The 
surface of the road for the remaining seventeen miles is 
simply perfection, and one can readily understand our 
President's enthusiastic opinion of it, when he remarked 
that it was the finest bit of road he had ever been on. 
After roughing it on the stony and loose roads of the 
Highlands, the sensation of ease and the pleasure of moving 
rapidly along a smooth surface can only be appreciated by 
those who have experienced it. The last two or three miles 
into Edinburgh are rough. 

This beautiful city, aptly termed " Modern Athens," is 
famous alike for the historical interest and the antiquity of 
the Old Town, and the imposing architecture and the 
picturesque views of the New ; but, as the sage Dr. Johnson 
remarked, it is "too well known to admit description." 
Siifiice to say there is enough to occupy the attention for 
several days, and no tour in Scotland would be complete 
without a visit to its splendid capital. 

Having been recommended to ride to Carlisle through 
Moffat, I set out for the latter place, which is 53 miles from 
Edinburgh, Peebles being the only place worth mentioning 
that is passed en route. The road for the first ten miles is 
decidedly hilly and the country bare and uninteresting ; 
when Penicuik is passed there is a rise of about four miles, 
after which it is down hill for the remaining eight miles to 
Peebles (22). The approach to the latter town is extremely 
pretty, as it is situated on the banks of the Tweed, and is 
almost surrounded by lofty hills. 

A somewhat indirect course has now to be pursued, the 
course of the river having to be followed, which flows west- 
ward to Lyne. About a mile from Peebles is Neidpath 
Castle, situated on a steep bank above the Tweed, which 
here winds between steep heights and wooded slopes, and 
affords some very pretty views. After Lyne is passed 
there is about four miles of hilly riding to Stobo. Here a 
comer can be cut by taking a turning to the left after 
passing through the village, the road which goes through 
Drummelzier, being a mile shorter than the one that is 
usually taken through Broughion ; it is a lovely ride, as it 
leads through narrow lanes and crosses and re-crosses the 
Tweed. The Moffat road is struck about two miles south 



of Broughton, whence the road is undulating and the 
surface excellent to the "Crook Inn." There are still 
sixteen miles to Moffat ; the road, said to be a grand one, 
is, I should say, a great fraud. No doubt it was good 
once, and the surface is still in parts like asphalte ; but it is 
strewn with small stones in some places, and in others is 
quite overgrown with grass. As it was rather dark and 
rain was falling when I traversed it, my bad impressions 
of it may be heightened, but I rejoiced greatly when 
the lights of Moffat became visible in the distance. The 
first ten miles from the " Crook Inn " are up hill as far 
as Tweedshaw's, and thence past the Devil's Beef Tub, 
where the|ground descends like a precipice from the side of 
the road to a great depth, it is down hill to Moffat. The 
" Annandale Arms," C.T.C., is recommended. 

The next stage to Carlisle is 45 miles, a very good road 
all the way. Two miles from Moffat is Beattock, and the 
road is undulating and the surface smooth to Lockerbie (16). 
It continues good through Ecclefechan (22) to Longtown 
(36) — the scenery being of a very tame character. The 
remaining nine miles to Carlisle is good, the surface being 
smooth, and nothing in the shape of a hill is met with. 
Here the Scotch Tour may be said to end, though after 
taking the train to Bradford I rode nearly all the way home 
via Wakefield, Doncaster, Newark, Grantham, Stamford, 
and the Great North Eoad. 

In conclusion, I need scarcely say the tour afforded me 
an immense'^amount of pleasure \ the bicycle enabling one 
to see, at a small cost and in a comparatively short time, 
more than most people can accomplish even with a very 
long holiday and an equally long purse. True, there are 
difficulties ; and the Scotch proverb is a true one which 
says, " Muckle pleasure, some pains." But the pains are 
instantly forgotten, while the pleasure lasts for ever, for 
the lovely scenes that so delighted you may be recalled 
over and over again, even to the smallest object — such an 
impression do they leave upon the mind. 

A. Lloyd. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
THE MEMBER'S BROTHER. 
Sir, — In reply to the letter of an "Ex-Member's-Brother," 
whose composition is easily recognisable as emanating from 
the constant pen of a ready writer, I must point out that, 
if I am correct in my surmise of his identity, he is not a 
case in point as he is a Racing Member, and therefore had 
the attraction of racing to encourage him to join. In my 
letter I distinctly stated I was far from wishing to prevent 
a member from bringing his brother to any Club Meeting 
in moderation, and yet an " Ex-Member s-Brother " writes, 
" I imagine * Wy vern * would not wish to exclude the 
Member's Brother from our Meets." Surely he cannot 
have read the letter he refers to. 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



199 



I hope our Captain will excuse me when I say I think he 
has failed to grasp the very evident point of my letter, 
viz. : to attack the abuse of the privilege, and not the 
privilege itself, and I am confident that what I have written 
will not deter the ordinary Member s Brother from joining 
in the pleasure of our Club runs, for he will see at once 
that it is not he who is the subject of this correspondence. 

I cannot understand the argument of the impecuniosity 
that permits an expensive bicycle to be bought and the 
cost of a Club tea to be indulged in, but Vill not allow the 
Club subscription to be paid. 

Yours, etc., Wtvben. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
Sir, — I am all in favour of the visitors — ^member's 
brothers included — receiving every inducement to join our 
runs, but there is one privilege I should like to see con- 
fined exclusively to members, and that is the right of scoring 
attendances for the District whose Club runs they attend. 
The averages made out at the end of each season, if they mean 
anything, are intended to show the relative amount of 
interest taken by each of the Districts in their Saturday 
runs, but with visitors included they are no such criterion. 
Year after year this unreasonable custom is adhered to 
with a strange persistency by most of the District Captains ; 
and yet not altogether strange, for it has its origin in the 
desire to see the District under their charge appear as 
prosperous as possible. In this spirit one or two District 
Captains have gone as far as to turn some of their runs 
into picnics, and with the assistance of their lady friends 
to run up their average to a tremendous extent. 

When blue book statistics are turned into such a ridicu- 
lous farce as this, one may well ask where will matters end. 
Some energetic individual, promoted to the honours of the 
District Captaincy, may shortly appear with a brace of 
infants in a double perambulator in his anxiety to keep up 
his average. 

Surely it is time some hard and fast rule were drawn up, 
under which all the District Captains would be obliged to 
compute these averages not merely for their own delectation, 
but for the benefit and information of the Club generally. 

Yours, etc., ^ 



THE TWENTY MILES PROFESSIONAL 
CHAMPIONSHIP. 

This Championship was run at the Belgrave Grounds, 
Leicester on Saturday evening. At six o'clock, the adver- 
tised time for starting, fully 10,000 spectators had assembled 
on the track, and these numbers were considerably augmented 
during the race. When the signal was given Newton went 
to the front and assumed the lead for the first five miles, 
which were comparatively speaking slow. Lees now made 
the pace very hot, the seventh mile being especially fast, and 
reaching the ninth mile Newton was lapped. The half 



distance was ridden in 30 min. 2 sec. Lees still leading, the 
others being in the following order : — Howell, Wood, 
Weston, and Newton. Lees still continued to force the pace 
and led until the beginning of the last mile, when HoweU 
came with a tremendous burst of speed and led, quickly 
followed by Wood. The three continued to ride at an 
astonishingly fast pace, and no alteration took place until 
half a lap from home, when Wood wrested the lead from 
Howell, and continuing to gain, won by fifteen yards from 
the last-named, who defeated Lees by two yards. The last 
mile was ridden in the remarkably fast time of 2 min. 31| 
sec. Appended are times at each mile, and also the 
duration of each several one

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
Harry Wilson has again created a sensation by lowering 
the existing records from 26 to 38 miles at the fifty miles 
championship race of the Surrey Bicycle Club. In the first 
hour he covered 19 miles 505 yards ; no amateur, excepting 
H. L. Cortis, having previously ridden so far in that time. 
• •*••• 

At 38 miles he slowed down and took refreshment, 
finishing the fifty miles at his ease in 2 hr. 52 min. 55 sec. Had 
he been pressed he would doubtless have done much better. 
As it was, Vesey, his most formidable opponent, was two 

miles and a quarter behind him at the finish. 

««*•*• 

If the times and distances are correct the professionals, 
who hitherto have been beaten out of the field by amateurs, 
have suddenly come out most effectively, and put some of 
the amateur records into the shade. What with over 20^ 
miles in the hour, and the mile in 2 min. 31f sec, we may 
well wonder what will come next. 

The Fbont Whebl Hub.

LONDON TO EDINBURGH. 

On Saturday, the 14th of J uly, I left Beddington with afnend 
to ride to Edinburgh. We rode round London vid Eangston 
and Uxbridge, and stopped at Luton the first night ; then 
on through Bedford, Uppingham, Melton Mowbray, Not- 
tingham, Mansfield to Worksop, where we were stopped by 
the rain ; the roads, as far as Mansfield, were very good, 
and the next morning we rode to Leeds vid Doncaster 
(roads bad), and on Wednesday on to Nelson via Bradford, 
Halifax, and Burnley. We could not go more than six 
miles an hour on account of the roads, which I think 
were the worst I have ever bejQ on. Thursday we 
went on to Kendal througl^^ttl^f and Eirby Lonsdale 
(roads and scenery splendia). Oh Friday through the 
lake district to Keswick, where we spent Saturday and 
Sunday. On Saturday it poured with rain the whole day, 
so we had to stop in the hotel and grumble. On Monday 
we left at 9 o'clock, and rode vid Cockermouth, Carlisle, 
Longtown, and Hawick, to Selkirk (about 94 miles). The 
natives in Lancashire took me for a Salvation Army officer, 
and wanted to know if I was " going to glory." The next 
day we rode vid Peebles to Edinburgh ; the country from 
Carlisle to Edinburgh is very pretty, and the roads are 
grand. As the boat for London did not start until Friday 
evening, we had plenty of time to see Edinburgh, which 
we found well worth seeing. 

We had rather a rough passage home, and our machines 
turned red from the sea-water. We arrived at Gravesend 
at 8.30 on Sunday morning, and rode home from there. 
We always stayed at C.T.C. hotels, which were found very 
comfortable. Altogether we rode about 500 miles, and 
only had one really wet day. Riding time, nine days. 
Wind nearly all the way N. or N. W. 

August 15, 1883. DouOLAS MgRae. 



QUILLS. 

" AristoUe and Pliny deliver it that the porpentine casteth off his qmlUi 
when he would attack, or defend himaelf, but Diosoorides affixmeth it a 
point of folly to believe it."— Sir T. BEOWira. 

Those who saw the Zulu " tooling " his conveyance, con- 
sisting of a tricycle, with a donkey attached, through West 
Wickham on Saturday, all agree that the effect was as 
dazzling as it was refined. 

• ««««« 
There is no little satisfaction in finding the spare treadle 

pin one has carried for months come in useful at last. 

" If you like the pickles, try the sauce." On the same 
principle, those who approve of the " Rucker " bicycle will 
do well to peruse the exhaustive description of the ''Rucker" 
bicycle which appeared in the last issue of The Field. 

• •««•* 

The Editor has returned from his too brief holiday, 



looking pale and worn. He has passed through many 
terrible experiences, including several storms in a teacup, 
and has carried away two jibs, a forestay, bobstay^ and 
other mysterious ropes, besides being in collision, and 
aground, or rather a-mud, seven times. Who shall say 
the Norfolk Broads are destitute of excitement ? 

• • • • • • 

He reports that Yarmouth is given over unto the letting 
and hiring of sociables, it being not uncommon to see half- 
a-dozen in procession of an evening on the Drive, with 
lamps brightly lighted. Tricycles also abound of all ddes, 
so that the bicycle seems quite rare and obsolete. 
«•««•• 

It is with sorrow that I learn that our worthy member 
the energetic secretary of the Clapton Lacrosse Club is about 
to resign the post he has held with such credit to himself 
and such advantage to the Club. 

• ««••• 

Another of our Lacrosse members, a well-known attendant 
for some years at the Club meets, is about to set at naught 
the traditions of the N.E., having so far forgotten himself 
as to contemplate quitting the *' self-denying state " for 
which the District is so noted. 

• •«•*• 

If the controversy about the merits of the celebrated 
grotto, now known as ''Gruggen's remains," continues, 
it will be necessary for the Captain to organise a General 
Meet there, and to afterwards bring the question before a 
General Meeting of the Club. Perhaps photographs of the 
interestmg ruins in question might appear in the Gazbtte.

THE MEMBER'S DOG. 
Who has not read, with bated breath and shuddering 
frame, Captain Marryat's thrilling description of the hair- 
breadth escapes from imminent peril of Snarleyyow ; but 
surely the member's dog is more of a Dog Fiend than the 
celebrated character we have mentioned. If it is possible 
for a living creature to do so, he gets nearer than a hair's 
breadth to the advancing felloe, and appears to take an 
insane delight in scratching his continuation " against the 
indiarubber tyre of one's front wheel. To a nervous man, 
we can think of few things more trying than the company 
of his best friend's favourite animal, which, by a curious 
coincidence, is always of an extremely virtuous character, 
and never dreams of behaving otherwise than as a model 
dog, scorning to give way to the temptations of doing 
anything but follow, without deviation, the meandering 
tracks of his master. This coincidence is always fully 
explained and duly expatiated on before the start 
after we have humbly protested against the obnoxious 
presence, but almost immediately after the start is 
effected a still more curious coincidence follows with 
clockwork regularity ; this virtuous animal, for the first 
time in its mortal career, emulates our Father Adam, 
and falla And what makes it fedl ? Not the irresistible 



influence of lovely woman, but the detestable yelp of 
a — we had almost said another — miserable cur. Away 
it goes at twenty miles an hour, only, however, to stop dead 
in about twenty yards, within a foot or two of the chal- 
lenging quadruped, and directly under our front wheel. 
We clench our teeth, pull the break hard on, offer up a 
small thanksgiving that we are insured in the " Accidental/' 
and eventually shave the penultimate hairs of the personifi- 
cation of virtue. Our friend immediately remarks, "Oh ! don't 
mind Trimmer, he never gets run over, you should not 
bother yourself about him." But that is all very well ; 
our nerves have received a shock which they take a con- 
siderable time to get over, and we have not even the 
consolation of ''six pounds a week." For be it remem- 
bered that the never-ending seconds that proceed the 
actual contact with our Mother Earth are by far the 
most unpleasant of a very unpleasant period in one's 
short span of existence, and we consider that six pounds a 
week for actual wounds is too much, and should be appor- 
tioned half to wounds and half to nerves. It is needless, 
perhaps, to state that this trifling episode, this delightful 
meeting of two sympathetic spirits, descendants no doubt 
of the immortal " Snarleyyow," takes place every mile and 
a half at a moderate computation ; and, when possible, the 
locality chosen is on a sharp descent, such as a steep railway 
bridge, especially when the railway bridge is dotted over 
with a cart or two, of course on its wrong side, a few friendly 
navvies, and the never absent irrepressible school-boy, cap 
in hand. We could dilate at considerable length on this 



Digitized by 



Google 



202 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



inexhaustible Bubject, but our space will not permit, so we 
will conclude with a humble petition to the Committee to 
refuse to permit any dog, which is not a Club dog, to attend 
Club runs, and to enact that the Club dog, or its master 
for it, do pay the usual entrance fee and guinea subscrip- 
tion ; that aforesaid Club dog must be vouched for and 
elected as the laws have decided the human member 
shall be vouched for and elected, and that the fund so 
raised be devoted to soothing the nerves of dog-offended 
members. 

RACE MEETING AT STAMFORD BRIDGE. 

In accordance with the decision of the Committee there 
will be a race meeting at the above grounds on Saturday, 
22nd September, at 3.30 p.m. The events will be : — 

One Mile Club Handicap. Three prizes: gold, silver, 
and bronze medals. 

Twelve Miles District Scratch. Three prizes : bronze 
medals. 

Half-mile Club Scratch. Two prizes : gold and silver 
medals. 

220 yards Flat Race for boys under 16 years of age, 
introduced by members. Three prizes. 

Entry fee for each of the first three events 2s. 6d, and 
for the Flat Race, Is.. The Twelve Miles District Race 



must be ridden on band fide roadsters ; only those members 
allowed to compete who have attended at least eight meets 
throughout the season. Each District to have three starters. 
Entries close on 17th September. A member of the Com- 
mittee has kindly offered the first prize for the flat race. 

Members will be admitted free on production of Club 
Badge. No tickets will be issued ; the admission to the 
outside world will be by payment of Is. at the gates. 

It will be seen from the foregoing that an entirely new 
departure is being undertaken. The afternoon is intended 
to be a purely Club affair, and every member taking part in 
the races or turning up on the ground will make it more so. 
It will serve all the purposes of a General Meet, without the 
bother of haviug to get to the destination by riding all round 
London, and will have one great attraction which the said 
General Meet is void of, viz., the presence of fellow-members' 
sisters, cousins, and aunts. E. S. Price, Hon. Sec. 

BOYS' RACE. 
I trust that members are arranging for all their brothers 
under 16 to enter for the flat race, and I hope it is under- 
stood, though the official notice does not so state, that 
this is a handicap, the handicap, of course, being chiefly 
by age. 

Theo. Godlee, Captain's Substitute.

SATURDAY RUNS. 



N.E. DiOTBIOT. 

Ist September. The weather was bad, and got worse ; 
but five is a poor attendance. Four of them left the 
Comer about the usual hour, and proceeded by the very 
usual route of the level crossing, and Chigwell to Passing- 
ford Bridge, encountering by the way a herd of wild 
cattle which threatened them dissolution, but fortunately 
abstained ; the wind was supposed to be behind them — it 
wasn't ; but that is a minor matter. The pace was, as usual, 
the best pace for the slowest rider, but the slowest rider rose 
to the occasion and wasn't identified much. Very little rain 
interfered before Navestock was reached. Tea was served 
in a sort of cupboard, and included, to the astonishment 



of the party, «An«i|W— quite a new thing in Club teas. 
Baldwin, who rode down hard and late, turned up just as 
the teapot was empty, but an early start was effected about 
7.30 in pitch dark and rain. A somewhat medium pace, 
as the leaders could not see the way, was adopted on the 
way home; Baldwin turning down to Chigwell Lane Sta- 
tion, and it is opined crossing the bows of, and delaying the 
District at the level crossing. Walthamstow was safely 
reached about 9.30. Present : Baldwin, S. G Hough, 
Kelsey, Lloyd, and Thbo. Godlbb, District Captain. 

N.W. DlSTMOT. 

Two members met at " Jack Straw's Castle " for the run 
to Elstree, and were undecided as to whether they should 
carry out the run or not ; but the expectation of meeting 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



203 



a large contingent of the W. proved too strong, and they 
started amidst adverse elements. A stoppage was made at 
Hendon Station for reinforcements, but all to no purpose, 
and the District rode via Brockley Hill (which is now in a 
very good condition) and Buahey Heath, reaching Elstree a 
little before six. A solitary W. man, in the person of 
Dr. Langmore, was found just starting back, so that our 
fond hopes of a large company were rudely dashed to the 
ground. It did not appear good enough to stop to tea as 
the weather was so threatening, although Elgood and a 
friend just then came up and decided to stop ; so leaving 
these two we started back against a gale and rain, reaching 
home about 7.30. I have heard since that no other 
member attended the run. Our D.C. was unavoidably 
absent through illness (?). Present: Elgood, H. B. Spencer, 
one visitor, and E. S. Pbiob. 

Another Account 
I started with a friend about 3.30, and rode along the 
Pinchley Road as far as Whetstone (no one turning up at 
Tally Ho 1 Comer), where we turned oflF to Totteridge, and 
rode through the lanes to Edgeware Bury, arriving at 
Elstree at 6 p.m. Here we met Dr. Langmore (W.), Price, 
and Spencer, none of whom, however, waited for tea. After 
a fairly-served meal at the "Red lion" we started for 
home, but were thoroughly drenched before riding half a 
mile. Happily the distance was short, and we got home at 
nine o'clock, apparently none the worse for our ducking. 

F. M. Elgood. 



W. District. 

1st September. The run to Elstree was anything but 
a success, for our only representative was Langmore, who 
rode out direct, he meeting a contingent of the N.W. 
there. However, rain threatening, it was decided to return 
without the usual tea-fight, so that anyone who went 
down late had the felicity to find no one there. Being 
delayed from starting unavoidably at the usual time, I 
endeavoured to make up for lost time by starting at 5.30, 
but rain descending before reaching Stanmore, I was forced 
to return home, though much against my will. Present : 
J. W. Langmore. H. C. Pigott, District Captain. 

P.S. — As next Saturday is the run to Woking Heath to 
meet the S.W., I hope as many as possible will join ua 
A start will be made at 3.45 punctually, and the route 
followed will be by Kingston, Esher, Gobham, Byfleet, to 
the "Maybury Inn," which is situate in middle of the 
Byfleet Common, two miles from Woking and five from 
Ripley, amid beautiful surroundings and extensive views. 
I am confident that all who pay this charming spot a visit 
will be amply repaid for the trouble of getting there. 

N.B.— No grotto. 

S.E. District. 
There was rather a poor muster at Addington on Saturday 
for the paperchase run to Downe. The threatening aspect 



of the weather in the afternoon no doubt prevented the 
pack from turning out in numbers proportionate to the 
occasion. The village clock pointed to twenty minutes 
after five, when the two hares, J. C. Oswald and the D.C, 
started down through the village before the wind towards 
Wickham. A sharp turn to the right at the cross roads 
took them up past Wickham Court into an intricacy of cross 
country lanes, more or less overgrown with grass, and 
sprinkled with stones. What with numerous false tracks, 
even the most sagacious hounds were unable to keep on the 
scent, and consoled themselves with a little cross country 
work, each on his own account. Two of the pack having 
made up their minds beforehand as to the way the hares 
ought to go, found themselves in the anomalous position 
of arriving before them. It is hardly necessary to add that 
their attentions had been exclusively confined to the direct 
road, and of course the D.C, on hearing that they had 
done fast time, booked them for the Stamford Bridge event 
forth with. Soon after our arrival the rain, which had been 
coming down in fits and starts during the run, began to 
show that it was really in earnest, and we were not sorry 
to find food and shelter at the *' George Inn." The wind 
howled outside, and the rain rattled against the window 
pane, so we put ofi* the evil moment as long as possible, but 
eventually made a start about half-past eight. As most of 
the party were, to a certain degree, blown home, the rain 
did not signify so much, but the sole representative of the 
Sevenoaks contingent must have had, it is to be feared, 
rather a bad time of it. Present : S. Herbert, J. C. Oswald, 
W. C. Oswald, F. B. Smith, S. P. Smith, Whealler,' F. H. 
Williams, and R. G. Francis, District Captain. 



Next Saturday the Croydon Division will leave the Fire 
Station at 4.30 for East Grinstead, and as a great many of 
the Crawley candidates intend coming, there is every chance 
of a good meet. R. G. Francis, District Captain. 



S.W. District. 
The uncertain and threatening appearance of the weather 
seems to have deterred the few remaining well-disposed 
members of the District from meeting at Kingston Station 
on Saturday last. To avoid the chance of once more 
becoming the victim of circumstances and having to carry 
out the run alone, in the painful event of members being 
either away for their holidays or for other causes unable to 
lend their much-coveted assistance, I had secured a com- 
panion by impressing a brother into the District service, 
mounted upon a borrowed plume or hired tricycle of very 
indifferent pattern, and one which it was found could only 
be driven at much personal inconvenience ; we therefore, 
for obvious reasons, generously withhold the name of the 
maker ! After waiting at Kingston until 4.45 p.m. and no 
member then appearing, we proceeded over Kingston Bridge 
in the direction of Sunbury. Upon nearing Hampton 



Digitized by 



204 LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



Court there were distant strains of music issuing from these 
well-known pleasure grounds which aroused our curiosity, 
and seemed to claim attention. Upon closer inspection the 
band proved to be that of the " Queen's Own Hussars," 
engaged in rendering several Scotch selections in a very 
pleasing and exceptional manner ; a habit which, it was 
understood, was of weekly occurrence ! At the termination 
of this interesting entertainment the outlook appeared any- 
thing but promising, and as rain began to seriously threaten 
the intended district expedition, Sunbury was finally aban- 
doned, and the return journey commenced, by Bushey 
Park. Present : G. H. Qruggen (visitor) and 

B. M. Gbuogek, District Captain. 

The run next Saturday is to the " Maybury Inn," Woking, 
when I hope that we shall have a good muster to meet the 
West. ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
ATTENDANCE. 
Sib,— It may be a comfort to your correspondent 
to know that the whole question to which he refers has 
been under the consideration of the Committee, and has 
been by them referred to a Sub-Committee, who are 
expected shortly to make their report, in doing which they 
will no doubt take his remarks into consideration. It is, 
however, refreshing to find anyone so completely satisfied 
as to what the averages in question are " intended to show," 
and who boldly states his opinion, though he admits he is 
entirely opposed to the views of "most of the District 
Captains," who might be supposed to have some experience 
and, for that matter, to the views of Committees for some 
years past. — ^Yours, etc.. One op the Sub-Commttteb.

N.B. DISTRICT. 
Sib, — It may be an interesting speculation who I shall 
select for the District Kace, but I am bound to say that if 
only three men send in their names it is easy. I wish those 
who are willing to ride would let me know. I hear of them, 
but not from them. Theo. Qodlbe, District Captain. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette, 
RACE MEETING, 
gut, — As I find myself clothed with a temporary autho- 
rity, I think it right to point out to members that, having 
regard to the strict privacy of the occasion, there is no need 
for a formal entry form for this meeting, but that a letter to 
the Secretary, with the half crowns, will be amply sufficient. 

I also wish to urge on members the importance of entering 
for the races ; they need not be afraid of unreasonably short 
starts, as the Race Committee will be sure to know them. 

If the D.C.'s will make a point of collecting entries next 
Saturday, they might save members some trouble. — Yours, 
Theo. Godlee, Captain's Substitute.

THE CODIFICATION OF AMATEUR LAW. 
Althoagh every different class of sport must of necessity 
have certain rales to meet the special needs arising in its 
own particular case, yet it is quite possible to frame a code 
of laws which, such differences excepted, jsball be applicable 
to all amateur sport alike. To take an example, it is neces- 
sary for every sport to have a definition of an amateur, and 
surely a single definition would answer the purposes of all 
classes of athletes. At present we have at least six associa- 
tions, namely, the Amateur Rowing Association, the 
Amateur Athletic Association, the National Cyclists' Union, 
the Swimming Association of Great Britain, the Amateur 
Boxing Association, and the National Skating Association, all 
of which differ more or less in their definitions of an amateur. 
With regard to the first named of these, a very great and 
at present apparently insuperable obstacle exists to its 
definition being rendered similar to those of most of the 
other associations ; but our present purpose is not to enter 
into a discussion on this point, but to glance at the possi- 
bility of a common set of rules for at least the A. A.A., the 
N.C.U., and the N.Sw.A. The differences between the 
codes of these associations other than those arising from 
the exigencies of the particular sport, consist chiefly in the 
wording of the various rules, the only important differences 
being that the A.A.A. prohibit their members from com- 



BNTS. 

FAOB 



Jottings on the Midlands 210* 

All Round the Wheel 210 

Correspondence 211 

Quilla 212 

Racing Fixtures 212 

Notice to Correspondents 212 



peting at sports not advertised to be held under their 
rules, and also forbid the introduction of professional 
contests at amateur athletic meetings ; whilst the N.C.U. 
reserve to themselves the right of authorising Amateur and 
Professional Races. To show the inconvenience of these 
differences, let us take the following case. Sports are held 
at which there is a professional race, and a competitor 
enters for a running and a bicycle race and starts in both ; 
the A.A.A., in accordance with their rules, suspend him, 
and although ho has broken no regulation of the N.C.U., 
they are bound by agreement with the former association 
to recognise this suspension. Obviously there is an absurdity 
here, and the sooner it is rectified the better. Any com- 
plication of rules ought to be avoided, as at county meetings, 
competitors, who perhaps only race once a year, cannot be 
expected to be up in all the niceties of amateur law as metro- 
politan athletes, who live, so to speak, in an atmosphere 
of it, are supposed to be. A simple and concise code of rules 
is what is needed, and the sooner our legislators turn their 
attention to this subject the better it will be for athletics in 
general. The cordial relations at present existing between 
the three Associations ought to render the task an easy one, 
and the approaching dead season of the year suggests that 
it is a convenient time in which to take up the subject.

on Monday 10th instant, the resignations of Messrs. J. T. 
Johns, William Johns, and C. A. Smith were accepted.

SATURDAY RUNS. 

N.R DlSTEICT. 

Sept. 8th. A very wet morning has a tendency to turn 
men's minds to other pursuits, however fine an afternoon it 
may ultimately be, and this may account for onlpr four 
members leaving Syborn's Comer. They were joined for 
about a mile by T. G. Nevill, who, we fear, hardly scored 



an attendance, as he was not actually propelling himself. 
Going round by Whip's Cross to try to meet someone, the 
Redbridge road was reached by way of Snaresbrook, then, 
proceeding by the most recent invention to Hog Hill, 
Romford was taken in the flank, and a sharp turn back 
by the watersplash brought the party to Beacontree 
Heath in time to meet S. P. Smith on his way to leave it. 
Thus reinforced by the Blackheath Division, the District 
proceeded to its destination, the "Bell" at Upminster. 
Here Baldwin, who had ridden down early, had very kindly 
ordered tea, and we were soon at work, and by the same 
reason ready for an early start. This was effected after an 
anxious wait in hopes of seeing the Treasurer, who was 
reported to be coming. The return route was taken by the 
Romford road ; Baldwin, who had tried it, perhaps wisely, 
took the train at Romford. Certainly the road was in 
parts at its very worst, even excepting the market carts, 
which did not add to the comfort. At Ilford the party 
separated. Smith leaving for Woolwich, *and the balance 
going by Redbridge, and comfortably and punctually 
reaching home. Present : Baldwin, Eelsey, Lloyd, E. D. 
Sewell, and Theo. Godlee, District Captain. 



N.W. DiSTEIOT. 

Sept. 8th. Three members turned up at Tally Ho! 
at the appointed time, and after waiting twenty minutes 
for the D.C., who did not arrive, they started down the 
North road against a stiff breeze. As we were late we did 
not think it good enough to go as far as Digswell Hill, the 
appointed destination, so at the sixteenth milestone turned 
to the right and made for Northaw, where a very plain tea 
was obtained. A start was made at eight o'clock and all 
went well to Barnet, where my handle>bar suddenly came 
in half. However, by walking the hills, this did not 
prevent my riding home. Silk overtook us the London side 
of Barnet, having been down the North road to look for us. 
Present : Hindley, Jackson, W. Silk, Jun., and 

W. J. Williams, for District Captain. 



Another Account. 
Being unable to arrive at Tally Ho ! Comer before five 
o'clock, I found the attendance very scarce ; so proceeded 
alone as far as Barnet, where, feeling unequal to riding 
any further, returned home meditating on the energy of 
our "District." 



Next Saturday the run is to Harrow — "Jack Straw's 
Castle," 3.45. A. H. LowN, District Captain. 



W. District. 

Sept. 8th. It was with mingled feelings of disappoint- 
ment and vengeance that I turned my back upon Savernake 
Cottage last Saturday punctually at four o'clock. Need I 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



207 



add that I was alone, for not one member out of the whole 
district turned out for one of the prettiest runs of the 
season. However, I rode on with a hopeful feeling that I 
should only have the eight miles to Kingston to ride in 
solitude, for I had calculated upon seeing a goodly company 
of S.W., headed by their archseologian. Alas ! what is the 
use of anticipating such pleasures in the inidst of so much 
uncertainty. The only visible being in the garb of a cyclist 
was E. W. Lumley, whom I had invited to attend as a 
visitor, and to accompany us on our run. I could only make a 
few improbable excuses to him for the absence of the two 
districts ; but what could excuses do to atone for the 59 
who were absent out of a possible 60 ^members. I would 
remind the members of the West of the extreme pleasure it 
affords me to carry out a run in solitude, whereas I had 
anticipated pleasant companionship. I rode from Kingston 
to Esher, Hersham, BytLeet, and on to Woking, where I 
arrived at six o'clock. After waiting half an hour for tea, 
hoping someone would come on, during which time I 
wandered over the heath, I sat dowi^ to a solitary and 
frugal repast. At seven o'clock I started for Bipley (5 miles), 
at which place I stayed until ten o'clock, after a pleasant 
and select musical evening. The run from Ripley was 
pleasant as usual, and I arrived at my destination in safety 
before midnight 

On Saturday next the run is to Harrow to meet the 
S.W. and N.W., but there is also a counter attraction in 
the S.E., where great interest is being taken in the Crawley 
run, and all will be welcomed — who care to go — by 
the S.E. D.C. An unexpected visit into Warwickshire will, 
I fear, prevent me from attending the run. 

Harold G. Piqott, District Captain* 



S.E. District. 
There ought to be little doubt that a fine day in early 
autumn is the day above all others for a ride. This opinion 
was not, however, largely shared by members of the S.E. on 



Saturday. Those who managed to tear themselves away 
from home and domestic pursuits, were rewarded by having 
one of the pleasantest runs of the season. Five men left 
the Fire Station at half-past four for East Grinstead. 
Crowds of tricycles were the chief feature along the road to 
Caterham. This was owing to the Fifty Miles Tricycle Road 
Championship, which was in course of being brought off 
along the road through Godstone, Westerham to Ightham and 
back. Marriott seemed to have just arrived, and we were 
informed by Hillier that he was the winner. Some of our 
party elected to go by Tilburstow, which was surmounted 
by Sidney Herbert — to judge from a distant view — in 
excellent form. Church and Williams were waiting 
for us at the "Railway Arms," and shortly after J. C. 
Oswald, who had been exploring in the Turner's Hill 
country, came in and made up our total to nine. We were 
sorry to miss Wyndham and the O'Reilly, as well as one or 
two other habituSs at this run, from the festive board ; still 
it did not prevent those who were present from enjoying an 
excellent repast. At eight o'clock the party showed signs 
of breaking up, and shortly after McRae, Williams, and 
the D.C, anxious to make the most of a young moon, left 
on their way to Brighton. Present : Church, S. Herbert, 
McRae, J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, F. B. Smith, F. H. 
Williams, J. B. Young, and 

R. G. Fbaitgis, District Captain. 



CRAWLEY. 

The Croydon Division will leave the Fire Station, 
St. Katharine Street, at 4.30 next Saturday, and meet 
the Blackheath men (who will come by 3.30 train from 
Ladywell) at the junction of the Brighton and East 
Grinstead at 4.50. The start will take place at five o'clock 
punctually. Tea at the " George Inn," Crawley, at 6.30. 
As this is the last run but one of this season, I hope all 
who can, possibly, will be present. 

R. G. Francis, District Captain.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



A TRIP TO DEVONSHIRE. 
I had intended riding down to Devonshire on my bicycle, 
but at the last moment a friend of mine was taken with the 
sudden desire to make a trip on a double tricycle, and so 
persuaded me to accompany him. Starting on Monday 
afternoon, July 30, we made our way through Hounslow 
and Staines, being delayed by numerous showers of rain, 
to the " Catherine Wheel " at Ilgham. After being well 
entertained here, we rode up the long hill out of the town, 
and after a distance of 3^ miles reached the " Wheatsheaf 
Hotel," Virginia Water (24 J), where we arrived shortly before 
ten p.m. The servants of the establishment had evidently 
been celebrating the close of the London season, and con- 
sequent cessation of their labours, by indulging not wisely 
but too well, and we were several times alarmed during the 
night by unwonted thuds as of beer barrels falling down 
stairs, which we subsequently ascertained were nothing 
more than the uncertain steps of unsteady "roysterers" 
laboriously making their way to their respective chambers. 
Rising early from our uneasy couches, after breakfast and 
a look at the pretty lake we proceeded through Bagshot 
and Blackwater to the Hartford Bridge Flats, where we 
encountered a tremendous storm of rain, which consider- 
ably delayed us and gave us a thorough "ducking," there 
being no cover, except furze bushes, for several miles. 
The roads from this point were such heavy going, although 
in the ordinary way I should imagine the surface to be 
perfect, that we were not sorry to put up for the night at 
a place called Overton (56^) about eight miles beyond 
Basingstoke. Here we made a great reputation for ourselves 
in consequence of the dashing pace we came down the steep 
and long hill into the town, feeling the utmost confidence 
in our mount. Sundry remarks as to our machines being 
the best sort of thing to travel on, came to our ears and 
were received with dignity on our parts, as we chuckled to 
ourselves that they might have altered their opinion had 
they seen us slowly pushing up some of the long and heavy 
hilk we encountered earlier in the day. 

After a bathe in the brook, which reminded me of the 
one at Chenies, except that it was not quite so deep, we 
rode to Andover (66i) to breakfast, during which we took 
the opportunity of having the tyre of one of the driving 
wheels cemented on. After much delay we made a start 
and reached Ludgershall (74), where care should be taken 
to turn to the right in the middle of the town. From here, 
the road lies along the border of Salisbury Plain, and with 
a strong head wind a more monotous and uninteresting ten 
miles cannot be imagined. However, on reaching Upavon, 
the road and country improve and we kept up a fair jxace 
to Devizes. Here we were well looked after at the " Castle 
Inn," and after dinner and a look about town, we rode to 
Melksham (lOOj) a distance of seven miles. The road 
being in good condition and with favourable gradients, we 
did our fastest time, accomplishing the distance in just over 



the half hour. The people at the " King's Arms " were 
very attentive with the exception of the ostler, who told us 
he had never cleaned a tricycle and was'nt going to do so 
now ; " however, we soon got a pail of water and showed 
him the way, much to that worthy's disgust. The following 
morning we were treated to a splendid swim in the river 
Avon and then started for Box, where we breakfasted with 
some friends, enjoying a long and gradual run down into 
the pretty little village. We arrived at Bath (112^) about 
11.30, and spent some time in looking around the Abbey 
and the old baths, which have just been brought to light by 
recent excavations. 

We had been recommended to take the lower Bristol 
road out of Bath instead of going direct to Wells through 
Radstock, as the roads and country are far more interesting. 
This we endeavoured to do, but took a wrong turning, and 
rode down a regular precipice which I should have been 
very sorry to have encountered so suddenly on anything 
but a double tricycle. Walking up a corresponding hill, we 
reached Gorston and the right road again, but the surface 
did not improve much, and the country began to get very 
wild. At High Littleton we walked down the steep hill, 
which comes very unexpectedly into the village at the 
bottom. The tower of the church at Chewton Mendip is a 
fine example of Somersetshire towers, and should not be 
missed by those interested in architecture. 

We now commenced to ascend the north side of the 
Mendips, and had it not been for two delightfully refreshing 
springs of the clearest and coolest water, I think in the hot 
sun we should have felt it rather more tedious than we did. 
However, the country was very pretty, and the panorama 
which was presented to us above Wells was such as is rarely 
seen. The country is visible for miles round, and we stood 
some minutes on the top of the hill lost in admiration. 
There is a B.U. danger board on the hill into the city, 
which is extremely steep, and obliged us to walk. Here 
(132i) we stopped the night at the "Star" Hotel (C.T.C.), 
which deserves every recommendation. We stayed until 
1 o'clock the next day, looking over the magnificent 
cathedral and chapter-house, the vicar's close, bishop's 
palace, etc., and after the purchase of a few photographs, 
regretfully left this pretty place to wend our way to Glaston- 
bury (137f), scarcely less interesting and historical. We 
spent some time there surveying the ruined abbey, the 
abbot's bam and kitchen, and then returned to luuch at 
the " Old George Inn," an extremely picturesque house, 
built originally for a hospital for the pilgrims who came to 
the abbey, when there was not room to lodge them in the 
abbey buildings. 

From here to Bridgewater (153^) the road is fairly level 
and of a very pretty character running along the top of the 
Polden HiUs. There is rather a steep hill to descend about 
two miles before Bridgewater, where we very nearly ran 
over a duck! The road to Taunton (164iJ ^ N. 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



209 



Petherton and Bath Pool is a deep red colour and has a 
very good sarface, so that we were soon comfortably 
quartered in "Clarke's Hotel" (C.T.C.) in front of the^ 
market place, which we found very satisfactory. The next 
day (Saturday) was market day and we amused ourselves 
until noon with watching the eccentricities of the cattle 
and pigs. The church of St Mary should on no account 
be missed as it has a splendid tower and a very fine double- 
aisled interior altogether, it is about the b&st parish 
church in Somerset. The road to Wellington (171 is 
mostly up hill. About a couple of miles oflF the road, there 
is an obelisk erected to the memory of the Iron Duke, who 
took his title from this place, but it can be plainly seen 
from the main road. There is no great interest in the 
remaining part of the road to Exeter (19d|) vid CoUumpton, 
which is rather up and down. At Broad Clist we made a 
deviation of a mile to look up some cider works we had 
heard a great deal about, and were disgusted to find no one 
there to give us a sample, for we were a little thirsty. 

At the " Bude Hotel," Exeter, we were very comfortable, 
and after service at the Cathedral the next morning we 
rode through some of the most hilly and picturesque 
country we had seen, and reached our destination (Paignton 
228) by way of Exminster, Dawlish, 'Deignmouth (212), 
Newton Abbot and Torquay (225). For some miles be- 
tween Kenton and Teignmouth the road runs close by the 
estuary of the river Exe and along the coast ; and the sea 
breezes and pretty views rendered this not the least 
delightful part of our ride. From Teignmouth, the direct 
road through Shalden is so rough and hilly that we were 
recommended to go round by Newton Abbot, a detour of 
some six miles. 

We stayed at none but C.T.C. hotels and were very well 
satisfied with them on the whole, especially at Wells and 
Exeter. The roads we encountered were very fair, though 
rather rough in some parts of Devon and Somerset. There 
can be no doubt that for those who wish to ride quietly 
small distances, there is nothing like a double tricycle for 
touring (although I myself should be the very last to forsake 
the two wheeler), and the advantage that they can with 
perfect security and comfort be ridden down hills which 
would be dangerous to any bicyclist who did not know them, 
is one to be considered. The only hills we found it 
advisable to dismount in descending were those into High 
Littleton, Wells and Teignmouth, and no doubt we could 
have ridden these with safety had either of us been better 
acquainted with our steed ; as it was, my companion had 
never been on a bicycle or tricycle in his life, and the latter 
was also a totally new experience to Juggins Junb. 



AN AIMLESS BAMBLE. 
The deluge of foreign tours which has lately swept 
through the pages of the Gazette, has induced me to send 
up a few notes on a six day's ride through England, from 



which I have just returned, in order that posterity may not 
be led to believe that the whole of the L.B.C. are unable to 
find any enjoyment in the roads and scenery of their native 
land. 

I left South Norwood on Friday, August 3rd, at 8.30, a 
later hour than the one at which I usually begin my first 
day's ride, but as I was going alone, I thought I might as 
well take things easily. My intention was to ride " some- 
where up North," a phrase which greatly took my fancy by its 
delightful vagueness, and the unlimited option it left me of 
turning back when I pleased. I had a hazy sort of idea that 
I might turn up at the Lakes if wind and weather proved 
favourable ; but I was far from having any fixed plan, and 
the sole determination to which I clung was to do a new 
road wherever it was possible. My luggage was contained 
in a M.I. P., which, with judicious stuffing, held a complete 
change of clothes with the exception of a coat,. all articles 
necessary for. the night, a map or two, and a variety of 
" smokements " — an item quite as necessary to the full 
enjoyment of a tour as any of the former ones. I found, 
however; that the bulk of the bag considerably interfered 
with mounting, inasmuch as I could only just grasp my 
handles, which are slightly kinked forward ; and even when 
I held them with the tips of my fingers after the manner 
of a billiard cue, the bag bounded about on my chest in a 
way rather suggestive of the after efiects of a heavy supper. 

I had to dismount twice on the way to London, owing 
to the erratic course pursued by two butchers in their carts 
— one article of whose rule of the road seems to be " when 
driving fast, shut both eyes and pull each rein alternately ; " 
at least this is the only way in which I can account for their 
peculiarly serpentine movements. In Grosvenor Place I was 
unlucky enough to encounter a procession of— say 40,000 — 
school children, all yelling like nightmares, who were going 
for a day at the seaside ; this necessitated a third dismount 
and a wait of several minutes till they had all gone by. I 
did not wish those children a pleasant excursion, on the 
contrary, I rather hoped they would all — well never mind, 
but the sea is a dangerous element. 

I was much struck with the improvements at Hyde Park 
Corner from a bicyclist's point of view ; in fact, from being 
one of the worst crossings in London one can now ride 
through it as easily as round a village green, to which indeed 
the whole place now beans a certain resemblance, the " Iron 
Duke " looking, to an imaginative mind, not unlike a colos- 
sal Quy Fawkes — rather uglier than the majority — already 
placed on a pjrre in time for the 5th of November. [Mr. 
Shaw-Lefevre may make what use of this hint he pleases.] 

After being considerably bumped along the Finchley New 
Road, I reached Barnet, where I halted for five minutes and 
had some shandygaff, the main result being that I slum- 
bered sweetly all the way to St. Albans, though there was 
a slight head wind which did its best to keep me awake. 
At the last-mentioned place I took the Luton road for Bed- 



Digitized by 



210 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



ford, which I found very good, but the heat being rather 
oppressive, I took the opportunity of a slight rise to dis- 
''mount, and lie on my back to admire the view as seen 
through the medium of tobacco smoke. Two miles out of 
Luton there is a slight rise, which continues to the 
verge of the town, there being a sharp descent at the finish. 
I put up at the " George "—I said before I was lazy— and 
sat in the garden for an hour or so, eating sandwiches and 
reading the paper. On leaving the town there is an almost 
imperceptible rise for three or four miles, with a corres- 
ponding descent, the surface being fairly good all the way ; 
the road is then level for several miles, till at Clophill there 
is a rise of about half a mile in length, with a steep and 
loose bit at the top, thence into Bedford is more or less 
level. Just before entering the town I passed a fat old 
farmer with about two hundred sheep, all or any of which 
he was very anxious for me to purchase; and on my 
pointing out to him that I should have some difficulty 
in taking even one with me, he offered to send it 
after me piecemeal — say a leg to Northampton, a shoulder 
to Nottingham, and so on. Declining his tempting 
offer, I made my way through the town on the road to 
Northampton ; I had only been this road once before, and 
that was early in the morning after a sleepless night, so that 
my recollections of it were the reverse of favourable. I was 
therefore agreeably surprised to find it very good for the 
first seven miles, irfter which it slightly deteriorated. As 
the evening was now closing in, I determined to go two or 
three miles out of my way to Olney — ^a very quiet little 
town, where the inhabitants, to judge by their appearance, 
subsist chiefly on soporifics. I put up at the " Bull " — a 
not uncomfortable inn, which had, I should imagine, in 
common with many others, raised its tariff in order to 
assimilate it to that proposed by the B.T.C. I drank vast 
quantities of milk and soda at supper, which is, in my 
opinion, far and away the best drink after a hot day's ride. 
Distance for first day, 72 miles. 

(To be continued), 

JOTTINGS ON THE MIDLANDS. 

The road from Derby to Lichfield (24 miles) is very 
nearly dead flat, fairly pretty country, barring the billiard 
table nature of things. The beer of Burton, the birthplace 
of malt and hops, resembles the relationship of fish to the 
seaside. The fish is all sent up to London, and you get it 
back just a day later than you eat your sprats in town. 
The same with beer at Burton ; it's a trifle mild, suggesting 
that the best has been sent away, and that the reputation 
of the place will keep the thousand and one inns going for 
many years to come. 

And this is so, for of course I should have drunk the 
beer, thirsty or not. Luckily I was thirsty, but the result 
would have been the same in either case. The amount of 
level ciossings in Burton is a caution ; I suppose for trucks 



to carry off some of the mountains of cades one sees lining 
the railway. 

Lichfield is a nasty place; durty and squalid. The 
"George" certainly is moderate in price and very com- 
fortable. The cathedral has a good east end, the rest is 
not much to brag of. The attempt at a cathedral close, 
consisting of a few oldish brick houses — one of them the 
bishop's palace— with a lot of pretty creepers about, is cer- 
tainly quaint. 

Coleshill, between Lichfield and Eenilworth, has a very 
steep hill in it. Going from London you had better walk 
down it, unlesa you've been crossed in love, and have 
insured in the new Cyclists' Assurance Society. 

Coleshill to Kenilworth, by Stone Bridge (where you 
cross the Coventry and Birmingham road), is a lovely ride, 
only rather flat; distance somewhere about ten. The 
whole thing, Lichfield to Leamington, through Coleshill, 
Stone Bridge, Kenilworth, and Warwick, is only six-and- 
thirty. Now don't tell me I went out of my way to go 
round by Warwick to Leamington. I know that ; but there 
is such a thing as a laudable desire to do a new road, 
though it is only two miles. 

Kenilworth to Coventry (5) is a glorious avenue of mag- 
nificent trees— the finest thing I know— go and see it 
yourself, and then call me liar if you can. See St. 
Michael's Church in Coventry, also the old Hall close by. 
The B.T.C. house here (Knight's) is Temperance ; I need 
not say I didn't go inside. The " Craven Arms " is very 
fairly comfortable. I think these are the only two decent 
sized inns in the place. If anyone hasn't got Howard's 
Boads of England and Wales, published by Letts, King 
William Street, let him get it at once ; price 6s., or with 
map of England 6s. It's a marvel of information about 
pretty well every road, direct and cross, surface, hills, 
scenery, B.T.C. and other inns, and a splendid index. It 
ought to be more known than it is. L. Sheddbr. 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
It having been brought under the notice of the executive 
of the N.C.U. that the man-holes to the sewers in the main 
road, Merton, projected above the surface of the road and 
were consequently .dangerous to cyclists, application has 
been successfully made to have the road raised to their 
level 

«••••« 

It was a pity that the north-westerly wind which blew in 
strong gusts across the Surbiton track on Saturday afternoon 
did not fall a little sooner than it did 

As it was, Wilson was obliged to start, and he made a 
very plucky effort to beat time. The first mile was done in 
3 min. If sec, but he gradually dropped behind time. 
The fourth and fifth miles were fast, and at half-time he 
was only 180 yards behind. At eleven miles he began to

tire, and three-qnarteis of a mile farther on he gave np^
