• ••••• 

The One Mile Invitation Handicap held daring the 
afternoon produced a splendid finish. Thorn (15), who 
started as the fastest loser, beating Gassall (140) by a foot. 
Speechly third, a foot behind Cassall, and Gaskell (scratch) 
the samedistance distance behind Speechly. Such a splendid 
finish must have been very gratifying to the hard-working 
N.C.U. Handicapper, who is joyful at the prospect of being 
"within measurable distance" of a quiet Saturday afternoon. 

The Twenty-Five Miles Professional Championship has 
been won by Wood of Leicester, who on nine successive 
occasions has proved himself superior to Howell the 
ex-champion. The policy of the latter was to induce Wood 
to force the pace, but though he reduced his pace to a 
crawl — so much so, that the twenty-fourth mile took four 
and a half minutes — ^he could not induce the Leicester man 
to lead, and it was not till the last lap that they commenced 
to race, when the latter had the race in hand and won by 
five yards. 

• «•••• 

The Fifty Miles Championship Tricycle Road Ride 
resulted in a victory for T. R. Marriott, who won by about 
four miles from G. Smith, W. Bourdon being third. Some 
interest was shorn from the contest by the breakdown of 
Lowndes' machine, when he had only ridden six miles. 

• ••••• 

Nixon could get no nearer than eighth. It is a noticeable 
fact that only three Humbers were ridden, and these secured 
the first three places. 

«•«•*« 

According to the Cyclist, the Speedwell B.C. cleared 
£200 over their meeting in aid of the charities of Bir- 
mingham last week. 

• « • • « 

The Five Miles Invitation Race, the most interesting 
event on the programme, was won by Chambers, who beat 
lUston by a length, the latter being usually more at home 
on a calm day. The Front Whebl Hub. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sib, — As last week the " Potterer " was left on deck in an 
unprotected state, with the fierce light of public opinion 
beating on him, I crave space to set him right with the in- 
telligent and generous L.B.C. public. 

I regret that " Dodo " has dashed aside the olive branch 
held out to him, and, ignoring our humble request to be 
allowed to bask undisturbed, has girded his loins (a pro- 
ceeding that possibly is necessary in consequence of the 
broken suspender), and sailed into the Pottering interest 
with that vigour of diction in which he excels ; language 



which warms one like the sun in spring, and at the same 
time possesses a breezy freshness that is as invigorating to 
the jaded mind as nine hours at Brighton for three shillings. 

" Aut Scorcher, aut nihil,'' is the gist of his summmg up. 
But why, "Dodo?" 

Surely there is room in the Club for the two classes to 
dwell together in unity. Now, if a young member were to 
join the Club and at once set up in business as a Potterer, 
he would perhaps merit the scathing rebuke of the energetic 
"Dodo but I believe I am correct in stating that the ranks 
of the Potterers are mostly recruited from the old members 
who in the past have been all that their District Captain's 
fancy could paint them, but with increasing years have slid 
into the reserve, and only join the colours on occasions like 
the University and General Meets, when the Captain rightly 
expects every man to do his duty. At other times leaving 
the nose-on-the-wheel-business to those who have not yet 
discovered the true and lasting charm of bicycling apart 
from an athletic exercise, the Potterer looks on his machine, 
whether bicycle or tricycle, as an accessory to help him in 
finding and then enjoying the beauties of nature or in 
pursuing his researches after the mysterious fossilized bones, 
if he has any yearning for them, even at the risk of being 
indirectly stigmatized as a dismal fraud. — ^Yours, etc., 

W. R Sbwbll. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
" Hotel de France," 

Eaux Chandes, 

Basses Pyr^n^es. 
Dear Sir, — ^The Pyrenean five may be surprised to hear 
that no bicycle has been seen here since their visit four 
years ago. They are well remembered, as also at Angoul^me, 
Auch, and elsewhere. F. B. Westcott (Trin. Coll., Camb.) 
and myself have ridden here from Havre (420 miles by 
road — the rest by train). For the next ten days we follow 
in the footsteps of the "five," then home by Toulouse, 
Avignon, Clermont, etc.— Yours, 
Sept. 7th, 1883. C. A. E. Pollock.

brick and loose stone in the road, is an acquisition on a

Rumour has it that Hindley is about to start for India. 
Everyone will, I am sure, join with me in wishing him bon 
voyage, and as he is only expected to be away for eight 
months, we shall hope to see and hear him again at the 
meets next season.

There are a great many riders who consider that a light 
from someone else's lamp is all they require. This view, 
however, is not shared by a Croydon magistrate, who 
recently fined a lampless bicyclist lOs. and 7s. costs, though 
the latter urged as a point in his favour that he was riding 

behind two other machines with the necessary lights.

This month's issue of the Wheel World contains a notice 
and a good portrait of J. S. Whatton, the comer sketches 
doing credit to his enthusiasm for the road as well as the 
track. 

• •••*« 

It appears to me that, by encouraging Lacrosse in our 
midst, we are nursing a serpent. Not content with their 
exhibition game at our race meeting, the Clapton Lacrosse 
Club, which consists chiefly of our members, have actually 
fixed their opening game on the afternoon of our last Meet, 
which above all others is one that should be well supported. 

Who is the new Secretary? Surely 1 he cannot be a 

member of the L.B.C. 

• ••••« 

W. J. Williams, W. Silk, and Dr. Langmore, have started 
for a tour in Switzerland. I hope we shall hear of any 

exciting adventures. 

• •«««• 

The Itmes, in a recent leader on bicycles and tricycles^ 
concludes by impressing upon riders, who are designated 
" the true lords of the road," that " their right is sure to 
be admitted ; " and warns them, while their sport is still 
young, not to be too tyrannical. 

Poverty, if combined with honesty, is always allowed to 
be eminently respectable. No wonder, then, that the 
member who had to ride from Blackheath to Woolwich, on 
his way to Upminster on Sauiirday, felt sincerely flattered 
when the small boy on a black pony, who had been his very 


pleasant companirn for three miles of the road, said at 
parting, " Good byo ; I live in that house across the common, 
and if you are hard up I am sure my father will lend you 

what you want." 

• • • • • «j 

The Romford *oad, the South-Easter's notion of an 
Essex road, is at present more than usually bad, and quite 
justifies the recently accepted teyot that the sea-serpent lies 
buried beneath it. 

I am longing for the 22nd. The Club Mile in particular 
takes my fancy ; with 300 yards start I hope to be well in 
it, and if I am not, I shall at any rate have the satiflfaction 
of having done my best to amuse the sisters, the cousins; 
and the aunts. ' 

The Half-Mile Scratch will sure to bring out a good 
field and, with Oliver Thorn in the splendid form he has . 

lately shown, it bids fair to be a very fast race. 

• • ♦ ♦ ♦ • 

The District Race, which, thanks to the excellent pro- 
vision about the eight meets, has barred all racing members, 
will prove interesting all through, though I fancy one or 
two of the districts will find some difficulty in putting three 

representatives on the track. 

• ^ • • • • 

'H. N. Sharp, who deserves great credit for the successful 
manner in which he promoted and has carried through the 
Tennis tournament, did a good thing when he arranged for 
the final to be played at Stamford Bridge, giving every- 
one a chance of witnessing some really first-class play.

ROAD COMPETITIONS. 

It must have been evident to most people interested in 
the subject that the question of racing on the road has for 
some time past been approaching the climax which it may be 
now considered to have reached. Its illegality has long and 
generally been admitted, still, when the traffic was not im- 
peded, and the proceedings were more or less of a private 
character, where was the harm ? The police had no posi- 
tive instructions to interfere, and bicycle races on the roads 
were openly held, and, if not actually countenanced, were 
winked at by the authorities. 

Such was the position at the beginning of this year, but 
a change has now come over the scene, and the equivocal 
status of road-racing is likely to be defined in such a 
manner as to do away with racing on the road altogether, 
while the privileges of the ordinary road rider stand in no 
small danger of being curtailed. When bicycle trials and 
handicaps of a comparatively private nature were barely 
tolerated, it was hardly likely that Road Championships for 
tricycles, open to the amateur world, and largely advertised 
in the sporting papers, would be viewed with equal in- 
difference. Anyone who witnessed the scene of confusion 
attending the start for the twenty-four hours Tricycle Road 
Ride must have felt a crisis was at hand ; and when a few 



BNTS. 

PAGE 

QuerulonB Queries by a Quizzing Querent 216 

QuiUfl 216 

Change of Addrefla • 209 

Racing Fixtures 216 

Notice to Correspondents , 216 



days ago, in a similar contest, two of the competitors were 
stopped by the police, no one could have been surprised. 
Stringent orders against all racing and furious riding have 
already been issued to the police in the metropolitan district, 
and matters will scarcely be allowed to end here. As parties 
interested, we cannot help looking on the steps taken 
against road-racing, as so many nails in the coffin of that 
offspring of infinite L.B.C. genius, the Bath Trial. 

Certain it is, that a race — call it a trial or road-ride if 
you please — when reasonably and privately conducted, may 
have little that is objectionable in it except perhaps its 
name, while it may be less likely to hinder the ordinary 
traffic, or to be productive of accident than some of the 
commonest phases incidental to an ordinary bicycle ride. 

A party of four or five, for instance, are out for a run 
together, when something upsets their equilibrium — mental, 
of course, and the result is a rapid transition from a sober 
sensible pace into a maniacal rush. Away they go, a spirit 
of reckless insanity possesses them all, as they vie with each 
other on each ascent. Helter-skelter through quiet lanes, 
then down they dash into some secluded village. The child 
shrieks and totters to the roadside affrighted. Who can 
say but some day he will stand, cap in hand, on the self- 
same spot ready for vengeance ? Even the village cur re- 
frains his innate obstructiveness, and Diogenes like, seeks 
refuge in his tub. 

But to turn from this, which is little more than a fanciful 
picture : it is far from improbable that the police authorities 
having discovered what a nuisance bicycle, and more

especially tricycle racing has become in its latest statue of 
development, will not trouble to draw any fine distinctions, 
but haying set their foot once for all on road competitions 
of all kinds, will proceed fiirther and cause endless incon- 
venience to wheelmen generally by rules and regulations 
strictly enforced as to the pace which is to constitute furious 
riding. By the shortsighted selfishness of the few, we have 
brought ourselves under the notice of the law, and as is 
usual in such cases, the interests of the many will have to 
suffer.

LACROSSE. 

Members are aware that a game of Lacrosse is to be 
played as an interlude at our Race Meeting on Saturday, 
and we give below the names of * the players who are, as will 
be observed, members and members' brothers. 

It may, perhaps, be of interest to members unacquainted 
with the game to know a few points as to the rales of 
the game. 

A game is won when the ball is sent between the goal- 
posts, which are six feet high and six feet apart. It must 
be sent below the top of the posts as in association football, 
but a tape across the top would inconvenience the goal- 
keeper. 

There are no boundaries to the field of play, and the 
game may be carried on behind the goal-posts, though a 
goal can only be scored from in front of the posts. 

There is no regulation in any way resembling " off side " 
at football. 



In an ordinary game there are twelve players on each 
side, but as the space at Stamford Bridge is limited the 
team has also been limited. 

Platebs. 



E. T. Sachs. 
Gerard Smith. 
C. Newman. 
A. C. Smith. 
R. Newman. 
Theo. Godlee. 
E H. Barrett. 
A. Murray. 



Memben* Brothm. 
Sydney Barrett 
A. P. Barrett. 
F. Sachs. 
H. Newman. 
H. Sachs. 
T. R. Sachs. 
F. W. Barrett 
E. H. Lloyd. 



In addition to the above there are several other members 
who play the game, among whom we may mention sach 
well-known names as Rucker, Jolly, Large, Berwick, Child, 
and Francis Godlee. 

We may add that the Clapton Lacrosse dub, which is 
largely composed of our own members, plays every Saturday 
during the winter, and on Thursday evenings during the 
summer, thus rarely, if ever, clashing with our Club Meets ; 
and it thus does not in any way set up as a rival to our- 
selves, but rather as an affiliated institution, and we trust 
that when the L.B.C. element is more predominant, the 
clashing of fixtures may be entirely avoided. 



SATURDAY RUNS. 



S.E. DisTEicr. 
The Crawley run with its unusual attractions, besides that 
of a fine afternoon, brought out a sufficient number of men 
to gladden the heart of the D.C. Exception might have 
been taken to the unorthodox way in which the run was 
conducted, especially in the face of the recent police regula- 
tions, but there is a charm in variety, even with the prospect 
of being arrested by some minion of the law for allowing 
your pace to exceed twelve miles an hour on the Queen's 
highway. 

About a score of men assembled at the starting place at 
five o'clock, which number included Christie, C. B. Wheeler, 
and his brother. Some surprise was expressed at the non- 
appearance of Stephen Smith, who is generally to the fore 
on these occasiona After giving him a few minutes grace^ 
the "Extraordinary," with Alfred Herbert up, led off at 
three minutes to five, followed by the others at their fixed 
intervals, the last to leave being L. B. Reynolds, who had 
one minute start, and was virtually scratch in the absence 
of Beck, Wyndham, and Thorn. At Redhill, Lloyd took 
the lead, and was never headed, winning somewhat easily 
in 1 hr. 4 min. 50 sec. from his start Three minutes later 
came Herbert and Sewell, the South-Easter securing second 
place by a bare two yards. Then followed McRae, Toung, 
L. B. Reynolds, and W. C. Oswald in quick suceession. It 
is only fair to mention several of the backmark men, well

up at the time, stopped to look after Jackson, who, riding 
a semi-racer, came a nasty spill on Earlswood Common. 

After a fair tea at the " George," daring which the

soon after eight ; most of the men rode back to Croydon, 
the North-Easters took train to London, and a small contin- 
gent proceeded to Brighton. The moonlit ride being not 
the least enjoyable part of the meet — or rather dispersion. 
A noticeable feature of this run was that three members 
scored their first attendance for the season, and earned the

ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 
The Surrey Meeting went off very well, the crowd being 
as great as ever. That interesting event, the Ten Miles 
Scratch Race, was somewhat spoilt by Wilson's fall, though 
the finish was an exciting one, Liles beating Brown of the 
Brixton Ramblers by a couple of yards, while Gaskell, who 
has twice previously carried off the Challenge trophy, was 
only a foot behind the second man. 

• « • • • • 

The Mile Handicap, for which there were 62 entries, was 
won by H. G. Clark, of the Eastbourne Club (90 yards). 
Crabb (90) and W. Brown (20), both of the Brixton 
Ramblers, finishing second and third respectively. 



H. R. Reynolds competed in the Exeter Sports at the 
end of last week, and though mounted on a strange machine, 
J. R. Walter's racer, he ran fourth in the Three Miles (with 
60 yards from West), and third in the Two Miles (40 yards 
start). 

' ♦ « ♦ • • • 

Rucker performed a variety of tricks on his machine, 
which were loudly applauded by the Devonshire folks. 

A marvellous "Facile" ride has been accomplished by 
0. H. Adams, who covered 241 miles in the 24 hours. The 
route was a circular one through Cambridge, Bedford, 
Newmarket, and Biggleswada

QUERULOUS QUERIES BY A QUIZZING QUERENT. 

Dedicated to " W. X. W." and the authorities generally. 

When will the Rules Sub-Committee present its report ? 

How many Gold Medals will they give away annually in 
excess of the legal quantity ? 

Will members still have to elect their District Captain 
every week ? (Vide Regulation 2, re Meets.) 

Will the area of the Districts be defined, or will Botany 
Bay still be included in the S.W. ? 

Will District Captains score the attendance of visitors at 
Club runs? 

Will lawn tennis on Saturdays be made a capital offence ? 

Will refrigerated vouchers still have to wait in the 
draughts till the Committee are ready to receive their 
reports ? 

Will the Annual Dinner be as good at the lower tables 
as it is at the Chairman's ? 

Will the S.E. give a Ball with the sanction of the 
Captain ? 

Will the Dramatic Society have a General Meeting and 
declare a dividend ? 

Will the Smoking Concerts be held in a well ventilated 
room ? 

Will the Editor quash these quasi-querimonious quodli- 
betical questions? 

QUILLS. 

" Aristotle and Pliny deliver it that the porpentine caeteth off his qnillB 
when he would attack, or defend himself, but Diosoorides affirmeth it a 
point of folly to believe it."— Sir T. Bbownb. 

The annual entertainment of the Otter Swimming Club 
will take place at the St. Marylebpne Baths on Tuesday 
2nd October, at 7 sharp. The racing will include an open 
scratch 100 yards, to which great interest is attached, as 
with the exception of the Championships, this is the only 
opportunity the cracks have of competing together. The 
programme will also comprise two open and two club 
handicaps, together with hurdle and tub races, and 

oroamental swimming. 

• «•••* 

This meeting has always been largely attended by 
members of the L.B.C. Tickets can be obtained of M. H. 
Dicker, R. and C. Newman, Marchant, W. R. Sewell, and 

Rucker, all members of the Otter, and probable competitors. 

• ••••• 

Owing to an unfortunate spill on the previous Wednesday, 
R. B. Hough was unable to join in the Crawley run, and I 
am sorry to say that he will not be well enough to represent 

the N.E. in the District Race. 

• » • ' • • ♦ 

I bear that his only reason for going down to Redhill 
was to encourage the members of his district, but I don't 
believe it. 

**•••• 

The O'Reilly and Stephen Smith were much missed at 
Crawley. Why did not the latter turn up? Was he 
training in the dark for the Club Mile ? 



H. R Re3molds is uiaking good use of his machine. He 

left Edinburgh and rode vid Blair Adam, Carlisle, Keswick, 

Windermere, Preston, Manchester, Runton, Derby to 

Leicester, where lie stayed a week, thence to Stourbridge, 

where he stayed another week, and then on with a firiend 

to Edgbaston, where he played in a tennis tournament 

(doubles), but survived one round only, from here he rode 

on via Oloucester, Wells and Taunton to Exeter. 

«••••• 

Having sprained his ancle, he scratches to Sharp in our 

tennis tournament, and we shall no doubt see some good ' 

play on Saturday between Naylor and the latter. 

• ••••• 

The North-East form was very conspicuous on the 
Crawley road on Saturday, and it is a pity an accident pre- 
vented R. B. Hough from furthering his district's success. 

The S.W. irrepressible was in high fettle at the statt, 
but when enquired for at tea he had miraculously dis- 
appeared no one knew where. 

The North-East are naturally great favourites for the 
Twelve Miles Roadster Race on Saturday, but I hear the 

S.E. novices are determined to make a race of it. 

• •«•«• 

Their D.C. tells me that they are still coming an, and that 
he hopes to deliver them At the post very fit, and trained to 
the hour. 

If the weather holds fine, and we are honoured by a 
large assemblage of the fair sex, our venture on Saturday 
is already an assured success. The company will be strictly 
L.B.C., and those who complained of not being able to 
find their friends at the Crystal Palace will liave no such 
difficulty on this occanjon. Porcupink

THE YOUNG ENTHUSIAST. 

Enthusiasm has its merits and its demerits ; by some it 
is considered a virtue, by others a vice. There is a tale 
told of a barber of Dunkeld, who, in a fit of enthusiasm, 
spent \reeks in making a very small wig, which was only 
just big enough to fit a nutshell ; and it was only when he 
had finished it, and had nearly lost his eyesight and driven 
himself mad over it, that he found out it was of no earthly 
value. So, for the benefit of his customers, he placed it in 
a prominent position in his shop in order to point a moral 
and adorn a tale, the moral and the tale both going to 
prove the absurdity of enthusiasm. 

But it has not, we must confess, convinced us that 
enthusiasm is necessarily absurd — far from it ; for we are 
of opinion that, when directed through proper channels, it 
is a quality worthy of the highest encouragement. The right 
channel, through which it is the duty of our members to 
direct their enthusiasm, is obviously the L.B.C., and it is our 
present intention to give as good a daub as we can of theToung 
Enthusiast, whose likeness has not hitherto figured upon the 
editorial easel. No definition of him is necessary, for who 
cannot recognize him at first sight? At the 'first Meet he 
attends, he shows by the confident manner in which he 
discusses Club matters that he has already got the contents 



of the Blue Book at his fingers' ends, and a start is no sooner 
made, than he makes some adverse remarks about the slow- 
ness of the pace, and the shortness of the runs, at which the 
D.C., who has already a pretty good idea of his character, 
smiles inwardly. Before tea is over he has confided to his 
new acquaintances his intention to ride in the Bath Trial — 
though, before he started, he made up his mind to keep 
it quite dark— but of course he is only going to try for a 
certificate. While the older members are amused at his 
exuberance, the D.C. chuckles and writes him down an 
acquisition, and on the following Saturday his expectations 
are realized, for it is raining hard when he rides to the 
starting-point, and on his arrival there, the only figure to be 
seen is that of the Young Enthusiast. And who has ever 
regretted the enthusiasm that turned him out on a wet day ? 
The rain may make the ride uncomfortable, but what a 
sociable feeling pervades the few who have braved the 
elements, and when home is reached what a satisfaction 
there is in having taken the usual Saturday's recreation, 
notwithstanding those rude remarks of the Guvnor's about 
Earlswood. 

As the season progresses the appetite of the Young 
Enthusiast grows, and he takes part in everything in turn, 
be it Bath Trial or Annual Race Meeting, Lawn Tennis 
Tournament or Crawley Run, Annual Dinner or Smoking 
Concerts, Dramatic Show or South-East Ball, he is sure to 
be there. His holiday is of course spent upon the wheel, 
and an account of it is duly written for the edification of 
his fellow members in these pages. By the end of the 



Digitized by 



Google 



218 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



season he is as well known in the Club as some members of 
five or six years standing. The Captain holds him in high 
esteem, for he knows what an inilaence he exerts on those 
around him, for there is nothing so contagious as enthusiasm, 
and he hopes in his heart that that particular District which 
has so troubled him by its unaccountable falling off will 
speedily give birth to one of the species, and regain its former 
prestige. That his light is merely a temporary flare, and 
that he will suddenly die out like a Catherine-wheel is a 
commonly expressed opinion. But we will let him work 
out his own future, and leave him where he is, one of the 
main props of the club, and a motive power in our midst. 

N.W. NOTICE. 
I do hope that members will make a great effort to attend 
the last official run of this season. Mill Hill is a very short 
run, and ought to induce both the old and new riders of 
this District to score an attendance. There will be a circular 
route taken by those who wish to lengthen the afternoon's 
exercise ; but tea will be ordered for six o'clock at the Inn 
famous for its black currant jam. 

A. H. Low, District Captain. 

S.B. DISTRICT.--IMPORTANT.. 
I sincerely hope that the last meet of the year at Oodstone 
on Saturday will be largely patronized. A great many men 



have promised to come already, and as some of the cripples 
and others are coming down by train we ought to have the 
largest meet of thd season. The Croydon Division will wait 
for the Blackheath men at the Fire Station until 4.30. 

R. G. Francis, District Captain. 
N.B.— There is a train from Cannon Street at 4.37, 
arriving at Caterham, about 1^ miles from the " Claydon 
Arms," at 5.25, and return trains at 8.50 and 10.10. 

S.R DISTRICT.— LAST RUN OF THE SEASON.

during his first year of office.

THE AUTUMN RACE MEETING. 

The Autumn Race Meeting is over, and taking into con- 
Bideration the lateness of the season and the absolutely 
private character of the proceedings, it must be pronounced 
a decided success. The attendance, as was expected, was 
certainly small, but, on the other hand, it was very select, 
and we are greatly indebted to the ladies who, despite a 
somewhat chilly afternoon, honoured us with their presence 
in considerable numbers, adding immensely to the enjoyment 
of the afternoon. 

Thanks to the admirable provisions of the Race Committee 
and the energy of the various officials the programme was 
carried through without a hitch. The only contretemps 
happened in the first heat of the Mile, when E. D. Sewell 
ran against the wood at the side of the track and fell. 
Happily the injuries were only of a slight character, 
though they were sufficient to prevent him starting in the 
Twelve Miles. There might, perhaps, have been another 



bicycle race, and had not the Half-Mile Scratch fallen 
through on account of the small entry, the programme 
would have been complete. 

The Lacrosse Match and the Lawn Tennis Competition 
were decidedly novel features, and the spectators showed 
very great interest in them. The most satisfactory item on 
the programme was the Twelve Miles Roadster Race, which, 
from the spectators point of view, was interesting through- 
out, and, as a means of bringing to light the otherwise 
hidden qualities of our young members, it is an event which 
should in the future meet with great encouragement. The 
following is the list of officials : Judge, M. D. Rucker, Jun., 
Starter, Ashley Barrett; Timekeeper, H. 6. A. Brown; 
Clerks of the Course, C. Newman and F. M. Williams ; 
Laptakers, J. W. Alison and W. A. Smith ; Referees, G. F. 
Beck and Theo. Godlee ; Handicappers, The Race Com- 
mittee. Appended are the details of the various events 
decided.

One Milb Hakdioap. 

First Heat. — 0. Thorn, scratch, ] ; A. Lloyd, 130 yards 
start, 2 ; W. C. Oswald, 110, 3 ; L. B. Reynolds, 70, ; 
E. D. Sewell, 150, 0. Sewell fell entering the straight in 
the first lap, and Lloyd took up the running. Thorn soon 
got past Reynolds, and catching Lloyd down the back- 
stretch, won as he pleased by eight yards. Oswald came 
with a grand spurt at the finish, and was only just beaten 
for second place. Time, 2 min. 58| sec. 

Second Heat. — 0. 0. M. Leeds, 60 yards start, 1 ; Theo. 
Godlee, 130, 2 ; S. P. Smith, 180, 3. The limit man was 
soon passed, and Leeds, catching Godlee at the end of the 
third lap, rode in the winner. Time, 3 min. 7 sec. 

Third Heat.— S. Herbert, 160 yards start, 1 ; R. G. 
Francis, 120, 2 ; H. Baldwin, 140, 3. Herbert was never 
approached, and won by several lengths. K G. Francis 
passed Baldwin in the second lap. Time, 3 min. 4| sec. 

Final Heat.— S. Herbert, 1 ; Leeds, 2 ; Francis, 3 ; 
Thorn, 0. About half-way round the second lap Thorn's 
back wheel tyre came off, and he was obliged to retire from 
the contest, which, judging from the distance he had picked 
up would have resulted in his victory. Leeds gained con- 
siderably on the other two, but though he passed Francis, 
he was unable to get up to Herbert who spurted gamely 
down the straight and secured the^ verdict by five yards. 
Time, 3 min. 2f sec. 

The Laobosse Match. 

The Lacrosse Match was very well received, especially by 
the fair sex. After the leading article and correspondence 
in the Gazette, we wanted to see a few of the Members' 
Brothers. The members playing evidently saw too much 
of them, as they got a beating of two goals to " love " 
(much more fun to have two girls to love). The result was 
attributable in a great measure to the play of Arthur Barrett 
and T. R. Sachs, both of whom were played in the picked 
team of England and Ireland v. Canada, and their side, 
under the captaincy of the latter, were more even, and 
played better together. No particularly good dodging or 
passing was shown, but Arthur Barrett got in one or two 
moderately long throws> and the goals were very ably 
defended by Sydney Barrett and E. T. Sachs, goal keepers 
of the Clapton and London respectively. 

The latter, who captained the Member's side, was called 
upon to stop a good number of hot shots from two of his 
brothers. He was watched with great interest by the 
spectators, expecting every moment to see him extract a 
ball or two from the hair of an antagonist, or take some 
out of the flags ; but he contented himself by keeping 
them out. It is not our intention of giving a critical 
resume of the play, as the time was so short that the men 
had no chance of settling down to woric and showing the 
game o£f to advantage, and being only the beginning 
of the season the players were somewhat out of form. 
On looking down the names, it would seem to be 



rather a family game, but this is due to the fact 
that men living together have more opportunity for 
practice, of which a great deal is necessary to make a 
skilful player. The continuation of brotherly love, we 
should think, received rather a severe strain at the hands 
of the brothers, Arthur and Ernest Barrett, when they did 
chance to meet one another. Great efforts were made to 
keep the game as much as possible to members and brothers, 
and this desire was only a little stretched when Godlee and 
Lloyd could not play, their places being filled by two men 
who will be related to the Club "as it were." Further 
particulars, however, on this point can be obtained from two 
old members. The Potters were not in it on Saturday; 
they had better join the new club talked of at Croydon 
should they wish to compete with the Barretts at Lacrosse. 
After the match some of our members spoke of going down 
to Walthamstow to try their hands at the game, where they 
will be heartily welcomed by the Clapton Club, whose execu- 
tive consists entirely of L.B.C. men, three of them also 
belonging to our Committee. As this club has altered its 
first fixture in deference, to the wishes expressed in the 
Gazette, they hope to secure lots of recruits from our ranks 
this season. 

220 Yards Flat Race (for boys under 16). 
H. S. Baldwin, 18 yards start, 1 ; G. Whalley, 18, 2 ; 
B. Tacagni, scratch, 3. Won by three yards, two yards 
separating second and third. Time, 27f sec. 

Final Tie of the Lawn Tennis Touenament. 
As H. R. Reynolds, last year's winner of the tournament, 
had scratched to H. N. Sharp on account of a sprained 
ancle, it remained for the energetic secretary to play off 
the final tie with W. E. Naylor. The court was not in as 
good condition as it might have been on account of the 
recent rain, but the spectators had the privilege of wit- 
nessing some first-class play. Naylor won by two sets to 
love^the first by six to love and the second by six to 
three. 

Twelve Miles Distkiot Roadster Race. 

(Three representatives from each district, who must have 
attended not less than eight meets ; that district to win of 
which two representatives first complete the distance.) 

0. Thorn, W. District, 1 ; D. McRae, S.E., 2 ; W. C. 
Oswald, S.E., 3 ; S. Herbert, S.E., 0; A. Lloyd, N.E., 0; 
S. G. Hough, N.K, ; 0. G. M. Leeds, W., ; A. Barrett, 
N.E., 0. Barrett, who took Sewell's place and rode a machine 
he could scarcely reach, retired early in the race, and when 
Leeds quitted the track at 6f miles the race was left to be 
fought out between the representatives of the N.E. and the 
S.E. Thorn, with a generosity that was much appreciated, 
simulated well and rode badly. As the race progressed the 
excitement grew, and each time the competitors came round 
the shouts for the respective districts were most enthusiasti- 
cally given ; it was anything but a procession, as spurting

seemed to be largely indulged in. When the bell rang all were 
well together. Thorn, taking the rest along at a good pace, 
kept the lead and won by five yards ; the three South- 
Easters came in together next, McBae just beating Oswald 
on the tape, Lloyd and Hough close up. The time which, 
considering roadsters were ridden is exceptionally good, is 
giren at the conclusion of each mile.

At the conclusion of the programme Miss Barrett kindly

TO DERBYSHIRE AND BACK. 

I have some idea that a large part of the road I traversed 
is familiar to a good many, and shall consequently not 
dwell on that subject at any length. 

I started at about five p.m. on the 8th September with a 
friend, and we wended our way down Clapton, Stamford 
Hill, and over Muswell Hill to Barnet, which, from much 
rain, was in deplorable condition ; in fact it always is, and 
the natives seem to know it, for, on remarking same to one 
of those creatures in the following words " Why don't you 
keep your roads better, governor," he replied, " 'Taint my 

fault, I pay my rates and taxes, but the s won't do it." 

After reaching Barnet the run through Hatfield and 
Hitchiu to Bedford (51) was grand. We stopped here all 
day Sunday, and on Monday morning made an early start, 
reaching Kettering (25) witliout event, for breakfast. No 
sooner had we arrived than the rain came down and didn't 
leave ofif for a good twenty-four hours ; however, we didn't 
mean stopping there, so started at one p.m. in spite of it, 
and rode through to Leicester (25) to dinner. On the way 
we indulged in a drink, and were highly amused at the 
serious tone in which the landlord told us that them things 
was going out, it being too much hard work. I need hardly 
say that on arriving at Leicester we were drenched, but, 
thanks to mine host, were provided with dry clothes. Same 
dry clothes, however, belonged to mine host himself, and I 
would bet big things he did not weigh less than twenty 
stone. I will not go into details, but will simply say we 
looked like two wretched paupers, and felt awfully hungry ; 
consequently, we proceeded to the coflFee room, and there 
met the gaze of several other visitors to complete our feelings 
of misery. 

The next morning, as it was still raining, we trained it to 
Derby (30), and had a first-rate breakfast at the " St. James's 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



Hotel," (C.T.C.) By the time breakfast was well out of the 
way, the weather had cleared, and our last drop of rain for 
the rest of the tour been seen. There is nought of interest 
at Derby, but the road to Matlock (17) is delightful as 
regards scenery (the Powers forbid that I should call any 
road in Derbyshire anything but vile in any other respect). 
We found picking blackberries and a smoke also delightful 
— in fact, be it said to our shame, I think we liked it better. 
On reaching Matlock our day's journey was done. The town 
is grandly situated, and well worth a visit. The caverns 
and the heights were duly inspected and admired, and in 
the evening to our intense disgust, though certainly to 
our amusement, we found ourselves actively engaged in a 
shooting competition with each other in a strolling fair. 

Feeling very much demoralised by the above, the next 
morning we vowed we would pay ourselves out and ride 
forty miles. Don't laugh, dear reader ; that is a big ride 
in Derbyshire, which we accomplished, more or less, by 
going through Chatsworth and the model village of Edensor, 
so well worth a visit, to Baslow (1 5), thence to Castleton 
(10), where we met two " Sociablists', " who were averaging 
about twelve miles a day. We passed them, firstly, a mile 
or two from that town, engaged in the entertaining pastime, 
outside an inn, of administering a red hot poker to the 
cement on their tyres. 

At Castleton the Blue John Mine was visited, but not 
without labour in the shape of getting up a very stiff hill 
about two miles long. Don't imagine, dear reader, that we 
rode it ; we had just had dinner, and required to rest twice 
in shoving up. But we were fully repaid, for the inside of 
the cavern is strikingly grand, and the huge overhanging 
rocks, with seemingly very insufficient support, very much 
impress the spectator, so much so in fact that you b^in to 
wish yourself well out of it. 

We reached Buxton (12), and put up at the " Shakspeare." 
There is no C.T.C. house here, therefore we should recom- 
mend the above. In the evening we were much edified by 
a glorious band and promenade in the gardens, -on leaving 
which we regaled ourselves with laughing until we tortured 
our sides, at a " Punch and Judy show." Moral, so far : 
Man does sometimes sink beneath himself. 

On the 13th September, the following day, we left Buxton 
and travelled vid Newhaven House to Ashbourne (20), just 
in time for the two o'clock ordinary with a party of farmers, 
and after a considerable amount of down-hill, although the 
roads were always the same as regards roughness. We 
communicated our opinion of the roads to the farmers in 
question, who were simply astounded, and declared them to 
be the best in the country. 

It is a grand thing to be just in time for dinner, one 
saves a good half-hour, for which we were very grateful as 
it gave us plenty of time for a stroll down to the river, a 
lounge on its banks, and a jolly swim. After which we re- 
mounted and went md Uttoxeter to Lichfield (27), and 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



221 



were tnily thankful to be able to move more freely along 
the roads, although we found that Derbyshire had com- 
pletely spoilt us for pace, and for the rest of the tour we 
were unable to do an3rthing decent in that respect. 

Lichfield has already been dealt with in the Gazette a 
few weeks back, as has also our road as far as Coventry, 
Eenilworth, and Warwick, which is splendid going. We 
stopped the night at Stratford-on-Avon. Those readers who 
have not visited this town, I should recommend to take an 
early opportunity. The inhabitants are enormously proud 
of their great Shakspeare. The first thing that strikes 
the eye is the new memorial, then the church where he is 
buried ; then we arrived at the " Shakspeare Hotel/' and 
were conducted to our room known as " Macbeth." After 
changing we descended to the co£fee room, but not before 
passing on the stairs a large oil-painting of the big man ; 
in the cofiee room there were four likenesses of him, and 
the walls were decorated with prints of various scenes in 
his plays. After tea we strolled forth, and various statues 
on the same subject met our gaze, and then the house was 
visited. We were told that 14,000 visitors annually go to 
see the house of his birth. Yankees abound in this district. 
The house is very interesting. Their great motto is — 

" Take him for aU in aU, 

We shaU never see his like again.'* 

I am a great admirer of Shakspeare, lut I never dreamt 
of hero-worship to the extent it goes on here. 

Don't miss the bath in the river at Stratford, it is worth 
the visit, if there were nothing else. We were considerably 
amused at the*speculations of two very small boys as to the 
price of our bicycles. One of them seemed convinced that 
they must have cost two shillings each. 

The following day was uneventful ; we started after 
breakfast and rode through Banbury (20) to Aylesbury (30) 
for dinner, and thence to Watford (23) for tea, where we 
stopped all day Sunday, and on Monday morning rode 
home to breakfast through Elstree, Bamet, and Muswell Hill. 

The road from Aylesbury to Watford, barring a longish hill, 
is the finest I was ever on. Asphalte could scarcely beat it. 

My experience of a bicycle tour is that riding every day 
gets monotonous and is a mistake, for although we had a 
very jolly time of it, and did nothing to fatigue ourselves, 
we should have enjoyed the riding itself very much more had 
we put an interval in once or twice. 

The distances I give are all within one or two miles, but 
in most cases are quite correct. As will be perceived, my 
object was to go sight-seeing, and not to try for a record. 

S. 6. Hough. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Dear Sir, — As one of the Pyrenean five I was much in- 
terested to hear that my once " frequent pardner," C. A. E. 
Pollock, was on our trail, and shall anidously await an 
account of his experiences. 



It may, perhaps, comfort some of those who have lately 
mourned over the threatened decadence of the Club, owing 
to the prevalence of a certain epidemic," to learn that a 
family party, consisting of a member and his wife, and a 
"members brother and sister," have been "pottering" 
through Normandy on two "Sociables." We rode from 
St. Male to Rouen, via Dinan, Mount St. Michel, Avranches, 
Mortain, Caen, and Lisieux, trained to Havre, and returned 
thence to Southampton. We covered rather over 300 miles 
in the three weeks, of which about 230 were in France ; 
riding days, 14. We carried 50 lbs. of luggage on each 
machine, including a folding indiarubber bath (weight 3 lbs.), 
which was an immense success. 

Trusting that the above experience may be taken as a 
proof that married members are not necessarily " done for," 
I remain, yours truly, 

G. R. HUTOHINQS. 



7o the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Morez, Dept. Jura (2400 feet), 

September 23, 1883. 
The Franco-Swiss Trio are glad to state have reached so 
far safely, having ridden from Dieppe. Weather most 
brilliant for first week to Dijon, since been unsettled. No 
incidents have occurred, except the front bearings of one 
machine locking twice, happily without a spilL Scenery in 
the Jura grand. Have taken all day doing 24 miles to-day, 
having to go over Col de la Savine, 3225 feet. Are hoping 
to reach Oeneva to-morrow, but have to cross Col de la 
Faucille, 4400 feet, first. Weather to-night promising. 
Result of nine days riding, 401 miles. 

W. J. WlLUAMS. 



A TOUR IN YORKSHIRK 
Monday, the 10th July last year, was the day fixed for 
a week's run in Yorkshire. As a week could only be spared 
for that part of the holiday I decided upon training it to 
Sheffield, and making that the starting place. In order not 
to lose time I took the 5.15 a.m. train — ^the newspaper 
express — ^from St. Pancras, and at 9 a.m. landed on the 
paving stones of the centre of the cutlery trade. Sheffield 
did not impress me with being a very desirable place for 
making a long stay, so after breakfast at the "King's 
Head " (good), and a short walk round, twelve o'clock saw 
me in the saddle, struggling over greasy paving stones 
towards Barnsley. Once free of the town it was grand 
going ; a mile or so before coming to Barnsley there is a 
capital opportunity for legs up, of which advantage was 
taken — the corresponding rise was not so cordially negotiated. 
Dinner at the " Royal " (good) ; there, as at almost every 
other place I dined at, the regular dinner time was one 
o'clock, when soup, hot joints, and pastry were always to 
be had, a pleasant change from the cold beef, which is the 
rule in the southern and western counties. Apropos of this, 



Digitized by 



222 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



has anybody ever had beef otherwise than cold in these 
districts ? I have made any amount of enquiries, but have 
never yet been able to elicit an affirmative. What an oppor- 
tunity in these days of records to make one here. Barnsley 
was left behind at about 3.45, and Wakefield reached an 
hour later, the road continuing good. This town is no 
less business-like in appearance than its neighbour, and the 
streets are all paved like those of all manufacturing towns 
in Yorkshire. After getting off the paving the road is 
very bad indeed — ^never before have I been over a rideable 
road so lumpy ; again and again was I almost jolted out of 
the saddle. To make matters worse, after a few miles 
of this sort of thing, paving, awful to contemplate, appeared 
over half the road. This execrable road continued, and it 
seemed as if a series of small earthquakes had operated 
thereon, until Hunslet was reached, when this paving of 
young boulders spread out over the whole road, and for two 
or three miles the travelling was about as bad as it could 
possibly be, the best riding being between the tram lines 
which run between Hunslet and Leeds. The latter place 
was reached about 6 p.m. I put up at the ''Queen's Hotel" 
(recommended) near the railway station, and after a wash, 
change, and tea, I went to the theatre and saw " Fun on 
the Bristol " — a more wretched affair I have never seen. 
I may mention here that I always send on a portmanteau 
with an entire change, and find the arrangement answers 
capitally. It does away with the necessity of a multum, 
costs little, and whilst washing, the " Boots " of the hotel 
can go to the station and fetch it. Leeds was left at 
about eleven next morning by the Whitehall Road en route 
for Halifax ; the road between these two places is 
hilly and bad. I learned afterwards that the best way 
is to go 1^ Bradford. A long hill has to be mounted 
out of the town, which, although rideable, makes one glad 
of a rest at the top. From here to Halifax the hills on the 
whole are favourable, but one's hill climbing powers are 
severely tested by some of them, especially that leading 
into Halifax. Here, indeed, a dismount is necessary at the 
top, for the descent through the town is extremely dange- 
rous. A stop was made here for dinner at the " White 
Lion " (good). I had intended riding on to Keighley (pro- 
nounced Keethly) by the direct road, but was fortunately 
warned in time, and advised to go via Bradford, which I 
did, and found the road very fair. There is a long grind 
out of Halifax on this road which, however, was successfully 
ridden, and then the road becomes fairly level. By this time 
I had become learned in the mysteries of the Yorkshire 
language, and enquired if it were not straat forward to 
Bratfoord," as if I had been a native. 

There are plenty of paving stones and tram lines to get 
over at the seat of the great Woollen Manufacture, then a 
capital road takes you to Keighley. Here I stayed the 
night, at, I think, the " Devonshire Arms ; " I liked the 
situation of the town, the neighbourhood being extremely 



hilly and pretty. The following morning I got the chance 
of a drive over the hills in a small trap ; it .was a glorious 
run, and the old fellow that drove took an interest in pointing 
out anything worth seeing in the neighbourhood, and had 
tales to tell of everything and of everybody. After two hours 
of this, and breakfast, a start was made for Otley, a splendid 
ride alongside the Wharfe for some distance, and through 
Ilkley. I had to get off for the last bit of one hill a few 
miles from Keighley, otherwise the road was. fairly level 
While near Otley a thunderstorm came on, and I greatly 
admired the effect of the lightning on the surrounding hills, 
although it was only by doing all I knew that I was able to 
get to Otley before the rain commenced. The storm only 
lasted an hour, so after dinner at the "White Horse" 
(good) I set off for Harrogate, which was reached at 4 p.m. . 
After securing a bed at the Commercial " (good) a good 
swim was obtained in the sulphur baths. The smell of the 
water is peculiar to say the least of it, and has the 
appearance of being very dirty ; yet these were only mild 
baths, the strong ones being, as I was informed, both 
dirtier-looking, and of a stronger smell. I feel particularly 
glad I did not go in for them. At 8 p.m. a visit was paid 
to the Spa, where I had a glass of " iron " water, and 
asked for one of ''magnesia," but, as the attendant 
informed me, it was impossible to hope to survive sulphur, 
iron, and magnesia, I had to forego the latter. The 
baud and singing at the Spa were very jolly, although there 
is a diminutive appearance about the building, which is 
a small imitation of that of Buxton. Harrogate struck me 
as being a very healthy-looking place, lying high, but is, I 
should imagine, dull. If ever a morning looked like rain 
it did so the following day, and it was coming down slightly 
as the town was left at 9.30. Bipon was left behind an 
hour later, the road being a very good one through Ripley ; 
as it looked very threatening no stay was made to see the 
cathedral. The road continued good, and it was an agree- 
able surprise to find that Looming Lane was capital going, 
as the description received of it was that it was only rideable 
at the sides. 

Six miles from Kipon the rain began, and it came down 
so hard that in a very few minutes my clothes were wet 
through, but no shelter being procurable there was nothing 
for it but to push on. The destination for the day was 
Darlington, and there my portmanteau had been sent, but 
the roads got so thoroughly bad, and as there were no signs 
of the rain giving over after having ridden 20 miles in it, a 
resolve was made to stay at Eichmond, where I arrived at 
1 p.m., and put up at the "King's Head" (good). The 
manager of the hotel offered to lend me a change, and, 
although he was but about half my height, the offer was 
accepted with pleasure. After struggling into the clothes 
a descent was made for dinner, and a delightful spec- 
tacle I must have presented to the other occupant of 
the room, a clergyman who had come up for an archery 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



223 



competition, and as he seemed decidedly uncomfortable and 
nervous of being in the same room with me, I had to ex- 
plain, at which he appeared considerably relieved. After 
waiting until about 4 p.m., as the rain showed no signs of 
abatement, and my things having been not only dried, but 
scorched, I decided to take train for Darlington. Richmond 
is an extremely pretty place, and should be very enjoyable 
in fine weather. At the North Country Quaker City I put 
up at the " Fleece " (good). 

The next morning was dull and looked very much like 
raining any moment, so as I wanted to see Scarborough, I 
decided to take the train. Whilst changing trains at York, 
I was surprised to hear my name rising far above the noise 
and bustle of the station ; I thought, perhaps, that I had 
suddenly become famous, and yet could not recollect having 
done anything more meritorious than the gaining of a 
Bath medal the year before, but a second later I was 
undeceived — it was but the voice of the future N.W. D.C. ; 
however, I was very glad to see him, and as he was going 
to Whitby with his people we were able to be together half- 
way to Scarborough. The Pride of Yorkshire was reached 
at 4 p.m., and after a change, and dinner at the Bull " 
(fair), a swim was indulged in but not enjoyed, the baths 
appearing to be the last resting place of the beetles and 
other insects of the neighbourhood. The evening turned 
out very fine and was spent at the Spa, which is decidedly 
quite too all but. Next day was a drencher, so I started 
home by train, arriving in time to ride to Whip's Cross to 
see the finish of the N.E. Paperchase, and to spend a very 
jolly evening with the District at the house of our D.C. 

I have not put in the distances in the above account, 
having forgotten them, but the mileage was not great. 
I found about 40 miles almost sufficient for a day's ride 
upon the average. R. B. HouaH. 

QUILLS. 

" Aristotle and Pliny ddiver it that the porpentine caeteth off his quills 
when he would attack, or defend himself, but Dioeoorides affinneth it a 
point of folly to belieye it.**— Sir T. Bbownx. 

A monkey on a gridiron is a familiar though offensive 

term to a bicyclist, but a tricyclist is now known as a 

monkey on the mouch. 

News has been received of A. W. Chippindale from 
Adelaide, South Australia, dated the end of July. He 
seems to have been having a rare good time of it. He says 
he had been up in the bush for an eighteen weeks' trip, 
during which time he had covered three thousand five 
hundred miles. 

He was just off on another expedition to Rockhampton, 
and the Far North in Queensland. He mentions that 
bicycling is on the increase, and in South Australia in 
particular, but of course only near the principal towns, as 
sandhills and stony plains without water are not adapted 
for the wily cyclist. 



He had just seen the result of the Bath Trial in the 

Field, and lamented that he was not ubiquitous enough to 

have been present in body, although he was in mind. He 

talks of being present next Whit Monday, however, as far 

as he can at present judge, and concludes by desiring to be 

remembered to all his friends. 

*««««« 

The 24th Annual Entertainment of the Ilex Swimming 

Club will take place at the Lambeth Baths, on Thursday, 

the 4th of October, at 7.0 p.m. The programme is an 

interesting one, and containa three open events, entries for 

which should be sent to the Hon. Sec, 0. 0. M. Leeds, 

Floating Baths, Charing Cross. 

• « « • ' ♦ • 

Saturday afternoon at Stamford Bridge was a very 
pleasant family party, where everybody seemed to know 
everybody. One reporter was observed, besides the repre- 
sentative of this Oazette, but he seemed out of his element. 
«««««• 

Several of the usual incidents of a L.B.C. Race Meeting 
were present. The N.E. District Captain rode his usual 
mile ; Stephen Smith, whose journey to the ground 
suggested a large family out for a holiday, once more did 
his duty as a Club member in a way that ought to make 
half the Club blush, if they weren't past that long ago ; 
Oliver Thorn was to the front as usual, and youth and 
beauty smiled as Y. and B. only do at our Club Race 
Meetings. 

• • • « « e 

Of course some members always disgrace themselves by 
being absent on these occasions, but words fail me to 
describe the iniquities of the member who was seen sneaking 
out of West Brompton Station on his way to the Kildare 
sports! I won't hold him up to universal execration 
by disclosing his name ; his conscience will probably be a 
sufiicient punishment. Only I fear a member who prefers 
the Eildare to his Club Race Meeting has'nt got a conscience, 

and does'nt read his Oazettb. The Eildare ! Ugh ! ! 

• •«••« 

After which outburst, I may remark that Thorn had very 
bad luck in the Mile, which he would probably have won 
had not his back wheel tyre come off. Sewell's tumble was 
unlucky, and another N.E. member was very lucky in not 
following his example. 

The Lacrosse excited great interest ; Arthur Barrett's 
throwing, and the goal-keeping of Sachs and Sidney Barrett 
being much admired. Charles Newman also distinguished 
himself in some position, the name of which your deponent 
knoweth not. 

I should have thought with a six foot goal the best way 
was to put two or three of the team into coats of armour, 
and stand them across the goal, then no ball could get 
through.

The Twelve Miles Race was the excitement of the day. 
Though the North-East had hard luck in losing the services 
of the Treasurer and Sewell through tumbles, they made a 
good fight of it, and it was anyone's race till the last lap. 
The South-East team rode splendidly, McRae especially so, 
and it was very refreshing to see five comparatively yonng 
members of the Club showing such form. The pace was 
rather too fast for the West racing men. 

• ««*«• 

The success of the S.E. team not only does credit to 
themselves, but also to their energetic D.C., whose dis- 
crimination in selecting the young blood and personally 
superintending their training is worthy of the highest com- 
mendation. 

• • • « « ft 

We shall no doubt see these so-called novices among the 

early arrivals at the ninth milestone on Whit-Monday next.

I hope all members will do their best to attend the last 
meet of the season on Saturday. Some members will also 

make it their first meet.

Barretts !

With the issue of this Number, the Gazette will cease to 
appear every week, and wiU be published monthly during 
the winter. ITi^ next Number will be published on Thursday, 
November 1st, and contributions intended for it must be 
sent in by 6 p,m, on Tuesday, October SOth. 



WINTER AMUSEMENTS. 
That we English " amuse ourselves sadly " is reputed to 
be a French axiom, but this would perhaps be revoked by the 
intelligent foreigner were he permitted to share in the 
joviality of a Smoking Concert or the festivity of a S.E. Ball. 
But tho' these and other winter amusements are pleasant, 
it is desirable at this, the close of our riding season, to decide 
as to the propriety of repeating them. That they involve 
much labour to their promoters is not to be doubted, certain 
also it is that they carry with them a good deal of expense ; 
but while men are patriotic enough to undertake the former, 
and members by their liberal purchase of tickets show them- 
selves willing to bear the brunt of the latter, we believe 
that only the best results to the Club can spring from well 
conducted winter amusements. Now the aim and object of the 
winter sport, be it Ball, Concert, Dinner or Dramatics, is to 
collect for an agreeable purpose, and make known to each 
other our members and their friends from all quarters of 
the wide domain of the L.B.O. It follows, therefore, that 
the cap and bells should not be donned without purpose, 
and that the show may be more than vox et prosterea nihil 
it is, we think, quite necessary to attend to two or three points 



of detail. Firstly, we hold it to be a crucial error to over- 
do the entertainment of whatever sort it may be. Now 
that the Committee has taken from D.C/s the organising of 
Smoking Concerts in their various departments this is leas 
likely to recur. Again, almost as necessay as the provision 
of good music for an audience is a careful attention to 
ventilation, acoustics, and facility of locomotion, and we 
venture to hope that our Sub-Committee will remember it 
is but an unprofitable evening that is spent by an unhappy 
member penned immovably in a corner and there saturated 
with carbonic acid, music, and tobacco smoke. These then 
are a few of the details connected with winter amusements, 
but as many others will occur to old members with experi- 
ence more varied than our own, we shall look forward to an 
edifying enlivenment of our dull season. 

ANNUAL DINNER. 
This event of the winter is fixed for Wednesday, 2l8t 
November, at the St. James's Hall Restaurant, Piccadilly. 
Special attention will be paid to the menu. Particulars 
will appear later on. E. S. Price, Hon. Sec.

Lacrosse Club, Tor kindly informing all his L.B.C. members

Theo. Godlee, N.E. D.C.

SATURDAY RUNS. 



N.E. DlflTEIOT. 

29th Sept. There was very bitter disappointment to 
those who were looking for extensive events on Saturday in 
the miserable state of the weather, which had been very 
bad, and was very threatening about the time the Districts 
were leaving home ; consequently only three members and 
a visitor started together, the visitor riding a tricycle 
to which he was not very much used. The pace at first 
was decidedly slow by the tramlines and Snaresbrook, and 
then the weather began to carry out its threats in good 
earnest. Shortly after George Lane, Ernest Barrett and 
his terrier were sighted on foot, and accompanied the run 
a short distance ; then Kelsey and the D.C. determined 
to get on a little faster, leaving Lloyd and his brother 
to come on somewhat slower. The leading couple fell in 
with the Treasurer also on foot, as his arm is not quite well 
yet, and he, too, following his custom at the Race Meeting, 
raced the riders some way. The rain had slightly abated 
by this time, and the D.C. having deposited Kelsey at the 
** White Hart," Abridge, where he speedily acquired the 
landlord's garments, returned to pick up the brethren and 
try to get dry in the effort. When the second instalment 
arrived, there was a feeling that five and Ernest Barrett 
might do, though poor for a final meet, and Weeks was 
warmly greeted when he came in drenched, though shortly 
after, Baldwin next turned up, having come partly by train, 
and then the Captain and Lyne rode in, making double 
figures. A very satisfactory tea was quickly dispatched, 
and a fire lighted, and then the company settled down to 
an hour or so of intellectual amusement, S. G. Hough turning 
up with reports of having a nasty spill, just as the Lloyd 
brothers decided to start. Hough's machine appeared badly 
damaged, so he joined the walking party, and the balance 
of five retreated quietly homewards in a good deal of 
drizzle. Present : Ashley Barrett, Ernest Barrett, Baldwin, 
R. B. Hough, S. G. Hough, Kelsey, Lloyd, Lyne, Weeks, 
one visitor, and TuEO. Godlee, District Captain. 



W. District. 

I think I comprised the West and South-West Districts 
on Saturday. I left home in the morning, and going by 
Kingston had lunch at the Anchor at Ripley, and afterwards 
rode by Newland's Corner and Gomshall to Dorking and 
Leatherhead, which I reached at 5.15. After waiting an 
hour, and drawing all the inns, not excepting the inhospit- 
able "Swan," I ordered tea at the "Bull's Head," and at 
7.30 proceeded homewards by Ewell and Wimbledon. It 
began to rain heavily while I was at tea, and continued all 
the way. This is not the first time I have represented these 
districts at Leatherhead. Roads splendid. No rain till 
6.30 p.m. 

J. S. Whattok. 



N.W. DiSTEicrr. 
Last Saturday's run was a wet one, but that ought not 
to be any excuse for the very small muster. At Tally Ho ! 
the thought was entertained for a few minutes that just as 
much profit would be derived from a retrogade movement 
as by carrying out the programme, but we could not afford 
any more blank runs, so on over unusually wet roads, this 
martyr rode to Mill Hill, got off, turned round, emptied 
the rain out of his shoes and rode home. Present : 

A. H. LowK, District Captain. 



I would take this opportunity of thanking those men 
who have given me such enegetic support, and have thus 
tended to make our meets such a success. Our district is 
making immense and rapid strides, and although to the 
ordinary observer this may not be visible ; and I have not 
the slightest hesitation in saying that if next year should 
fulfil the promises Iteld out by the actions of this, we shall 
be able to show an unprecedented average in the annals 
of the L.B.C. for attendance. Now that the season's work 
is fresh in the memory it would be a good thing to make 
suggestions concerning the N.W. meets, that whoever is in 
office next year should have the benefit of them. 

A. H. LowN, District Captain. 



S.W. District. 
September 29th. — A large and distinguished company 
appeared on Kingston Bridge at 4.10, and after several 
preliminary runs round the Market Place to pick up members, 
the Meet started at 4.40 in a heavy shower for Leatherhead. 
Half a mile from the start, shelter was taken under a tree, 
and after some discussion, it was resolved to change the run, 
and proceed to Ba^rnes vid Kingston Hill and the Roehampton 
Lane. This new route was carried out in a storm of rain. 
Present : the Reporter, J. Gatey, and a " Coventry Rotary." 
Query, Where was the District Captain? Query, Where 
were the other members ? Joseph Gatey.
