LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE, 227 



S.R District. 
The last meet of the year seemed to touch a sympathetic 
chord in the breast of not a few on Saturday, and that in 
spite of the weather not being of the most propitious order. 
Threatening it certainly did look at the time fixed for the 
start from Croydon, and in the locality of Blackheath it 
did more than threaten ; for a storm of rain and hail came 
down in such earnest as to deter anyone but the most 
intrepid from venturing out. However, South East energy 
rose to the occasion with an effect that was quite astounding. 
Beck and the D. C. rode over by Sanderstead and took up 
up a position of vantage on the top of Godstone Hill. 
As they directed their gaze down towards Caterham, 
winding up the hill there appeared a long thin line of grey, 
which gradually resolved itself into a formidable array 
of no less than eighteen men. Among the first to 
become recognisable were Barker and J. G. Oswald on 
the double tricycle, then the three heroes of Stamford 
Bridge, and shortly after six stalwart forms — a veritable 
band of brothers — showed that the great Potter demon- 
stration was, so to speak, a fait accompli. While an 
exhibition of the tandem, its merits and peculiarities, was 
being carried on outside the "Clayton Arms" (no extra 
charge), the stragglers came in apace. The O'Reilly rode 
in from Brighton, F. B. Smith from Sevenoaks. It was not 
long before they were all assembled in the old room which 
has become associated with the memories of so many last 
meets of the season. After some practice in the first rule 
of arithmetic it was discovered that tlie party consisted of 
no less than thirty members and three visitors. As three 
men had not stayed for tea the district record was tied," 
and the question whether it would be beaten or not was 
for sometime held in the balance. Tea was over, and the 
customary tribute to the "setting sun" having been paid 
by the ex-D.C. in a speech brimming over with eloquence 
and bristling with compliments, when Hume Young, 
apparently with an eye to dramatic e£fect, stepped into 
the room, and was instantly greeted with ringing cheers 
which must ha?e awakened the ghostly tenant of the 
haunted chamber if he was dosing at the moment. The 
absence of a pianist was much to be deprecated; still, 
songs and recitations were the order of the day, the efforts 
of J. C. Oswald, J. F. Potter, and H. 6. A. Brown being 
warmly applauded. "Auld lang syne" brought the 
evening to its close. After such a successful Meet, 
who could grumble at the rain and darkness going 
home — for it did rain, and it was dark. On starting, the 
long procession of lamps seemed to reach from the Inn to 
the top of the hill. At Whiteleaf, the Blackheath Division 
turned off, and by eleven all parties had reached home — or 
ought to have done. Present : Barker, Beck, Belfield, 
C. A. Brown, H. G. A. Brown, Church, Day, Gerich, 
Halliday, Hartridge, Alf. Herbert, Arthur Herbert, S. 
Herbert, McRae, Morley, The O'Reilly, J. C. Oswald, W. 



0. Oswald, C. E. Parker, A. L. Potter, L. Potter, J. F. 
Potter, J. W. Potter, L. B. Reynolds, J. H. Scrutton, Sharp, 
F. B. Smith, S. P. Smith, E. F. Smither, S. E. Whealler, 
F. H. Williams, J. B. Young, J. W. Young, three visitors^ 
and R. 6. FiXANCis, District Captain. 



HONOUR TO WHOM HONOUR, 
Every member of the Club who takes the least interest 
in its welfare will be glad to read an account of the 
enthusiastic exhibtion of feeling which was displayed on 
Saturday last in the well-known room on the first floor of 
the " Clayton Arms " at Godstone. 

It was the concluding run of the season — of the South- 
East season ; the weather had been anything but fine in 
the morning, and the afternoon sun was obscured by dull 
grey clouds ; still, the roads were not so very bad, and 
most of us hoped to be able to count a quarter of a century 
of members at all events. By the time tea was commenced 
thirty-three had actually arrived, and at the conclusion of 
a vigorous half hour's cup-emptying and knife and fork 
exercise, one of the members rose to say a few words on 
behalf of the South-East District in recognition of the 
great services rendered^by the Distdct Captain during his 
year of office. 

He said it was a pleasant thing to have the privilege 
of addressing the members present on that occasion, because 
their to-day's run marked the close of the most successful 
season of any District in any year since the Club was 
formed. Record for a club run had that day been equalled, 
and, perhaps, before the evening was over would even be 
beaten, and the average attendance record had been alto- 
gether smashed on the head, for the average number of 
members at each meet during the season was no less than 
15J, a truly satisfactory proof of the interest felt in the 
runs. To what was all this mainly due but to the energy, 
popularity, and able leading of their District Captain, R. G. 
Francis ? The men came, not simply because they were asked 
oyer and over again — as was proved by the large number of 
regular attendants—but because .they took a real pleasure 
in runs so well chosen, and the arrangements of which were 
so well looked after ; because they delighted to combine 
friendly interchange of thought with healthy exercise of 
body, and knew that if they wanted to get pleasure out of 
a run they could not do better than follow their good 
leader. Nor was it only on account of his success as 
District Captain that he had been so popular ; his contri- 
butions to our Gazette were of a very high order, and his 
usefulness to the Club in this respect was much greater 
than most people imagined. Nature had blessed him with 
a facile pen, and if he ever had a mind to try he would not 
find it difficult to make a name in the world of letters. 
He (the speaker) read an extract from a letter he had 
received from the Editor of the Gazette referring in terms 
of warm praise to the value of the writings of R. G. Francis. 



Digitized by 



228 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



There waa one point more upon which he felt bound to 
touch, and that was the District Captaincy of 1884. He 
had spoken to several members upon the subject, and was 
convinced he was expressing the feeling of the whole Dis- 
trict when he said he hoped most sincerely that the present 
District Captain would consent to hold the position for 
another year. This was no mere compliment ; it was a 
spontaneous and very earnest desire, and though the tenure 
of the office brought a great deal of work with it, and in- 
volved the sacrifice of much time (which would be par- 
ticularly felt during a second season) he trusted that 
the very fact of such a brilliant success having been 
achieved would weigh as ^ powerful argument in favour of 
a compliance with the District's undoubted wish. In 
conclusion, the speaker thanked the District Captain most 
heartily in the name of the whole District for the many 
good things he had done and said for it. 

The cheers that were' then given for the evening's hero 
were loud and long, and before (they had had time to 
subside he got up, and in a few telling sentences 
thanked the members for the way they had received 
what had been said about him. He desired to call 
particular attention to the support he had received 
from some of the men of his district, notably the 
Oswalds, the Herberts, and S. E. Whealler, the latter 
having won the attendance medal The S.E. District had, 
he said, during the present year more than maintained 
their reputation on the racing path ; the/ held the District 
Challenge Cup, the Ladies' Challenge Cup, and, but for 
their efiforts, the matches against the Universities would 
have been defeats instead of victories. He anticipated 
great things from the young rising S.E. racing men, and 
as to the attendances at the meets, he only hoped that 
next year they would be better even than this. 

Soon after this, and while we were blowing clouds and 
enjoying song, there was a rattle and sort of scuffle at the 
door. The trusty Stephen was dragging in another mem- 
ber, and terrific cheers and shouts were heard because 
record was thus beaten, and the red-letter day for Club 
meets was now fixed for the 29th September, 1883. 

" Auld Lang Syne " was the signal for the breaking up 
of our party, and everyone went home pleased with the 
memory of the season now over, pleased at the prospect of 
an equally agreeable one next year, and, moreover, pleased 
at the thought that he — ^yes, he himself— had been the 
beater of record, for without any one of us would it not 
have been a tie ? 

Happy S.K District ! H. G. A. Beown. 



WHY WE DON'T COME, 
We all know the economical use to which ''good 
intentions ''-are generally supposed to be put in another 
place, and we also know that ''excuses" are a class of 
literature not always very politely spoken of ; and it has 



sometimes occurred to this deponent how much the average 
D.C. has to answer for in these two directions. 

In the first place, how often does the postcard, or personal 
application of the D.C, induce a member who generally 
absents himself really to intend to come,'' an intention which 
he either expresses to the official in question, or records in 
the proper repository to be duly discarded when the time 
comes. Well this is bad enough, and the habit of not 
carrying out these intentions is enough to make a reason- 
ably energetic official shudder ; but it is nothing to his 
responsibilities in the second category. '' Why don't you 
come out ? " is a natural enough question. The hope of 
cornering a man and extorting a promise to attend is well 
worth the trouble ; but, there, the habitual non-attender is 
never cornered. Long '^practice has made him much too 
clever for that. It is probable that for the whole season, 
or longer, he has had a series of varied — ^very varied — 
''reasons," he never admits they are "excuses," for not 
attending. One day some friends were coming to tennis, 
and " his people " would have made such a row if he had 
come ; another, he did think he could have come, but his 
old friend What's-his-name begged him to play in the 
" old Who-do-you-call-it's " cricket match because he was 
in such a hole, and how could he decline ? If it hadn't 
been the old Who-do-you-call-it's he would have come, 
of course ; and so on to the end of the chapter. The river 
makes men more callous ; they admit that they have 
" taken to the river," and clear off a lot of runs at one go 
in that fashion ; and the man of many friends is also a 
very difficult subject to tackle — they always are "particular 
friends," and give some quite "original" point to the 
delinquent's absence, till the D.C. is tempted to wonder — 
is " there a nearer one still and a dearer one," as the poet 
has put it in rather a different connection. 

Another class of member has a difficulty with his machine 
week after week. The excuses vary — something is bent ; 
the maker will not return it ; the tyre is off, or loose ; a 
young brother has been letting it fall et hoc genus omne. 
And then we have, though we hardly like to admit it, the 
man whose machine is a bright one, so he doesn't like to 
bring it out in the rain. It is a poor excuse, and deceives 
no one ; but there are few but the very regular attenders who 
believe that bad weather does not spoil a run. It doesn't, 
or at least anyone who attends a Club run on a wet day 
says so, and the very fact that so many men are unwilling 
to admit mere weather as a " reason " tends to show that 
they hardly feel it a sufficient one, yet we fear that it is 
after all one chief and prime cause of absence with all but 
the incorrigible. 

I cannot close without one more; it is unique but 
picturesque, and it is genuine. The member deeply regrets, 
but he has observed three large spider's webs of antique 
structure spun in and among the spokes of his bicycle, and 
from back to front wheel. Of course he can't move the 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



229 



machine, as it would probably occasion a dreadful catas- 
trophe if the spider family were at home ; he has therefore 
sent in a formal notice to quit, which he trusts will receive 
due attention and save legal expense — and in the meantime 
his D.G. waits expectant. Ooethe. 

To the Editor of the London Bicych Club Gazette, 
Sir, — I was sorry to see it expressly assumed by the 
writer of the leading article in the Gazette of Sept. 20th 
(which I have only just seen) that road racing is in itself 
positively illegal. I believe that to ensure a conviction 
under the Highway Act, either an obstruction must be 
proved, or else furious driving " to the common danger** 
As neither of these annoyances to the public seem to have 
been caused even by the late open tricycle road races, I 
should doubt whether the police authorities have not acted 
ultra vires in issuing their recent notice. I am by no 
means defending open road races on principle, but I do 
think it a great pity to assume, as the writer of the above 
mentioned article appears to assume, that we have been 
breaking the law for the last seven years. I think that 
bicyclists and tricyclists are, as a class, the best behaved 
and most careful of wayfarers, and that they are generally 
too timid in asserting their rights. — Yours, etc., 

D. T. C. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir, — I should like, with your permission, to add a few 
notes to the very interesting accounts which have lately 
appeared in your columns. 

1. Leeming Lane, far from being habitually bad, has 
the reputation of being one of the best roads in Yorkshire. 
Certainly nothing could have been much better than it was 
in the summer of '79. 

2. A traveller between Harrogate and Bipon should on 
no account omit, as your correspondent seems to have done, 
seeing Fountain's Abbey, which stands only some two miles 
off the road. 

3. In Ripon '* Yorkshire Pudding " is tojbe had at[its best. 

4. The farmers were quite right in saying that the Ash- 
bourne road from Buxton is the best in the neighbourhood, 
and it did not strike me as so very execrable. 

5. So ardent a bather should not have missed the Buxton 
swimming bath ; it is a particularly enjoyable one. 

6. The Punch and Judy show at the same place well 
deserves the tribute paid to it. It is, without exception, the 
finest thing in the whole range of the P. and J. profession. 

7. Stoueleigh Abbey, as well as Warwick Castle, is shown, 
and is well worth waiting for. 

8. The road between Bath and Wells is no more direct 
by Radstock than by Marksbury and High Littleton, each 
being twenty miles. The former goes right over Coombe 
Down, and is extremely hilly all the way. For the latter, 



go along either Bristol road and turn to the left at the 
"Globe Inn." 

9. The " Old George" at Glastonbury, though picturesque, 
is not at all a good place at which to have dinner. 

10. At Taunton the C. T. C. house is, I believe, good, 
but is quite eclipsed by the Coffee Palace which is indeed 
palatial, and is much more used by bicyclists. 

11. The reason why cider is difficult to get in Devon and 
Somerset in August is that the old is finished and the new 
not yet brewed. I have sometimes enquired in vain at three 
or four places in succession. 

12. Exeter to Dawlish I should have said was one of the 
best bits in South Devon. Perhaps the epithet "most 
hilly" applies only to the rest of the way to Torquay, 
which is so. — Yours truly, J. S. W. 

AN AIMLESS RAMBLE. 

(GONTIKUBD FROM PAOI 210.) 

On the following morning I got up at 7.30, having had a 
grand sleep for 9} hours, and while digesting my breakfast 
I roamed through the town of Olney in search of the 
picturesque. I regret to say that my search was ineffectual, 
the only noteworthy object being a shop window filled with 
memorials of Cowper, who, it appears, was bom here, in the 
shape of wooden pincushions and needlebooks ; the con- 
nection between poetry and haberdashery was not so obvious 
as to make me anxious to take any away with me. 

The road to Northampton was a succession of slopes for 
two miles, then level till it sejoined the main road, which I had 
left overnight. The surface was fair to within two or three miles 
of Northampton, when it got very bumpy and wet. I walked 
through the town, which I found quite as unpleasant as usual. 
I have always thought that if the lower classes in that 
abode of lasts were to wash more and swear less the 
casual stranger might find the town more attractive than 
it is at present. As I had already been to Leicester through 
Market Harborough, I thought I would try the alternative 
route through Welford, which turns to the left 1^ miles out 
of the town. The surface was splendid at first, in fact it 
was good all the way to Leicester; there was, however, 
hardly a level hundred yards for several miles, slope following 
slope in almost endless succession, though none of them 
were severe, with the exception of one about a mile in 
length near Spratton, which the heat made rather un- 
pleasant. I stopped at Thomby (24), and again at 
Wigston (40), both times for a drink, the weather being far 
too hot for solid food. Leicester I had the misfortune to 
find en fite, the town being full of circuses, volunteers, 
and other festive folk ; the result was that all the women 
of the town had turned out, each with four or five 
babies, and planted themselves in a double line down 
the gutter of the main street. I should think there 
were 25,000 babies present on that occasiob, and all 
of them we^e yelUng I I was glad when I found myself 



Digitized by 



230 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



outside the town ; I don't like babies. The surface to 
Loughborough was fair, with a bumpy half mile or so here 
and there ; there were a few pretty stiff slopes, but after 
Mount Sorrel the road became more level. At Lough- 
borough I turned to the right for Nottingham, the turning 
iu the (own being rather hard to find. With the exception 
of a few clear spaces the road was very loose the whole way 
into Nottingham, almost dangerously so in parts ; but the 
M.I.P. steadied the machine wonderfully, and quite did 
away with a slight tendency to kick, which is one of the 
very few faults it possesses. There is a bad hill down at 
Bunney, very steep, and covered with loose stones ; this is 
the only one that needs a dismount between Loughborough 
and Nottingham. Two traction engines with a number of 
waggons were standing at the top, having apparently just 
climbed the hill ; though how they managed it I put 
down as being " one of those things which no fellow can 
understand." I put up in Nottingham at the "Wellington" 
(C.T.C.), which was comfortable but very noisy, being situate 
just opposite the Midland Station ; the result was, I slept 
about one hour out of the whole night. Distance for day, 
68 miles. 

Next morning it was raining hard and showed no signs 
of clearing up till past noon, when I started for Mansfield 
in a slight shower. Trusting too much to my former recol- 
lections of the town I took a wrong turning, and had gone 
a mile or so along the Alfreton Road before I discovered my 
mistake ; but after burrowing about in back slums for some 
time I emerged on the Mansfield Road with the determi- 
nation to make more use of my tongue for the future. 
There are three or four slopes up out of Nottingham with 
slight falls in between, the surface being extremely bad, 
partly, I daresay, owing to the rain, but chiefly to the very 
sparing use of the stone-breaker's hammer. I suppose we 
must wait till the millenium for parish authorities to find 
out that stones as big as melons are not the best things 
wherewith to make a highway. After Redhill (5) the surface 
got better, and the improvement became more marked as I 
advanced further into Sherwood Forest— or, rather that 
country which once was Sherwood Forest, for a great portion 
has been transformed into arable land, or, at all events, 
despoiled of its finest timber. Six miles from Mansfield 
there is a long slope up, about two miles in length, with a 
very loose bit in the middle, and then after a fall there is a 
second rise of about three quarters of a mile covered with 
stones from top to bottom. Judging from the small amount 
of traffic I saw on the road, I should say there is a great pro- 
bability that the next generation will find it in precisely the 
same condition. At this pointthe weather and the roads, and 
the fact that I had nothing to eat since breakfast, combined 
to make me look anxiously about for some means of getting 
a meal, but houses there were none, so in despair I took 
refuge in a pipe, which I have often found to be by no 
means a bad way of appeasing a hunger which cannot be 



gratified. At last Mansfield was reached, and a large tea 
being quickly disposed of I began to consider what part of 
of the world I should make for next. I was assisted in my 
deliberations by two Yorkshiremen, who said the Rother- 
ham Road was very bad, and advised me to make for 
Chesterfield. It turned out on cross-examination that 
their remarks applied to the paths at the side and not to 
the roads themselves, and, in fact, they seemed rather 
puzzled that I should want any information at all about 
the latter. But it is only over Lancashire cobbles that my 
morality utterly vanishes, and accordingly I scorned their 
suggestions. Chesterfield, however, seemed a comfortable 
distance, so I took their advice and the left-hand road in 
the town. For the first two miles it was level, but almost 
immediately after entering Derbyshire the hills began, and 
continued all the way to Chesterfield. There was only one, 
however, up which I had to walk, viz., a long ascent, loose 
and steep at the bottom, about eight miles from Mansfield. 
The surface was bad the whole way, but I was in that con- 
tented state of mind which takes no account of bad roads, 
and endured the joltings with an equanimity begotten of 
much tea and buttered toast. The scenery was very pretty 
to within two miles of Chesterfield, where I entered the 
black country, which, with its tall chimneys, roaring furnaces, 
and mountains of ashes, presents an appearance rather in- 
teresting than beautiful. I reached Chesterfield at six, and 
made my way to the Angel " in the market place, then, 
having changed my clothes and borrowed a prayerbook from 
the landlady, went to church. The building is, as the 
guidebooks say, " by no means destitute of architectural 
pretensions," but is chiefly noticeable on account of its 
spire, which is twisted and distorted in a most remarkable 
manner, so that, says Paterson, "it appears to lean in 
whatever direction it is approached." One side is marked 
by a large dint, as though it had been struck by an 
enormous hammer. Report says that the builder com- 
mitted suicide as soon as he had finished it, declaring that 
the work of his life was accomplished, for he could never 
build another spire to touch it. And I do not think he 
could. The town is quaint and old-fashioned, with steep 
winding streets which rather reminded lae of Durham. 
After supper I was lucky enough to fall in with a traveller 
who seemed to be a sort of polyglot Mudie on the roam, 
for producing Moliere's Plays, Scott's Novels, and Faust 
from his bag, he offered me any I liked to read. I read 
half a play and then, having interviewed the landlord, 
boots, and ostler on the subject of the weather, went 
gloomily to bed, because they persisted in asserting that 
the following day would be fine. Distance for day, 28 miles. 

Monday, 6th. Of course it was raining when I got up ; 
that ill-omened prophecy the night before had irritated the 
Clerk of the Weather, and he had turned on the tap to 
show how little he cared for morbal prognostications. 
Having examined my map at breakfast, I decided on going 



Digitized by V^O^^ltT 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



231 



to Sheffield, and had no reason to grumble at the road for 
several miles. It led through a series of mining villages 
for the first four miles, the inhabitants of which — ^it being 
Bank Holiday — were engaged in sports and pastimes of 
various descriptions, each of the men having a dog at his 
heels and a bludgeon under his arm — ^two unvarying 
accompaniments of the miner out for a holiday. The 
surface was by no means bad and the hills slight, though 
as I got near Sheffield they became steeper. All the way 
the natural beauty of the country was spoilt by the smoke 
and cinders of the mines, ** cloud-capped towers " rising as 
far as the eye could see ; only the " towers " were brick 
chimneys and the "clouds" poured out of their tops. 
About two miles from Sheffield the rain, which hitherto 
had been slight, came down in torrents, and in a few 
minutes I was wet through; this made me cross, for 
when I want a bath I prefer having it in private and 
undressing first ; to use the Queen's high road as a 
common shower bath struck me as showing a want of 
respect to Her Majesty's possessions. Induced by these 
excellent reasons, I made up my mind to seek fresh fields and 
pastures new where there was less rain and fewer bumps. 
Sheffield, as seen through a vista of falling rain, struck me 
as being the most unpleasant town I had ever been in ; 
for miles and miles there is nothing but paving stones and 
tramlines, the former being very greasy. I walked through 
the town, having put on my saddle-cover, which is, in my 
opinion, a greater blessing than even " the Pickwick, 
the Owl, and the Waverley Pen." Having reached the 
centre of the town I stopped at the " King's Head" (B.T.C), 
where I could get no fire to dry my things, and only got a 
drink after a long delay. Being anxious not to leave a tag 
on my map I decided to make for the nearest place I knew, 
and then, if the weather was no better, to take the train 
home. I accordingly started for Doncaster and walked 
nearly the whole way to Rotherham (6 miles), the road 
being at first greasy paving stones, and then without 
exception the worst macadam I have ever been on. By 
dint of sitting on my M.I.P., however, and riding carefully, 
I managed to avoid being thrown off, and the mere thought 
that I was leaving Sheffield was enough to console me to a 
great extent. Nowadays, if anyone irritates me, I do 
not indulge in Saxon expletives, but merely tell them to 
" go to Sheffield ; " if I am in a great rage, I add ** on a 
wet day." Human malice cannot add an3rthing to the 
latter wish. 

After Rotherham the road got softer and less bumpy, 
with two or three stiff hills ; but having at last got away 
from mines and factories I felt equal to any amount of 
mud, which was just as well, as I had plenty of it. About 
five miles from Rotherham a long up-hill begins, the top of 
which is not reached for more than three miles ; after this 
it is falling ground all the way into Doncaster, a distance 
of four miles. The road improved after the top of the hill, 



and the sun having come out and the rain left off, I found 
the last four miles very pleasant. I stopped at the 
" Elephant " in Doncaster for some lunch, where I found 
everything very good and the people most attentive. The 
town was full of horses — ^a fair being held there that day — 
and as one man was generally considered enough to lead 
twelve or thirteen animals, every street presented a scene 
of hopeless'confusion. Having now joined my tag, I held 
myself at liberty to take the train if I chose, but the evening 
seemed likely to turn out fine, so I again changed my mind 
and rode south for Bawtry, where I turned to the left for 
Gainsborough, in Lincolnshire. The Great North Road 
was perfectly dry, and seemed like a cinder path after the 
bumps of the morning ; and though the surface grew rather 
worse after Bawtry, yet the beauty of the country made 
amends for that. Eight miles from Gainsborough there was 
a steep up-hill, from the top of which there ought to have 
been a good view but was not. It was 7.30 by the time I 
reached Gainsborough, but as the next place at which I 
could get a bed would be Lincoln (20 miles), and having 
besides a general distrust of Lincolnshire roads, I deter- 
mined to stay where I was, and accordingly put up at the 
" White Hart." Gainsborough struck me as being one of 
the quietest, most out-of-the-way places I had ever seen. 
As the only way of accounting for the total absence of 
population, I asked the landlady if the town had been 
visited with a pestilence ; but as she said it had not, I put 
the matter down as a social conundrum, to be thought out 
in leisure moments. Distance for day, 52 miles. 

Tuesday, 7th. I was anxious to leave Gainsborough by 
the Market Raisin Road — ^that is to say, go due east for 
about ten miles, until I reached the London and Hull Road, 
then to turn down it to Lincoln ; unfortunately, however, 
there happened to be a number of cross-roads leading direct 
to Lincoln, and as the natives had never heard of Market 
Raisin, and could not understand my wishing to go round 
by the main road, I was led a fine dance through the town 
before I found a man intelligent enough to understand what 
I wanted. He told me there was not a better road in the 
kingdom than the Market Raisin one ; and if the good man 
spoke true, I am afraid the kingdom must be in a very bad 
way, for I found the road about as rough as they make them, 
composed chiefly of huge rocks partially submerged in a sort 
of whitish clay, not unlike oolite. After reaching the Hull 
Road, and turning to the right for Lincoln, the surface grew 
slightly better, but the whole way it was shaky, and com- 
municated that unceasing vibration to the machine 
which is felt in going over badly set wood pavement, 
and which is even more annoying I think than the occa- 
sional jolts in macadam. The road was perfectly straight 
with a slope here and there, and a fine panorama of 
the ugliest country in England extending on both sides. 
For miles and miles not a human being was in sight, but 
at last I caught up a tourist who, with guidebook and 



Digitized by 



232 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



timbrella, was tramping along the grass at the side of the 
road. I concluded he was searching for mushrooms, for I 
could see no other "objects of interest in the locality" 
where he was. Five miles, out I caught sight of Lincoln 
cathedral, far ahead, jast at the point where the two sides 
of the road seemed to meet in one. On entering the city 
I was surprised to find it so small and quiet, but after 
passing through the cathedral close I discovered that the 
real town, with its legion of chimneys, lay in a valley at 
the foot of the hill on which the cathedral stands. The 
south front of the cathedral, being the one turned towards 
•the town, is very finely carved, and would well repay a 
lengthy inspection.

represented the S.B. at Hampton Court in 1878, but in the 
account in Vol. I. of the Gazette the number b given as 32.

largest number of S.R members, has beaten the record ; and 
that it was accomplished on such a miserable day as was 
Saturday is very much to his credit

THE ANNUAL DINNER. 

The mention of a Club Ball has upon some of our 
members a rather similar effect to that which a red rag is 
supposed to have on a bull. Like Polyphemus, "they rage, 
they melt, they bum," and will not be satisfied till some 
opponent has figuratively bit the dust. The title at the 
head of this column is not indeed such a stormy petrel, but 
will awaken widely different feelings in different members. 
There is the severe ascetic — "The London Bicycle Club 
was formed to bicycle, and not to dine, to dance, or to play 
tenuis." This class of member looks with some suspicion 
on Club Teas as relaxations from the narrow ways of cycling 
virtue to primrose paths of dalliance ; last year's Picnics in 
the South- West filled him with horror, and he was found 
at Stamford Bridge last month in a state of collapse at the 
idea of La Crosse and Lawn Tennis appearing in the pro- 
gramme of the London Bicycle Club. However, he is not 
so dangerous as he looks, and will doubtless bury the 
hatchet and smoke the pipe of peace in St. James's Hall 
without audible complaint or serious qualms of conscience. 

li\ great contrast to him is the sybarite of bicycling, who 
regards the bicycle club mainly as an instrument for pro- 
curing forms of amusement other than bicycling. Dear to 
him is the Lawn Tennis Tournament, a little marred by the 



proviso that ties should not be played off on Saturdays. He 
always has six lady's tickets for the Race Meeting, and is 
resplendent at the S.E. Ball, while the Dramatic Society 
considers him one of its pillars. Indeed, were it not for 
these little diversions, many members of the Club would 
be unaware of his existence. Needless to say, he considers the 
Club Dinner an admirable institution, if only there was not 
quite so much " wheel " in the speeches. 

Between these extremes we find the moderate member, 
who, if we are not mistaken, regards a well managed Club 
Dinner as a very desirable thing. There is a good old 
custom, unfortunately fading away before the influence of 
scientific farming, by which after the last sheaves have come 
to the barn in the laden waggon in the mellow light of the 
harvest moon, farmer and labourers, all who have shared in 
the work, sit down to the Harvest Supper and toast their 
" Harvest Home." So at the end of our season of sport, 
when the last meets have met, and the few last postcards of 
the District Captain have fallen with the withered leaves of 
autumn, we too may well gather round the festive board, 
and celebrate in company our " Harvest Home." Members 
from town and country, London and the Universities, North 
and South, East and West (for the Club Dinner has the 
useful purpose of reminding us of the existence of one or 
two Districts we might have forgotten), all may meet and talk 
over sport past and to come. Our Captain gives ns the 
history of the year, and has the privilege, which no one 
else should have, of making a long after-dinner speech. 
The musical talent of the Club delights us ; our leading 



Google 



Digitized by 



234 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



members exhibit themselves oratorically on their hind legs 
with more — or less — fluency, as the case may be ; the toast- 
master performs the ** Comedy of Errors," and the wily 
waiter attempts to evade the " sixpenny rule." The only 
fault we have to find is that so few of our members come. 
True — ^we usually sit down 70 or 80 strong, but what is 
that to a Club of 240 or so. We have beaten some records 
this year, though fewer than we should have. Cannot we 
make a record at the Club Dinner, and ensure its success 
and our own pleasure ? 

COMMITTEES. 
At a meeting of the Committee held on Monday, 
29th September, the following gentlemen were elected 
members of the Club : — 

Name and Address. Proposer. Seconder. 

John Thomas Crier, 16, Bridge 

Ayenue, Hammenmith W. B. Sewell. H. Bamett. 

E. Warner Lnmley, Woodaide, 

Surbiton H. G. Pigoti. M. D. Rucker, Jon. 

It was decided to hold a Smoking Concert on one of the 
first ten days of November. H. G. A. Brown, A. Lloyd, 
S. P. Smith, and F. M. Williams were appointed as a Sub- 
Committee for the purpose, to which the name of R. Q. 
Francis was afterwards added 

It was also decided that the Committee present to the 
C.T.C. Consulate, at Geneva', the copies of the Gazette 
containing Mr. Cobb's tour. 

The Editor was authorized to present, on behalf of 
the Club, a bound volume of the Gazette for 1882, to the 
Library of the N.C.U. 

At a Meeting of the Committee, held on Monday, 
29th October, at the " City Arms Tavern," the resignation 
of H. W. Bridges was accepted with much regret. 
Guy Du Maurier's resignation was also accepted. 

It was decided to hold a Club Paperchase on foot on 
Boxing Day, and to give three prizes for the first three 
hounds. 

The question of prizes came under discussion, but it was 
ultimately decided that they should be given. 

The question of the place of our Annual Race Meeting 
in 1884 was then discussed, it being necessary to come to 
some immediate decision, owing to the various grounds being 
engaged so long beforehand. After a long discussion, it 
was resolved : — 

That the Captain be infltnieted to engage the Stamford 
(' Bridge Groondfl for oar 1884 Annual Race Meeting for some 
" oonvenient Saturday in June." 

E. S. Pbioe, Hon. Sec.

OCTOBER RUNS IN THE SOUTH-EAST. 
October 6th. Though unofficial by name as well as by 
nature, the run to Westerham was attended by no less than 
thirteen men. The absence of the ordinary routine was 
noticeable in the way that men dropped in quite casually in 
ones, twos and threes at the " King's Anna" First Barker 
and Oswald on the tandem rode triumphantly down the hill 
into the town, followed at a respectful distance by Day and 
the D.C. Tea was laid for four, when McRae, Hartridge, 
and W. G. Oswald appeared. Soon after F. B. Smith rode in 
from Sevenoaks, followed by Beck, with F. L. Adam on anew 
purchased tricycle, and accompanied by his terrier. Trap, a 
pattern toall members' dogs, and lastly the brothers Whattou. 
On account of our unexpected increase in numbers, the supply 
of provisions was not quite equal to the demand ; still a fair 
meal was enjoyed. The homeward journey commenced at 
an early hour for South-East runs. Present : F. L. Adam, 
Barker, Beck, Day, Hartridge, McRae, J. C. Oswald, W. C. 
Oswald, F. B. Smith, A. B. W. Whatton, J. S. Whatton, 
P. H. Williams, and R. G. Francis. 



October 13th. A splendid afternoon favoured the run 
to Brighton. Beck and the D.C. left the Fire Station at 
a quarter past four, and were accompanied as far as Crawley 
by S. Herbert, Whealler, and W. C. Oswald. Riding 
through without a dismount, they lighted their lamps at 
Hand Cross and reached the "Glo'ster," Brighton, at a 
quarter past eight, after a glorious moonb'ght run for the 
latter half of the way. Nobody else appearing they pro- 
ceeded to discuss a substantial tea, and after sitting up 
until twelve, Itetired to bed somewhat depressed with the 
idea that two at Brighton was not much of an attendance. 
It was somewhere after that mystic hour when the Captain, 
with McRae and Whealler in attendance, gained admittance. 
It came to light afterwards that they had fallen in with 
Barker, J. C. Oswald, and J. F. Potter at Crawley, aud 
after a protracted carousal at the ''George," which did 
not break up until past ten, had followed in the tracks 
of the two who had gone before. Next day, a southerly 
wind lent its assistance on the homeward stretch, but 
the trio who tarried by the sea-waves — which were not 
sad for once in a way — until late in the day came in 
for more wet than they bargained for. Present : A. Barker, 
Ashley Barrett (Captain), G. F. Beck, S. Herbert, D. McRae, 
J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, Whealler, and R. G. Francis. 



October 20th. Though unable to take part in the run, 
the D.C. was at his post at the Fire Station, at the time 
appointed. The tandem was again to the fore, and its riders 
positively revelled in the mud which was the predominating 
feature of the roads. In spite of this, as well as patches of 
stones which had begun to make their appearance, six men 
followed out the whole run to Bletchingley, and had tea at 





the " White Hart." Present : Barker, Clements, S. Herbert, 
McRae, J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, L. Potter, F. H. 
Williams, one visitor, and R. G. Francis. 



October 27th. The last unofficial run fell on a glorious 
day — quite genial for so late in the season. Six men left 
Croydon soon after a quarter past four, and were met by 
Beck, Day, and the D.C. on West Wickham Hill. The 
ascent on to Hayes Common proved uncommonly glutinous, 
and altogether too much for Barker's backbone, which 
suddenly gave way just as the rider was showing the young 
idea how hill riding ought to be done. Having deposited 
the remains of his steed in a cart shed, the unfortunate one 
was not to be done out of his tea, and pursuing his way on 
foot, appeared at the Maxwell Arms " just as the rest of 
the party had commenced operations. After tea came pipes 
and music, and though the piano was not slightly but 
muchly below pitch, Mr. DoyJe, of the Rovers B. C, was 
equal to the occasion, and his songs and accompaniments 
were much appreciated both by the audience and by those 
who gave assistance in the singing line, among whom J. C. 
Oswald, J. F. Potter, Beck, and Day showed their usual 
talents. It was close on nine o'clock before a veiy pleasant 
evening was brought to' an end by a general dispersal in the 
direction of home. Present : Barker, Beck, Day, Clements, 
S. Herbert, McRae, J. C. Oswald, W. C. Oswald, J. F. 
Potter, F. B. Smith, Whealler, F. H. Williams, one visitor, 
and R. G. Fbancis, District Captain. 



UNOFFICIAL RUN IN THE N.W. 

Now that the official runs for this year are over, some of 
the members of the above District actually expressed a 
wish to get up a scratch ride ; so, on Saturday the 13th 
October, Elgood and Jackson were discovered outside the 
*' Park Road Hotel," or, as it is more generally known. 
Tally Ho ! Corner, at four o'clock. 

It having been decided to go to Little Berkhempstead 
a start was made, and scarcely had we arrived at Barnet 
when Lyne and Stunt were overtaken, having prudently 
avoided the high road and chosen the better path along 
lanes. The quintette then rode steadily on until their 
destination was reached, a few minutes after the arrival of 
R B. Hough and Lloyd. In consequence of the absence 
of the " good lady of the house," tea was not commenced ' 
for about an hour. Cyclists are proverbially hungry, but 
on this day the appetites seemed to be very easily appeased, 
though the Dean of Bangor would have held up his hand 
in horror at the manner in which the tea appeared and | 
disappeared. During repast, various men kept alighting at 
the inn like autumn leaves. Eelsey and Long were the j 
first, then the Continental tourist of the W. District, and I 
lastly, but not least, our Hon. Sec, who soon after leaving 
home broke the crank of his bicycle, returned, borrowed

a tri., ran over a child, then further on broke the spring, 
had it repaired at Barnet, and arrived triumphant. A 
pleasant evening liaving been spent lamps were lighted, 
and in spite of a diversion being attempted by our Sub- 
Editor a mount was accomplished, and for anything I know 
to the contrary all got home sober and serene. Present : 
Elgood, R. B. Hough, Jackson, Kelsey, Langmore, Lloyd, 
Long, Price, Stuart, and 

A. H. LowN, District Captain. 



THE BOXING DAY PAPERCHASE. 
The 'above will be run throughout on foot, and it is 
hoped all members will assist in making it a success ; also 
that they will not allow themselves to be prevented from 
attending by. possible bad weather, since it will be carried 
out in spite of any difficulties of that nature. This being 
a case in which " the more the merrier" applies, I hope, in 
addition to coming out in force, they will also bring with 
them our friends and future members — ^their brothers — and 
also any other friend who may be interested in the 
sport. A Sub-Committee to arrange all details has 
been appointed, and a full notice will be published 
in next Gazette. At present I may say that it is 
under consideration to start the hares from Croydon at 
noon, and, after running a circular course for about four 
hours, to return to the starting point, where, at the *' King's 
Arm»," Katharine Street, hunters and hunted will dine 
amicably at 5 p.m., with presentation of prizes to the first 
three hounds, and a little music to follow before returning 
to town. This is a new departure, and I trust all L.B.C. 
men will do their best to aid in making it a success. On 
behalf of the Sub-Committee I will say that no pains shall 
be spared to provide good sport and a pleasant evening for 
our members and guests. 

Ashley W. Babrett, Captain. 



LEAVES FROM THE DIARY OP A D.C. 

The trials of the office of District Captain have been so 
often alluded to in the columns of the Gazette, that now 
my active service is over, and I have leisure to chew the cud 
of reflection, I am tempted to give the Club the benefit of 
my experiences in the hope of removing some of the 
erroneous, or, rather, exaggerated ideas that exist on the 
subject. I am fully aware of the roseate tints that ever 
hang over the stem realities of the past, and that like a 
mountaineer who has climbed some precipitous ascent, I 
may be inclined to look lightly on the toil now the task is 
accomplished. Still after making allowance for this tendency, 
I hope to show that a District Captain is not that poor 
unenviable creature many suppose him to be, condemned to 
a work both wearisome and unprofitable. 

The most prominent of the unpleasant features perhaps 
in the office are its disappointments. To take an instance 
which is only too common — the weather disappointment. It 



may be that the morning of some favourite meet dawns fine 
and bright, " the dear little dickey-birds carol away " under 
the D.C.'s bedroom window. He, too, feels blithe and gay, 
for has he not done his utmost to secure a good Meet? 
After breakfast the sky has clouded over ; at luncheon time 
it drizzles; at three o'clock — that critical moment in 
Saturday runs — it rains a deluge ! The spirits of our 
unfortunate friend fall, and reside at zero. He, probably, 
will spend the afternoon plougliing through the mud alone, 
or with one or two companions as moist and as miserable- 
looking as himself. He will, we can imagine, try and look 
cheerful as he sits down to tea, with a Niagara in miniature 
flowing down his back — a feeling as of a cold poultice about 
his nether limbs. But as he works his way homeward, the 
further he gets the more the feeling comes home to him 
that it was not really so very bad after all, while next 
morning he finds himself saddled with the conviction that 
he had had an enjoyable afternoon, and that the other men 
were simpletons for staying at home. Be this as it may, 
he had done his duty. 

There is another disappointment, which, though not so 
conspicuous to a casual observer, yet has a nescio quid 
atnari, and a marvellous subtlety in the sting it inflicts. 
In this case the weather leaves nothing to desire ; the roads 
follow suit — and still they come not ! Granted that a wet 
afternoon is excuse sufficient, how now ? Of course pre- 
texts will be fortlicoming in shoals, probably of the fashion 
so ably described by "Goethe" in the last number of the 
Gazette. But surely there must be some men in a District, 
even if it be numerically small, who would hail the oppor- 
tunity of a ride under these circumstances, however much 
they may dread liquefaction in wet weather. 

In the majority of cases of total failure I do not hesitate 
to say that the fault is that of none other than the District 
Captain himself. Of course he does not see it in that light. 
If men were certain that be the weather fine, wet, or doubt- 
ful their chief would be at the starting place at the time 
appointed ; that there was always one man at least who 
followed the run in its entirety, however uninviting the 
prospect, is it to be imagined that he would perform his 
penance alone ? A District Captain has only to create this 
confidence, and he will soon cease to complain that " men 
will not turn up at the meets." Of coui-se if he who is 
endued with authority fights shy of the rain, or falls a prey 
to the influences of those " other attractions," ought he to 
be surprised to find his flock dwindling from similar causes ? 
Example is better than bushels of postcards, and a regular 
attendance is worth more than all the " whips in the 
Gazette that were ever printed. 

As to those other attractions, a D.C. is but mortal ; but 
though in his weaker moments the whisper of the siren 
voices may fall with a soft sweet cadence on his ear, what- 
ever in his heart of hearts may be his opinion on rival 
pleasures and pursuits to his District and Club, he will 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 237 



appear the stern disciplinarian who views such things with 
a fierce contempt. There will always be a certain amount 
of grievance caused by those incorrigibles, denizens of every 
District, who will not be entrapped into attending a single 
meet by any device or snare, however artfully laid ; who 
persist in looking on their old Club-run dajrs as " dreams 
of the past," which they are not apparently anxious to 
dream again, still this is a state of things which should not 
be altogether unexpected, and cannot be classed under the 
heading of disappointments. The two cases given above 
may be taken as fair samples, sufficient to show the seamy 
side of a D.C.'s existence. 

In agreeable contrast, there are the numerous pleasant 
surprises which blossom spontaneously along his path. When 
a fine afternoon treads hard on the heels of a wet morning ; 
when, in spite of contrary winds and weather, a fair sprink- 
ling of the district show that they are not all of the butter- 
fly order ; or the appearance of some familiar face long 
missed from the tea table. These are few out of many in- 
stances, any one of which would be sufficient to gladden 
the heart of a District Captain. As the season goes on 
he will find himself surrounded by an inner circle of 
followers, a kind of corps d'elite, who will assist the 
runs, not by their presence only, but by their in- 
fluence on others. Not the best pleasing will be 
the quasi patriarchal feeling with which he will come 
to regard the younger members of his District. There 
will probably be more or less of the wobbler about 
them when they first join, but soon they begin to feel their 
legs, and ere long are winning laurels for themselves and 
District. No need for reminding postcards in the case of 
these. The D.C. knows they will come to the meets because 
they like them. It may be said it is because they know no 
other amusement. If this be so, then their ignorance is 
his heart's delight, and their inexperience one of the main- 
stays of the Club. 

However hard his work, and whatever the visible result, 
a man who has once been District Captain can never look 
on it as time and energy misspent. Ho will have acquired 
a stake in the Club, which will last long after mere cyclical 
mania. He may leave his home or even his country, but 
his interest in the old District — ^liis District that was — will 
remain " a thing of gladness and a joy for ever." 

DOBO. 



CAMBRIDGE NOTES. 
This is by no means as dull a season here in bicycling 
circles as is the case elsewhere. We came up for the most 
part during the second week of October, and observed that 
the first fixture of importance was the Club Run to 
Haverhill, returning to the favourite " Swan " at Linton 
to tea. There were present eleven members, a full moon, 
and a howling wind, and we discovered that an unusually 
large number of men had been touring in the " Long." 



The Club "Double," which attended, has been most 
popular, and has already (in six months) paid ofi' two-thirds 
of its original cost. It has also, in the hands of energetic 
members, been instrumental in introducing to cycling 
certain learned professors, deans and other dignitaries. 

It was decided after a close division in Committee to con- 
tinue for this year, at all events, the Fifty Miles Road Race, 
and it was got ofi" on Friday 26th, resulting as follows : — 
J. S. Whatton, Trinity (holder), 1 ; A. W. Rumney, Trin. 
Hall, 2 ; C. W. Pagan, Trin. Hall, 3. There was not much 
wind, but the roads could hardly have been worse, and 
these were the only starters. The representative coun- 
cillor and the Irish tourist shared the lead for 35 miles, 
when Whatton drew away up a hill, and finally won by 
4f miu. Rumney rode strongly, but lacks judgment, 
and is somewhat careless of stones and other obstacles 
by which he was several times nearly prostrated. The 
winner was thrown in the first half-mile by a dog which 
emerged &om under a cart, but by a flying dismount 
over the handles he managed to land on his feet, 
a bent bar and two broken hind-wheel spokes being the 
only injuries. He rode a 58-inch " Rucker;" Rumney also 
rode a " Rucker," and Fagan a " Surrey Machinist." 

The path races are fixed for November 5th, and among 
them is the first tricycle race ever promoted by the 
University Club. It is to be worked as is a time race in 
rowing by means of " stations," and the Club is canvassing 
among the C.U.R.V. to supplement its one pistol, which 
itself is not as deadly a weapon as could be desired. Four 
will be wanted. The Freshmen's event should be a very 
warm thing ; the chief aspirants are a Magdalen man on a 
62-inch " Humber," a member from Trinity Hall with a 
60-inch " Rudge " racer, and a brother of that L. C. Carr, 
whose finish with F. E. S. Perry at the last L.B.C. meeting 
will be remembered. The Two Miles Scratch will go to the 
elder CaiT or to A. B. W. Whatton, the former for choice. 
Neither E. H. Brown nor J. S. Whatton are in training. 

We are already appreciating our good fortune in having 
secured the services of Mr. J. W. L. Glaisher as our new 
President, and are confidently looking forward to a most 
successful season. Posqabon. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sir, — If reference is made to the Report of the Committee 
holding office in 1878, it will be found that the attendance 
of the S.E. at the Hampton Court Meet in that year was 
34. When writing the Echo to which " Porcupine " calls 
attention in the last issue, I thought that the Committee's 
Official Report was more likely to be correct than that 
published in the Gazette, and that preference should be 
given to the former as the higher authority. — Yours etc.. 

Rambler.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



7o the Editor of the London Bicycle Chib Gazette. 

Sir, — If we refer to the Report of the Committee for 
the year 1878, we find that in that year the Club severed 
its connection with the Bicycling Press and established 
the Gazette. We also find that at the close of the 
year the numerical strength of the Club was 240 active 
members. Now since the date that census was taken 
the Club has scarcely altered its numbers, and we are 
now as strong as then ; but in all the previous reports 
of the Committee we find, if we look at them in the 
Older they appeared, that the Club had been gaining 
numbers largely. Suddenly the growth of the Club 
appears to have ceased, and, curiously enough, the cessa- 
tion is coeval with the birth of our cherished Gazette 
and our severance with the press. Now it is surely a 
vital question, and one that should be calmly considered, 
whether we do right in having no relations with the 
Press, and whether the fact that the Club's name has 
not been kept before the public is the reason that we do 
not increase. That the L.B.O. is well known amongst 
clubs and clubmen no one will deny ; but is it well known 
amongst the rising generation of bicyclists?— the men 
that, as yet, belong to no club, but are on the look out 
for a good one to join. Surely our self-imposed isolation 
is the reason why so many ^ther clubs, whose names are 
constantly before the outside public in the Press, increase 
and multiply iu the way they appear to do. When a 
man first takes to bicycling it is natural tliat he should 
take in the journals dedicated to our sport, and from 
them gather something of the various clubs. If I may 
instance myself, I may say that when I first learned 
to ride I had a great wish to join a dub, and the first 
time I ever saw the words London Bicycle Club was 
in a periodical which gave a list of the various clubs 
and their officers then extant. Something iu the pro- 
spectus of the L.B.C. attracted me, and I did my best 
to make the acquaintance of a member and get put 
up for election. Surely my case is not an isolated one, 
and are we not doing ourselves great injury by our policy 
of abstention ? I do not wish to suggest that we should 
go to the other extreme and constantly land ourselves in 
print like Pears' Soap, but we might invite representatives 
of the principal paper or papers to our Dinner and our 
Smoking Concerts. The Dramatic Society has always in- 
vited members of the Press to its performances, and has 
never been forbidden to do so by the Committee, although 
the reports of the various papers have been reprinted in 
these columns, and the Committee cannot therefore plead 
ignorance of the doings of its planet. Why should the 
planet be allowed to do what the fixed (emphatically fixed) 
star is forbidden ? Is it too late to redeem the error of our 
ways, and to put aside the false pride which is pre3dng on 
our vitals ? — ^Yours, etc.. 

Imprimatur. 



A EIDE ACROSS FRANCE INTO SWITZERLAND. 
(1.) How to avoid Paris. 

Notwithstanding the disparaging remarks with reference 
to Foreign Tours which have occasionally appeared in the 
pages of this Gazette, I don't feel called upon to say much 
by way of apology for sending you a short account of our 
Franco-Swiss Tour of 1883. It is, of course, most desirable 
that men should know something of the beauties of their 
own country before going to view the sights of foreign lands. 
But this is not a charge which can be justly brought against 
cyclists generally ; our own Club contains many members 
who have visited almost every corner of the British Islands. 
One of your contributors sneers at the man who can be 
interested in studying "the dirty habits of foreigners." 
Well, if some of their habits may be objectionable, we might 
take a lesson from them in others, and the man who can 
repeat in these days the Johnsonian dictum ''As to manners, 
sir, they have none, and their customs are beastly," only 
shows himself to be very ignorant or very prejudiced, or both. 

Starting from Victoria at 8.40 a.m. on September 13th 
to catch a boat advertised to leave Newhaven at 10.30, we 
felt pretty confident of reaching Rouen before evening. But 
the funereal pace at which we left Newhaven at once showed 
us we had reckoned without the tides, and we learnt on 
enquiry that, as the tide was then falling, we could not 
possibly get into Dieppe harbour before 5.30 p.m. And so 
it proved ; we arrived off Dieppe about half-past four, but 
had to lay to for an hour before we got the signal to enter 
the harbour. By the time we had got our machines, etc., 
through the custom house it was past six, and it being 
obviously impossible to ride 36 miles in an hour of daylight, 
there was nothing for it but to make ourselves comfortable 
where we were. So after booking our portmanteau, con- 
taining greatcoats and walking boots, to Geneva by " grande 
Vitesse,'' we put up at the " Chariot d' Or " (B.T.C. house) 
in the Rue de la Barre, and found it satisfactory. 

Leaving Dieppe next morning at 8.30, after a light break- 
fast, we hoped to reach Rouen about twelve, but again we 
were disappointed, for on dismounting at the top of the steep 
hill leading into Maromme (6 miles from Rouen) Williams 
discovered that he had lost the screw which fixed his handle 
bar. After walking back along the road for nearly a mile 
the screw was found, but all attempts to fix it proved fruit- 
less. The machine had just returned from a " thorough 
overhaul " at the hands of the makers, and this ill-fitting 
screw was only the first of a series of breaks-down which 
bothered the owner and his companions throughout the 
tour. 

On reaching Roueu we first sought out a serrurier, in- 
tending to leave the machine to be repaired whilst we had 
lunch ; then Williams thought he would like to see the job 
finished, and it proved rather a longer one than might have 
been expected, so that it was past two before we got our . 
food, and the doctor, who could have eaten the serrurier 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



half an hour before, had got beyond the hungry stage and 
could eat uothing. After lunch we had a look at the 
Cathedral and St. Ouen — ^two out of the three had done 
Rouen pretty thoroughly the year before — and then made 
a start onward. We got on capitally for twenty miles, 
when the doctor began to discover that a brandy and soda 
and piece of dry bread was not much to bicycle on, and 
after struggling on for another couple of miles up and down 
hill, he came to a dead stop. We were still two miles from 
Gaillon and it was beginning to get dark, but on explaining the 
case to some country people who happened to pass, one of 
them very kindly got some bread from a neighbouring 
cottage, and the invalid quickly recovering himself we were 
not long in reaching Gaillon, where we put up at the 
" Hotel d'Evreux " and were soon enjoying a good supper. 
Distance from Dieppe, 62 miles. 

A few miles from Gaillon, on the road to Les Andelys, is 
the ruined Chateau Gaillard, worth visiting for the sake of 
the splendid view of the valley of the Seine to be had from 
its walls ; but unfortunately we had not time to make the 
excursion. Leaving Gaillon soon after eight, we made good 
progress till we were within three or four miles of Mantes. 
Here Williams' front wheel suddenly locked, and after 
condescending to be wheeled backwards for a short distance 
obstinately refused to move at all. As we were discussing 
the possibility of taking the wheel out there and then, an 
old woman came by in a country cart, and on being 
appealed to she readily consented to give the disabled 
machine and its owner a lift into Mantes, Silk and the 
Doctor riding on ahead to see about lunch. Just as this 
was ready Williams appeared, having had a terrible jolting 
on his springless conveyance; after lunch we borrowed 
some tools and took the machine to pieces, but could not 
discover the cause of its eccentric behaviour. 

This road to Paris has already been described in our 
Gazette, so I need say nothing about the 22 miles between 
Mantes and St. Germain. At the latter place we turned off 
to the right for Versailles (8 miles) where we arrived just 
at dusk, and put up at the " Hotel du Coin d*Or," in the 
Avenue de Sceaux CB.T.C), which I can only describe as 
fair. I can't, however, suggest a better, for I should fancy 
that the large hotels, of which there are several in Versailles, 
would be found very expensive. Distance from Gaillon, 
53 miles. 

I hope that what follows will not be misunderstood, or I 
shall incur the wrath of a good many of my friends. I 
don't wish to assert that Paris is not a very nice place, or 
that it is a place to be avoided as a general rule ; but it is 
not a paradise for the bicyclist. The outskirts and many 
of the main streets are paved, and on the one stretch of 
good road where itwould be pleasant — viz., from the Barri^re 
de TEtoile by the Champs Elysdes to the Place du Carrousel 
— riding is not allowed. Getting across Pari? with a bicycle 
is therefore a very tedious business, and involves walking a 



good many miles. Last year the majority of our party 
trained from St. Germain into Paris and out again to 
Mdlun, in which case there is only three and a half miles 
to walk — from the Northern terminus to that of the P.L.M.^ 
Company. Or you may ride on through St. Germain to 
St. Cloud, whence steamboats run every half hour through 
Paris to Bercy, which is close to Charenton, where you join 
the main road to Geneva, etc. ; but as this road is paved for 
ten miles out of Paris, there is still some walking to be done. 

Probably the best plan for the rider who only wants to 
get across Paris is to follow the route taken by the doctor 
last year and by the whole party this year, viz., from St. 
Germain to Versailles, and thence by a good road to 
Choisy-le-Roi, where you cross the Seine and join the 
Geneva road about six miles from Paris. To get to Versailles 
from St. Germain you turn to the right by the artillery 
barracks, down hill ; then there is a steep hill, paved, to 
climb through Marly, keeping to the left, still uphill, on 
reaching a reservoir. From the top of the hill it is an easy 
ride to Versailles, with one dismount for a short piece of 
pwo^. Working your way round to the front of the palace 
at Versailles, the second turning on the right down the 
broad central avenue is the Rue des Chantiers, paved. But 
on getting clear of the j^avS there is nothing to dismount 
for, except perhaps a level railway crossing near Sceaux, 
until you reach pa'o4 again at Choisy-le-Roi. It is 8 miles 
from St. Germain to Versailles, 13 from this place to 
Choisy, and another mile to the junction of the road from 
Paris. 

(7b he corUintied). 



ALL ROUND THE WHEEL. 

ExTKAOBDiKARY BicxcLiNa Pbrfoemancb.— Mr. W. F. 
Sutton, of the London Scottish Bicycle Club, left Wood 
Green, London, at 12.2 a.m. on Saturday, and rode to 
Worksop, in Nottinghamshire (139:1 miles), returning to 
Kneesal (151|) and then going by Tuxford (157|) to Ret- 
ford (165), where he turned and rode througli Newark, 
Grantham, and Stamford to Tempsford, in Bedfordshire, 
completing at 11.58 p.m. a distance of 258f miles. The 
ride has been properly vouched for and the mileage checked 
by two diflferent road books. The roads were heavy, a con- 
siderable fog prevailed during the night, and the wind was 
unfavourable on the return journey from Retford. Mr. 
Sutton held the record last year for the 24 hours (222 
miles), but this distance having been since beaten by three 
gentlemen he determined to try again, and after two failures, 
owing to the state of the weather, he has again succeeded 
in beating all previous records. The double tricycle record 
(180 miles) is held by Mr. Sutton in conjunction with Mr. 
James Lennox, of Dumfries. 

«««««• 

One of the most important features of the recent Council 
Meeting of the Union, was the adoption by the Council of 



Google 



Digitized by 



240 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



the scheme for regulating professional racing. The present 

chaotic state of professionalism was proved by the fact that, 

while a professional body, claiming to be representative, had 

approved the scheme previous to its submission to the 

Council, immediately on its passing, a meeting at Leicester, 

including many of the leading professionals, strongly 

condemned it. This latter meeting was, however, worked by 

the ground proprietors, who seem to think the large sums 

they derive from professional racing are threatened. 
• ••••• 

The most important business of the evening, however, 

was the unanimous adoption of a rule authorising the 

executive to suspend any rider competing at sports not 

held under the rules of the N.C.U., or under rules sanctioned 

by them. This assimilates cycling law to that of the A. A. A. , 

and will prove a very valuable aid to suppressing the evil 

connected with gate money and hole-and-corner meetings. 

Our Committee must either hold our Race Meeting under 

N.C.U. rules, or get our present rules, which are almost 

identical with the N.C.U. code, sanctioned by the Union. 

For the sake of example the former course seems preferable ; 

and our racing men must be careful what race meetings 

they ride at, especially in the country. 

The Front Wheel Hub.

Should any of our members be desirous of playing foot-

play two teams every Saturday, and I need scarcely add 
that the club is well managed when I state that Oliver 
Thorn is Secretary. The ground, a first rate one, is at 
Putney.

vast capabilities under its able exponents. Barker and 

J. C. Oswald.

It is said that the racing talent of the S.E. cannot hold 
a candle to it on the level, that up-hill it leaves the stur- 
diest hill climber labouring hopelessly in the rear ; while 

the descent of hills like Westerham are mere child's play. 
««•*•• 

One hears, occasionally, quaint receipts for training diet. 
A high dignitarr in the Club, I won't mention names, in- 
formed me that he never knew what real moving was like 
until the other day after a supper of pork sausages and rum 
shrub. 

• ••••• 

There is supposed to be a vast deal of energy still en 

reserve in the S.E. District, and as this is to be thrown into 

the South-east Ball, another record is likely to be disturbed. 
« • • • • • 

The poor Captain complains that he is spending most of 

his time in convincing members that they will have to pay 

for the two Smoking Concert tickets sent out with this 

number. The origin of their mistake is not very evident ; 

but the misguided ones entrench themselves behind the 

wording of the last circular from the Sub-Committee, from 

which they may draw any inference they please, but they 

will have to pay — ^and that's all about it ! 

• • ♦ • • ♦ 

Now is the time for the member s brother to disport him- 
self, and at the Boxing day Paperchase he may safely turn 
up in force, since "Wyvern," his implacable foe, is wintering 
in the south of France, beyond reach of Gazette. Those 
who know our Cluh Junius tell me he has aged considerably, 
has grown a long white beard, and is trying to restore his 
shattered nervous system, by avoidance of all sources of 

irritation and excitement. 

«••*«•

WINTER RIDING. 
From the luxuriant crop of festivities which spring up, 
mushroom like, at this season of the year, it might not un- 
reasonably be imagined that such a thing as bicycle riding 
was quite out of the question. The six months of plenty 
in the way of Club runs have given way to six months of 
dearth, when wheel ways are almost entirely deserted. All 
but supposed enthusiasts are content to retire with their 
machines, so to speak, into winter quarters. The machines 
themselves are allowed to rust in the peaceful seclusion of 
some outhouse, or are wrapped up in cotton wool and oil 
until the next spring, according to the disposition of their 
owners. The racing man goes out of training and lives on 
the memories of past victories, or betakes him to his home- 
training in the hope of future successes looming in the dim 
distance. Some of our men take their exercise in violent 
doses of football, or else more prudently (? Ed.) seek initia- 
tion into the mysteries of Lacrosse. Pew there are who, in 
the face of less congenial conditions continue the sport which 
has occupied their shrine of devotion during the summer. 
There are, it must be owned, some whose energy is of so 
spasmodic a character, that to them a month or two of rest 
is almost an absolute necessity. Apollo's bow was not always 
strung, and next year they will come out again as giants 
refreshed ; but to the majority bicycling is something more 



than a spasm, and for them winter riding ought not to 
be an impossibility, though their scope for action will be 
necessarily limited. 

Let it not be imagined that we would advocate the con- 
tinuance of Saturday runs throughout the year. It is not 
the frugal fare of the Club tea, followed by the glorious un- 
certainty of a ride home in the dark through mud, stones, 
and ruts that will tempt men out in the winter mouths. It 
is rather in the day-lit certainty of the early afternoon or 
of an occasional holiday that the opportunity for riding 
may be sought and often not sought unprofitably. 

It is quite true that there is hardly any pastime on which 
the weather has a more direct influence than that of 
bicycling. None can welcome the change from dry and 
even surface, where the wheels seemed to slip along by 
themselves, to the era of sloughs and stones where at times 
it requires the greatest exertion to move at all ; still, these 
are obstacles which may be often overcome or circumvented. 
Even in the worst specimen of an English winter, occasions 
will arise when the roads are something better than passable. 
It is then that the venerable " Stassen," or Irish roadster 
so long deserted, may be again called into requisition ; and 
we think the game will be found to be worth the candle. 

We are hearing plenty just now about Balls, Concerts, 
et hoc genus omne, and we hope some of our readers may 
be induced to follow our advice during this dead season 
and not give up riding altogether. We need hardly say 
that we shall be glad to receive the result of their 
experiences for the Gazbtte.
