THE ANNUAL DINNER. 

This, the chief festivity of the winter season, was held at 
St. James's Hall, on Wednesday, the 21st of November, 
when nearly eighty members and visitors seated themselves 
(as per diagram below) at the festive board. The proceedings 
were remarkable for great enthusiasm and a fine display 
of Club "solidarity.*' The music was also of a high 
character, Naylor's flute solo, and Dutton's and Schmettau's 
songs being much appreciated After dinner the President 
gave the toast of " The Queen," which called forth a very 
loyal and noisy rendering of the National Anthem. 

The Rev. W. d'A. Crofton said : — Mr. Chairman and 
^ Gentlemen. In rising to propose the next toast upon the 
Ibt, that of " Success to the London Bicycle Club," I know 
that I am bringing forward a subject which will be most 
heartily received, not only by yourselves, but also — and 
more especially— by my fellow guests. When I think of all 
that I ought to say on this subject, I feel like a man 
suddenly called upon to make the vain attempt to ladle out 
Niagara with a sieve. (A laugh). The L.B.C. is the mighty 
Niagara, and I the unworthy wielder of the sieve. No 
sooner do I dip my mind — so to speak— into the greatness 
of the L.B.C., and rise to bring before you some few speci- 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



243 



mens of the same, than I find I offer jon only a few drops 
from a perfect river of merit — a result caused, not by lack 
of greatness on the part of the L.B.C., but by the inability 
of myself to do full justice to the subject that I have in 
hand. If, therefore, in speaking of the various poiiy» of 
excellence of the L.B.C., I omit the names of many 
who contribute to its greatness, pray blame the sieve- 
like nature of my memory which has allowed their 
names to fall through and be lost. In the L.B.C. 
there are men who at the council table of the National 
Cyclists' Union — ^men such as Scrutton, Stephen Smith, 
Reynolds, and the younger Whattou (cheers) — carry others 
irresistibly along with them by the powerful and straight- 
forward nature of their course. There areothers who, arrayed 
in the familiar black war paint of their tribe upon the 
war path — men such as Wadham Wyndham, Oliver 
Thorn, Beck, Butler, Reynolds — whether at Stamford 
Bridge or at the Crystal Palace (laughter), out distance 
others in the race, (Cheers). There are others who, 
turning aside from the rushing excitement of the racing 
path, prefer the quiet eddies of the road — men such 
as Alison, Charles Newman, and Weeks. (Cheers). And 
besides all these, there is the general body of the members — 
such men as Francis, Brown, Price, Hough, and Lloyd — who 
form that continuous stream of merit of which there seems 
to be an inexhaustible supply. And finally. Gentlemen, 
there is your Captain, Mr. Ashley Barrett, whom, in con- 
tinuance of my metaphor, I can best compare to that 
lighthouse upon Goat Island in the centre of the Horse- 
shoe Fall — beaming down with approval upon those who, 
by the continuous work of many years, have made so proud 
a position for the Captain of their Club to occupy (cheers), 
a position however, which, as he no doubt could tell you, 
though seemingly so firm and peaceful, is surrounded and 
undermined by a continuous flow of cares. I have much 
pleasure, gentlemen, in proposing — " Success to the London 
Bicycle Club," coupled with the name of your Captain, 
Mr. Ashley Barrett. (Prolonged cheers). 

AsHLET W. Barbett was received with prolonged 
cheering as he rose to respond. Briefly apologiriug 
for a cold, which he said had not yielded to the com- 
bined forces of tallow candles, spirits of camphor, and 
mustard ball, he continued as follows : — I confess I have 
watched with a good deal of anxiety the way in which you 
would receive the toast which Mr. Crofton has so pleasantly 
proposed, and your kind reception of it takes a considerable 
weight off my mind, for it convinces me that you have 
forgotten and forgiven a certain little misadventure in con- 
nection with the luggage of the unfortunate Bath candidates 
on Whit-Monday last. (Laughter). I thank you then, 
Gentlemen, very heartily for your kindness this evening, and 
still more do I thank you for that loyal support you have 
rendered to your Captain during his year of office. His work^ 
though very honourable, is also one of some anxiety, and 



though outwardly he may smile and do his best to smooth 
his troubled brow, his position is, if I may be allowed the 
simile, not unlike that of the man who pitches his tent on 
the slopes of Mount Vesuvius, and knows not at what hour 
of the night the flood of lava may roll down and overwhelm 
him. However, the lava has not come ; and that the Club 
thrives as it does I attribute very much to the support of 
those whom I am proud to term my friends — to Godlee, 
Scrutton, Stephen Smith, and Fred Williams — and whom 
I am now glad to thank for help rendered at all times and 
on all subjects, in a manner as unostentatious as it was 
pleasant. (Cheers). Though last, not least, I must also 
thank my brother officer, B. S. Price for that cheery, good 
natured smile, and the business aptitude he has brought to 
bear upon the work. (Cheers). I fear at times it has been 
pretty heavy, and that I have shifted it, perhaps all too often, 
from my own shoulders on to his. Gentlemen, the time- 
honoured custom for your Captain to give at the Club 
Dinner a sketch of the events of the past year must be 
followed, and I confess I undertake it with misgivings. 
Where so mucji has to be said one cannot but feel that 
there is a chance of leaving out what is important and 
dwelling too much upon comparatively insignificant details ; 
therefore I must beg your indulgence towards what I may 
call my unofficial report. Well, I am pleased to say it is 
a good one (cheers), and I don't think even the veteri^n 
croakers (a laugh) who periodically spring up and tell us the 
Club is going to the dogs, will dispute that when they have 
heard what I have to say. I say it is a good one, for though 
we have not this year beaten many records, still records 
are not everything, and though we can enjoy life a good 
deal in a quiet way without them, I am pleased to say 
that I can, and I shortly will, quote two instances in which 
we have got to the top of the tree. Firstly, you will 
be glad to hear that the additions to our Club out- 
number our resignations by seven, 25 men have joined 
this year and 18 have resigned ; and here I must refer to 
the loss we have sustained in losing two of our oldest 
members — Charles Taylor and Ernest Carr. This is the 
first time a Captain in replying for his Club at our 
Annual Dinner has had to refer to such a painful 
topic, and I am very sorry it has come to me this evening. 
Charles Taylor I knew not. Those of his friends in 
the S.W., and he had many who did know him, speak 
of him as a genial companion and a warm-hearted man. 
Poor Ernest Carr I did know, and in losing him I feel that 
I have myself lost a friend. To refer to what is more 
pleasing, let me congratulate Arthur Herbert upon his 
return from Mentone improved in health, and able, as in 
May last, to take up his old stand upon the top of 
Marlboro' Hill, and there look after the well-being of his 
fellow clubmen in their long Whit-Monday ride (cheers) ; 
also let me say how much pleasure it gives me, and all who 
know him, to see once more among us one of our oldest 



Digitized by 



244 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



members, who has been at all times among the first to 
support every L.B.O. movement, and whose bad accident 
when riding down from the triple Meet at Dunstable in May 
last we all regretted deeply — I need hardly say that I refer 
to Walter Meyer. In the matter of road riding, and this 
after all is our raism cF&tre and the backbone of our 
existence, we have done our duty ; and our Meets have 
been fairly well attended, although our District Captains 
have not baited their traps this year with such charming 
attractions as romantic picnics and sentimental water 
parties. (Laughter.) The S.E. as usual, the brightest 
jewel in the Captain's crown (hear, hear, and laughter), again 
takes first place, and here is my first record ! Its average 
this year, gentlemen, amounts to 15.7 per run — the best 
average by a good deal that any district has ever hitherto 
reached. This splendid result comes mainly from the energy 
and popularity of its District Captain, R. 6. Francis 
(hear, hear) — a man who never spares himself when he 
has work to do ; also let me remind you, it is in part a 
natural sequence to the good work done in his District 
by his predecessors, Fred. Williams, Barker, and Brown. 
In the West our new and popular member, Harold Pigott, 
has fought a good fight with such heavy odds as leagues 
of macadam and preponderance of racing talent against him. 
Those who remember how hard some of us fought to estab- 
lish the W. District — the latest oflfspringof L.B.C. genius — 
will mark its progress with much interest under the manage- 
ment of our able new broom. I cannot but tell him that 
I think the present is a somewhat critical time in the history 
of his division ; however, if any man can make a success of 
the West H. C. Pigott is the man to do it (hear, hear), and 
none will be more pleased than shall I when that is obtained. 
So much for District Meets. Of Oeneral Meets we have 
had three : one at Halliford, when 54 men sat down to tea, 
as against 11 who attended the meet held there in the pre- 
vious year, though I am bound to confess that on the last 
occasion wind and weather were enough to keep anybody 
but a Spartan in bed. Our second General Meet was with 
Oxford and Cambridge at Dunstable, when 67 L.B.C. men 
attended, as against 63 members of our Club when the triple 
meet was held in that town in 1881. 35 Oxford and Cam- 
bridge men rode down to meet us in May last and, consider- 
ing that this town of straw is about as three-cornered a 
place to get at as Timbuctoo, these large numbers testify to 
the friendly feeling that is growing each year between 
the London, the Oxford, and the Cambridge men. In 
the N.E. we find our old D.C., Theo. Qodlee, has done 
good work, and with what — ^for the " little District " — was 
last year thought to be a hot average of 8.3 to beat, he has 
this year scored 8.8, and his July Paperchase will be long 
remembered in North-Eastern annals for the capital after- 
noon's sport that it afforded. In the S.W., R. M. Qruggen — 
the best of good fellows, and most genial of District Cap- 
tains (hear, hear) — has worked hard to increase the 



popularity of his runs ; but he has, I fear, suffered from a 
lack of material on which to work, and I know you will all 
congratulate him upon the recovery from his recent severe 
illness. In the N.W. we have rather suffered this year, and 
that from causes which, I hope, with the coming of the 
next riding season will have passed away. First, three or 
four of our steady-going riders over there have migrated to 
other districts, then one N.W. D.C. (Palmer Dalton) had 
to resign, half-way through the season, from ill health; 
and A. H. Lown, whom the District elected to fill his place, 
had, I fear, a hard fight to keep his men together. Let 
him, however, remember that the Club knows how hard it 
is for a D.C. to collect, half-way through the season, a District 
once scattered, and knows, too, that he worked his best. 
Let him remember that his efforts were not wasted, that he 
has in the N.W., ready to his hand, a nucleus for a 
large and thriving District, and that it is no new thing 
for other Districts to be visited by just such a passing 
cloud as is now resting upon the Northern Heights. 
Our third General Meet was at Harrow, and the 29 men 
who were there, will not soon forget the golden sunset that 
lightened up the valley, and the pleasant half-hour's chat in 
the prettiest of gardens on that warm August evening. 
Our time-honoured Easter Run from Bath came off as usual, 
and I had the pleasure of meeting eight L.B.C. men down 
there at our old headquarters ; also I started with them, 
but what befel them after Devizes, I know not. Truly when 
a Captain is expected to scorch up from Bath with the 
Whit-Monday flyers, his lot is not a happy one. This 
brings me to the Road Trial, and here I am pleased to say, 
though the Treasurer may frown, that ten medals were 
claimed— the Enamelled for a second time falling to the 
redoubtable H. R. Reynolds with the fast time of 7 hrs. 
28 min. for the 100 miles. (Cheers.) The great interest 
still felt throughout the Club in this event is shown by 
there having been 23 starters from Bath, of whom 16 rode 
through under ten hours. Well, gentlemen, in reference to 
this event, we hear of rumours of war, but threatened folks 
live long, and we may be sure that so long as we continue to 
carry it through in the future, as we always have done in the 
past, without in any way endangering tha safety of the 
public, we shall run no risk of interference at the hands of 
the authorities. (Cheers.) Concerning the adventures of 
our members, at home and abroad--of Dalton's romantic ride 
on the Goodwin Sands (a laugh), or of Barker's sentimental 
journey in the North of France, I have no time to speak ; but 
hasten on to racing matters. Here comes in my second record ! 
This year the tide has turned — ^fortune has favoured us, and 
I hope her smiles are but an earnest of future glory. 
Thanks to the grand riding of Reynolds, Wyndham, 
Jephson, F. L. Adam, Beck, and Oliver Thorn (cheers), we 
have this year beaten both Oxford and Cambridge in both 
matches in all three events. I am bound, however, to say 
our work was rendered much less difficult by events that 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



245 



we all alike regret — the illness of the two 'Varsity Captains, 
J. S. Whatton and W. F. M. Buckley. Here let me acknow- 
ledge, on behalf of my Club, the genial hospitality of the 
Cambridge men on the evening of the race ; and to G. N. 
Latham of Ours, and of the C.U.Bi.C, let me express my 
own thanks for that kindness which he showed to me, and 
in doing which I am sure he designed to pay a compliment 
on behalf of his men — to the London Bicycle Club. 
Well, Gentlemen, we have no champions this year in 
our ranks, but we have lots of rising talent ; and when 
we see the Club Miles at our summer and autumn 
meetings won by the young brothers of such old L.B.C. 
men as Oswald and Herbert, we find good reason for 
hoping and believing that the racing prestige of our 
Club will not fail in the future. (Hear, hear.) Now, 
Gentlemen, I hope I have said almost enough to con- 
vince you, if that were needed, that we have not wasted 
our time this year. I should like to tell you of our 
Dramatic Performance last winter, when what is called ** a 
large and fashionable audience " at 8t. George's Hall were 
delighted by the histrionic talent of our members, and the 
clever acting of charming Miss Ivan Bristowe (hear, hear) 
— but time flies. Then, again, there are two Smoking 
Concerts to do justice to. But the music of the last, with 
our friend Mr. Little as Corney Grain in " He did, and he 
didn't know why " is still present with you. (Laughter). 
Then there is the great question of encouraging the Tricy- 
clists, to which, thank goodness, you gave an effectual 
quietus last summer (cheers), showing plainly (as I think 
very rightly) that you intend to remain a Club of bicyclists 
— neither more nor less. Then there is the Member's 
Brother and his friend " Wyvern" (now in France) — there are 
Gruggen's remains at Oatlands Park — ^there is Brown's 
farewell to Francis and his reply, like that of the dying 
swan, sweetest before death. (A laugh). These and others 
crowd before me, but I leave them, and if I have wearied 
you, as I fear I must have done (No, no), please don't blame 
me, but search out " Porcupine," and^ visit your rage upon 
him for saying the Captain is bound to make a long speech, 
for he has the toast of the evening, and all the glories of 
the L.B.C. to recount." (Loud cheers.) 

Thbo. Godlbb was well received on his proposing the 
toast, " Other Clubs." His having the same toast entrusted 
to him as he had two years ago reminded him, he said, of 
the gentleman who, hearing a mendicant's dolorous story for 
the second time, remarked that he had heard it before and he 
did not like it then. (A laugh). He feared that a like remark 
might now be made to him. But when he remembered that 
the name of Mr. Hampton Roberts. was joined with the 
toast, he was sure it would meet with a hearty reception. 
(Cheers). While the toast-list was yearly decreasing in 
length, this particular toafit was increasing in importance ; 
for it was ever assimilating the toasts which succeeding 
captains thought it their duty to suppress. The Bicycle 



Union, the Visitors, and others had successively been 
omitted from the list ; and he apprehended that these were 
now to be included in the toast of *' Other Clubs." 
Though he would be perfectly justified in devoting half an 
hour to the recital of the excellencies of " Other Clubs " 
(Oh ! and laughter), he should yet confine himself to the 
Clubs that were represented. First, of course, came the 
Oxford and Cambridge Clubs (cheers), between whom and 
themselves the tie was so strong that "our University 
Division" might not improperly express their relation. 
He hoped this tie would long subsist, and even grow in 
strength. Among other Clubs who had representatives 
present were the Belsize, the West Kent, the Wanderers, 
the Pickwick, and the Civil Service ; and then there was 
a gentleman present whose Club it would be hard to 
specify — namely, Mr. Lacy Hillier. (Cheers). It used to 
be said that Mr. Hillier belonged to all the metropolitan 
Clubs and most of the country ones. (Laughter.) In con- 
clusion, he asked to include in the toast such visitors as 
were not strictly Club-visitors, and who might otherwise be 
left out of the toast-drinking. 

R. P. Hampton Roberts responded for " Other Clubs." 
As a member of his own club he was not, he said, supposed 
to know anything of other clubs ; but in his temporary 
position he was supposed to know a good deal about them. 
Each club, it seemed to him, had its speciality ; thus the 
charm of river picnics was associated with the Wanderers, 
the flavour of learning with the University clubs. And so 
with all. For him such advantages were supposed to have 
no charm — but they had. He admired whatever was good 
in his neighbours. At the same time he felt that there was 
no representative of another club present but had a secret 
< thought, while admitting the many excellencies of the 
L.B.C., that it would be better if modelled more on the 
basis of his own particular club. Speaking in his temporary 
capacity as the concentrated essence of all whom he repre- 
sented, and who for the moment must be supposed to be non- 
existent and to have sunk their souls in his (a laugh), he 
' thanked them. They of other clubs had listened with in- 
terest to the doughty deeds of the L.B.C. ; and with one 
voice they exclaimed, " long may it flourish ! " (Cheers). 

C. H. F. Christie said the toast he had now to give was 
one which makes every good man feel what was described 
in extreme youth and infancy as the still small voice of 
conscience." The officers and committee of the L.B.C. were 
a body of gentlemen who, for no possible good to themselves, 
undertook an amount of work and bother, and gave up an ex- 
tremely large part, and very often the whole of their spare 
time for the good of the Club, and often for the benefit 
of individual members. As he had said, they derived no 
advantage for it ; but do they, he asked, get what they 
have a right to expect — gratitude ? (A voice — " Yes.") 
As they all knew, they had rattling good officers ; but as 
it was natural to bully somebody or other, they were often 



Digitized by 



246 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



very much aggravated at general meetings. There was 
one officer who had a most unpleasant post — that, namely, 
of Treasurer. (Oh, yes ! and laughter.) The Treasurer 
was the one man who did not want to be let alone, and he 
was the one man who was persistently let alone (laughter) ; 
and how small the trouble of acknowledging those little 
notes with their inclosures ! But in many cases he did not 
receive such inclosures ; and hence the despairing appeals 
in the Gazette — '* Will members be good enough to pay 
their subscriptions?" The speaker could feel for that 
Treasurer. It was very rough on him. (A laugh.) They 
were an ungrateful lot ; and it was when this toast was 
proposed that they recognised the extent of their ingratitude. 
He urged each member to drink the toast in a proper 
manner, and show that he was not simply a discontented 
grumbler. Let them show that they were Englishmen 
after all, and drink this toast heartily. (Loud cheers.) 

R B. Hough responded. Thanking the company for 
the cordial response with which Mr. Christie's appeal had 
been met, he said he regarded the Annual Dinner as one of 
the brightest means of keeping up the happy understanding 
of the Club, and he took the present opportunity of thanking 
members generally for their energetic co-operation with 
those who take the official yoke upon them, which by this 
heartiness was made so light. Personally the work had been 
a great pleasure to him during the past two years ; which 
was a period that would ever in his mind be associated with 
pleasant memories. (Cheers.) But to-night he had to sound 
a pathetic note. He referred to the announcement of the 
retirement from the official roll, of the familiar names of 
Scrutton and Price. (Loud cries of " no ! ") It was 
unhappily a fact. During the past three years they had 
done excellent service, which the speaker was sure was 
appreciated by all. (Cheers.) In conclusion, he asked the 
members to accord in the future that hearty support which 
they had bestowed on the Officers and Committee in the 
past, and more especially to stand by them next Monday, 
when a resolution was to be proposed, which, if carried, 
would be equivalent to a vote of want of confidence in the 
Executive. (" No ! no ! " The S.E. corner of the table 
being observed to be much excited.) 

3. P. Smith proposed the health of *' The Racing Mem- 
bers." He observed that the next best thing to being a 
racing member one's self was to propose the health of the 
racing members. Although the season had not been so 
good as last year (Oh !), yet several new men had given 
promise of great things to come, so that the future of the 
Club in this respect would doubtless be worthy of the past. 
He mentioned one name — Beck. (Loud cheers). It was 
true they had no records, but he believed that had it not 
been for the unfortunate accident to Buckley, they would 
have had records. Next year he hoped would see Buckley 
on the path again. (Cheers). They had also lost the ser- 
vices of W. K. Adam, who he hoped would come back the 



the stronger for his voyage to New Zealand. (Cheers). 
Among others who were coming to the front in a remarkable 
way were Butler, the younger Whatton, and Oswald. 
(Cheers). He ought also to mention Mr. Oliver Thorn. 
(Loud and prolonged cheers). He would at all times have 
been welcomed as the brother of his brother, and he had 
made a position which next year it will be be hard to equal. 
(Cheers). 

0. Thoun returned thanks for his racing brethren and 
himself. He remarked that they had a good many promising 
men who would worthily take the place of those who had 
long been on the path, and thus the prestige of the L.B.C. 
would be sustained. (Cheers.) 

M. D. RucKER, at the request of the President, proposed 
the toast of "The Visitors," coupling with it the name 
of Mr. Edward Turner. Mr. Turner belonged to the 
Cambridge University Club, which with them was a very 
strong recommendation. 

Mr. Turner ably responded. He paid a graceful compli- 
ment to the President, whom he referred to as a fine 
example of " muscular Christianity." His personal expe- 
rience of bicycling was confined to a single attempt, when 
the machine ran with him into a window, the result being 
such severe injuries that he never attempted riding again. 
(Laughter.) He concluded by recalling the names of a 
few bicyclists who have distinguished themselves in the 
world of intellect — Keith-Falconer, Scrutton, C. A. Pollock, 
and others, and reasoning therefrom that mental vigour 
and bicycling were in some way connected ; and as rowing 
and cricketing seemed to lack any such connection, the 
case in favour of cycling was made all the stronger. 

H. 6. A. Brown proposed the toast of "The L.B.C. 
Gazette," joined with the name of the Editor, T. E. 
Scrutton. Asking himself what was the distinctive feature 
of the L.B.C., he said he felt inclined to say after the 
manner of Dr. Johnson " we abound in features." In the 
first place they, had a President who could ride ; second, a 
committee which was able to work and accomplish a great 
deal ; and third, racing men who would certainly compare 
with the members of any other club. Again, there was the 
Annual Bath Trial. But of all the features of the Club, 
there was none more important or admirable than the 
Gazette. It had become respectable in point of age, and 
the excellence of its matter and the able way in which it 
has been edited by a succession of clever men had, he 
believed, mainly contributed to its success. Mr. Scrutton 
and his assistant, Mr. Lloyd, had fulfilled their duties in a 
way that commanded the admiration and gratitude of the 
Club. (Cheers). He begged to join their names with the 
toast. (Loud cheers). 

T. E. Scrutton in replying, expressed his pleasure at 
hearing from Mr. Brown, what doubtless caused previous 
editors to writhe, that the Gazette this year had become 
respectable. He had, however, to lay the praise on those

to whom it was due, on his friend the Suh-Editor (cheers), 
on whom had fallen the work of the dull season, while he 
was posing as the ornamental figurehead. Then there were 
the band of contributors on whom each Editor placed his 
reliance for the work of the Gazette — Godlee, Barrett, 
Francis, Williams, Brown, and others who preferred to blush 
unknown. Especially he referred to his two friends, N. H. 
and N. H., Jun., whose acquisition he regarded as the fea- 
ture of his year of office. He might use them as illustra- 
tions of the wisdom of sending in contributions to the 
Editor without waiting to be asked. He was ashamed to 
say he never suspected either of these gentlemen of any lite- 
rary genius until he saw their first MSS, and now he ranked 
them among his most valued contributors. (Cheers). It 
was hardly necessary to justify the existence of the Gazette, 



but he regarded it as the chief cause of the high tone of 
Club feeling and sympathy which, he believed, made them 
pre-eminent among other large clubs. (Cheers). 

H. C. Pioott proposed the toast of " The Ladies," and 

R. G. Fbancis responded. 

P. Dalton (in the absence of G. F. Beck) gave the 
health of " The Chairman," which was drunk with musical 
honours and ** three times three." 

The President responded in his usual sparkling style. 
He observed that he had had thoughts of resigning the 
presidency, which was loudly opposed ; but on his adding 
that he was about to give up the bicycle for the double 
tricycle, the cheers were renewed. 

The gathering closed, as usual, with " Auld lang syne " 
sung with joined hands, and foot on table.

LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



THE TANDEM BICYCLE. 

The Kucker " Tandem," of which the ilhistration below is 
a sketch, is clearly the Bicycle tocommend itself to the riding 
world, as the risk of falling which so often deters many from 
bicycling is entirely done away with, and it thus vies with the 
popularity of the tricycle, which is ridden by many who 
consider comparative immunity from danger not dearly pur- 
chased at the expense of a couple of miles an hour in speed, 
and prefer pottering to being tlirown headlong from the 
saddle at any odd moment. But it must not be compared 
to the various other Safety Bicycles, for in most of them 
either the advantage of height, or the position for driving, 
is given up for that idea of safety. With the " Tandem " 
the rider is exactly over his work, and without risk of going 
over the handles. 

The new invention of course is the straight backbone, 
weighing 16 lbs., doing away with the small wheels entirely, 
thus reducing vibration and friction to a minimum, whilst 
the pace up hill is much increased, owing to the rake being 
foi'wards instead of backwards as in the ordinary Bicycle. 
It can be fitted to any two " Rucker's," so that should one 
rider not be able to be present, the other can use his own 
wheel with a hind wheel as a single machine, rather than 
stay at home. 



Hills, however steep, may, on the " Tandem," be ridden 
down with impunity — Westerham, hitherto unrideable, 
having succumbed ; whilst large bricks, carts, or dogs, only 
afford enjoyment and recreation. 

The two South-Easters who have been riding it, 
swear by it, and any loss of dignity at first sustained 
by them, such as clinging to walls to mount, or riding 
at night to avoid observation, is easily forgotten in the 
proud moments of drawing Latin from the Club Scholar, 
or reducing demons of the McBae " tjrpe to beads of 
perspiration. 

Certain it is that great pace can be obtained on it, and 
should it become generally used, we may confidently expect 
the Bath record to be beaten. 

It requires little or no practice from the ordinary bicycle 
rider, and the advantage of two friends being together 
whilst touring, etc., is evident to all. 

That many more will be on the road next season 
we hope, and that also the present expressions of wonder 
and excitement exhibited by the rustic and other intel- 
ligent males when passed by the " Tandem " will assume 
that stolid indifference which is now caused by the ordinary 
bicycle. 

Zulu. 




SMOKING CONCERT. 
Our winter campaign was successfully opened on Wed- 
nesday, November 7th, in the Queen's Room of the " London 
Tavern," Fenchurch Street. Through the thick smoke, not 
of cannon, but of "the calumet — the Peace-pipe," we were 
enabled after a short space of time, during which we ex- 
perienced a severe tingling about the eyes, to discover an 
unusually large muster of members, who,'from the enthusiasm 



they displayed at the conclusion of the various items, were 
unanimous in their appreciation of the proceedings. 

Oliver Thorn opened the programme with a song, " Out 
on the deep," and F. N. Large, C. Y. Lyne, and Gr, E. 
Schmettau then rendered the terzetto " Vanne a colei che 
adoro " with very good taste ; aft^r which W. A. Smith 
gave " The brave old Temeraire " in a very spirited manner, 
and Mr. 6. L. Hillier, in " Sharps and Flats," quite upset 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE 



CLUB GAZETTE. 



249 



the audience as lie retired amidst loud applause to " the 
bo-de-ga." After R. Newman had sung "Sailing," C. E. 
Sbannan displayed his abundant fund of humour in the 
scena, ** The Jackdaw of Rheims/' and that most pathetic 
song, " My Mary Jane." C. Y. Lyne and G. E. Schmettau 
both scored successes, the former in " The Vagabond " and 
the latter in "Good night, fair maid," to which, as an 
encore, he added " Til sing thee songs of Arabi," which 
completed the first part of the programme. 

At this point £. T. Sachs treated the company to a most 
entertaining conjuring performance, which quite justified 
the Captain's epithet of *' world-renowned," and it would 
be difficult to say which of the tricks was the most 
mysterious, as all attempts to solve them on our part 
have resulted in utter failure. 

The programme was continued with the trio, " When the 
wind blows," which was very much appreciated. H. W. 
Bridges provoked much laughter in " The Chinaman with 
the Roman Nose," which was given as an encore to " The 
Little Powder Monkey." Mr. Campbell Rae Brown, who is 
evidently an efficient in the art, recited " Kissing Cup's 
Race," his own composition, and also the " Progress of 
Madness," with great effect. The next item on the pro- 
gramme, " He did, but he didn't know why," was given with 
great humour by Mr. C. P. Little, who put such an amount 
of expression into it, that we laughed to the extent of 
being obliged to hold on to a neighbouring chair. After 
great applause, the topical song by Oswald and Rucker, 
which contained some capital points, followed ; and then 
Mr. Little, in response to loud calls, sang " Eighteen and 
three " in very comical style. 

W. A. Smith brought a most successful evening to a close 
with singing " Tell her I love her so," in which he appeared 
to great advantage. 

The thanks of the Club are due to all those who helped 
make the evening a success, and more especially to Mr. 
C. P. Little, who accompanied throughout most willingly 
and ably. 

To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 
Sib, — I think with " Imprimatur " tlmt a policy of 
isolation does not tend to our welfare, and for some time I 
have thought that our Club has sought too much a monkish 
seclusion from the world, while it has neglected those means 
that most tend to increase its ranks. Barnum, the worldly 
wise, says that, " having £lOO in his pocket wherewith to 
start business I would spend £99 thereof in advertisement." 
Doubtless he is right, for the day has passed when merit 
unaided can triumph. Competition, so keen that it hardly 
leaves room to live, has destroyed its chance, and merit 
must now climb on to the housetops and shout and 
advertise in the Daily Telegraph, Without advocating a 
return to the old days, when every detail of our Club life 



was reported in the weekly public press and commented 
upon too frequently in no friendly or impartial spirit, yet 
I think we have, to our own hurt, for the last few years 
neglected to cultivate such relationships with the papers as 
might have secured for us friendly reports of our Annual 
Dinners, Smoking Concerts, and of other events we have 
undertaken. By all means let our Gazette be complete in 
itself, as it certainly has been ; let it give the L. B. C. man 
all necessary news about what goes on within and without 
his Club; but let us remember, as " Imprimatur " points 
out, that the public press is a most important agent in 
informing the outside world and the rising generation of 
wheelmen of the existence of an L. B. C. 

Our old members disappear and as fast or even faster 
than they go, new blood should come into our system. 
Growth should more than keep pace with decay, and this 
will, I think, be brought about if our committee do but 
avail itself discreetly of the great power of the Fourth 
Estate. It must be noted that now-a-days the only 
information an unattached rider gets of our Club is the 
brief report of our Yearly Race Meeting, or perhaps he 
may see an occasional paragraph commenting upon the 
undesirability of Road Races in general, and of the L. B. C. 
Road Trial in particular. Of such an exclusive policy the 
natural outcome is that candidates for membership are 
proposed only by members, and that rarely, if ever, do we 
find outsiders, unacquainted with any of our men, eager to 
join our ranks. This last, which I take to be one of the 
best proofs of vitality is, I fear, wanting ; but it might, I 
think, be brought about if more pains were taken to inform 
outsiders in an unostentatious and legitimate way of the 
advantages they would derive from membership of our 
Club. I must not close without saying that anything 
approaching a touting advertisement would be as repugnant 
to my feeling as to that of any other L.B.C. man ; 
but I must quite dissent from those who say that our Club 
is now as large as it need be, and I strongly believe that 
good would come from frequent reference in the public 
papers to our Club work, and from a liberal encouragement 
of visitors at our Club Runs. Let me point out that our 
policy lately has tended in the direction of stifling these 
two sources of growth. Octopus. 



To the Editor of the London Bicycle Club Gazette. 

Sir, — I have read, with some astonishment, the letter of 
** Imprimatur " in your last issue, and have vainly tried to 
make its end fit into its beginning. Even if we assume that 
the facts which he mentions are related to one another by 
more than accident — if " false pride " or " self-imposed 
isolation " are " preying on our vitals," why are we not one 
penny the worse for the process ? but to quote once again, 
why are we " now as strong as " before we were afflicted ? 

If the result of our maintaining our Club at its very 
satisfactory strength, and filling up our ranks every year as 



Digitized by 



Google 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. 



the old members retire is " the reason why so many other 
clubs increase and multiply in the way they appear to do/' 
it occurs to me that we are benefiting cycling generally, at 
no cost whatever to ourselves. 

But joking apart, I think the real fallacy in your corres- 
pondent's letter is the idea that increasing is the one thing 
to be aimed at. I do not care to contest the theory that 
the Gazette has reduced our elections, though I very much 
doubt it ; but assuming this to be the fact, I do not think 
we are losers. The L.B.C. is, we are proud to believe, the 
most first-rate collection of athletes in the metropolis. 
It is, I venture to think, quite large enough for working 
purposes, and I think we may trust to our own members to 
introduce their friends, and to sing our praises to their 
friends, without seeking such a position as, say the L.A.C.» 
which, while placing us in an exalted athletic position 
would, I fear, seriously interfere with the social element 
which is necessarily so important a factor in a bicycle club, 
whether it be given to winter amusement or confine its 
attention strictly to bicycling proper. 

I think it only right to add that I have not the dimmest 
notion who " Imprimatur" is, and I hope he will not think 
I am abusing him, as I doubt not that he is a most valuable 
addition to the Club.— Yours, etc. W. X. W. 



QUILLS. 

" Aristotle and Pliny ddiver it that the porpentine casteth off his quills 
when he would attack, or defend himself, but Diosooridee affinneth it a 
point of foUy to believe it."— Sir T. Bbowni. 

The Smoking Concert was, all things considered, a great 
success. True, it was a bad room for singing, and true, 
there were draughts, but the men present (150), were not 
crowded and seemed to enjoy themselves ; and it is really 
very difficult to .get, for anything like a reasonable sum, a 
room in which •so many men can be put, which shall be 
well ventilated and singable. 

If I may find fault, the men wouldn't take up the 
choruses. It adds greatly to the ''go" of a concert of that 
sort if every other song at least has a good chorus in which 
everyone joins ; men take far more interest in the music, 
and the whole entertainment is put on a firee and easy 
footing, which helps its success greatly. 

The Dinner was as usual marked by one feature, in which 
as far as I know, it surpasses most other dinners — the 
amount of noise. It is very gratifying as an evidence of 
Club good feeling to have one prominent member after 
another received with " great cheering and musical honours/' 
and there was, if possible, more than the usual enthusiasm 
this year. 

In fact the toast of the L.B.C. bid fair, under the 
manipulation of Hampton Roberts last year, and Crofton 
this, to become fixed in one "common form." 

Mr. A. proposed the toast of the evening, " the health of 



the London Bicycle Club. " He said, " when he contemplated 
the greatness of the Club, he felt his own unworthiness. 
(No, no). When he mentioned that the L.B.C. had on 
the racing path, B. (loud cheers) ; C. (ditto) ; D. (ditto) ; 
and the rest of the alphabet, each letter received with 
great enthusiasm ; that it had on the road, E. (loud cheers, 
and repeat [same form) ; at the Union, F. (and as before) ; 
he need say no more, but ask them to drink, etc." Mr. A. 
resumed his seat amid great cheering, having been on his 
legs twelve minutes, of which two were speech and ten 
applause. 

• ••••• 

Speculation was rife a^ to the reason why the toast of 
the L.B.C. was so early in the list. It was at last discovered 
that the Captain, anxious to enjoy the pipe of peace, had 
arranged to get his speech and his misery over as soon as 
possible. 

Twenty-seven North-Easters were reported to be at the 
dinner ; this shows the self-sacrificing heroism of the "little 
district." Tliis is further established by the fact that the 
N.E. average this year, is, in comparison with the number 
of members in the district, better than the S.R 

The S.E. comer of the table was not remarkable for its 
quietness. This is said to have been caused by the Editor's 
having fallen as a North-East lamb among South-East 
wolves, and being usually in deadly combat with four of 
them at once as to Club politics ; the only diversion being 
furnished by a well-known ex-Editor, who impartially 
attacked everybody. [Note. — We were a noisy. Ed.] 

I am requested by the Editor to state that only a few of 
the dinner speeches are reported verbatim. Some others 
are curtailed at the request of the speakers; some on 

account of demands of space. 

• ••••• 

I regret to hear that some prominent members of the 
Committee contemplate resignation in consequence of the 
vote of Monday night. I earnestly hope they will 

reconsider their determination. 

• • • • • • 

0. P. Coleman has been elected on the executive of the 

National Cyclist's Union. 

• • • • • • 

There is to be a large contingent of the North-East at 
the S.E. Ball, and I hope the Western Districts will follow 
their example. Pobcupdse. 

SPECIAL GENERAL MEETING. 

The Special General Meeting, called at the requisition of 
H. 11. Heynolds and others, was held at the " City Arms " 
on the 26th November. 

The Captain took the chair, and 44 members were present. 

H. 6. A. Brown proposed, and R. G. Fkakcis seconded — 

That the Anniud Bsce Meeting for 18S4 be held at the 
CrjBtAl PalAoe." 

H. G. A. Bbown said, that the most prominent racing 



Digitized by 



LONDON BICYCLE CLUB GAZETTE. •» 251 



men were in favour of his proposal ; that the track was far 
better, and more 'convenient for people. He announced 
that the Directors had agreed to put up a grand stand, and 
alleged that, from a financial point of view, the merits of 
the two grounds were equal. 

A. W. Babrbtt explained that the Committee were not 
definitely pledged to Stamford Bridge, and then proceeded 
in a very amusing speech to dwell on the disadvantages of 
the Crystal Palace. Two female cousins and their brother 
could be taken to Stamford Bridge for three shillings, but 
it would take 12s 6d to treat them to the Crystal Palace. 
When there, they stood in a sloping loose box given over to 
the British rough, with no seats, shelter from rain, or re- 
freshments. In finance there was no room for argument ; 
the Crystal Palace Meeting was a rank failure ; we had lost 
£10 this year, and should lose £20 next year. Oliver 
Thorn, Wyndham, and Butler, as racing men, were in favour 
of the proposal. 

T. E. ScauTToN spoke against the motion on grounds of 
finance and general inconvenience. 

S. P. Smith, in supporting the motion alluded to the 
number of meetings competing with the Crjrstal Palace 
Meeting last year. 

F. M. Williams urged that the fact that, apart from the 
Committee, a quorum of members was not present showed 
that the Club had confidence in the Committee's decision. 

A. H. LowN, as a N.W. man, supported the motion, as 
did also H. K Rbtnolds. 

Olivee Thorn opposed the motion in the interests of 
racing men. He knew a good many ladies (loud cheers), 
and Stamford Bridge was the most convenient place for 
seeing them. 

C. Nbwman (against), Alfred Herbert, Arthur Her- 
bert, Coleman (for), A. Eoch (against), and M. D. Ruokbr 
abo spoke. 

H. Q. A. Brown replied. He hoped the result would be 
that the Meeting would be always held at the Palace ; and 
dealt in some detail with the arguments of the opposition. 

On a division the members were : — For the motion, 26 ; 
against, 13 ; (five members did not vote): 

The motion was therefore carried. 



A Rn)E ACROSS FRANCE INTO SWITZERLAND. 
(OoMTnruxD from paox 289.) 

We Spent Sunday at Versailles, going to the English 
church in the morning, and spending the afternoon in seeing 
part of the palace — ^it would take a week to see the whole — 
and grounds and listening to the band. Unfortunately we 
could not see the wonderful fountains, as they only play 
on the first Sunday in each month. 

Next morning we had a fair run as far as Choisy, which 
we had to walk through, and then to get over three miles 
of pav4 on the main road in the same style ; so were glad 
to stop at Villeneuve-St. -Georges (18 miles) for lunch. 



Thence we had a better road, though with firequent paved 
villages, through M^lun (34 miles from Versailles) to 
Montereau. M^luu is a clean little town, with a rather 
handsome hotel de vUU, etc., but after our experience at 
the " Hotel St. Nicholas" last year we ^id not feel disposed 
to stop there. Three or four miles out of Montereau we 
overtook a local telegraph messenger on a machine con- 
sisting of a 86 or 40-inch front wheel, over which he sat, 
and two small wheels behind, between which was a box in 
which he carried his d^ches. It was guiltless of rubber, 
and made as much noise as a fire engine ; but the pace at 
which the owner got along, especially uphill, was really 
surprising : it is needless to say, however, that we soon left 
him behind. Almost directly afterwards we were challenged 
by a man with a fast trotting horse, which was also satis- 
factorily disposed of, though not quite so easily. The 
approach to Montereau is pretty— down a winding hill 
overlooking the Seine. The " Hotel du Grand Monarque " 
being full, we put up at the Croix Verte," and had no 
• reason to regret the change. Distance for the day, 
52} miles. 

From Montereau to Sens (22 miles), through Pont-sur- 
Yonne is an easy run, except that several long paved 
villages have to be walked through between the first and 
last named towns. Things used to be worse in this respect 
than they are now, for when Norman Morris passed this 
way in 1876, there were four and a half kilometres of pav6 
in the open country near Villeneuve-le-Guyard. This has 
now been taken up ; they were at work upon the last por- 
tion of it when we passed this year. 

Starting from Montereau about 8.30, we hoped to lunch 
at Sens and sleep at Tonnerre (67 miles) but, as usual, we 
were disappointed. We had not gone a mile before Williams' 
front wheel again locked suddenly, — a nasty fall over the 
handles being only avoided by great presence of mind and 
agility. The troublesome process of taking ofiT the crank 
and undoing the bearings had to be gone through by the 
roadside, with the result of finding that the outer 
(adjustable) plate of the bearings was badly cracked. We 
found also that the set-screw which should have fixed this 
plate was broken and did not act, thus allowing the plate, 
which moved very easily, to be screwed up gradually by the 
firiction of the hub until the balls were jammed. After 
some discussion Williams very pluckily decided to go on, 
and take his chance of a cropper, rather than break up 
the party. 

Thanks to this detention and the/MZv/ above referred to, 
we did not reach Pont-sur-YonneXl4 miles) till past eleven ; 
so stopped and had a very good cUjsuner at an hotel 
(de TEcu) with a most unpromiring exterior, which we had 
passed as hopeless until we were assured there was no other. 
After lunch the Doctor was dispatched, in charge of the 
chambermaid, to the telegraph office for the purpose of 
telegraphing to London for a new bearing to be sent to 



Digitized by 



Google 



252 



LONDON BICYCLE. CLUB GAZETTE. 



Dijon. The old gentleman, who was found presiding over 
this somewhat primitive establishment, said he had never 
been called upon to telegraph to London before, and seemed 
to feel highly honoured at l^ing requested to do so, spelling 
out the English words most carefully, and taking a great 
deal of trouble over the message. 

After leaving Pont-sur-Yonne only the towns are paved, 
or, at all events, very few of the villages,' and it is no 
longer necessary to dismount every two or three miles on 
account of those infamous French cobbles. 

The cathedral at Sens is well worth a visit ; it contains 
some curious old painted glass, and the trSsors preserved in 
the sacristie are very interesting. These consist of some 
very fine old tapestry, splendid Limoges enamels, ivory 
carvings, and a number of valuable antiquities which have 
been brought here from different churches in the diocese 
for safe keeping. Near the further end of the town is a 
very curious old timber-built house, to which an extra- 
ordinary amount of antiquity is assigned. 

Between Sens and St. Florentin there are two roads. 
The main road through the town goes through Joigny, and 
makes a very considerable circuit, following the course of a 
river ; it is, I believe, nearly level. But there is a more 
direct, though rather hilly, route vid Cerisiers, and this we^ 
determined to take. Turning to the left near the entrance 
to the town, we followed the road to Troyes as far as 
Malay-le-roi, where we turned off to the right for Cerisiers. 
So far there is nothing difiBcult, but the hill out of Cerisiers 
is a stiff one, and is best walked, though one of our party 
rode it. After this the road ascends gradually for several 
miles, but all quite ridable and with good surface. At the 
141st kilometre stone (distance from Paris) a long run 
down begins and continues to the junction with the other 
road near St. Florentin. Going the other way the gradients 
would be less favourable, and probably the longer route 
would be best. It is ll\ miles from Sens to Cerisiers, and 
16 miles on to St. Florentin. On arriving at the latter 
place we found we should not be able to reach Tonnerre 
Defore dark, so put up at the " Hotel de la Porte d'llo," fair, 
at the upper part of the town. A bagman with a swivel eye, 
who sat opposite to us at the table d'/iote, afforded us great 
amusement, first trying to show off his importance by 
bullying the waiter roundly, and then emptying almost the 
whole of a large dish of wild strawberries on to his own plate, 
after making a great show of politeness in helping us first. 
Distance for the day 49} miles. ^ 

(7b be continued). 



THE BOXING DAY PAPERCHASE. " . 

The arrangements made by tt^'Siib-Committee appointed 
for this purpose are as follows': — Tlie hares will be started 
from the King's Arms," Katharine Street, Croydon, at 
one p.m., and after running a more or less circular course, 
will return to the starting point about four p.m. At the 
" King's Arms," washing and dressing accommodation will 
be provided (at a charge of 3d. each), and warm baths for 
those who may require them (at a cost of Is.) At five 
p.m. dinner will be served (at a charge of 3s each), and 
as a piano will be provided it is hoped that members .will 
not forget to bring a few songs clown with them. The 
Committee also hope that members and friends who may 
not be able to join in the Chase will come/to the Dinner, 
and assist afterwards at the social evening. 

Oliver Thorn (W.) and A. Barker (S.E.) have been 



appointed hares, and J. C. Oswald (S.E.) and A. W. Barrett 
(N.E.) will act as pace-makers. By this last arrangement 
the pack will be kept in hand, and will work more or less 
together until four or five miles from home, when the pace 
makers will give the word " to scatter," and the hounds 
will make for Croydon by any route th^y please. 

Prizes will be given to the first three hounds, being 
members or memb^'s brothers, that either catch both hares, 
or failing that, first reach the "King's Arms"; but the 
pace makers are empowered to disqualify from a prize any 
one who refuses to work with the rest of the pack until 
the before mentioned order has been given, or who covers 
any of the distance otherwise than on K>ot. 

No entry fee will be required, and members are invited 
to introduce anv friends who may be interested in the 
sport. Those who intend to run are requested to inform 
me by Deeember 20th of their intention, though their not 
having done so need not prevent them from joining at the 
last moment. Also, I shall be glad to receive the names of 
those who wish to dine at the " King's Arms," that the 
necessary arrangements may be made. No rule as to dress 
can be laid down, since some members intend running in 
knickerbockers and stockings, some in trousers and socks, 
and others in University costume; but probably those 
adopting the last will be in a considerable minority. The 
hares will start at one o'clock punctually, but in the event 
of there having been continuous frost, and consequently 
skating, the Chase will be postponed. 

The train for East Croydon leaves Victoria, 11.50 a.m. ; 
London Bridge, 12.5 p.m. 

Ashley W. Barrett, Captain. 

42, Finsbury Square, E.C. 
{Far ik$ SufhOonmiUee appomUd to conduct the abwe,) 



PRACTICE FOR THE PAPERCHASK 
On the following Friday evenings (November 30th, 
December 7th, December 14th, December 21st), some 
members of the N.E., and two or three men from other 
districts will rendezvous at Forest Gate Station, Essex, at 
7.30 p.m., and Snaresbrook Station at 8.45. We shall be 
glad of the company of any who may care to join us in a 
short run of an hour or so over good gravel paths. A train 
leaves Liverpoot Street Station at 7.10 p.m., and the 
opportunity for cl^anging into running dress, with washing 
accommodation, etc., will be provided for those who may 
wish for it. Ashley W. Barbbtt.
